meta.source: The Guardian World Cup 2026 complete player guide

players.name: Melvin Mastil
players.bio: Most Algeria fans know very little about French-born Mastil, who was only called up a few months before the World Cup, but he has long been a fan of the Fennecs. Mastil, who qualifies for Algeria through a grandfather, told L'Équipe: “I grew up supporting the Algerian national team. When I saw them at the 2014 World Cup and the 2019 Africa Cup of Nations, it made me dream.” Now he has the chance to build a long-term future with his grandad’s country. The 26-year-old has spent his entire professional career in Switzerland and has most recently been on loan in the second division with Stade Nyonnais. With Algeria in need of a refresh in the goalkeeping department, Mastil cuts an intriguing profile, standing at 1.93m and captaining his club. His potential ceiling appears higher than that of other Algerian goalkeepers in Europe, making him a strong candidate to serve as a reserve keeper for now.

players.name: Aissa Mandi
players.bio: The consummate professional, with more international caps than any other Algerian, having passed the 100 mark back in 2024. Perhaps his former coach with the national team, Djamel Belmadi, described him best when he said: “Aissa has natural leadership skills and a significant impact on the players, both in matches and in training. He’s a good person and a positive presence within the group. Even his lack of speed, he compensates for with intelligence and sound judgment when it comes to taking initiative.” Mandi is one of two veterans in this squad, alongside Riyad Mahrez, who was also in the 2014 World Cup squad that pushed Germany to its limit in the Last 16. He has a big role to play this summer, on the pitch or off it, in being a calming, authoritative presence for the younger defenders. When Algeria qualified for this summer he wrote online: “Since 29 March 2022 only one thing has been on our minds: bringing Algeria back where it belongs: to the World Cup. Pride and determination for what comes next."

players.name: Achraf Abada
players.bio: A real late bloomer, Abada made his international breakthrough at the age of 26. For much of his career the centre-back went under the radar, playing in Algeria for MC El Eulma and ASO Chlef, but he really turned heads with his displays at last year’s Fifa Arab Cup in Qatar, impressing with his speed, athleticism and relentless aggression. In January he signed for USM Alger on a lucrative long-term deal, with the deal featuring a big sell-on clause and another clause reportedly stipulating that his new club would fund his old club’s pre-season training camp this summer if Abada made the World Cup squad. Good news for ASO Chlef, then. There are still areas for improvement when it comes to playing with the ball at his feet, but his defensive instincts alone earned him his call-up this summer.

players.name: Mohamed Tougai
players.bio: Algeria did not announce their World Cup squad until 31 May, significantly later than most of the other participating nations. By then, there were few surprises left. Journalists stationed outside the airport had already photographed and filmed players arriving for camp, gradually piecing together Vladimir Petkovic's squad before the official announcement. The notable exception was Tougai. The defender had returned to Algeria several weeks earlier from Tunisia, where his club endured a disappointing campaign and failed to win the league title for only the second time in the past decade. His inclusion raised eyebrows, particularly as Petkovic has selected six centre-backs for the tournament. Among them, Tougai is arguably the least versatile. A traditional, no-nonsense defender, he prioritises winning physical duels and clearing danger over building attacks or playing line-breaking passes through midfield.

players.name: Zineddine Belaid
players.bio: A reliable, if unspectacular, centre-back. Belaid’s greatest achievement came in 2023 when he captained USM Alger to a CAF Confederation Cup title. A brief stint in Belgium with Sint-Truiden followed, before a big-money return to Algeria’s most decorated club, JS Kabylie last year. Belaid is unlikely to start matches, but he is capable of slotting into defence when Algeria need to protect a lead, particularly against aerial threats. At the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations he made three appearances and scored a headed goal from a corner against Equatorial Guinea, which he then dedicated to his late father, writing online: "My first goal with the national team, on Africa's biggest stage. I felt your presence with me, dad. This goal is dedicated to you, to my family, to the coaching staff who believed in me, and to the wonderful Algerian supporters.”

players.name: Ramiz Zerrouki
players.bio: In a team of twinkle-toed ball-players, Zerrouki runs against the grain. Schooled in the Ajax academy, he has mastered the game’s fundamental principles: always in the right position at the right time, with his passes tending to be safe and horizontal. He provides a balance to a team that can be unpredictable, freeing up his more forward-thinking teammates. He has earned the trust of successive Algeria coaches since his debut in 2021, but for a country that prides itself on technical flair, he isn’t a fan favourite, and often ends up being the first player to take the brunt of the blame when things go wrong. He spent last season on loan from Feyenoord back at one of his former clubs, Twente, in a bid to secure more regular game time, and his spot in this summer’s squad. It paid off.

players.name: Riyad Mahrez
players.bio: Very few footballers can match what Mahrez has achieved in the game. Among his accolades, the Algeria captain boasts five Premier League titles, a Champions League winner’s medal, two FA Cups, three League Cups, PFA and CAF player of the year awards, and many other individual and club honours. The only blemish on his career for Algeria fans is that lack of a stellar display at a World Cup. In 2014 his only appearance came in a 2-1 defeat to Belgium where he registered 37 touches, was dispossessed three times and was substituted in the 71st minute. He did not feature again as Algeria surged to the Last 16. Now aged 35 and nearing the twilight of his career, he is determined to lead Algeria into the quarter-finals of the World Cup for the very first time. Such an achievement would elevate his status in African footballing lore. Still, some fans argue that he no longer has the legs to justify a starting place for Algeria, and that it may already be time to hand the mantle to rising talents such as Feyenoord’s Anis Hadj Moussa.

players.name: Houssem Aouar
players.bio: Premier League fans might recognise Aouar’s name from the many transfer rumours he was involved in during his final year at Lyon in 2023. In the end none of the moves came off, and instead he joined Roma, looking solid there under José Mourinho in a 3-5-2 system. His stay was brief, though: the following year he was drawn to a big-money move to Al-Ittihad, with his mentor, Karim Benzema, playing a key role in the move. Aouar, a former France youth international who was born in Lyon, switched international allegiances in 2023, saying: “It had been going through my head for a while, but I didn’t want to make an approach to Algeria myself because I was afraid of coming across as an opportunist. But when the president held his hand out to me it seemed like it was just meant to be. It means a lot to me. In all honesty, after choosing to play for France I regretted it.” With Algeria his role is to drift between the lines, receive on the half-turn, and quickly move the ball from midfield into the attackers’ feet. Don’t be surprised to see him chip in with a goal or two, either, arriving late into the box.

players.name: Amine Gouiri
players.bio: Plenty of Lyon players have been dubbed “the next Karim Benzema” since the former Ballon d'Or winner rose to prominence in the mid-2000s. While all have understandably fallen short, Gouiri is perhaps the one who most closely resembles Benzema stylistically. “Karim likes to be involved in the game, by both creating chances and scoring goals,” Gouiri told Fifa.com. “Even though stats are incredibly important for a centre-forward these days, what I love is being part of the game. Combining the two is great.” With Algeria he does that to solid effect, typically starting the match as a target man before switching positions with his teammate Mohamed Amoura on the left wing. From a technical standpoint he has very few weaknesses, rarely misplacing a pass or shanking a shot. However, he does have a tendency to, at times, let the game pass him by. He will need to increase his number of touches on the ball if Algeria’s attack is to click on all cylinders in North America.

players.name: Farès Chaïbi
players.bio: Terraillon, on the outskirts of Lyon, is probably best known for producing Karim Benzema. But when I visited the suburb before the 2022 Champions League final, locals wanted to talk instead about a youngster at Toulouse named Farès Chaïbi. “He’s the full package and wants to play for Algeria,” a teenager named Younes told me. A few months later, Chaïbi won Ligue 1 Rookie of the Year, reflecting his eight goals in 41 appearances across all competitions. That debut season earned him a move to Eintracht Frankfurt, but in Germany he has blown hot and cold. A broad-shouldered midfielder capable of playing on the wing or through the middle in a box-to-box role, his set-piece delivery from corners and free-kicks makes him particularly valuable to Algeria; the side has steadily improved in that area under Vladimir Petkovic.

players.name: Anis Hadj Moussa
players.bio: The most exciting dribbler in the side, and that isn’t an easy title to claim. A Paris native, Hadj Moussa joined Feyenoord in 2024 and has made a huge impact, becoming vice-captain and one of the Eredivisie’s most outstanding talents, regularly leading the dribbling rankings and, under Robin van Persie, hugely improving his end product. “It’s interesting to see how he is developing,” the former Arsenal striker said recently. “I don’t think he’ll take Riyad Mahrez’s place for Algeria because he’s a tremendous player, but Mahrez has hinted that he’ll retire after the World Cup, which would free up the spot for Hadj Moussa.” While Van Persie is probably right that Mahrez stays ahead in the pecking order, Hadj Moussa is still expected to play significant minutes, often coming on when Mahrez’s ageing legs begin to fade around the hour mark. He is another player linked with a possible move to England this summer.

players.name: Nadhir Benbouali
players.bio: Benbouali has often felt that the footballing world has not been fair to him. As a teenager, he was not offered a professional contract by his hometown club ASO Chlef, despite scoring more than 30 goals in the youth leagues and despite his father working as a doctor for the senior team. "I never understood why," he told Algerian outlet El Heddaf. "Yet, everything in life happens for a reason and I was lucky enough to sign with the best academy in Algeria, Paradou AC." At Paradou, Benbouali developed into one of the Algerian league's most prolific strikers, but a call-up to the national team remained elusive. It was only a few months ago, after finishing third in the Hungarian league's scoring charts and helping ETO FC secure the league title, that he finally earned his reward. Benbouali is Algeria's only true target man, a physically imposing striker who excels in aerial duels and offers a different dimension in attack. Even if he does not start, he is likely to play a role during the World Cup.

players.name: Jaouen Hadjam
players.bio: Were it not for the exceptional talent of Rayan Aït-Nouri, Hadjam would be Algeria’s first-choice left-back. A product of the famed Parisian banlieues, Hadjam combines the technical assurance he developed as a youngster with mature defensive reliability. His quality on the ball means he can also do a job as a left-footed right-back; so he and Aït-Nouri could work in the same line-up. But breaking into the starting XI remains an uphill task given the competition ahead of him. Reports last winter suggested that Besiktas had reached an agreement with Young Boys for his transfer, only for the move to collapse after the player failed to agree personal terms with the Turkish side. He was at the centre of a row in 2023 when his coach at Nantes, Antoine Kombouaré, dropped him for a Ligue 1 match because he refused to break his fast during Ramadan. Kombouaré insisted: “It’s not a punishment. It’s not a controversy. I support those who fast, but on matchdays, you should not fast. I set the rules.”

players.name: Hicham Boudaoui
players.bio: Riyad Mahrez may be Algeria’s most famous name, and Ibrahim Maza its most exciting prospect, but the Fennecs’ most indispensable player is definitely Hicham Boudaoui. Algeria has no shortage of inventive attacking midfielders who can dribble and score. What it often lacks is a midfielder capable of covering as much ground as Boudaoui does on a consistent basis. In one passage of play, you can catch the Bechar native shielding the backline like a traditional No 6, then surging forward in a more box-to-box role to support his wingers, and even drifting into the No 10 pocket. He regularly ranks among the top runners in Ligue 1 in terms of distance covered, averaging between 11km and 12km per match, and will be tireless in the middle for Algeria this summer. He was being linked with clubs including Brighton, Marseille and Galatasaray before the tournament; the list of suitors is likely to grow.

players.name: Rayan Aït-Nouri
players.bio: You have to be brave to lunge in on Rayan Aït-Nouri when he has the ball at his feet. It’s no exaggeration to describe the France-born full-back as one of the best dribbling defenders in world football, a defender who looks effortlessly comfortable carrying the ball into the final third and turning defensive sequences into attacking ones. He made the decision to represent Algeria in 2023, saying: “Algeria is my roots … When I’m in Algeria, I’m always happy; my mood changes as soon as I arrive there. The people are smiling, they’re friendly, everyone has their hands on their heart; it’s magnificent.” With the national side his form has not always been as strong as it has at club level, with mixed displays at last year’s Africa Cup of Nations, but his pre-World Cup form at City – whom he joined from Wolves for £31m last year – was encouragingly strong. He should be a huge asset this summer.

players.name: Oussama Benbot
players.bio: Benbot started the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations as Algeria's backup goalkeeper, and his tournament ended in unlikely disappointment. After the Fennecs won their opening two group-stage matches, coach Vladimir Petkovic rotated much of his squad for the final group game against Equatorial Guinea. Benbot, however, remained on the bench as third-choice goalkeeper Anthony Mandrea was given the nod. Frustrated by the decision, the USM Alger player announced his international retirement in a statement on Instagram after the tournament. His absence, however, proved short-lived. Injuries to Luca Zidane and Melvin Mastil prompted the Algerian Football Federation to reach out and make clear that they still counted on him. Benbot, who had an outstanding season with USM Algiers and helped the club win the Caf Confederation Cup against Zamalek, agreed to return. "Perhaps I made the decision [to retire] too quickly. I had a discussion with the president of the federation to explain my situation and what was on my mind. I thank the president of the federation because we found common ground and he understood my position," he said.

players.name: Rafik Belghali
players.bio: Belghali came dangerously close to missing this tournament. After Algeria’s quarter-final exit at last year’s Africa Cup of Nations against Nigeria, he went viral for chasing the referee down the tunnel in Marrakesh, venting his frustration at the officials. He said later: “After the final whistle, I approached the referee to shake his hand. His reaction surprised me and, in the heat of the moment, I briefly followed him to ask why. There was no aggression or intention of confrontation. I do acknowledge, however, that I let the situation drag on longer than it should have.” A ban followed but, fortunately for the Paris-born wing-back, the suspension was limited to 2027 Afcon qualifiers. Over the past year he has been a key asset for both Verona and the national team. While he can be exposed in one-on-one defending, his engine is relentless, constantly surging forward to provide width and overlap Riyad Mahrez, who likes to drift inside. That dynamic is a crucial part of Algeria’s attacking structure.

players.name: Mohamed Amine Amoura
players.bio: It took a 19-year-old Amoura just a few minutes to announce himself in his senior debut for ES Setif, scoring twice in the derby against arch-rivals CA Bordj Bou Arreridj. The double set the tone for a career defined by explosiveness. From ES Setif to Lugano to Royale Union Saint-Gilloise and now at Wolfsburg, he has consistently risen to new challenges whether deployed on the left wing or as a false nine, with his acceleration and directness making him a constant threat. During the World Cup qualifiers Amoura led the African scoring charts with 10 goals and is now widely regarded as Algeria’s most decisive attacking weapon. In February, though, he made negative headlines when he mocked a Congolese supporter known for spending matches at the Africa Cup of Nations standing still with his right arm raised in the style of a famous statue of the country’s revered first leader Patrice Lumumba. Amoura, who mimicked the fan then fell to the ground as though the statue had been toppled, later apologised online: "I wasn't aware of what the person or symbol in the stands represented. I simply wanted to joke around, in a good-natured way, without any ill intent or desire to provoke anyone.”

players.name: Nabil Bentaleb
players.bio: Twelve years after his last World Cup appearance, Bentaleb's career has come full circle. In 2014, the then 19-year-old midfielder was a regular starter for Tottenham and had just impressed at the World Cup, with a seemingly bright future ahead of him. However, recurring disciplinary issues disrupted several spells at clubs from Spurs to Schalke, and he even found himself without a team during the 2020-21 season. Bentaleb gradually rebuilt his career in France, returning to his hometown club, Lille, where his football journey began. His greatest challenge in life came in June 2024 when he suffered a heart attack during training. After consulting with his former teammate Christian Eriksen and receiving a pacemaker, he fought his way back into the Lille side, became captain, and produced one of the best seasons of his career. "He never let go. He believed in himself, and he believed in the people who looked after him," said Lille coach Bruno Genesio.

players.name: Adil Boulbina
players.bio: He’s an international newcomer, but Boulbina already has one stand-out moment for Algeria on his CV. In his first outing at last year’s Africa Cup of Nations, coming on as a sub against DR Congo, he scored a stunning goal late in extra-time to send Algeria through to the quarter-finals. He was trained in the famed Paradou Academy, widely regarded as one of the best in Africa, and finished the 2024-25 season as the top scorer in the Algerian Ligue Professionnelle 1 with 20 goals. That form earned him a big-money move to Al-Duhail, where he reunited with former Algeria coach Djamel Belmadi. He is unlikely to start this summer, but his directness and ability to change games make him a valuable option off the bench.

players.name: Ramy Bensebaini
players.bio: Not many centre-halves have nutmegs and chipped finishes in their highlight reels, but Bensebaini is one of those. A regular for Borussia Dortmund, he is Algeria’s standout defender. Gifted with a wand-like left foot, he is central to Algeria’s build-up play from the back. Beyond his composure on the ball, he brings intensity, pace, and physical presence, although he can be prone to lapses in concentration. That vulnerability was evident this year in the Champions League play-off tie against Atalanta, where he was repeatedly at fault before conceding a penalty and being sent off. Just over a decade ago he was on trial at Arsenal, but it came to nothing, due to paperwork. "I spent time there, a month on trial, in 2015," Bensebaini told Ouest France in 2019. "I was training with the first team and I was playing matches with the reserves. I had spoken several times with Arsene Wenger. In the end, five coaches decided my future. Four of them gave me the thumbs up and would have taken me, but one of them said it was impossible to sign me, because I didn't have a European passport and didn't have enough international caps."

players.name: Ibrahim Maza
players.bio: Aged just 20, Maza is clearly going places. A fine attacking talent, he excels at drifting into pockets of space, receiving the ball on the half-turn. His low centre of gravity and powerful lower body allow him to ride challenges and show off the technical quality that sets him apart, with incisive passes and fearless dribbles. Since arriving at Bayer Leverkusen he has drawn plenty of comparisons to Florian Wirtz. Not that he is pleased about it. “I don’t like being compared like that,” Maza told the club’s website. “He became the most expensive signing in the Premier League, and I hope I can be at his level someday, but I’m not there yet.” Born in Berlin, he played for Germany up to Under-20 level before declaring for Algeria last year. It was a calculated decision. “It’s purely for sporting reasons, because it aligns better with my footballing future,” he said. “In the German national team, the competition for my position is extremely fierce, so it's tough to break into the starting eleven or find space in that squad. From a sporting perspective, representing Algeria gives me a greater chance to play in the World Cup and compete at the highest level. That's why I made this decision with full confidence."

players.name: Luca Zidane
players.bio: “If I called him up, it’s because he deserves it,” Vladimir Petkovic told the Algerian press after a few eyebrows were raised by Luca Zidane’s first appearance in the national squad last year, the manager denying that the young keeper’s famous dad had influenced his decision. During the Africa Cup of Nations, Zinedine senior attended three of Algeria’s matches to support his son and, at times, it did feel like he overshadowed Luca’s impressive performances in goal. The Granada keeper, who played for France at various youth levels before declaring for Algeria, isn’t the tallest No1 at 1.83m, but he is a good communicator, and his distribution is excellent, which allows the side to play out from the back with composure. Whenever he makes a save, Algerian fans chant “Zizou, Zizou, Zizou.”

players.name: Yacine Titraoui
players.bio: “The Algerian Verratti” is how Titraoui is often seen by North African fans. No Algerian player is better at sitting in front of the defence and dictating play with precise, progressive passing than the Charleroi midfielder. His pass completion rate hovers around 85%, and he excels at reading the game and making timely interceptions. Another trait he shares with Verratti, though, is a tendency to pick up bookings. After two full seasons with Charleroi, he will be hoping that a positive World Cup showing this summer could secure a move to one of Europe’s elite leagues. In the months before the tournament he was linked with several clubs in England including Leeds, Sunderland and Wolves.

players.name: Farès Ghedjemis
players.bio: Ghedjemis enjoyed a breakthrough campaign this season, scoring 15 goals in 37 matches from the wing as he helped Frosinone secure promotion back to Serie A after a three-year absence. He is regarded as one of the standout players in Serie B and has generated considerable excitement among supporters ahead of next season. At first glance, Ghedjemis appears to fit a familiar mould for Algeria. A left-footed winger born in the suburbs of Paris who operates primarily on the right flank, he belongs to a generation heavily influenced by Riyad Mahrez's style of play. However, there is one key difference. Unlike the many gifted Algerian wingers who prefer receiving the ball to their feet, Ghedjemis is constantly looking to make runs in behind opposition defences. That attribute is presumably what convinced Petkovic to select him ahead of other creative wide players such as Ilan Kebbal, Badredine Bouanani and Mohamed Bachir Belloumi.

players.name: Samir Chergui
players.bio: It didn’t take Algeria fans long to work out what Chergui would bring to the national side when he came off the bench for his debut last year. No nerves, no hesitation - just an all-action display featuring a heated running battle with an opponent. Afterwards, goalkeeper Alexis Guendouz captioned footage on social media: “Chergui, my soldier.” In a squad characterised by technical flair, neon boots and expensive hairdos, Chergui is a balding old-school no-frills defender who just gets on with the job. He has matured since his early days at Auxerre where he was released after a series of disciplinary incidents. He kept working and was given a second chance by Paris FC, and has fully repaid their faith. He shares his stories with the club’s younger players. “I tell them to appreciate how lucky they are to be there. My example should serve as a lesson for them."

players.name: Juan Musso
players.bio: Musso’s career has been marked by progress from a very young age, even before he reached the level of maturity typically associated with goalkeepers. After taking his first steps at a local club in his hometown, San Nicolás, he joined Racing’s youth academy at 18. Two years later he won the league as the third-choice goalkeeper and made his official debut at 21. A season later, Udinese took a gamble on his potential and it paid off: three years later they quintupled their money as he moved to Atalanta for €20m. He is now the backup for Jan Oblak at Atlético Madrid, and increasingly looks a capable replacement: this season, with Oblak sidelined with a muscle strain, Musso became the first goalkeeper this century to keep a clean sheet in each of his first four appearances in La Liga (though he then shipped seven goals in his next three). “He is an important figure within the squad and the dressing room,” Diego Simeone says. “He brings energy wherever it’s needed and contributes much more than just his goalkeeping skills.”

players.name: Leonardo Balerdi
players.bio: Marseille captain will miss the tournament with a calf injury he picked up in training. No replacement has yet been named.

players.name: Nicolás Tagliafico
players.bio: He is one of the few remaining players Lionel Scaloni inherited from the 2018 World Cup squad. In time Tagliafico, through consistency and a high level of performance, has become a key figure. Having come through the ranks at Banfield, the left-back is known for his total dedication, commitment, and tireless work rate. On social media, as well as on his own streaming channels, he often shows his family side alongside his partner, influencer Caro Calvagni, and their two French bulldogs, Galo and Pepu. He also reveals a more intellectual side, showing interest in a wide range of topics beyond football - his favourite hobby is drawing, of which he says that “concentrating on just one thing helps to clear the mind”. After successful spells at Independiente and Ajax he now plays for Lyon. While he is not particularly tall he makes up for it with excellent timing and solid marking.

players.name: Gonzalo Montiel
players.bio: The man who united a nation in tears of pure joy by converting the penalty that decided the 2022 World Cup final, and with a single kick became immortal. “It was a unique, dreamlike moment,” he said. “So many things flashed before my eyes, sacrifices my family and I had made to get me to this position. When I scored, I burst into tears. It was a unique moment that defied explanation.” A product of River Plate’s youth academy, he returned to the club in early 2025 and is now one of its key figures. Although he began his career as a centre-back, Marcelo Gallardo moved him to right-back due to his physical attributes – a decision that proved inspired. In addition to the 2022 World Cup, he also won both Copa América titles of the Scaloni era and the Finalissima. He has a son named Thiago, is fond of tattoos, and shows great personality on the pitch – and especially in shootouts. As a child he had such unusually prominent cheeks that he was given the nickname El Cachete - the cheek - which has stuck with him ever since.

players.name: Leandro Paredes
players.bio: Paredes is a symbol of the Lionel Scaloni era with the national team. He was part of the generational shift when the coach arrived and has remained a constant presence ever since. A natural leader in the locker room, competitive and strong-willed, Paredes combines elegance, physicality and temperament on the pitch. He is known for his excellent vision and passing ability, as well as his strong ball recoveries. He came through the ranks at Boca Juniors, the club where he currently plays as captain, having returned there last year after 11 seasons in Europe that took him to teams including Roma, Paris St-Germain and Juventus. He is very close to key players in the squad such as Lionel Messi, Ángel Di María and Rodrigo De Paul and is considered one of the team’s leaders. Beyond football his other sporting passion is padel, which he plays frequently. “I play whenever I get the chance but I’m not the one who’s good at it,” Paulo Dybala said once. “Leandro’s the one who’s great, no doubt about it.”

players.name: Lisandro Martínez
players.bio: Martínez is a highly valued defender for the national team. His aggressive style and strong character have earned him the nickname El Carnicero, The Butcher. Though he is not very tall (1.75 m), he is excellent in the air and complements this with great technique. He began his career at Newell’s Old Boys, stood out at Defensa y Justicia, and made the leap to Europe when he was transferred to Ajax aged 21. He joined Manchester United three years later. He is a player deeply connected to his country, to the point that he has the Qatar World Cup tattooed on his body and often posts about current events in Argentina. He shows this passion in every interview, where he also tends to express his emotions openly. His main weakness is injuries; he has suffered some serious setbacks that have prevented him from consistently maintaining a starting spot in the national team, and after suffering a knee ligament injury last year said there were times “I didn’t want to play any more … I felt empty”.

players.name: Rodrigo De Paul
players.bio: Lionel Messi's footballing bodyguard. When he played for Racing and during his early years in Europe, De Paul was an attacking midfielder who stood out primarily for his creativity. But under Scaloni's guidance with the national team he has been transformed into a box-to-box player with great physical attributes - his nickname is Motorcito, Little Engine – key to the team's balance. As for his personality, he likes to visit his old neighborhood in Sarandí when he comes to Argentina, he's fun and outgoing (his humor is very important to the group), and he is a self-declared fashion fanatic: the varied looks he sports at each call-up often generate a lot of comments on social media. He's in a relationship with the famous Argentinian singer Tini Stoessel and currently plays for Inter Miami alongside his great friend Messi, after a four-year stint at Atlético Madrid.

players.name: Valentín Barco
players.bio: Barco's story is one of overcoming adversity and of reinvention. Part of the Argentinian national team's generational shift, he was born in the town of Veinticinco de Mayo and, as a child, travelled 450km at least four times a week in his father’s Renault to train at Boca Juniors, which didn’t have room for him in its academy residence. That dedication eventually paid off: he made his first-team debut at 16 and excelled at Boca as a left-back, earning a move to Brighton and he now shines at Strasbourg as a midfielder. “Having him in the squad there is the option to play him at left-back, though right now he’s doing very well as a midfielder,” Scaloni said earlier this year. “He can play across the midfield and is a very good asset. Now he can carry a team on his shoulders. The team practically plays however he wants.”

players.name: Julián Álvarez
players.bio: Born in the little village of Calchín, Córdoba, Álvarez worked tirelessly to become the star he is today. He had trials with Boca Juniors and Real Madrid aged just 11, and quickly made a name for himself at River Plate: at just 18 he was part of the squad that won the historic 2018 Copa Libertadores, and his footballing career exploded in 2021, when he scored 24 goals and provided 15 assists in 46 matches, lifting three titles. In doing so El Araña (The Spider) caught the eye of Lionel Scaloni, and he played a crucial role in the 2022 World Cup before moving to Manchester City, where he played for two seasons before he was signed by Atlético Madrid. His great goal-scoring instinct, his intensity in pressing and his ability to link up with midfielders make him one of the most complete strikers in the world. While he was in Manchester his older brothers Rafael and Agustín played for Abbey Hey in the North West Counties League First Division South; since his move to Madrid they have signed for that city’s newly-created Argentinian diaspora side, Club Argentino de Fútbol.

players.name: Lionel Messi
players.bio: To become the best in the world (and, for many, the greatest in history), Messi had to spend his teenage years away from home. Since Newell's Old Boys couldn’t afford the growth hormone treatment he needed, he moved from Rosario to Barcelona, with his father Jorge, at the age of 13 for a trial. By the time he left to join Paris St-Germain 21 years later, he had won seven Ballons d’Or and the hearts of the world. To his innate talent, he had to add a mental fortitude that proved crucial throughout his career – especially in never giving up after losing finals with the national team, which he endured at the 2014 World Cup and the 2015 and 2016 Copa Américas. This resilience ultimately led him to guide his country to two continental titles and to the pinnacle of success in Qatar. He will turn 39 during the group stage, and is now spending the final years of his professional career at Inter Miami, where he is also part of the club’s ownership group. There, he is surrounded by friends football has given him, such as Luis Suárez, and is enjoying his 'last dance' while helping build a new era for the club, including a stadium that will feature a stand named in his honour.

players.name: Giovani Lo Celso
players.bio: Giovani Lo Celso will finally have a chance at redemption after what happened in 2022, when he suffered a muscle injury shortly before the start of the World Cup and had to watch the team win the trophy without him. “Every kid dreams of being in the most beautiful tournament in the world. I tried everything I could, but there was no way,” he wrote at the time. Despite this setback, he still travelled with the team. He had already been part of the squad that won the 2021 Copa América and the Finalissima, and he was also included in the squad for the 2024 Copa América. A product of Rosario Central, he is a creative midfielder known for his excellent left foot and a level of talent that has taken him to clubs such as PSG and Tottenham, as well as spells in Spain with Villarreal and Real Betis. Off the pitch, he is very family-oriented and maintains close friendships with Leandro Paredes and Ángel Di María.

players.name: Gerónimo Rulli
players.bio: Rulli is a goalkeeper who combines technique with great resilience. Although he has spent much of his time with the national team in the shadow of Emi Martínez, his professionalism has turned him into a silent pillar of Lionel Scaloni’s era. He was part of the squads that won the Copa América in 2024, the Finalissima, and the World Cup in Qatar, where his role in supporting the group was fundamental. He emerged from Estudiantes de La Plata, where he broke clean-sheet records and caught everyone's attention at a young age, and had stints at major European teams such as Manchester City, Real Sociedad, Villarreal and Ajax before joining Marseille in 2024. Rulli is known for keeping a low profile and accepting supporting roles without losing his competitive edge, but at club level he is now a first choice and says the move to France has reignited his passion for the game. “I feel comfortable and happy,” he says. “I feel fulfilled in every way.”

players.name: Cristian Romero
players.bio: Romero is one of the most respected figures in the current Argentinian national team. With an aggressive style of play and outstanding technical ability, he quickly earned the admiration of Argentinian fans and also of Tottenham, where he plays today and serves as the team captain. Nicknamed Cuti since infancy (because at the time his sister Aldana couldn’t say his actual name) he marries speed in anticipating plays with excellence in one-on-one duels with the added bonus of bringing a goal threat from set-pieces. He emerged from the youth divisions of Club Atlético Belgrano de Córdoba, but his career took off in Italy, where he played for Genoa and Atalanta – but not, despite being on their books for a couple of years, Juventus – before making the leap to the Premier League. He was one of Lionel Scaloni’s key players in the conquests of the two Copa Américas, the Finalissima, and the World Cup in Qatar.

players.name: Exequiel Palacios
players.bio: One of the most technically gifted midfielders to emerge from River Plate’s academy in recent years, Palacios was just 20 when he won the Copa Libertadores with his childhood club. Real Madrid came close to signing him, but injuries ultimately derailed the deal. Instead he joined Bayer Leverkusen in early 2020, with his current contract – extended last year – running to 2030. “Pala’s value to us has increased every season,” the club’s sporting director, Simon Rolfes, said. “His vast international experience as well as his outstanding strategic and individual qualities as a No 6 make him a key part of the team.” Born in Famaillá in Tucumán province, Palacios was welcomed back after the 2022 World Cup win to be declared an Illustrious Citizen. “He is a role model for all the young people in the interior of the country, and especially for those from Tucumán,” said the vice-governor, Sergio Mansilla.

players.name: Nicolás González
players.bio: Just as football brought González great joy with his two Copa América titles and the 2022 Finalissima, it also cruelly cost him his place at the Qatar World Cup, due to a muscle injury he suffered a few weeks before the tournament began. “It’s been my dream since I was a kid, and just when I was close to achieving it everything fell apart,” he said. This versatile, hard-working and prolific left-footed midfielder still plays a crucial role in Lionel Scaloni's side. His emergence in 2019 was surprising, and he quickly became one of the coach's favorite players. He developed and debuted at Argentinos Juniors, made the leap to Europe with Stuttgart and then played for Fiorentina and Juventus before arriving at Atlético Madrid. He is a close friend of Alexis Mac Allister, as they are from the same age group and played together in the youth ranks at Argentinos Juniors.

players.name: Thiago Almada
players.bio: Born in El Fuerte, the poor neighborhood of Buenos Aires that also produced Carlos Tevez, Almada started playing on the dirt fields of Club Santa Clara and today showcases his talent at Atlético Madrid, who paid €20m to bring him from Botafogo in 2025. He first came to attention as a youngster in Vélez Sarsfield's youth academy – and was named on the Guardian’s Next Generation shortlist in 2018 – and more so after he moved to Atlanta United and became one of the brightest talents in the MLS. He was part of the Argentina squad in Qatar at just 21, though he played for only six minutes, and has since become one of the most important players for the head coach Lionel Scaloni. Nicolás Otamendi calls him “a leader”; the Greek striker Giogrios Giakomakis, a teammate at Atlanta, said: “Thiago’s talent is amazing. His skill, his vision, it’s incredible.” He is known for his great technical ability, and a style of play that is a clear demonstration of the Argentinian street football tradition.

players.name: Giuliano Simeone
players.bio: Born in Rome because his father, Diego Simeone, was playing for Lazio at the time, Giuliano is nevertheless very Argentinian. He was brought up there and came up through River Plate's youth system until he was 16, when he moved to Atlético Madrid, the club that employs his father, and now benefits from his tireless work rate on the right wing. Inevitably he has faced down accusations of nepotism, people who told him he was in the team “only because I was Cholo’s son … people told me that a lot when I was a kid. Eventually it became normal to hear it. I don’t know if I’ve shut more than one mouth, but I’ve always tried to focus on myself. I’m a Simeone and I’m proud of my name, but I want to make a career under my own name, Giuliano.” He has also made Lionel Scaloni focus on him, and he is part of the national team’s process of generational renewal. He is demanding of himself (he says he watches every game again at home) and very close to his siblings, brothers Giovanni and Gianluca – both professional footballers – and sisters Francesca and Valentina. Football runs in the family.

players.name: Nico Paz
players.bio: Paz was born in Tenerife because his father, the former Argentina defender Pablo Paz – who played in the 1998 World Cup – was based there at the time, but he never doubted which country he wanted to represent. Nico, one of the Albiceleste’s most promising talents, is one of the gems secured by Scaloni’s staff thanks to their extensive scouting work in Europe. They had been tracking him since his time in Real Madrid’s youth academy, and his first call-up came with the Under-20 team in 2022, when he was just 17. “I love both countries, but in the end, I decided to represent Argentina,” he said. “I think it’s the country that represents me the most, because of how football is lived there and because of my father. It’s an honor to follow in his footsteps.” He has just finished his second season at Como but Real still own half of his playing rights and could seek to bring back a player Francesco Totti has described as “original, creative and mischievous” and said could “return to Madrid and become one of the best in the world”.

players.name: Nicolás Otamendi
players.bio: Otamendi may have grown to become one of the heroes of the national team but it has taken him time to reach that status, having been part of a generation that endured regular disappointment, alongside players such as Sergio Agüero, Javier Mascherano and even Lionel Messi, before the great redemption of 2022. After playing in the 2010 World Cup under Diego Maradona he was peripheral for several years, and was not picked in 2014, but this will be his fourth global tournament. A strong player with a huge personality, nicknamed El General because of his leadership (and his saluting goal celebration) he is captain and leader at Benfica. He is very good in the air, quick to close down opponents and physically strong. He carefully manages his diet (“I eat chicken, rice and salad practically every day, and only allow myself a little treat after matches”) and never stops training, even during his holidays. His tattoos are legendary. He got his first first at the age of 14 and they include Walter White, the protagonist of Breaking Bad, and Messi holding the World Cup trophy.

players.name: Alexis Mac Allister
players.bio: Although he may go unnoticed off the pitch, on it he reliably catches the eye. A product of Argentinos Juniors' youth system and with a stint at Boca Juniors, he debuted for the Albiceleste in September 2019 and won the 2022 Finalissima, the World Cup, and the 2024 Copa América, in addition to standing out for the Under-20 and Under-23 teams. A great passer of the ball, versatile enough to play in different positions across the midfield, and with an eye for goal, he made the leap to Europe when he joined Brighton in 2019 and since 2023 has been playing for Liverpool, who paid €60m for him. He is the son of former left-back Carlos Mac Allister and his brothers Kevin and Francis are both professional footballers. He likes to keep a diary and declares himself a "tattoo fanatic": he is decorated with, among other things, the names of his closest relatives, the date of his debut in the First Division, Jesus, a roulette wheel with the shirt numbers he has worn in his career and a drawing of himself as a child playing football.

players.name: José López
players.bio: López’s path to the World Cup has been tough and anything but direct. As a youngster, after joining Lanús’s youth academy following his release from Independiente, he had a loan spell at Club Atlético Colegiales de Tres Arroyos, which competed in Argentina’s amateur regional leagues. He needed to regain his confidence in football, and that’s exactly what he did: he scored plenty of goals and, upon returning to Lanús, got a few chances with the first team. From there his reputation spread and, in 2022, at the age of 21 he was sold to Palmeiras, where he still plays. To overcome those ups and downs, his mental strength, humility and the values instilled in him by his parents were crucial. Born in San Lorenzo, he is the son of a father who worked as a fisherman, travelling between Mar del Plata and Patagonia. Nicknamed Flaco – Skinny – he made his debut for the national team in October 2025. He is a powerful striker with very good technical ability and a natural instinct for goal.

players.name: Lautaro Martínez
players.bio: The second-highest scorer of Lionel Scaloni's era, behind only Leo Messi, Martínez has become one of the most important strikers in the history of the Argentinian national team. He played a key role in their Copa América victories in 2021 and 2024, as well as the 2022 Finalissima, although an ankle injury prevented him from showing his best in the 2022 World Cup. Born in Bahía Blanca, his career is the envy of any footballer: he began playing at Club Liniers in that city and then passed to Racing, where he progressed through the youth ranks and made his first-team debut. He has been playing for Inter for eight years, where he is the captain (winning two Serie A titles and reaching a Champions League final, among other achievements). His many tattoos include the motivational phrases "What doesn't kill me makes me stronger" and "Live the life you love. Love the life you live". His wife, Agustina Gandolfo, has posted on Instagram videos of Lautaro engaging in activities including playing the piano, drawing (which he resorts to when he can’t sleep) and doing the dusting.

players.name: Emiliano Martínez
players.bio: Martínez is one of the most important and beloved players in the history of the Argentinian national team. He quickly won over the fans with his charisma and cunning on the pitch, in addition to his achievements as a two-time Copa América champion, a World Cup winner, and a Finalissima champion. His penalty shootout heroics and outstanding saves – such as the famous stop against Randal Kolo Muani in the World Cup final against France – have turned him into a fan idol. His effusive celebrations have gone viral worldwide and are often imitated by supporters. Nicknamed Dibu since childhood, because of his resemblance to the main character from the Argentinian animated series Mi Familia es un Dibujo (My Family is a Drawing), he began his career at Independiente but signed his first professional contract with Arsenal in the Premier League. His path was not easy, as he went through several loan spells before finally earning a move to his current club, Aston Villa – becoming the most expensive Argentinian goalkeeper in history in the process – and a starting spot in the national team. He is a player who openly expresses his emotions and brings attention to mental health, often highlighting the importance of working with a psychologist throughout his career.

players.name: Enzo Fernández
players.bio: The speed with which Fernández integrated into the Argentinian national team is all the evidence you need of his immense talent: he debuted just two months before the start of the 2022 World Cup, made the squad at 21 years old and with only three friendly matches under his belt, and by the end of the group stage he was in the first XI. Father of Olivia and Benjamín, he has played for Chelsea since late 2022 when the €121m they spent to bring him from Benfica made him the most expensive Argentinian player in history. He won titles with River Plate and during his loan spell at Defensa y Justicia, enjoys fashion (he models for a clothing brand), and has tattoos on his back including a large lion, the World Cup trophy, and the phrase "God's timing is perfect". Less perfectly timed was his admission that quite fancies a move to Madrid, which saw him dropped for a couple of games earlier this season. He was named Enzo in honor of Enzo Francescoli, a River Plate legend. In 2024 he apologised to his Chelsea teammates in a private message after he was caught on video chanting racist and homophobic slurs about members of the France team.

players.name: Facundo Medina
players.bio: Medina never made his first-team debut for River Plate, yet he still holds the club close to his heart. In his own words, it lifted him out of the shantytown and helped shape him not only as a footballer but as a person, providing the tools that define him today as a high-quality left-footed defender. “It will always be my home because it’s where I was developed and raised,” he once said. Born in Villa Fiorito – the same neighborhood as Diego Maradona – he began his career as a left-back before transitioning to centre-back. He was soon transferred to Talleres, where he truly came into his own: he became a regular starter, earned a place in Argentina’s youth teams, and secured his move to Europe with Racing Lens. Thanks to his profile and consistent performances, he quickly caught the attention of Lionel Scaloni, who handed him his senior debut at just 21 years old. Now he is set to play in his first World Cup alongside several of the heroes of Qatar. This season, he has shown an excellent level while playing for Marseille.

players.name: Nahuel Molina
players.bio: Argentina’s starting right-back at the last World Cup, Molina continues to hold that position. Emerging from the youth ranks of Boca Juniors, where he arrived at a very young age from his native Córdoba but never nailed down a place in the side, his career gathered momentum at Defensa y Justicia and Rosario Central, before he made the inevitable leap to Europe. Hard-working but quite introverted, and always faithful to his close friends and family, Molina’s career truly got lift-off at Udinese, opening the doors of the Albiceleste. Today, he is an Atlético Madrid player and became an elite full-back thanks to his speed and endurance. He stays away from controversies and is an infrequent user of social media. In January he celebrated the birth of his daughter Allegra (with Barbara Occhiuzzi, his partner now for more than a decade). For Lionel Scaloni, he is a very important player in a difficult position ahead of the World Cup.

players.name: Mathew Ryan
players.bio: You could say the Socceroos captain has been around the block. The 33-year-old has played for 12 clubs across eight countries – including Brugge, Brighton and AZ Alkmaar, and even brief spells at Arsenal and Roma – in a career spanning more than 15 years. Now on the cusp of appearing at his fourth World Cup, the boy from Plumpton in Sydney’s west is the latest in a long line of quality Australian keepers, including Mark Bosnich and Zeljko Kalac. Another one of his predecessors, Mark Schwarzer, was his childhood hero and a teenage Ryan was at Sydney’s Olympic stadium in 2005 to witness Schwarzer and co famously beat Uruguay on penalties to qualify for Australia’s first World Cup in 32 years. Ryan now leads Australia to their sixth straight World Cup but in an alternate universe, he could be wearing a Three Lions shirt in North America – he also holds a British passport.

players.name: Milos Degenek
players.bio: A cross-border transfer in the year of a World Cup will always make fans nervous, but if there is a player who can pull it off then it surely must be Degenek. His move from Serbia to Cyprus with Apoel means he has now played in seven different countries, including the US. The muscular and composed central defender has appeared for the Socceroos more than 50 times, although he has never been a first XI lock. A serious heel injury in 2023 threatened to end his high-level career, but he returned to the Socceroos setup last year. In camp in November, he told the ABC winning a World Cup wasn’t hard to get your head around. "You get on the plane, you fly back to your base, and you go again and again,” he said. “And then in July, we all fly back to Australia and celebrate the World Cup”.

players.name: Alessandro Circati
players.bio: In the dressing room at Parma several years ago, Circati walked up to goalkeeper Gianluigi Buffon – who helped ruin the Socceroos’ World Cup dreams in 2006 – seeking advice. The teenager had to choose between Italy, his country of birth, and Australia, where he grew up, over who he would represent at international level. “What do you feel inside? Forget about everything else,” Buffon said, according to a Circati interview with ESPN. The young defender chose the Socceroos and has, despite losing most of a year to an ACL injury in 2024, already captained his national side by the age of 22. As a composed and skilful centre-back already proven in Serie A, Circati comes into the 2026 World Cup as the Socceroos’ most polished defender. There may be no one in the Australian squad more important than him to the coach, Tony Popovic.

players.name: Jacob Italiano
players.bio: After making his Socceroos debut against Canada in 2025, Italiano said it had been “a long journey” to get his first cap. But really, he was only 24 and the wait only felt so long because he packed his bags to join the youth pathway a whole decade earlier. Known for his pace and skill moving the ball up the wing, Italiano is also a versatile player, as comfortable in an attacking midfield position as he is at left-back or right-back. Despite calling Borussia Mönchengladbach home for five years, Italiano never got his chance to play in the Bundesliga, instead racking up more than 100 appearances for the reserves. But things are looking up after a move across the border – Italiano is now a regular starter for Grazer AK in the Austrian Bundesliga. With Lewis Miller suffering an achilles injury, Popovic may look to Italiano to step up.

players.name: Jordan Bos
players.bio: The attacking left-back has continued his steady rise from Melbourne City to Feyenoord while gradually becoming a key creative outlet for the national side. The Eredivisie giants have a long history of providing a home for Australian internationals and Bos has used the added advantage of Dutch heritage to not only settle quickly but make a stunning start to life in the Netherlands. Bos lifts the intensity of those around him with his pace and tireless running, and loves getting high up the pitch, overlapping relentlessly and delivering early crosses into the box. Despite his light frame the 23-year-old is prepared to get stuck into a tackle though his lack of defensive awareness can cost him at times. But with his high energy and enthusiasm just what Popovic expects from his players, Bos could spend time on a wing where his better traits are more effective.

players.name: Jason Geria
players.bio: A favourite of Ange Postecoglu, who oversaw his development at Brisbane Roar, Melbourne Victory and the national set-up, Geria started his career as a member of the Australian Institute of Sport football programme in Canberra before joining Brisbane’s youth team in 2011. A year later Postecoglou signed Geria at Victory, where he became a regular name on the team sheet. Geria played for Australia multiple times at youth level before Postecoglou (again) called him into the Socceroos squad bidding to reach the 2018 World Cup. He made his debut in a friendly against Greece in 2016 and eight years later, after stints in Japan and multiple A-League clubs, Tony Popovic recalled him to the Socceroos squad for 2026 World Cup qualification.

players.name: Mathew Leckie
players.bio: Leckie made the 23-man squad for the Russia World Cup in 2018, but it was at the tournament in Qatar four years later when he cemented himself as a Socceroos cult hero. The versatile winger provided one of Australian football’s all-time moments when he jinked inside Danish defender Joakim Mæhle, then back outside, before firing low past the despairing dive of Kasper Schmeichel to score the only goal of the game and send the Socceroos into the Last 16 - and fans assembled in the early hours back at home into raptures. Now 35, this will be Leckie’s fourth World Cup. At his first in 2014 his explosive acceleration and direct running made him one of Australia’s standout players and attracted interest from scouts from several big clubs, although he has always maintained he had no regrets over signing for German second division club FC Ingolstadt just a month earlier.

players.name: Connor Metcalfe
players.bio: One of Australia’s quiet achievers, the Melbourne City academy product initially burst on to the scene and captained the club when aged only 22 before making the move abroad. Metcalfe joined national team leader Jackson Irvine at St Pauli in 2022 and was key to the club’s promotion in his second season in Germany. A technical and tenacious midfielder, Metcalfe presses aggressively, is deceptively strong in a tackle and increasingly confident in dictating the tempo with the ball. But he is more likely to let his football do the talking, while letting teammates like Irvine instead fit in with their club’s social conscience. “It's pretty cool, all punk rock and that,” Metcalfe has said about playing for St Pauli. “I'm not punk rock one bit. I’m basic. Basic as. Short back and sides, that’s what we can call it.”

players.name: Mohamed Touré
players.bio: “If he carries on at this rate, he’ll be the greatest striker of all time.” So said one online comment after Australia’s latest next big thing announced himself to the Championship with a hat-trick on his full debut and a total of five goals in his first four appearances for Norwich. Touré was born in a Guinean refugee camp before moving to South Australia at a young age, and he counts two brothers - Al-Hasan and Musa - as fellow professional players, while his dad, uncle and sister have also played at a high level. After making his Socceroos debut at Wembley in October 2023 he said: “You have to thank your parents, the struggles they go through when you’re younger to get us to playing. Sometimes we forget where it all started and who actually put in the work from the start.” The family lived opposite a football pitch and that’s where the Touré boys spent hours honing their ball skills together. A debut for Adelaide United followed at the age of 15 before he headed to Europe, where his continued development first attracted the interest of Tony Popovic.

players.name: Ajdin Hrustic
players.bio: The versatile midfielder continues to tease as the playmaker that Australia is too often left crying out for as he battles the highs and lows of an injury-plagued career. Hrustic can make the game look easy with his rhythm on the ball and dazzling feet, defence-splitting passes and a powerful left boot when in sight of goal. His set-pieces remain a threat for club and country, most memorably shown when he unleashed a thunderbolt against Japan during qualification for the last World Cup. Born in Melbourne to a Romanian mother and a Bosnian father, Hrustic is fluent in multiple languages, which helped him step off the Australian player pathways at the age of 14 and try his luck in Europe. He has since played for clubs in England, Austria, Germany, the Netherlands and Italy, and is now bringing his creative spark to the Dutch top tier.

players.name: Awer Mabil
players.bio: It’s not often a footballer’s off-field feats match those on it, but Mabil is a rare case. Born to South Sudanese parents in the Kakuma refugee camp in Kenya, Mabil started playing football at the age of five, without footwear and using a rolled-up sock as a ball. His experiences before leaving the camp for Adelaide at the age of 10 shaped the person he is today and his humanitarian work as an adult earned him the Young Australian of the Year award in 2023. His foundation, Barefoot to Boots, is a not-for-profit organisation which aims for better health, education, policies and gender equality for refugees. On the pitch, Mabil made his debut for the Soceroos in 2018 and has gone on to make 38 appearances, scoring 10 goals, at the time of writing.

players.name: Paul Izzo
players.bio: A few years ago, Izzo leaned on his car’s horn and sang the name of Bruno Fornaroli out the window as his then-Melbourne Victory teammate received a surprise call-up to the Asian Cup. Now, he is off to the World Cup himself. Becoming a regular part of squads after coach Tony Popovic’s ascension to the Socceroos role – having previously played for him at Xanthi and Victory – Izzo made his full international debut against New Zealand post-qualification and hasn’t looked back since, making a record eight saves in a man-of-the-match performance against Canada the following month. Ryan’s form in La Liga means he is probably the No 1, but it is testament to Izzo’s rise that he has made that a question at all during the build-up to the tournament.

players.name: Aiden O’Neill
players.bio: The combative midfielder brings balance and bite to his national and club teams as he plays with an edge first and elegance second. Born and raised in Brisbane, O’Neill found his feet as a teenager in the lower leagues in England before returning to Australia on loan with his hometown A-League club. His potential truly started to shine through after a move to Melbourne City where he became the captain and a midfield enforcer. O’Neill points, barks and organises the team from the centre of the pitch and took on another leadership role with Belgium club Standard Liège before moving to New York City last year. O’Neill is now setting standards in the Big Apple while occasionally allowing his mind to wander to home comforts. “I own a farm as well, that's something I'm pretty passionate about,” he said. “We run cattle there, so that's a big part of my life off the field.”

players.name: Cameron Devlin
players.bio: At the time of writing Devlin is yet to see the field for the Socceroos during the Tony Popovic era, but his irrepressible tenacity in the midfield for Hearts challenged the Glasgow-duopoly on Scottish football has proven impossible for the coach to ignore for World Cup selection. After bouncing around both his hometown clubs in Western Sydney and Sydney FC, Devlin experienced his A-League Men breakout with Wellington in 2019. However, it wasn't the Phoenix who netted a fee for his move to Scotland; that would be the Newcastle Jets, who signed him as a free agent in late June of 2021, only for him to then move to Edinburgh two months later.

players.name: Kai Trewin
players.bio: The ball-playing defender has played with a maturity well beyond his years since debuting for Brisbane as an 18-year-old. He soon cemented his place with the Roar as a composed centre-back who is strong in one-on-one contests and comfortable stepping into a defensive midfield role. A move to Melbourne City has proven to be a stepping stone to joining their sister club in the US as he builds valuable experience that should hold him in good stead with the national team. He made surprise headlines in the build-up to this summer after he became the latest player to be caught up in an Instagram trend that aimed to make relatively low-profile World Cup squad members famous before the tournament began. Trewin had 3,000 followers before being singled out; he now has nearly 130,000.

players.name: Aziz Behich
players.bio: Amid a surfeit of loping defenders and towering backline hybrids available to Popovic, Behich zipping down the left is a comforting reminder of diminutive fullbacks of yore. He appears to have put a hamstring injury last year behind him, and his zest for the game and zeal for the contest means he will compete with Jordy Bos to start for the Socceroos in North America. There may be some Euro-centric snobbery among Socceroos fans, but it is a testament to the quality of Behich that he has remained an enduring member of the national team despite choosing to return home to play in the A-League Men in 2023. No player took part in more of the Socceroos’ World Cup qualifiers than Behich, who was also recognised by his peers by being named the PFA’s men's footballer of the year for 2025.

players.name: Nestory Irankunda
players.bio: Irankunda “couldn’t stop smiling” when he scored his first Socceroos goal in 2024. His young shoulders bear a weight no other Socceroos player can know – unhesitatingly hefted upon him by the Australian media the moment he started finding the back of the net for Adelaide United as a 15-year-old in 2022. The former national coach Graham Arnold sought to shield him from the expectations associated with the label of “most exciting young Australian footballer ever”, but the winger’s confidence and skill with the ball at his feet and his eye for a spectacular goal have made it impossible to dampen the excitement. After a record-breaking move from the A-League to Bayern Munich in late 2024, Irankunda then signed with Watford in July 2025, scoring his first goal a month later from a free-kick outside the area in the very style that earned him the weighty tag to begin with. Tony Popovic has a star in his hands – and his challenge is to help it shine at a World Cup.

players.name: Patrick Beach
players.bio: A former softball player, the 22-year-old Sydneysider was a defender until he reached the Under-14 level, when he asked to make the switch to between the posts. Starting in the NSW development programme at Mount Druitt Town Rangers, Beach headed overseas to trial at teams in Northern Ireland and Ireland before a final trial at Premier League-bound Ipswich Town. Eventually signing with the Central Coast Mariners in 2023, it wasn't until he was picked up by Melbourne City in 2024 that he made his A-League debut. Within 12 months he was an A-League champion. In March of 2025, he earned a first call up to Socceroos camp before later that year making his debut against Venezuela - earning man-of-the-match honours at the first time of asking.

players.name: Harry Souttar
players.bio: “You’ve got to love Souttar. You’ve got to absolutely love him.” So said commentator Pat Nevin after a particularly impressive outing from the towering centre-back in a hard-fought 1-0 win over Denmark at the 2022 World Cup. Many agree with Nevin and it’s easy to understand why Souttar became an instant fan favourite after making his debut for Australia in 2019. Two goals in that game against Nepal were followed by another two in his second full international v Chinese Taipei (remarkably he has a total of 11 in 36 games at the time of writing). But it is not just his goal threat that has observers purring – a no-nonsense, body-on-the-line playing style made him a regular starter under previous coach Graham Arnold, before an achilles injury disrupted his preparations for North America. Born in Aberdeen, he qualifies for the Socceroos through his West Australia-born mother and, at two meters, he has the distinction of being the tallest outfield player to have played for Australia. Only goalkeeper Zeljco Kalac has been taller.

players.name: Cristian Volpato
players.bio: The prodigious talent ended years of speculation over whether he would commit to Australia – the country of his birth – or Italy with accepting a bombshell call-up to the Socceroos squad on the eve of this World Cup. The previous head coach, Graham Arnold, said that Volpato had turned down the chance to play at the 2022 tournament in Qatar, which came a few months after the player appeared to mock the Socceroos on Snapchat following a 2-0 defeat to Japan in Asian Cup qualifying. The 22-year-old can play as an attacking midfielder or winger with his versatility helping to establish him as a regular at Sassuolo over the past three seasons – and earn him selection with Italy’s youth teams in recent years. “When you have a talented player, I'm not sure what the risk is,” the Socceroos coach, Tony Popovic, said of the selection curveball. Now it’s over to the Sydney-born and raised Volpato to live up to the hype.

players.name: Cameron Burgess
players.bio: Late last year, a defender’s worst nightmare befell the 30-year-old. Playing against his former club Ipswich, a luckless Burgess found the back of his own net, not once, but twice. It prompted him to issue a statement to Swansea fans. “As a player, I hear your criticism and you won’t hear any excuses from me,” he wrote. “I am my own worst critic, believe me, and it’s my sole purpose to put things right on the pitch.” To be fair, both own goals could be considered unlucky, but his reaction spoke to the man Burgess has become, having started his professional career at Fulham before making several moves around the lower reaches of English football and landing at Portman Road, where he helped Ipswich to successive promotions from League One to the Premier League. Another of the Australian contingent to have been born in Scotland – he made the move down under at age 11 – Burgess knows his place in the starting XI in North America is not guaranteed. “Every time you get the chance to play, it’s a real honour, and something none of us take for granted,” he said.

players.name: Jackson Irvine
players.bio: The Australia midfield leader plays with the calm authority and tactical nous of someone who has seen the grind of the lower leagues and now relishes playing on the grandest stages for club and country. Born and raised in Melbourne, battle-hardened in Scotland and England, Irvine is thriving in Germany. Since arriving at St Pauli in 2021, he has become the heartbeat of their midfield, the club captain, and a key player in their return to the German top tier. The promotion celebrations were so wild that Irvine forgot to take his passport to his own wedding two days later. The otherwise thoughtful and articulate Socceroo is not afraid to express his political and social views and, in April, hit out at Fifa for awarding a peace prize to Donald Trump. “As an organisation, you would have to say decisions like the one that we saw awarding this peace prize makes a mockery of what they’re trying to do with the human rights charter,” he said. Yet a lighter side hides in plain sight, with Irvine’s many tattoos including one on his right leg of Simpsons character Moe Szyslak.

players.name: Nishan Velupillay
players.bio: Having not played a part in the past four international windows, Velupillay is something of a bolter in Tony Popovic’s squad, and admitted recently he has missed the Socceroos environment. “I feel like you always get an extra boost of confidence when you come in here,” he said. The one-club man burst onto the scene for Melbourne Victory in 2021, becoming a regular first-team starter and winning the club’s young player of the year award. Velupillay worked his way into the Australian Under-23s set-up before being handed a chance to impress in a Socceroos World Cup qualifying squad; he went on to score in back-to-back games against China and Indonesia last year.

players.name: Paul Okon-Engstler
players.bio: Son of former Socceroos player and now national team assistant Paul Okon, Okon-Engstler is eager to create his own legacy while remaining mindful of the influence his father has had on his career. "He's always been such a big inspiration for me and for my brothers ... anything to do with football, he's honest,” Okon-Engstler says. “He gives his honest opinion and what he thinks we need to improve on.” The 21-year-old played youth football at Club Brugge in Belgium before he made the move to Portuguese giants Benfica, making eight appearances for their second team before securing a move to Sydney FC in 2025. In the US and Canada, he will try to put the disappointment of the Sky Blues’ recent A-League grand final defeat to Auckland behind him.

players.name: Lucas Herrington
players.bio: The youngest player selected in Australia’s World Cup squad at 18, Herrington has already tested himself against several of the game’s greats during his fledgling career - since signing for Colorado in MLS, he has counted the likes of Lionel Messi, Rodrigo De Paul, Thomas Müller and Son Heung-min as opponents. Herrington started his career at Brisbane Roar, where he was crowned their young player of the year after an impressive debut season. He became the youngest player since Nestroy Irankunda to debut for Australia with appearances in friendlies against Cameroon and Curaçao this year, describing the experience of representing his country “the best feeling in the world". The young Queenslander shapes as one of Socceroos brightest prospects and one to keep an eye on.

players.name: Tete Yengi
players.bio: There is a fair amount of excitement about Yengi, despite having largely flown under the radar before being called in at the last minute to the Socceroos pre-World Cup training camp in Sarasota. For many years the 1.98m striker might not even have been the best attacker in his family, with his brother Kusini taking that mantle, but this year he has leapfrogged his older brother and staked his claim for national team selection. On loan to Japanese first division team Machida Zelvia from Livingston, Yengi helped his side reach the Asian Champions League Elite final, where he enjoyed a moment of internet fame after being headbutted by the much shorter Al Ahli defender Zakaria al Hawsawi.

players.name: Alexander Schlager
players.bio: Schlager heads to the World Cup with the edge over Patrick Pentz in their long-running battle to be Austria’s first-choice goalkeeper. Schlager missed Euro 2024 because of injury and admitted watching the tournament on television was painful. “Watching the buildup and the aftermath and all the emotions around it became too much, so I decided that I would only turn on the TV when the game was starting,” he said. Schlager is a complete modern keeper: strong on the line, commanding in his area, sharp with his feet and comfortable in the buildup. Nicknamed “Gassi” – from Gastberger, the surname of his biological father – he only decided to become a goalkeeper at 15. His explanation was simple: he liked throwing himself around and getting dirty. Schlager has also taken courses in nutritional science and recently discussed his hair transplant procedure.

players.name: David Affengruber
players.bio: Only a handful have switched Austria for Spain. So when Affengruber won the league with Sturm Graz in 2024 and, despite his strong displays at centre-back, chose to move to Spain’s second division, the reaction was one of surprise. “Experts can say and write what they want. It doesn’t affect me. I go my own way,” he said later. Many predicted the move would bury him. Instead, Affengruber quickly became a key part of Elche’s promotion campaign and then established himself in La Liga too. At Elche they call him “Kaiser” or even “Affenbauer”, a nod to Franz Beckenbauer. He came to football through his three older brothers, was spotted young and moved to RB Salzburg’s academy, where independence came early: “I had to do my own laundry at 13.” It shows in his game too: Affengruber is a strong, intelligent and highly capable ball-playing defender who shines in buildup play without losing his edge in duels.

players.name: Kevin Danso
players.bio: “As a kid, I wanted to be an action movie star,” Danso says. Kylian Mbappé can attest to his imposing physique: the France forward broke his nose after jumping face-first into Danso’s back at Euro 2024. Born in the Styrian town of Voitsberg to Ghanaian parents, Danso moved to England at six but has remained closely connected to Austria. Alongside his godmother, he founded DANnSO, an organisation supporting families in difficult circumstances in his hometown. After a standout season with Lens in 2024, a proposed move to Roma collapsed when medical tests revealed scarring on his heart. Following a minor procedure, he went on to win the Europa League with Tottenham. Known for his authority in the air, his reliability defending crosses earned him the nickname “magnet head” from his former manager, Thomas Frank.

players.name: Xaver Schlager
players.bio: In Austria, there is a running joke that once the last fan has left the stadium and the lights have gone out, Xaver Schlager is still chasing the ball. He is a cornerstone of Rangnick’s system: a presser, a ball-winner and exactly the sort of player opponents hate to face. That he can do plenty with the ball as well became clear at Leipzig and, above all, with the national team. Michael Gregoritsch has said his teammate “has also developed enormously in terms of leadership.” Schlager, who spent part of his childhood at boarding school – “a tough school, but a very educational one” – is a grounded person who, in 2025, opened a restaurant in his home town of St Valentin called Dorfrichter. Injuries have set him back in recent years, but he appears to be getting back to his best just in time for the World Cup.

players.name: Stefan Posch
players.bio: Nicknamed “Stefan Porsche” by Bologna fans, the versatile 29-year-old moved to Como and is currently on loan at Mainz. It was at Bologna that Posch made his name as a full-back after moving from centre-back. “At first I thought it was just a limited period of time and that I would move back into the centre,” he once said. Instead, he made the role his own. Posch, who is a managing director and shareholder in a property company, brings real drive going forward with his excellent passing, dynamism and a clear eye for goal. Under Ralf Rangnick, when fit, he has been one of Austria’s more understated constants in recent years. In 2020, he was made an honorary citizen of his home town, Kraubath an der Mur.

players.name: Nicolas Seiwald
players.bio: One of Ralf Rangnick’s most trusted players, at the time of writing Seiwald has missed just 13 minutes of competitive action under the head coach – when he was subbed out late in a 10-0 win over San Marino.
