At Salzburg, the defensive midfielder progressed from the under-nines to the Champions League while still living in his childhood bedroom in the village of Kuchl. He may not yet have fulfilled his childhood ambition of “being the best player at FC Barcelona, being the best player in the world”, but he has developed into a key figure in Leipzig’s midfield. “He is our pulse and never stops,” his coach Ole Werner says. Seiwald tends to favour the safe option in possession but is formidable out of it, a relentless presence in the press.

players.name: Marko Arnautović
players.bio: Once an extremely polarising figure in Austria due to run-ins with police, teammates and opponents, Arnautovic has matured into an elder statesman. These days you are more likely to find him in negotiations for a new public holiday with the Austrian president than anything else off the pitch. The 36-year-old demanded a new public holiday after Austria qualified for the World Cup; president Alexander Van der Bellen suggested they might have to win the whole thing for that to happen. In March they met, taking selfies and coming to an agreement, although they did not reveal what it was. Arnautović  surpassed Toni Polster as Austria’s most prolific international goalscorer during the World Cup qualifiers, and is known as a leader in the locker room. He may retire after the tournament but will remain his country’s most capped player for a while – he is currently 20 ahead of second-placed David Alaba.

players.name: David Alaba
players.bio: Possibly Austria’s greatest footballer of all time, Alaba may be in the autumn of his career, but he remains central to the national team. Injured at Euro 2024, he was still named by Ralf Rangnick as a non-playing captain and remained one of the driving forces in a squad whose cohesion is as important as individual quality. He is the face of the generation that has transformed Austrian football, with his buildup play, reading of the game and versatility earning the nickname “God” from Pep Guardiola. A four-times Champions League winner, Alaba showed how much the national team means to him when he said: “Qualifying for the World Cup is something I rank right at the top of my career.” Talent runs in the family: his Nigerian father once had a No 2 hit in the Austrian charts with Indian Song while his sister is also a musician. As for this summer, Alaba has no doubt: “Austria will have a successful World Cup, I’m convinced of that. I can feel that we’re going there to make history.”

players.name: Marcel Sabitzer
players.bio: The Dortmund midfielder is set to reach 100 caps during the World Cup, and will become only the fifth Austrian man to do so. Known for his quiet, almost reclusive off-pitch demeanour, Sabitzer is omnipresent on the field, usually deployed as a roaming left winger and involved in almost every attacking move. At 32, he has maintained his performance level thanks to rigorous discipline. “I haven’t opened a pack of crisps in the last five years,” he confessed in 2021. Sabitzer is not afraid of confrontation and has clashed with club coaches in the past, though never with Rangnick – at least publicly. Not one to take results lightly, he skipped an international friendly to recover mentally after having lost the Champions League final in 2024. Sabitzer married Katja Kühne, a winner of the German edition of The Bachelor, in 2025.

players.name: Florian Grillitsch
players.bio: A defensive midfielder with playmaking qualities, Grillitsch is an exceedingly rare breed in Austria. After an impressive spell with Hoffenheim, he appeared set for a transfer to a bigger club in 2022. Talks with Milan, Fiorentina and other clubs collapsed, however, with Fiorentina’s sporting director publicly criticising the conduct of Grillitsch’s father in negotiations. The player subsequently returned to working with an agent and, after two months as a free agent, signed for Ajax. Grillitsch’s second stint at Hoffenheim ended early after he was deemed to not fit into head coach Christian Ilzer’s system, prompting a move to Braga. “If I speak, I’m in big trouble,” he said, channeling his inner José Mourinho. While showing very little emotion to the outside world, Grillitsch is known as a joker within the squad.

players.name: Michael Gregoritsch
players.bio: Gregoritsch has had a tendency to shape the trajectory of Austrian football. Most recently, it was his equaliser against Bosnia and Herzegovina that sent Austria to the World Cup. He keeps delivering even when everyone else – apart from Rangnick – seemed to have stopped believing in him. Once the youngest-ever Austrian goalscorer in the Bundesliga, Gregoritsch has matured during a long career that has taught him to have perspective. “I don’t have to pretend I’m the fastest,” he once said. He has also helped shape how the national team is seen in public, urging people to “pull themselves together” and move “far away” from right-wing thinking, saying football shows what solidarity really means. Speaking immediately after Austria’s qualifier against San Marino, played a day after the attack in Graz, Gregoritsch said he had been deeply shaken because he had grown up “10 minutes from there”, adding that “sometimes football matches are not that important”. On the field, he remains exactly what Austria need: a target man who works, occupies defenders, creates space for midfield and still scores hugely important goals.

players.name: Florian Wiegele
players.bio: Wiegele is hard to miss. The Viktoria Plzen goalkeeper stands 2.05m tall, and became the tallest player ever to appear for Austria when he made his international debut as a substitute against Ghana in March. Wiegele’s rise has come late: it is only in the past 18 months that his career has really taken off. He spent years in Austria’s lower leagues before breaking into professional football with DSV Leoben at 22. Plzen signed him in 2024 and he became first-choice there in November after a loan spell at Grazer AK. Wiegele said his international debut “was an honour” and joked that the microphone had to be lowered for his interview. Unsurprisingly, he is dominant in the air, but he is also comfortable with the ball at his feet. That is despite the fact he still wears rugby boots, as football boots are not available in his size.

players.name: Patrick Pentz
players.bio: Usually the backup to Schlager, Pentz excelled as Austria’s No 1 at Euro 2024. After another convincing performance against South Korea in March, the coach, Ralf Rangnick, admitted he was spoilt for choice. Outstanding with the ball at his feet and a sharp shot-stopper, Pentz still divides opinion: at 1.83m, he is considered short by modern goalkeeping standards. After tough spells at Leverkusen and Reims, he is enjoying life in Copenhagen as Brøndby’s undisputed No 1. “In Denmark, people respect that footballers have private lives,” he says. Denmark is a fitting destination for this avid collector of Harry Potter Lego sets, although that was not the reason he turned down more lucrative offers last summer. “You have to ask yourself if it’s worth it,” he said. “I do appreciate the great life we have here in Copenhagen.”

players.name: Saša Kalajdžić
players.bio: Kalajdžić’s career reads as an extraordinarily painful series of what-ifs. What if he hadn’t broken his foot in 2018? What if his ankle ligament hadn’t ruptured the same year? What if he hadn’t suffered a torn ACL in 2019, and again in 2022? And yet again in 2024? When fit, Kalajdžić has had a fair claim to be Austria’s best striker, including a goal against eventual champions Italy in their Euro 2020 last-16 game. Standing two metres tall, his ball control and vision is much better than expected. Kalajdzic looks like a playmaker trapped in the body of a striker, his skills a reminder of his footballing education as a midfielder. Austrian legend Ernst Baumeister converted him to the forward line at the age of 20, arguing that “even as a defensive midfielder, he was the most dangerous attacker”.

players.name: Philipp Lienhart
players.bio: Lienhart’s senior career began with a 13-minute cameo that no longer officially counts. In 2015, while still a teenager, he came on for Real Madrid in a Copa del Rey tie against Cádiz, only for the result to be annulled because his side had fielded an ineligible player. His appearance vanished from the record, but not from memory. “One thing I took from [the coach Zinedine] Zidane was that as a centre-back, you should always try to speed the game up: few touches, crisp passes – and driving the play from the back”. Lienhart’s path led him to Freiburg, where he has become a mainstay and one of the Bundesliga’s most reliable central defenders. “I’m not a showman,” he once said of himself, and it shows in the way he plays: uncompromising and physical, but also intelligent, composed and quietly dependable. The fact he was born in Lilienfeld, a town at the foot of the Muckenkogel – one of the birthplaces of alpine skiing – only makes his story feel even more Austrian.

players.name: Phillipp Mwene
players.bio: Reliable left-backs are in short supply but the 32-year-old has established himself as one of them, making him an important player for Ralf Rangnick’s team. Mwene solves problems on the left flank; he is a dynamic worker, sharp in defence and energetic going forward. In Swahili, the phrase is hakuna matata – no problem – which was also the name of his father John’s Kenyan restaurant in Vienna. His mother worked as a nurse at Vienna’s AKH, one of Europe’s biggest hospitals, which brought its own advantages. “When I came home from playing football with scrapes and bruises, she could treat me straight away,” he once said. Joined Mainz for the first time in 2018 and when he left for PSV three years later the then-coach, Bo Svensson, said it “hurt personally”. Now back at the Bundesliga club, he has regained his starting place.

players.name: Carney Chukwuemeka
players.bio: Chukwuemeka arrived, scored and instantly made people take notice. Brought on against Ghana in March for his first appearance after becoming eligible for Austria, the midfielder marked the occasion with a goal and celebrated in disbelief as his new teammates swarmed around him. “When the ball went in, I couldn’t believe it. I want to do it again straight away,” he said afterwards. He said it in English, because he speaks only limited German. One of the first local phrases he picked up was “Bist deppat, oida?”: Austrian slang equivalent to “Are you crazy, man?” Born in Vienna to Nigerian parents, who moved to England when he was very young, Chukwuemeka came through the Aston Villa academy before joining Chelsea in 2022. Rangnick is unlikely to care that he made almost 100 senior appearances before completing 90 minutes, in Dortmund’s defeat to Hoffenheim in April. As a dynamic No 8 with pace, drive, goal threat and an eye for the right pass, he looks a major addition to Austria’s squad.

players.name: Romano Schmid
players.bio: Once hailed by Austria legend Andreas Herzog as “one of Austria’s biggest talents”, Schmid scored within his first 15 minutes of game time for his childhood club, Sturm Graz – becoming the first player born in the 2000s to score in the Austrian Bundesliga. Nearly nine years later, he has spent most of his career with Werder Bremen, with rumoured moves to the Premier League yet to realise. Standing at 1.68m, he may be Austria’s shortest squad member, but his low centre of gravity makes him a formidable presence in one-on-ones. A technically adept playmaker, Schmid is often deployed on the right side by Rangnick, and, at the time of writing, he has featured in 29 consecutive matches under Austria’s head coach.

players.name: Christoph Baumgartner
players.bio: Has been ruled out with a thigh injury. Replacement not yet announced.

players.name: Konrad Laimer
players.bio: Laimer had plenty of doubters when he left RB Leipzig for Bayern Munich on a free transfer in 2023 but, after failing to establish himself as a first-choice midfielder, he has reinvented himself as a right back, occasionally filling in on the left. Laimer is the epitome of a workhorse and, having been educated at Salzburg’s Red Bull academy, the perfect disciple for Ralf Rangnick’s Gegenpressing doctrine. “I want to be as unpleasant as possible on the pitch and I like it when things get physical,” he told Sports Illustrated this year. “One-on-one duels and intensity have always been important parts of my game. He added with a smile: “I can probably be a bit annoying on the pitch, although my wife would say that goes for off the pitch too!” Austria’s footballer of the year in 2025, Laimer is known as a down-to-earth guy who enjoys a game of chess or a round of golf in his spare time.

players.name: Patrick Wimmer
players.bio: Wimmer is a rarity in modern football: he is not an academy product, nor is he a polished system player. Rejected by St Pölten, he ended up in the lower leagues at Gaflenz and only got his chance at Austria Vienna after a club vice-president pushed to sign him, going so far as collecting video footage of Wimmer’s matches. Perhaps that is exactly what Rangnick values: a player that can be wildly unpredictable, direct in attack, and a strong dribbler capable of unexpected passes. Wimmer grew up on a farm in a tiny village in Lower Austria with just 16 households. He was nudged into football more by his grandmother than by his own ambition, and was even an international weightlifting champion between the ages of 10 and 14. He also trained as a mechatronics technician and says becoming a professional footballer was never really the plan. One Opta stat from 2023 captured his quality: among players with at least 500 minutes in Europe’s top five leagues, only Kevin De Bruyne, Wimmer and Lionel Messi had averaged more than one major chance created per game.

players.name: Alexander Prass
players.bio: After progressing through Red Bull Salzburg’s academy, Prass joined Sturm Graz on a free transfer in 2021. “I never got the feeling that they really wanted me,” he said of Salzburg. In Graz, he developed into an influential, high-energy presence and played a key role in the side that ended Salzburg’s decade-long title run in 2024. His assist for the decisive goal on the final day captured his style: a risky, almost ill-judged surge from midfield that came off more through sheer force than ball control. Consistent performances at Sturm, followed by an impressive Euro 2024, earned him a move to Hoffenheim. Used primarily as a left-back by Ralf Rangnick, Prass can lack composure in one-on-one situations but compensates with relentless running down the flank.

players.name: Marco Friedl
players.bio: It was not love at first sight between Friedl and Ralf Rangnick, that’s for sure. “Nobody is talking to me, so I’ve given up. I’m really angry,” the defender said in September 2024 after again being omitted from the Austria squad. Despite Rangnick’s preference for a left-footed option at left centre-back, it took the Werder Bremen captain until last year to establish himself. Friedl came through Bayern Munich’s academy, where he would occasionally stay in David Alaba’s guest room. “He is like a little brother to me,” Alaba said at the time – though Friedl’s performance as a ball boy for Bayern’s games left room for improvement. “Once, he was daydreaming and I had to shout at him to throw me the ball,” Alaba recalls. Now a respected figure at his club, Friedl is valued for his leadership and for speaking up in difficult situations.

players.name: Paul Wanner
players.bio: Ralf Rangnick was pleased in the spring. “If we were a club, you could say it had been a very successful transfer window,” he said, referring in part to Wanner becoming eligible to play for Austria. Born in Dornbirn to an Austrian mother and a German father, Wanner was the subject of a long tug-of-war between the two countries. “In the end, I had to listen to myself and I wanted to play for Austria. I’m convinced it was the right decision,” he said. Wanner came through Bayern’s academy and once held the club record as their youngest-ever Champions League starter. Now being developed as a No 6 at PSV, Wanner also made an immediate impression for Austria with a lively display off the bench against Ghana in March. Technically gifted and sharp in tight spaces with a strong left foot, he is capable of adding more than just squad depth.

players.name: Michael Svoboda
players.bio: For a Viennese footballer playing in Italy, the question ought to be simple enough: espresso or melange? Svoboda’s answer is surprising. “Neither. I don’t drink coffee,” he said on a podcast. He has shown examples of resistance elsewhere, too. Venezia’s captain tore his cruciate ligament last year but returned seemingly stronger than ever. Ralf Rangnick seems to have taken a liking to the centre-back, who has remained on Austria’s radar since his first call-up in 2024. That almost never happened, though. “I missed the first call because my phone was on silent,” he once recalled. Svoboda’s profile is an unusual one: a robust, physical but also dynamic defender, and a late bloomer who made his way from WSG Tirol to Venezia. Has made his mark in Italy, where defensive toughness and tactical discipline are held in such high regard.

players.name: Alessandro Schöpf
players.bio: Perhaps the biggest surprise in Ralf Rangnick’s squad, given that Schöpf’s season with Wolfsberger did not exactly demand attention. Even so, his qualities have clearly not gone unnoticed. The well-travelled midfielder has long been valued for his technique, dynamism and the quality of his passing. When he moved to Vancouver Whitecaps after over a decade in Germany, he seemed to find renewed confidence and something like a reboot for his career – along with a fine home for his two golden retrievers, Mila and Levi. “There are great beaches. And Stanley Park is a beautiful place. Our dogs will love it there,” said Schöpf, who is also known as “Ale” – in the German pronunciation, so not the beer. Even before his move back home, he had made his target clear: the World Cup. He is joining LASK this summer.

players.name: Thibaut Courtois
players.bio: Look who’s back! Courtois was left at home by Belgium for Euro 2024 because of a back-and-forth argument with the then head coach Domenico Tedesco. The German-Italian didn’t come out of it well, either, being fired soon afterwards. His successor, Rudi Garcia, flew to Madrid to convince Courtois to return to duty, and he agreed – much to the distress of Koen Casteels, the man who had stepped up to replace him. Casteels promptly quit the national team, saying: “It's strange that they are rolling out the red carpet and welcoming him back with open arms … As of today, I am no longer available." This summer is another chance for Courtois to show the world exactly how good he is. He knows it will be difficult to win the World Cup, but maybe he can add another record to his collection, which includes the record for most saves in a Champions League final (nine against Liverpool in 2022). Or could he win the Golden Glove again, as he did in 2018? His wife, the Israeli model Mishel Gerzig, will surely be in the US to support him, which he’ll appreciate if he isn’t glued to his phone. She once called him “the king of mobile phones” because of his fixation, saying he would regularly bump into door frames while using it.

players.name: Zeno Debast
players.bio: One of the many excellent products of Anderlecht’s academy, Debast had been hoping to become the next Kevin De Bruyne, playing as an attacking midfielder until he was 15. But then, partly due to his growth spurt, he was moved back, and excelled in his new role. He says he learned a lot from the current Wales manager Craig Bellamy, who was then coach of Anderlecht’s Under-21s: “Before I worked with him, I had never tackled a player in my life”, he said. “He also taught me to be meaner.” Now, with his great long-range passes and growing physical power, he is the leader of Belgium’s back line. That’s despite facing big competition for places at club level, with Sporting sometimes deploying him as a holding midfielder instead. Everyone in Belgium has the feeling he has a big career ahead of him. He’s known by his nickname, Scampi, which was given to him as a child by his mother. It fell out of use until a teammate, Benito Raman, discovered it on Facebook and eagerly brought it back into circulation.

players.name: Arthur Theate
players.bio: As a youth player, Theate moved from Genk to Standard Liège, then back to Genk and finally to… Standard. But he didn’t make the cut and was rejected even by clubs from the third division before being given a chance by KV Oostende. From there on, things moved quickly for the left-footed and long-haired defender, who idolised Carles Puyol. Bologna, Rennes and Eintracht Frankfurt all paid millions to sign him. Reflecting on his difficult early years, and the push it gave him, he told Humo: “I don’t feel any grudge. On the contrary, I say thanks to all those who rejected me.” He’s a big F1 fan, so will be keeping an eye on the four races scheduled during the World Cup. And if he performs well, he might even receive a message from the double Olympic champion cyclist and former football player Remco Evenepoel. At the national youth level they shared a room, and they’re still in touch.

players.name: Brandon Mechele
players.bio: Mechele is already a Club Brugge legend, holding the record for most appearances in European competition (101) and sharing the record for most league titles (six) with his teammate Hans Vanaken. But it hasn’t all been plain-sailing: there was a time when he didn’t feel appreciated, with the board buying a new central defender almost every year. “Then I hung my head and walked around in frustration”, he told HLN. “It’s tiring that I have to keep proving myself to the outside world.” Yet he never thought of leaving. He started and wants to finish his career in Bruges, where he rides his bike to training. He was really disappointed to be left out of the Belgium squad for previous tournaments, but now he’s finally made it. His close friend Tibo Debaillie will be happy: he plays for Canadian Football League team BC Lions in Vancouver, where the Red Devils face New Zealand.

players.name: Maxim De Cuyper
players.bio: In September last year, Antoine Semenyo, then at Bournemouth, knocked De Cuyper into the advertising boards, injuring the Brighton full-back’s knee in his debut season in the Premier League. He spent much of the rest of the season on the bench, but could still be an important player for Belgium at this World Cup. For years Belgium had to deploy central defenders out wide, such as Jan Vertonghen and Thomas Vermaelen, but with De Cuyper they’ve finally found a natural left-back who also offers an attacking and goal-scoring threat. The curly-haired kid from Knokke is a proper Club Brugge product, with a strong aversion to their biggest rival. “In kindergarten, when he was only three years old, he refused to colour a drawing of a plum”, his mother told Nieuwsblad – because purple was Anderlecht’s colour. De Cuyper’s favourite meal before a game? Sausage with apple sauce and potatoes.

players.name: Axel Witsel
players.bio: The oldest Red Devil. Witsel made his international debut in 2008 when teammate Nathan De Cat hadn’t even been born. Witsel is now 37 but still standing strong thanks to his work ethic, which, said his father Thierry, with whom he runs a football academy, is “similar to that of Cristiano Ronaldo”. In recent years Witsel also started skipping carbs in the evening and he wears a WHOOP bracelet to track his running, sleep and overall health. He didn’t play for Belgium for almost three years, to focus on his club career, but he admitted that he regretted that decision. He is expected to play a key role this summer, but more off the pitch than on it. If he does get more minutes than anticipated, though, we’ll be able to admire his famous tattoos once more. Apart from the paternoster on his chest, his arms are now fully inked, including the names of his kids, and an image of a roaring lion.

players.name: Kevin De Bruyne
players.bio: What do Jamal Musiala, Gavi and Anthony Gordon have in common? They all played in the KDB Cup, the now 10-year-old prestigious youth tournament named after (and supported by) Kevin De Bruyne. His own younger years – back when he frequently ran away from training because of arguments with coaches, was supporting Liverpool and went on a trial at Arsenal – seem a long time ago now. He arrives at the World Cup as an elder statesman, a Manchester City legend, with 422 games, 108 goals and 19 trophies, and one of Belgium’s last “golden generation” members. He certainly hopes to perform better than at the previous World Cup. If not, he’ll be flying home on his 35th birthday, since it falls right after the New Zealand game. But whatever happens, the family’s future looks bright after footage emerged of his seven-year-old son Rome scoring some of his dad’s trademark goals at Real Casarea, a club in the Napoli area.

players.name: Youri Tielemans
players.bio: This year Tielemans celebrates his 10th anniversary as a Red Devil. Belgium fans still remember him as an 18-year old at his squad initiation ceremony singing Cheerleader by Omi in front of his teammates. And now, in his fifth major tournament, he wants to play a key role for the first time. And he should, because Rudi Garcia has named him captain, which is quite some honour with Thibaut Courtois, Kevin De Bruyne and Romelu Lukaku also in the squad. Tielemans was the least controversial choice, though: not only does he speak Dutch and French fluidly, he is loved by nearly everyone. Besides his great feet – he scores screamers with both, such as his unforgettable winner for Leicester in the FA Cup final in 2022 against Chelsea – he always puts the team first. Negative stories? You won’t find any. He also never talks about his private life, so it took a while to double check that he now has three daughters rather than two.

players.name: Romelu Lukaku
players.bio: Some strikers don’t score 89 goals in their entire career. Lukaku arrived at this World Cup having scored 89 for his country alone. That’s over twice as many as any other Belgian player – and why his nickname “Big Rom” isn’t just a reference to his bulky 1.91m frame. At this tournament he will be on a mission. Four years ago, in Qatar, he wasn’t fully fit and missed some big chances as a substitute against Croatia. “Afterwards, on vacation, for a week straight I was crying every day”, he told Koolcast later on. Apart from his sons Jordan and Romeo, it was the legendary Thierry Henry, former assistant of the national team, who gave him the mental support he needed. Last season Lukaku had his share of bad luck with his hamstring injury, which led to a falling out with the coaching staff and board at Napoli as the striker opted to complete a big part of his rehab in Antwerp. But even without many minutes to his name in 2025-26, defenders will still fear his strength and efficiency.

players.name: Leandro Trossard
players.bio: Ten years ago Trossard was being relegated to Belgium’s second division with OH Leuven. A decade on, he’s a World Cup player who has just spent a season at the heart of a Premier League title challenge. He’s not immune to criticism, though, with Belgium media and fans often saying he doesn’t perform at the same level for his country as he does for his club. In a press conference in October, for instance, a reporter even told him that he always looks less happy when he’s with the national team. Trossard smiled, flashed his white teeth and countered: “No idea where that comes from. I’m always happy when I’m on the field.” He hopes to show that at the World Cup and, if he finds the net, watch out for him putting on his imaginary glasses – his signature celebration. Also watch out for any Belgium matches refereed by the French official Clément Turpin, who is Trossard’s doppelganger.

players.name: Jérémy Doku
players.bio: How many times will we see Doku hit the “Griddy” (his trademark dance celebration) at the World Cup? Belgium fans didn’t see it often enough last season, but with five goals and three assists the speedster and prolific dribbler was still one of the most important players in the qualifiers. If he stays fit and performs well fans will surely be treated to another episode of his “dokumentary” on YouTube; previous episodes have included discussing his faith, and taking viewers to Ghana, where his parents were born. His dad was already well-known, though, thanks to a story he told about the time his boy, then in the youth system at Anderlecht, was being shown around Liverpool’s training complex ahead of a possible transfer. His dad told Nieuwsblad that, when they were being shown around, the Liverpool staff seemed twitchy because “we were talking about Chelsea quite often. Liverpool were afraid that Jérémy would sign there instead. But we were just talking about our youngest daughter. Her name is Chelsea.”

players.name: Senne Lammens
players.bio: “Senne started out as a striker,” his dad Eddy told Nieuwsblad: “He shot from every angle and scored with ease.” But his son was even better with the ball in his hands. He focused on that, it quickly paid off – and last summer he became the most expensive goalkeeper sold by a Belgian club when Manchester United paid Antwerp £18m for him. He quickly silenced the pundits and United icons who wrote him off without knowing him – yes, you too, Peter Schmeichel. This is his first World Cup, but he won’t be nervous. Like Edwin van der Sar, he’s known as the “ice rabbit”, and keeps a journal of his thoughts to stay calm. “Before and after the game, I just write key words down and my thoughts a little bit,” he says. “Because, sometimes, if you don't do that, it can be a lot going through your mind. It helps me stay calm and stay in the moment, and not overreact. I write key words, trigger points. [It means] you don't have to think a lot during the games, mentally you are already prepared for the game and what it's going to be like. You’re not going to be so surprised. I think that is important.”

players.name: Mike Penders
players.bio: Left-footed, 2m tall, developed in Genk’s academy and signed by Chelsea – it’s no surprise that Penders is often compared to Thibaut Courtois. It was remarkable, though, that after only one senior league game he was signed by the London club for £17m last year – especially as they already had six goalkeepers. Since then, on loan at Strasbourg, he has worn the No 39 on his back, a tribute to his granddad Nico, his biggest fan. Nico told Belang van Limburg last year: “When he started playing as a little boy I was always there, and later at Bregel Sport and Genk as well. I think I only missed maybe ten of his training sessions in the past 15 years. Even when he was with the first team and the sessions were closed, they would let me in. Something that will always stay with me is that he came to give me a kiss on the sidelines before a match. In a packed stadium, he didn't let any of that bother him. And did you know that even his number 39 is a reference to me? I was born in 1939. He will play with that number at Chelsea as well.”

players.name: Dodi Lukébakio
players.bio: “I’m so grateful. After my first goal I was even in tears,” he said after his sublime performance against the US at the end of March. “This means so much to me.” The emotional response was explained by the winger having suffered an ankle fracture, which ruined half of his debut season at Benfica, and left him fearing it would ruin his chances of playing at his first major tournament. But it all worked out and he has now made it to the biggest stage. Now the world will know who Dodi is, although there are still doubts about the origin of his name. According to his mother, it refers to Dodi Fayed, who died in an accident in Paris alongside Princess Diana less than a month before Lukebakio's birth. But according to his father, Dodi stands for Don de Dieu, which is French for ‘gift from God’. The last explanation pleases him most, since he’s very religious himself.

players.name: Thomas Meunier
players.bio: The NBA legend Karl Malone was called “The Mailman” because he always delivered, but Meunier was literally working as a postman before he turned pro. Another big career switch soon followed while he settled in at Club Brugge: he began as a left winger, then moved all the way across the field to become a right-back – and a very good one, too. His natural ability in the position earned him a big move to Paris Saint-Germain in 2016 where he won a string of honours, and then to Dortmund in 2020. Now 34, he said recently he hopes to keep playing until he is 40. But if the end comes sooner than that, he won’t be bored away from the game. Besides having three kids, he also has his businesses in Bastogne, where he grew up, including a rotisserie chicken restaurant, and his love for art. He likes to visit museums and even used to have a picture of Salvador Dalí’s The Persistence of Memory as his phone screensaver.

players.name: Koni De Winter
players.bio: Belgian fans first heard the name De Winter eight years ago when, without even having made his senior debut at Zulte Waregem, the 16-year-old was signed by Juventus. He became Juve’s youngest starter ever in the Champions League and relished the chance to train with Cristiano Ronaldo and learn from Leonardo Bonucci and Giorgio Chiellini. But in the end he needed loan spells at Empoli and Genoa to gain experience before Juve sold him to Genoa – a decision they may already regret in Turin. Last summer Milan paid over £17m to sign him, and he has since become a key player there. Now we know why his youth coaches used to compare him to an all-time great. “He was a bit like the young Vincent Kompany,” Bert Wets told HLN. “He had lots of elegance, flair and fancy tricks.” Those qualities used to mean some costly mistakes, but they are becoming rarer. His first name, Koni, wasn’t his dad’s first choice. “As a child I watched Milan’s Champions League matches on TV and played the Rossoneri on my PlayStation. My dad watched them even before I was born. He liked Seedorf and wanted to name me after him, Clarence.”

players.name: Charles De Ketelaere
players.bio: A Flemish tennis champion at the age of ten, De Ketelaere could have followed the path of his idol Roger Federer. Luckily for Belgium fans, he made a different choice. Explaining how he made the decision as a youngster, he told Nieuwsblad: “Always getting private lessons from an adult coach or playing with your friends: what do you think a boy of that age likes best?” His personality in tennis was all about aggression, being admonished for swearing and smashing his rackets. In football, he couldn’t be more different, often regarded as perfect son-in-law material. Maybe his laid-back nature was one of the reasons why, despite his talent and three titles with Club Brugge, he didn’t succeed at Milan, where the competition and pressure are huge. In 40 games he didn’t score a single goal. At Atalanta, however, the versatile forward plays a very important role, and has a Europa League winner’s medal to his name already.

players.name: Joaquin Seys
players.bio: Maxim De Cuyper’s successor at Club Brugge, just with blonde curls instead of brown. As a young boy Seys enjoyed skating, despite all the cuts and bruises, but he chose to dedicate himself to becoming a footballer instead. Around the age of 15 he lacked strength, but coaches stuck by him, recognising he was a late bloomer – and he grew stronger year by year, eventually earning his first professional deal. And although he’s left-footed, he soon established himself as a fine right-back. You might assume he’s named after the famous actor Joaquin Phoenix, but in fact it comes from Joaquín Cortés, a Spanish flamenco dancer. That was explained by his father, with whom he shares a close bond. They both have the words “sempre con voi” tattooed on their bodies, which is Italian for “always with you”.

players.name: Diego Moreira
players.bio: Moreira comes from pure football stock. His father Almani Moreira was a fan favourite at Standard from 2001 to 2006, and his grandfather on his mother’s side, Helmut Graf, also played for them from 1976 to 1982. Born in Liège, he came through the youth ranks at Standard but left at 16 for Benfica, where he was outstanding during their Uefa Youth League winning campaign. He later made his senior debut, but refused a new contract and left, passing through Chelsea and Lyon before finding stability at Strasbourg in Ligue 1. Internationally, he represented Portugal at youth level up to the under-21s, before switching to Belgium at senior level. He’s seen as a modern, versatile performer who can play as a winger or drop back to full-back on both sides.

players.name: Hans Vanaken
players.bio: Vanaken likes to collect things. The playmaker already has six Belgian titles (with Club Brugge) and three Golden Boots for best player in the Belgian league, but his collection of football shirts is equally impressive. He has around 350, many of them obtained by swapping with opponents. One is missing though: Lionel Messi’s international shirt. He’s already got one of the GOAT from when he played against Paris St-Germain. “I have to thank our president, Bart Verhaeghe, who went into their dressing room,” he told Sporza. But Messi’s light blue and white Argentina jersey is at the top of his wishlist. Could they meet at this World Cup? One thing he doesn’t like to collect is yellow and red cards, but he does love playing cards with his teammates while travelling for away games. He’ll know what to do during the long flights in the weeks ahead.

players.name: Timothy Castagne
players.bio: Life has never been easy for Castagne. As a child he suffered from exophoria, with his eyes drifting slightly outwards. That condition corrected itself, but, after sustaining a head injury in a match for Genk in 2015 he was left with severe double vision and migraines, and needed eye surgery, sidelining him for months. Then came his first major tournament with the national side – Euro 2020 – and another serious collision, in a group-stage win over Russia, which fractured his eye socket and meant more surgery. “On the plane home, all I could do was cry”, he told Nieuwsblad – but he still considers himself lucky. Had the impact been slightly higher it could have ended his career. But five years on from that trauma the versatile right-back, who can also do a job on the left, is an established Premier League player. He’s also a real family man. He’s been with Camille, the daughter of his agent Zénon Melon, for many years; their second daughter was born on Valentine’s Day.

players.name: Alexis Saelemaekers
players.bio: Saelemaekers won his first cap in 2020, but was ignored for all major tournaments since then, once because of injury. However, he has fully convinced Rudi Garcia of his worth, and only missed one game during the World Cup qualifiers. That was no surprise because he has just had his best season ever at Milan. A right-winger, who can also play as a full-back or wing-back, he was virtually an ever-present in Milan’s side, and signed a new five-year deal – quite a turnaround after he was loaned to Bologna and Roma in the two previous seasons. Goalkeeper Asmir Begovic, who was on loan at Milan in 2020 when they first signed Saelemaekers, recently talked about the forward’s self-confidence, telling Talksport how he may have got ahead of himself a bit during his early days in Italy. “The first few days after he arrived he was quiet, but two weeks later he came to the club in his new Porsche. The first thing Zlatan Ibrahimović did was to take his keys. He told him: ‘Listen, you’re taking this car back to the garage. If not, I’ll do it myself.’”

players.name: Nicolas Raskin
players.bio: When Rudi Garcia became Belgium’s head coach, he pulled a rabbit – or should we say a Raskin – out of the hat. The then 24-year-old Rangers player had never been called up, but out of nowhere he immediately became a mainstay in the midfield – just as his former manager Hein Vanhaezebrouck had predicted in his column for Nieuwsblad six years earlier: “One day,” Vanhaezebrouck wrote, “he’ll be an undisputed starter for the national team”. Despite being about 15cm shorter than Amadou Onana, Raskin has still scored five headers in the last two seasons and compensates for his lack of height with tons of energy and quick feet. He’s always been a little pitbull, nicknamed “the Raskinator” at Standard, but it was Philippe Clement who helped him improve tactically: “Nico was a fireman. He often went to put out fires elsewhere, which caused problems in his own zone,” he told HLN. “Now he no longer does that.”

players.name: Amadou Onana
players.bio: Onana could have won the African Cup of Nations final this year, since he was born in Senegal and only moved to Brussels at the age of 11. But he has always played for Belgium, where he is known as a stylish player, including off the pitch. He once reported for international duty wearing a men’s skirt. “Fashion is a realm where I find expression and creativity,” he says. “I enjoy experimenting with different looks and have even dabbled in designing my own pieces. Fashion, much like football, is a way for me to connect with people and share a part of myself with the world.” On the pitch, he is the Marouane Fellaini of his generation, not only because he played for Everton, but also because he’s a real powerhouse. And the best is yet to come. “One day, I’ll play for one of the five biggest clubs in the world”, he told La Dernière Heure. Who would have thought? Not the people at Anderlecht or Zulte Waregem, where his talent was overlooked. It was thanks to his sister and agent Melissa that he got noticed. She bought a video camera to film her brother at training, and sent the footage to numerous European clubs.

players.name: Nathan Ngoy
players.bio: Ngoy started out as a striker in Anderlecht’s youth academy, where scoring goals came naturally. And that instinct is still there. At the end of February, he enjoyed a dream week. In the Europa League, the Belgian with Congolese roots came off the bench in extra time against Red Star Belgrade and scored the winner to send Lille through. Just days later, he struck again, netting a 94th-minute decider against Nantes after an assist from Thomas Meunier. His conversion into a defender happened at the academy when coaches realised his pace and technique would work well at the back. At 16 he left for rivals Standard, and went on to make his professional debut in 2021, scoring his first goal as a professional in a 3-2 comeback win against … Anderlecht. Now at Lille, whom he joined last year, he has quickly become a key player. He can also play at full-back when needed.

players.name: Matias Fernandez-Pardo
players.bio: “My heart lies with Spain. Am I sure about who I want to play for? Yes, Spain. Absolutely sure.” Those were the words of Fernandez-Pardo in Marca in 2024. Yet he has now emerged as a surprise inclusion in Belgium’s World Cup squad, with Rudi Garcia favouring him to Loïs Openda, who had too little playing time. Fernandez-Pardo grew up in Belgium, with a Spanish father and an Italian mother – meaning Italy was also an option for his international future. At 15, he left Lille for Gent. “He played like Eden Hazard, but without end product”, said his former coach Hein Vanhaezebrouck. “I showed him clips of Real Madrid, where Vinícius Júnior often drifts from striker to the left wing to become playable. Matias picked that up and became unstoppable that way.” Lille paid €10m last summer to bring him back to northern France. He initially featured on the wing but since March has been used centrally. With five goals and three assists, the lightning-fast striker has pushed Olivier Giroud out of the starting XI.

players.name: Nikola Vasilj
players.bio: Goalkeeping runs in the Vasilj family: Nikola’s father, Vladimir, was part of Croatia’s squads at the 1998 and 2002 World Cups, while Nikola himself represents Bosnia and Herzegovina, something that, in this part of the world, nobody really finds unusual. Unlike many modern elite keepers, Vasilj was never considered a major prodigy and spent years on a treadmill of loans, reserve football and lower league action before finally reaching the Bundesliga with cult club St Pauli. As a child he was a huge admirer of Manuel Neuer, and eventually he found himself playing against his idol himself – even if their recent relegation might put an end to such occasions. Despite his aggressive style and spectacular reflex saves, teammates describe him as unusually calm and reserved away from football.

players.name: Nihad Mujakic
players.bio: Few players in the Bosnia and Herzegovina squad have taken a stranger route than Nihad Mujakic, who has somehow ended up playing for Sarajevo, Hajduk Split and Partizan Belgrade, three clubs whose supporters rarely agree on anything beyond football mattering far too much. Inside the Bosnia squad he is probably understood differently than outside it. The national team coach, Sergej Barbarez, values him less as a polished defender and more as a player capable of handling uncomfortable moments without overthinking them. Every national team tends to carry at least one footballer the supporters argue about constantly - Mujakic might be that player for this generation.

players.name: Dennis Hadzikadunic
players.bio: Hadzikadunic’s career has felt unusually unsettled for a player still in his twenties. Since leaving Malmö for Rostov in 2018, he has spent years moving between countries and clubs almost season after season, passing through Sweden, Russia, Spain and Germany before arriving in Italy, largely because Fifa regulations after the invasion of Ukraine repeatedly forced temporary moves away from Rostov. At one stage he admitted he had grown tired of constantly adapting to new dressing rooms, coaches and cities. Born and raised in Malmö to Bosnian parents, Hadzikadunic represented Sweden at every youth level before eventually choosing to switch allegiance to Bosnia and Herzegovina. Calm on the ball and elegant in possession, he has long been viewed as one of the more technically refined defenders in the national team setup.

players.name: Tarik Muharemovic
players.bio: Muharemovic speaks five languages and carries himself with a calmness that often makes people forget how young he actually is. Born in Slovenia to Bosnian parents, raised in Austria and developed in Italy, his story already stretches across half of Europe. His rise has been remarkably fast: Juventus scouts initially travelled to Austria to watch another player at Wolfsberger, but ended up signing Muharemovic instead. Since then, the left-footed defender has developed into one of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s most highly-rated young players, notably comfortable stepping into midfield and progressing the ball under pressure. In Italy there is already a growing feeling that a much bigger transfer is probably only a matter of time.

players.name: Sead Kolasinac
players.bio: Nicknamed “The Tank”, Kolasinac built his reputation on aggression, loyalty and old-school toughness. Born in Karlsruhe to Bosnian parents who fled the war, he worked as a nightclub bouncer as a teenager while coming through Schalke’s academy. At Schalke he became a cult figure because of his uncompromising style and connection with supporters, before later generating global news after confronting armed thieves during an attempted robbery involving his Arsenal teammate Mesut Özil in London in 2019. One of only two players in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s squad with previous World Cup experience, Kolasinac is also remembered for the unfortunate own goal he scored after just three minutes against Argentina at the Maracanã in the country’s first-ever match at the tournament in 2014.

players.name: Benjamin Tahirovic
players.bio: Tahirović’s rise to elite football happened unusually fast. Born in Sweden to Bosnian parents, he went from the lower divisions of Swedish football to José Mourinho’s Roma in the space of only a few years. Ajax then signed him as one of Europe’s most highly-rated young midfielders. But his career has already contained more turbulence than most players experience in a decade. After difficult periods in Amsterdam and Denmark he unexpectedly became part of a public controversy before Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup playoff against Wales, when Sergej Barbarez claimed Brøndby’s Welsh coach Steve Cooper had wilfully conspired to rule him out of the game. Barbarez later apologised to Cooper, who had in fact dropped the player for failing to “live up to the values” of the Danish club, but the episode revealed how important he had already become for the national team at 23.

players.name: Amar Dedic
players.bio: There are two very different sides to Amar Dedic’s character. Off the pitch he comes across as calm, polite and slightly reserved; on it he plays with constant aggression and intensity, always sprinting, pressing and demanding the ball. Coaches have trusted him from a very young age because of that mentality. Born and raised in Austria to a Bosnian family that left the country during the war years, Dedic has often spoken about the sacrifices his parents made while building a new life abroad. Salzburg brought him into their academy at 13, and his journey from there has taken him through the Champions League and Marseille to Benfica. Despite still being only 23, he already has more than 25 caps for Bosnia and Herzegovina and enters this World Cup as one of the leaders of a new generation.

players.name: Armin Gigovic
players.bio: Gigovic spent most of his youth career in Sweden’s national setup, even captaining their Under-21s, before eventually deciding to represent Bosnia and Herzegovina at senior level, despite developing alongside a hugely talented generation of Swedes, including names such as Anthony Elanga, Lucas Bergvall and Roony Bardghji. Born in Lund to Bosnian parents, he built his reputation through intensity, pressing and constant running rather than spectacular football. His most important moment in a Bosnia shirt arrived in Bucharest in March 2025. Bosnia entered the World Cup qualifiers without a victory in their previous seven matches and under enormous pressure after years of disappointment. Gigovic scored the only goal against Romania, securing one of the national team’s most eye-catching away wins and changing the mood around the squad almost overnight.

players.name: Samed Bazdar
players.bio: Partizan supporters started calling Samed Bazdar “the new Vlahovic” before he had even turned 18, at which point the original Vlahovic was himself only 22. Juventus scouts reportedly followed him as a teenager, while Serbian media quickly built the usual Balkan mythology around another tall, technical striker coming through at Belgrade. Bazdar himself once joked that he was “annoying people at Partizan” with constant extra training sessions, adding: “That’s how Dusan did it too.” After representing Serbia at every youth level and even making one senior appearance, his decision to switch to Bosnia and Herzegovina under Sergej Barbarez and Emir Spahic became one of the region’s biggest national-team stories. Technically gifted and unpredictable, Bazdar belongs to the generation Bosnia hopes can help the national team recover from the inevitable departure of Edin Dzeko, even if his club career has recently lost momentum after the early hype surrounding him – he has been only an occasional starter since joining Polish side Jagiellonia Bialystok on loan in January.

players.name: Ermedin Demirovic
players.bio: Before Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup playoff Demirovic jokingly promised he would buy beer for the entire Stuttgart stadium if his side qualified. After they defeated Italy he actually followed through, standing behind the bar and personally serving free beer to supporters in Stuttgart. The gesture was particularly notable because Demirovic himself does not drink alcohol. “I’m a man of my word,” he said afterwards. Born in Germany to Bosnian parents, Demirovic is one of the rare players of his generation who represented Bosnia and Herzegovina at every single youth level before eventually reaching the senior national team. His club career was far less straightforward than many expected: released by Hamburg and pushed through loans in France, Spain and Switzerland before eventually establishing himself as one of the Bundesliga’s most complete forwards with Augsburg and Stuttgart.

players.name: Edin Dzeko
players.bio: Long before he became the defining figure in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s football history, Dzeko was sold by Zeljeznicar to Czech side Teplice for a relatively small fee because few people inside Bosnian football truly believed a tall, physically underdeveloped striker from Sarajevo could possibly become one of Europe’s elite forwards. He had grown up playing football between buildings and shelters in Sarajevo during the war, later describing football from that period as “an escape from reality”. What followed became the greatest career Bosnian football has produced. Bundesliga titles with Wolfsburg, Premier League triumphs with Manchester City, captaincy at Roma, Champions League finals with Inter; Dzeko eventually became Bosnia's all-time leading appearance-maker and record goalscorer, and remains one of their key players at the age of 40. Inside Bosnia, though, his importance long ago moved beyond football itself. Younger players in Sergej Barbarez’s squad often speak about him less as a teammate and more as the reference point of an entire generation.

players.name: Mladen Jurkas
players.bio: Things have moved quickly for this teenager. This time last year, Jurkas was gaining first-team experience in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s second tier; barely 12 months later, he finds himself on the plane to the World Cup. Jurkas made his senior debut for Borac Banja Luka at just 17 and this season established himself as the club’s first-choice goalkeeper. Borac won their fourth league title while he kept 16 clean sheets in 31 appearances – numbers that initially earned him a place on Bosnia’s reserve list for this summer. But after Osman Hadzikic got injured in a warm-up friendly against North Macedonia, Jurkas received a late call-up, turning what had already been a remarkable year into something even more improbable for the 18-year-old.

players.name: Ivan Basic
players.bio: Basic was still a teenager when people around his first club, Zrinjski Mostar, started describing him as “old-fashioned” in the best possible sense – not because of the way he looked or spoke, but because of the way he understood football. While many young midfielders enjoy rolling out tricks or flashy skills, Basic built his reputation through honing positioning, timing and an unusual sense for controlling rhythm. His career has since taken a route very few Bosnian players imagined for themselves. After winning the Bosnian title with Zrinjski, he moved to Russian side Orenburg during one of the strangest periods in modern European football, before later continuing in Kazakhstan with Astana. Away from the spotlight of major leagues, he quietly developed into one of the more tactically reliable midfielders of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s new generation.

players.name: Ivan Sunjic
players.bio: Even before Sunjic arrived in England in 2019, when Birmingham City paid around £7m to bring him to the Championship, he had the reputation of being one of Dinamo Zagreb’s most important young players, a captain, a Croatia international, a midfielder with youth and Champions League experience. Instead of glamorous Premier League nights, though, most of his next four years were defined by survival fights, managerial chaos and some of the most physically demanding football in Europe. Born in Zenica and raised in Croatia, Sunjic switched his international allegiance from Croatia to Bosnia and Herzegovina in 2024. For many supporters the move felt symbolic, as a player once seen as a key part of Croatia’s future chose instead to play for Bosnia even while the national team was in a period of renewal.

players.name: Amar Memic
players.bio: Only a few years ago Memic was playing in the second tier of Bosnian football with Radnik Hadzici, far away from the spotlight or serious national-team conversations. His rise since then has been rapid even by modern football standards: Slovenia, then Czech football, then Viktoria Plzen and European competition, all in the space of four years. Sergej Barbarez’s decision to call him up for the senior national team in 2025 initially surprised many supporters in Bosnia and Herzegovina. But inside the squad, Memic quickly earned respect because of the qualities that define almost his entire game: pace, direct running and relentless energy with and without the ball. Barbarez praised his mentality and willingness to work for the team, while teammates describe him as one of the easier personalities to integrate into the dressing room.

players.name: Amir Hadziahmetovic
players.bio: When Amir Hadziahmetovic injured his knee during Hull City’s push for the Championship play-offs under Sergej Jakirovic, fears immediately flared that his World Cup was over. The Bosnia and Herzegovina FA publicly confirmed the injury was serious, surgery was expected and recovery in time for the tournament looked impossible. Which made his name appearing on Sergej Barbarez’s final squad list one of the biggest surprises of the announcement. Barbarez later explained that doctors believed Hadziahmetovic could still recover without surgery before the World Cup, though with significant risk for his long-term future. He chose the tournament anyway. That decision probably explains him better than any tactical description could. Calm and intelligent, if rarely spectacular, Hadziahmetovic has spent most of his career being appreciated more by coaches than by highlight compilations. Since leaving Zeljeznicar for Turkey as a teenager, he quietly developed into one of Bosnia’s most tactically disciplined midfielders.

players.name: Dzenis Burnic
players.bio: Burnic’s football education took place at Borussia Dortmund, one of Europe’s most demanding academies, where he was considered among the club’s more promising midfield prospects during his teenage years. He won multiple youth titles with Dortmund and made his Champions League debut at just 18 – one of only two first-team appearances for the German giants – but his senior career turned into something far less glamorous than many expected: loans, relegation fights and constant battles for stability shaped the first half of his career. Born in Germany to Bosnian parents, Burnic represented the land of his birth throughout the youth categories before switching to Bosnia and Herzegovina in 2024, the result of many years of consideration: after receiving his first senior call-up he admitted that playing for Bosnia had been something he had thought about “since childhood”.

players.name: Nikola Katic
players.bio: During Plymouth Argyle’s famous FA Cup victory over Liverpool in 2025, Katic lost a tooth early in the game, kept playing and then swore live on television afterwards because, as he put it himself: “I fucking love it.” It was probably the perfect summary of the defender Bosnia and Herzegovina supporters had already grown to love. Tall, aggressive and uncompromising, Katic belongs to a generation of centre-backs that treats defending as a personal battle. Before choosing Bosnia at senior level, he represented Croatia in youth categories and built his early career through the lower leagues of Croatian football before Slaven Belupo gave him his breakthrough. Rangers later signed him during Steven Gerrard’s rebuild of the club, where his physical style quickly made him an Ibrox favourite.

players.name: Kerim Alajbegovic
players.bio: Before most supporters in Bosnia and Herzegovina had even seen him play a full senior match, clips of Alajbegovic’s performances had already spread across social media. The hype only grew after Bayer Leverkusen, having honed him since 2021, sold him to Red Bull Salzburg last summer and he started playing regular, senior first-team football for the first time. To say he shone is an understatement: Alajbegovic was named the Austrian Bundesliga’s newcomer of the season, his performances good enough for Bayer to trigger their buy-back clause before the end of March. Simon Rolfes, their managing director for sport, said the player had “not only fulfilled the high expectations placed in him in Salzburg, he has even exceeded them in a very short space of time”. What has surprised people even more than his talent is his complete absence of fear. Despite being only 17, Sergej Barbarez trusted him to take penalties in both World Cup playoff shootouts, while Alajbegovic himself changed the rhythm of both matches after entering from the bench. Inside Bosnia and Herzegovina he is already viewed not only as the country’s biggest prospect, but as one of the future faces of the national team.

players.name: Esmir Bajraktarevic
players.bio: Years before he became one of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s biggest talents, a home video filmed in Appleton, Wisconsin showed a five-year-old Bajraktarevic playing football in his backyard wearing an outsized Edin Dzeko shirt, celebrating after putting the ball into a small goal. The clip went viral after Bajraktarevic chose Bosnia and Herzegovina over the United States at senior level. “When people ask me where I’m from, I say I’m Bosnian,” said Bajraktarević, whose parents reached America after fleeing the Srebrenica genocide. “I grew up in a Bosnian family. I speak Bosnian with my parents. It’s my blood, it’s my roots.” There was something poetic about his senior international debut: coming off the bench against the Netherlands in 2024, within five minutes he had set up a goal for Dzeko himself. Fearless in one-on-one situations and seemingly untouched by pressure despite his age, he quickly became one of the faces of Sergej Barbarez’s new generation, and scored the decisive penalty in the shootout victory over Italy that secured Bosnia’s place at the World Cup.

players.name: Stjepan Radeljic
players.bio: At 2.01m tall, Radeljic is impossible to ignore on a football pitch, but his career has been much less straightforward than his physical presence suggests. Before establishing himself at the highest level, he spent years moving through reserve football, loans and smaller clubs in Croatia, Germany and Moldova, never really being seen as a major prospect. Patience eventually paid off. After helping Sheriff Tiraspol during the most successful period in the club’s history he found real stability at Rijeka, where he developed into one of the leaders of the side that won the Croatian title in 2025. Although naturally associated with aerial duels and physical defending because of his height, Radeljic’s reputation is as an expert in positioning, discipline and consistency.

players.name: Martin Zlomislic
players.bio: Zlomislic took a relatively quiet route to the highest level, gradually building his reputation through consistency rather than hype. After developing at Siroki Brijeg, he moved to Rijeka and established himself as one of the more reliable goalkeepers in Croatian football, helping the club negotiate title races and European nights. While Nikola Vasilj remains Bosnia and Herzegovina’s undisputed No 1, Zlomislic offers the squad an experienced and dependable backup option. He rarely attracts much attention, which for a goalkeeper is usually a good sign. Coaches in Croatia have often praised his composure, positioning and ability to stay calm in difficult moments.

players.name: Haris Tabakovic
players.bio: Tabakovic’s participation in the World Cup was thrown into doubt in May when he fractured a metatarsal in a league game against Hoffenheim. It was initially assumed that he would have to withdraw, but he insisted he was “doing well” and “will travel to the World Cup”. At 31 it is a chance he may not get again. Born in Switzerland to Bosnian parents, he spent years moving through reserve teams and unsuccessful transfers, scoring goals almost everywhere without ever convincing people he belonged. Then came the breakthrough almost nobody expected: in his late twenties Tabaković suddenly exploded at Hertha Berlin, scoring 22 goals in 32 games and earning a move to Borussia Mönchengladbach and, more importantly, a Bosnia and Herzegovina call-up. Nearly two years elapsed between his debut and his first international goal but then got four in five games, including the late equaliser against Italy that, a penalty shootout later, earned Bosnia their World Cup place.

players.name: Nidal Celik
players.bio: Before he had even turned 18, Celik was wearing the captain’s armband at Sarajevo, a club where it is usually reserved for older players with years behind them. Inside Bosnian football that was treated less as a publicity stunt and more as confirmation of something coaches had been quietly repeating for years: that Celik carried himself differently from most players his age. Tall, calm and unusually mature in possession, he belongs to a generation of Bosnian defenders shaped as much by modern positional football as by old Balkan ideas of toughness. Lens moved for him in early 2025, making him one of the most expensive transfers in the history of the Bosnian league. For many supporters, though, the detail that mattered more was where he came from. Celik is a Sarajevo boy in the purest sense: academy product, captain, and one of the few major talents in recent years who genuinely felt tied to the club before moving abroad.

players.name: Jovo Lukic
players.bio: Lukic’s path to the Bosnia and Herzegovina World Cup squad has been anything but straightforward. While many attacking players from his generation developed abroad early, he spent years building his career through Bosnian football before moving to Romania in 2024, where his first season at Universitatea Craiova was a failure; only two goals in 31 appearances and growing doubts about whether he belonged at that level. Then everything suddenly changed. Since moving to Universitatea Cluj last summer Lukic’s form has exploded, finishing the campaign as Romania’s top scorer and forcing his way into Sergej Barbarez’s plans. “Even if I don’t play, it’s a big thing for me just to be there,” he said. Lukic joked this season that he had not scored a hat-trick “for five years” before finally doing it against Hermannstadt in December, dedicating all three goals to his young son and wheeling out a celebration the boy had asked for.

players.name: Ermin Mahmic
players.bio: Only a few days before Sergej Barbarez announced Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup squad, Ermin Mahmic was still officially an Austria youth international. Then came one more successful late-night operation from Barbarez and sporting director Emir Spahic, who convinced the Slovan Liberec midfielder to switch allegiances just in time for the tournament. What made the story even more unusual was that Mahmic had already changed direction once before. Born and raised in Austria, he briefly represented Bosnia at Under-19 level before returning to Austria’s system, only to now switch back again. “You can’t believe how happy and proud he is to be with us,” Barbarez said after Fifa approved the change. “He has the profile of a player we don’t have much, a player who will continue to develop, with the aim that Bosnia will have good players for the next 10 years.”

players.name: Alisson
players.bio: Goalkeeping is in the family’s DNA. Alisson’s great grandfather played as a keeper in his home town club and his father wore the gloves in every match with his friends. His mother was also a goalkeeper, but in handball. And his older brother, Muriel, showed Alisson the way by turning pro and being a role model for him at Internacional. Alisson joined Roma in 2016 and has been at Liverpool since 2018, winning every possible trophy, including the Champions League. Religion plays a big part in his life, and there are videos of him baptising Roberto Firmino and Fred's wife. "There is a small distinction: I’m not religious, I’m a follower of Christ," he told Folha de São Paulo in 2020. "When we talk about religion, it’s very much tied to traditions and factors that somewhat tarnish the rich history of Christianity. Jesus is much more than religion.”

players.name: Wesley
players.bio: At 16 Wesley almost saw his dream of becoming a footballer collapse. His youth club, Figueirense, fell into financial turmoil, having to cut its academy entirely. He was then rejected at Avaí before joining the modest Atlético Tubarão, only for Covid to hit. “At that moment,” he said last year, “I thought about stopping playing and starting working. It seemed like a sign of destiny: I wasn't supposed to play football. So I started working with my mom and sister in a restaurant where my mom was a chef. I worked there as a parking attendant and by then I had little faith in football.” But his sister encouraged him to try again, he returned to Tubarão, was given a trial by Flamengo, and “that changed everything”. By 19 he was a regular in Flamengo’s first team, drawing admirers from across Europe, and Roma paid €25m for him last year. The move was an eye-opening experience; as he prepared to fly to Italy, he said last year, his phone beeped. “I received an SMS from Paulo Dybala. He asked how I was, told me to send him a message if I needed anything. When I read it, I jumped and started shouting around the house to my wife: 'Dybala just sent me a message!'"

players.name: Gabriel Magalhães
players.bio: Now one of the world’s best centre-backs, Gabriel had a stuttering start to life in football. As a young teenager he left his São Paulo home to join Avaí’s academy, but returned after a week with homesickness. After speaking to his father, Gabriel called Avaí coach Diogo Fernandes who agreed to have him back. “We knew this could happen as the first problem a footballer faces is being far away from their family,” Diogo said in 2020. “We also knew that Avaí would be one of the few clubs that would accept Gabriel. He was skinny and very tall, a left-footer, but he was a bit soft in challenges and couldn’t head the ball too well. We were right in the end, though: he was the biggest transfer in Avaí history [when sold to Lille in 2017] and a superb player … He wasn’t built by talent, but by his will to improve his shortcomings and learn that he could surpass any difficulties life throws at him. We knew he could be a top player from the start.” After three years in France, he joined Arsenal for £27m. Known at the Emirates as Big Gabi, to avoid confusion with namesake Martinelli.

players.name: Marquinhos
players.bio: Described as “a leader on and off the field” by his club manager, Luis Enrique, Marquinhos has been one of the best in his position for more than a decade, shining since he joined PSG from Roma in 2013. As a boy he enjoyed playing in goal; marshalling the defence has always been in his nature. Now, as Ancelotti’s international captain, he will be in charge of lifting the trophy if Brazil win it this summer. He had won 10 Ligue 1 titles by the time he finally laid hands on the Champions League trophy – for the first time – last year, and praised Luis Enrique for making it happen. "He is certainly one of the best coaches in the world, and he shows that to us every day. He is a very demanding coach, very clear with the players, and he always brings something extra.”

players.name: Casemiro
players.bio: One of Real Madrid’s all-time greats. When he moved to Manchester United in 2022 he was asked by reporters about one of the many new things he would have to get used to, namely no Champions League football. Was it something that bothered him? “Well, I have five already,” he smiled. Since then, his time at United has been mixed – a sharp decline, but, this season – his last at United – a major recovery, finding form just at the right time for this summer. Ancelotti told reporters last year: “There are no players in Brazil with the same characteristics as Casemiro.” He is known, too, for quiet leadership, and told the Guardian in 2020: “It’s just my personality. I am very, very, very calm, even if my playing style is aggressive and everyone can see that. It’s my mum’s values: be polite, treat everyone the same. You can’t lose that, ever. You don’t need to shout to lead.” His correct name is Casimiro; he became, and stuck with ‘Casemiro’ instead when São Paulo spelt his name wrong on his shirt. “I played very well that day and I’m a very superstitious person, so I told the club: ‘There’s no need to change it.’ It was a one-match mistake; now I’ll carry it with me forever."

players.name: Alex Sandro
players.bio: No matter where he goes, Alex Sandro collects trophies. After Athletico Paranaense (2008-2010), Santos (2010-2011), Porto (2011-2015) and Juventus (2015-2024), he came to Flamengo in 2024 and since then he has won every trophy there is to win in South America. He owes much of his speed, precision and technical focus to having spent plenty of time as a youngster doing capoeira, the traditional Brazilian non-contact martial art. “Even in Italy some coaches told me that some of the movements resemble capoeira,” he said. “My mother did it, my brother too; we have a history within it.” He always had a solid work ethic, too: as a boy he sold CDs and DVDs on the streets. His brother Flavio told Globo in 2022: “It was his way to get money to buy candy or whatever else he wanted.”

players.name: Jose Vinicius Junior
players.bio: Almost everyone in Brazil knew what was coming from Viní Jr, right from the start. The hype began when he was a pre-teen at Flamengo, and he delivered. In 2017, at 17, he agreed a £39m move to Real Madrid, where he grew into the symbol of the team’s success post-Cristiano Ronaldo. Form hasn’t always been consistent, including with the Seleção, but if there is one man who knows how to get the best out of him it is Carlo Ancelotti. In 2024, Vinícius said of his then club manager: “He's done everything for me. He's always given me confidence, told me off when I needed it. He cares about me as much as I care about him. He's changed me as a player, not on the pitch, but in the way I think.” He has repeatedly been the victim of racist abuse in La Liga, and has repeatedly spoken out against it. He said in 2024: “I just want to play football but it’s hard to move forward … I feel less and less like playing. But it never crossed my mind [to leave Spain] because if I leave Spain I give the racists exactly what they want.”

players.name: Bruno Guimarães
players.bio: At the age of three Guimarães was in hospital with pneumonia, and again two years later. Doctors encouraged his parents to get him active to help his recovery and strengthen his lungs. First they tried swimming, but he didn’t like it. Then came football, and he didn’t look back. It was a tough road to the top of the game though. At 16 he joined Audax in São Paulo, where, he told UOL in 2022, “I shared a room with 22 other boys, some rats, and a few leaks.” He severely doubted himself at Audax, crippled by nerves, and said he owed everything to his coach there, Fernando Diniz. “I used to be terrified of playing soccer, feeling sick and even vomiting before games, skinny as a rail, with no strength whatsoever, listening to Diniz tell me 'you're going to be a spectacular player'. And I just thought 'this guy is crazy'. He told me I would be good at any profession I chose because I was dedicated and determined … He's a unique guy, a fundamental person. I root for him and we're still in touch.” Guimarães’s club future is unclear amid links to Manchester United and Chelsea.

players.name: Matheus Cunha
players.bio: Cunha was used regularly by Tite during the 2022 World Cup cycle, but, left on the fringes of the Atlético Madrid first team, missed the cut for Qatar. “I felt bad, really bad,” he told The Guardian last year. “I suffered a lot. I felt bad not to have played the World Cup and I felt even worse because I didn’t go to the World Cup because I wasn’t playing for my club.” Moving to Wolves turned things round for him. “Sometimes all we want in life is affection. People think we have everything, but we’re human beings, too. We need a bit of understanding - we have our difficulties. Wolves gave me that joy back.” He played his way back into international contention, earning last year’s £62.5m move to Manchester United. Carlo Ancelotti is a big fan of his style and versatility, so he could become Brazil’s fifth different No9 in the last five World Cups – a position without a regular ‘owner’ since Ronaldo stopped playing.

players.name: Neymar
players.bio: Seventy-nine goals in 128 international appearances: a formidable record for a striker at his fourth World Cup – but it’s obvious that the 34-year-old is a shadow of the player he once was. And that’s before you consider the calf injury he picked up just before the tournament, which will leave him, at best, lacking full match fitness in the early stages. He hasn’t played for the national team since 2023 due to injuries and patchy form since his return to Santos, but was still given the nod by Carlo Ancelotti ahead of rivals such as João Pedro and Richarlison, seemingly because of his aura. "I know full well that Neymar is much loved, not only by the public but also by the players," Ancelotti said. "This is ​a factor, because we have to consider the atmosphere that will surround Neymar's call-up.” He remains defiant in the face of criticism, and continues to embrace his off-pitch celebrity status. In 2020 he showed off his acting abilities when he made a cameo as a monk named João in episodes six and eight of his favourite Netflix series, La Casa de Papel, in 2020.

players.name: Raphinha
players.bio: As a teenager, Raphinha almost gave up on the sport. He started out playing in a social project club in Porto Alegre and was frequently dismissed for being too skinny, failing a series of club trials, shaking his confidence. But eventually he was scouted by the Avaí academy and moved from there to Portugal’s Vitória Guimarães, where he made his professional breakthrough. Since then, via spells in France, England and now at Barcelona, he has steadily developed into one of the fastest, most threatening wide attackers in the game, a nightmare for opponents. He finished 2024-25 with 34 goals scored and 22 assists in 57 games, but only fifth in the Ballon d'Or rankings, which didn’t go down well. “I think I deserved to be first for what I delivered during the season, for the titles I won, for the numbers I achieved, and for everything I contributed on the pitch. I think I deserved to win."

players.name: Weverton
players.bio: One of Carlo Ancelotti's many surprise picks in this final squad, Weverton turns 39 in December. Why did he get the nod over younger rivals?
