Ancelotti said simply: “In some positions, like goalkeeper, we prioritise experience.” Choosing a seasoned pro as backup may have been prompted by concerns over Alisson's fitness and Ederson’s patchy form going into the tournament, and he will be an able backup option if he’s called upon in what will be his second World Cup. He was a key part of Palmeiras’s success from 2018 to earlier this year when he moved to Grêmio on a three-year deal, though age has started to show – and he has never been an expert penalty saver. His reaction to being called-up went viral online: a video showing him appearing to faint when hearing his name. “My heart was racing,” he explained later. “Jokes aside, my fall was from relief, emotion, and everything that goes through your mind during that moment.”

players.name: Danilo
players.bio: One of the few players to have won both Champions League and Copa Libertadores titles. Last year his header secured Flamengo their fourth Libertadores in the final against rivals Palmeiras. A right-back as a youngster, he adapted to the centre during his years at Juventus, and is now a rock in Flamengo’s backline, and their captain. Being a leader is something he has done since childhood when he took care of his three younger brothers while his father, a truck driver, and mother, a housekeeper, were away. "I prepared a feeding bottle for each one, because after the bottle they would fall asleep. I babysat all of them a lot," he told Globo TV. One of the brothers, Dener, said: “Danilo has always been that super-protective guy. Giving advice, scolding … He has also always been very studious, always top of his class.” The multilingual Danilo still prefers reading books to playing video games. “I read a little bit of everything, but what I like most are books that talk about human interactions, relationships, and stories of overcoming challenges and resilience.”

players.name: Bremer
players.bio: Gleison Bremer’s dad liked to name his children after important people. One of Bremer’s brothers is called Kennedy after John F, while Bremer was named after Germany’s 1990 World Cup final goalscorer Andreas Brehme. “There are three brothers in the family and we were all named after someone important, revered or admired,” Bremer told The Telegraph in 2023. “Andreas Brehme was a player my father liked very much.” When Brehme died in 2024, Bremer posted online: “You were my dad's idol. For me, you will always remain an example of sportsmanship.” Bremer joined Juventus from Torino in 2022, making his Brazil debut the same year. Dominant, speedy and fierce, he says he owes a lot of qualities to playing street football as a kid. “I learnt how not to lose. I learnt how to be aggressive in the streets. I also learnt how to be a little bit sneaky.” When he wasn’t playing football or studying as a boy, he was helping his family on the farm or selling ice cream. “My mother used to make the ice creams, and I would help her put them in the freezer so I could sell them the next day, which was market day. She would give me half of what I made so I could go play soccer in the neighbouring town, or play video games," he told ESPN.

players.name: Léo Pereira
players.bio: The last five years at Flamengo have been a rollercoaster for Léo Pereira. Stuck on the bench, he considered leaving the club, but, following Dorival Jr’s arrival for a third spell as coach, he grew into one of the pillars of a hugely successful team. The former Flamengo manager Filipe Luís said last year: "To me, he is the best centre-back playing in Brazil". Pareira earned his first cap in March this year, starting against France, and while his performance wasn't great, with the first goal originating from him losing a battle with Kylian Mbappé, his ability to start attacking moves from the back, his precise passing and good positioning convinced Carlo Ancelotti he deserved a place in this summer’s squad. “My time at Flamengo has been marked by my resilience and by the fact that I, my family, and those closest to me never stopped believing,” he said after his first call-up. “I had a bad start at Flamengo but, from 2022 onward, I managed to find very good consistency and win several titles with my teammates.”

players.name: Douglas Santos
players.bio: The 32-year-old left-back has had an on-off relationship with the Brazil national set-up. He earned his first call-up back in 2013 but struggled to build on that, and, in March last year, having played for Zenit St Petersburg since 2019 and being given Russian citizenship in 2024, was on the verge of switching his international allegiance to Russia - until he was put on Brazil’s provisional squad list for the World Cup qualifiers. “I’ve always had a lot of respect for Russia, as it’s the country where I have built so much of my career,” he told Fifa.com in May. “But wearing the Brazilian shirt has always been the dream, so that took priority.” His inclusion in this squad is the realisation of a lifelong dream. “My first clear memory is of the World Cup in 2002, watching the matches with my family, as well as the anticipation before each game. That really marked my childhood.”

players.name: Fabinho
players.bio: Since joining the Portuguese club Rio Ave at 18, Fabinho has lived outside Brazil, and has been in the Saudi Pro League captaining Al-Ittihad since 2023. The most successful years of his career were at Liverpool, winning the Champions League in his first season, the title in his second. His decision to leave three years ago, after 219 appearances in five years, surprised many. “Jürgen Klopp called me and he asked me what I wanted,” Fabinho says. “I said that I’d heard the offer and liked it but that I wanted to hear from him what he thought. He said that if there are any players who want to leave, he won’t hold them back. It was a speech that kind of opened the door for me to leave. I left in peace and happy with my decision.” He brings a wealth of experience and leadership to the squad this summer. When he got the call, he wrote online: “Another opportunity to live one of the greatest honours of my life: to play a World Cup for my country. "Today and always grateful to God! Let's go!"

players.name: Danilo Santos
players.bio: Back in 2020, the Palmeiras Under-20s were training against the first team’s reserves, watched by the first team head coach, Vanderlei Luxemburgo. A skinny 18-year old quickly caught his eye. "He was everywhere,” said former Brazil manager Luxemburgo. “He didn't pass backwards or sideways, just to the front. I thought: ‘Holy shit, what a player’. I wanted him.” Danilo made his first team debut shortly after was signed by Nottingham Forest in 2022 for a reported £16m. But on the opening day broke his ankle, meaning he featured just 13 times in that first season. Botafogo brought him back to Brazil for £21m in 2025, and, in March this year, he won his first cap in the Carlo Ancelotti era. In May he talked about why he always seems so composed in the middle. “When I’m on the pitch, I don’t think about anything else,” he told Fifa.com. “I feel completely free. It doesn’t matter what’s happening in the match, who we’re playing against, whether we’re playing well or not. I always feel at ease – like I’m having a kickabout back in Fazenda Coutos, just enjoying a game, playing with my dog, Pit. Everything feels very natural out there.”

players.name: Endrick
players.bio: It’s been a rollercoaster few years for Endrick. At 16 the Palmeiras prodigy signed a pre-contract deal with Real Madrid in a €70m move, dubbed the greatest Brazilian prospect since Neymar. In his first season there, when he turned 18, he had ups and downs, showing flashes of brilliance. But then it turned sour: Carlo Ancelotti left, Endrick was injured, and when he returned to fitness, new coach Xabi Alonso barely used him at all. It was a reassuring talk with Ancelotti, by then Brazil’s coach, which lifted his spirits; when he joined Lyon on loan last year Endrick said Ancelotti had “told me to go out, play and develop your skills where you can play and be happy." In France, he has rebuilt his confidence and showed off those qualities, forcing his way back into the Brazil set-up. In 2023 he talked about handling criticism. “My mother and my sister say that I’m cold. People say I have a heart of ice and I’m very cold with the decisions I make and what I say … I used to be angry and try to counter the criticism. But then I understood that I didn’t need to prove it, I didn’t need to show the opposite. I’m Endrick. If they want to insult me, I won’t see it.”

players.name: Lucas Paquetá
players.bio: “I don’t think Flamengo needed me, but I needed Flamengo.” Paquetá, having been cleared last year of spot-fixing following a two-year investigation, was determined to leave West Ham and go home. The stress he was under was evident in November when he was shown two yellow cards, both for dissent, in their defeat by Liverpool. He later responded online to a clip of Sky pundit Rob Green accusing him of “ridiculous behaviour” by writing: “It’s ridiculous to have your life and career affected for two years without any psychological support from the federation. Perhaps this ridiculous behaviour is just a reflection of everything I’ve had to endure and, it seems, have to continue enduring! I’m sorry if I’m not perfect.” When he arrived back in Brazil, with his £35.5m move from West Ham to Flamengo the most expensive incoming transfer in the country’s history, he said: “I've dreamt so much about this moment. All I want is to enjoy this happiness of finally being home. I want to be happy, bring joy to the nation, and give my best.” His ability to dictate play as a box-to-box midfielder make him a one-of-a-kind player in Ancelotti’s squad, and sure of a starting spot if fit.

players.name: Luiz Henrique
players.bio: A potent game-changing option from the bench for Carlo Ancelotti, who said before the tournament: “Luiz Henrique has extraordinary talent. He's very strong physically, fantastic in one-on-ones.” The speedy winger left Botafogo for Zenit St Petersburg in January last year. He’s known for his Black Panther mask goal celebration, which dates back to his time at Betis when one of the coaches said he looked like the Marvel superhero. “This idea appealed to my friend, who composed a song for me and suggested that I wear a mask and display the Black Panther gesture during celebrations,” he told Russian paper Izvestia last year. “He emphasised that it would catch on, kids and fans would love it.” But it’s not his favourite superhero. “I really like the Flash, because he's so fast. I'm also often called fast. So out of all the superpowers, I would choose the speed that Flash has.” He also loves Lionel Messi, and named his dog after him. “Yes, it's true. I named him Messi because he is also light and, in a good way, he reminds me of him.”

players.name: Gabriel Martinelli
players.bio: Martinelli has already helped Brazil’s Under-23s win gold at the Olympics. Can he now help end Brazil’s spell of no World Cup titles in 24 years? This is his second chance, having also featured in Qatar. He arrives in the US on the back of a title-winning club season, and remains as cool and composed as ever, brushing off pressure. In 2022 he reflected on his childhood, saying how he loved football, but rarely dreamt of making it as a professional – until, that is, the World Cup came round. “When the flag is painted on the front of your house,” he wrote in the Player’s Tribune, “when you have 15 family members in your backyard, the TV is rolled out, there are balloons, fireworks, honking horns, your cousins are running up the stairs and your uncle is at the barbecue … how can you not? You are watching the Seleção and you’re thinking: ‘imagine wearing that shirt at a World Cup.”

players.name: Ederson
players.bio: Playing with Ederson is like having an extra ball-playing defender in the backline. During his eight years at Manchester City he was often the starting point for attacking moves – a quality that owes a lot to his upbringing at São Paulo FC where he idolised Rogério Ceni, the top-scoring goalkeeper of all time, who hit 131 goals in his 25-year career. Luiz Batista da Silva Júnior, the club’s then youth goalkeeping coach, told the Guardian in 2018: “We had a plan to produce Ceni’s successor, so our training sessions with the boys had a big focus on footwork. Although he was very shy, Ederson paid attention to all the exercises and learnt everything quickly.” In 2023 Ceni told Ederson: “I feel you are the extension of my career, how I was. I can see myself when I see you play.” In 2025 Ederson told the BBC about a ritual that helped him through his career in England. "I have one superstition. I play every game in the same boxers. Eight years, the same boxers." He left City for Fenerbahce last year.

players.name: Roger Ibañez
players.bio: The son of a Uruguayan mother and a Brazilian father – whom he used to help in the family's carpentry shop when he was a boy – Ibañez was eligible to play for Uruguay, but opted to commit to the country where he was born. Raised at Fluminense's academy, he moved to Italy in 2019 with Atalanta, then won the Conference League as a key member of José Mourinho's Roma in 2022. A year later he joined Al-Ahli for a reported €30m. One of the fastest centre-backs at the tournament, he also brings versatility, is able to operate at right-back or as a defensive midfielder. Making a World Cup squad has long been his dream, and when it finally happened, he wrote online: “This is the meaning of dream come true. In 2020 I said it openly, 2022 I kept quiet, but 2026 the story will be different. It is with great gratitude that I look back and am proud of the journey so far. Thank you to my family, my friends, my teammates who were part of this journey … God bless Brazil!"

players.name: Igor Thiago
players.bio: It’s been a gruelling journey to the top. Aged 13, Thiago was doing odd jobs to help keep the family afloat after the death of his father. While his mum earned a living as a street cleaner in their home city of Cidade Ocidental, he found himself being a bricklayer’s assistant, a fruit carrier at the market, and a car washer. “I had friends who wanted me to steal with them,” he told the Guardian in January. “They weren’t really friends, of course. They were acquaintances from the street, but they wanted me to use drugs, to follow the wrong path in life. Many people in my city live a reality similar to or worse than mine at the time. Many people have alcoholic parents, who use drugs, who were abandoned.” His football career sparked into life when Cruzeiro offered him a trial. He moved from there to Bulgarian champions, Ludogorets, in 2022, then to Club Brugge, and finally to Brentford for a reported £30m in 2024. Two knee injuries meant he missed almost all his first season, but his second was stellar. “I believe I am ready,” he said. “All I know how to do in my life is score goals. God has prepared me for this moment, and if he allows me, we will bring the sixth World Cup title to Brazil.”

players.name: Rayan
players.bio: In 2018 sports outlet Globo Esporte posted online about a youngster they’d spotted in Vasco da Gama’s youth system: “Take a note of this name, Vasco fans: Rayan, age 11, scored 115 goals in 2017.” Eight years later and Rayan has just arrived at his first World Cup on the back of a brilliant Premier League season with Bournemouth, and with the world at his feet. He moved to England in January for £24.7m from Vasco, where he had been since the age of six, having scored 20 goals in 57 games for Vasco in 2025. He hit the ground running in England, taking full advantage of the gap left behind at Bournemouth when Antoine Semenyo moved to Manchester City. He only made his Brazil debut in April, and his future looks immense. And he has it all planned out. “I see myself, in five years, playing in Europe, for Bournemouth or another team that signs me,” he said in March. “In five years, I hope to play for the most powerful team in the world."

players.name: Dayne St. Clair
players.bio: A gentle giant off the pitch, St. Clair is a different character on it and at 1.90m, he is an intimidating sight for attackers. Armed with quick reflexes and strong hands, he became Minnesota United’s second ‘Wonderwall’ during his time at the club – the first being the standing-room only section at their ground. After winning the MLS Goalkeeper of the Year award in 2025, he said: “I almost feel like there’s a wall behind me and the wall on the line that means no ball’s going to go in.” The Wonderwall had a Bon Jovi-based tribute song for him, too: “Whoa he’s got great hair / whoa-oh, Dayne St Clair / with his hands, he’ll save it I swear / woah-oh, Dayne St Clair.” He moved to Inter Miami for the 2026 season, becoming teammates with Lionel Messi. “Any time that guy's on your team, the expectation is to win and to lift trophies,” he said. “To have those expectations and that pressure is a privilege.”

players.name: Alistair Johnston
players.bio: Born into a football-loving family, Johnston used to wake up at 4.30am on Saturday mornings in British Columbia to watch English Premier League matches live, and has never lost that love for the game. Enthusiastic, hard-tackling and full of natural leadership and passion, he is loved by Celtic fans for understanding what it means to represent their club, ever since the day he arrived back in December 2022. He told the Guardian last year: “I see what the club means to people. I understand that if I’m a fan of a club like this, what would I like to see from my players? I’m honest with my work, defensively love a tackle, love being physical. I’m a bit football mad. I love the city. [It’s] the biggest fish bowl in all of Europe. Everyone knows who you are. You don’t know if they love you or hate you but you know they know who you are. I’m not famous outside of maybe a 30-mile radius of Glasgow city centre … but what a radius.” An excellent passer and relentless one-on-one defender, he can play in multiple positions but has all of the tools required of a modern full-back.

players.name: Alfie Jones
players.bio: A casual locker room chat with Canada winger Liam Millar when the pair were at Hull together in the 2024-2025 season proved career-changing for Jones. The Bristol-born defender mentioned to Millar that he had a Canadian grandmother, so his clubmate pointed out the possibilities. What followed was a whirlwind period for Jones, who went through the process to earn citizenship and made his Canada debut against Venezuela in November last year. A hulking 1.90m centre-back known for aggression, ball-playing skills and aerial prowess, he was named Hull’s player of the year in 2023 – chosen by then-manager Liam Rosenior – before joining Middlesbrough last year. Jones had an injury-disrupted season, but regained sharpness in time to take his place in this summer’s squad. “I feel there's a connection there, it's in my bloodline being linked to Canada, and I'm a very emotional guy,” he said last year. “So, the thought of putting on the Canadian shirt and just doing the best I can to represent the country is what drives me.”

players.name: Luc De Fougerolles
players.bio: At one stage last year De Fougerolles, whose dad was born in Montreal, had more appearances for Canada than he did as a club professional – evidence of what a big impression the youngster has made in the international setup. Tasked to step in as Canada dealt with a rash of injuries in 2023 he immediately looked at home, showing positional awareness and composure on the ball. Born in London, De Fougerolles joined Fulham’s academy aged eight, and signed his first professional deal in 2023, winning scholar of the year. He joined Belgian Pro League side Dender that July to continue his development. “Meeting new teammates, living in a different country, everything about it has been completely different for me,” he said last year. “I’ve only ever known Fulham. To change that around, to go and live alone in Belgium has been tough, but my teammates have made it easy and it’s been a great group. I’ve really enjoyed my time here.” He was named Canada’s young player of the year for 2025.

players.name: Joel Waterman
players.bio: Waterman made history in 2020 as the first player from the new Canadian Premier League to transfer to an MLS club, when he joined CF Montréal from Cavalry FC. That proved a shrewd decision for Waterman, who had been playing at the Canadian university level in 2018 before joining Cavalry. He has grown into an important player for Montréal, becoming one of the club’s captains during his last few seasons. A former midfielder, he is comfortable on the ball but also aggressive defensively, which has helped him become a regular in the national setup and for his new club, Chicago Fire. Waterman is also part of a big sporting family: he is married to Carli Grosso, sister of Canada midfielder Julia Grosso, who is engaged to the Houston Astros baseball star, Jeremy Peña.

players.name: Mathieu Choinière
players.bio: It’s not often that a player gets to live out the dream of becoming a star player with their hometown team, but Mathieu Choinière experienced that with CF Montréal, earning two MLS All-Star game places during his seven seasons at the club. His consistency didn’t go unnoticed. He made the leap into European football with Grasshoppers in 2024, then moved back to MLS with the Swiss side’s parent club, Los Angeles FC, last year. LAFC’s co-president, John Thorrington, praised his “experience, intelligence, calm authority and competitive edge … we’re also excited about the opportunity for Mathieu to represent his country on the biggest stage and proud to support him in that ambition.” In an LA team filled with star names, Choinière fits in perfectly with his work ethic and his versatility, having played as a wing-back and winger for Montréal before settling into central midfield. He is also one of Canada’s best set-piece takers.

players.name: Stephen Eustáquio
players.bio: While others can claim to be the face of this Canadian team, Eustáquio is undoubtedly the heartbeat. A passionate leader, some of his best performances for club and country have come on the biggest stages, as he showed with Portuguese giants Porto, where he won multiple trophies. A tireless box-to-box runner, he brings huge versatility, equally adept as a box-crasher, a ball-progresser, a deep-lying defensive blocker, and even a centre-back – a coach's dream. Born in Ontario, not far from the US border, Eustáquio moved with his Portuguese parents back to Portugal when he was seven, where he began his journey into professional football. He moved from Porto to LAFC on loan in February this year, adding strength to their star-studded team. Canada teammate Richie Laryea says Eustáquio’s importance to the national side cannot be overstated: “He does a lot of the dirty work that people don't really recognise.”

players.name: Ismaël Koné
players.bio: At his best, Koné is a sight to behold, gliding across midfield, finding space and leaving defenders in his wake. He has the ability to light up games, as he showed in a 2024 friendly between Canada and France when he stood out despite being surrounded by World Cup winners. Born in Côte d’Ivoire, he moved with his mother to Montreal aged seven and was soon making his name as a teenager with the city’s MLS side. Koné was fast-tracked from there to Europe, first to Watford in 2022, then Marseille two years later. He joined Sassuolo last year, initially on loan before the move was made permanent in February. He might be softly spoken, but Koné has a fierce competitive spirit and is determined to improve his consistency. Last year, Jesse Marsch labelled him a work in progress. “I’ve made it very clear to him that we all believe in his talent,” said Marsch, “but we need to refine him as a soccer player, on the discipline and concentration of what he does on the pitch … I’m definitely not trying to limit his talents and his creativity, but I’m trying to add more tactical discipline.”

players.name: Cyle Larin
players.bio: Whatever happens this summer, Larin will forever be a cult hero in Canada for his displays during the 2022 qualifying cycle when he scored 13 goals in 16 games, helping Canada reach their first World Cup since 1986. Known for his smart movement in the box and first-time finishing, he can be a real handful as he showed during a brief spell at Real Valladolid where fans coined the term “Larinisimo”. Jesse Marsch said in March that Larin’s loan to Southampton was perfect timing. “It’s exactly what he needed. He's playing outstanding, he’s made a huge impact and will come into the World Cup in form, which is exactly what we need. I couldn’t be more excited for Cyle right now." When he signed for the Saints, Larin, who had already played in the US, Canada, Turkey, Belgium, Spain and the Netherlands, told their website that playing in England “has always been an itch. The style of play, the way of football in England is something I’ve always wanted to live and breathe.”

players.name: Jonathan David
players.bio: “He's the smartest player I've ever coached,” says Jesse Marsch. David really is a cerebral striker, a two-footed finisher with the workrate of a midfielder. He has undergone quite the journey from timid youngster playing in the Canadian capital, Ottawa, to becoming a global star with Juventus. It was his bold gamble as a teenager to sign with Belgian side Gent instead of staying in Canada that accelerated his road to the top. David then moved to Ligue 1 side Lille and immediately played his part in their surprise 2020-21 title win, before blossoming into one of the best strikers in Europe across his half-decade in France. Having been heavily linked with a move to England, David told The Guardian last year: “For me, watching the Premier League, the biggest difference it’s just the intensity. The game is always at a high-end tempo, always 100% back and forth for 90 minutes.” Known as The Iceman for his cool, unflappable nature, David sees himself as “someone that’s always calm. As a striker, someone who’s relied on to score goals, I think that quality comes in handy.” After a slow start in Turin, the striker earned more game time under Luciano Spalletti as the season unfolded.

players.name: Liam Millar
players.bio: Raised in Liverpool’s academy, Millar has gone on to build a solid career, with loans at Kilmarnock and Charlton followed by a breakthrough period in Switzerland with Basel. He moved back to England with Preston on loan in 2023, then signed for Hull on a permanent deal in August 2024. Millar sustained an ACL injury that October, needing surgery and a lengthy recovery period, but regained his strength and form thanks to “working my backside off for 12 months”. Millar is from a football family: his grandad Malcolm played for Sunderland, while his dad Alan turned out for Charlton. Alan went on to work in the TV and film industry as an electrician on Game of Thrones, Peaky Blinders, and X-Men, while mum Jo-Ann worked as a make-up artist on movies such as Resident Evil, Suicide Squad and Dune.

players.name: Tani Oluwaseyi
players.bio: A real team player, Oluwaseyi has a great engine, always running as hard to help defend as he does when attacking. A late bloomer who didn’t become an MLS regular until he was 23, his work ethic and knack for finding himself open in the box at perfect moments led to the coining of the term “Tani Time” at Minnesota United. His performances there earned a big move to Villarreal last year where he continued to develop his all-round game, saying: “Playing and training in Spain has already made me a smarter player.” Born in Nigeria, Oluwaseyi moved with his family to Mississauga, Canada at the age of 10. He spoke last year about what it would mean to represent Canada at a World Cup: “To be honest, I think until it happens, until we hear the [national] anthem, it won’t feel real. But I’m really excited for it.”

players.name: Derek Cornelius
players.bio: A striker until his teenage years, Cornelius still has some solid attacking instincts, as shown when he scored a fine long-range free-kick in a 1-0 friendly win against Wales last year. “I was working on it in training this week,” he said. “I felt confident and was in a good space, and I got good contact and it went in.” Typically, though, he’s known as an old-school centre-back, not shy to throw himself in front of, or at the ball in order to protect the box. With a CV that includes stops in Serbia, Germany, Canada, Greece, Sweden, France and now Scotland, Cornelius brings plenty of experience, but has also had a difficult season with Rangers, his loan spell undermined by injury problems. He will be hoping to prove the doubters wrong alongside Moïse Bombito in the heart of Canada’s defence.

players.name: Jacob Shaffelburg
players.bio: It’s not often that a player’s hair earns its own nickname, but Shaffelburg’s golden locks were known as the “Tennessee Waterslide” at his previous club Nashville SC, a nod to how the mullet flows behind him when he’s sprinting. His game may not be as flashy as his hair, but he is still an effective, tireless runner who knows how to strike the ball with power and technique. Born in Nova Scotia, Shaffelburg has form for rising to the moment on the big stage, having shone for Canada at the 2024 Copa América. He moved to Hollywood with LAFC last December, and arrives at this World Cup in a positive mood. Shaffelburg says playing under Jesse Marsch suits him: “The style of play just kind of fits me. He tells me just to get in and run behind, and that's all I want to do in general. It's right up my alley.”

players.name: Moïse Bombito
players.bio: At the last World Cup Bombito was 22, watching Canada on TV from his college campus. Now he is Canada’s rock at the back, the key who allows Jesse Marsch to play his high-pressing system. A late bloomer, Bombito was a winger until a coach suggested his pace, technique and physicality would make him a top central defender. Thanks to that positional shift he had a rapid rise to the MLS, earning a first Canadian call-up in 2023. His displays at the 2024 Copa América earned him a big move to Ligue 1 with Nice. “I chose Nice because of the human factor,” Bombito said last year. “You know, many top clubs look for a player who performs well. But they don't care about their mentality, their background, or what their family does. But I felt like the organisation here focused on who I am … This has all happened very quickly for me, I started playing at professional level when I was 23 years old … this club knows it will take time for me to adapt. I am very proud of my choice.” Bombito, who wears the number 64 in tribute to the Montreal-area bus he used to take to school, is currently recovering from a tibia fracture sustained in October. Marsch, who says the centre-back “can be our best player and is a future captain of this team,” is hoping he can win his fitness race for the World Cup.

players.name: Maxime Crépeau
players.bio: Less than a month out from what was supposed to be his first World Cup in 2022, Crépeau suffered an unfortunate leg injury, one that ruled him out of the tournament and kept him out of action for almost a year. If you were to ask him if he’d change a thing, he’d say no. The injury came on a game-saving stop in the 2022 MLS Cup final between Crépeau’s LAFC and Philadelphia Union, which LAFC went on to win. "You don't cut corners [as a keeper],” he said later. “You have to go all out, and I went all out. This is how I play." A smaller keeper at 1.81m tall, Crépeau certainly plays much bigger than his height, as he is unafraid to protect his box and his net. With his quick feet and strong reflexes, he loves playing in the biggest games, as he showed when he helped Canada reach the semi-finals of the 2024 Copa América. He speaks French, English and – due to his wife’s family being from Chile – Spanish, and his little daughter likes to mix it up. “Sometimes my daughter will start a sentence in French, will finish it in Spanish, and in the middle she'll put one or two words in English in there.”

players.name: Tajon Buchanan
players.bio: In a world where modern wingers can sometimes look robotic, Buchanan is old-school: an unpredictable attacker who loves to take on defenders using an arsenal of body feints, stepovers and flicks. A late bloomer who emerged from the NCAA college system, he made his name with MLS side New England Revolution from 2019. Buchanan developed the defensive side of his game so well that he played as a full-back for Club Brugge and wing-back at Inter in Serie A. He has always been most comfortable on the wing, though, and a move to Spain with Villarreal last year allowed him to return to that position, to the benefit of club and country. A quiet, serious character, Buchanan has had to show real resilience to progress in the game. His father died when he was just seven, and he left home at 15 to move to Colorado with his friend’s family. “I’m more introverted. It all comes naturally to me,” he told The Athletic. “Since I was a teenager, I’ve been away from home, alone, trying to chase my dream.”

players.name: Owen Goodman
players.bio: There’s no doubting Goodman’s commitment to Canada. The young keeper, who was born in London but lived in Ontario from the age of five to 13, hired an immigration lawyer last year to help him get a Canadian passport, with one eye on the World Cup. The persistence paid off as he passed his citizenship test, and earned a senior Canada call-up. “Once I’ve got my mind on one thing, there’s nothing that’s going to stop me. No one’s going to get in my way,” he told the Athletic, having previously played for England at youth level and turned down an approach from Nigeria while he was waiting for the application to be processed. When it was, he said: “I had shivers go down my spine a little bit … I was waiting so long you start to lose a bit of hope. I wanted to jump for joy.” A talented shot stopper with a big frame, Goodman is an exciting prospect, and has developed on loan from Palace at AFC Wimbledon, Huddersfield and most recently Barnsley, with the next step likely to be a Championship loan.

players.name: Alphonso Davies
players.bio: When Davies moves his feet, he flies – and it certainly felt like he had wings when he leapt to head home Canada’s first-ever World Cup goal in 2022. “When I ran to the corner flag, I looked to my left and I just saw all my teammates running towards me. When they all came around me, a lot of players were grabbing my jersey, pushing me, shoving me. I had Liam Millar headbutt me at one point, he was so excited. We both looked at each other and started laughing." It made a big impact in the stands, too: his parents, who had fled the civil war in Liberia to Ghana, where Davies was born in a refugee camp, before finding safe haven in Canada, were in the stadium watching. “They told me how proud they are of me and when I scored the goal, my mother teared up a bit. My parents are people of few words, they don't really say too much. But it really showed on their faces.” Back in 2018, aged 17, he gave a speech about his background to Fifa delegates in Moscow as part of the Canadian bid to host this World Cup. Davies then joined Bayern Munich from Vancouver Whitecaps, blossoming into one of the world’s best left-backs, and winning everything available to him along the way.

players.name: Ali Ahmed
players.bio: As a youngster, Ahmed failed trials at Enfield Town, Cheshunt and Tooting & Mitcham. Now he’s part of a World Cup squad, and brings huge versatility to Canada’s mix. He broke through at Vancouver Whitecaps during three eye-catching years in MLS where he featured at full-back, wing-back, central midfielder and attacking midfielder before specialising as a winger – a move which helped him blossom into one of the league’s best talents, and earn a transfer to Norwich in January. Ahmed is an effective if unorthodox dribbler who likes to use body feints – a nod to his strong love for basketball. He says that life in England has added grit to his game: “Before I came into the Championship, everyone I spoke to talked about the intensity and the physicality of it and it has lived up to every single word. I think that's good for me – it's an area I want to improve.” Reflecting on his return to England, five years after that series of trials at non-league clubs, he said: “Football is crazy and every player has their own journey to get to where they are. But if you told me then that I would be back in five years playing in the Championship, I don't know if I would have believed it.”

players.name: Jonathan Osorio
players.bio: A rare one-club man in the modern game, Osorio has been with his hometown team, Toronto FC, for 14 seasons, winning an MLS Cup, Supporters’ Shield and three Canadian Championships. A rambunctious box-crashing midfielder in his younger days, he has scored some big goals for club and country, such as his cheeky backheel against Tigres during the run to the 2018 Concacaf Champions Cup final, and his crucial goal for Canada against Mexico at the Estadio Azteca during World Cup 2022 qualifying. One of the true faces of Canadian soccer, and closing in on 100 caps, he brings a wealth of knowhow to this summer’s squad, with Jesse Marsch praising him pre-tournament for his “intensity, covering the ground, being hard to play against, being a dictator of the rhythm of the game … Oso’s experience, his stability, his mentality, his personality – he brings so much to the group.”

players.name: Richie Laryea
players.bio: It’s a cliché but it fits Laryea perfectly: he leaves everything on the pitch, every time he plays. It doesn’t matter if he’s making an overlapping run forward, getting stuck into a tackle or stepping up for a teammate, Laryea does it at 100%, playing on the edge without crossing the line. “When it gets hostile, I get even better,” he says. “I get in and around the line, then I stay there and don’t go past it.” A serious attacking threat, the Toronto FC man specialises in low crosses and winning fouls, but is also a capable 1v1 defender who can play both at left and right-back. He was arguably Canada’s best player in 2025 and his form has allowed Alphonso Davies to operate further forward for the national team. Laryea had an unsuccessful spell at Nottingham Forest, signing in 2022 but returning to Toronto after six months, initially on loan. “No negatives. A lot of positives,” he said about the Forest move. “I think it's helped me a lot to grow as a player and a person, especially off the field. Other people might have different perspectives … maybe people thought I was unhappy or miserable, but I think it was the opposite."

players.name: Niko Sigur
players.bio: Most players know their preferred position but Sigur, who has played as a defensive midfielder and full-back for club and country, is keeping his options open. “If the coach trusts you in those [different] positions,” he said, “then you have a bit of an advantage over other players.” Sigur, who holds dual citizenship of Canada and Croatia, should be a very handy Swiss Army knife of a player for Jesse Marsch this summer. He was heavily linked with Celtic and Wolves in the January window, and could be the focus of more speculation once the World Cup is over. Sigur told The Athletic last year: “I think it’s in Hajduk’s best interest to sell me at some point. It’s a matter of time. And I think I’m ready for it. I want it to make sense. I don’t want to go to a huge club and go on loan or sit on the bench. I want to play.”

players.name: Promise David
players.bio: No relation to Jonathan, Promise David has become a fan favourite thanks to his smile, personality and 1.96m frame. An injury scare put his involvement this summer in doubt, but he recovered to earn his place in Marsch’s squad, and deserves it for his drive and commitment. As one unfortunate Antwerp defender learned last season, you can try to literally rip the shirt off David’s back and that still won’t stop him from scoring. Despite his size, David is far from a one-dimensional centre forward, often earning praise for his deft feet and soft touch, which has allowed him to rack up an impressive and varied goal catalogue. Since arriving in Belgium from the Estonian league he has developed his all-round game, without losing his instinctive scoring touch, and helped lead Union Saint-Gilloise to their first league title in 90 years. He has been linked with several Premier League clubs, but his current side will want big money.

players.name: Nathan-Dylan Saliba
players.bio: Saliba’s progress as a youngster with hometown side CF Montréal was rapid. Within 12 months of his club breakthrough he made his Canada debut, was named one of the best Under-22s in MLS, and then moved to Belgian side Anderlecht last year for an undisclosed fee, with Montréal retaining a sell-on clause. “His qualities are insane,” former teammate Ismaël Koné told said at the time. “He’s one of those players that I can’t wait to flourish. Because he has so much to show, a lot of gifts to give. And I just can’t wait for you guys to see where he’s going to end up, and how big he’s going to be for the country.” There was plenty of praise, too, from Jesse Marsch: “He has a really good combination of intelligence, athleticism, technical ability, tactical awareness, responsibility, commitment to the game, a good eye for good attacking plays … we really believe he can be an important player for us.” Saliba’s parents are both Haitian and he speaks three languages: French, English and Haitian Creole.

players.name: Marcelo Flores
players.bio: Has been ruled out with an ACL injury. Replacement not yet announced.

players.name: Josimar ‘Vozinha’ Dias
players.bio: One of the living legends of Cape Verdean football and, along with Ryan Mendes, the only player to play at every major tournament Cape Verde have featured in, Josimar ‘Vozinha’ Dias is a national treasure. Arguably the country’s most beloved footballer, he only left the country at 25, turning professional when he signed for Angolan side Progresso do Sambizanga. Six months later he made his Cape Verde debut in a 2-0 win against Cameroon that helped to send the country to their first ever Afcon in 2013. “The first game he played I was scared because I didn't know what he was capable of,” Fernando Neves, Vozinha’s captain and centre back on the day, told the Guardian. “But after 10 minutes I wasn't scared. For me he's the best goalkeeper in Cape Verde's history.” Now Vozinha will make his World Cup bow just a week after turning 40.

players.name: Ianique ‘Stopira’ Tavares
players.bio: Praia’s favourite son, Ianique dos Santos Tavares was born and raised in the Achada de Santo António neighbourhood of the nation’s capital. After breaking through at Sporting Praia, he moved to Portugal before spending a decade with Fehérvár in Hungary, where he now has citizenship. Named after French footballer Yannick Stopyra, the red-haired right-back has been with the Blue Sharks for nearly two decades. He retired from international football in June 2024 but was convinced to re-join the national team for the World Cup qualifying campaign “Even if he doesn’t play, Stopira is one of the most important players in this team,” the technical director, Rui Costa, told the Guardian. Stopira underlined that importance when he scored the third goal in Cape Verde’s 3-0 win over Eswatini to seal qualification in his hometown.

players.name: ‘Diney’ Borges
players.bio: Despite spending more than a decade in Portugal, at the last World Cup Borges had to cheer against the Lusos, instead supporting his adopted nation Morocco. The aggressive, battling centre-back was enjoying an unexpected spell with Moroccan club ASFAR Rabat where he enjoyed four successful years. Borges has been with Cape Verde for nine years but has only got a proper run in the team in the past 12 months. An ACL injury to star defender Logan Costa thrust Borges into the fray and he took his chance. He struck up a solid partnership with Pico Lopes and only Dailon Livramento scored more goals in qualifying for the Blue Sharks. If Costa returns to start, Borges will consider himself a very unlucky man.

players.name: Roberto ‘Pico’ Lopes
players.bio: Surely a lot of Irish people will be supporting Cape Verde at the World Cup, thanks to the boy from Crumlin, via São Nicolau. Roberto ‘Pico’ Lopes was born and raised in Dublin but was called up to the Blue Sharks for the first time via LinkedIn. “I didn’t understand. I just thought it was a welcome message… so I ignored it,” Pico told CNN in 2024. The Shamrock Rovers defender finally put the Portuguese message through Google Translate nine months later and has not looked back after making his international debut in 2019. The shirt-tucked-in, no-nonsense centre back has become a mainstay at the heart of the Cape Verdean defence ever since, playing in both the 2021 and 2023 Afcons. It’s a remarkable rise for the 34-year-old who a decade ago was working as a mortgage advisor while playing football part-time. Despite not speaking any Portuguese and slowly learning Kriol, Pico is a fan favourite and as one of the federation’s vice presidents told the Guardian, “a future captain” of the Blue Sharks.

players.name: Logan Costa
players.bio: In a team full of talented journeymen, Logan Costa is perhaps Cape Verde’s sole elite player. The French-born centre-back (and cousin to Dailon Livramento) broke through at Toulouse where he scored twice in the final of the club's historic Coupe de France win before moving to Villarreal. The powerful ball-playing defender is the only Cape Verdean playing in one of Europe’s top five leagues and is the nation’s most important player, having formed a solid partnership with Pico Lopes. “He has all the necessary ingredients to become even stronger than he already is,” former Blue Shark and current scout at West Ham, Georges Santos, says. “Villarreal are a very good team, but I could see him play for Barça, Real Madrid and Atlético as he is a quick and intelligent player and can defend.” Suffered an ACL injury during pre-season, only returning to the pitch in Villarreal’s penultimate game of the season. His fitness will likely define whether the Blue Sharks fail or succeed in North America.

players.name: Kevin Lenini Pina
players.bio: Born and raised in Praia, Pina thought any hopes of a football career were over when he emigrated to America. But a chance encounter with former Blue Sharks captain Carlos ‘Caló’ Morais changed everything. “I saw him playing in a street game in Brocton and went to his father to convince him to come back to Cape Verde,” Caló told the Guardian. After six months, Pina failed trials at Casa Pia and Benfica before signing with Chaves. He’s now a Russian league champion with Krasnodar and the midfield engine for Cabo Verde. “I still have the letter from Benfica saying he's not good enough,” laughs Caló.

players.name: Jovane Cabral
players.bio: Born on Santiago island, Cabral moved to Portugal when he was 14 to join Sporting’s academy, where he spent a decade. He was a key cog in Ruben Amorim’s side that ended the club’s 19-year title drought, with the former Manchester United coach describing him as, “perhaps the most important player [at the club] when we first arrived”. His success in Portugal prompted him to turn down a call up to Cape Verde (having only featured in one friendly), instead wanting to try to naturalise as Portuguese and play with Cristiano Ronaldo et al. A series of injuries and subsequent loss of form derailed both his club and international career. The winger eventually returned to the Blue Sharks and heads into the World Cup without a club having ended his time with Estrela Amadora.

players.name: João Paulo Fernandes
players.bio: Born in Cidade Velha on Santiago island, Fernandes’ story is a classic example of the challenges of becoming a professional footballer in Cape Verde. From his electrician father sacrificing hours everyday to ferry him to Praia for training as a kid to failing to move to Portugal initially because of a work permit issue. The left-back who can play in midfield now plies his trade in Romania with FCSB. So popular is João Paulo with the club that when he initially struggled to get a work permit, the club owner and former Member of European Parliament Gigi Becali said, “I’ll just have to intervene, as a parliamentarian, to write an official letter for his work permit."

players.name: Gilson ‘Benchimol’ Tavares
players.bio: Born in Praia, Tavares moved to Portugal as a child, playing for various local clubs in Lisbon as a right-back. It wasn’t until he was signed by Estoril that he was moved up front to play as a forward as a 19-year-old. A strong start to life in Estoril led to links to clubs around Europe, including Nice, Monaco, Milan and Juventus. Eventually he signed for Benfica on a loan before making the unexpected move to Russian side Akron Tolyatti in 2024. Despite the left-field career choice, Benchimol has been a mainstay of Bubista’s side. In March 2022 he became the first and only player to score a hat-trick for the Blue Sharks when he put three past Liechtenstein in a friendly.

players.name: Jamiro Monteiro
players.bio: The creative heartbeat of Cape Verde, Jamiro Monteiro is another of the national team players who is returning “home” to the US having spent five successful seasons split between Philadelphia Union and San Jose Earthquakes. Capable of playing deeper in midfield or as a No 10, Monteiro is combative and a solid ball carrier, but he credits his development as an attacking threat to the man who gave him his professional breakthrough at Dutch club Cambuur, Arne Slot. “He saw that winning balls back was one of my strengths, and I combined that with playing more forward. That’s how I’ve grown as a player,” he told Voetbal International.

players.name: Garry Rodrigues
players.bio: There are more players born in Rotterdam than any other city in the Cape Verde squad and Rodrigues is the elder statesman of the group, becoming the first Dutch-born player to represent the Blue Sharks in 2013. But for the mercurial winger, there was only one nation he ever wanted to represent. “My father was always there for me with my football and it was a dream for him that I would play for the Cape Verdean team,” he told FourFourTwo in 2024. Despite coming through Feyenoord’s prestigious Varkenoord academy he failed to secure a professional contract for almost four years after being released. He played amateur football in The Netherlands while working night shifts in the Dutch postal service. Sixteen years on from that he has enjoyed a career that has taken him to clubs such as Galatasaray, Fenerbahce and Olympiacos while amassing more than 60 caps for Cape Verde.

players.name: Marcio da Rosa
players.bio: The victim of Vozinha’s success and longevity. Despite being a solid goalkeeper ever since he starred in an Under-19s match against Portugal played during a tropical rainstorm, Da Rosa has never had his chance with the national team. He came through at EPIF, one of Cape Verde’s most prestigious football academies but struggled when he made the move to Portugal in 2015. After bouncing around the lower leagues there, he has now made Bulgaria his home with PFC Montana. With the emergence of CJ dos Santos, even once Vozinha retires after the World Cup, da Rosa may find it hard to claim the No 1 shirt he has been heir to for nearly a decade.

players.name: Sidny Lopes Cabral
players.bio: Three years ago, Cabral was playing in the fifth tier of German football. Three months ago he made his Champions League debut against Real Madrid. It has been quite a journey. Born in Rotterdam, at 23 Cabral has already spent time in the Netherlands, Sweden and Germany before breaking out as a star for Portuguese side Estrela Amadora from whom José Mourinho took him from to Benfica in December. While predominantly featuring as a left-back for Cape Verde, he is capable of playing every position on either wing and is completely two-footed. After scoring with both feet as part of a hat-trick earlier this season, when asked which foot he prefers, he shrugged and said “it doesn’t matter”.

players.name: Deroy Duarte
players.bio: Deroy is the younger of the two but is the more successful. He was called up a full two years before Laros and has become a starter alongside Kevin Pina in the heart of the Blue Sharks’ midfield. A powerful holding midfielder, content to sit deep and let Pina and Jamiro Monteiro bomb forward, Duarte is one of the key cogs of Bubista’s side. But despite the younger brother being the better of the two, Deroy sees them as a package deal with the national team. "I actually haven't thought for a second about the possibility that we might not be selected," Deroy told Espn after qualification. "Surely the national coach can't do that? Call up one of us and not the other?"

players.name: Laros Duarte
players.bio: “He’s one of my best friends. Since we were little kids we dreamed of becoming professional footballers, and now we’ve achieved that and we’re facing each other. So it’s definitely special,” Laros Duarte told Sparta Rotterdam’s inhouse media when he faced off against his younger brother Deroy for the first time for PSV Jong and Jong Sparta eight years ago. Now the midfielders are going to the World Cup together. Laros paved the way for his brother at Sparta Rotterdam’s academy, having joined as a five-year-old. The brothers are inseparable, perhaps best exemplified as the pair ended Cape Verde’s World Cup qualification against Eswatini together in the centre of the park.  "This has been a dream since childhood, to experience things like this together. A World Cup with Cape Verde, that is truly indescribable," Laros told NOS.

players.name: Yannick Semedo
players.bio: "It's amazing [seeing him play for Cape Verde],” Leovigildo Ribeiro, the man who discovered Semedo as a child, says. “It was very emotional. I’m like a father to him.” Semedo, who’s real father passed away when he was just a boy, was spotted at a youth game in his hometown Cidade Velha by Ribeiro who brought the ball-winning midfielder to Praia to play for Celtic da Praia (Ribeiro’s family club who were inspired after watching Jock Stein’s Lisbon Lions). Originally a centre-back Semedo now plays as a holding midfielder having enjoyed a career in the lower leagues of Portugal. Now Semedo could be lining up against Rodri, Pedri and co at the World Cup. "It's a dream [seeing Semedo at a World Cup],” says Ribeiro. “I'll be in the US to watch Semedo and the Selecao play."

players.name: Willy Semedo
players.bio: Semedo chose the road less taken. Despite being born and raised in the suburbs of Paris, Semedo’s football opportunities were extremely limited. After failing to break through into professional football in France, he made the bold decision to move to Cyprus to play for fourth division side Alki Oroklini. In four seasons with the club he earned three promotions. After stints around Europe and Saudi Arabia the pacy winger landed back in Cyprus with Omonia Nicosia, whom he powered to a first title in four years. He is arguably Cape Verde’s most in-form player at a club level. He was the Cyprus Sports Writers’ Player of the Year in 2025 and, this season, became the first player to register double digits in goals and assists in the Cyprus first division’s history.

players.name: Telmo Arcanjo
players.bio: One of Cape Verde’s long list of players whose family members also played for the Blue Sharks, Telmo Arcanjo followed in the footsteps of his older brother Fabio. But unlike his brother, Telmo has become a key part of Cape Verde despite suffering an ACL tear in May 2023. He described the recovery period as “14 months of immense pain, sacrifice, and a longing to step onto the pitch again”. The Lisbon-born attacking midfielder helped Vitória de Guimarães to win the Taça da Liga this season, the club’s third ever piece of silverware. In Portugal Arcanjo is best known for a moment in October when as he was coming off the bench against Famalicão but had his phone in his hand. There was an explanation though: he was talking to head coach, Luís Pinto, who was in the stands having been sent off in their previous match.

players.name: Dailon Livramento
players.bio: Some people are destined for greatness and Livramento was one of them. The son of Cape Verdean musical icon Marizia do Rosario, Livramento made his singing debut as a one-year-old when his baby talk was featured in Marizia’s song Daily dedicated to him. The forward himself sings and his brother Jerzy is part of Dutch hip-hop group Broederliefde. Despite only being called up for the first time in 2024 he has already notched seven goals for The Blue Sharks, four of them crucial in qualifying, including both of the goals that sent Cape Verde to the World Cup against Cameroon and Eswatini. “When I scored I pointed to my wrist,” he told the Guardian after the match about his now iconic celebration. “It’s time to go to the World Cup.” In Daily, Marizia describes Livramento as her “everything in the world,” little did she know that he would come to mean just as much to the rest of her tiny island homeland.

players.name: Ryan Mendes
players.bio: The Greatest Of All Time, Ryan Mendes is synonymous with the Blue Sharks. He is the nation’s captain, top scorer, most capped player and at the World Cup could become the first centurion in Cape Verdean history. The right winger was an explosive talent, moving to the famous Le Havre academy where he came through alongside Paul Pogba, Benjamin Mendy and later, Riyad Mahrez. It was actually Mendes who Leicester were looking at when chief scout Steve Walsh visited le Havre in 2012. A member of Le Havre’s official supporters group told the BBC: “Ryan Mendes for sure was the better player. We expected him to reach a higher level than Riyad. Ryan was a fantastic player but he got a bad injury.” That ankle injury set back Mendes’ career at club level, but the 36-year-old has never let down his country and will captain the nation to their first World Cup.

players.name: Nuno da Costa
players.bio: Another of Cape Verde’s late bloomers, Da Costa didn’t turn professional until he was 24. The versatile forward was studying at college while playing for Sporting Aubagne in the fifth tier of French football before signing his first professional contract with Valenciennes. While in France he was at the centre of a storm when beIN Sports pundit and former French international Daniel Bravo, incredibly, described a Da Costa goal for Strasbourg as, “not bad for a black guy.” A year later, in 2020, the forward capable of playing anywhere across Cape Verde’s attack, signed for Nottingham Forest but left 10 months later. He later accused Forest of being a club that, “move players around and replace them like objects”.

players.name: Steven Moreira
players.bio: A former French youth international, for Steven Moreira the World Cup is a home coming. Since signing with Columbus Crew in 2021 he has been one of the most consistent defenders in the MLS. In 2023 he lifted the MLS Cup with the Crew and a season later, he was named Defender of the Year. Cape Verde’s starting right back will have to hope the Blue Sharks don’t come up against Scotland at the World Cup. The 31-year-old was spotted in the crowd when Rangers beat Celtic 3-1, leading to the sacking of Moreira’s former coach Wilfried Nancy. The defender became public enemy No 1 after the match when he defended Nancy saying, “I really think that the MLS is way, way better than the Scottish League. I was very surprised how bad it was. That’s just my opinion.”

players.name: Carlos ‘CJ’ Dos Santos
players.bio: Despite the number of Cape Verdeans living in the US exceeding the number living on the islands, goalkeeper CJ Dos Santos is the only American-born player in the team and if he made his debut at the World Cup would become only the second American-born player to represent the Blue Sharks. Born in Philadelphia, the shot-stopper spent time in Benfica’s academy before joining Inter Miami. Last year he moved to new franchise San Diego FC where he starred in the club’s record-setting first season in MLS. In the playoffs he fractured his nose, forcing him to now wear a face mask earning him the nickname “Batman”. So excited by Dos Santos’ potential were the Blue Sharks that he was called up for their March friendlies before he had even been approved to switch nationality.

players.name: Wagner Pina
players.bio: “Whoever bet on him as a right-sided player made a winning bet," Pina’s former youth coach Marcio Moreira told A Bola after the Lisbon-born player had been turned from a midfielder to a full-back. Moreira went on to boldly claim Pina would make a move to one of Portugal’s giants. But the now Turkish-based full-back had other ideas. Speaking to ZeroZero last year he said: "I want to reach the Premier League and play in an international competition for Cape Verde." With Pina heavily linked with a summer move to Fulham, he’s on his way to fulfilling both in the space of two months.

players.name: Kelvin Pires
players.bio: Pires caused quite a stir in 2019 when he was called up for the Blue Sharks while still playing for Batuque FC in Cape Verde. No Cape Verdean had represented the national team while playing domestically for over six years. A year later, the centre-back traded the beaches of São Vicente for the mountain valleys of Slovakia where he spent four years before moving to Finland where he now plays for Seinäjoen Jalkapallokerho. Ironically, Pires’ only goal for Cape Verde came against Finland in March when the two sides met in the Fifa Series.

players.name: Helio Varela
players.bio: It’s not the first time Varela has been called up for an international tournament with Cape Verde, but he’ll be hoping this one goes differently than Afcon 2023. Having made his debut with the Blue Sharks just a few months before the Afcon he was included in the squad but eventually refused the travel, deciding to stay with his club Portimonense. The decision made Varela and the club public enemies with Cape Verde’s football federation president, Mario Semedo, who called the club and head coach, Paulo Sérgio, “petty” for pressuring Varela to not go to Afcon. “They put enormous pressure on him not to come to the national team,” he said. Thankfully for the right-footed left winger, everything seems to have been forgiven.

players.name: David Ospina
players.bio: Understudy to Vargas after the best part of a decade as No 1. When he was little, during trips to the supermarket, Ospina would abandon his parents in search of the footballs. To begin with, he didn’t want to be a goalkeeper. He had all the makings of a striker: he could shoot with both feet and had an eye for goal. However, when the keeper in the Alexis García academy team didn’t turn up one day, Ospina put his hand up and went between the sticks. At 18, he was winning league titles as Atlético Nacional’s first-choice keeper. His international debut came almost by accident when No 1 Miguel Calero was sent off in a match against Uruguay and Ospina was thrown on in his place. Over 125 caps later, the 38-year-old arrives at his third World Cup. He has two FA Cups with Arsenal, playing the 2017 final against Chelsea, and a Coppa Italia with Napoli to his name.

players.name: Daniel Muñoz
players.bio: Born in Amalfi, home of some of the biggest gold mines in Colombia, Muñoz was a late bloomer. He was a massive fan of Atlético Nacional and often went to watch their games. At 18, having been rejected by various clubs, Muñoz turned his back on football. He wanted to go to the United States to find a job but he couldn’t get a visa. Finally, the Total Soccer team opened their doors to him. “It wasn’t easy to come into a place and play with kids younger than you and knowing that some of your teammates were already professional,” Muñoz said. At the age of 21, he went pro with Águilas Doradas in Rionegro before attracting the interest of the club of his dreams, Atlético Nacional. From there he left for Genk in Belgium and was a major part of the Crystal Palace side that won the club’s first major trophy, the FA Cup, last year.

players.name: Jhon Lucumí
players.bio: Destined to become a footballer. The day he was born, Colombia were knocked out of the 1998 World Cup by England, bringing an end to the Carlos Valderrama generation. It was at the club that Valderrama left for Europe, Deportivo Cali, where Lucumí started out. Scout Michel Arango saw him playing on a pitch in Cali’s San Luisito neighbourhood: he was only 10, playing as a striker, but stood out because of his height. Arango suggested he would be better off as a defender. He was playing for Cali’s first team at 17, making his debut as a left-back because the coach, Fernando Castro, was without one. Coached by the former Colombia centre-back Mario Yepes, Lucumí secured a move to Europe with Genk and is part of the Bologna team that reached Champions League proper and won last year’s Coppa Italia.

players.name: Santiago Arias
players.bio: The former PSV and Atlético Madrid full-back almost chose to pursue a different sport to football. “At school we had to do a sporting activity. I was ready to pick taekwondo because I really liked the karate films, but I chose football in the end,” Arias once told Bocas. It was his father, Raúl, who persuaded him. “He was a big football fan,” Arias said. “He was an Atlético Nacional supporter. We would go to the ground together. Everything was about football with him, 24/7. It’s thanks to him that I am where I am.” It wasn’t at Nacional where he started his career but La Equidad, via the Alexis García academy. García was manager of the first team and handed Arias his debut. Raúl never saw his son play for Colombia – he was tragically shot dead in 2009 when shielding children from armed robbers outside a school.

players.name: Kevin Castaño
players.bio: Castaño’s parents split up shortly after he was born and his mother left their home town, Itagüí, for work. His father, Richard, raised him alongside his grandmother, María Noelia. Richard was also Kevin’s first coach – he was hired as a youth coach by Itagüí Ditaires (now Águilas Doradas) in 2008. “Kevin was football mad,” Richard told El Tiempo. “He played local tournaments and caught the eye. I put him up front and he always scored goals. He had a good strike on him and was a great leader.” When Kevin made his first-team debut for Águilas in 2020, his father was in surgery after being admitted to hospital with Covid. After his operation the first thing Richard asked for was to watch the game back. Castaño received his first international call-up in 2023 and, after brief spells in Mexico and Russia, 25-year-old is now at River Plate.

players.name: Richard Ríos
players.bio: A key cog in Néstor Lorenzo’s midfield, he once dreamed of being a futsal star but fate had other ideas. Started playing football on the streets of Vegachí, in the Antioquía region not too far from Medellín. The region is known for its gold mines but also for producing sugar cane and sweet treats containing guava fruit. “Football was a good alternative to heading down the wrong path,” Ríos once said. He caught the eye playing for Colombia at an Under-18 South American futsal championship in Brazil and was given a trial at Flamengo. “Leaving your country is not easy for anyone but I was motivated by that. Those of us from outside Brazil see it as the country of football,” he said. That mixture of Antioquían grit and Brazilian magic helped him recover from a serious knee injury while playing on loan in Mexico and go on to land a move to Benfica after impressing at the Club World Cup last summer.

players.name: Luis Díaz
players.bio: Anyone who watches the strong and combative Díaz play for Bayern would not think that he was once placed on a special diet in order to put on 10kg. That was at Barranquilla FC, a feeder club for Atlético Junior, where he came recommended by the legendary Carlos Valderrama. ‘El Pibe’ spotted him playing for Colombia at a Copa América for indigenous communities in 2015, where John Jairo Díaz was the coach. “Valderrama was practically my boss,” the coach said. “He would say: ‘That skinny kid is going to play, he’ll make it as a pro’. And look what happened.” Díaz’s father, Mane, was another key person in his career. Mane’s kidnapping by armed guerrillas on the Colombian-Venezuelan border, and safe return, made headline news around the world in 2023 when Díaz was playing for Liverpool, where he won the Premier League, an FA Cup and two League Cups.

players.name: Jorge Carrascal
players.bio: In just eight months at Flamengo, Carrascal has won the Copa Libertadores, Brazil’s Serie A and the Carioca state championship. However, his career was almost over before it began. When he turned 18 he moved to Sevilla, who signed him despite a serious knee injury. He had surgery and was able to return for Sevilla’s B team but then his knee went again. During the process of a second operation he got a bacterial infection and was in danger of having his leg amputated. Fortunately he managed to recover. After his debut in Colombian football his Millonarios coach, Ricardo Lunari, compared him to Juan Román Riquelme. When he signed for River Plate, they called him the new Neymar. Carrascal didn’t like the comparisons. “Every player has their own career and wants to make a name for themself,” he told Olé. “Let’s hope one day a player will be nicknamed Carrascal. You never know!”

players.name: Jhon Córdoba
players.bio: There are a few father-and-son duos that have played for Colombia – but only one where both have found the net for the selección. Córdoba inherited his eye for goal from his father, Manuel Acisclo, who scored three times in 15 appearances in the 1980s. ‘El Triciclo’ (the tricycle), as he was known, was not just his mentor and biggest supporter but also a critical voice. “Jhon is a very strong person,” Manuel told Caracol Deportes. “I don’t like to talk to him before games. We have a chat afterwards but I tend to steer clear of football. The next day, when he’s calmed down, we’ll talk. If he’s made a mistake, I tell him: ‘You did this badly and this is why.’” Manuel is very demanding and used to keep tallies. “I scored 153 goals. He’s got a long way to go,” he would say. After spells in Mexico, Spain and Germany, 32-year-old Jhon has already passed that figure.

players.name: James Rodríguez
players.bio: Not the first member of his family to play at the World Cup. His father was part of the Colombia squad at the Under-20 edition in 1985. After Wilson James and Pilar Rubio split up, it was Rodríguez’s stepfather, Juan Carlos Restrepo, who encouraged him to follow his dream. He came through at Envigado, one of the best academies in Colombia. “Juan Carlos is an obsessive; he took it upon himself to make James a star. He deserves a lot of credit,” said Silvio Sandri, Rodríguez’s first agent, in 2014, the year he won the Golden Boot at the World Cup in Brazil and sealed a blockbuster move to Real Madrid. The two-time Champions League winner is now playing in MLS at 35 after some globetrotting that took in Merseyside, Qatar, Greece, Brazil and Mexico. This will be his third World Cup.

players.name: Jhon Arias
players.bio: Quibdó is, perhaps, the poorest capital city of Colombia’s 33 departamentos and that is where Arias’s journey towards professional football began. It wasn’t easy – and he had to move abroad to get a chance to shine. “I’m from a beautiful place, where there aren’t many opportunities; that’s where this kid with dreams was born,” he told El Colombiano. “All those experiences and difficulties that I had as a kid have given me the certainty that all that effort was worth it.” From Quibdó, Arias went to Cali and then to youth sides in Mexico. After being rejected by Tijuana he went through four clubs back in Colombia before his performances for Santa Fe in the 2021 Copa Libertadores earned him a move to Fluminense, where he won the continental title in 2023. After a spell in the Premier League with Wolves, the 28-year-old is back in Brazil with Palmeiras.

players.name: Camilo Vargas
players.bio: Knows a thing or two about ending curses, having done so at two of South America’s most traditional clubs. In 2012 he helped his first team, Santa Fe, win their first league title since 1975. And that had a lot to do with his mother-in-law, Sandra Merino. She didn’t like football but started to take an interest when her daughter, Ángela, got together with Vargas. A devout follower of Christ, Sandra told the Santa Fe directors that the team was being affected by witchcraft. Despite initial scepticism the club invited Sandra to meet the team and she cleaned their feet. After that the players took to the field barefoot before matches. It worked. When playing in Mexico in 2021, Vargas helped Atlas win their first title in 70 years. The shy goalkeeper rarely has much to say in front of the media and has the patience of a saint: he only replaced David Ospina as Colombia’s No 1 in his late 30s.

players.name: Yerry Mina
players.bio: Despite his lankiness as a youngster, Mina wasn’t very good at heading the ball – something he would make up for later in life. He had trials at Deportivo Cali but didn’t make it. He also played for América de Cali, when he used to hitch a ride on lorries to save money on bus fares. He ended up at Deportivo Pasto where two of his teammates, Marlon Piedrahíta and Camilo Pérez, started to urge him to make the most of his lofty stature. By the time he signed for Santa Fe he was a major aerial presence. He played a key role in the Copa Sudamericana triumph in 2015 and earned a move to Palmeiras before a big break at Barcelona. He was Colombia’s top scorer at the 2018 World Cup, each of his three goals coming with his head, including a late equaliser against England in the last 16. After a five-year spell at Everton, 31-year-old has become a favourite in Sardinia.

players.name: Gustavo Puerta
players.bio: There are five professional football clubs in the departamento of Valle where Puerta was born, but he couldn’t force his way into any of them – despite impressing at football schools in the area. He sought his fortune in the capital with second-tier Bogotá FC, from where he made the massive jump to Europe with Bayer Leverkusen. Xabi Alonso was there waiting for him. “It’s a privilege to be around people of his ilk and this level of players. He was one of the best in his position. He has taught me a lot – he helps you explore your potential,” Puerta said. After a spell in the Championship with Hull, he has found a home in northern Spain with Racing, this season helping them reach La Liga for the first time in 14 years. The former Colombia Under-20 captain scored on his first senior cap against New Zealand last November.

players.name: Juan Portilla
players.bio: Portilla’s first steps in football were adventurous. His father, an electrician by profession, signed him up to a small football school in Cali but couldn’t afford to keep him there. Shortly after, one of his father’s friends recommended him to Boca Juniors de Cali. His senior debut came for Universitario de Popayán, owned by the same people as Boca, but Portilla wanted to expand his horizons. He tried out for a third-tier side in Jacksonville in the United States and then again for a team in Chile’s second division, Melipilla. His love for his family brought him back to Colombia, where he eventually broke through at América, the local rivals of the team he supported as a boy, Deportivo Cali. After a spell at Talleres in Argentina, he moved to Brazil last January.

players.name: Jefferson Lerma
players.bio: It took the Crystal Palace midfielder a long time to gain the trust of his coaches as a youngster. After being rejected by three clubs, he ended up at Atlético Huila. Fed up at his lack of opportunities, Lerma went back to his home town, El Cerrito, across the other side of Andes. When Álvaro de Jesús Gómez came in as coach of Huila in 2013, he asked which of the Under-20s could make the step up to the first team. When he was told about Lerma, he went in search of him and told him to come back, even paying his travel expenses. Soon he was a starter for Huila, two years later he was in Europe and, in 2016, he played for Colombia at the Olympics. His inclusion in the 2018 World Cup squad was controversial because he had not played a part in qualifying. Lerma almost quit the selección in face of the criticism but he was convinced to stay on. He earned a move to Bournemouth that summer and is now an integral part of the national squad. Won the FA Cup with Palace in 2024-25.

players.name: Johan Mojica
players.bio: Has played in Europe for almost 12 years now and his physical attributes have allowed him to thrive in Spain and Italy. He wasn’t always blessed with such a physique. His first club, Academia Compensar, in Bogotá doubted that he could make it in the game. “When Johan arrived, he was underweight for his age … we couldn’t see how that boy was going to improve,” Jorge Caracol Serna, a coach at Academia and future manager of Colombia’s Under-15s and under-17s, told Gol. “But his technique and ability to read the play were so good that we gave him some time.” They made him a long-term plan to help him gain weight and gave him a monthly alimentary supplement. “The only thing that grew was his hair,” joked Serna. Now 33, Mojica has played for a host of clubs in La Liga as well as Atalanta in Serie A.

players.name: Willer Ditta
players.bio: In qualifying Ditta played only two games, starting once, and watched another three from the bench. He felt he needed to be doing more to earn a spot on the plane to North America, so he decided to train in his own time. “It’s for me to keep improving on a personal and sporting level. We decided that we would work hard in these two months before the World Cup to improve in a physical and technical aspect,” he told journalist Adrián Esparza Oteo. Born in Ibirico, Ditta grew up in Barranquilla in the north before moving to La Jagua de Ibirico with his mother. There weren’t many opportunities to play football, he was rejected by the regional side and ended up playing “for fizzy drinks” outside Valledupar’s stadium. Pablo Zuleta, the coach who had initially discarded him, saw him playing in a school tournament and realised his mistake. After a spell in Argentina, he has established himself in Mexico.

players.name: Juan Camilo ‘Cucho’ Hernández
players.bio: Earned the nickname ‘Cucho’ by chance. He once turned up to a game in his home town Pereira with his head shaved and his friends starting calling him ‘Cuchu’ after former Inter and Real Madrid midfielder Esteban Cambiasso. That eventually turned into ‘Cucho’. Football is in his blood: his parents, Néstor and Yanet, met while playing a tournament and fell in love straight away. From a young age he joined his parents at tournaments and started to play himself, eventually turning out for the Risaralda regional team. That was where Hernán Lisi, the coach of Deportivo Pereira, spotted him. “I want that player!” he shouted when he saw him play. After scoring twice on his debut for Colombia, the 26-year-old has struggled to establish himself.

players.name: Juan Quintero
