players.bio: Only two players have scored goals for Colombia in more than one World Cup. One is Juan Cuadrado and the other is ‘Juanfer’, who, having scored against Ivory Coast in 2014, snuck a free-kick under the wall against Japan four years later. Quintero is known for coming up with the spectacular. He won goal of the tournament for his strike against El Salvador at the 2013 Under-20 World Cup and an extra-time stunner helped deliver River Plate the 2018 Copa Libertadores title against their superclásico rivals Boca Juniors. But away from football, Quintero has had to live with personal tragedy. His father went missing in 1993, when he was two years old. “I have the right to know what happened to my father and that is what I want to know because I’ve suffered and I’ve seen my family go through it,” Quintero said in 2020, when Eduardo Zapateiro, who was investigated as part of his father’s case (the pair had an argument shortly before Quintero’s father’s disappearance), was named commander general of the Colombian army.

players.name: Jaminton Campaz
players.bio: Cheated death to become a footballer. As a kid in Chontal near the southern border with Ecuador, he made money from fishing and, one day, was bitten by a snake. One of his colleagues squeezed the wound and stopped things from getting worse. Campaz comes from a family of footballers. His mother, Betzabet, was a goalkeeper; his brother, Mike, played for Equatorial Guinea and his cousin, Carlos Darwin Quintero, played for Colombia in 2014 World Cup qualifying and had a long career in Mexico and MLS – he still plays for Millonarios. Campaz fought for a career in his own right, trying out at Deportivo Pasto, but his mother forgot to sign a document and he was forced to leave. He also failed a trial at América de Cali. It was Mike who took him to Tolima, where he started his journey as a pro.

players.name: Deiver Machado
players.bio: Machado has come a long way since taking his first steps in football on a 14-metre-long pitch built by the local community in the small town of Tadó in Colombia’s Chocó departamento. His family were displaced by violence and went to live in Santa Cecilia, over the border in the Risaralda departamento, and that’s where a nine-year-old Machado had try-outs at Deportivo Pereira. Machado had to wait to make his first-team debut after the club’s future was plunged into doubt by relegation. One of his brothers suggested he go to Medellín to have a trial at Atlético Nacional. The 32-year-old has found a home in France, where he has played for Toulouse, Lens and now Nantes. At the start of May, he said his “main aim” was to go to the World Cup. By the end of the month, he was in the squad.

players.name: Davinson Sánchez
players.bio: It can be tricky for South American players who are not from Brazil or Argentina to seal a move to Europe, let alone be greeted with great fanfare. “Who the hell is Davinson Sánchez?” asked one Tottenham fan online when the defender signed from Ajax in 2017. By then he had already won a Copa Libertadores medal with Atlético Nacional and enjoyed a bright start in the Eredivisie. He was Spurs’ record signing at £42m but had to win over his doubters before becoming a key part of the team that reached the 2019 Champions League final. But what if he hadn’t become a footballer? “I’m good at maths, so I would have studied something to do with numbers. I don’t know, something like engineering or administration,” he told Bocas magazine in 2018, the year he made his World Cup debut. Now a two-time Turkish Super Lig champion with Galatasaray.

players.name: Álvaro Montero
players.bio: If anything marks out Montero it’s his enormous self-confidence. When he arrived at Vélez Sarsfield last year, he said: “I’m a goalkeeper who is very difficult to score against – that’s my best quality. After that, we have ups and downs, strengths and weaknesses.” Montero tried his luck abroad with São Caetano in Brazil and San Lorenzo in Argentina but barely played. It wasn’t until he was back in Colombia with Tolima that he started to gain confidence. They had only won one league title in 63 years but with Montero in goal they won two in four years, shocking Atlético Nacional and Millonarios in second legs away from home. After the 2021 final Montero celebrated by giving the referee a big but unrequited hug from behind, pulling up his shorts. When the Tolima coach, Alberto Gamero, went to Millonarios, Montero told him: “Boss, if you want to win trophies there, you’ll have to take me with you.” He did and they won three of them.

players.name: Luis Suárez
players.bio: Not that one. However, the former Granada striker has had an outstanding first season in Portugal and some of Europe’s biggest clubs are looking at him. But this time last year the picture was very different. Yes, he was scoring goals in Spain’s second tier with Almería, but he wasn’t really in the frame for the national team. What’s more, he was coming off the back of an injury that had affected his mental health. “I went through depression, which I’m still coming out of bit by bit with some work and help from psychologists and people close to me. I’d hit rock bottom,” he told Marca. The support of his partner, Carolina, has been key. After scoring almost a goal a game in the Primeira Liga this season, the 28-year-old is now a permanent fixture for Colombia.

players.name: Carlos Andrés Gómez
players.bio: Has picked up his fair share of nicknames during his short career. When he first came through at Millonarios they called him ‘Diablito’ (the little devil) because of his mischievous style of play. He’s also been called ‘Yanfri’ after a kid that went viral on social media who apparently looked like him. One nickname he doesn’t like is ‘Tinito’ – his style of play is similar to Tino Asprilla – because Asprilla was an Atlético Nacional man, Millonarios’s big rivals. Gómez had a tough start to life. “As I kid, I grew up watching some of my friends get killed and saw others follow the wrong path. But my whole family has always been into football,” he wrote in Chocó 7 Días. He also admitted that he himself had almost gone down the wrong path: he tried to rob someone once but got caught. The 24-year-old has two passions: motorbikes and dancing. “Wherever I go, I always do my neighbourhood's popular dance. It’s a way of expressing where I come from,” he said.

players.name: Dominik Livakovic
players.bio: Not even the most famous footballer in his (wider) family; through his mother Manuela he is related to Josip Skoblar, the 1971 European Golden Shoe winner, who scored 44 goals for Marseille that season. As Dominik, who also played basketball as a kid and had enrolled at a university to study international relations, was making his way in senior football, his father Zdravko — a politician and state secretary at the time — spent five months in detention for his alleged involvement in a big corruption case. In 2022, Livakovic became only the third goalkeeper in World Cup history to save three penalties in a single shoot-out; then he also saved Rodrygo’s shot in another shootout against Brazil in the quarter-finals.

players.name: Josip Stanisic
players.bio: The 26-year-old Munich-born versatile defender has won the league every season in his professional career since his debut in April 2021 and, while that may not sound so outlandish given that he has always been under contract with Bayern, it is also worth noting that he did it in 2024 as well, when he was on loan to Bayer Leverkusen under Xabi Alonso. Stanisic played for Germany Under-19’s but switched to Croatia and still very vividly remembers his first call-up. “We were in the car, the whole family, and the call was on speakers,” he recalls. “I laughed, couldn’t believe it, but then I saw my sister cry. My dad also cried, my mum cried too. It was one of the most beautiful moments in my life.”

players.name: Marin Pongracic
players.bio: “Football was not my only priority,” the Germany-born defender, now 28, recently said in an interview. “I should have done things differently — eat better, go to bed on time, do some additional training in the gym… What I was missing the most was a fully professional approach and discipline,” he explained, talking about his earlier years and saying he has learned from his past mistakes. Pongracic had been going through a rough time in the Bundesliga and spoke publicly about his struggles, especially with Mark van Bommel, his coach at Wolfsburg. “I was going mad and wanted to have a go at Van Bommel. But that’s not how things are done, you have to sit with him and talk like a politician,” he said. Eventually he decided to move to Italy and he is now an important player for Fiorentina. With the national team he is still considered a bit of an outsider.

players.name: Josko Gvardiol
players.bio: He is now one of the best defenders in the world, but at 16, Gvardiol wasn’t happy about how his development was going and even considered quitting football altogether and joining his father, Tihomir, in selling fish at the Dolac marketplace in Zagreb. In the end he took a mental map that a family friend made for him and hung it on the wall of his room. “SUCCESS,” it said in capital letters, while three other words were highlighted: discipline, top club and plan A. The Little Pep, as he’s been nicknamed from his very early days, has achieved it all, while remaining a remarkably down-to-earth guy who never lets fame get to his head. But what if things hadn’t worked out as a footballer, what would he have done instead? “Make pastries,” he quickly said when asked that question.

players.name: Duje Caleta-Car
players.bio: Just in case you are wondering, “car” doesn’t mean automobile in Croatian; it actually means “emperor”. Having left home at 16 to join Red Bull Salzburg academy, he was the youngest member of the 2018 World Cup silver-winning squad and developed into a strong, tough defender who is also confident on the ball. He is, however, prone to occasional blunders. Recently he conceded a penalty that media in Spain called “the most surreal in a 100 years”. Playing for Real Sociedad against Alavés in the Cup, he stopped the opposing forward Toni Martinez by pulling his shirt over his head, leaving the player to spin disoriented in the box. Alavés, however, missed the spot kick. Caleta-Car’s wife, Adriana, a former model and a law graduate, is a popular social media influencer.

players.name: Josip Sutalo
players.bio: Only 14 years old when he left his Metkovic home to move 320 miles up north and join the Dinamo Zagreb youth academy, he is an exciting and quick ball-playing centre-back who is calm and highly professional on and off the pitch. For the national team he has been crucial in transforming the previously ageing Croatia backline. At the 2022 World Cup, the veteran Dejan Lovren was chosen ahead of him, but Sutalo has now amassed more than 30 caps, hardly ever disappointing. His move to Ajax in 2023 did not get off to the best start as he was among the players criticised for the team’s poor form but he has now made more than 100 appearances for the famous club and seem to have cemented his starting place in Zlatko Dalic’s side as well.

players.name: Nikola Moro
players.bio: The defensive midfielder, who switched from Hajduk to rival Dinamo’s youth ranks at the age of 11, has an unusual and a bit old-fashioned hobby for a footballer: “I love enigmatics and particularly enjoy solving crossword puzzles,” he says. Despite representing - and usually captaining - Croatia at every under-age level, as well as being one of the best players in the domestic league for years, it took a long time for him to break into the senior national team. He got his chance in a 2022 friendly against Bulgaria when he was 24 and another one in late 2024. Strangely, he is still on the margins despite being a regular for high-flying Bologna for years.

players.name: Mateo Kovacic
players.bio: “My game is to open space for others,” Kovacic once said, adding that it is “a big strength” for him. Often in Luka Modric’s shadow and not always fully appreciated for his contribution, Kovacic’s value is best noticed at times of his absence, so fans in Croatia are hopeful he’ll make a complete recovery from injury in time for the World Cup and be at his best once again. A devout Catholic, sometimes referred to as the “Altar Boy” - because, well, he used to be one and met his future wife while she was in the choir. He is a serial winner and he has collected four Champions League titles (three with Real Madrid, one with Chelsea) despite only playing in one final, and that was for 10 minutes.

players.name: Andrej Kramaric
players.bio: For 16 years Kramaric took the same tram from his Zagreb home to the Maksimir stadium, where he trained with Dinamo, having started at the age of six. He was obsessed with goals and, by his own count, scored 452 of them in official matches for various age categories. And yet his career only really took off when he moved to Rijeka and particularly after embracing a more withdrawn role, thriving between the lines. Kramaric is a forgotten member of Leicester’s 2016 Premier League winning side, having been loaned out to Hoffenheim halfway through that season. A decade later, he is still there, and now also one of Croatia senators, having scored 36 goals in his 114 appearances at the time of writing, looking for his World Cup swan song.

players.name: Luka Modric
players.bio: Two decades with the national team the once-generational player is closing in on 200 caps - an obscene number. The captain has still got it, at the age of 40, and has been a key player for Milan this season. The national team coach, Zlatko Dalic, calls him “the engine of this team”. Also the most-decorated player in Real Madrid’s history, having won 28 trophies despite only joining them shortly before his 27th birthday. Modric really is larger-than-life and his personality is complex and layered, from the war child-come-Ballon d’Or story to his legal troubles. “What people don’t realise about Luka,” said the late Tomislav Basic, one of Modric’s early coaches whom the player called his “second father”, “is that he is largely self-taught. For many days and months, this under-nourished, small kid played with the ball by himself in a parking lot by a refugee hotel, banging it against the wall and then controlling it.” This was where his trademark moves, like the outside-of-the-foot trivela or the half-turn when receiving the ball, came to be.

players.name: Ante Budimir
players.bio: A true late bloomer who became an Osasuna legend despite only joining them at age 29 -his Croatia debut came a few months later - Budimir has sometimes been described as a “poor man’s Mario Mandzukic” mainly for his hard work off the ball and strong presence in the air. A war refugee from Bosnia, he is also a really nice guy and a university student of economics and business who lists Roberto Benigni’s La vita è bella as his favourite film. Soon 35, he may finally be Croatia’s first choice up front. Oh, yes - he’s also the protagonist of one of the worst penalties ever taken in football, the bizarre effort against Valencia that caused mass facepalming on the home bench and in the crowd. Just google it.

players.name: Ivor Pandur
players.bio: Most fans in Croatia will only vaguely remember the goalie whose surname is one of the slang terms for a policeman; he left his native Rijeka after just one season in senior football and has since been playing abroad — for Verona, Fortuna Sittard and now Hull City. And although he was part of every national youth squad category from Under-15s to Under-21s, at the time of writing he is yet to make his debut for the senior Croatia team. Pandur is a yoga practitioner and a religious person, and his views on the Championship, the league where he currently plays, are rather interesting. “It’s brutal, a bit like rugby, it makes you tougher”, he says. “In every game you get punched with either an elbow or a knee. As long as I’m healthy, I’m enjoying it.”

players.name: Nikola Vlasic
players.bio: Described as “a motor monster” by his own dad, Josko, a former decathlete, Nikola was a well-known talent in his country years before 2014, when he was included in the Guardian’s inaugural Next Generation list. His older sister, Blanka, was a world-conquering athlete in high jump and Josko, a renowned fitness coach, worked with Nikola from the age of four. “We played everywhere,” Vlasic Sr said. “Even in the living room or in the elevator… I’d never let him lose. Once I accidentally beat him in a game and he cried a river of tears.” Failed to make his mark in England on two separate occasions - with Everton and West Ham - but is an important player for Torino and has scored some crucial goals for Croatia over his 60 appearances.

players.name: Ivan Perisic
players.bio: While it’s true that Croatia have been Luka Modric’s team for nearly 20 years, Perisic has arguably been just as important for them for most of that time. Now 37, he has had a brilliant season for PSV, joining them after fulfilling his dream of playing for his childhood club, Hajduk Split, whom he had to leave at age 17 in order to save his father’s chicken farm from going bust. Perisic played in Belgium, Germany, Italy, England and is now in the Netherlands, ever evolving as a player, even after a bad cruciate ligament injury that threatened to end his career. This is surely his last big tournament and he is still at full strength.

players.name: Mario Pasalic
players.bio: At the age of 17 Pasalic had the world at his feet. He was considered a top talent a move abroad only seemed a matter of time. Then, suddenly, his upward trajectory was stopped by a staph infection and he had to spend the next six months in hospital. “At times I thought I wouldn’t be able to continue playing,” he said. A year later, however, he scored both goals for Hajduk in a derby win against Dinamo and was signed by Chelsea the following summer. After five loan spells and not a single appearance for the Blues, he found a home at Atalanta, where he is now a legend after eight years at the club. Pasalic is often Croatia’s ‘joker’ card, filling in wherever Zlatko Dalic needs him.

players.name: Martin Baturina
players.bio: He trained gymnastics for seven years before deciding to focus on football. Once a top talent in the Hajduk Split academy before his dad, the Croatian league icon Mate Baturina, controversially decided to move both him and his older brother, Roko, who now plays in Denmark, to bitter rivals Dinamo. “I was just a kid and it wasn’t my call,” he says. Wonderfully gifted but never consistent enough and perhaps not appreciating the importance of tracking back, Baturina used to be seen as a bit of an old-school advanced playmaker - a classic Balkans No 10, if you will - who might not have what it takes to succeed in today’s football. However, he has matured playing under Cesc Fàbregas this season at Como and has also proven his value for Croatia.

players.name: Petar Sucic
players.bio: “I prefer the real life,” the 22-year-old Inter midfielder recently said when asked why he doesn’t use any social media. Growing up on a farm, he said he always loved being outdoors. “I had a very happy childhood and liked that lifestyle. I’d help my family milk cows and that was fun.” When Sucic made his debut for Dinamo less than three years ago, he was a little known outsider who played for Bosnia and Herzegovina’s youth teams and no one expected much from him. But within a year he had not only established himself in the first team but also switched to representing Croatia and was called up for the senior squad. At the end of the following season, he was sold to Inter and is now a starter for both club and country, where he is seen as a successor to the iron-lunged Marcelo Brozovic.

players.name: Kristijan Jakic
players.bio: You could argue that Jakic is Croatia’s footballing Peter Pan, an instantly likeable character who never really grew up. He collects Pokemon stickers, Hot Wheels toy cars and famous players’ shirts, while his many tattoos include tributes to his grandparents as well as roses, tears, a gladiator and a tiger. He says he never liked school and enjoyed farming the land with his grandad much more: “I was 14 when I first drove a tractor. I enjoyed making wine and planting potatoes. I still enjoy that even now when I visit my family,” he says. Now at Augsburg in the Bundesliga, he was Croatia’s unlikely hero in the qualifiers, playing right-back because of an injury crisis. His natural home is in defensive midfield.

players.name: Toni Fruk
players.bio: The only player still in the domestic league but that is not to say that he doesn’t have experience from playing abroad, having first joined Mouscron in Belgium at age 17 and then Fiorentina’s Primavera side. The talent was never in question, but his development had been hampered by a “mysterious” injury that caused him severe joint pain, while the club doctors insisted it was “all in his head”. He took a step back and returned to Croatia, eventually becoming the top flight’s star, making it to the national team and is now waiting for another move abroad. A creative attacking midfielder or a false nine who could be Croatia’s secret weapon at the World Cup, he started his career as a goalkeeper.

players.name: Igor Matanovic
players.bio: Croats, war refugees who had nothing and didn’t even speak the language.” He recalls a visit to the bombed and abandoned house where his mother’s family used to live. “Among the ruins, there was still an album with photos of Michael Jackson that she collected. They were just teenagers and suddenly they needed to start a whole new life.” Born in Hamburg, Matanovic opted to play for Croatia and is still making a name for himself both in the Bundesliga and with the national team. In his hometown, he owns an Oakberry franchise that sells acai bowls and smoothies, which has its benefits: “I love that feeling when you just go to the counter, take what you want and don’t have to pay for it.”

players.name: Luka Sucic
players.bio: The jury is still out on the Real Sociedad midfielder, with the main question being whether he can become Croatia’s new creative leader once Modric and Kovacic retire. Now 23, he has had more than his share of ups and downs, at times looking like a true footballing sensation while in others he has struggled with injuries and managerial changes. He definitely has something special and has shown it on many occasions but now he needs to be more consistent to truly progress. The midfielder is actually a cousin to Petar Sucic, although neither of them were born in Croatia - Luka grew up in Austria but played for Croatia as early as the Under-15s and does not even hold Austrian citizenship.

players.name: Luka Vuskovic
players.bio: Two days after his 16th birthday, he made his senior debut for Hajduk Split in the derby against Dinamo; a week later, he scored his first goal. He has always been effortlessly dominant among older players that his Croatia debut at age 18 already seemed overdue. A true force not only in defence but also in build-up play and in attack, he scored seven goals in Belgium last season and five for HSV since last summer; among them the 2025 Bundesliga Goal of the Year, a spectacular scorpion kick finish. Coming from a multi-generational football family — his brother Mario is also a HSV player — Vuskovic is now finally ready to introduce himself at the big stage and show why everyone thinks he’ll be a superstar for many years to come. Tottenham signed him for a bargain £10m in 2025 and surely they won’t loan him out again?

players.name: Dominik Kotarski
players.bio: “I have no regrets,” says the 26-year-old who has had a somewhat peculiar career path. “Things that didn’t happen are not worth thinking or talking about.” A product of Dinamo Zagreb youth academy, where he came from a lower league side Tondach Bedekovcina, he refused to sign a professional deal, instead moving to Ajax before turning 18. Four years later, he was back in Croatia at HNK Gorica, without ever appearing for Dinamo, Ajax or any other club (he did play for Jong Ajax, the Dutch team’s reserve side). A really good season there earned him a move to PAOK in Greece and then, amid rumours of interest from various big European clubs, he chose FC Copenhagen as his next destination.

players.name: Marco Pasalic
players.bio: After graduating from high school Pasalic’s dreams of becoming a professional footballer still looked a bit distant. So after training at his hometown club, Karlsruhe, he would work at construction sites for his brother’s company, installing flooring or completing interior designs, sometimes even on graveyard shifts until 3am or 4am. He was also a hardcore supporter of the Vatreni who travelled across the continent to see them play: “To the other workers I’d say, ‘One day I will play for the Croatian national team,” he recalls. Spells with Stuttgart and Dortmund’s reserve sides followed and Pasalic was already 21 years old when he made his “proper” senior debut. But what a debut it was, for Dortmund in the DFL-Supercup Klassiker against Bayern (he only played 12 minutes but still). After just one further appearance, he joined Rijeka in 2023 and was called up for Croatia within three months before being sold to Orlando last year.

players.name: Martin Erlic
players.bio: Only a few days after playing in the 2023 Nations League final against Spain, Erlic was in the field helping out at his family farm. “When I was a child, we lived under the same roof with farm animals, only a wall separated us,” he once said in a revealing interview. “There were hard times and we collected bottles to sell them… no shame in that though,” he added. Erlic left his country for Italy at the age of 16 and struggled with many club changes, loans and injuries. When he made his senior international debut in June 2022, the call-up was so unexpected that he had to miss his brother’s wedding in order to play. “I felt sorry,” he says. “But this is what all the training and the sacrifices I made have come down to.”

players.name: Petar Musa
players.bio: The Moose, as they like to call him there, is pretty big in Texas these days. He’s known for his scoring and a goal celebration that includes putting his open palms with fingers spread to the side of his head, imitating antlers. In Croatia, though, it took him a long time to be taken seriously and as a genuine option up front; he never played for a big club at home and moved abroad aged 19 without having scored a senior goal. That has changed; after seven years in Czechia, Germany and Portugal - including Champions League experience with Benfica -  he signed for FC Dallas in 2024 and has developed into one of the MLS’s leading strikers. His mother, Jagoda Obradovic, played for the women’s national team in the mid-1990s.

players.name: Eloy Room
players.bio: It was during the 2014-2015 season when Eloy Room received a phone call from Patrick Kluivert, the then Curaçao head coach who invited the goalkeeper to represent The Blue Wave. Many other-Dutch born players were to follow. The keeper, who is known for his sharp reflexes, has experienced many memorable moments on international duty. Take the day Lionel Messi asked for his shirt. While most players have to chase the Argentinian’s shirt on this occasion it was Messi himself – who had just scored a hat-trick against Room – who approached the goalkeeper. “When the match was finished, he was basically standing right in front of me and made a gesture of swapping shirts,” Room recalls. It was possibly to do with the fact Messi had just scored his 100th goal for Argentina and wanted Room’s shirt as a memory of the milestone but he did compliment the goalkeeper for “some good saves” during the match.

players.name: Shurandy Sambo
players.bio: Most people would have given up if they had suffered a second ACL tear at just 19 - but not Sambo. Remarkably, he drew inspiration from his first injury lay-off. “During my second rehab, I still had the notes from the first one. I kept comparing the numbers, always trying to do better – even if it was just rowing 15 metres further,” he told Sportnieuws. Born in Geldrop in the Netherlands and developed in the PSV Eindhoven academy, Sambo grew up in a sporting family. “My brother did boxing and my sister did gymnastics,” he explains. “My father played football in Curaçao, so for him it’s extra special that we’ve now qualified for the World Cup.” His father also played a key role in his development. “I always trained extra with him,” says the full-back, who combines technical ability with strength in one v ones and a constant drive to push forward.

players.name: Juriën Gaari
players.bio: After starting out in the Dutch amateur game, he and a teammate came up with an original idea: they were going to email professional clubs in search of an opportunity. “We drafted a message at his house, looked up email addresses on club websites, and then it was copy and paste,” he told Voetbal International. “Sometimes we got a reply, often we didn’t.” The approach paid off, though, as Gaari was invited to train with Excelsior Rotterdam and eventually earned a place in the academy. After several years there, he returned to the amateur game. And It was during his time at Kozakken Boys that his international journey began. After a match against BVV Barendrecht, his teammate Raily Ignacio informed him that the then Curaçao manager Remko Bicentini was waiting outside. The two had a discussion and Garri was suddenly an international player. He was part of Curaçao’s Caribbean Cup win and first ever Gold Cup participation in 2017 – still as an amateur. Has been playing in Saudi Arabia since 2024 and is currently with first division side Abha.

players.name: Roshon van Eijma
players.bio: When Van Eijma signed his first professional terms with Roda JC, he picked up an extra job delivering pizzas to top up his wages. “Sometimes I’d turn up at someone’s door with a pizza and they’d recognise me from a match. It took a bit of getting used to at first, but it’s funny too,” the centre-back told ELF Voetbal. He had to be creative, just like in his early days at Roda, where he started on €150 a month, making ends meet with help from his agent and parents. “The club gave us a studio to live in. I stayed there with other players, it felt more like a student house,” he says. A physically strong, commanding presence at the back for Curaçao, Van Eijma played for Preußen Münster and TOP Oss before landing at RKC Waalwijk in 2024.

players.name: Sherel Floranus
players.bio: Having represented several Dutch youth national teams, he switched allegiance to Curaçao national football team in 2023. The decision quickly paid off, as World Cup qualification was secured after a 0–0 draw away at Jamaica last year, sparking what he described as “really the best party ever”. Afterwards, footage emerged of teammates attempting to wake him by using a horn on the flight from Jamaica to Curaçao. “I knew it was going to be a long day so I told everyone I was going to sleep for an hour,” Floranus told ESPN. That did not work out but he had another opportunity to catch up on his sleep. “Luckily I managed nine hours on the flight back to the Netherlands,” he added. A cousin of the former Newcastle United defender Jetro Willems, Floranus is a versatile player, capable of playing at right-back, left-back and centre-back.

players.name: Godfried Roemeratoe
players.bio: When Godfried Roemeratoe was 14, he left his family home in Zeeland to join the FC Twente academy in the east of the Netherlands. In his spare time, he spent endless hours on buses and trains to make the commute back home, where his brother Renzo was playing for Sparta Rotterdam. Technically gifted and physically strong, Roemeratoe broke through at Twente and, after a loan spell at Willem II, played for Israeli side Hapoel Tel Aviv. During that time, he made his bow for Curaçao, where his second match was against the newly crowned world champions Argentina. “Playing against Lionel Messi was something I never even dared to dream of,” Roemeratoe told Omroep Zeeland, where he recalled the moment when he was warming up and the Argentinian No 10 stepped onto the pitch. “It honestly felt like I was in a movie, but then you realise it’s real and you actually see him walking around there. That was surreal.”

players.name: Juninho Bacuna
players.bio: The brother of Leandro, Juninho ensures that the Bacuna family is well-represented in the squad while their elder sibling, Johnsen, played at a very good amateur level. The brothers achieved a special feat in 2015 when they all played in cup finals. Juninho (Groningen) and Johnsen (Harkemase Boys) won their games against PEC Zwolle and Staphorst while Leandro (Aston Villa) lost to Arsenal. They all like to play with the No 7, although Leandro opted for the No 10 jersey for Curaçao. Their father, John, also played around 30 games for the Netherlands Antilles. The well-travelled and versatile Juninho left Groningen for the Premier League and Huddersfield in 2018 and played for Rangers and Birmingham before stints in Saudi Arabia, Turkey and the Netherlands.

players.name: Livano Comenencia
players.bio: Not long after scoring one of the most crucial goals in Curaçao’s Wold Cup qualification campaign, Livano Comenencia made sure the moment would be preserved for future generations. Having scored the opener in the pivotal 2-0 win over Jamaica he went to the Fifa museum in Zurich – where he plays his club football – to donate his match shirt from the game. “Livano’s shirt represents a fresh and incredible chapter in the global game," the Fifa museum director, Marco Fazzone, said. Much like Curaçao’s journey, Comenencia’s career has already included a wide range of experiences, with a solid education at PSV and the Next Gen team of Juventus, before settling in Switzerland.

players.name: Jürgen Locadia
players.bio: When Locadia joined Brighton from PSV for £15m in 2018 he looked set for a long and successful Premier League career. However, it didn’t quite work out like that and he left the south-coast club on a free four years - and several loans - later. He played in Iran, China and Spain before suddenly finding himself without a club after the 2024-25 season. A few months later, ahead of Curaçao’s crucial World Cup qualifier against Jamaica, he received a phone call from the national team manager, Wouter Jansen. “He wasn’t in the squad at first, but then Gervane Kastaneer got injured and I knew Jürgen had trained for himself,” Jansen recalls. “We were on our way from Bermuda to Jamaica and [the head coach] Dick Advocaat said: ‘give him a call, he can join us’. He then started that game and did a really good job in our 0-0 draw, which qualified us for the World Cup.” It fits Locadia’s remarkable story of the last years, including fleeing Iran during the Mahsa Amini protests and being served by robots in a restaurant in China.

players.name: Leandro Bacuna
players.bio: One of the squad’s most capped - and experienced - players as he chose to represent Curaçao back in 2016, while he was still playing in the Premier League with Aston Villa. Patrick Kluivert was the head coach then and Bacuna followed in the footsteps of the goalkeeper Eloy Room, who had opted to play for Curaçao the year before. The team became incredibly close-knit, something reflected in how they honour Jairzinho Pieter, a former national team goalkeeper who died while travelling with the squad. Pieter was known for leading the team’s daily prayers – a ritual still observed today, with Bacuna placing a necklace belonging to him at the centre of the team huddle. “His passing was very difficult at the time and it is still very difficult,” Room said. “That made the dream of reaching the World Cup come even more to life, because it was also truly his dream. It gave us even more motivation.”

players.name: Jeremy Antonisse
players.bio: When Curaçao qualified for the World Cup after drawing 0-0 in Jamaica, Antonisse must have been particularly relieved. In injury time he gave away a penalty for a challenge on Jamaica’s Dujuan Richards - or so he thought before it was overturned by VAR. Moments later the final whistle sounded and he could join his teammates in the celebrations, which would continue into the following day when the squad was honoured for several hours on an open bus through Willemstad. The creative winger, who can also be deployed as an attacking midfielder, spent more than 10 years at the PSV academy. After a loan spell at Emmen, he moved to Moreirense in Portugal, before signing for Greek side Kifisia last year.

players.name: Sontje Hansen
players.bio: The last time the Netherlands won the Under-17 Euros, in 2019, Hansen led the line and scored in the final against Italy (4–2). He was seen as a huge prospect in the Ajax academy, but it did not quite happen for him in Amsterdam so he moved to NEC Nijmegen, where he impressed to the degree that Middlesbrough gave him a four-year contract. It was Dick Advocaat who convinced him to represent the Curaçao national team and he scored on his second appearance in a 7–0 World Cup qualifying win away at Bermuda. He is now preparing for his first major tournament with Curaçao, with his cousin Ar’jany Martha also part of the set-up.

players.name: Tyrese Noslin
players.bio: Noslin knows all about how quickly things can change. At the end of the 2023-24 season, he was told that he would not be able to continue playing for the Almere City reserves in the Tweede Divisie was not an option. He then decided to take things into his own hands and approached the manager of Telstar, Anthony Correia. “Tyrese suddenly sent me a message,” Correia told Voetbal International. “We then invited him for a trial at Telstar. He quickly proved to be the kind of player I like: a boy with tireless commitment. And a really nice lad. But he still needed to improve in terms of playing quality. So we started to work on that.” They did just that and Noslin helped Telstar win promotion to the Eredivisie before scoring the winner on his home debut for Curaçao in a 3-2 win against Bermuda.

players.name: Kenji Gorré
players.bio: The career of Gorré shows how setbacks can lead to growth. After spending more than a decade in the Manchester United academy and later playing for Swansea City, he became addicted to gambling. “I went once, then twice, and suddenly I realised: hang on a second, nowadays I go every day,” he told Voetbal International. Gorré realised he had a problem and worked on it, a process that eventually inspired him to help others, via a mindset coaching platform, where he and other footballers were able to openly discuss mental health challenges and life beyond the pitch. On the pitch, he rebuilt his career, spending five years in Portugal and is currently on the books of Maccabi Haifa. The technically gifted winger has been a member of the Curaçao national team, where his father Dean Gorré is the assistant coach, since 2019.

players.name: Ar'jany Martha
players.bio: Nurtured as a winger in the Ajax academy, Martha was suddenly moved to left-back during a season with the reserves – a switch that eventually led to his first-team debut. He adapted quickly and played his first five games in the Eredivisie with the senior side in that position. “I find playing left-back pretty OK,” he said with a smile after a match against Heerenveen. “The difficult thing is you drop back two lines. In the beginning, I found it a bit difficult.” After speaking to his coach, family and friends, he was advised to “just switch the button and do your thing there” and says he eventually felt “I’ve got it in me”. Having developed he moved to Beerschot in 2024 and now plies his trade in League One with Rotherham United.

players.name: Jearl Margaritha
players.bio: Margaritha knew that hard work was required to make it in professional football. In the end he had to be patient as well. After being released from the Groningen and Emmen academies he dropped into amateur football with VV Hoogeveen at the age of 19. “It was a difficult period, but I thought: OK, if I really want this, I have to show that I stand out at this level and that I want to move up,” he told the Keuken Kampioen Divisie website. Within six months, he had earned a move to Almere City FC. He stayed on track with spells at TOP Oss and Sabah before a move to Phoenix Rising in 2024. A creative winger with sharp acceleration, Margaritha joined Beveren this season, where he played a key role in promotion with seven goals and 12 assists. The title was already secured on 22 February, making it the earliest promotion in the Belgian second tier’s history.

players.name: Brandley Kuwas
players.bio: Nine years ago Kuwas became the subject of a song bearing his name, produced by his close friend and rapper Jonna Fraser. The song was a tribute to their friendship, detailing how they spent their youth together, sharing dreams of becoming an artist and a footballer. They both went on to achieve those goals and when the song was released, Kuwas was flying high with Heracles Almelo in the Eredivisie. He even got a phone call from Danny Blind, the then Netherlands head coach, saying that he was being monitored for a call-up. In the end he was not selected and shortly afterwards he chose to represent Curaçao. He has ended up wearing orange at club level though, playing for Volendam, where he started his career.

players.name: Armando Obispo
players.bio: Joining PSV at a young age he played together with Cody Gakpo (Liverpool) and Jordan Teze (Monaco) among others. Hugely talented he had several injury issues but came back stronger each time. He is now a reliable centre-back for the first team and has won the league three years in a row and gained valuable Champions League experience. Having represented the Netherlands at every youth level from Under-15 upwards, a route to the senior team looked within reach, but the call never came and opted to play for Curaçao, the country of his father. Obispo stands out for his strength in one v ones and his build-up play. “He’s also an excellent header of the ball and has an outstanding left foot,” says his PSV manager, Peter Bosz.

players.name: Gervane Kastaneer
players.bio:
Kastaneer was a promising talent at ADO Den Haag and had already signed a pre-contract at German side FSV Mainz, when disaster struck during a game against 
PEC Zwolle. Shortly after opening the scoring, the ball struck his eye, causing a torn retina. The team doctor immediately took him off but Kastaneer was not happy and said “Come on, man, I can just play with one eye, or not?” – believing he had only got pitch pellets in his eye. The injury turned out to be far more serious, leading to a lengthy lay-off and the cancellation of his move to Mainz. Since then, he has played with 30% vision in one eye, but has continued his career and it has not been a bad one. He has played for clubs such as Kaiserslautern, Coventry City and Hearts, before moving to Spain, Indonesia and Malaysia, where he plays for Terengganu FC.

players.name: Joshua Brenet
players.bio: Brenet once set a remarkable record during his time at PSV Eindhoven, going 53 consecutive league matches unbeaten. At the time he even made his Netherlands debut alongside Wesley Sneijder, Arjen Robben, Virgil van Dijk and Memphis Depay. He won three league titles and two Dutch Super Cups with PSV before moving to Hoffenheim, where he played under Julian Nagelsmann. Brenet fell out of favour after the future Germany coach left and he returned to the Netherlands to rejuvenate his career at Twente, before going abroad again to Qatar (Al-Rayyan), Scotland (Livingston) and Turkey (Kayserispor). The attacking full-back, known for his pace and technical ability, has been part of the Curaçao national football team setup since 2024.

players.name: Tahith Chong
players.bio: One of the few players in the national team setup actually born in Curaçao, the creative midfielder moved with his family from Willemstad to Rotterdam at the age of nine after being offered a place in the Feyenoord academy. Seven years later he moved again with his family - this time to England - after Manchester United offered him a place at their academy. He continued to develop and later trained with the first team under José Mourinho, who gave him his first senior appearance in a friendly. Competitive minutes followed under Ole Gunnar Solskjær, including a substitute appearance in the famous 3–1 comeback win at Paris Saint-Germain. “It’s been a special journey,” Chong told Voetbal International a few years ago. “My parents left everything behind in Curaçao for my dream.” Now the circle is complete with Chong back on the island playing for the national team while representing Sheffield United in the Championship.

players.name: Kevin Felida
players.bio: Regarded as a huge prospect at second-tier side FC Den Bosch, for whom he made more than 130 appearances before earning a transfer to RKC in the Eredivisie. However, the defensive midfielder did not break through there and returned to his former club. A ball-winning player with great stamina, he has been dubbed the “Bossche N’Golo Kanté”. One of his best friends in football is the Manchester United forward Joshua Zirkzee. Both grew up in Spijkenisse and spent a lot of time together, but at and outside school. Just after Zirkzee had made his debut for Bayern Munich in December 2019, the two would meet up during the Christmas break to go for runs in the evenings. Felida made his debut for Curaçao in 2021 after receiving a call from then-head coach Guus Hiddink and eventually earned his first cap under Patrick Kluivert.

players.name: Riechedly Bazoer
players.bio: One day in 2012 Bazoer was at the Manchester City training ground to have a look around their academy. “I went to Manchester with my brother, and the idea was that we would finalise everything there. We got a tour of the club, and head of academy Patrick Vieira came to speak to us, a real legend. It was all very impressive,” the defensive midfielder told Voetbal International. The move didn’t happen in the end as the most important person had not had her say: his mother. She felt that her son would benefit from a more gradual development in the Netherlands so Bazoer joined Ajax and worked under coaches such as Frank de Boer and Dennis Bergkamp. From there he moved on to Wolfsburg, Vitesse and AZ Alkmaar, before joining Konyaspor in 2024. After six friendly appearances for the Netherlands, he switched international allegiance to Curaçao last year.

players.name: Deveron Fonville
players.bio: Only last season, Fonville was still playing amateur football with IJsselmeervogels, having arrived from fellow amateur side USV Hercules – who produced one of the biggest cup surprises in recent memory by beating Ajax in 2023. A product of the PSV Eindhoven and FC Utrecht academies, Fonville had to make back-up plans in case he never made it as a footballer. “I suddenly had to look beyond football and start working to earn money,” he told De Gelderlander. “I first worked in a warehouse and later as a recruitment consultant.” Everything changed last summer when he signed for FC Dordrecht. After just four appearances, he earned a move to NEC Nijmegen, the club where he had been on trial in 2023, but which fell through due to a registration issue. It completed a full circle - with two invaluable years of amateur football in between, which gave him senior football experience, before continuing to rise up the professional game.

players.name: Tyrick Bodak
players.bio: After spells in the academies of PSV Eindhoven and De Graafschap, Bodak is now at Telstar but still awaiting his senior club debut. However, he already has several caps for Curaçao, having - unlike many of this teammates - risen through the country’s age-groups to make his first-team debut at 19  while playing for PSV’s reserves and studying hotel and event management. “I’ve wanted to open my own bed & breakfast since secondary school,” he told TIO Business School, outlining his plans beyond football. Bodak even did an internship at his former teammate Mitch Apau’s chip shop, which is situated at the Telstar stadium, where Bodak is currently behind Ronald Koeman Jr in the pecking order. Sport runs in the family: his father and two of his brothers focused on baseball while another brother is also an active sportsman.

players.name: Trevor Doornbusch
players.bio: When Trevor Doornbusch made his international debut in the summer of 2023, it came as something of a surprise as he had not featured in a single match for FC Dordrecht that season. As a youngster, he studied clips of Iker Casillas on YouTube and spent four years in the academy of Ajax before dropping into amateur football. His form earned him a move to Heerenveen and, although he never made the first team, he did represent the Netherlands at youth level during that time. Eventually, Doornbusch – known for being good with his feet and his long-range passing – had his breakthrough at Telstar, where he made his professional debut and continued his development as a modern goalkeeper.

players.name: Matej Kovar
players.bio: A favourite in the Czech national team since last autumn. In the space of a few days he helped seal two play-off successes via penalty shootouts. Before then he had only saved one spot-kick in domestic football, but he excelled in the scrap for a World Cup spot like nobody else in the Czech squad. “It feels like a dream,” he said after the wins over Republic of Ireland and Denmark. Kovar is a huge talent, calm by nature and mostly phlegmatic. As a teenager, he left a small Czech town to join Manchester United. He has since won three league titles in three different countries: Czechia, Germany and the Netherlands. Although he’s usually quiet and modest, he thrives in the big moments and coaches joke that he brings luck. “It wasn’t any great luck, I simply did my homework,” he said while the whole country was lauding him. Still, speculation persists over whether he will be the first-choice goalkeeper for the World Cup.

players.name: David Zima
players.bio: Zima can come across as a rather unusual young man, especially in his public appearances. At first glance, you cannot tell what kind of day he’s having. After the first question, you still don’t know how the interview will go. Zima – meaning “winter” – is not a flashy star, despite the fact his career quickly took off as a teenager. At 19, with just 156 minutes in the domestic league, he was signed by Slavia Prague from Sigma Olomouc for more than €1m, a fee rarely seen in Czech football for such a player. The move did not faze him. “I have no reason to be nervous,” he said. “The pitch is always the same size. Opponents don’t scare me.” A year and a half later, he moved to Italy for four times that fee, joining Torino. In January 2024, he returned to Slavia Prague to restart his career and, at €4m, became the most expensive signing in Czech league history. Now a four-time domestic league champion, he is widely praised for his precise approach to defending, though critics still say he can be too laid back, and prone to defensive lapses.

players.name: Tomas Holes
players.bio: Fans of human anatomy should take a look at Tomas: the centre-back has what may well be the biggest calves at the tournament. He’s not particularly tall, but is pure muscle. He’s also one of the most experienced players in the Czech squad and is a quiet leader at Slavia Prague, where he has won four league titles, the latest as captain. He comes from a region where cross-country skiing is more common than football but fell in love with the game competing with his two brothers. With no proper pitch in his village, he used to cycle three miles to training. Through consistency and hard work, Holes has gradually developed into a leader without ever acting like a superstar. Despite Czechia being a largely secular country, he is a devout evangelical Christian and says: “Faith is the most important thing for me, apart from my family. I often feel like guardian angels help me on the pitch.” Away from the pitch, Holes enjoys the quiet life with his two daughters, often going fishing and reading history books before bed.

players.name: Robin Hranac
players.bio: Hranac grew up in a small town in western Czechia, far from major academies – so when his talent became clear, that had to change. At 14, he travelled 60 miles to a boarding school in Plzen while his parents worked across the border in Germany. The sacrifices paid off. These days he has a calm and reserved exterior, and can appear shy, but that disguises the real fight and passion inside him. ”Robin takes a lot of hits but never reacts or gets angry,” says his dad. “He has an extremely high pain threshold.” After just one impressive season in the Czech league he broke into the national team, marked Cristiano Ronaldo at Euro 2024, and earned an €8m move to Hoffenheim in 2024. Best deployed in the middle of a back three.

players.name: Vladimir Coufal
players.bio: Long overlooked due to his slight build, Coufal was told at 17 by coaches at Czech club Banik Ostrava that he would never make a living from football. He was quick and determined, but too small and easily outmuscled in duels. At that time, he hated football. “But I promised myself I’d prove them all wrong. I’m a bit of a madman who likes chasing his dreams,” he said. True recognition only came at 26, when he was signed by Slavia Prague and went on to win three consecutive league titles before making a surprise move to the Premier League. “At first I thought they had the wrong name. Why would they sign a veteran?” he joked of his move to West Ham. In London he enjoyed a fairytale spell, culminating in winning the Europa Conference League. But after five years he found himself on the periphery with the Hammers. Again, though, he refused to give up. Now 33, he has rebuilt his career at Hoffenheim and is playing arguably his best football.

players.name: Stepan Chaloupek
players.bio: One of the breakout stars of the recent Czech league season, one of the country’s best out-and-out defenders and a champion with Slavia, Chaloupek’s performances were such that he attracted interest from Roma and Fiorentina in January. A year earlier, however, he wasn’t even in the starting lineup after a tough time with injury. He’s a hard-working, disciplined and reliable defender – a nightmare for attackers due to his uncompromising style. Off the pitch, he is quiet and reserved. Had football not worked out, he says he might have become a police officer. “I liked the idea of being fair, principled and standing up for the truth,” he says. Chaloupek was born with a cleft palate and underwent surgery at the age of three. “I was mocked by other kids because of it. It was hard, but it made me stronger.” He later considered plastic surgery as he still breathes through only one nostril.

players.name: Ladislav Krejci
players.bio: Krejci’s childhood dream of playing in the Premier League turned into something of a nightmare last season as Wolves endured defeat after defeat, suffering relegation to the Championship. The centre-back, however, has quickly – and to the surprise of some – turned into one of Rob Edwards’ most valuable players. Over the past four years, his rise has been rapid. He won two league titles with Sparta Prague, became the most expensive signing in Girona’s history, before a loan to the Premier League last summer. Krejci follows a simple motto: ”If you feel you should do, decide or change something, don’t hesitate. No one else will do it for you.” He used to struggle with discipline and picked up too many yellow cards. Yet since March, he has been captain of the national team and led with distinction, scoring in the playoff games against Republic of Ireland and Denmark. “I believed in myself because I know what I can do,” said the 27-year-old.

players.name: Vladimír Darida
players.bio: An unassuming character, when Darida played for Freiburg from 2013 to 2015 he rode a scooter to training. He got rid of it when he moved to Berlin with Hertha, though. “It wasn’t really safe in Berlin traffic,” he said. “My father took it back to Czechia and I gave it to my sister.” It wasn’t an easy journey into professional football: he almost had to quit the game when he was 16 when his growth stopped, leaving him struggling to handle the physicality of the game. But, via hard work and a gluten-free diet, he developed the strength needed. He spent seven years at Hertha before moving to Aris in Greece in 2022, and returning to Czechia last year with Hradec Kralove. He brings huge experience, having made his international debut back in 2012, and captained the team at Euro 2020. After that tournament he retired from international football, saying: “I experienced beautiful moments in the national team, but now, I’d like to spend much more time with my family and my young son.” But he was tempted back in March this year, returning, aged 35, to help them in the World Cup qualification play-offs.

players.name: Adam Hlozek
players.bio: A real talent, who could make a big impact. Hlozek has played in Germany since 2022 when Bayer Leverkusen took note of his record at Sparta Prague: a senior debut at 16, scoring 40 goals and making 36 assists in 132 games, and winning the Czech Cup. He joined for an initial €13m, but only stayed for two years, having grown frustrated with a rotational role, moving to Hoffenheim for an initial €17m. Hugely versatile, he’s a threat out wide or through the middle. He’s a big fan of tattoos, with a giant lion on his left thigh. “It has a deep, personal meaning for me and gives me strength,” he said last year. “Leo is my star sign and I identify with the animal. The lion is known for its fighting mentality, exudes an aura and is the king of the wilderness. It took five and a half hours in the first sitting, then we took a break and after that it was another five and a half hours. But I'm happy that I got through it. I like it a lot."

players.name: Patrik Schick
players.bio: Schick’s goalscoring gift was obvious from a very young age. Playing for Vestec, a village near Prague, he didn’t celebrate his goals, said coaches, but would instead just pick the ball up, take it to the centre circle and ask for the game to be resumed. When he missed a big chance, he would cry and had to be substituted. Aged eight he was inspired by watching Wayne Rooney play for Manchester United at Sparta Prague. He recalled: “I thought that I want to do this – never get a normal job, and earn a living like him.” It worked out well. He played a grand total of 36 league minutes in Sparta Prague’s first team before Sampdoria decided they had seen enough, paying €4m for the gangly 20-year-old. He signed for Roma a year later in a move that could have been worth up to a club record €42m, but it didn’t really work out there: he ended up on the wing, was loaned to Leipzig, then was sold to Leverkusen in 2020 for €26.5m - which is where everything clicked. In April this year, he scored his 100th goal for them in all competitions. He helped Czechia qualify for the first time in 20 years via the playoffs. He was 10 when they last played at a World Cup. “I vividly remember the games. The Czech fans have waited a long time for this.”

players.name: Jan Kuchta
players.bio: An experienced forward who made his senior debut for Slavia Prague back in 2016, and now, a decade later, turns out for rivals Sparta. Kuchta also had spells in Russia with Lokomotiv Moscow and Denmark with Midtjylland. Life with the international side hasn’t always been easy: in November 2023 he was one of three players – with Jakub Brabec and Vladimir Coufal – kicked out of the training camp for going clubbing two days before a Euro 2024 qualifier against Moldova. Aggressive and powerful on the pitch, he’s more nurturing off it, raising chickens, rabbits, ducks and geese at his family farm. When he moved to Moscow in 2022 and had to leave them behind he said: "Chickens are part of my life, I was used to going to them in the morning, then again after training, it cleared my head. It will take a while to adjust. I can’t take them there, it’s -17 degrees… The chickens will just have to wait until I am home, and I will pay a lot of attention to them again."

players.name: Lukas Cerv
players.bio: Cousin of teammate David Doudera, Cerv made his international debut two years ago, soon after joining Viktoria Plzen from Slovan Liberec, and was the only newcomer in the national side’s Euro 2024 squad. He wasn’t expecting the call when it came. “I didn’t really believe it at first,” he said after arriving at the team’s training camp. “I went on vacation, so when I got the call I had to ​​leave my family there and fly away. I left them there to enjoy the warmth and sunshine. And here we are in the rain and fog. But it's really incredible." Settling in as part of the national set-up wasn’t too hard, though: “I know a lot of guys from the Under-21s and I have Doudis [Doudera], my cousin, and the guys from Plzen here, so everything is fine. But I'm probably hanging out with Doudis most.” Two years on and he’s fully integrated, and an experienced, reliable option for this World Cup squad. His surname translates as “worm”.

players.name: Mojmir Chytil
players.bio: A tall, experienced option up front, Chytil made quite a splash on his international debut in 2022 - scoring a ten-minute hattrick in a friendly against the Faroe Islands. He’s known for being physical and fearless, a real handful, and could be an important weapon this summer. He spent most of his early career at Sigma Olomouc before 2023’s move to Slavia Prague where, again, he had a scoring debut, netting against Hradec Králové on the opening day. The club’s manager, Jindrich Trpisovsky, described him well: “The first thing that comes to mind about Mojmir is the wider benefit he brings to the team: his fighting spirit, his annoyingness for defenders. He has very good ball coverage, perhaps the best back-to-goal game in the league. He is a team player, combative, with excellent physical qualities.”

players.name: David Jurasek
players.bio: When Jurasek moved from Slavia Prague to Benfica for €14m three years ago, becoming one of the Czech league’s most expensive exports, it seemed Czech football would finally have a full-back at a top European club. His father even flew to Portugal by private jet for the signing ceremony, as Benfica set his release clause at a whopping €80m, an indication of the regard in which they held him. However, a serious ankle injury disrupted his progress, and he lost his place in the Benfica first team. Jurasek rebuilt his career during a loan spell at Hoffenheim, before returning to Slavia in January. “I just hope I don’t bring bad luck,” he confessed before helping them to the league title – the first title success of his career. With a strong left foot, excellent stamina and acceleration, a big physical presence and a great crossing ability, Jurasek has just about every attribute you would want from a left-back.

players.name: Pavel Sulc
players.bio: Widely linked with a big move after this tournament – this summer is a real chance for Sulc to showcase his talents. Soon after being voted the best Czech player of 2025 he joined Lyon from Viktoria Plzen for an initial €7.5m, handed the formidable task of filling the void left behind by Rayan Cherki. But he shrugged off that pressure and stepped up with some superb displays, earning plenty of praise from supporters. The reception surprised him. “It’s been incredible,” he said in April. “Honestly, I didn't expect so much love from fans or that it would happen so quickly … It’s my first experience in one of the top five leagues in Europe, and the football in Czechia is simply much less demanding. The coach has been brilliant with me.” He’s happy to be versatile, too. “I'll always do my best, even if they put me as a centre-back!”

players.name: Jindrich Stanek
players.bio: Stanek has never got anything for free in his career. He started out as an ice hockey player and was a promising forward, a talent he inherited from his father. But he chose to leave the ice in pursuit of a football career and went straight from Sparta to England, where he was a backup goalkeeper at Everton, facing shots from Romelu Lukaku, Kevin Mirallas and Samuel Eto’o in training. “I felt like I was in another world. I was taking a battering, but I didn’t want to give up,” he later recalled. He dreamed of following in the footsteps of compatriot Petr Cech, but had to take the long way round. He even spent a season in the Czech third division to build his confidence. ”In hindsight, I feel like a cat with seven lives,” he said of his nomadic career. “That’s why when I go to the zoo, I spend most of my time with the tigers. I look for inspiration in them.” He eventually settled with Viktoria Plzen, where his career took off, leading to a move to Slavia Prague in 2024.

players.name: Lukas Provod
players.bio: An imposing 1.88m presence out wide, he is pacy as well. Provod rose through the ranks at hometown club Viktoria Plzen, then had a series of loans as he established himself, first at Banik Sokolov, then Ceske Budejovice and finally Slavia Prague, whom he joined on a permanent deal in 2020. “It feels unbelievable,” he said when he signed, “it feels like a beautiful story. It all happened really fast. It’s a great challenge for me, and I did not hesitate. Slavia is the biggest and most successful club in Czechia, so it was clear immediately.” He made his international debut the same year in a Nations League game against Slovakia. He missed Euro 2020 due to injury, but made the squad for 2024 and scored in the opener against Portugal. A versatile performer, he could also be called upon to do a job more centrally, or up front.

players.name: Michal Sadilek
players.bio: At 16, Sadilek made a bold move. Instead of following his older brother Lukas through the academy system in his home country, he left for the Netherlands, joining PSV. Maybe the time apart did them good. “As kids we were together every day,” remembered Michal, “and sometimes like all little boys we got on each other's nerves, fought and the like.” But these days they couldn’t be closer. “I’m proud of my brother. We write to each other after every match, and when there's a chance, we talk about everything.” He moved to Twente in 2022 for three years, before returning home with Slavia in 2025. His international career hasn’t been without its ups and downs: he made his debut in 2021 but had to pull out of the Euro 2024 squad after injuring his leg when he fell off his tricycle at the training camp. The FA said the incident happened when he drove the three-wheeled bike through a pothole and crashed, but they didn’t criticise him. “The accident happened in the player's free time, which he was allowed to spend as he pleased. He did not violate team rules or other internal agreements."

players.name: Tomas Chory
players.bio: A 1.98m powerhouse who isn’t afraid of getting stuck in. In January he embraced his bad boy image, saying: “People don’t like me? That actually makes me feel good.” But in May things reached a tipping point: he was sent off for elbowing an opponent in the head in a chaotic Prague derby, his third red of the season, prompting Slavia’s furious chairman, Jaroslav Tvrdik, to transfer-list him. “Tomas is clearly unable to control his behaviour,” said Tvrdik. “With this behaviour, he does not belong on the field.” The league’s disciplinary committee banned him for six games, saying the elbow was “not an accidental event … neither emotions nor the assertive style of play that Tomas Chory presents can be an excuse for such an action”. He has plenty of experience, and has spent his whole club career in Czechia apart from a loan spell in Belgium with Zulte Waregem. He made his international debut in 2023, scoring and assisting in a 3-0 Euro 2024 qualifying win over Moldova.

players.name: Jaroslav Zeleny
players.bio: One of the select few footballers who fans have forgiven for playing for both rival Prague clubs. An easy-going character, Zeleny is in no way a rebel or troublemaker, but he can be impatient, particularly when driving his car. “It annoys me when someone dawdles at the lights because they are on their phone,” he says. “That’s when I lean on the horn. Otherwise, I’m calm. People say you need to be ruthless in top football, but it’s not in my nature.” Zeleny is unlikely to change his nature now as he approaches his 34th birthday. He has never left the Czech league, making over 300 appearances since his debut for Hradec Kralove in 2010. Earlier in his career, he may have lacked the courage to move abroad; now he’s nearing the end of his time as an evergreen of Czech football. Fittingly, his surname translates to “green” in English.

players.name: David Doudera
players.bio: Like his Slavia teammate Tomas Chory, Doudera had a lively Prague derby in May, with both shown red cards as the game descended into a mess of crowd violence. Doudera’s red was for abusing the referee; Slavia’s chairman, Jaroslav Tvrdik, responded by transfer-listing him. The defender later apologised, writing online: "I'm sorry for the whole situation. To the fans, to the whole of Slavia, to Czech football. These things shouldn't happen in a civilised world.” He now has a chance to redeem himself this summer. He made his international debut in 2023, a year after his move to Slavia. He said soon afterwards: “My dad and I always say that my story is worth a book. But I don't want to brag about it, it's just that I have it in me. Each of us has a story and everyone got here somehow. I wasn't a great talent from a young age. I was never in the youth national teams … I'm proud of myself for what I've accomplished.” Teammate Lukas Cerv is his cousin.

players.name: Tomas Soucek
players.bio: It wasn’t an easy season for Soucek as West Ham struggled, but he remains a fundamental part of Miroslav Koubek’s national side – a brilliantly energetic 1.93m force in midfield. Last year he was named Czech Footballer of the Year for the fourth time. He was brought up in Havlickuv Brod, a town with a population of 23,000 people, and comes from a sporting family: his dad a football coach and his mum a runner. “She runs half-marathons and marathons and sometimes when I was younger I went with her to the forest,” he says. “And sometimes when I was in pre-season I would go with her to run in the forest. I think I got my stamina from her.” Last year he published his autobiography, in which he revealed he had considered retiring early during what were “two years of horror” spent with insomnia and depression. He improved with medical help, and said his trademark “helicopter” goal celebration rose from the experience: “From match to match, I focused mainly on not sleeping, which actually disturbed me doubly. And that’s why the helicopter was created, my goal celebration. I start to spin slightly and enjoy the feeling of taking off. I like it when fans call me a helicopter. It’s a symbol of what I’ve done, what I’ve overcome, and where I am. It expresses my inner feeling that I can fight.”

players.name: Lukas Hornicek
players.bio: Hornicek has spent roughly a third of his life in Portugal, where he moved aged 17. He was based in a boarding school 1,500 miles from home and was homesick, but never gave up his dream of a career in football. “That decision took away part of the youth I could have spent with friends, but I followed my dream. Even if I could turn back time, I wouldn’t change it,” he says. Nicknamed “Horny Czech“ in Portugal because of the difficulty of pronouncing his surname, he’s now a rising star in Czech goalkeeping. He started in Braga’s youth team and spent two years as a backup, before a spectacular breakthrough last season. He kept a clean sheet every other match and became known for saving penalties in the Europa League. Boasting high levels of concentration, he has improved his distribution and work on the ball in Portugal and has even been linked with Real Madrid of late. “It’s a shame my grandfather didn’t live to see [this]. He was my biggest supporter,” he says.

players.name: Alexandr Sojka
players.bio: Raised in a footballing family. Sojka’s dad Jindrich was chairman of SK Klatovy for over 20 years, and his older brother plays for them now. Alexandr has gone the furthest in the family, though. He returned to his first club, Viktoria Plzen, this year when they activated a buy-back clause, re-signing him from Hradec Kralove. “We decided to activate the option," said the club’s sporting director, Martin Vozábal. "The goal is to increase competition in midfield and he performed very well during the autumn.” He has proved himself as a technically-gifted, versatile performer. His international inclusion was an overwhelming moment for his dad. "The whole family lives for it,” Jindrich told iSport. “The only pity is that his grandfather, a big fan of Plzen since childhood, did not live to see it. Sasa [Alexandr] is finally showing what is in him. People in this region live for football and they appreciate that a boy from Klatovy can reach such a level."

players.name: Hugo Sochurek
players.bio: The baby of the squad, Sochurek was still 17 when the final selection was announced. A product of Sparta’s academy, he made his club debut last year, and his international debut in a warm-up game against Kosovo in May. But his cool, fearless approach convinced the coach, Miroslav Koubek, he was ready. After his first-team breakthrough at Sparta, coach Brian Priske said: “He plays like he's over 30. The first-team players immediately took him in and when they saw what he could do with the ball.” Sochurek’s youth coach Pavel Drsek told Eurofotbal.cz in May: “He's made a huge improvement in the last year, he’s grown bigger, he has strong thighs, it's hard to get him off the ball. He’s not a typical Czech midfielder, he likes the ball, his first thought is to go forward … And he's a great guy, a pure soul, but quiet. Once I handed him the captain's armband before a match and he immediately told me that he could not be the captain. He is not a classic leader, but he is clearly a driving force.”

players.name: Denis Visinsky
players.bio: A promising young winger who knew what he wanted from the moment he first kicked a ball. “I first played in the village where I was born, for AFK Horin, at the age of three,” he recalled. “Then we moved and I started playing for Melník. When I was six, we played a tournament somewhere, I don't know where exactly, and Slavia Prague scouted me there. So I went to Slavia for a trial and joined them when I was six.” He made rapid progress, and last year signed for Viktoria Plzen from Slovan Liberec. Viktoria’s sporting director, Daniel Kolar, called him “an extraordinary talent, which he confirmed in the Liberec jersey. Despite his still young age, he has already played almost 80 league matches. We have a clear development plan with him and we intend to push his potential even further.” Technically gifted, he has pace and an eye for goal – a useful option this summer. Models himself on Kevin De Bruyne.

players.name: Yahia Fofana
players.bio: “In my all-time African XI, I’d have Yahia Fofana in goal” – those are the words of Fofana’s Côte d’Ivoire head coach, Emerse Faé. It’s a superlative that demonstrates the faith that Faé has in Fofana after just three years working together. The goalkeeper arrived in the international set-up in 2023 and is a product of the Le Havre academy. He was named player of the season at Angers in 2024-25 before moving to Turkey. For Ivorians, his penalty save against Mali in the quarter-finals of the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations, which Côte d’Ivoire won on home soil, is his finest moment. Quick reflexes are the greatest strength of their first-choice goalkeeper.

players.name: Ousmane Diomandé
players.bio: The composed centre-back was a revelation with his interceptions and intelligence in the back four when he arrived in the national setup in 2022, quickly winning a starting spot. He fell down the pecking order during the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations and is now third or fourth-choice in the middle of defence. Nevertheless, he’s a reliable member of the squad and will be ready if called upon in North America. The 22-year-old’s first experience of European football came with Mafra, a third-tier club in Lisbon. Now he’s at the top table in the Portuguese capital with Sporting and has already won the Liga Portugal twice.

players.name: Ghislain Konan
players.bio: Emerse Faé’s preferred option at left-back. Konan had been around the national setup since 2017 before he spent the second half of 2024 without a club and was dropped. But, after six months of little game time at Burgos in Spain, he has played his way back into the Côte d’Ivoire fold during a consistent season at Gil Vicente and was first-choice left-back at the last Afcon. Konan’s qualities are largely defensive, where he is a strong battler in his own third. Faé can count on his experience of playing in five countries on three continents throughout his club career, including spells at Vitória Guimarães, Stade Reims and Al-Nassr.

players.name: Jean Michaël Seri
players.bio: The veteran provides balance to Emerse Faé’s midfield from his position in front of the back four, his experience in a defensive sense just as valuable as his calmness at bringing the ball out from the back. The 34-year-old has played club football across three continents during his career, notably in the Premier League with Fulham. When his dream move from Nice to Barcelona broke down in 2017, he was not best pleased: “I exploded. The walls shook,” he told Mundo Deportivo. “Nice had promised me one thing but they didn’t keep their word and asked for more money.” After winning his first cap in 2015, a year after Côte d’Ivoire’s last World Cup appearance, this tournament is likely to mark the final step on an international career that included the 2023 Afcon title.

players.name: Wilfried Singo
players.bio: Singo is at home among modern full-backs. He’s quick, strong and good on the ball, as effective going forward as he is in his own defensive third. After winning his first Côte d’Ivoire cap in 2021, he spent a year out of international football for personal reasons before a return in 2023. Having missed the last Afcon through injury, he will renew his rivalry with Guela Doué, who impressed in his place in Morocco, for the right-back spot this summer. The 25-year-old can also operate as a centre-back in either a back three or a back four. Singo initially wanted to be a goalkeeper like his father, who played amateur football. “But when I saw my friends scoring goals and all that, I decided to become an outfield player,” he said while at Torino.

players.name: Séko Fofana
players.bio: Fofana and Côte d’Ivoire have not always been a match made in heaven. After switching his allegiance from France in 2017, the box-to-box midfielder was absent from multiple squads in the years that followed until he played a starring role in the team that won the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations on home soil, starting in the final against Nigeria.
