Having lost his starting spot in recent times, playing only a bit-part role at Afcon 2025, the 31-year-old has put together a good enough run of form in the second half of the season at Porto – winning the league title – to warrant a place on the plane.

players.name: Odilon Kossounou
players.bio: The 25-year-old has all the hallmarks of a modern centre-back: tall and strong, assured on the ball and aggressive without it. He has partnered Evan N’Dicka in the Côte d’Ivoire backline since 2024 and it is a rock-solid pairing – Emerse Faé’s side did not concede a single goal in 10 matches in World Cup qualifying. After winning titles in Belgium with Club Brugge and Germany with Bayer Leverkusen, last summer’s permanent move to Atalanta has given him the opportunity to improve on a different side of his game. “In Serie A it is very important to be awake tactically,” he has said.

players.name: Franck Kessié
players.bio: The 29-year-old Côte d’Ivoire captain is arriving at his first World Cup. His first cap came shortly after Brazil 2014, the last time Les Éléphants reached the tournament. Kessié established himself in the national team in 2016 and has been a favourite of every coach since, winning his 100th cap in March. During the celebrations to commemorate this milestone, the president of the Ivorian federation, Idriss Diallo, laid down Kessié’s World Cup challenge: lead the team for at least six games, into the quarter-finals. Ivorian football’s poster boy has two African titles to his name, at Under-17 and senior level, and has won the league in Italy and Spain. He left Barcelona for Saudi Arabia, where he is a double Asian Champions League champion.

players.name: Ange-Yoan Bonny
players.bio: Previously uncapped and only naturalised a few weeks ago, the 22-year-old Inter striker is in contention to lead the line for Côte d’Ivoire at this World Cup. Emerse Faé will be hoping he can fit into his system as a lone striker having played alongside a partner for much of last campaign. Now a Serie A and Coppa Italia winner in his first season at Inter, scoring a handful of goals in all competitions along the way, Bonny knew he had to pass his French school exams to earn his move to Parma in Italy. “My mother threatened me: ‘Listen to me Yoan, if you don’t pass your baccalauréat, I promise you we won’t sign the contract,’” he told France Football. Bonny never knew his father and was brought up by his mother, who bought him his first football shirt: an Inter one, with Samuel Eto’o’s name on the back.

players.name: Simon Adingra
players.bio: Adingra has won his place back after being omitted from Emerse Faé’s squad for Afcon last December. The 24-year-old former Brighton winger is creative, a sharp dribbler and loves taking players on one-on-one. After falling victim to a scam targeting young footballers in Benin as a teenager, he landed in Europe with Nordsjælland. He was named best young player at Côte d’Ivoire’s victorious home Africa Cup of Nations – setting up both goals in the final – but his career has not quite kicked on. An average return to the Premier League with Sunderland ended in January, when he was loaned to Monaco. He is out of contract this summer.

players.name: Yan Diomandé
players.bio: The 19-year-old is certainly one to watch this summer. His form in Germany, where he has added goals to his game in his first season at the elite level, has made him a contender to win Tuttosport’s prestigious Golden Boy award, given to the best player under 21 in Europe. Diomandé is an eye-catching dribbler and has tormented defenders in the Bundesliga since arriving from Leganés last summer for €20m. The big question is: can he handle the pressure of what comes next? If he can inspire Les Éléphants into the World Cup knockout stages and beyond, who’s to say one of Europe’s biggest clubs won’t come in with a mammoth offer?

players.name: Elye Wahi
players.bio: The former France youth international turned down a Côte d’Ivoire call-up three years ago but a senior cap for Les Bleus failed to materialise. After bursting on to the scene in the Champions League with Lens, the striker has had a difficult couple of years with Marseille and Eintracht Frankfurt but he has found his shooting boots again on loan at Nice with seven goals since January, earning him another opportunity with Les Éléphants in March. “The people around me – my agent, my family – didn’t really want me to come to Nice,” Wahi told L’Équipe. “By signing for Nice in January, I was jumping into the wolf’s mouth, but I was sure of my decision.” Mobile and fast, he has the attributes to play as a loan No 9 or alongside a strike partner.

players.name: Christopher Opéri
players.bio: Opéri travels to North America as a late replacement for Clément Akpa, who had to pull out of Emerse Faé’s 26-man squad owing to a thigh injury. Opéri, a 29-year-old left-back, made his international debut two years ago but hasn’t been able to establish himself. He was included in last December’s squad for the Africa Cup of Nations but featured in just one game – the last group game against Gabon. However, he has the sort of attributes you would expect from a modern full-back, knowing how to defend and get forward in equal measure. He can also deliver a high-quality cross into the box or pick a teammate out with a cutback. “There’s a positive pressure,” he has said about playing for Côte d’Ivoire. “With the supporters, you get the sense that there’s a real fervour behind us.” After winning promotion to Ligue 1 with Le Havre, Opéri has been in the Turkish capital since last summer.

players.name: Oumar Diakité
players.bio: Diakité offers something different to most other players in this squad: pure power from a wide position. And he is an important presence off the field too as one of the younger voices in the dressing room who will keep spirits high – a must-have for any national team looking to go deep into a major tournament. His nickname, “Wrou Wrou”, represents his never-say-die attitude on the pitch, a trait which helped him earn a first senior call-up aged 18 and has kept him around the squad since. After backheeling in Côte d’Ivoire’s winning goal against Mali in the 2023 Afcon quarter-final on home soil, Diakité was sent off for a second yellow for taking his shirt off in celebration. He returned from suspension to come off the bench in the final against Nigeria.

players.name: Amad Diallo
players.bio: Amad’s first major tournament with Côte d’Ivoire at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations lived up to the hype. He started four games, winning the player of the match award in three of them, as he led Emerse Faé’s attack from the right wing. The quick-thinking dribbler already carries a lot of the responsibility when it comes to creating and scoring chances. Côte d’Ivoire know the 24-year-old Manchester United forward will have to be at his best if they are going to progress through the rounds this summer. “I’m not trying to be Côte d’Ivoire’s best player,” a humble Amad said during the last Afcon. “I am just trying to do my best.”

players.name: Mohamed Koné
players.bio: Third choice in between the posts, this will be another tournament for the 24-year-old to gain experience in the national set-up. He has been around the senior side since 2024 after impressing for the youth teams but is yet to make an appearance (at the time of writing). With good reflexes and speed off his line, Koné has the attributes to become a regular one day. After going to Afcon in December, he established himself as Charleroi’s No 1 in the final months of the Belgian season. Came through the Le Havre academy and made his professional debut in January 2024.

players.name: Guela Doué
players.bio: Ivorians always know Doué will give everything for the shirt. The former France youth international, who is the older brother of PSG’s Désiré, chose to represent Côte d’Ivoire at Under-23 level. “Since I was little, I always wanted to play for Côte d’Ivoire,” he says. “The orange shirt, the elephant. It’s my homeland. I’m proud to wear this shirt. I was really proud when I told my dad about it.” Emerse Faé’s Swiss army knife, Doué is best at right-back but can more than fill in as a defensive midfielder. As effective going forward as in his defensive work, the 23-year-old showed his worth at his first Afcon in January and this World Cup could open doors for the Strasbourg player.

players.name: Ibrahim Sangaré
players.bio: Called “Little Yaya Touré” when he was younger, the Nottingham Forest man can play in any position in midfield and has formed an effective partnership with Franck Kessié in the national team – it remains to be seen who else will join them in what is likely to be a midfield three. Sangaré’s qualities are largely in the defensive side of the game – tackling and haranguing opponents – but he is a good runner with and without the ball too. The 29-year-old has been part of the national setup since 2019 and acts as a go-between for the old stalwarts and the new generation. After a difficult early spell, including contracting malaria at the 2023 Afcon, Sangaré has settled in England and is one of Forest’s most consistent performers.

players.name: Nicolas Pépé
players.bio: “The World Cup is a childhood dream,” Pépé said recently. “It’s the biggest competition in world football. I’m 30 now and I think this will be my only chance to play in one because there’s no guarantee I’ll be in the national team by the time I’m 34.” After playing at four Afcons and failing to qualify for two World Cups, the winger faces the challenge of his life. The skilled dribbler remains one of the key players of this Côte d’Ivoire side – and one of the loudest voices. A big presence in the dressing room, Pépé often shares videos of his teammates on social media. The former Arsenal winger is serious about playing for the national team too: “I have given my head for this team by shaving my Mohican since Under-17 level,” he said, a way of showing his respect for the badge.

players.name: Emmanuel Agbadou
players.bio: The former Wolves defender has more power, physicality and technical ability than the average centre-back. Agbadou is the pastor of the national team and often carries messages about his relationship with God on a T-shirt under his matchday shirt. “Badobre” is his nickname - it means ‘buffalo’ in Agbadou's native language, Bété - and he is still patiently awaiting his big breakthrough with the national team. His first cap came in 2020 but he is yet to establish himself as a starter and watched the 2023 Afcon triumph from home. This World Cup is an opportunity for him to become an international regular and build on an impressive second half of the season in Turkey.

players.name: Evan Ndicka
players.bio: Ndicka had his pick of three national teams at the start of his career. Born in France to a Cameroonian father and an Ivorian mother, he eventually chose to represent Côte d’Ivoire in 2023 after playing for France all the way from Under-15 to Under-23 level. After making his first steps in football in the Parisian suburbs, the defender signed for Auxerre at 19. Reliable and reserved, on and off the pitch, Ndicka is a born leader. Known as the ‘minister of defence’, he has brought solidity to the Côte d’Ivoire backline thanks to his consistency. The only left-footed centre-back in the Ivorian squad is attracting interest from big clubs and will be in the shop window in North America this summer.

players.name: Evann Guessand
players.bio: Guessand is often labelled as Faé’s teacher’s pet by the Ivorian press. The pair first worked together when Faé was his coach with Nice’s Under-19s. Guessand earned his first Côte d’Ivoire call-up in 2024 and has become one of their first-choice attacking players. A quick, powerful forward who can play out wide or through the middle, he will have a battle on his hands to secure a starting place this summer. He did not hit the ground running after his €35m move to Aston Villa last summer and did not make much of an impact on loan at Crystal Palace since January.

players.name: Alban Lafont
players.bio: Lafont’s first call-up last year came as a surprise to most because his links to Côte d’Ivoire were not well known. The goalkeeper had come close to a senior France call-up and could also have played for Burkina Faso, the country of his birth. Lafont’s mother, who represented Burkina Faso in handball and later became a member of parliament, had an Ivorian father. The 27-year-old joined up with Côte d’Ivoire as the No 2 but is now pushing Yahia Fofana hard for the starting spot. At 1.96m Lafont is confident when coming to claim crosses and his frame covers the goal well. Has already had a long and successful career with almost 300 Ligue 1 appearances with Nantes and Toulouse, winning the French Cup. He spent the 2025-26 season on loan in Greece.

players.name: Bazoumana Touré
players.bio: Côte d’Ivoire’s “TGV” (high-speed train) is the second-youngest player on the plane to North America. The Hoffenheim forward is an old-fashioned winger – he is left-footed and plays off the left – with his main aim to set up teammates rather than shoot himself. The 20-year-old is mature for his age and capable of pulling the strings in midfield too. Pace is his main asset and that makes him a dangerous proposition off the bench against tiring defenders. “Bazou” may come across club teammate Oliver Baumann in the Germany goal during the group stage (although chances of that receded when Manuel Neuer was called up). “He’s a club legend and like a mentor. He helps me with everything,” Touré said recently.

players.name: Parfait Guiagon
players.bio: One of the squad’s creative sparks, Guiagon’s favoured position is in a traditional No 10 role, where he has the freedom to operate infield or drift out to the left wing. A silky dribbler who is most dangerous in gaps between the lines, the 25-year-old has the quality to dictate games when Côte d’Ivoire have more of the ball. After a series of trials – including at Barcelona and Monaco – he spent his first few years in Europe in Israel, most notably performing for Maccabi Tel Aviv, and now plays in Belgium. Called up by Emerse Faé for the first time last year, he is one of those going to their first major tournament this summer. Included in the Guardian’s Next Generation series as a 17-year-old in 2018.

players.name: Christ Inao Oulaï
players.bio: Inao Oulaï was bullish after being handed his first senior call-up aged 19 last November. “If I manage to set foot on the pitch, I think the Ivorians will remember my name because I’m not going to think much about it: I’m just going to throw myself into it,” he said. This sums up exactly what Inao brings to the national team: risk-taking, energy and, most of all, not a care in the world. The midfielder is the face of the next generation of Côte d’Ivoire footballers and went to his first major tournament at Afcon in January. He’s a contender to be the revelation of this World Cup, both within his national team and more widely.

players.name: Lionel Mpasi
players.bio: Mpasi is a curious paradox: a backup goalkeeper at a Ligue 1 club fighting for survival yet an undisputed starter – and often outstanding – for DR Congo. A late bloomer, Mpasi came through at Paris St-Germain, but he did not truly emerge until he played in France’s second division with Rodez. French-born, he had to wait until his mid-20s to establish himself as a starter. His defining moment came at the 2024 Africa Cup of Nations when he converted a decisive penalty against Egypt, smiling as he walked up, a moment that elevated him to cult status among Congolese fans. "My parents are from DR Congo, and I still have family there. I've never had the chance to visit, but I'd love to," he told Leopardsfoot in 2021 before his first cap. Like a few dual nationals, he discovered his connection to the country through football and has since become one of its most beloved figures.

players.name: Aaron Wan-Bissaka
players.bio: For Congolese fans, the return of “The Spider” was a decade in the making. Initially called up by DR Congo at youth level in 2015 while still a winger, he experienced a heavy 8–0 defeat against England. That - and the lack of structure at the time - pushed him toward switching allegiance to the country he grew up in. As his career took off with Crystal Palace and Manchester United, he repeatedly turned down DR Congo, hoping for a call from Gareth Southgate that never came. With the national project improving and England’s depth at right-back growing, Wan-Bissaka eventually made the switch. "I wanted to join the national team when I was ready,” he told BBC Africa. “Only I know when I am ready and not when others decide.” Since earning his first cap in September 2025, his performances have quickly silenced those who once questioned his late commitment.

players.name: Steve Kapuadi
players.bio: Born in France, developed in Belgium, launched in Slovakia, revealed in Poland and eventually capped by DR Congo – Kapuadi has taken the long road. The towering centre-back established himself at Legia Warsaw, where strong performances in the Conference League helped him achieve a personal goal: representing his father’s country. But it was back in 2015,  during the Africa Cup of Nations, that the dream truly began. Watching the Leopards, led by Yannick Bolasie, return to Kinshasa with a bronze medal left a lasting impression on him. “That’s when something clicked,” he told Leopardsfoot. “Seeing the players come back to Kinshasa with the medal was inspiring and beautiful to watch.” At 1.96m, Kapuadi brings size, aggression and aerial dominance to the squad. Though only fourth-choice at centre-back, his left-footedness offers variety while his cheerful personality has made him popular within the group.

players.name: Axel Tuanzebe
players.bio: One unconventional goal in the 99th minute against Jamaica was enough to end 52 years of waiting for an entire nation. “Chancel told me to attack the ball on the corner,” Tuanzebe smiled afterwards. “I scored with my stomach, but that’s not my problem. The game is over and we’re qualified.” Born in Ituri, the defender instantly became a symbol of pride in his conflict-scarred home region after sending DR Congo to the World Cup. A former Manchester United academy captain, Tuanzebe once looked destined for a Premier League career but recurring injuries stalled his progress. Elegant on the ball, modern in his defensive approach and naturally charismatic, he hesitated for years before committing to DR Congo. Since making the switch, however, he has become indispensable. In his first 11 caps, DR Congo lost only once: against Algeria at Afcon. Nearly lost his thumb in 2024 after a freak accident in the kitchen while doing the washing up.

players.name: Dylan Batubinsika
players.bio: Unapologetically old-school, Batubinsika provides depth behind the preferred defensive pairing of Chancel Mbemba and Axel Tuanzebe. Trained at Paris St-Germain and a former France youth international, Batubinsika long kept the idea of representing DR Congo in the back of his mind before finally committing in 2023. “I always thought about it, but the call from coach Sébastien Desabre at the beginning of 2023 was decisive,” he explained. “He has played a huge role in the team’s recent progress. Everything felt structured, so I knew it was the right moment to begin my international career.” Over a dozen caps later, he has become a regular in Desabre’s squad. Comfortable in physical battles and dominant in the air, Batubinsika may not be the flashiest defender in the group, but his reliability and experience make him an important option.

players.name: Ngal’ayel Mukau
players.bio: Like his close friend Noah Sadiki, Antwerp-born Mukau chose to represent the land of his ancestors long before reaching senior level, committing to DR Congo as early as the Under-21s. “It’s a source of pride for me and my family,” he said after his first youth appearances. “Wearing this jersey means a lot to me, and I want to keep wearing it in the future.” For Belgian football, his decision hurt even more because, in terms of pure talent, Mukau may already be one of the most gifted players in the Leopards squad. Tall yet elegant, blessed with a refined left foot and natural flair, he belongs to the category of players people simply enjoy watching. A modern box-to-box midfielder capable of recovering possession, progressing with the ball and eliminating opponents, he has reportedly attracted interest from Barcelona. Reserved in French – his first language is Flemish – Mukau remains a fan favourite among Congolese supporters.

players.name: Nathanaël Mbuku
players.bio: His youthful face, infectious energy and deep love for Congolese music have made Mbuku a fan favourite. Despite his small frame, his excellent balance and low centre of gravity make him extremely difficult for defenders to contain. “My role is to bring freshness, youth and enthusiasm – and to help the team keep progressing,” he said before the playoff against Jamaica. Still only 24, Mbuku has yet to fully translate his potential into consistency at club level. But the former France youth international has found a real place within the national setup, whether as a starter or an impact substitute. Blessed with a dangerous left foot and natural unpredictability, he is often given freedom by Sébastien Desabre to drift between the lines.

players.name: Samuel Moutoussamy
players.bio: He never scores, never assists, and yet few players are more essential to DR Congo than Moutoussamy. The most-used outfield player under Sébastien Desabre, the 29-year-old is the foundation of the Leopards’ midfield. Tireless and disciplined, he presses, harasses, intercepts and recycles possession for 90 minutes – or longer if needed. Not always spectacular, but consistently reliable, Moutoussamy’s work rate and fighting spirit set the tone for the entire team. Born to a Caribbean father and a Congolese mother, he has become one of the symbols of a generation that experienced repeated heartbreak before finally reaching the World Cup. “We’ll leave our souls on the pitch to get the win,” he told Leopardsfoot before the playoff against Jamaica. “We can’t play with fear. We have to attack, take risks and give everything we have.” That warrior mentality is exactly what DR Congo hope to carry into the World Cup.

players.name: Brian Cipenga
players.bio: A year ago, very few Congolese supporters even knew who Cipenga was. Some openly complained when Sébastien Desabre surprisingly called up the little-known winger from Spain’s second tier for the World Cup playoffs. Without him, however, DR Congo might never have qualified. Introduced against Cameroon, Cipenga immediately changed the game, exhausting defenders with his quick feet before delivering the corner that led to Chancel Mbemba’s decisive goal. He repeated the same trick against Jamaica, this time assisting Axel Tuanzebe’s historic winner. “My country also has a right to happiness,” he posted on Instagram after qualification. “After 52 years, God has blessed us to go to the World Cup.” Born and raised in Kinshasa to a family originally from Kisangani in the north of the country, he now looks set to become DR Congo’s luxury supersub.

players.name: Théo Bongonda
players.bio: Once considered a future Belgian international, Bongonda finally became a Leopard in 2022 – and Congolese fans embraced him immediately. “I came through the Belgian system, but when I saw that Congo was more concrete than Belgium, and showed more love and desire to have me, I told myself you should always go where people truly want you,” he said. Technically gifted, explosive in one-on-one situations and capable of slicing through defences with his left foot, Bongonda quickly became one of the most exciting players in the squad. His dribbling ability and low centre of gravity have earned him an inevitable comparison among Congolese supporters: “Théo Messi.” It is a nickname likely to echo loudly again this summer if he produces moments of magic on the World Cup stage.

players.name: Gaël Kakuta
players.bio: Some Chelsea supporters may remember Kakuta as one of the club’s lost wonderkids. Kakuta, who made his Chelsea debut under Carlo Ancelotti in 2009, was viewed as one of the brightest talents of his generation – a gifted playmaker whose career never quite reached the heights many predicted. “Hatem Ben Arfa and Gaël Kakuta are the two greatest dribblers I’ve seen play, apart from Messi and Neymar,” Eden Hazard once said. “Maybe they didn’t always make the right career choices, maybe there are regrets somewhere, but they are players who made me dream.” Now at the 15th club of his career, Kakuta never truly found stability. The explosive legs of his early 20s are gone, but his left foot still carries magic. Not a guaranteed starter anymore, he remains capable of producing moments nobody else in the squad can see.

players.name: Joris Kayembe
players.bio: An attack-minded left-back, Kayembe plays with flair, risk and a clear taste for dribbling. His adventurous style can sometimes leave space behind him, but Congolese fans appreciate the excitement he brings. Called up relatively late at international level at 29, he has mostly operated as Masuaku’s understudy. Yet thanks to the latter’s recurring physical issues, Kayembe has accumulated more than 20 caps in just two years. “I chose Congo with my heart, not for sporting reasons,” he said after joining the Leopards setup. “The coach called me at the right moment. Congolese football is restructuring, the working environment is good and competition for places is strong.” A deeply religious Christian, Kayembe often speaks about putting God at the centre of his life – something he says helped him overcome the serious injuries that slowed parts of his career.

players.name: Meschack Elia
players.bio: Elia developed the explosive pace that now terrifies defenders on the dusty pitches of Nsele, a semi-rural commune located nearly 90 minutes from central Kinshasa. His rise began during the 2016 African Nations Championship, which DR Congo won, before spells with TP Mazembe and eventually a move to Switzerland in 2020. At Young Boys, Elia won four league titles and was named the best player in the Swiss league in 2023. Despite his achievements, he has often been unfairly criticised by sections of the Congolese public – something that still fuels him. “I was too emotional,” he admitted after qualification. “We were insulted too much after failing to qualify in 2022. But I always said that before retiring, I had to play in a World Cup.” His story took a tragic turn a year and a half ago following the death of his four-year-old son, news that deeply affected the entire country.

players.name: Noah Sadiki
players.bio: By choosing DR Congo before the age of 20, Sadiki sent a strong message to a generation of dual nationals still hesitating. Had he opted differently, the midfielder – who grew up in Belgium – would likely have been part of the World Cup with the Red Devils anyway. But his early commitment to Congo has made him a fan favourite. Nicknamed the “national cadet” by supporters, he is already seen as a future captain, admired for his maturity and leadership despite his young age. A versatile midfielder, Sadiki operates as a dynamic box-to-box presence for club and country. After a promising breakthrough season in the Premier League with Sunderland, the World Cup offers him the perfect stage to further elevate his profile.

players.name: Timothy Fayulu
players.bio: First called up in 2019 while emerging as one of the most promising goalkeepers in Switzerland, Fayulu was initially seen as the future of DR Congo. But he suddenly turned his back on the Leopards after accepting a Switzerland Under-20 call-up instead. He even went live on Instagram to defend his decision. Humiliated, DR Congo fans branded him a traitor. As his international prospects with Switzerland faded, Sébastien Desabre surprisingly recalled him four years later as a third-choice goalkeeper despite lingering hostility from supporters. Then came November’s Caf playoff against Nigeria. Thrown on specifically for the penalty shootout, he produced two decisive saves to send DR Congo to the intercontinental playoffs. Carried by his teammates after the match, the goalkeeper — who spent last season on loan in Armenia — completed one of the most unexpected redemption arcs. “Whether I play or not, when my country calls me, I’m always ready,” he said during Afcon.

players.name: Cédric Bakambu
players.bio: Few players looked more emotional than Bakambu after the final whistle against Jamaica. When he chose to represent DR Congo in 2015 – during his peak years at Villarreal – he instantly became one of the country’s biggest stars. “Through football, I was able to reconnect with my roots,” he said. “When I arrived for my first call-up, I already knew there was excitement around the national team on social media. But the emotions I experienced in Kinshasa were on another level.” Since then, Bakambu has become far more than just the leader of the attack. He evolved into one of the main faces and ambassadors of the Leopards project. Behind the scenes, he also played a key role in convincing dual-national players to join DR Congo. After a decade marked by goals, heartbreak and near misses, Bakambu will finally play in his first World Cup at the age of 35 – and likely as a starter.

players.name: Charles Pickel
players.bio: Born and raised in Switzerland’s highly structured environment, Pickel was initially shocked by the organisational chaos during his first DR Congo call-up in 2021. For a while, it genuinely made him reconsider his international future. Two years later, however, he returned – and established himself as one of the most important figures in the Leopards midfield. “I almost never came back,” he joked upon his return. An energetic box-to-box midfielder with relentless stamina, Pickel lives with two very different personalities. Off the pitch, he is calm, friendly and constantly smiling. On it, he transforms into an aggressive, confrontational competitor who never backs down from a duel – the type of player who will happily go forehead-to-forehead with an opponent if needed.

players.name: Fiston Mayele
players.bio: Born in Mbuji-Mayi, the diamond-rich city in south-central Congo, Mayele is one of the rare representatives of what Congolese people often call the “deep Congo” within the national team – players who were developed locally rather than abroad. Aggressive and blessed with a striker’s instinct, Mayele combines precise finishing with strong ball control and relentless intensity. After breaking through in DR Congo, he dominated the Tanzanian league to such an extent that he still enjoys cult status there. His move to Egypt with Pyramids elevated him further, culminating in the club’s historic Caf Champions League triumph in 2025, when he was named player of the year in African club football. Despite the growing recognition, Mayele remains known for his humility and work ethic. “When you start rising, people naturally talk about you,” he says. “What matters is staying focused, continuing to improve, and showing more every time. I remain who I am – humble and simple.”

players.name: Yoane Wissa
players.bio: Congolese federation on Facebook hoping for one thing: a chance to play for his country. Thirteen years later, his success in the Premier League has made him one of the greatest ambassadors of Congolese football. Accessible, humble and down to earth, Wissa’s journey has been anything but conventional. Before becoming a striker, he was a goalkeeper. His first season at Newcastle was disrupted by an injury suffered on international duty, preventing him from reaching the heights many expected after his form at Brentford, where he became the first DR Congo player to score more than 10 goals in a Premier League season. “The truth is, I’m not the most talented guy,” he once admitted. “What pushes me forward is that I love working hard – and I guess I’m not doing too badly.” Loved by Congolese supporters, who affectionately nickname him “Kovo” (“the bald one”), Wissa will carry huge expectations heading into the World Cup.

players.name: Matthieu Epolo
players.bio: Being a starting goalkeeper in a top division at 19 is already rare. Being captain at that age says even more. Epolo’s rise at Standard Liège has been remarkably fast. Blessed with sharp reflexes, strong anticipation and unusual maturity for his age, Epolo represents a new generation of Belgian-Congolese dual nationals who chose DR Congo early rather than waiting for Belgium. “From the beginning, I knew I would choose Congo,” he told Le Soir. “I just had questions because, unlike the rest of my family, I had never been there.” One of the people who helped convince him was his childhood friend Noah Sadiki. If he fulfills his potential, Epolo is expected to become DR Congo’s long-term No 1. His main area for improvement remains his distribution – one of the few weaknesses in an otherwise promising profile.

players.name: Chancel Mbemba
players.bio: “Boulot, palais.” (‘’Work, house’’ in Congolese-French slang), the phrase Mbemba used in an interview to describe his lifestyle has since become his nickname in DR Congo. It reflects the discipline that defines him. Previously dubbed “the Chancellor” or even “demi-God” by supporters, Mbemba commands immense respect in his country. With over 100 caps and the captain’s armband, he is the undisputed leader of the Leopards. Strong in one-on-one situations, comfortable on the ball and quite effective in front of goal – a reminder of his early days as a striker – he is the backbone of the team. Born into a poor family of eight children in Kinshasa, Mbemba is a rare success story in a country where thousands of talents go unnoticed every year due to a lack of structure. Trained in the country, then developed at Anderlecht, he built a solid European career, including a spell with Newcastle. His journey could have reached even greater heights were it not for long-standing doubts surrounding his age – a controversy that has followed him despite his consistency at the highest level.

players.name: Simon Banza
players.bio: Twenty-three goals in Portugal. Twenty-two in Turkey the following season. Banza’s scoring record suggests he probably deserved a bigger spotlight. Instead, the former Lens academy striker is now in the United Arab Emirates, quietly turning chances into goals wherever he goes. A hard-working centre-forward with intelligent movement and strong hold-up play, Banza has built his reputation on consistency at club level. Yet with DR Congo, things have been more complicated. Despite regular call-ups, he has scored only twice at the time of writing. He is also known for celebrating goals with the famous smile of the Joker – a direct reference to Joaquin Phoenix’s character. “It’s a film with a really beautiful and intelligent message,” he has said.

players.name: Gédéon Kalulu
players.bio: Before Aaron Wan-Bissaka’s arrival, Kalulu had established himself as one of DR Congo’s most reliable right-backs. Kalulu has since stepped aside without complaint – and has continued to deliver whenever called upon. A product of Lyon’s academy, he comes from a football-mad family. He is the younger brother of Aldo Kalulu (formerly of Swansea), the older brother of Pierre Kalulu (Juventus) and Joseph Kalulu (Pau FC). Four professional footballers from the same household. “It’s always a pleasure to talk about my brothers,” he said in 2022. “It’s rare in football, so naturally people speak about it.” Technically very adept, Kalulu has just returned from a serious knee injury.

players.name: Edo Kayembe
players.bio: No relation to Joris Kayembe, Edo was born in the Kasai region of central Congo where the surname is common. One of the few genuinely homegrown players in a squad largely built around the diaspora, Kayembe’s journey carries particular symbolic weight for Congolese supporters. “I’m very happy to have gone from my country to the Premier League in just five years. It’s a great challenge,” he said when signing for Watford in 2022. Quiet and reserved off the pitch, Kayembe transforms once the game starts. Hard-working and equipped with a fierce long shot, he has become one of Sébastien Desabre’s trusted midfield options. He may not be the most gifted player in the squad but his versatility, physical commitment and rise from the Congolese league to English football have earned widespread admiration.

players.name: Arthur Masuaku
players.bio: Congolese fans have a very particular relationship with Masuaku, nicknamed “Le Têtu National” – roughly, “the nation’s stubborn man.” The former West Ham defender rarely smiles and his strong personality has long divided opinion. But behind the stern expression lies one of the most technically gifted players in the squad. Tricks, composure, unexpected passes and occasionally moments of brilliance, like the outrageous free-kick he scored at Afcon 2023. In 2018, Masuaku famously walked out of a DR Congo camp alongside Gaël Kakuta, frustrated by poor organisation around the team. “I have a problem with people who justify these issues by saying: ‘That’s just how Congo is.’ Are we forced to stay mediocre?” he later said. Years later, he acknowledged the progress made around the Leopards setup: “Today, it feels good that people finally see us as a serious nation.”

players.name: Hernán Galíndez
players.bio: After being released as a youngster by Rosario Central, his hometown club in Argentina, Galíndez rebuilt his life in Quito and embraced Ecuador as his new home and identity. Now, with more than 300 domestic matches played and unforgettable moments such as sealing World Cup qualification against Argentina, he stands as a national icon, but he doesn’t like to boast. “I’m not a superhero, I’m just a football player and nothing more,” he says. As a seven-year-old in Rosario, testing himself out as a keeper for the first time after the regular No1 was injured, he came up against a lively young rival. “I played in this tournament and only conceded one goal. To this day, my mom has a plaque from that day'. That goal was scored by a certain Lionel Messi.” Now, at 38, Galíndez is a figurehead for the side.

players.name: Félix Torres
players.bio: Torres surprised everyone with his progress. A skinny boy from Esmeraldas, he didn’t look like an obvious future dominant international centre-back. But Daniel Peña, a club coach on a scouting mission in 2015 to check a different player training for Alianza del Pailón noticed Torres instead, and signed him up. Peña reflected in 2023: “This boy had good ball control, good positioning. He made difficult things look easy. The downside was that he didn't know how to head the ball and he was very skinny. But these days his physique and his heading are his greatest strengths.” A proper leader, he made his international debut in 2019 and brings maturity and work ethic to the back line.

players.name: Piero Hincapié
players.bio: One of the most complete Ecuadorian defenders of his generation, known for tactical intelligence, clean ball distribution and versatility as a centre-back and left-back. Educated in the Independiente del Valle youth system, he moved to Talleres before shining at Bayer Leverkusen, and joined Arsenal on an initial loan deal last year. “It only took one conversation to make me feel sure about the decision that I was taking to come here. I'm over the moon …  I want to win trophies for this club, to make a name for myself, and to leave a mark.” A full international from a young age, he has already played in a World Cup and the Copa América, and is developing into a real leader at the back.

players.name: Joel Ordóñez
players.bio: Ordóñez is a towering centre-back who has become a pillar at Club Brugge, known for his maturity and defensive intelligence. Born in Guayaquil and raised in the Independiente del Valle youth system, he moved to Belgium in 2022 and steadily built his reputation, growing from a raw prospect into a reliable starter and international option for Ecuador. His rise reflects focus, patience, and consistency. One of his early coaches said it could have been a different career trajectory though, recalling his boyhood desire to be the star man up front, to “be Enner Valencia … to be our Balotelli.” His future at the back though looks extremely bright at club and international level. He has the mentality to become one of the top Ecuadorian defenders of his generation, and has been repeatedly linked with a Premier League move.

players.name: Jordy Alcívar
players.bio: A balanced and intelligent midfielder, Alcívar has become a key figure at Independiente del Valle as a central pivot and organiser. Born in Manta, he stands out for his defensive solidity, clean ball distribution, tactical discipline, and versatility across the line. Developed at LDU Quito, where he was known by the nickname “La Bochorna 44” (“We played Free Fire [a shooter game] online and I needed a nickname. I chose that one and they’ve been calling me that ever since”), he gained international exposure with Charlotte FC in MLS before returning to Ecuador in 2023, where he has since lifted major trophies, including the 2025 Serie A and Copa Ecuador. A full international since 2021.

players.name: Willian Pacho
players.bio: From the humble streets of Quinindé, Pacho rose meteorically from Independiente del Valle to conquer Europe, becoming the first Ecuadorian to win the Champions League with PSG's historic 5-0 thrashing of Inter last year. He’s known for being humble and respectful, qualities he says were instilled early on. “My mum always instilled in me that you have to respect people always, be kind on and off the pitch … I've always said that, above all things, you have to respect everyone.” She died when she was 51 and his No 51 shirt honours her memory. Also watch out for his trademark wrist bandage – a lucky charm from a hand injury sustained at Antwerp. “I fractured my hand, then I had to bandage it, and right after I scored a goal against Australia. Since then I’ve always used the bandage, because it feels good.” His first senior Ecuador call-up shocked him, as he had not made any appearances at youth level. “I went straight to the first senior call, it was incredible, I didn't expect it … I called my family, told them and they all screamed, they were so happy.” Similarly, when he joined PSG from Eintracht Frankfurt in 2024: “I called my sisters, and they cried a lot.”

players.name: Pervis Estupiñán
players.bio: The explosive left-back arrived at Milan last year in a landmark deal, becoming the first Ecuadorian to wear the Rossoneri shirt. It has been quite a journey. As a boy he combined football with helping on his mum’s meatball stand selling empanadas. His football career began properly at LDU Quito in 2015, taking him via Watford, Granada, Almería, Mallorca, Osasuna, Villarreal and Brighton before arriving at the San Siro. Speaking at his unveiling he said he had “received so many messages from Ecuador. Everyone is claiming they’ve been Milan fans since birth, just because I’m here. I just hope they all buy the shirt.” His early club performances drew criticism, but even under pressure, Estupiñán remains mentally strong. He brings plenty of know-how to this summer’s squad.

players.name: Anthony Valencia
players.bio: Like Jackson Porozo, Valencia did his chances of being included in the final 26-man squad no harm by finding the net in Ecuador’s friendly victory against Saudi Arabia the day before the announcement. The 22-year-old showed his striker’s instinct to score his first international goal, prodding a first-time shot past the goalkeeper in New Jersey. In a post-match interview Valencia could not stop smiling and thanked his mother watching at home. “She’s not here but I’m sure she’s very happy,” he said. The attacker has spent almost his entire senior career at Royal Antwerp, playing a bit-part role as the club won their first Belgian league title in 66 years in 2023. Not a prolific goalscorer by any means, Valencia is comfortable playing behind the main striker or even as a right wing-back.

players.name: John Yeboah
players.bio: A versatile forward, Yeboah was born in Hamburg and was eligible for Germany and, through his parents, Ghana and Ecuador. He played at youth level for Germany from the Under-16s to Under-20s, but ultimately chose Ecuador in 2023. Comfortable as a winger, second striker or attacking midfielder, he is known for agility, close control, sharp movement between lines, and ability to unbalance defenders in one-on-one situations. His career path through Germany, the Netherlands, Poland, and now Italy with Venezia has shaped a resilient, adaptable footballer with a strong mentality. Talking in January about his decision to choose Ecuador he said: “Germany is a nation that has given me so many opportunities both in football and in life, but my heart and soul obviously belong to Ecuador. I'm ready to give my all for the national team.”

players.name: Kendry Páez
players.bio: Ecuador’s generational talent, a left-footed prodigy full of creativity and fearlessness. Developed at Independiente del Valle, he made his debut in the top flight at just 15 and quickly drew global interest; Borussia Dortmund and Manchester United were linked before he agreed a £17m move to Chelsea in 2023. After a development spell in France with Strasbourg he joined River Plate this year. He has been a senior international since September 2023 when he made his debut aged 16 years and 131 days in a World Cup qualifier against Uruguay – 11 days short of beating Diego Maradona’s record of being the youngest male player to play for a South American national team. A month later he scored against Bolivia, becoming the youngest ever Conmebol World Cup qualifiers goalscorer. In January, Chelsea coach Liam Rosenior called him “a kid who has a gift … He's an ultra-talented player”, while teammate Moisés Caicedo said: “Everyone knows that he is great and if he does his part and dedicates himself every day, in training and outside, he will be a total great. The talent he has is incredible."

players.name: Kevin Rodríguez
players.bio: Emerging from Imbabura in Ecuador’s Serie B, Rodríguez’s rapid rise surprised fans, with his call-up to the 2022 World Cup squad as a second-tier player heavily criticised. But that marked a gear shift in his career: soon afterwards he signed for Independiente del Valle, and made a quick impression on overseas scouts with his height and strength. Known as “La Rola,” he moved to Belgium with Union Saint-Gilloise in 2023, making his debut in a Europa League group game against Toulouse. He struggled for goals at first, but hit form just at the right time to secure his place in this summer’s World Cup squad.

players.name: Moisés Ramírez
players.bio: With sharp reflexes, calm distribution and a strong mentality, Ramírez made a quick start to his career, winning major titles and national call-ups at a young age. But his early career wasn’t just defined by trophies; he was also shaped by pressure and criticism after some high-profile mistakes, forcing him to grow up fast. His overseas loan from Independiente del Valle to Real Sociedad in 2019 helped him develop and become more resilient. He said: “The criticism made me stronger personally, I matured and made better decisions.” He now plays his club football in Greece with AE Kifisia.

players.name: Enner Valencia
players.bio: Known as Superman for a reason. His nation’s all-time leading scorer came into this tournament having scored a record six World Cup goals for Ecuador, and is still going strong at 36 after rejoining Pachuca, where he once lifted the Liga MX Golden Boot. From sleeping in Emelec's stadium as a kid and raising the money for his first pair of boots by milking cows on his dad’s farm to shining at West Ham, Everton, Tigres, Fenerbahçe, Internacional, and now back in Mexico, his journey has been full of drama, and a little chaos, too. During a notorious 2016 World Cup qualifier against Chile in Quito he was playing with an arrest warrant looming over him due to a dispute over child maintenance payments, and noticed police loitering on the touchline to grab him at the final whistle. So, with 82 minutes on the clock, he collapsed, was taken off on a stretcher wearing an oxygen mask and rushed to an ambulance, which was then chased by the group of police officers around the running track. He escaped to a clinic; the dispute was later resolved.

players.name: Alan Minda
players.bio: An explosive winger, Minda is a product of the Independiente del Valle academy, rising through youth football with early experiences in LigaPro, the Copa Libertadores, a South American Under-20 Championship, and a Under-20 World Cup, on the way to becoming one of Ecuador’s brightest attacking hopes. Known for his dribbling, acceleration, and ability to break defensive lines, he found the net in the Copa América and World Cup qualifiers. Now continuing his development at Atlético Mineiro after signing a five-year deal in January, Minda is fearless and decisive in transitions. He said previously his mindset is to “have full confidence, and that I shouldn’t be afraid to make mistakes … I follow the dream I’ve had since I was a kid: to play, move overseas, and help my family.”

players.name: Pedro Vite
players.bio: A talented left-footed creator. He made his competitive debut in 2019 in Serie B with Independiente del Valle’s reserves, Independiente Juniors, before his senior Serie A debut in 2021. In August that year, aged 19, he made the leap to MLS with Vancouver Whitecaps, where his progress – in terms of consistency, maturity and leadership – was rapid. Last year he moved to Mexico with Pumas UNAM. He has spoken openly about his struggles with homesickness as a youngster, and about mental health more broadly. “When I went to Quito, to Independiente, it was difficult because I was very close to my mum. I left when I was 12, and I cried until I was 14 or 15. I couldn't adapt to Quito. I liked my teammates, but I missed being with my mum. Our voices would break when we could finally speak! I suffered, and I've had a psychologist since I was 12. People need to change how they see that in their minds; they think that if you have one it's because you're unwell or crazy, but a psychologist helps you grow as a person."

players.name: Jordy Caicedo
players.bio: A classic No 9, Caicedo brings power, presence, a scoring instinct and strong heading ability. In 2019 he spoke about his first steps in football, growing up in the humble neighbourhood of Nuevo Pilo in Machala. “It was economically poor there, but there were many boys and we didn't see the poverty. We used to play street football in neighborhoods with rich kids, we bet money and we always won. We were the best. We used the money to buy bread and soda.” When he wasn’t playing he was helping his dad keep the family afloat. “My father sold coconut and tamarind juice, as well as coconut candy, on the street and on the beach, and I helped him." That stopped when, at 13, Caicedo was scouted at his local club. At 17 he turned professional with Universidad Católica de Quito. His career since has taken in spells in Brazil, Bulgaria, Mexico, Turkey and Spain, and he moved to Argentina with Huracán last year.

players.name: Ángelo Preciado
players.bio: Preciado, raised in the Independiente del Valle academy, came through a difficult childhood to make it this far. When he was 16, his father, who had closely guided his son’s development, was jailed. The shock made Preciado even more determined, and he has since grown into a key figure for Ecuador. He helped secure his dad’s freedom, and told him: “Now you can see that little boy you had by your side taking part in the greatest tournament in the world.” He may be best known to some for an incident in February 2018 when he went viral online after TV footage showed him using a corner flag to defend himself during a mass brawl in the Under-20 Copa Libertadores.

players.name: Denil Castillo
players.bio: A resilient presence in the heart of midfield, Castillo has developed hugely since moving to Denmark with Midtjylland in 2024, emerging as one of the club’s most promising young prospects. He left home at a very young age to chase his dream of making it in professional football, with his career path taking him from Ukraine’s Shakhtar Donetsk during wartime conditions to a brief spell at Partizan Belgrade on the way to Denmark, where he says he feels safe, settled, and confident again. Calm on the ball, tactically intelligent, and mentally strong, Castillo carries his story with pride, recalling his time in Ukraine: “You could hear the bombs every day, but I had to stay focused and keep going.”

players.name: Gonzalo Plata
players.bio: An explosive wide player who can be a real threat from the right wing or as an inside forward. He started out at Independiente del Valle, where he credits coach Juan Carlos León with changing his mindset. “I was very argumentative as a child, I would get annoyed by everything on the field. I would get sent off because the opposing players would hit me or insult me, and I would fall for their provocation. I was also very lazy, I didn't like to train much, I didn't like to run, I didn't like the physical aspect. He made me change that way of being, that way of playing, to put much more pressure on myself.” His club career journey has taken him via Portugal’s Sporting, Spain’s Real Valladolid and Qatar’s Al Sadd to Brazil with Flamengo since 2024. It hasn’t always been smooth progress, though: in 2021 Valladolid issued a statement “deploring” his behaviour after he was involved in an early-hours traffic accident. Plata said: “There’s no justification for what happened. It was a bad act on my part. I let myself get carried away with nightlife.”

players.name: Nilson Angulo
players.bio: Angulo’s senior career started in 2021 with LDU Quito, where his talent quickly drew attention from overseas scouts. He moved to Belgium’s Anderlecht in 2022 on a five-year deal, and his consistently productive displays in their first team earned links with a host of Premier League clubs before Sunderland signed him for £17.5m in the last winter window. The club’s then-sporting director, Kristjaan Speakman, called Angulo “an incredibly exciting prospect, quick and creative [who] excels in all the core actions required to impact the final third”. Angulo himself said: “My development as a player in Anderlecht was great, but I hope to keep improving even more in Sunderland. I believe in my abilities.” He grew up watching Neymar skills videos. “My idol is Neymar. When I played with my friends, I always tried to imitate him.”

players.name: Alan Franco
players.bio: A strong, versatile midfield operator, another who came through the youth system at Independiente del Valle. His career path has been shaped not only by football, but by personal loss: his father Pedro Franco died during the pandemic in 2020, just days before Alan signed for Atlético Mineiro. He has dedicated subsequent trophies and goals to his father. An adaptable performer, he was tipped as a future striker by one of his youth coaches, but has been deployed in defence by the national team. “I can play in any position I have to,” he said last year. “I'm a midfielder, I'm a full-back. Things change; in football, you have to be multifunctional. It doesn't bother me; it's something I like, and it's a positive thing for me to adapt.”

players.name: Gonzalo Valle
players.bio: Valle has established himself as a reliable, mentally strong keeper who gained continental recognition with a huge performance in the 2025 Copa Libertadores, making a string of key saves against São Paulo at the Morumbi. In January, though, Valle made headlines for the wrong reasons, detained after failing a breathalyser test during a traffic stop in Quito. He served a 20-day sentence, paid a fine and was given a driving ban. LDU took disciplinary measures, too, but supported him through it. He also won backing from Miguel Ángel Loor, president of the Ecuadorian Professional Football League, who said: “He will surely come out of this problem soon and continue his career in an impeccable way, being who he is: the best keeper in the country.”

players.name: Moisés Caicedo
players.bio: From Santo Domingo's dusty pitches to breaking the British transfer record, Caicedo is on quite a journey. When Chelsea paid Brighton £115m in 2023 they gave him an eight-year contract, a huge financial show of confidence in his ability. And, after what he called “a difficult start, I felt the pressure”, he has lived up to that billing at Stamford Bridge. A calm, sensitive character, his childhood nickname “Niño Moi” – “The Boy Moi” – has stuck, and he launched his Niño Moi 23 Academy in Guayas last year, saying he hoped it would “shape both sporting talents and exemplary citizens, grounded in good values”. Faith drives him: “I pray at every match, win or lose … I ask God to see us through each match without injuries.” He arrived at the World Cup carrying over a suspension following his red card in last year’s qualifier against Argentina, a match which began with his mum, Carmen, walking out to deliver the matchball to the referee.

players.name: Jeremy Arévalo
players.bio: One of Ecuador’s most promising young strikers, combining physical power with technical quality and modern centre-forward intelligence. Born in Spain to Ecuadorian parents, Arévalo was developed by Racing Santander’s academy and made a £6.5m move to Stuttgart last January. With dual nationality, he played for Spain at under-18 level before committing his future to La Tri and making his debut last November  against Canada. Stuttgart board member Fabian Wohlgemuth said: “He already has a lot of attributes a striker needs, he’s still young and we see a lot of potential. We want to unlock it with him.” He was at the centre of controversy last year amid reports that Ecuador’s coach, Sebastián Beccacece, had left him out of a friendly after staff felt Arévalo had turned up to the camp overweight. Arévalo responded simply: “I am here to keep developing, step by step. I am incredibly proud of every minute I get to play with the Ecuadorian national team."

players.name: Jackson Porozo
players.bio: Ecuador have some exceptional talent in the centre of defence. Willian Pacho and Piero Hincapié faced off in the final of the Champions League in May. But away from these decorated elite centre-backs, the Mexico-based Porozo gives his manager options. Called up aged 22 to his first World Cup in Qatar, he was drafted into Gustavo Alfaro’s starting XI for the second group game against the Netherlands as Ecuador switched from a back four to a back three. Porozo is a better fit than anyone else on the right of a back three. At 1.93m, he’s also a big threat at set pieces and glanced in a free-kick in a warm-up friendly against Saudi Arabia. He has put his versatility down to playing football on the streets as a youngster. “I have the attributes to play in any position,” he said a few years ago. “My father always wanted me to be a footballer. Thanks to God, the opportunity has come to play professionally.”

players.name: Yaimar Medina
players.bio: Medina’s first cap didn’t come in just any game – age 19, he came off the bench in Curitiba to make his international debut against Brazil. He was still playing for Independiente del Valle at the time and getting used to the idea of playing out of position – he started out as a left-winger. “I don’t feel like a guest, I feel like I’m part of the group and I’m here to earn my place,” Medina said before his debut. A few weeks after that baptism of fire – which ended in a 1-0 defeat – Medina was on his way to Europe, making the familiar first step among Ecuadorian players to Belgium and Genk. In a demonstration of the faith Sebastián Beccacece has in the left-back, the only other qualifier that Medina featured in was the return fixture against Brazil – when Ecuador kept a clean sheet in a goalless draw. Versatility and enthusiasm are the sort of qualities that have earned Medina a seat on the plane to North America.

players.name: Mohamed El-Shenawy
players.bio: El-Shenawy was forged in the lower tiers of Egyptian football before finding his way to the top. He joined Al Ahly's youth system as a teenager and spent his formative years there before leaving in 2009, first to Tala'ea El Gaish, then Haras El Hodoud, and later Petrojet, where he spent three seasons honing his craft. He returned to Al Ahly in 2016 and has been the club's first-choice goalkeeper for most of that time, racking up over 350 appearances in all competitions. Internationally, he was Egypt's No 1 at the 2018 World Cup in Russia, the Pharaohs' first appearance in 28 years, and has kept 40 clean sheets in 75 caps for his country at the time of writing. After winning MOTM for Egypt against South Africa at the 2025 Afcon, he said: “This award isn’t just for me. It is for the 28 players in the squad. We play as the heart of one man for one nation,” before adding that the goalkeeper union is “all one. We play for Egypt together and represent 120million people.” At 1.91 metres, he is known for his imposing aerial presence and shot-stopping reflexes, but can be error-prone. Now 37, he faces a battle to start at this World Cup against the younger Shobeir, but his experience and stature in Egyptian football make him an invaluable member of the squad.

players.name: Yasser Ibrahim
players.bio: Ibrahim made a name for himself at Smouha, where he spent four seasons before joining Al Ahly in 2019 and becoming one of their most dependable defensive pillars at the club, making more than 230 appearances. He has a knack of popping up with the odd important goal too, making international headlines in 2021 after a first-half double against Saudi Arabia’s Al Hilal in the Club World Cup and scoring Egypt’s winner in extra-time against Benin in the last-16 at the 2025 Afcon with a looping header. At 33, he is among the most experienced defenders in the squad and his international career has seen a late bloom, with just 16 senior caps to his name at the time of writing.

players.name: Mohamed Hany
players.bio: Hany has spent his entire career at Al Ahly where he has become one of the most decorated right-backs in African club football. He was promoted to the first team in 2014-15 and has since made more than 390 senior appearances for Al Ahly, winning eight league titles, four CAF Champions League trophies, and six Egyptian Super Cups. Hany’s play style is defined by his pace and stamina that allows him to operate as an attacking threat down the right flank. He has 40 international caps for the national team at the time of writing and has been a regular participant in Al Ahly's memorable continental campaigns.

players.name: Hossam Abdelmaguid
players.bio: One of the most highly-rated young defenders in Egyptian football, Abdelmaguid joined Zamalek's system as a teenager and has become one of the club's most important players. He won his first league title in 2020-21 and added a second the following season, then starred as the club won the CAF Confederation Cup and the CAF Super Cup in 2024. Abdelmaguid – at 1.94m – is a dominant aerial presence who can play out from the back. On competition within the team, he said: “I always work and do what I have to do. Who will play and who will not play is up to the coach. What I have to do is to kill myself on the field and whatever the coach wants me to do, that's it. It’s done.” Still only 24, he is considered one of Egypt's best defensive prospects for the next decade. His contract with Zamalek ends in the summer and according to his agent and brother, Abdelmaguid has had interest from West Brom and Anderlecht among other European clubs.

players.name: Ramy Rabia
players.bio: Ramy Rabia joined Al Ahly's academy and made his first-team debut at just 17 back in 2010, quickly establishing himself as one of the brightest young defenders in Egyptian football. During his two stints at Al Ahly – interrupted by a short and largely unsuccessful spell at Sporting in between – he won 10 Egyptian Premier League titles, six CAF Champions League trophies, and four CAF Super Cups. Rabia, a seasoned international with more than 40 caps for Egypt, has seen fluctuating involvement with the national team over the years but 2025 marked his most active year for his country, playing the most matches in a single calendar year under the national team coach, Hossam Hassan. The 32-year-old is a vocal leader with physical strength and composure in high-pressure moments.

players.name: Mohamed Abdelmonem
players.bio: Abdelmonem is one of Egypt’s most promising young defenders but he wasn’t always destined to play as centre-back. “I started off as an attacker,” he said. “When I was 10 years old, my club was at a tournament, facing a team with a really strong No 9. He was absolutely destroying our defenders, hitting and pushing them. My coach told me to go back and play man-to-man against him … I played a really good game against him and from then on, the coach saw me as a defender. I would call my dad, crying, telling him that I wanted to play as a No 10 … Now, I try to be the best defender I can be, while also scoring goals.” Abdelmonem joined the Ligue 1 side Nice from Al Ahly in 2024, becoming the first Egyptian player to represent the southern French club. The 27-year-old spent his formative years at Al Ahly, clocking 115 appearances across three seasons and winning 10 major honours. He gained further recognition outside of the continent after stellar performances during Egypt’s run to the final at the 2021 Afcon. However, eight months into his Nice career, he ruptured his ACL and has only just made his way into first-team training.

players.name: Mahmoud Hassan ‘Trezeguet’
players.bio: Trezeguet's nickname, borrowed from the French football legend David Trezeguet, has been with him since his teenage years and has defined a career as colourful as any Egyptian footballer's. He broke into Al Ahly's first team at 18, helping them win back-to-back CAF Champions League titles in 2012 and 2013. He made his biggest European impact at Kasimpasa in Turkey, becoming the seventh Egyptian to reach double figures in a European league and setting a new record for goals by an Egyptian in the Turkish top-flight. Aston Villa signed him in 2019 where his goals helped them avoid the drop in 2020. He later returned to Turkey before moving back to Al Ahly. He has over 90 caps and more than 20 goals for Egypt and his contributions to the 2018 World Cup qualifying campaign established his legacy in Egyptian football history. “I’ve played in the best league in the world, the Premier League. I’ve played in front of incredible crowds in Turkey. I would give those all up if I needed to,” Trezeguet said. “I would never give up representing Egypt.” Now, at 31, he arrives at this World Cup as one of the squad's most experienced players and as a player whom many of his teammates grew up watching.

players.name: Emam Ashour
players.bio: Ashour hails from a football family and as a child, he trained with the older age groups at Ghazl El Mahalla and nearly quit the game when youth coaches overlooked him: “I played at El Mahalla only because my brothers played there,” he says. Ashour eventually earned promotion to El Mahalla’s first team and soon became one of Egypt’s most eye-catching talents. In 2019, he signed for Zamalek, where he helped the White Knights win domestic titles and forced his way into the national team, later even moving briefly to Midtjylland in Denmark, before joining his former team’s bitter rivals Al Ahly in 2023. There, Ashour’s flair and scoring really took him to the upper echelons of domestic football and the midfielder became the league’s top scorer in 2024-25. Injuries, however, have been recurring after a broken collarbone cut short his Club World Cup in 2025. Ashour is deployed as an attacking No 10 for Egypt, roaming between the lines, creating chances, and typically partnering other playmakers in a free role.

players.name: Hamza Abdelkarim
players.bio: The youngest player in this group by some distance, and one of the most exciting prospects in Egyptian football. He came through Al Ahly’s academy, played for Egypt at Under-17 level, and then joined Barcelona B during the winter transfer window on loan, with an option to buy. Abdelkarim, the first Egyptian player in the Catalan club’s history, has already started to make a name for himself with their Under-19A side, scoring five goals in seven league matches. His senior Egypt call-up this summer is clearly a gamble, but not a wild one. Hossam Hassan has left out more established forwards and kept a teenage striker who has barely started his senior career, but the attraction is obvious: Hamza, known as “The Egyptian Haaland”, is tall, mobile, and a natural penalty-box player in a squad that otherwise leans heavily on wide forwards and midfield runners for goals. He is unlikely to be asked to carry Egypt’s attack, but if the side needs a late body in the box, the youngest player in the squad may suddenly become extremely important.

players.name: Mohamed Salah
players.bio: The Egyptian King needs little introduction, and his career has become a permanent argument against ceilings. In December, he became the player with the most goal involvements at a single club in Premier League history, with 277, surpassing Wayne Rooney. No one has won more Golden Boot awards in the league’s history than his four (joint-top with Thierry Henry), and he is the highest scoring foreign player in the league. Salah has led Egypt to two Africa Cup of Nations finals in 2017 and 2021, and he was named in the team of the tournament on each run. His defining national team moment, however, remains the stoppage time penalty against Congo in October 2017 that sent Egypt to the 2018 World Cup and ended a 28-year absence from the competition. Since announcing that he will call time on his storied nine-year spell at Liverpool, he has played with a certain freedom, despite not being at his best this season. Salah has scored 65 international goals at the time of writing, second on his country’s all-time list and chasing the record held by Hossam Hassan, Egypt's head coach. As captain and the focal point of the squad, he remains the player around whom successive coaches have built their plans.

players.name: Mostafa Ziko
players.bio: Mostafa Mohamed Zaki Abdelraouf – AKA Ziko – has taken the long road into the national team. From Shibin El-Kom he worked his way through Haras El Hodoud, ZED and Pyramids to take his place among Egypt’s elite. His proud mother says his rise came after “years of effort and challenges”, adding that he had to take responsibility too early in life when his father died when Ziko was just 11. His Egypt debut could hardly have gone better. In the warm-up friendly against Russia in Cairo he came on and scored the only goal of the match with a second-half header. Afterwards he said that “even if I had scripted the scenario myself, I could not have imagined my debut going this way”. Ziko is not expected to be a starter this summer – Egypt’s positions up front are crowded, with Mohamed Salah, Omar Marmoush, Trezeguet, Ibrahim Adel and others ahead of him. But his value may be as a bench player who can cover several attacking roles and arrive without the burden of expectation carried by the bigger names.

players.name: Haissem Hassan
players.bio: Haissem Hassan's international eligibility story is a tale of multiple competing claims. Born in Paris to an Egyptian father and Tunisian mother, and having represented France at youth level, he was sought by two national federations. Tunisia confirmed they had approached him; Egypt also made contact. Hassan made his decision on 15 March 2026 to represent Egypt, expressing his desire to compete at the World Cup. He launched his professional career with Châteauroux in France at just 16 in 2018, then moved to Villarreal in 2020, spending time in their reserve ranks before loan spells at Mirandés and Sporting Gijón. In August 2024, he joined Real Oviedo in Spain's second division, before being promoted to La Liga, and has become a regular starter since. The 24-year-old is predominantly a right-sided attacker, quick, direct, and comfortable in 1v1 situations.

players.name: Ahmed Fatouh
players.bio: A product of Zamalek's youth academy, Fatouh was a vital part of the side that won back-to-back league titles in 2021 and 2022. His crossing ability and technical skill have made him a favorite of successive Zamalek and Egypt national team coaches but in August 2024 he received a one-year suspended prison sentence, a 20,000 EGP fine (£275) and a revoked license after a fatal car accident killed a 55-year-old police officer. Examination confirmed he was driving under the influence of drugs. He also agreed to pay the victim's family 12m EGP for restitution. Addressing rumours that Zamalek had covered the payment, Fatouh said: "I paid the entire weregild myself. It was my mistake.” The case left his career in limbo for months and he is attempting to rebuild his reputation and form.

players.name: Hamdy Fathy
players.bio: A combative defensive midfielder whose career has taken him from the trenches of Egyptian football to continental glory with Al Ahly. Fathy began his professional career with Damanhour, moved to Enppi in 2015, then to Petrojet one year later, and joined Al Ahly in 2019. At Al Ahly, his career truly flourished, quickly establishing himself as a regular starter and achieved impressive success, winning three each of the Egyptian Premier League, the Egyptian Cup, the Egyptian Super Cup and the CAF Champions League. He then moved to the Qatari side Al-Wakrah in 2023. In the summer of 2025, Al Ahly brought him back on loan specifically to participate in the 2025 Club World Cup. Fathy is also a national team regular, with 62 appearances to his name at the time of writing, playing as a disruptor in front of the defence. “I can play as a No 6, as a centre-back in a three, in a two. Whatever the coach wants, I will do,” he says. “I have no preference. What matters to me is only the best interests of the team.”

players.name: Karim Hafez
players.bio: Hafez left Egypt young, making his way through Belgium, Cyprus, France and Turkey, and was often linked with moves back home. Several years ago, when asked about Al Ahly and Zamalek, he said that he didn't “intend to return to Egypt” – but football, as usual, had other plans. He made the move home in 2022, joined Pyramids, and grew into a hugely consistent, experienced option on the left. His international career has been intermittent. Hafez spent four years outside the squad before returning in May’s friendly win over Russia. He’s not the quickest left-back in the squad, but he gives Egypt balance, a natural left foot, and cover in a position where Hossam Hassan has few perfect solutions. He can be profound on social media, once writing: “We need to accept that we won’t always make the right decisions that we’ll screw up royally sometimes – understanding that failure is not the opposite of success. It’s part of success.”

players.name: El Mahdy Soliman
players.bio: A seasoned veteran whose career path has threaded through much of Egyptian club football, Soliman is now the elder statesman of the goalkeeping corps. The 39-year-old began his professional career with Enppi in 2008 and went on to play for several other Egyptian clubs before joining Zamalek in 2025. Standing 1.89 metres tall, Soliman is a composed, experienced presence in goal, praised for his composure and his ability to play with his feet, traits that have seen him compared to modern sweeper‑keepers.

players.name: Mohanad Lasheen
players.bio: Lasheen is a player who knows the value of hard work. He was a part of the Alassiouty (now Pyramids) squad that won promotion to the Egyptian Premier League, scoring the winning goal that guaranteed their ascent five weeks before the end of the season. He is a versatile central midfielder with a keen eye for a pass and a strong defensive work ethic. When Lasheen joined Egypt’s squad in 2025 during the World Cup qualifiers, he did so while battling injury. At the time, he said that from the start of the camp, the players understood the weight of the qualifiers and that he was “racing against time” to be fit. With 21 caps, he remains a vital part of the national team's midfield rotation and was deemed fit just in time for the World Cup.

players.name: Nabil 'Dunga' Emad
players.bio: Nabil Emad, better known as Dunga, came through the lower ranks of Egyptian football before moving to Pyramids, then Zamalek in 2022, where he became a defensive midfield mainstay. He won the Egyptian Premier League, the Egyptian Cup, CAF Confederation Cup and CAF Super Cup there before, in the last winter window, joining Saudi Arabia’s Al-Najma. His nickname has been with him a long time, ever since one of his junior coaches compared him to the Brazilian World-Cup-winning legend. For the national team, Dunga provides Hossam Hassan with a more conservative midfield option. While he may not be as flashy as Emam Ashour or as expansive as Marwan Attia, he will certainly be useful when Egypt need to win duels and second balls. His place this summer was thrown into doubt days after the squad was announced when he was the subject of allegations of rape made by an Italian woman. His lawyer, Shaaban Saeed, strongly denied the claims, saying his client was victim of "a campaign to distort the player's image".

players.name: Marwan Attia
players.bio: Attia is a hard-working central midfielder known for his engine and passing. He came up through the ranks at Al Ittihad Alexandria, breaking into their first team in 2019. He spent four seasons as a steady presence and even captain before moving to Al Ahly. “I have been playing football since I was seven, and my forever dream […] was to wear that red jersey,” Attia said. “When I knew Al Ahly wanted me, I didn’t think twice. I felt in my bones that I would be living my childhood dream”. He has emerged under Hossam Hassan as the foil to more attack-minded teams though he can also surge forward on occasion. At the 2025 Afcon, he burst into the box to score a brilliant opener against Benin, and later whipped in a cross for the winner. Off the field, Attia has shown quiet determination in tough times, losing his father in 2023, yet immediately returning to action to play a key role in securing a treble for Al Ahly.

players.name: Ibrahim Adel
players.bio: A lightning-fast left winger, Adel emerged from the Port Said scene, scouted at the age of eight. The son of the former Zamalek forward Ashraf Adel, he inherited his family's love of the game. His explosive speed and eye for goal drew attention quickly and he earned a move to Pyramids in 2018, helping them win their first two trophies ever, the African Champions League and the Egyptian Cup with his swagger and long-range strikes earning him the nickname “Baby Hulk”. Getafe tried to sign him, even flying him to Spain, but Pyramids held firm.
