Adel later said: “The club wants to keep me… and I respect their decision.” Now on loan at the Danish club FC Nordsjælland, where their Egyptian sporting executive, Hossam El Zanati, said the club had followed him for over three years and needed a “superstar” to open a pathway from Egypt. He was named best player at the 2023 Under-23 Africa Cup of Nations and has 21 senior international caps with three goals.

players.name: Mahmoud Saber
players.bio: Saber, now with ZED, has taken a winding route through Egyptian football: from Nogoom FC to Pyramids, then loans and short spells with ZED and Smouha before returning to ZED again in 2025. At 24, he has already played for the senior national team 14 times despite only making his debut in 2024. Saber is known for his ability to find space in crowded midfields and his precise through-balls. He is right-footed, tactically adaptable, and can play as an attacking midfielder or off the left when required.

players.name: Omar Marmoush
players.bio: In another life, Marmoush might have donned the red-and-white colors of a different team. “After about three years of being in Germany, Canada contacted me,” the forward has said. “They told me they knew I had citizenship and wanted me to join their setup. The coach called me personally. But [my decision] had already been made a long time ago. The Egyptian national team, to me, is above anything else.” At 17, he made his mark at Wadi Degla; then joined Wolfsburg, Stuttgart and St. Pauli before his Eintracht Frankfurt performances secured a transfer to Manchester City. “Without him it would not be possible to qualify for the Champions League and reach the FA Cup final,” said his club manager, Pep Guardiola. “He’s a special player. I see him more as a striker not a proper winger.” Hossam Hassan has yet to follow Guardiola’s advice, often deploying Marmoush on the flank. Still, with 46 caps and 10 goals for the national team at the time of writing, he and Salah are the first names on the team sheet.

players.name: Mostafa ‘Oufa’ Shobeir
players.bio: Growing up, Shobeir could hardly have escaped football's gravity. His father, Ahmed Shobeir, is one of Egypt's most celebrated goalkeepers as the man between the sticks at the 1990 World Cup and at Al Ahly over a 13-year career. His son has followed in those footsteps, joining Al Ahly's academy before making his professional debut in 2020. Since then, he has established himself as a reliable presence for his club that is always calm under pressure. The 26-year-old kept nine consecutive clean sheets in the CAF Champions League – a competition record – on the way to his third continental trophy during the 2023-24 season and Fifa described him ahead of the Club World Cup as "impregnable".

players.name: Tarek Alaa
players.bio: One of the youngest members of the squad, Alaa is a versatile player who can operate at right-back or in the defensive midfield role with equal composure. Currently playing for Zed FC, on loan from Pyramids, he has made his mark as a technically confident player comfortable on the ball in tight spaces. Reports have suggested Al Ahly are keen to sign Alaa as an understudy and eventual replacement for Mohamed Hany. With just one international cap to his name at the time of writing, Alaa is still developing, and the World Cup represents an opportunity for one of Egyptian football's promising younger profiles to prove himself on the biggest stage.

players.name: Ahmed Sayed ‘Zizo’
players.bio: Zizo has carved out a reputation as one of Egyptian club football's most clinical, goalscoring wingers. His finest campaign came in 2021-22 with Zamalek, when he finished as the league’s top scorer with 19 goals. When his contract expired in June 2025, Al Ahly, Zamalek's fiercest rivals, moved swiftly to sign Zizo on a four-year deal, making it one of the most controversial transfers in Egyptian football. “How could Al Ahly not sign the best player in Egypt, especially since he was a free agent?” said the former Egyptian and Zamalek goalkeeper, Nader El-Sayed. “The Zizo deal wasn’t spiteful. He’s the best player in Africa, and he came on a free transfer. Al Ahly is similar to Real Madrid.” The 30-year-old brings pace, technique, and an unerring eye for goal to Egypt's wide attacking options. “I always wanted to be a footballer,” he says. “I never thought about what level I could reach, didn’t even dream about playing for Egypt, but I was always going to be a footballer. There was no other option.”

players.name: Mohamed Alaa
players.bio: The youngest of Egypt's four goalkeepers, Alaa is an intriguing wildcard selection. The 26-year-old has established himself as one of the more capable shot-stoppers outside the country's three traditional giants at El Gouna. Last season, Alaa was the main reason El Gouna survived relegation, keeping two clean sheets in three playoff matches. His inclusion in Egypt's squad for the first time during the March international break reflects Hossam Hassan’s willingness to look beyond the big-club establishments for reliable talent.

players.name: Jordan Pickford
players.bio: Likes to make himself heard. James Tarkowski says his Everton teammate is “top-end” but “crackers … me and him fall out constantly. He shouts my name constantly from behind me so I'm constantly looking at him and he's just barking at me to bark at someone else. And I can never really understand what he's saying because he's 40 yards away, shouting, screaming at me. I'm like: 'Will you just come and tell me what you want’ …” Last year he broke the record for consecutive England clean sheets. He would have been called Jordan Pigford but his dad, Lee, changed the family name because he was tired of being called Piggy at school. Pickford says that if he wasn’t a footballer, he’d be in motocross. “I grew up watching it. We would stay in motorhomes and caravans at the racetrack as kids and it was mint. It gives me proper adrenaline, and it is one of the things I love.”

players.name: Ezri Konsa
players.bio: Signed for Charlton at 11 after starting out at Senrab FC, the Sunday League club in London’s Forest Gate which also produced, among others, Jermain Defoe, Sol Campbell and John Terry. Konsa has the word “blessed” tattooed on his left hand. “I got it in my second season at Charlton,” he said in 2021. “It reminds me how far I’ve come and how blessed I am. It was difficult growing up where I grew up. There was a lot of crime and violence, but I am lucky to have an older brother who kept me out of trouble and kept me grounded. It was tough, but me and my family and friends dealt with it and we got out of it. Hopefully what I’m doing now can inspire the next generation.” He signed for Villa from Brentford in 2019 for £12m, and made his England debut against Brazil in 2024. Has royal backing from Villa fan Prince William. Konsa: “He called me a Rolls-Royce once. That got stuck in my head. I’ll take that all day. It was really nice.”

players.name: Nico O’Reilly
players.bio: A City academy graduate having signed aged eight, O’Reilly’s rise has been rapid. A full debut in 2024, a first England call-up a year later, and now a World Cup. “It’s mad, I never thought I’d be in this position so soon,” he said last year, insisting his breakthrough hadn’t changed him. “I've still got the same friends, I still do the same stuff, I still go to Tesco with my mum.” He says mum Holli is his inspiration, and she recorded a surprise video message for him when he signed his new City contract last September, saying: “I always remember, when you were about three months old, I looked at you and I knew you were special. I said to Nana he is special and she laughed at me like she does, and says everyone thinks their kids are special. But son, I knew you were, and always believed in you, and always will. Don’t ever change. You’re absolutely smashing life, and I love you.”

players.name: Declan Rice
players.bio: The most expensive British player ever at £105m, just ahead of £100m Jack Grealish. Rice was released from Chelsea’s academy aged 14 and joined West Ham after a trial. He stayed there until 2023 having become one of only three Hammers captains, with Billy Bonds and Bobby Moore, to lead the club to a major trophy. Now a Premier League champion with Arsenal, he made three appearances for Ireland before committing to England in 2019. Also loves golf, and did some modelling at London Fashion Week in 2024, wearing a navy jacket with white lace-up tie. “You know what, it was so good,” he told Sky. “I love fashion, this is really special.” Has talked previously about developing his own look, after growing up with “mum always telling me how to dress … I’m always looking for a pair of trousers that sit above my shoes well. Not too low, not too high. That’s a key thing for me, how a pair of trousers sit on shoes.”

players.name: John Stones
players.bio: Made his England debut 12 years ago, and has won everything at club level. “I never thought I'd be a professional, let alone playing for England, playing in the Premier League, never mind winning the Premier League,” he said in 2023. A Barnsley academy graduate, known as the ‘Barnsley Beckenbauer’, he’s always been keen not to be seen as too cultured on the ball. “Of course, I’ll kick it into Row Z if necessary,” he said a decade ago after his £47.5m move from Everton. “I do the dirty stuff that every defender should do and should be good at. The rest of it is a bonus, where I can play out and start attacks.” He’s popular among teammates; last year City teammate Bernardo Silva revealed why he named his dog after Stones. “I was thinking of getting a dog and I said: ‘John, one day if I get a dog, I will name it after you. Would you be happy about it?’ He said ‘no you can’t’. But then I just did it. I like the name John. John Silva, I think it’s a good name.”

players.name: Marc Guéhi
players.bio: The second Côte d'Ivoire-born England player after his former Palace teammate Wilfried Zaha. Left Chelsea for Palace, via a loan at Swansea, in 2021, and won the FA Cup there. Son of a church minister, he called last summer’s collapsed move to Liverpool and subsequent move to City in January: “God’s plan [coming] to full fruition.” Known for being calm and thoughtful, but he has his eye on a different future when football’s over. “I'd love to be a WWE wrestler,” he told the BBC. “Growing up, watching WWE … It can be dangerous but the performance side of it … I'm a good waffler on the mic … making people believe you're the bad guy or the good guy.” Spends his spare time reading or in the cinema “because no one goes to the cinema now, so it's nice to be in a cinema on your own”, and has plans to resume drumming. “I drummed at church for a long time. I drummed at school. I don't have a drum set but I really want one. That's a really good way to enjoy music and escape.”

players.name: Bukayo Saka
players.bio: Feels like a long-term fixture in England squads, having made his debut six years ago, but is still only 24. His first name, chosen by his Nigerian grandmother, means “adds to happiness”; his nickname, “Little Chilli”, was given to him by former teammate Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang due to his spicy shooting in training. “He just kept calling me it and then it became a thing, and now it's a big thing.” In 2024 Nandos brought out an official Peri Peri Saka sauce, with Saka saying: “I’ve been going to Nando’s since I was little so to have my own Peri-Peri sauce is quite mad.” During three months out with a torn hamstring last year he bought a new puppy, Tucker the cockapoo, and got stuck into reading, with one book in particular, Eckhart Tolle’s The Power of Now: A Guide to Spiritual Enlightenment, striking a chord. “Sometimes I’d think: ‘Oh, am I going to come back in the best shape?’ Or, in the past: ‘What could I have done to prevent injury?’ But all that is not necessary. One of the best things I took from the book is to always ask yourself: what’s necessary in this moment right now? And try to live that way.”

players.name: Elliot Anderson
players.bio: Whitley Bay-born Anderson was named in the senior Scotland squad for the first time in August 2023, eligible to represent them through his grandmother. But he changed his mind soon after. “I left camp after a few days and decided I just needed more time,” he said after being called into the senior England squad last year. “I didn’t want to dive into anything, so I’m really thankful I’ve done that now. I’m definitely English. It’s just I hadn’t really been called up by England through the age levels when I was younger so I thought I’d take the opportunity. But once I got that belief that I was good enough to play for England, it hit me.” Newcastle sold him to Forest in 2024 to meet Premier League profitability and sustainability rules. Anderson is another graduate of the famous Wallsend Boys Club, alongside Alan Shearer, Peter Beardsley and Michael Carrick. His older brother Wil appeared on the 11th season of Love Island, entering the villa on Day 12, and being dumped on Day 37.

players.name: Harry Kane
players.bio: Overtook Pelé’s international scoring record last year, but it wasn’t until last summer that he finally lifted a club trophy, when Bayern won their 34th Bundesliga. “It was a weight on me,” he said. “As the years go by you start to think: ‘Is it going to happen?’” It was a rough road to the top: at 19 he was loaned to Norwich, missed a sitter on his debut, played five times, scored no goals. He also had a hard time on loan at Leicester – which partly motivated his creation of the Harry Kane Foundation, supporting mental health charities. “There wasn’t just a straight line to the top [for me]. I had to really go through some tough times. I’m learning more and more [about mental health] and for sure it’s something that I’ll really try to take on and help as much as I can when my career has finished.” In his spare time he plays golf, collects watches and plays chess, inspired by watching The Queen's Gambit. He told GQ in 2024: “Since that came out I've started to learn and I'm about 1200 rated on the 10-minute version on Chess.com. It's fairly decent.”

players.name: Jude Bellingham
players.bio: Raised in Stourbridge, Bellingham used to turn up at Birmingham for training aged 14 in his burgundy Priory School uniform. Striker Lukas Jutkiewicz said: “We’re doing finishing drills and this kid’s joining in with the first-team, he’s faking to shoot, putting senior pros on their arses, chopping and then rolling it into the other corner. I thought to myself – and I know I wasn’t the only one – ‘Just who is this?’” Bellingham graduated from Loughborough College with a BTec Level 3 Certificate in Sport in 2021, completing it remotely after moving to Borussia Dortmund. Commercial deals include Louis Vuitton, Lucozade, his own JB Adidas signature collection, his own app, and, in 2024, modelling men’s underpants for Kim Kardashian’s Skims brand. He had an uneasy moment with Thomas Tuchel last year when the England manager told TalkSport about Bellingham’s “edge” during games which “can be a bit repulsive”. Tuchel clarified later: “I used this word unintentionally. There was no message, no hidden agenda. I created the headlines and I’m sorry for the upset. I should have known better.”

players.name: Marcus Rashford
players.bio: MBE-holder, author and poverty campaigner who took on and beat Boris Johnson twice. Rashford arrived at the World Cup on a high after a restorative season at Barcelona. Last October he pointed to the high turnover of managers at Manchester United as a reason for his inconsistency there: “I feel like I’ve been in an inconsistent environment for a long time … In order to be consistent in anything, not just sport, you need consistent variables in your life. We’ve just had so many changes.” He was raised in Wythenshawe, the youngest of five, with his mum, Mel, working three jobs to keep the family going. His experiences informed his off-field work since. He co-wrote The Breakfast Club Adventures book series, and the self-help bestseller for children You Are A Champion. It starts with this dedication: “To every young person who is trying to find their way. To my mum, for helping me believe that dreams can come true.”

players.name: Tino Livramento
players.bio: Croydon-born to a Portuguese dad and Scottish mum, Livramento was a striker for his boyhood club, Roundshaw, before joining Chelsea’s academy aged seven. He left in 2021 before making a first-team appearance, joining Southampton, where he suffered a serious ACL injury, taking a year to recover. “Mentally, it put me through a lot of things,” he told the Telegraph in 2024. “It made me grow up, massively. I was 19, I was already living away from my family. I was going through a lot of things. I didn’t know whether I was going to return to the same level or be able to improve.” But he rebuilt his strength and proved he’d lost nothing, earning a £32m move to Newcastle, where fans have tweaked their old Tino Asprilla chant in his honour. “I’m not anything like Asprilla. I’ve never done anything wild at a training ground. I’m aware of the stories about him, a little anyway. I’ve not done anything wild, don’t worry about that. I’m pretty boring you know. But the chant is amazing, every time I hear it I want to smile.”

players.name: Dean Henderson
players.bio: Henderson left Manchester United to join Palace in 2023 after an eye-catching loan at Forest, and his reputation keeps growing. Highlights so far include saving Omar Marmoush’s penalty at Wembley to help Palace win their first FA Cup, which he later credited to “my magic cap … when I put it on I knew we had a chance”. He’s often seen leading Palace fans’ Shakira tribute song about that Cup victory during and after games. He says he loves going to England training camps because “sometimes day one is a special night and we get brownies and custard. That’s always something to look forward to. A day before a game is rice pudding for dessert. The rice pudding here is sensational, it’s got little white chocolate chips in it. It’s something I always look forward to.”

players.name: Jordan Henderson
players.bio: A vocal leader, fresh from a convincing comeback season in English football with Brentford. Was heavily criticised in 2023 after leaving Liverpool for Saudi Pro League club Al-Ettifaq, given his previous support for LGBTQ+ rights, and was booed by fans during an England game soon after. He ended his Saudi  contract after 24 weeks and moved to Ajax, but was left out of the Euro 2024 squad. When England made the final, he called a van-hire company and made the seven-hour journey to Berlin with his family to watch, surprising England fans at service stations along the way. “When we needed the toilet or something, or popping in the garages, it was quite funny to see some of the fans,” he says. “Actually, it was quite nice to be with them. But obviously I would have rather been with the team.” He earned an international recall last March. Was awarded an MBE in 2021 for helping drive the #PlayersTogether NHS fundraising campaign during the Covid pandemic.

players.name: Dan Burn
players.bio: Released by Newcastle aged 11 just before Christmas in 2003. Burn said it was “devastating” but “I was pretty crap, if I’m honest”. By 13 he was down to nine fingers after a fence-climbing accident, and at 16 he was working at Asda in Blyth pushing trolleys. “Normally the lads who are getting picked up to play in the youth teams are getting picked up around 15, 16. I didn’t end up signing for anyone. I went to sixth form and was pushing trolleys. If you had said then that I would be in the Premier League, I’d have laughed at you.” But he kept going in non-league and Darlington signed him at 17, starting a journey which, in 2022, featured a £13m return to Newcastle from Brighton. When he scored in the Carabao Cup final and won his first England call-up in 2024, the Asda in Blyth framed a “Burn 33” high-vis trolley-pusher jacket in tribute. Store manager Dean: “We’re hoping that Dan will pay us a visit soon, along with the trophy.”

players.name: Kobbie Mainoo
players.bio: When Mainoo became the first English teenager to score in an FA Cup final for 43 years in 2024, Paul Scholes wrote on Instagram: “Read a few comparisons between me and this kid. Don’t waste your time, he is 10 times the player I was at 19.” His progress at United was smooth until he came up against Ruben Amorim’s 3-4-2-1. His half-brother, the former Love Island star Jordan Mainoo-Hames, wore a “free Kobbie Mainoo” T-shirt during a 4-4 draw with Bournemouth, but regular football didn’t come again until Amorim left. Restored by Michael Carrick, who had worked with him at youth level, Mainoo proved his worth. “When there's new managers, they have their way they want to play,” Mainoo said in May, reflecting on his time in exile. “If they think you don't fit that, then you don't fit that. I'd say my family and my friends helped me see the light at the end of the tunnel. They knew it would swing back my way at some point. I just had to be patient."

players.name: Morgan Rogers
players.bio: Rogers says his loan spells at Lincoln, Bournemouth and Blackpool – before his move to Middlesbrough in 2023 – “were everything to me”. He told The Times: “I had to go through a lot of struggles, a lot of pain, a lot of hard days … I had to find myself as a player, and I had to find that out in the lower leagues – what works in a professional game – because what works in [Man City] academy football is not the same in League One or the Championship.” He moved to Villa for £8m in 2024. He says he was “a bit of a geek for football knowledge” as a boy: “Learning players, learning teams and stuff. Me and my dad were quite big on it – he’d ask me questions and I’d answer them. I’d be able to name random players across the world and stuff. I’d try and be the cleverest person in the room when it came to football.” He has multiple tattoos, including one on his left forearm listing the birthdays of his parents and three brothers. “That is my favourite one. My most sentimental one.”

players.name: Anthony Gordon
players.bio: Arrives at the World Cup on the back of one of the most surprising transfers in recent years as Barcelona paid £60.7m plus add-ons to sign him from Newcastle. He was bullish during his presentation saying “There is a burning fire in my belly to win here. [I bring] my intensity, work rate, technical ability. I like to score goals, assist and help the team win games.” It is quite a turnaround for a player who, released by Liverpool aged 11, joined Everton and stayed until 2023 when, via a controversial transfer request, he joined Newcastle for £40m. His senior England debut came against Brazil in 2024. He’s a fan of motivational books, with favourites including Winning: The Unforgiving Race to Greatness by Tim Grover and Never Finished: Unshackle Your Mind and Win the War Within by the retired Navy Seal and ultra-marathon runner David Goggins. During Euro 2024 he memorably turned up to face a regular England press conference covered in cuts having fallen off an electric bike while trying to take a video on his phone, having misunderstood the breaks. “The bike has not just slowed down, it has fully come to a stop … and I haven’t.”

players.name: Ollie Watkins
players.bio: Scored the last-minute goal that sent England to the Euro 2024 final. “I don’t think I’ve hit a ball that sweet before.” He said it felt surreal for someone who, a few years earlier, had been struggling to get into Exeter’s side in League Two. “I never imagined it. Obviously you can dream but I’m a realist.” His pre-match routine involves music: “The closer it gets to kick-off, I like to listen to heavier hip-hop, so 50 Cent and his old school Get Rich or Die Tryin' album or Rick Ross – something like that.” But he chose to sing Never Too Much by Luther Vandross as his initiation song when signing for Villa. Gets his love of music from his mum, Delsi-May, a professional singer, who told the Times in 2021 how she first spotted Ollie’s football potential by the seaside at home in Torquay. “We used to take the kids out, and there was a little machine where you put a 2p in, and a little rubber ball fell out. He’d stand there and you’d drop it, and he’d kick it. He never used to miss. He was only nine months old.”

players.name: Noni Madueke
players.bio: Signed by Crystal Palace when he was nine, he switched to Spurs three years later and, after starring at England youth levels, was approached by Manchester United. But he rejected them, opting instead to leave England aged 16 for PSV Eindhoven. “I just wanted to be integrated into a first-team setup as quickly as possible.” He told GQ he learnt Dutch quickly because “I didn't want anyone talking shit about me on the field … Like, ‘I know exactly what you're saying!’” He shone there, and Chelsea signed him from PSV for £28.5m in 2023. Arsenal paid £48.5m for him in 2025 – a deal criticised by some Arsenal fans with a #NoToMadueke campaign due to his mixed form, and the fee. “I took it in my stride. You are in the public eye, people can say what they want.” He’s one of the England squad’s fashion fans. “I feel like if you look good, you feel good, you play good. Football, music, fashion, it’s all connected for me. It’s how I express who I am, and each one feeds into the other.”

players.name: Eberechi Eze
players.bio: Last summer Eze was flying: an FA Cup winner with Crystal Palace, an emerging England regular, and a new £60m signing for boyhood club Arsenal. But – apart from a memorable derby hattrick against Spurs, and two in the return fixture – he has struggled to make a regular impact in a title-winning season at the Emirates. But he has plenty of resilience to draw on, having been through the trauma of being released by multiple clubs, including Arsenal, as a youngster. Looking back he said he coped thanks to “my trust and faith in God, I just allowed it to fuel me and fill me up, which was important. It doesn't mean that it wasn’t difficult. It's still difficult to get through those times. I remember thinking: ‘why is this so hard? But the love for football never, never changed.” He’s also a keen chess player, having been taught to play by former Palace teammate Michael Olise. Eze won the £15,000 first prize in a Chess.com amateur tournament in May last year, beating YouTuber Sapnap in the final.

players.name: Ivan Toney
players.bio: The big surprise in Thomas Tuchel’s squad – to most people at least, but maybe not to the man himself. Having left Brentford for Al-Ahli in 2024 he told the Guardian the following year that the level of football in the Saudi Pro League was “on a par with the Premier League … If Al-Ahli were in the Premier League, we’d be close to the top four. People shouldn’t turn their noses up.” His experience of playing in the heat could have helped him edge out Dominic Calvert-Lewin, Dominic Solanke and Danny Welbeck for a place in this squad. He says critics should “go and try to play in a sauna and see if they can cope.” He missed the 2022 World Cup due to the investigation into breaches of FA betting rules which led to an eight-month ban. “The biggest punishment for me was missing out on the World Cup,” he said in 2023. “I felt like somebody's out to get me to stop me from playing for England. That was the lowest point.” Famed for his no-look penalty technique, including one during a shoot-out in Euro 2024. “I never look at the ball,” he said later. “Some people may see it as crazy. But it is just my routine.”

players.name: James Trafford
players.bio: Returned to City - where he started aged 12 - from Burnley last year, after keeping 29 clean sheets in 45 Championship games. He grew up on his parents’ farm in Cumbria, and his move to City in 2015 made the local paper, headlined: “James, 12, signs for mega-rich Man City.” His dad told the Times & Star: “He helps us get the sheep into the field – it's one of his favourite jobs. But I've never said that he must farm. You've got to let them go their own way … we're extremely proud of him.” James junior told the paper: “I'm not nervous. The first-team players are in the same place we train. I've met Gaël Clichy and Vincent Kompany, and I've seen Joe Hart training. It's really exciting." He still keeps his hand in whenever he goes home to the farm. “When I was with dad last time I was bedding up all the sheep and putting hay out for the ones outside. We’ve always done that.”

players.name: Reece James
players.bio: A Chelsea academy graduate, James started out as a striker because “I idolised Didier Drogba … He played in my team, he played in my position and I always looked up to him. I just wanted to be like him, score like him and celebrate like him.” But his coaches saw it differently, moving him first to midfield, then, aged 15, to right-back. “I hated it for two or three years. But then one day it just clicked.” He and Lauren are the first brother and sister to play senior England football in the modern era. They give each other space, though. Lauren said in 2022: “Anything to do with football, we both know what we need to do so we just leave the other one to it. But outside of football, we have a great relationship. I think with our age gap that helps, with it only being two years … He is a funny guy.” He overcame three difficult seasons of injury and fitness problems to be ready for this summer.

players.name: Djed Spence
players.bio: Spence says he keeps a mental note of all his doubters. He was sent on loan three times by Spurs before establishing himself, and was also loaned out when he was at Middlesbrough in 2021, with then-manager Neil Warnock warning him he’d be in non-league football in five years unless he changed his attitude. After winning promotion with Forest, who he joined on loan from Boro, he tweeted a photo of himself with the trophy, smoking a cigar, and tagged Warnock. “I wouldn’t say I particularly have a list but I do have a mental note of people who doubted me, for sure, and it does feel good to prove them wrong,” he says. “I’ve always had that from a young age, I’ve always had to fight for my position.” When he made his England debut last year he became the first Muslim to do so for the senior men’s side. “It’s a blessing, it’s amazing. I was surprised, really, the first ever. If I can do it, you can do it. It’s [important] not just for Muslim kids but for any child of any faith. Put your mind to something and you can do it, man.”

players.name: Jarell Quansah
players.bio: Joined Liverpool aged five, captained them at youth level, and broke into the first team in 2023. But two years later, after 58 appearances, he joined Leverkusen for £30m and scored five minutes into his Bundesliga debut. He said in March that leaving for Germany had been a “no-brainer … I just wanted to play. I felt like I could play at the top level, the Bundesliga's a top league. I think you just have maybe a gut feeling. I took time to myself to reflect on everything that was going on at Liverpool, everything I thought I could've done better and what was best for my career really, that was it. When the Leverkusen guys came in and I saw everything that they wanted - I think, from that moment, I knew it was the right decision." He won his first England cap last year against Albania, calling it “one of the greatest days of my life”. His brother Keenan plays for Slough Town.

players.name: Brice Samba
players.bio: Samba was once the back-up to Steve Mandanda at club level, but partnering him in the France national team – for a long time – seemed improbable. After Marseille, Samba had stints at Nancy, Caen and Nottingham Forest, helping the latter to earn promotion back to the Premier League in his final season at the club. Promotion was a parting gift, with Samba crucial in a penalty shootout in the playoffs. "My journey is a bit mad," he admits. "It may seem mad, but I always believed [in earning a France call-up]." Down in the Championship, it certainly seemed a long way away, and even more surprising was his decision to leave Forest, just at the point that they had returned to the top flight. The decision paid off though; one year later, he was playing in the Champions League with Lens and had made the France team.

players.name: Malo Gusto
players.bio: The defender recently outlined his big ambitions. "My goal is to be the best in the world in my position," he said in March. To do that, there is work to be done. "I'm not with Les Bleus to sit on the bench my whole life," he has also said. He is currently not first choice for club nor country, even if Reece James's ability to play in midfield has afforded him more minutes for Chelsea this season. Gusto is driven to succeed in the sport, even if he has vocalised his misgivings about the football world more generally. "Honestly, I don't like it at all. At the start, it was a dream, a parallel world, but now I'm in it, I see the back side; it doesn't necessarily align with my values." He says he plays to make his family proud, and he will look to do that for France this summer.

players.name: Lucas Digne
players.bio: For a player who has represented the likes of Lille, Roma, PSG and Barcelona, you wouldn't expect that the majority of his appearances for France would have come while playing for Everton and Aston Villa. While at some of Europe's elite clubs, the left-back role was never truly his own, but it is now. “I feel as though I’m playing the best football of my career,” he says. “I am a happy footballer, a happy man, and a happy father. I’m loving what I’m doing.” Digne attributes his current status to the Aston Villa manager, Unai Emery. "He took me to the next level," says Digne. For the full-back, it was initially going down a level that has allowed him to make that step up.

players.name: Dayot Upamecano
players.bio: Football has given Upamecano a voice that he had struggled to find. "When I was at school, I had a lot of difficulties … I had a stammer. Some of my schoolmates made fun of me," the Bayern Munich defender once said. "But on the pitch, I have always been [vocal]. People said: 'Dayot, he doesn't speak a lot, but when he's playing football, he shouts!'" He added: "People have often told me that I'm not the same on the pitch as I am off it. In life, I am quite calm and reserved but when I get on the pitch, I am like a lion coming out of a cage!" He has been doing more than just fine, growing as a presence for club and country and asserting himself as a first-choice option for both. Joined Bayern Munich in 2022 from RB Leipzig.

players.name: Jules Koundé
players.bio: Didier Deschamps has previously referred to France's problem of producing full-backs, and within that context, Koundé has emerged as a solution, albeit not a willing one initially. "I learnt to accept it because, subconsciously, I think you create blockages by being negative, and I must admit that I have in the past," says the Barcelona defender, a centre-back by trade but who now plays on the right for club and country. His discomfort in the position relates to the attacking demands. He is not the most eye-catching in that sense, but he certainly catches the eye off the pitch thanks to his bold fashion choices. "It is a part of my personality; I like to dress well," says Koundé, who has made the right-back slot his own at international level.

players.name: Manu Koné
players.bio: Koné's trajectory isn't an unfamiliar one in French football. He left his formative club, Toulouse, when he was still just a teenager, to join Borussia Mönchengladbach. His performances there earned him a move to Roma where he has truly flourished. Pretty much all of his development has happened outside of the French gaze. "I worked so hard, under the radar, you could say," he said back in September. Reaching the France national team is one thing, but heading into the World Cup, he is very much a contender for a starting spot. Didier Deschamps is a big fan of Koné, describing him as "a complete, modern midfielder". The France manager added that the Roma midfielder is "underrated". Play a prominent role in the World Cup and he will be thrust out of the shadows and into the light.

players.name: Ousmane Dembélé
players.bio: In any normal team, the parts would be fitted around the Ballon d'Or winner, but that is not the case with France. Speaking alongside Didier Deschamps at an awards ceremony at the end of the 2024-25 season, Dembélé detailed why his move into the No 9 role for PSG had proved so beneficial, to the extent that he was the catalyst behind Les Parisiens' maiden Champions League trophy. Dembélé knows that Deschamps won't follow Luis Enrique's lead. "He still plays me on the right!" joked Dembélé. "I'll put you on the left," responded Deschamps. In any case, the central role is Mbappé's. On a serious note, getting the best out of Dembélé remains something of a headache for Deschamps. The Ballon d'Or winner's form at club level has not been replicated at international level, where he has just seven goals in 58 appearances at the time of writing.

players.name: Aurélien Tchouaméni
players.bio: Big-money moves always garner the most scrutiny, but judging their success is not always easy, especially for a midfielder, whose effectiveness is not necessarily reflected in stats. But there is a telling one: nearly 200 appearances for Real Madrid in under four years. Those have come since Tchouaméni’s €100m move from Monaco back in 2022. Despite the presence of Toni Kroos and Luka Modric for much of that time, to have racked up so many appearances is a testament to his importance. He is equally crucial for France; on his current trajectory, he could be on course to make 100 appearances for Les Bleus. Jules Koundé, who knows him well, having come through the academy with him at Bordeaux, describes Tchouaméni as a "discreet and calm" character and that transmits on the pitch, where his ability to screen the defence is so valuable for club and country. There was nothing calm about his confrontation with Real teammate Fede Valverde in May, however, with the Uruguayan ending up in hospital, requiring stitches.

players.name: Marcus Thuram
players.bio: It must have been a proud dad moment for Lilian Thuram who, in a viral video, was seen waving off his sons, Khéphren and Marcus, on their way to international duty. Like kids being bid farewell by a parent, the pair looked a little embarrassed as they got into the car that would take them into Clairefontaine. Marcus is a very different player to his father – and his brother, too – he has gradually worked his way up the ladder to lead the attack of one of Europe's elite sides. He has a strong record for Inter, but even more importantly, an excellent relationship with strike partner, Lautaro Martínez. He has yet to create that within the France national team, hence his meagre output (three goals in 33 games at the time of writing) and more limited impact. His father scored twice for Les Bleus, and while he has at least outdone Lilian on that front, he will never better his father's 142 appearances for the national team.

players.name: Kylian Mbappe-Lottin
players.bio: Mbappé is chasing records in North America. Starting the tournament four goals away from Miroslav Klose’s all-time World Cup record of 16, the France forward has an obsession with writing history. However, it is collective titles that drive Mbappé. After winning Ligue 1 in his first full season at Monaco, he piled up the trophies at PSG before joining Real Madrid in 2024. The goals have flowed at Real to the tune of 86 in 103 games, but the major honours have not followed. There was fierce criticism over a trip to Sardinia in May while he recovered from an injury, with some Real fans accusing him of already looking ahead to the World Cup. Club issues aside, Mbappé remains essential for France. The teenager who won the World Cup in 2018 and earned the Golden Boot in the last tournament is now aiming to captain his side to victory this summer.

players.name: Michael Olise
players.bio: Olise had a multitude of options but only ever had eyes for France. The Bayern Munich forward, born in London, could also have represented England, Algeria or Nigeria at international level. "I have this connection with France," said Olise following his first call-up for the senior side. Creating a connection with his France teammates was initially tough for Olise, who has had a language barrier to overcome, as became evident during his first appearance before the media. "When I think that there is potential, I persist," said Didier Deschamps. He was right to. Olise is the long-term successor to Antoine Griezmann, who was so valuable for Les Bleus for a decade prior to his international retirement. Olise's nonchalance, both on and off the pitch, make him a difficult player to decrypt; an enigma of sorts, but an extremely talented one.

players.name: Bradley Barcola
players.bio: When you play for France's biggest club, there is scrutiny. Barcola has not been spared that analysis and, at times, he has been criticised quite intensely. After some middling performances in the Champions League, one pundit called him a "little lamb". However, not everyone's opinion matters to Barcola. "I have very few friends, so when they say 'you're doing something silly', it's because they're right. When they tell me that I played well, it's because they're right. I don't listen to what is said outside," he says. That doesn't mean he doesn't hear it. "[My friends] are the ones who send my things that can be read in the press. Sometimes we laugh about it, but it doesn't go any further," he adds. By shutting out the noise, Barcola has become an important player for both club and country.

players.name: N'Golo Kanté
players.bio: When Kanté left Chelsea off the back of a season where injuries limited him to just nine appearances in all competitions, he was written off, especially when the midfielder chose to continue his career in the Saudi Pro League. But Deschamps is loyal; his selection choices have proven that in recent years, and all the France manager needed to be convinced to reintegrate Kanté was for him to play. He did that in the Gulf State. Not only was Kanté back for Euro 2024, he even once wore the captain's armband for the very first time. His humility has become his trademark, but it can also have the effect of masking his great ambition. "I don't simply dream of playing at the World Cup, I want to win it," said Kanté, whose days in Bleu, many thought, were numbered. He arrives at the World Cup having played at Fenerbahce since the start of February.

players.name: Adrien Rabiot
players.bio: It is paradoxical how a player whose career has been so volatile can be so regularly reliable. That's Rabiot: the player who refused a place in Didier Deschamps' reserve squad back in 2018 and then left formative club PSG in acrimonious circumstances soon after. His spell at Juventus allowed him to regain his place in the France squad. He has been a mainstay at international level ever since, but drama has continued to follow him at club level. In the image of the club itself, Rabiot's time at Marseille was chaotic. There was a legal battle with PSG after an offensive banner from the capital club’s fans, targeting Rabiot’s mother, as well as fisticuffs with Jonathan Rowe last summer that brought about a swift exit. At Milan, he benefited from the guidance of Max Allegri, whom he described as a paternal figure. Calm has returned for now; it remains to be seen if Allegri’s departure in May changes that.

players.name: Ibrahima Konaté
players.bio: Konaté has been cast in the “Robin” role both for club and country: always the sidekick, never the main hero. At club level, he was in the shadow of Virgil van Dijk, and in the national team, he is often partnered alongside William Saliba, similarly considered one of the best centre-backs in world football and included in the Premier League's Team of the Year three seasons running. It is fitting, therefore, that Konaté is a big manga fan. He said that he will often watch fight scenes to bolster his "determination" pre-match, despite derision from his Liverpool teammates. After deciding to leave Anfield this summer, Konaté may need to exude more “main character energy” before too long – but, now in his prime years, the Frenchman certainly has time on his hands to grow into the role. In January he had to deal with the sudden death of his father but, fittingly, scored on his return and the whole team, including Alisson, who rushed all the way from his goal, celebrated with the defender.

players.name: Mike Maignan
players.bio: There was understandably a lot of scrutiny when Maignan replaced Hugo Lloris as France's No 1. When the former Tottenham shot-stopper hung up his gloves at international level, he did so as the most capped player in the nation's history. Lloris wasn't always spectacular – perhaps he never was – but he was very reliable. And so in that sense, Maignan has represented perfect continuity. The fact that he is rarely spoken about can only be viewed positively. Following in the footsteps of a legend can be daunting but Maignan has handled that, even growing into more of a leadership role inside the France dressing room. "I like to speak," he says. In 2021 he was subjected to racial abuse in a game against Juventus and wrote eloquently on Instagram: "I am not a 'victim' of racism. I'm Mike, upright, black and proud.”

players.name: William Saliba
players.bio: Saliba doesn't lack self-awareness or confidence. "I am not the best defender in the world, but yeah; one of the best," says the Arsenal centre-back. If there is still progress to be made, Mikel Arteta believes that Saliba will make it. "I think that he will go to a new level in the next few years," says the Arsenal manager. Getting to his current level took time and multiple loan moves back to his native France. During that time, there were questions over whether Saliba would ever make it at Arsenal. But they were no longer posed when he returned to the Emirates for the third and final time in 2022, becoming an integral part of the Arsenal defence. He now speaks of being a "leader" for France: the next step to becoming the best defender in the world? Those are the kinds of questions now asked.

players.name: Warren Zaïre-Emery
players.bio: Zaïre-Emery was billed as France's next wonderkid, but he came back down to earth in 2025. Having become the youngest player to play for PSG when he debuted at the age of 16, he was in the France squad before he had turned 18. Scoring on debut only increased the noise. "The sky is the limit. I've never seen a player that young being so mature," said Thierry Henry, who worked with Zaïre-Emery during his time as France Under-21 manager. Fewer minutes and a loss of form saw him return to the France Under-21 side last autumn. "Confidence is playing a big part," he admitted. "It is up to me to mentally go and free myself, to play how I know I can, to have that carelessness when I was young." Still only 20, he worked his way back into the PSG side this year and back into the senior side, too.

players.name: Théo Hernández
players.bio: Théo, like Lucas, has followed the same career path as his father, Jean-François, who, during his playing days, featured for the likes of Marseille and Sochaux. The Hernández brothers are not the only siblings involved in the France national team to follow in the footsteps of their father. Marcus and Khéphren Thuram, sons of Lilian, are both regularly involved – but unlike Lilian, Jean-François is not exactly present. “He watches [his sons] alone, because he cries from virtually start to finish," Jean-François' eldest daughter, and stepsister of the Hernández brothers, once told France Football. That was part of an investigation into Jean-François' whereabouts. He famously went missing, without any trace, back in 2004, when the pair were very young. The investigation found that Jean-François had left for Thailand before returning in 2020. He remains estranged, but will look on from afar as his two sons likely once again compete for the same left-back berth.

players.name: Désiré Doué
players.bio: Off the pitch, you are given the picture of a player who is very regimented. Those who have worked with him praise his "seriousness" and "maturity" when coming through the ranks at academy level. "Everything is oriented towards performance [with Doué]," says the QPR head coach, Julien Stéphan, who worked with him at Rennes. Overall, it is an image completely at odds with the free, expressive and dazzling player that we see on a pitch, week in, week out at PSG. In a world of backwards, sideways football, Doué's instinct is to provoke a one-on-one situation. He is being tipped for an even brighter future with Stéphan saying he has the potential to be a Ballon d'Or winner. Fitting for a player whose name literally translates as “desired talented”.

players.name: Lucas Hernández
players.bio: Lucas Hernandez has struggled to catch a break; injuries have always struck at the cruellest times. A starter in the team that won the World Cup in 2018, he missed almost the entirety of the 2022 edition in Qatar after rupturing his anterior cruciate ligament 13 minutes into Les Bleus' opener against Australia. He was struck with another ACL injury at the end of the 2023-24 season, ruling him out of Euro 2024. And now he is struggling to get into the Paris St-Germain team because it just so happens that, in Nuno Mendes, Les Parisiens have arguably the best left-back in the world, consigning Hernandez to a back-up role and naturally limiting his minutes. His status at his club is not without consequence at international level but he has still made the squad.

players.name: Jean-Philippe Mateta
players.bio: For his teammates, Mateta's dream of playing for Les Bleus was not taken too seriously. “At Crystal Palace, right at the start, when I wasn’t even playing, I spoke about the France team in the dressing room and I had teammates like Wilfried Zaha who were laughing," he said. Those comments weren't meant as an attack on Zaha, even if the Ivorian did take it that way. But even a year ago, the France national team looked like a pipe dream, but one made possible by Mateta's rise to prominence in recent seasons. Fourteen goals in the 2024-25 Premier League season paved a way into the national team setup. Given his chance, he flourished, netting two goals in his first three appearances at senior international level.

players.name: Robin Risser
players.bio: Since BlueCo's arrival, Strasbourg have looked to hoover up many of the best talents across France. However, Risser, who was already within their ranks having graduated from their academy, evidently was not considered one of them. A loan in Ligue 2 with Red Star the previous season earned plaudits, but he would not make a first-team appearance for Le Racing, whose past two seasons have been devoted to developing the goalkeepers on Chelsea's books. Their loss. Risser carved out a role in a title-challenging Lens side and the level of interest that he is reportedly garnering is proof that he can reach the highest level. To do so, however, he must handle the pressure of expectation and in that regard, there is margin for improvement. His worst performance of the season – perhaps uncoincidentally – came with France's goalkeeping coach looking on from the stands.

players.name: Rayan Cherki
players.bio: Cherki has always expressed his disdain for stats. It is clear where his focus lies. "Robots are good but magic is better," he recently told France Football. "It's good to complete 99% of your passes, but it's even better to produce five or six moments of genius. It's frustrating because many coaches look for players who run the most or jump the highest. For me, that's not football. My aim is to make old-style football fashionable again." Cherki, despite his best intentions, may not have the power to define where football is heading, but he does have the ability to bring us joy when we watch it and he has done that ever since breaking through at Lyon, only now he does so with greater effect and rightly earns greater plaudits. He arrives at the World Cup after a superb season at Manchester City, which included a sensational solo goal against Arsenal in the league and some showboating against the same opponents in the League Cup final. Not everyone enjoyed his keepy-uppies, though, with Ben White clattering into him moments later.

players.name: Maghnes Akliouche
players.bio: Didier Deschamps quickly discovered Akliouche's character. "He is quite introverted," said the France manager. His timidness also translated on the pitch during his first stint with the France squad back in September. It was an opportunity missed for the Monaco forward, but Deschamps is one to give second chances and Akliouche seized his. As he came out of his shell off the pitch, he began to flourish on it, too, delivering the types of performances that got him into the national team setup in the first place. "France has so many players on a top level, but I cannot see a player with the same skills as Maghnes," said his former coach at Monaco, Adi Hütter. Like his competitors for a place in the squad, the likes of Rayan Cherki and Michael Olise, Akliouche is blessed with technical quality, but he has now started to add goals and assists to his game, too.

players.name: Maxence Lacroix
players.bio: Through his words and his actions, you get the impression that Lacroix is very selfless. Through an initiative from his club, Crystal Palace, he has given out bags of clothes to homeless people in south London and he has also participated in development projects in his native village of Ajat. "I grew up watching my mother, a doctor, being at other people's service," Lacroix told L'Équipe. "So I have always had this idea that helping others was the most beautiful thing that you can do. I don't see it as a chore but rather an activity that makes me flourish.” He has also been in the business of making dreams come true. He recalls being stopped by an elderly man on the street who, after Palace's semi-final victory in the FA Cup last year, told him that he wanted to see Palace win a trophy before he died. Lacroix said that he thought of that man when Palace lifted the trophy at Wembley. Having arrived in London from Wolfsburg in 2024, Lacroix only had two caps by the time he was called up for the World Cup squad.

players.name: Manuel Neuer
players.bio: Without doubt the most talked-about selection in the World Cup squad. As recently as the spring, there had been no sign that Neuer, who had retired after Euro 2024, would make a return to the national team after all. Then, in early April, the 40-year-old put in a masterclass against Real Madrid in the first leg of Bayern’s Champions League quarter-final. He was man of the match, preserving a 2-1 win with saves and composure, and driving Kylian Mbappé and Vinícius Júnior to despair. Just like the old days. Even though he made a mistake in the second leg, hardly anyone doubts that Neuer is a better goalkeeper than Oliver Baumann, who turned 36 at the start of June. “Everyone knows what aura and quality Manu has, what he gives a team,” Julian Nagelsmann said. Whether Nagelsmann’s last-minute decision to recall Neuer (and demote Baumann from No 1) has any effect on the players’ trust in the coach will only become clear in the coming weeks.

players.name: Antonio Rüdiger
players.bio: The most-criticised German international of them all at the moment and Julian Nagelsmann has admitted that Rüdiger is a polarising figure. That has little to do with his performances, though, and much more with his behaviour in certain instances. There was the throat-slitting gesture towards Atlético Madrid’s fans, for which he was fined, and the tantrum after the 2025 Copa del Rey final against Barcelona when he tried to throw ice at the referee and called him, among other things, a “freak” (“Missgeburt” in German). He was suspended for six games. Add to that the challenge on Getafe’s Diego Rico in March and there is a pattern emerging. “When you’re under that much criticism as an international, then you start thinking,” Rüdiger later told the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung. He knows he has had incidents “that were clearly over the line” and admits he has not lived up to his responsibilities. “I don’t want to be a source of unrest, I want to provide stability and security,” he says. Remarkably, in more than 80 games for Germany he has never been sent off. For Real, after some injury problems, he was a fixture in the team towards the end of the season but, for Germany going into the World Cup, Tah and Schlotterbeck were ahead of him in the pecking order.

players.name: Waldemar Anton
players.bio: “The defensive tower”, as he is known in the German media, is very popular – apart from in the Stuttgart metropolitan region. VfB fans have not forgiven him for moving to Dortmund after Euro 2024, even though he had just extended his contract at Stuttgart shortly beforehand. “I originally had no intention of changing clubs – but then Borussia Dortmund came in. A top club that had just reached the Champions League final,” he said. He plays solidly, unspectacularly, and almost always well. He also dares to play a risky pass. “Waldemar Anton is an extremely reliable defender with a big heart,” Nagelsmann once said. He was born in Uzbekistan as Wladimir Anton but when his parents moved to Germany in 1998, his first name was changed to Waldemar. Short form: Wowa.

players.name: Jonathan Tah
players.bio: Defensive leader at Bayern and defensive leader of the national team. Finally. At 19, Tah was named Germany’s best young footballer but, as time went on, many gave up on the idea that this huge defensive talent would ever become an important international. “There were injuries, dips in form, tough moments. People expected me to take on responsibility early. Maybe I put too much pressure on myself,” he told Die Zeit two years ago. Under Xabi Alonso at Leverkusen, however, Tah became the best centre-back in the Bundesliga: strong in duels, good in the air, hard to dribble past, and with few mistakes. Alonso may, in fact, have saved Tah’s career. On Instagram, the 30-year-old describes himself as “dedicated, ambitious and open-minded individual, striving for the best version of himself.” That’s worked out pretty well in recent years. Tah will play his first World Cup match in North America.

players.name: Aleksandar Pavlovic
players.bio: The son of a German mother and a Serbian father is a Munich native and has been wearing the Bayern shirt since he was seven years old. That is a good starting place to become a club legend and ever since Thomas Tuchel promoted him to the first team three years ago, he has been part of Bayern’s starting XI. These days he is also a starter for the national team. His role model? Sergio Busquets. “Busquets was very strong both with and without the ball,” Pavlovic once told the club magazine 51.”He was always available for a pass and always had an eye on his surroundings. On top of that, he was very solid defensively and covered a lot of ground.” He also admires Rodri. Pavlovic clearly sees himself as a No 6 but to become a world-class player in that position, needs to improve defensively.

players.name: Joshua Kimmich
players.bio: It is a pity there are not two Joshua Kimmichs: one for central midfield, one for defence. Then the endless debate about which position he is more suited to could be avoided. For Bayern’s manager, Vincent Kompany, he is the first-choice No 6. Under Julian Nagelsmann he was first at right-back, then briefly returned to midfield, and now is meant to do everything at once as a defensive playmaker. In build-up play, he moves from right back into the centre, which can be risky if he loses the ball. Luckily enough, he does not lose the ball that often. Recently moved past Jürgen Kohler and into the top 10 of Germany players with most appearances. With Bayern he has won the Champions League and 10 Bundesliga titles, but nothing yet with the national team. After the group-stage exit in Qatar, he spoke with tears in his eyes of the most difficult day of his career: “I’m a bit afraid of falling into a hole.” He has certainly worked his way out of it and, as captain, main motivator and “shirt-tucked-in” advocate, enjoys great respect in Germany.

players.name: Kai Havertz
players.bio: The Arsenal forward loves donkeys. Really loves them. “It started when I was young, I somehow developed a love for them. The guys know about it too, and some of them joke about it,” he once confessed. “In general, a donkey is a great animal.” Arrives at the World Cup after another injury-interrupted campaign, having only been playing regularly in the spring. This intelligent striker is comfortable in many positions. Nagelsmann may use him as a centre-forward, but values his versatility. Scored the winning goal in the 2021 Champions League final for Chelsea against Manchester City but, despite making his Germany debut in 2018, he has not reached 60 caps yet.

players.name: Leon Goretzka
players.bio: The 31-year-old Bayern midfielder has arguably the most turbulent on-off relationship with Julian Nagelsmann in German football. In Munich, they once formed a powerhouse duo and even as the Germany coach initially selected the player, before surprisingly dropping him for Euro 2024 with the all-powerful Toni Kroos returning to the squad. Then peace talks were held. “A discussion was necessary; it was the basis for further cooperation,” Goretzka told Die Zeit. The therapy – probably candlelit and with a tactics board – worked. Goretzka is no longer a starter at Bayern and is set to leave the club at the end of the season so to achieve anything with Germany in the summer, he needs to rediscover his true strength: scoring goals.

players.name: Jamie Leweling
players.bio: Leweling almost did not play for Germany at all. His father is from Ghana and the country’s FA approached Leweling early on, but the youngster politely declined. “Because I’m German,” he said after making his Nationalmannschaft debut in 2024. “Of course I have roots there but I grew up here in Germany and that makes me German.” Born in Nuremberg, he has Bundesliga experience with Greuther Fürth, Union Berlin and Stuttgart. He arrives at the World Cup on the back of his best season ever. Known as a player who works extremely hard and someone who always does extra sessions, he is quick and has decided some Bundesliga games almost single-handedly. Scored the only goal of the game on his international debut against the Netherlands.

players.name: Jamal Musiala
players.bio: At 17, he scored in the Champions League and, at 19, was one of few Germany players to come out of the World Cup with his reputation intact, or even improved. The three goals he scored at Euro 2024 made him an international star. But then, at the 2025 Club World Cup, it all came to a shuddering halt with a gruesome injury suffered in a collision with Gianluigi Donnarumma. He was out for six months as Germany qualified for the World Cup and Bayern got off to a near-perfect start. He is not quite the same player he was before the injury but started and played well in the incredible 4-5 Champions League defeat at Paris Saint-Germain in April. To protect him, Nagelsmann left Musiala out of the friendlies in March amid some uncertainty over whether he should go to the World Cup. “If I feel that something isn’t right in my game,” says Oliver Kahn, “then I have to work on myself until I’m ready again.” Musiala, who earns more at Bayern than the England captain Harry Kane, must be careful not to be overtaken by the likes of Lennart Karl, who is five years younger.

players.name: Nick Woltemade
players.bio: Woltemade’s rise has been rapid. Within a year he went from being a squad player at Stuttgart to becoming a €70m Premier League signing and a Germany international. His unorthodox style – at 1.98m tall, somewhat awkward – quickly made him a fan favourite. “Sometimes I lie in bed at night and think: wow, this is all so crazy,” he told Zeit Campus. In Newcastle, he lives in a shared apartment with his best friend. “We have breakfast together, chill in the evening, and watch football when I’m not playing.” After a promising start at Newcastle, however, Woltemade has lost his starting place and watched the Champions League tie against Barcelona from the bench. Still, he seems to be in favour with Julian Nagelsmann and scored four goals in World Cup qualifying.

players.name: Oliver Baumann
players.bio: Germany’s No 2 – who until a few weeks ago was still No 1 – is known above all for being fairly unknown, even though he has made more than 500 Bundesliga appearances. That is partly because he played the majority of those matches for Hoffenheim. It is also because Baumann has only been an international goalkeeper for about 18 months. He took over the spot from the injury-plagued Marc-André ter Stegen. Baumann is dependable, and he proved that during a difficult World Cup qualifying campaign. He told Zeit: “I believe that one of my greatest strengths is consistency.” Baumann is unlike many of his predecessors, who were the sort of figures you had to fear (at least on the pitch): Toni Schumacher, Oliver Kahn, “Mad” Jens Lehmann. By contrast, Baumann is someone you could trust to, say, look after your pets. “I’m not a fan of big statements,” he said. “I think what should count are my actions on the pitch.”

players.name: Pascal Gross
players.bio: He returned “home” in January – not to Mannheim, where he was born, but to the English seaside town of Brighton. He played there for seven years before moving to Dortmund, his first major European club. In the end, however, he wasn’t able to make an impact with the Bundesliga side and made the switch back. Once seen as a potential successor to Toni Kroos, Gross has not quite lived up to that.  A curious fact: he suffers from a grass allergy – quite inconvenient for a footballer. To avoid getting rashes, he tends to play in long-sleeve shirts. “I actually train in a base layer for my legs as well, no matter the weather,” he says. For this World Cup, it could get very hot for the midfielder.

players.name: Maximilian Beier
players.bio: The only player to have been born in the east of the country. Beier is a big darts fan. When he scores, he sometimes celebrates by throwing an imaginary dart. What Beier especially admires is the composure of darts players such as Luke Littler. “I find it hard to imagine what that tunnel must be like when he is in it, that absolute focus,” he once told Kicker. Beier is light-footed, quick, carefree and capable of a flash of inspiration. The Dortmund forward thrives on intelligent movement off the ball, rather than one-on-one dribbling. At the World Cup he is unlikely to be more than an impact substitute, but he will certainly not cause any trouble because of that. When announcing the squad, Julian Nagelsmann praised Beier’s diligence. In a FAZ questionnaire, Beier said that the most important coach of his career had been Patrick Schrade, one of the coaches at the youth academy boarding school of Energie Cottbus.

players.name: Nico Schlotterbeck
players.bio: The kind of defender many German football fans love: he always throws himself fully into challenges, is strong in the air, loves a sliding tackle and celebrates after winning duels. Playing against Schlotterbeck hurts. He also scores goals every now and then, after which he shows the crowd his impressive biceps. He can sometimes work himself into a frenzy and then is not always in control, becoming a security risk in his own penalty area. It would perhaps help if he took a leaf out of Fabio Cannavaro’s book of clever defending; the Italian was his role model after all. The only left-footer among Germany’s centre-backs, he is arguably in the starting XI for exactly that reason.

players.name: Angelo Stiller
players.bio: Was initially not included in the squad for the March friendlies, which Julian Nagelsmann said would give a clear indication of who was going to the World Cup. However, after injuries to Aleksandar Pavlović and Felix Nmecha, he was not only called up, but started both wins against Switzerland and Ghana. Strange? Not in the coach’s world. “Stiller is an outstandingly good footballer with enormous potential and very consistent performances,” Nagelsmann says. Pavlović goes into the World Cup as the favourite to start at No 6, but who knows what will happen.

players.name: Florian Wirtz
players.bio: In Germany, Wirtz is known as the player who made Bayer Leverkusen’s miraculous league and cup double in 2024 possible, ending Bayern Munich’s long-standing dominance. He has also shone for Germany, including a sensational performance in the 4-3 victory against Switzerland in March, but his major breakthrough is yet to come. Has had an up-and-down first season at Anfield after his £100m transfer last summer with his manager Arne Slot saying things will only get better. “Full credit to Wirtz on the pitch and the gym, putting in the work,” the Dutchman says. “If someone is struggling physically at the start, you have to keep them in to improve and he was an example of that.” When his pre‑Euro 2024 ranking of potato dishes on the German football federation’s TikTok account went viral (he put a “normal” potato at No 1 to much public amusement), he responded: “I don’t find it entertaining at all.” At the World Cup it still remains to be seen whether he will be given the central role his talent warrants.

players.name: Nathaniel Brown
players.bio: “He already ranks among the best full-backs in Europe,” Frankfurt’s sporting director, Markus Krösche, told Bild in April. That may be a bit of an exaggeration but it is true that Brown has had a very, very good season for an otherwise disappointing Eintracht side. Brown is super quick, strong in his dribbling, a goal threat and – not entirely unimportant for a defender – very hard to get past. With an American father he could have played for the USA, but, after making his international debut in 2025,  it now seems Germany have found a future solution to the problematic left-back position. Or has he already made that his own? “I’m expecting very, very much from him,” Nagelsmann says. “Maybe his time has already come now, but it will definitely come, sooner or later.”

players.name: Leroy Sané
players.bio: The general belief in Germany was that Sané had probably missed his chance of making the World Cup squad by moving to Galatasaray in 2025, but here he is. He has not been outstanding in Turkey but staked his claim with two goals and an assist in the crucial World Cup qualifier against Slovakia. And, in truth, he possesses everything a world-class player needs, apart from maybe his weaker foot (the right one). Has been criticised for not making the most of his career but still has stockpiled two Premier League titles and four Bundesliga shields. Not too bad. Early on in his career he had a large tattoo of himself inked on his back.  “Today I wouldn’t have done it,” he admitted to Der Spiegel.

players.name: Nadiem Amiri
players.bio: Amiri’s selection was a bit of a surprise. His international career began under Joachim Löw, but he was out of the picture for a long time afterwards, only returning to the fold last year. Julian Nagelsmann still knows Amiri from their Hoffenheim days, when he helped the then 16-year-old begin his professional career, and has praised his “exceptional finishing” and hunger. After picking up a heel injury playing for Mainz, the midfielder missed the March friendlies. In the Bundesliga and in Europe, though, his numbers showed why Nagelsmann could not do without him: 17 goals and three assists, with hardly any other German midfielder posting figures similar. He also made a decisive contribution to World Cup qualification with his first Germany goal against Northern Ireland last September. His cousin Zubayr has over 30 caps for Afghanistan.

players.name: Alexander Nübel
players.bio: Nübel has been with Bayern Munich for six years now, joining to be ready when Manuel Neuer retired. And since then has waited, and waited, and waited. There have been loan spells, to Monaco and long-term to Stuttgart but it is debatable whether he will ever be Bayern’s No 1. Nübel is outstanding in one-on-one situations and shots from close range but sometimes struggles with efforts from further out. This is presumably why the national team coach, Julian Nagelsmann, has made Baumann his No 1, with perhaps fewer highs but also fewer lows. Nagelsmann has praised Nübel for being supportive of Baumann. In other words: a good second-choice goalkeeper.

players.name: David Raum
players.bio: There is one title Raum definitely holds: that of the most tattooed German international. His chest reads “Living the dream” – because he gets to be a professional footballer. At one point he had almost given up on that dream, as he recounted in a YouTube video published by his club RB Leipzig. Because he was not always playing at Greuther Fürth, he thought: “OK, maybe football isn’t going to work out after all.” But his career took off after he joined Hoffenheim on a free in 2021 before RB Leipzig paid €26m for him a year later. He started every game for Germany at the World Cup in Qatar, then lost his place, then won it back. Julian Nagelsmann, values him for his fighting spirit, among other things. Raum’s strengths are in attack as he is capable of whipping in cross after cross, though a fair few of them end up missing their targets. After his career he wants nothing more to do with football. Instead, he could imagine working as a DJ.

players.name: Felix Nmecha
players.bio: Born in Hamburg, raised in England, and developed at Manchester City, that is Nmecha’s back story. But despite that, and English being his first language, the German FA secured his international future early, along with that of his brother Lukas. Felix recently extended his contract at Dortmund and is expected to be one of the pillars of the club’s future. He has developed into one of the most physically dominant central midfielders in the league, combining that with good technique and a good shot. Nmecha is happy to talk about his religion and has said that “Jesus is my everything”. Three years ago he liked and shared transphobic and homophobic content on social media, which led some Dortmund fans protesting about him joining the club. Nmecha claimed that “some things had been taking out of context” and added: "l'm a Christian and I love all people. I also do not discriminate. I hope all the fans will give me a chance to get to know me and hopefully see that I am a great person."

players.name: Malick Thiaw
players.bio: Thiaw can do something that almost certainly no other German international can: speak Finnish. His mother is Finnish while his father comes from Senegal. It was his father who taught him: “We should be grateful for everything we have”. He moved to Milan at the age of 21 and it was there – no surprise – that he developed an interest in fashion. Mystifying. What’s wrong with three-quarter-length trousers and sandals? Joined Newcastle for €35m last year and immediately established himself as a regular. A quiet leader. Newcastle’s manager Eddie Howe describes him as “very, very calm, pretty quiet, but very, very focused.” According to his teammate Nick Woltemade, he can’t head the ball but somehow nodded home two goals in one Premier League game this season.

players.name: Assan Ouédraogo
players.bio: The replacement for Lennart Karl, who had to fly back to Germany after a torn thigh muscle. It came as something of a surprise that Julian Nagelsmann called up Ouédraogo rather than Said El Mala. He has long been regarded as one of Germany’s brightest attacking talents – the youngster from Ruhrgebiet made his debut for Schalke at the age of 17. However, for RB Leipzig, who Ouédraogo joined in the summer of 2024, he has made only 25 appearances in two years. His problem: he is constantly sidelined by injuries. But when he is fit, the skilled dribbler produces moments of brilliance. He needed just two minutes to score his first international goal. His appearance against Slovakia in November remains his only cap to date. Although Nagelsmann did not originally include him in the squad, it is entirely possible that Ouédraogo will not just be on holiday in the US, but will also get important minutes on the pitch.

players.name: Deniz Undav
players.bio: Undav was by far the most clinical German striker this season but, for some reason, Nagelsmann does not seem to rate him. He scored the winning goal in the March friendly against Ghana but afterwards the coach offered no praise, rather a critical backhand. “He wasn’t very involved in the game, had no actions before [the goal],” Nagelsmann said. “I didn’t think his performance was good.” The coach’s comments may have stemmed from Undav saying before the game that he felt he should start rather than just being a substitute, a task Nagelsmann had assigned him in the now infamous “role talks”. Nagelsmann was not entirely wrong, but saying it publicly was unusual. Someone has to put the ball in the net and, in a team without many natural finishers, Undav – a modern-day Gerd Müller – is an exception.

players.name: Lawrence Ati-Zigi
players.bio: Ati-Zigi never really wanted to be a keeper. “My friends made me, and I have lived with it,” he said. “I started playing football at the age of six as an outfield player. But one day we didn’t have a keeper, so I went between the posts, and that was it.” It goes without saying that the switch paid off. He has had a very solid national team career, representing his country at three Afcons (2019, 2021, 2023) and being first-choice at the 2022 World Cup in Qatar. He stands as the most experienced keeper in the squad this summer, having made his debut in 2018, seven years before the current number one, Benjamin Asare. He has always been a fan of drawing and painting, which helps him stay cool on the pitch. "Errors in football don't upset me," he said. "The most important thing is to keep calm. And my hobby fits very well with that. I love drawing everything that comes into my mind, including lots of fun things."

players.name: Alidu Seidu
players.bio: Educated at the Jean-Marc Guillou Academy in Ivory Coast, Seidu joined Clermont Foot in 2019, and made his first-team debut a year later. In 2024 he moved to Rennes on a four-year deal, but suffered an ACL injury that November. It has been a long road back. “It was tough because I was in a lot of pain,” he told Ouest France. “But I quickly saw it as a challenge. I have been through worse … I grew up in a very tough, very violent area. Being around friends who were into that lifestyle and hanging out on the streets made me a bit aggressive, mean. We walked the streets with knives or machetes, I committed thefts, vandalism, and small-time trafficking. My mother was always crying, and sometimes I ran away at night to avoid my father punishing me.” When he was attacked with a machete during a fight he had to spend three months in hospital. “I lost a lot of blood, I couldn’t speak… Everyone thought I was going to die. So I’m lucky to have become a player, to be here. I always try to turn the negative side into something positive, it’s in my character.
