My uncle pushed me towards football because he knew I had something. He always supported me, and football saved my life.”

players.name: Caleb Yirenkyi
players.bio: Another product of the famous Right to Dream Academy, Yirenkyi joined Danish side FC Nordsjælland in 2024 and was the toast of Ghana fans after two appearances in last year’s Kirin Cup. A powerful, skilful and versatile performer, adept box-to-box or as a right-wing back, he is often compared to Michael Essien, who is now one of his coaches in Denmark. On Essien’s influence, Yirenkyi says: “He can coach and impact everyone and anyone. We learn a lot from him and are very grateful to have such a guy.” Yirenkyi arrived at the World Cup after months of transfer speculation linking him with England’s biggest sides. His club and international teammate Prince Amoako Junior told Joy Sports in January: “He doesn’t see himself the way we see him because he’s a very humble guy. But Caleb is insane, he’s crazily good. He’s a superstar, and I tell him so every time.”

players.name: Jonas Adjetey
players.bio: After making 29 starts in 32 appearances in his breakthrough campaign and enjoying four years with Basel, Adjetey moved to Wolfsburg for a reported €10m at the start of this year, and made swift progress, improving his consistency, timing his tackles better, and perfecting long diagonal balls. His development, and Mohammed Salisu’s unfortunate ACL injury, have made him almost an automatic pick for Ghana this summer. He had a tough upbringing in Teshie, a suburb of Accra. “We didn’t really have much,” he told Wolfsburg’s website. “You go to school, play football and help your parents. Three things, every day.” When he needed football boots he had to get up at 5am and go to a construction site with his friends. “I had to work. I helped out there so I could afford them.” Then he lost his father in a car accident. “I am very sad that he can no longer witness what he invested so much time and passion in … My parents gave everything for my sister and me.”

players.name: Thomas Partey
players.bio: Part of the Ghana side for a decade, Partey is at his second World Cup. A powerful defensive midfielder, he won the Europa League and appeared in the 2016 Champions League final with Atlético Madrid, and became the most expensive Ghanaian player ever when he joined Arsenal four years later for £45m. Four days after he left Arsenal for Villarreal in 2025 he was charged with five counts of rape and one count of sexual assault relating to alleged incidents between 2021 and 2022. Partey pleaded not guilty to all those charges at Southwark crown court in September, where a trial date was set for 2 November 2026. In February of this year he faced two new counts of rape after another woman came forward to police. In April he pleaded not guilty to those two allegations of rape. His defence lawyer, Jenny Wiltshire, said: “Thomas Partey continues to deny all charges against him. He has cooperated with the police throughout.”

players.name: Abdul Mumin
players.bio: It was touch and go whether Mumin would make it to North America. He only returned to competitive football in April after 13 months out following an anterior cruciate ligament injury. He sustained the injury just as he was in a golden spell of form, proving himself one of Rayo’s most consistent performers, and left him facing months of gruelling rehabilitation. But he got there – and international coach Carlos Queiroz was determined to include him for his La Liga know-how, composure and aerial dominance. He’s a solid role model, too. In 2024, when Rayo beat minnows Atlético de Lugones 6-0 in the Copa del Rey first round, Mumin spent half-time playing football with the host club’s ballboys and posing for photos with them. The club posted online later: “ We will always be happy for Abdul's success. Our ballboys haven't forgotten his kind gesture of playing with them during halftime of the Copa del Rey match.”

players.name: Abdul Fatawu
players.bio: Capable of some extraordinary moments, including three goals from the halfway line, one for Ghana’s under-20s, one for the under-23s, and one for Leicester against Ipswich in December last year which, measured at least 65 yards, was the longest-range Championship goal on record. His manager at the time, Marti Cifuentes, said: “It’s a brilliant moment from Abdul. He tries it a lot in training. He likes to look for those situations. He always pushes himself to be better.” Fatawu told the BBC: “I always try this from long range, and when I hit this one I didn’t have any doubts. I feel amazing.” He sustained an ACL injury in November 2024 but worked his way back, proved his form, and has again been linked with Premier League clubs.

players.name: Kwasi Sibo
players.bio: Until fairly recently, Kwasi was the lesser-known of the Sibo brothers. His brother Simon always drew the focus, having captained local side Liberty Professionals and represented Ghana’s youth national teams in the late 2010s. However, Kwasi truly upstaged him after helping Real Oviedo to secure La Liga promotion for the first time in 24 years. Not that Simon has conceded supremacy, though. “When I come down for holidays, Simon still trains me,” says Kwasi. “He is still my coach, and every now and then, he will teach me a trick or two! But me playing for Ghana is the fulfilment of a family dream. My brothers have always wanted one of us to play for the Black Stars.” The name Sibo in the Lambossie language (of the Sissala ethnic group in Ghana’s Upper West Region) means a big family of warriors, and Kwasi is true to that: combative, athletic and relentless on the ball.

players.name: Jordan Ayew
players.bio: After 16 years in the national team, Ayew arrives this summer on the back of a gruelling club season with Leicester, whom he joined from Palace in 2024. But while his displays have been mixed in England as the Foxes struggled, his consistently productive form for Ghana has shown what a key role he can still play at international level, with seven goals and six assists during World Cup qualifying. He was also given the captaincy, saying later: “I am living a dream and I don’t know when it will end. I never thought I would become captain, it was never in my mind, but I believe in destiny and I’m hoping for more.” He is the third Ayew to have captained the Black Stars after his legendary father Abedi, who wore the armband from 1992 to 1998, and his older brother, André, captain from 2019 to 2024.

players.name: Brandon Thomas-Asante
players.bio: It has been a prolific 12 months for Thomas-Asante. Last May, he opened his international account against Nigeria in the 2025 Unity Cup, and a year later, he helped spearhead Coventry’s Championship title charge with 13 goals in 33 appearances. It has been a remarkable rise for a player who rose through the ranks from the English National League and will play top-flight football after 10 seasons as a senior professional. “I’m really eager to keep my head down, keep ready because I’m so passionate about my nation,” Thomas-Asante said on heading to his first major tournament after success with Coventry. He has been rewarded for his great efforts this season with the iconic Ghana No 10 shirt, previously donned by Stephen Appiah, who captained the Black Stars to their World Cup record quarter-final finish in 2010, and André Ayew.

players.name: Antoine Semenyo
players.bio: Arsenal, Millwall, Reading and Tottenham turned Semenyo down as a youngster, and Palace let him go at 15 after eight weeks on trial. “I’ve never been so upset in my life about football. I was crying to my dad,” he told The Guardian in 2024. “I stopped playing for a year, just focused on school.” But, via a youth academy programme he earned a trial at Bristol City in 2017 and signed his first professional contract there. He had three loans spells before establishing himself, then joined Bournemouth for £10m in 2023, and Man City for £64m in January. “Sometimes I get memories flash up on my phone from when I was out on loan, and I compare them with now,” he said. “You go on a trip down memory lane and it is like: ‘Wow, you’ve come a long way.’” He has the word ‘chosen’ tattooed on his back. He told GQ: “I looked at [basketballer] Jayson Tatum's tattoos – he has the name of the street he grew up on and other stuff, and it said ‘chosen’ on his back. I was like, you know what? This resembles me. I feel like the chosen one, I feel like God's chosen me to be in this position.”

players.name: Joseph Anang
players.bio: A former England Under-20 keeper, Anang emerged as an unlikely option for Ghana in May last year when he was called up for the Unity Cup at Brentford in London. His debut came last November in a 2-0 loss to Japan in the Kirin Cup. A solid shot-stopper with quick reflexes, he was born in Accra but moved to England with his family as a teenager. “My parents both went abroad at different times to get a better life for our family,” he said in 2024. “My dad was an electrician and went to the US, but he has now retired, then my mum moved to the UK to work first as a cleaner, then she did a nursing course and worked in a care home for people with dementia. I lived with my grandparents and my one sister and two brothers, and followed my mum here a few years later.” He signed a scholarship deal with West Ham in 2017 but did not make a first-team breakthrough in seven years there. After solid loan spells at Stevenage and St Patrick’s, he signed a permanent deal with the League of Ireland Premier Division club in 2024.

players.name: Christopher Bonsu Baah
players.bio: Blessed with pace, close control and remarkable dribbling ability, Bonsu-Baah has long been highly rated, with some of the world’s biggest clubs circling him when he was just 14. "I was allowed to trial everywhere: at Borussia Dortmund, Barcelona, Manchester United … But because I was not yet 18, I couldn’t get a professional contract. It’s good that I have a lot of patience," he told De Zondag. In 2023, he left the lower-tier side Shooting Stars in Ghana for Sarpsborg in Norway, but stayed just four months before Belgian Pro League side Genk signed him on a five-year deal. Last year he swapped Belgium for Saudi Arabia’s Al-Qadsiah, managed by Brendan Rodgers, and made his Black Stars debut.

players.name: Gideon Mensah
players.bio: One of the most experienced players in the Black Stars squad, having made his debut in 2019 against South Africa in an Afcon 2021 qualifier in Cape Coast. He does, though, have an error in him, which is why Ghana’s coach, Otto Addo, has been through five other options in the left-back slot, trying Patrick Kpozo, Ebenezer Annan, Derrick Köhn, Abdul Fatawu Hamidu and Isaac Afful. But Mensah’s experience and positivity count for a lot. In 2019 he nearly made a dream loan move to Barcelona, to be deputy to Jordi Alba. When his agent told him about the offer, he recalled in 2024: “I couldn’t resist thinking about the next flight to Spain”. But the deal collapsed when news of the agreement leaked, breaching a confidentiality agreement. “The next day the news was everywhere in Ghana that Barcelona wanted me,” he told Flashscore. “But nobody knows what could happen in the future. The opportunity could come back again.” His Instagram bio: “Keep Praying, Keep Planning, Keep Pushing.”

players.name: Elisha Owusu
players.bio: Born in France to Ghanaian parents, Owusu started out in Lyon’s academy. He signed his first professional deal with them in 2017, but didn’t make a breakthrough there, moving instead to Sochaux on loan in 2018, then to Belgium with Gent a year later. He joined current club Auxerre in 2023. A solid, dependable leader in midfield, he has captained Auxerre and brings plenty of know-how to this squad. He made his international debut in the 2022 World Cup play-off tie against Nigeria, and spoke in February about his ambition. “It would be a big blessing to play in the World Cup, but I try not to think about it too much. For me life is about process. I take it day by day, and leave the rest to God.”

players.name: Benjamin Asare
players.bio: While at Hearts of Oak’s rivals, Great Olympics, in June 2021, Asare broke his leg in the derby against his current employers. He missed 18 months of football and was advised to consider retiring, but made a remarkable comeback, and within two years was Ghana’s first-choice goalkeeper. His introduction to the national team was a remarkable breakthrough. After Ghana conceded seven goals in six games and finished bottom of the Afcon 2025 qualifying group, Asare was drafted in, one of a raft of changes. It made him, at 32, the oldest Hearts of Oak player to make his Ghana debut, and the first of their keepers to represent the national side for 20 years. His impact was stunning: four clean sheets in five matches as Ghana secured World Cup qualification. In November last year he saved Hwang Hee-chan’s penalty in the Kirin Cup against South Korea, cementing his growing reputation.

players.name: Baba Rahman
players.bio: The former Chelsea defender is back for Ghana after a long absence, having been in self-imposed exile since 2023 when he was booed by fans during an Afcon qualifier against Central African Republic in Kumasi. He told Joy Sports last year: “The booing didn’t affect me during the match – as a professional, you learn to deal with that. But after the final whistle, as I walked to the team bus, it really sank in. It was tough to take. So I felt it was the right time to focus on my body and recover properly.” His fine form in Greece last season means he returned to the national set-up this summer in a confident mood. He spent eight years on Chelsea’s books from 2015 to 2023 but only made 23 appearances, spending most of that time on loan or in recovery having suffered two serious knee injuries. He nearly gave up after the second, in 2019. "I said to my family I was quitting," he told the BBC in 2024. "But Dr Paco [Biosca, ex-Chelsea medical director] said that he had seen other players come back and promised that if I was patient and mentally strong that I could do it too. I slept on it and then decided I wanted to fight for my career. I am grateful that football has given me a second chance. I'm just trying to enjoy it to the last moment."

players.name: Jerome Opoku
players.bio: Lambeth-born Opoku has had a nomadic route to the top. A Fulham academy product, he suffered an ACL injury aged 17, then had three loan spells, at Accrington Stanley, Plymouth and Danish side Vejle before joining Portuguese side Arouca in 2022. He moved from there to Turkey’s Istanbul Basaksehir in 2023. His form since has been impressive, and he was linked with a return to the Premier League during the last winter window. He played it down. “As I always say, it’s God’s plan, so I think that if I just keep putting in the performances, whatever comes will kind of reflect on where I could be, or where I should be,” he told World Soccer Talk. “For now, I’m here in Istanbul, and that’s what I’m focused on, but whatever happens is really God’s plan.” He made his Ghana debut in 2023, and said securing World Cup qualification last year was “just amazing … when I was on that pitch, and once the whistle went, I looked around me, I saw everyone's faces, I saw my family's faces … It made me feel like I was a part of something much bigger than just football. You see how much it means to literally everyone in the country.”

players.name: Iñaki Williams
players.bio: In 2021 Williams declined initial overtures from the Ghana Football Association. He had played once for Spain already, in a 2016 friendly, and told The Guardian: “I was born in Bilbao. I won’t ever forget my family roots, but I feel Basque and can’t con anyone. I don’t think it would be right to take the place of someone who really deserves it and who feels Ghana 100%.” But when Ghana came calling again having secured World Cup qualification in 2022, Williams changed his mind. “I still wasn’t clear about it until I went to Ghana with my parents, my family and girlfriend. We went to see the country, the family in Accra and Kumasi. It was emotional, like the whole city was waiting when I got out of the car. It was incredible, incredible. And that’s where everything changed. That’s when I went to talk to my grandad. I told him there was a chance that I could play for Ghana at the World Cup and what did he think. He said straight away that he didn’t have long left to live and he dreamed of his grandson playing for Ghana. There was nothing left to think about then.” His younger brother Nico also plays for Athletic Bilbao, and for Spain.

players.name: Augustine Boakye
players.bio: Boakye left Ghanaian football in 2021, signing a four-year deal at Austrian club Wolfsberger AC. After moving to Saint-Étienne three years later, he spoke openly about one of his biggest ambitions. “It would be a dream to play for my country, but I’m not going to demand it and say I have to. The call-up could come today or tomorrow, I don’t know when – but when it comes, it will be good, and I will be ready.” He was made to wait, but it was worth it: his first call-up coming as part of this summer’s squad. He knows his role is to feed the forwards. “I am a No10 who loves to assist. I love it most when I assist my striker because I think that when the striker scores more it gives the team more confidence. I see the striker as the main man and so when he’s not scoring and he gets frustrated it affects the whole team.” Boakye’s childhood hero was former Ghana midfielder Sulley Muntari. “He was my idol growing up because my mum loved him so much. So I watched him, and also loved him.”

players.name: Kojo Peprah Oppong
players.bio: It has been a meteoric rise. Only three years ago he was still in the second tier of Ghanaian football, with Attram De Visser Academy, a club co-owned by the former Black Star, Godwin Attram. That summer he left for Swedish side IFK Norrköping, where, via a loan spell at GIF Sundsvall, he quickly proved his potential – so much so that in August last year Nice paid €2.5m to sign him. Florian Maurice, sporting director at Nice, said: “What drew us to him was not only his speed, his mobility and his aggression in duels, but also his ability to play in different systems.” He settled fast, playing league and European football, and was soon being linked with Premier League clubs. “I’m surprised,” Oppong told Get French Football News last year. “I didn’t expect to play this much in the beginning, but I’m now playing a lot – and I expect a lot from myself.” He made his Ghana debut in the World Cup qualifier against Comoros in Accra last October. His future looks bright.

players.name: Kamaldeen Sulemana
players.bio: Pure pace. During a second-half cameo against Uruguay in the 2022 World Cup, winger Sulemana clocked 22.18mph, the fastest time recorded by any player at the tournament, surpassing the lightning-quick Alphonso Davies and Nico Williams. “I wasn’t even running fast,” he reflected later. “It was when I was running back, you know, we were down 2-0, and then it was like five minutes to go, and there was a counter against us, so I had to cover back." He joined Atalanta from Southampton for £22m and is in his sixth year with the Black Stars, with this his second World Cup and fourth major tournament overall. He used to be known for his athletic backflip goal celebration, having been taught gymnastics as well as football at Ghana’s Right to Dream Academy. But no longer. “My parents told me not to do it any more because it’s dangerous,” he said in October last year. “I did it against Juventus, but it was just an adrenaline rush, spontaneous. I won’t do it again.”

players.name: Derrick Luckassen
players.bio: It’s been quite a journey to this World Cup. Born in Amsterdam, Luckassen started out at AZ’s youth academy and made his senior club breakthrough there. He joined rivals PSV on a five-year deal in 2017, then had a series of loans, at Hertha and Anderlecht, then Turkish clubs Kasimpasa and Fatih Karagumruk. He moved to Cyprus with Pafos in 2024, helping them win the title in his first season, and, on 5 November 2025, it was his headed goal that gave the club their first ever win in the Champions League proper – a shock league stage 1-0 victory over Villarreal. After representing the Netherlands at youth level without making a senior breakthrough, he finally received his first Ghana call-up this March. He made the final cut for this summer when Alexander Djiku was ruled out with a hamstring injury.

players.name: Ernest Nuamah
players.bio: Nuamah emerged as one of Ghana’s most promising offensive talents from Right to Dream, one of West Africa’s most prestigious academies. After developing his creative and pace-driven style in Denmark, he joined Lyon on loan before signing permanently with the Ligue 1 club. In 2024, a deal with Fulham had been agreed but the move collapsed after Nuamah disappeared during his medical. His agent confirmed the player’s deep affection for Lyon, leading to the club owner, John Textor, to apologise for attempting to pressure the winger into the transfer. In 2025, he ruptured his anterior cruciate ligament and a year later he needed only three substitute appearances for his club to convince Carlos Queiroz that he should go to the World Cup. The 22-year-old says: “You should expect the Nuamah you know. I’ve trained very hard, and with the support of my teammates, I think I’m ready to give everything I can for this nation. It hasn’t been easy for me, getting an injury. I’ve worked really hard to get to this point and I can’t wait to give my all.”

players.name: Prince Kwabena Adu
players.bio: Six years ago Kwabena Adu, then at Ghana’s Bechem United, was named on the Guardian’s Next Generation list of 60 of the best young talents in world football. But in 2021, just before leaving to represent Ghana at the Under-20s Africa Cup of Nations he sustained a career-threatening ligament injury which kept him out for two years. "It was a really difficult moment in my life," Adu told Flashscore last year. "It was tough so I went back to my hometown to stay there and relax my mind. At that point I wasn't thinking about playing football again. Being injured for such a long period made me question a lot of things." But he did recover, working his way back to form and fitness, and made a move to Czech giants Viktoria Plzen in August 2024. His icon: "My style is very similar to Luis Suárez so I watch his videos a lot. The way Suárez scores, runs, his movement, with and without the ball, and off the pitch. I like everything about him and really learn a lot from him. I even still watch him in the MLS.”

players.name: Marvin Senaya
players.bio: At the start of the year not many would have expected the 25-year-old to be in the World Cup squad but, having made his national team debut in March against Austria, he excelled against Wales in May and is now competing for a spot in the starting XI. The France-born right-back started out at Strasbourg and has worked his way up the leagues – including a spell in Switzerland with Lausanne – before he joined Auxerre in 2025. He could have chosen to play for Togo – his father is Yao Mawuko Senaya, who won 26 caps for the country of his birth between 1998 and 2008. In March, Marvin became one of only a few footballers to have been called up to two national teams for the same international window as both Ghana and Togo included him in their squads. “I feel deeply honoured to represent my country,” he said. “Ghana has a rich footballing history and I will wear the shirt with pride, loyalty and commitment.”

players.name: Johny Placide
players.bio: Johny Placide’s career has been built on patience, resilience, and authority earned the hard way. Developed at Le Havre, he worked his way through French football before becoming a trusted last line of defence for Bastia. Not flashy, but fiercely reliable, Placide is known for sharp reflexes, strong positioning and safe hands, qualities that earned him the nickname “The Guardian.” He commands his area vocally, excels under pressure, and reads the game with the calm of a veteran. Born in Montfermeil, France, to Haitian parents, he chose to represent Haiti, embracing his roots with pride. A mainstay since 2011 and often captain, Placide has guided younger teammates through demanding campaigns, becoming a pillar of stability during the historic qualification push. As the dream of 2026 became reality, he declared: “We’ll be there to enjoy it and make the people proud.”

players.name: Carlens Arcus
players.bio: From the spirited streets of Port-au-Prince, where he enjoyed what he describes as “a very happy, very peaceful childhood”, to the roar of Concacaf Gold Cup finals, Arcus has worn challenges with pride, like badges. Fast, fearless in attack and possessing tremendous stamina, he embodies the modern full-back: defender first, creator when it counts, and competitor always. A pillar of the Haiti national team since 2016, Arcus delivered the cross that sealed Haiti’s first World Cup berth since 1974, ending a 51-year drought. “I always had two dreams in my life,” he said afterwards. “I’d already achieved the first, which was to sign a professional contract. The second has just come true.” Arcus first caught the eye in France with Auxerre, where his overlapping runs and consistency made him indispensable, before refining his game with Vitesse Arnhem in the Netherlands.

players.name: Keeto Thermoncy
players.bio: When Thermoncy pulls on Haiti’s colours, he carries two identities in every stride. Raised in Switzerland but fiercely connected to his Haitian roots, the young defender once explained his bond with the national team: “When you look at Haiti’s history and all its people have endured for decades, wearing this shirt means far more than football.” It is the kind of reflection that reveals the maturity behind one of Haiti’s brightest young prospects. Forged in the disciplined academy corridors of Young Boys (but still to make a first-team appearance), Thermoncy is a modern centre-back: quick across the turf, commanding in the air and calm enough to launch attacks from deep. At over 1.80m, he marries athletic power with elegant timing, gliding into tackles with the assurance of a seasoned professional. His senior Haiti debut in World Cup qualifying felt less like a first step than a statement. Now, Thermoncy already looks built to anchor Haiti’s next generation.

players.name: Ricardo Adé
players.bio: They call him The Wall for a reason. Ricardo Adé doesn’t just defend; he commands the game from the back, snuffing out attacks before they even begin. Quick on his feet yet unbeatable in the air, he reads the pitch like a grandmaster, his fearless streak anchoring Haiti’s defence for years. Adé’s story is even more inspiring than his performances. Early in his career, he was left stranded in Thailand after a failed move, surviving on little more than bread and condensed milk for a week; an ordeal that forged the resilience behind the player. At Liga de Quito, his booming headers and calm distribution have turned tense matches into triumphs, helping him lift four domestic trophies. “Defending is about reading the game and being ready for the battle,” he says, and every duel proves it. Off the field, Adé carries the same quiet authority, representing Haitian identity with pride and professionalism wherever he goes.

players.name: Hannes Delcroix
players.bio: Born in Haiti and raised in Belgium, Delcroix grew up between two football cultures, Caribbean spirit and European precision. At Anderlecht he polished his craft; at Burnley he learned the bruising art of survival – and the need for patience. “My characteristics are someone who’s calm, off the pitch and also on the pitch,” he says and, after moving from Waalwijk to Burnley in 2023, he had to be calm as he waited for his chance. Eventually he realised it would not come, and after he was left out of their squad list at the start of the season he sought “a club that would guarantee me plenty of playing time and an important role”. He found that at Lugano. His international debut followed in October and he swiftly became a regular starter. Quick across the turf and dominant in the air, Delcroix is the kind of centre-back who steps into midfield with calm authority before launching a 40-yard diagonal as if it were second nature. Coaches praise his timing; teammates talk about his voice: steady, composed, uncompromising.

players.name: Carl Fred Sainté
players.bio: In Haiti’s Grand-Goâve, long before he became used to stadium floodlights and the MLS spotlight, Sainté learned to play on dusty pitches where the ball bounced like a question. He answers those questions now with authority. Tall, elegant and deceptively quick across the turf, Sainté is a midfielder who covers ground effortlessly, wins headers with timing, and threads passes through traffic as if he can see moves before they happen. He helped Violette to a Haitian league title, lifted the MLS NEXT Pro crown with North Texas, and climbed into Major League Soccer with Dallas. For Haiti, he plays with pride stitched into every tackle, calm amid chaos, brave in the air. Teammates call him the Metronome, because when Sainté moves, the whole team finds its rhythm. “We didn’t just qualify, we carried the heartbeat of a nation onto the pitch,” he wrote after Haiti sealed their World Cup spot. “Haiti has been through so much, but we keep showing the world what resilience looks like.”

players.name: Derrick Etienne Jr
players.bio: There is something electric about Derrick Etienne Jr when he squares up a defender: a shimmy, a burst, and suddenly he’s streaking down the flank. Born in Virginia but raised on a diet of Haitian pride, he followed his father – also Derrick – into the national team and was in turn followed by his brother, Danielle. When he first committed to Haiti, he spoke with the eagerness of a player embracing more than just football: “I’m just excited to get started… I think I’ll have a chance to be part of something special.” It proved more than words, and became a mission. Fast, skilful and surprisingly strong in the air, Etienne thrives on big moments. With the Columbus Crew he lifted the MLS Cup in 2020, one of several honors in an MLS journey that has been shaped by resilience and reinvention.

players.name: Martin Expérience
players.bio: There’s something fitting about Martin Expérience’s surname. On the pitch, he plays with experience beyond his years. Born in France but proudly tied to his Haitian roots, he carved his path through the rugged lower divisions before earning his place with Nancy, where he is swapping obscurity for packed terraces and bigger stages, as part of the team that won promotion to Ligue 2 in 2025. When Haiti sealed their place at the 2026 World Cup, Expérience could hardly contain himself. “Playing in a World Cup is extraordinary, no matter whom we face,” he said - before the draw paired them with Brazil, the country of his childhood idol, Neymar. Since debuting for Haiti in 2021 he has brought steel and stamina to the Grenadiers’ back line, a modern full-back shaped by hard miles, big dreams and a flag he wears with pride.

players.name: Duckens Nazon
players.bio: Known for his pace, physicality, and knack for finding the net on the biggest stages, Nazon certainly doesn’t drift through matches, he crashes into them. Raised in the Paris suburbs, he learned early that space has to be earned, not given. That edge followed him from Lorient’s academy to stadiums across continents – his current employers, the Tehran-based Esteghlal, are the 11th team of his senior career, which has included stints at Coventry, Oldham and St Mirren and periods in Bulgaria and India. He is known as The Beast, and not for nothing. Nazon has a habit of saving his best for the loudest nights: World Cup qualifiers, Concacaf battles, moments when nerves tighten. Before the decisive qualifier against Nicaragua Nazon gave a speech in the dressing room: “Here in Haiti, some people only have a few dollars in their pockets. They have nothing else. They only have us,” he said. “If we don’t win, they have nothing left. Today, we have the chance to give them a smile. We can make them cry with joy. They are counting on our feet. Please, don’t let them down.” His words did the trick.

players.name: Jean-Ricner Bellegarde
players.bio: If midfield were a chessboard, Jean Ricner Bellegarde would be the queen: fast, versatile, and impossible to contain. Born in Colombes, France, he honed his craft in the local youth systems. At Strasbourg, he earned a reputation for darting runs, sharp vision, and fearless tackles, traits that carried him to Wolverhampton Wanderers in the Premier League. Nicknamed The Architect, Bellegarde thrives on dictating tempo, threading passes that seem impossible, and sparking counterattacks from nothing. Switching allegiance to Haiti after representing France at youth level, he brought flair and composure to the national side, inspiring young players across the Caribbean. “He has a natural ability to accelerate and hurt the opponent,” his former Strasbourg coach Thierry Laurey once said, a line that captures perfectly the burst, intelligence, and unpredictability that define Bellegarde’s game.

players.name: Louicius Deedson
players.bio: The first thing you notice about Deedson  is the blur: by the time defenders react, he is already gone. From Haiti to the United States – where he originally moved aged 15 – via the windswept grounds of Denmark, for Deedson, every sprint feels personal, every goal a message home. He plays on the edge of chaos: skilful, always driving forward as if the pitch was tilting in his favour. For Haiti, he has become more than a threat: he’s a spark. He scored the opening goal in the game against Nicaragua that sealed his nation’s place at the World Cup, a strike loaded with history. Deedson isn’t defined by trophies, yet, but by the feeling he leaves behind. “This is the best feeling in my career so far,” he said of that goal. “It’s my feeling, and then the fans’ feeling, and then my family feeling back home.” Off the pitch, he is quieter, grounded by his journey. Those close to him speak of a player deeply attached to Haiti, carrying its flag with pride wherever he goes like football is not just a career, but a responsibility.

players.name: Alexandre Pierre
players.bio: In the cages of Aubervilliers, just north of Paris, Alexandre Pierre learned to fly. A son of Haitian heritage, he would hurl himself across pitches long before he had proper gloves, chasing a dream that stretched far beyond the périphérique. Today, at 1.91m, he commands his box with authority. Quick off his line and blessed with cat-like reflexes that turn certain goals into gasps. Pierre’s path wound through Stade Lavallois, Angers and Strasbourg, where he signed his first professional deal in 2021, later sharpening his claws on loan at Annecy. For Haiti, he has become part of a new generation daring to think bigger, featuring in Concacaf competitions and World Cup qualifiers. Calm but fiercely competitive, Pierre plays like a man still diving on concrete. Every save personal, every clean sheet a promise kept.

players.name: Duke Lacroix
players.bio: Before he was marauding down the flank for Haiti, Duke Lacroix was a student athlete at the University of Pennsylvania, balancing lectures - he got a degree in sociology, and followed it with a master’s in sports psychology - with dreams that stretched far beyond campus. Fast, fearless, and blessed with a sprinter’s engine, Lacroix plays left-back like a winger in disguise. He bombs forward, whips in crosses, wins his headers despite not being the tallest, and recovers with impressive pace. Since committing to the Haiti national football team in 2023 Lacroix has worn the colours with pride, attacking every duel as if it were personal. Outside football he is involved in politics and social activism, as vice president of United Black Players, president of the USL Players Association, the union representing players in the United Soccer League, and working for Onrise, who provide mental health support to athletes and others in high-pressure roles.

players.name: Leverton Pierre
players.bio: There’s a story they tell in Tabarre, Haiti, of a skinny kid chasing balls over cracked concrete, carrying dreams much bigger than his boots. That boy became Leverton Pierre, a tireless midfielder. His style is simple but deadly: he breaks lines with a crisp pass, tracks back with dogged determination, and reads the game like a veteran. On a cool night in 2017 he signed his first pro contract in France with Bastia, and ever since he’s been the kind of player coaches love. Pierre’s career may not glitter with headlines, but he has a Ligue 2 title and has become a mainstay for Haiti, turning out in three Concacaf Gold Cups and in all but one of their qualifiers for this World Cup. Fun fact: He once chased a dog that stole the match ball across the neighbourhood, only stopping when it buried the ball in someone’s garden.

players.name: Ruben Providence
players.bio: On the pitches of Paris St-Germain’s academy, Providence was the kind of winger who turned training into theatre: stepovers, bursts of pace, and defenders left chasing shadows. That instinct was shaped in his early days at the club but by the time he moved to Roma in 2019, the raw flair had found structure, shaped in one of Europe’s most demanding environments, where he picked up youth honours and a sharper tactical edge. Fast and skilful, Providence plays with the freedom of a street footballer and the discipline of a graduate from two European giants. His spell at TSV Hartberg added steel to his silk, bringing goals, assists and a taste for the fight. Now at Almere City in the Dutch second flight, he plays with freedom and intent. In 2025 he chose to represent Haiti, a decision rooted in pride more than convenience. Providence isn’t just chasing a career; he’s chasing a story worth telling.

players.name: Lenny Joseph
players.bio: Joseph has always played like he has somewhere to be. There is something of the street footballer about him: sharp, fearless and always playing as if the next touch might change everything. Raised in Paris and polished in the academy of Paris St-Germain, he grew up in a world where flair was currency and survival meant standing out. His rise through French football eventually led him to Budapest and Ferencvaros, where European nights and domestic success have sharpened his edge. Lightning over the first five yards, clever in the air despite his lean frame and skilful enough to unpick defenders in tight spaces, he carries that rare ability to make chaos look choreographed. Still only 25, he plays with a restless hunger: the kind of striker who seems to treat every run as a statement. For Haiti, Joseph represents more than promise: he looks like the next headline waiting to happen.

players.name: Danley Jean Jacques
players.bio: On the bustling pitches of Haiti’s Petit-Goâve, Danley Jean Jacques tackled as if survival was on the line. Friends swear he hated defeat so much he replayed matches in his head before sleep. That fire carried him from Don Bosco’s domestic title-winners to the hard grind of Metz, and on to Philadelphia in 2024. Quick across the grass, powerful in the air and fearless in the duel, he plays like a storm at the heart of midfield. In France Jean Jacques bulldozed through games, helping Metz secure promotion to Ligue 1. For Haiti he has scored vital goals, fists clenched, eyes closed in gratitude, as the Grenadiers chased history. Five years after leaving Haiti for France he has not lost his love of Haitian culture and cuisine - his favourite is diri ak sos pwa, rice with a black bean puree. “That’s the best,” he says. “Especially when my mother cooks it.” Will feel particularly at home when Haiti play Brazil – the game will be played at Philadelphia Stadium, his home ground.

players.name: Wilson Isidor
players.bio: Isidor was the boy in Rennes who chased football like it owed him something, darting between cars on makeshift alleyway pitches, celebrating imaginary goals. Tall and quick with a predator’s instinct, he is equally dangerous in the air and on the turn, blending force with surprising grace. His journey took him from Monaco’s youth ranks to trophies in Russia with Zenit St Petersburg, where he lifted league and cup silverware before sprinting into Sunderland’s attack. He had represented France through the age levels but in March, Haiti finally got its man, Isidor confirming his long-awaited decision on social media: “I’ve seen all your messages … you asked for me, you waited for me, and here I am.” He made his full debut against Iceland, and scored his first goal in the same game. He brings real pedigree to Haiti’s forward line, the promise not just of goals but the ignition of a nation’s World Cup dream.

players.name: Yassin Fortuné
players.bio: There’s a story told at Arsenal about a teenage Fortuné leaving defenders chasing shadows, his pace impossible to cage. But away from the pitch, the story softens. Raised in the Paris suburbs in a tight-knit family, Fortuné remains deeply grounded, more likely to spend time with loved ones than chase headlines. Arsenal signed him from Lens at 16 but he never played for their first team, moving to Sion in Switzerland three years later. Last summer he joined the Portuguese second-tier side Vizela and within a month was a full international, having represented France at various youth levels. Fortuné is explosive, skilful, and surprisingly strong in the air. Teammates often describe a quiet character, focused, someone who lets his football do the talking. Yet there’s a playful side too, characterised by music, dressing room laughter, and that unmistakable street-football edge that never quite left him.

players.name: Frantzdy Pierrot
players.bio: When Pierrot launches himself into the air, time seems to pause. Then the net ripples. Nicknamed “The Mountain”, the Haitian striker makes headers feel inevitable, overpowering centre-backs in the air while surprising them on the turf with soft touches and clever layoffs. Born in Cap-Haïtien but brought up in Massachusetts, his journey to Europe was anything but straightforward, passing through American college soccer before he learned to thrive under pressure on bigger stages in Belgium, France, Israel and Greece. At AEK Athens he seemed set to become the reference point up front, the man teammates search for when matches tighten and crosses start flying. But injury pushed him out of the team towards the end of last year, and he moved on loan to Turkey’s Caykur Rizespor in February. Off the pitch Pierrot keeps a low profile. A regular for Haiti, and averaging an impressive 1.5 games per goal, he carries his country’s expectations with quiet pride, reflecting a personality that is more measured than flashy: focused, grounded, and built around collective effort rather than individual spotlight.

players.name: Josué Casimir
players.bio: On the sunlit pitches of Guadeloupe, Josué Casimir learned football the hard way. No space, no time, just instinct. He grew up in a household where the game was less a hobby than a language. By the time he reached Le Havre’s academy, he already played with defiance. Coaches quickly learned not to cage him. “Let him run,” one reportedly joked, and run he does. Casimir is electric: quick over five yards, fearless in the air though he is not particularly tall, and blessed with a winger’s swagger. He doesn’t just beat defenders; he teases them. He plays with a smile you can almost hear. But beyond the flair lies something deeper. He played youth football for Guadeloupe, for whom his brother Stevenson plays in defence, but choosing Haiti, his parents’ homeland, gave his career a different heartbeat.

players.name: Jean-Kévin Duverne
players.bio: Jean‑Kévin Duverne’s story is one of quiet tenacity and hard‑earned respect. On a sun‑drenched pitch in French Guiana in 2024, when he lifted his arm skyward after clasping that first Haiti cap, there was a palpable thrill, not just another defender making his debut, but a Grenadier staking his claim at international level. The moment was the culmination of years in France’s unforgiving leagues, where he learned to mix his raw pace with unwavering defensive grit. This season he has  plied his trade in Belgium with Gent on loan from Nantes, this fearless, athletic defender is as comfortable marshaling the backline as he is sparking attacks from deep. Duverne has become a symbol of pride for Haitian fans, a guardian at the heart of their hopes and a story that’s still unfolding. Off the pitch, he’s quiet but intense, a steadfast presence whose story is still being written one fearless tackle at the time.

players.name: Josué Duverger
players.bio: On a humid night during Haiti’s qualifying campaign, Josué Duverger stood alone beneath the floodlights, the noise rising, the stakes even higher. He was just 17 when he chose to represent Haiti, a teenager with gloves slightly too big and similarly outsized reserves of courage. Born in Montréal and honed in Portugal’s academies, Duverger blends Latin technique with Caribbean fire. Quick, brave in the air and spring-heeled in one-on-ones, he is the kind of goalkeeper who seems to grow taller as shots fly toward him. At club level he has posted impressive clean-sheet runs, including a 50% shut-out rate during a season in Portugal’s Campeonato. There have been bruising lessons, including a humiliating double-miskick own-goal against Canada, the country of his birth and which he had represented at Under-17 level, but Duverger’s story is not about mistakes. It is about response: Dive. Rise. Repeat.

players.name: Wilguens Paugain
players.bio: Paugain’s CV reads like a movie script: born in extreme poverty in Thomazeau in Haiti he was adopted aged five by a family in rural France and was spotted by Nancy-Lorraine aged 12. However, he suffered a serious injury at 18 that derailed his career, forced him out of the game for eight months, cost him the chance of a professional contract and pushed him to work for a while delivering pizzas. But he fought back, and proceeded to play his way through dressing rooms scattered across Cyprus, Latvia and Austria, each stop adding a new layer to his game, before reaching Belgium. Paugain is the kind of full-back who sprints first and thinks later, but his instinct is normally proved right. He mixes raw athleticism with a growing technical edge, surging forward as if the touchline were a runway. “This is an opportunity for the world to stop neglecting Haiti,” he says. “When people talk about the country it is natural disasters, political instability and gangs, rarely about anything positive. I hope the World Cup will bring respect back to our team and our country.”

players.name: Dominique Simon
players.bio: Simon’s journey has been anything but ordinary. From France to Estonia, Georgia, Czechia and now Slovakia, Simon has built his career the hard way, gaining experience across Europe. There is something distinctly Parisian about the way he plays: calm under pressure, technically polished and always a step ahead. Formed in the demanding academy of Paris St-Germain, he learned early that survival in elite football depends as much on intelligence as flair. Quick across the ground, sharp in the tackle and surprisingly strong in the air, he mixes steel with subtlety, capable of breaking up play one moment and launching attacks with a single crisp pass the next. Simon embodies Haiti’s new-generation diaspora talent: cultured, resilient and quietly ambitious. The kind of midfielder coaches trust, teammates lean on and opponents rarely enjoy facing.

players.name: Woodensky Pierre
players.bio: In the noise of Haiti’s Cité Soleil, where football is played on instinct and survival, Pierre learned early that hesitation is expensive. Friends recall a skinny kid chasing games from sunrise to streetlight, fearless in the air despite his size, already barking instructions like a veteran. Now at Real Hope FA, Pierre is a midfield anchor with edge and elegance: quick over short distances, and skilful enough to slip out of tight spaces before snapping into a tackle. He rose through Violette, collecting domestic honours and earning recognition as one of Haiti’s most reliable young enforcers.  “Work hard and believe in yourself,” he says, a mentality shaped by his upbringing. With his eyes on success at  the 2026 World Cup, Pierre plays like a man on a mission, carrying not just the ball, but the hopes of where he came from.

players.name: Alireza Beiranvand
players.bio: It has been nearly a decade since Beiranvand established himself as Iran’s first-choice goalkeeper and he gained widespread popularity after stopping Cristiano Ronaldo's penalty at the 2018 World Cup. In recent years, however, he has been surrounded by controversy owing to some unusual statements and his departure from Persepolis. His performances for the national team have declined since his move to Tractor, leading some critics to call for his removal from Iran’s starting lineup. His famous penalty save against Ronaldo has become so ingrained in popular culture that it has been recreated in recent years in two Iranian TV series, The Rebel and The Savage, as part of their storylines. In addition, following his standout performances at Russia 2018, a film based on his turbulent life story was produced – another strong showing this summer could inspire a sequel.

players.name: Saleh Hardani
players.bio: One of Hardani’s many tattoos reflects his approach to life: patience, silence and effort. At the start of the current season, he found himself on the bench at Esteghlal and it seemed unlikely he would earn a place in the national team for the World Cup. However, he fought his way back into the starting lineup, becoming one of the team captains and earning a spot in Amir Ghalenoei’s squad. Despite his relatively short stature, Hardani possesses a strong, muscular build. This physical strength helped him deliver a standout performance against Sadio Mané in a match against Al-Nassr in the AFC Champions League Elite last year. In March, while Hardani was with the national team, his home in Tehran was severely damaged in a bombing attributed to the United States and Israel. In response, he said: “May it be sacrificed for my beloved people of the country.”

players.name: Ehsan Hajsafi
players.bio: Hajsafi has featured in all nine of Iran’s matches across the last three World Cups, but when he was not called up to the national team over the past two years, it seemed his international career might have come to an end. However, the underwhelming performances of other contenders at left-back convinced Amir Ghalenoei to recall him just months before the World Cup, giving Hajsafi the chance aged 36 to become the first Iranian player to reach double-digit World Cup appearances. One of the key reasons behind Hajsafi’s long and successful career is his calm, disciplined, and controversy-free lifestyle. On the pitch, however, he is known for his intensity: he compares his style of play with a particular emoji: “I’m aggressive on the pitch and I don’t joke with anyone. That comes from the demands of the game – everyone has to come with 100% focus and readiness. You can use the red angry emoji to describe me in matches, because I’m very serious and intense.”

players.name: Shoja Khalilzadeh
players.bio: Shoja – whose name in Persian means “brave” or “fearless” – is the kind of defender who is never intimidated, whether in on-field battles or off-field confrontations. He has consistently been among the players with the most yellow and red cards in the Iranian league. Recently he was involved in a high-profile dispute with his longtime friend and former teammate Hossein Kanaani-Zadegan, which led to the two unfollowing each other on Instagram. The pair, who had long formed the backbone of Iran’s defence, exchanged taunts during a Tractor v Persepolis match in the Hazfi Cup by mimicking each other’s goal celebrations. After being benched by Carlos Queiroz at Qatar 2022, Khalilzadeh is expected to be a key starter under Amir Ghalenoei this summer.

players.name: Milad Mohammadi
players.bio: After eight years of playing for clubs in Russia, Belgium, Greece and Turkey, Mohammadi – whose twin brother, Mehrdad, plays in Iran for Tractor – returned to the Iranian league in 2024 with Persepolis. However, playing for his childhood team did not go well for him and, after a decade as a regular with the national team, for a while he lost his place in the squad. That downturn coincided with the decision by the then Persepolis coach, former Bayern Munich striker Vahid Hashemian, to deploy him as a right-back, but after Hashemian’s departure Mohammadi was moved back to the left, where he rediscovered his form and with it his international place. Mohammadi may sometimes hesitate in key moments, but as a fast and combative left-back he can be a major asset when in top form. With his ability to contribute in attack as well as defend solidly, he can make life difficult for opposing right wingers.

players.name: Saeid Ezatolahi
players.bio: If Ezatolahi’s goal against Spain at the 2018 World Cup had not been disallowed for offside, he would have become the scorer of one of the most important goals in Iranian football history at just 22 years old. But the flag went up, and it was not to be the only disappointment for a player once regarded as one of the brightest talents of his generation. Exatolahi joined Atlético Madrid at the age of 17 but instead of reaching the pinnacle of European football he has had an itinerant career. In October 2025, Ezatolahi captained the Iranian national team for the first time in a friendly match against Tanzania. Following the game, he wrote on Instagram:“I have made a pact with myself to always fight for my dreams, to fear no match, and to always put Iran’s honor above everything else.” A tall, elegant midfielder, Ezatolahi combines defensive duties and deep playmaking with a willingness to join attacks and contribute goals. When he was ruled out of the 2019 AFC Asian Cup due to injury he provided a rap to a song, Balatar, supporting the side.

players.name: Alireza Jahanbakhsh
players.bio: Jahanbakhsh is one of the few Iranian stars who has never played for either of Tehran’s two major clubs, Persepolis or Esteghlal. At the age of 19 he turned Persepolis down in favour of moving to the Netherlands with NEC Nijmegen. That decision partly explains his relatively mixed popularity among some football fans in Iran. Jahanbakhsh is from Gilan Province in northern Iran, a region whose people are often stereotyped for having prominent facial features. During his time at Brighton, he was involved in a confrontation with Manchester City defender Kyle Walker. Reflecting on the incident, he said: “He touched me and hit my nose. I’m from the north of Iran, and we have big noses – it’s hard to miss!” At 32, Jahanbakhsh is more of an impact sub than a certain starter, having lost some of the explosive pace he displayed during his peak years at Alkmaar, between 2015 and 2018, when his incisive runs and spectacular long-range goals made him one of the Eredivisie’s most feared players.

players.name: Mohammad Mohebi
players.bio: Mohebi can be considered one of the most unique – and at the same time one of the unluckiest – wingers in this squad. He is a player who, had certain moments in his football journey unfolded differently, might have far more international appearances to his name. Even so, his statistics in a limited number of matches are impressive: 34 international caps, 13 goals, and nine assists, meaning direct involvement in 22 goals at the time of writing. For a winger, especially at national-team level, that is a remarkable return. Mohebi was first called up to the national team in 2019, and enjoyed a dream debut against Cambodia by recording two goals and one assist, but after playing three times in just a few weeks he did not feature again for more than three years. Mohebi is the childhood friend of left-winger Mehdi Ghayedi, a fellow native of the coastal city of Bushehr, and the simultaneous presence of the two in the lineup can be particularly exciting to watch.

players.name: Mehdi Taremi
players.bio: For years, it looked like Mehdi Taremi would be better known for off-field shenanigans – a four-month global ban from football related to a contract dispute with Turkish club Rizespor, a relationship with the famous Iranian actress Sahar Ghoreyshi – than for anything he might do on the pitch. But his move to Portuguese club Rio Ave in 2019 changed everything, and he later went to Porto and Inter, where he was able to fully showcase his abilities. He won league and cup titles with Porto and reached the Champions League final with Inter, while his spectacular bicycle kick for Porto against Chelsea was nominated for the Puskás Award and voted Goal of the Season in the Champions League. Taremi is a striker who excels at running behind defensive lines, and even minimal contact is often enough for him to go down and win penalties – a trait that has earned him a controversial reputation in Portugal. He is Iran’s captain and star. With two goals against England in the 2022 World Cup, he is already Iran’s all-time top scorer in the tournament.

players.name: Mehdi Ghaedi
players.bio: Soon after Ghaedi joined Esteghlal in July 2017 he became one of the youngest players in the history of Iran’s Pro League, but it quickly became clear that his path to the top was not going to be smooth: the following January, driving at high speed, his car veered off the road and crashed, causing the death of a friend and sending him to intensive care. Since then a combination of injuries and off-field issues have limited the international appearances of a player known by the nickname Bashu, after a famous Iranian film character. Ghaedi is 1.66m tall, for which his childhood friend and occasional Iran international Mohammad Mohebi has the following explanation: “If I’m not mistaken, we were 12 or 13 years old playing at youth level,” he remembered. “I played a through ball to him, he went one-on-one, dribbled the goalkeeper, but the keeper fouled him by hitting his ankle. That’s when Mehdi broke his ankle, and it affected his growth. That’s why he stopped growing and remained short.” Despite this, Ghaedi remains a winger capable of changing games on his day with his combination of technique and explosive pace.

players.name: Ali Alipour
players.bio: A previous top scorer in Iran’s Persian Gulf Pro League league, Alipour has spent most of his career in two spells at Persepolis, interrupted by four years in Portugal with Maritimo and Gil Vicente. He has been a consistent goalscorer, particularly while playing in Iran, but fluctuations in form have prevented him from securing a permanent place in the national team, and more than eight years after his debut he has only a dozen appearances, most of them in the last year. However, the controversial omission of Sardar Azmoun for this World Cup created an opportunity for Alipour to push his way into the final squad. He may not get much playing time, but he has already been thinking about his goal celebration: “In international matches, it’s not possible to reveal anything under the shirt, but if it were allowed, I would definitely display the flag of my country, Iran. And God willing, if I get the chance and can be one of my country’s soldiers, I will kiss the Iranian badge after scoring.”

players.name: Payam Niazmand
players.bio: Iran’s fourth-choice goalkeeper at the 2022 World Cup (Iran were the only team to take four keepers) has closed the gap to Alireza Beiranvand to such an extent that now just a single slip from the Tractor man could see Niazmand become Iran’s No 1. Having joined Persepolis at the start of the 2025–26 season, Niazmand said in his first interview: “The greatest dream of any goalkeeper is to play in the World Cup. I was at the last World Cup, but I didn’t get the chance to play. This time, I’m far more motivated, and I hope to get minutes on the pitch.” This season, under the guidance of Emilio Álvarez – David de Gea’s former Manchester United goalkeeping coach – he has shown significant improvement. However, what has so far prevented Niazmand from becoming Iran’s first-choice goalkeeper is a drop in confidence during international matches. Could it be different this year?

players.name: Hossein Kanaani-Zadegan
players.bio: A physically imposing defender, Kanaani-Zadegan is known for making headlines not only with his performances but also with his outspoken interviews. One such moment came when a leaked voice message caught him inviting an unidentified woman to share a romantic moment with him on the national team bus. The backlash led to his suspension for two matches. Like Shoja Khalilzadeh, he lost his starting spot after Carlos Queiroz returned ahead of the 2022 World Cup, featuring for only 45 minutes against England. After Quieroz’s departure, he commented under the coach’s farewell post on Instagram: “You betrayed Iran, not me and Shoja.” Later, when Queiroz took charge of Qatar, Kanaani-Zadegan scored twice in Iran’s 4-0 victory, taking what many saw as revenge. Reflecting on his only appearance at the 2022 World Cup, he once said: “If Rashford had played on my side, I would have killed him.”

players.name: Saman Ghoddos
players.bio: Carlos Queiroz’s decade-long spell with Iran had a significant impact on the national team, and one of his lasting legacies is the integration of dual-nationality or second-generation Iranian players born outside the country. One such player is Saman Ghoddos, born in Malmö, Sweden, to Iranian parents. He even represented Sweden in two full internationals but, when he realised he would not be included in Sweden’s 2018 World Cup squad, he accepted Queiroz’s invitation to play for Iran. This decision marked a historic turning point. One of his set-piece deliveries against Morocco in the 2018 World Cup led to an own goal, helping Iran secure one of its most memorable victories in World Cup history. At club level Ghoddos made his name at Östersund under Graham Potter, where he helped the club win the Swedish Cup and was named Swedish Forward of the Year. As his career progressed, he gradually moved into deeper positions. At Brentford under Thomas Frank, he was even deployed as a full-back. For the national team, however, Ghoddos is typically used as a hard-working central midfielder who contributes in attack, with a powerful long-range shot.

players.name: Rouzbeh Cheshmi
players.bio: Standing 1.94m tall, this defensive midfielder is best known for his stern look and furrowed eyebrows – unless you mention the most unforgettable moment of his career: his stunning goal against Wales at the 2022 World Cup, a spectacular long-range strike from outside the penalty area. “I don’t even shoot in PlayStation,” Cheshmi said. “But when the ball came to me, I couldn’t hear anything – not the stadium, not even my teammates asking for a pass. It was truly God’s work.” A former captain of Iran’s Under-23 team, he is also capable of playing as a centre-back. Against England, also in 2022, he was placed in the heart of Iran’s five-man defense but delivered a poor performance: “Yes, I was responsible for two goals,” he admitted. Whether he has another difficult night or scores another decisive goal at the 2026 World Cup, Cheshmi now has even more important reasons to smile: his twins, Salman and Mila, who were born a few months after the Qatar World Cup.

players.name: Mehdi Torabi
players.bio: Torabi was in Iran’s World Cup squad in 2018 but never made it onto the pitch, and four years later was back in the squad and back on the bench, though this time he was used by Carlos Queiroz in all three matches. However, until recently, his chances of making the 2026 World Cup squad seemed extremely slim. But Amir Ghalenoei named him in his squad, nearly two years after Torabi’s last international appearance. He is one of the few Iranian footballers who openly expresses his religious and political beliefs. He famously celebrated a goal for Persepolis in 2019 by lifting his shirt to reveal the message: “The only way to save the country = obedience to the Supreme Leader”, causing considerable controversy. “This celebration was entirely my own idea, and you can be sure that no one instructed me to do it or influenced me from anywhere,” he said. “Before deciding on this celebration I had thought everything through and knew there would be both positive and negative reactions.”

players.name: Aria Yousefi
players.bio: Yousefi is one of three right-backs in Iran’s squad, but the last friendly before the announcement of the final squad suggested that Amir Ghalenooei may use him in multiple positions. In that match against Gambia, Yousefi started as a right-back, but in the second half he moved into midfield and even scored a goal. Speaking about his positional changes and playing as a winger, he said: “I am a player who, since I was 18, has played in different positions based on the coaches’ decisions. Now, with the approval of Mr Ghalenooei and the coaching staff, a new position has been assigned to me. Wherever the coach decides, it is my duty to play.” Yousefi was included in the squad despite not being called up for Iran’s March friendlies. However, after Danial Eiri was unable to join the team due to visa issues, Ghalenooei invited Yousefi, and he ultimately managed to secure a place.

players.name: Amirhossein Hosseinzadeh
players.bio: Hosseinzadeh has been the best player in the Iranian league over the past two seasons. In 2024-25 he finished as the top scorer in the league with 14 goals and played a key role in Tractor’s first-ever league title. This season he was leading the scoring charts when the outbreak of war forced a pause in competition. Over the past year, he has also scored five goals for the national team, showing that he is ready to shine on bigger stages. Regarded as one of Iran’s main attacking hopes for the World Cup, he believes the team can progress deep into the tournament: “Exactly from the day the World Cup draw was announced, Coach Ghalenoei told us that our goal must be to get out of the group – and not just one round, but we should aim to go even further, two stages beyond that. All the players have sworn together that, God willing, we will advance from the group this time and create a surprise.”

players.name: Ali Nemati
players.bio: Nemati may not have many chances to feature at the World Cup but simply making the squad is a significant achievement for a player who came close to giving up football at 18. He nearly missed a trial for Iran’s youth teams due to arriving late and, not long after, a motorcycle accident left him with a broken arm and collarbone, sidelining him for an entire year. However, he worked his way back to the top level and earned his first cap at the age of 29 during the 2026 World Cup qualifiers. Off the pitch, the versatile defender has also shown a compassionate side. Some time ago, he helped secure the release of individuals imprisoned for financial debts by paying off what they owed.

players.name: Shahriar Moghanloo
players.bio: Moghanloo is the most unusual squad member from Iran to the 2026 World Cup. He was not included in Amir Ghalenooei’s initial 30-man list. However, after Roozbeh Cheshmi’s injury scare, he was called up as a replacement – and in the end, even though Cheshmi did not drop out, Moghanloo remained in the final squad. He is a tall striker, and Ghalenooei will probably use him in the final minutes, especially when the team relies on crosses from the wings to attack. Two seasons ago, he was the top scorer in the Iranian league, but he is yet to replicate that brilliance at Kalba. Still, the absence of a strong aerial threat in the initial squad explains Ghalenooei’s selection. Moghanloo is from Zanjan, a city famous for its sharp knives. To earn playing time at the World Cup, he will need to be as sharp as the knives from his hometown.

players.name: Mohammad Ghorbani
players.bio: Ghorbani was part of Iran’s national team camps in the build-up to the 2022 World Cup but did not make the final squad. However, over the past four years, with experience gained in the Russian and Emirati leagues, he has become a regular presence in the national team setup, and there was never any doubt that Amir Ghalenoei would pick him in his final squad, even if his place in the starting XI is not secure. Ghorbani is one of those versatile players who over his career has featured in multiple positions, including left-back, centre-back, and even behind the striker, but in the national team he plays only as a defensive midfielder. Like many members of the squad, Ghorbani has spoken of his optimism about Iran’s chances of progressing beyond the group stage. After the draw, he said Iran could beat New Zealand 1-0 or 2-0, take at least a draw against Belgium and Egypt, and qualify for the next round.

players.name: Seyed Hossein Hosseini
players.bio: “I had visualized it beforehand and was very happy to be playing in the World Cup. I had worked very hard and was glad to have achieved my goal,” Hosseini said about playing against England at the 2022 World Cup. But the biggest lesson he took from that match was how quickly a dream can turn into a nightmare: after coming on for the injured Alireza Beiranvand, his 70-minute appearance ended with conceding six goals, as Iran suffered their heaviest defeat in their World Cup history. Hosseini also played the full 90 minutes in the second match against Wales – keeping a clean sheet – but has now dropped to third choice. Still, he doesn’t lack for confidence: “I’ve been the most in-form goalkeeper in Iran in recent years. The others haven’t been that exceptional.”

players.name: Ramin Rezaeian
players.bio: No player in Iranian football embraces showmanship quite like Rezaeian. Having played for all three of Iran’s biggest clubs – Persepolis, Esteghlal and Sepahan – he rarely does anything without adding a touch of flair. He etched his name into Iranian football history at the 2022 World Cup by scoring Iran’s second goal against Wales. Still, judging by his pinned Instagram post, his encounter with Cristiano Ronaldo at the 2018 World Cup appears to hold even greater personal significance. Speaking about that match on a TV show, he said: “I really like Ronaldo, and it felt strange to play against him. I messed around with him a bit … I even told him: ‘If you don’t like it, switch sides and go play over there.’” Rezaeian is an attack-minded right-back, which often leaves space behind him for opponents to exploit.

players.name: Dennis-Yerai Eckert Ayensa
players.bio: Eckert is the fifth German-Iranian to be called up by Iran, but the first whose links with the country go back not to parents but to grandparents: his mother is Spanish, his father German, but his paternal grandfather was born in Iran and the famous Iranian actress Anahita Dargahi is an aunt. “My aunt Anahita supports me in everything,” he said on Instagram. “I’m proud to have such an amazing aunt.” A former Germany Under-19 international, Eckert was first linked with Iran only last September. “My father is half Iranian but he doesn’t have a passport. My grandfather is Iranian, but he’s dead. So that makes things difficult,” he said. “I wouldn’t even know where to start to apply for citizenship.” But by March he had dual nationality and an international call-up – though not clearance from Fifa, and he was unable to play – and in early May his passport was issued. At 29, and as a striker who has reached double figures for league goals only once, for Ingolstadt in the German third division six seasons ago, he is an unlikely but intriguing presence at the World Cup.

players.name: Danial Eiri
players.bio: Eiri has been one of the breakout stars of Iranian football over the past couple of seasons and has already attracted significant interest from other clubs. He is a strong defender who is capable of building play from the back, and whose aerial ability makes him a threat in both penalty areas – he scored twice in a recent behind-closed-doors training match with the Iran squad. The young defender has performed so well in the recent season that Mehdi Pashazadeh, the former Bayer Leverkusen defender who played for Iran at the 1998 World Cup, publicly called for him not just to be picked for the World Cup, but to start. After one of Malavan’s cup victories this season, a video surfaced showing Iri dancing exuberantly in the dressing room. So even if Eiri does not get a chance to play, in the event of Iran winning any of their World Cup matches, he can still showcase his abilities off the pitch.

players.name: Amirmohammad Razzaghinia
players.bio: The youngest member of Iran’s national team at this World Cup is a young midfielder who, after playing for the Under-16, Under-17, Under-20, and Under-23 teams, has delivered outstanding performances in the Iranian league over the past two seasons and was able to earn his place in the senior international side at the age of just 20. He made his full debut against North Korea last June and prefers to play as a defensive midfielder, though if he gets within sight of goal he also has a powerful and dangerous shot. In one of his few TV interviews, held a few days before the announcement of the final World Cup squad, Razzaghinia was unable to answer the host’s quiz questions about Iran’s previous World Cup appearances, the clip quickly going viral on social media. In that same interview, he said: “The match against New Zealand is our hardest game because it is the first match. We must definitely win this game, and after that we will think about the following matches.”

players.name: Fahad Talib
players.bio: When Talib suffered a serious knee injury on the eve of Iraq's crucial 2024 Asian Cup Last-16 tie against Jordan, not many believed he, then 29, would return to the same level that had seen him go to the 2013 Under20 World Youth Cup in Turkey and the 2016 Olympics in Rio. Talib had made his name in Iraq's last World Cup qualifying campaign, replacing the injured Jalal Hassan, where he appeared in eight of Iraq's 10 qualifying games for the 2022 World Cup and earned rave reviews. Following the injury and, after almost a year out on the sidelines, the goalkeeper returned to form with Iraq Stars League club Al-Talaba and earned a recall to the national side, where he has made 20 appearances since making his debut in 2017. A goalkeeper like his father and elder brother, Fahad came through the Ammo Baba football school and later joined Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya in 2008, breaking into the first team at just 16 and becoming a regular a few years later.

players.name: Rebin Sulaka
players.bio: The 1.92m journeyman centre-back has come a long way from when he first put on an Iraqi jersey more than a decade ago. Back then Sulaka, who was born in Iraq but grew up in Sweden, was only at the start of his career, understanding just a few words of Arabic as his parents spoke mainly Chaldean Neo-Aramaic at home. Now 34 years old with more than 50 appearances for Iraq, Sulaka has a good command of the Arabic language, allowing him to communicate with the rest of the squad and help ease new players into the team. After starting his club career in Sweden, he has played in nine different countries, including Norway, Serbia, Bulgaria and Switzerland, and is now enjoying his second stint in Thailand. He will be the second Iraqi player from Ankawa, a suburb of Erbil, to play at the World Cup after Basil Gorgis, who was sent off early against Belgium in 1986 in a case of mistaken identity.

players.name: Hussein Ali
players.bio: Before Ali made his debut in 2023, the right side of defence had always been a troublesome position for the Iraqi team. He wore the jersey for the first time at the King's Cup in Thailand against India. "It was a dream come true to play for my father's country,” he recalls. Ali represented Sweden at every youth level and spent almost two years considering whether to play for his father's country of birth, a place he had never visited. His father had left Iraq in 1989 to settle in Sweden, where Ali was born. The right-back came through the Malmö FF academy from the age of five, starting out as a centre-back. Looking to play first-team football, he signed his first professional contract with Örebro, making his Allsvenskan debut at just 17. In 2022, he moved abroad for the first time, spending three seasons with Eredivisie club Heerenveen. For the 2025-26 season, he joined Polish side Pogon Szczecin, located close to the Baltic Sea.

players.name: Zaid Tahseen
players.bio: Tahseen was not even in the matchday squad for Iraq's first qualifying game of their 2026 World Cup campaign, but for their 21st match in Monterrey against Bolivia, the 25-year-old centre-back was one of Iraq's star performers. At the final whistle, after a tearful phone call to his family back home who were up in the early hours to watch the victory, the defender said: "I don't know if I am in a dream or living real life. When the game finished, I looked around, I didn't know what to do. This is the best morning in Iraq's history and the best day in all of Iraq. It won't be forgotten." The 1.93m-tall centre-back from the city of Najaf left school at 16, later playing at the 2024 Olympics in Paris. Tahseen, who now plays his club football for Pakhtakor in Uzbekistan, has 25 Iraq caps at the time of writing after making his first start in a 0-0 draw with Ecuador in 2022 at Atlético’s Metropolitano Stadium.

players.name: Akam Hashem
players.bio: In the dying minutes of the victory over Bolivia that sent Iraq to their first World Cup in 40 years, Akam Hashem turned to his defensive partner, Zaid Tahseen, and said, "Brother Zaid, do you know, we're going to be legends of Iraq." With the Iraqi defence under pressure, his teammate could only respond with a desperate cry to continue defending. Seconds later the final whistle blew. After his first start for Iraq, against Palestine in 2025, Hashem was selected to take a routine doping test – when he finally came out to the car park, he discovered the team bus had already left without him! Born in Erbil, he started his career at local club Peshmerga (meaning “those who face death”), a lower-division side representing the Kurdish region's internal security forces, where he played alongside his elder brother Rekar. He later moved to the city's top club, Erbil, in 2020 and has made a name for himself in the Iraqi League, moving to Baghdad to first play for Al-Shorta and now Al-Zawraa.

players.name: Manaf Younis
players.bio: It was Younis who scored the dramatic winning header in the 3-2 win over Oman - in the final minute of extra-time - to see Iraq lift the Gulf Cup trophy for the first time in 35 years in 2023. The defender had a premonition he would score. "A day before the game, Fahad Talib and I were praying in the room. I finished my prayers and told Fahad, ‘What will happen if tomorrow, in the last minute, I head it in and we win!’" Born in Tikrit, Younis was brought up by his mother after his father passed away when he was only eight.
