Originally a striker, his 1.90m frame and aerial ability convinced coaches to play him in defence after breaking into the Salah-al-Deen first team. He moved to Baghdad in 2019, signing for Al-Karkh, a club under the presidency of the late Sharar Haidar, who had a policy of scouting players from the provinces. It was there Munaf made his name, winning the Iraqi Cup in 2022 and breaking into the national side. He later joined Al-Shorta, winning three consecutive league titles from 2023.

players.name: Youssef Amyn
players.bio: At 16, the Essen-born winger travelled to Baghdad to try out for the Iraq Under-19s, but the coach did not select him, considering him too young. The player, whose mother would tell him he kicked a ball before he could walk properly, was not going to be deterred after facing similar rejection by one of his youth coaches at Borussia Dortmund, who found the winger to be too small. Amyn responded the only way he could, on the pitch. He joined Viktoria Köln, becoming the youngest player to play for the club at 17, and represented Germany's Under-19s, scoring on his debut. With the Iraqi FA looking to Europe for talent, he was one of the newcomers selected. Twelve minutes after coming on for his debut in Iraq's first game in the 2026 World Cup qualifiers in Basra against Indonesia, Amyn scored. " It’s such a special feeling to play for your country, and then to score a goal – in a World Cup qualifier as well."

players.name: Ibrahim Bayesh
players.bio: Ibrahim Bayesh is something of an enigma. He was once described by former Iraq coach Srecko Katanec as the fittest player in the Iraqi league, while every coach who has taken charge of the Iraqi team has selected him as a regular starter. However, maybe because of his direct playing style (more pragmatic than creative) and the way he conducts himself off the field, Bayesh is not widely appreciated by fans. He is an industrious and dogged battler who has played in almost every position for Iraq since making his debut in 2017, bar goalkeeper, left back and centre-back. The player, with 74 international appearances at the time of writing, continues to be selected, despite calls for him to be dropped, while his recent remarks about the selection of expatriate players under former coach Jesús Casas are unlikely to endear him to supporters. He stated there were “players from outside Iraq who come and don't care about the national team,” adding some received preferential treatment, a reason why they failed to qualify directly from their qualifying group, with players he had never seen before being selected for the first time.

players.name: Ali Al-Hamadi
players.bio: When Al-Hamadi made his debut for Iraq in a World Cup qualifier at 19, the Maysan-born Scouser had just made the biggest decision of his career, leaving Swansea in search of regular first-team football. The gamble paid off as Al-Hamadi eventually joined Ipswich and became the first Iraqi player in the Premier League. His journey to England’s top tier via Wimbledon and Wycombe showed his goalscoring prowess, mentality, drive and character. His career with Iraq so far has seen its fair share of ups and downs. After finally earning a start, he was substituted after just two minutes when his team went down to 10 men. Al-Hamadi then spent a spell out injured and saw red in Graham Arnold's first game. Having been in and out of the team because injuries he made an emotional goalscoring return in Moterrey, heading in the opener in the playoff victory over Bolivia.

players.name: Mohanad Ali
players.bio: When Ali, also known as “Mimi”, first burst onto the scene at the 2019 Asian Cup, many predicted he would become Iraq's greatest ever striker, but it has not worked out that way. After leading Al-Shorta to their first Iraqi league title in six years, he had offers from Juventus, Manchester City and Benfica but his club instead negotiated a $2m move to Qatar's Al-Duhail, receiving a new team bus as part of the deal. Despite the transfer allowing him to buy a home for his family, he failed to settle and made loan moves to Al-Sailiya, Portimonense and Aris Thessaloniki. He played little football during this spell after suffering two cruciate ligament injuries, which kept him out for two years. He returned, rediscovering his goalscoring touch at Al-Shorta, before moving last season to Dibba in the UAE.

players.name: Ahmed Qasim
players.bio: Last year, the left-footed right-winger moved to the MLS with Nashville for $4.2m, and has been playing against Luis Suárez, Thomas Müller and childhood hero Lionel Messi. “If you had told me 10 years ago, I wouldn’t have believed you, he is my idol,” he says. Qasim has been linked to Iraq’s national team for some time after it emerged he was eligible through his mother, who was originally from the city of Mosul. His father was born in Kuwait, while his paternal grandparents were Jordanian and Palestinian. Born in Sweden, he made his first-team debut at just 15 for local club Motala. At 17, he moved to Elfsborg in the top flight, earning a call-up to Sweden’s youth teams, representing the Under-17s, Under-19s and Under-21s. He was recently asked about his national team ambitions. “You were in Mexico and you felt the love of the Iraqi fans. If I’m not playing so much or if I play, they always support me. I feel the love from the people and I hope one day I can give back.” Two weeks later, he received his Iraqi passport in Detroit.

players.name: Jalal Hassan
players.bio: Iraq's 35-year-old captain and undisputed No 1 made his 100th international appearance before the home World Cup qualifying tie with the UAE last November, becoming only the third Iraqi goalkeeper (after 1986 World Cup captain Raad Hammoudi and 2007 Asian Cup winner Nour Sabri) to achieve the landmark. Worked as a labourer when he first started playing, recalling how he would train barefoot while wearing his work clothes. The Diwaniya-born keeper was first spotted at 19 and fast-tracked into the national team after impressing with the youth setup, making his international debut against Jordan 15 years ago. Jalal has appeared in three Asian Cups as a starter and at a record six Gulf Cups, captaining Iraq to victory at the 25th edition in Basra as Iraq lifted the trophy for the first time in 35 years. The veteran remains a key part of the Lions of Mesopotamia, holding the joint record for the most appearances in World Cup qualifiers for an Iraqi goalkeeper (23) and the most clean sheets (14).

players.name: Ali Yousef
players.bio: One of the few players to have represented all four of Baghdad's big clubs, Yousef is currently with Al-Talaba, known as the “students’ club”. The 30-year-old battled back after knee surgery last year, earning a recall to the national side under Graham Arnold. He had suffered the injury just four days after coming on in the 2-1 World Cup qualifying defeat to Palestine in Amman, ending an encouraging run of international appearances. The striker had been one of the new players brought into the team after a disastrous start to its 2022 World Cup campaign. Began in the Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya youth setup, and came into the first team at 18. Supporters nicknamed him “The Don” because of a slight resemblance to Cristiano Ronaldo, before he moved on to play for Al-Zawraa and Al-Shorta. A calm and composed forward, Yousef can operate as a central striker or on the flanks.

players.name: Zidane Iqbal
players.bio: Before Iqbal made his debut, a journalist was told by Dick Advocaat’s assistant that the Dutchman thought from the young players, only Iqbal, then of Manchester United, was ready for the first team. It perfectly sums up the footballer. With a name like Zidane, he has always had a lot to live up to, coming up through the ranks at United from the age of eight to make his first-team debut at Old Trafford in a Champions League match in 2021. Born in Manchester, the son of a Pakistani father and an Iraqi mother from the city of Samawah, Iqbal became the youngest player to represent Iraq in a World Cup qualifier aged 18, when he came on as a late substitute in a 1-0 defeat to Iran under Advocaat's successor, Zeljko Petrovic. He is finally settling into the team, with 22 appearances and two goals at the time of writing, including the winner against Indonesia during the play-offs in Jeddah. “It was crazy when I scored, everybody's screaming, but I'm not lying, I heard my mum scream."

players.name: Ahmed Maknzi
players.bio: A late replacement for Ahmed Yahya, who had to pull out because of injury. Ahmed Maknzi’s actual name is Ahmed Hassan, but he is popularly known by the name of his grandfather – pronounced like MacKenzie. The left-back has no Irish or Scottish ancestry, but according to his family, the name derives from a type of rifle imported from Britain during the early 20th century. Known in Iraq as a Maknzi, the weapon is believed to have been imported by Gray MacKenzie & Company, founded in Basra in 1869. The son of a major general, Maknzi was born in Baqouba, Diyala province, but began in the youth teams of Al-Zawraa in the Iraqi capital, breaking into the first team in 2017, before moving to Erbil, Al-Najaf, and now Al-Karma. First selected by Jesús Casas in early 2024, making his debut at the 2024-25 Gulf Cup against Bahrain.

players.name: Amir Al-Ammari
players.bio: A leader, the polyglot midfielder speaks Arabic, English, and Swedish, bridging the language barriers within a squad which has a significant contingent from Scandinavia, where many Iraqis settled after decades of conflict. Al-Ammari is one example. His parents met in Kuwait and left after the 1991 Gulf War to settle in Sweden, where he was born. After dropping out of football for a year, Al-Ammari finally found his feet at top-flight side Halmstad. In the same year, Dick Advocaat called him up for a key World Cup qualifier against South Korea, impressing in a 0-0 draw for a hard-fought point in Seoul. Since then, the 28-year-old has become a mainstay for Iraq, scoring the dramatic late penalty in the 17th minute of stoppage time against the UAE to reach the inter-confederation playoff in Monterrey. The player revealed he had no time to celebrate as he was catching a flight back to Poland, where he plays his club football for Crocovia: “I was on my way to the airport, in the back of the car, I was sitting and eating gifflar [small Swedish cinnamon buns].”

players.name: Ali Jassim
players.bio: One of the brightest talents in Asia, Jassim can light up a game with a moment of magic, but something he has not done lately. From early on, though, his raw talent was apparent, making his debut for Al-Karkh at 14. Then his father passed, leaving Jassim and older brother Abbas – also a footballer – as the family's main providers. An unpolished gem, under the guidance of Luay Salah at Al-Kahrabaa, he thrived. At 19, he led Iraq to the 2023 Under-20 World Cup finals, and was then called into the senior national side, later scoring the winning goal to qualify Iraq for the 2024 Olympics. On the same day Jassim scored the winner in Iraq’s opening game against Ukraine at the Olympics, he signed for Como. “We think Jassim can flourish with us,” said Cesc Fàbregas. However, after arriving late to pre-season from the Olympics, he played just eight minutes in Serie A and - frustrated and wanting to play - he went out on loan to Almere City in the Eredivisie in 2024-25 and then Saudi Pro League club Al-Najma.

players.name: Aymen Hussein
players.bio: Hussein is a remarkable footballer with an extraordinary back story. He comes from Hawija, which was one of the most volatile regions in the world after the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq. His father was killed by Al-Qaeda in 2008, and then, in 2014, his brother was abducted by Islamic State; he is presumed dead with his whereabouts still unknown. It was under these dreadful circumstances that the striker began his playing career. Since making his full international debut in 2015, Hussein’s time with Iraq has been a turbulent one. He has faced heavy criticism from fans and the media for his lack of goals. On one occasion, his coach ran on to the pitch to instruct the striker not to take a penalty. However, he is now in Iraq's top five all-time top scorers list, and struck the winner against Bolivia that saw Iraq qualify for their first World Cup in 40 years. His arrival in the US this summer wasn’t smooth: he was reportedly held and questioned for nearly seven hours at Chicago’s O’Hare airport. Hussein was finally allowed in, but the team’s photographer was barred from entering the US.

players.name: Kevin Yakob
players.bio: Yakob, whose middle name is Enkido, a figure in ancient Mesopotamian mythology, always had links to the country where his grandfather emigrated from in 1971. He started at Assyriska (a Swedish club formed in 1974 by Assyrian immigrants) and then Häcken, where legendary Iraqi player Youra Eshaya once coached. Yakob finally made his debut for Iraq against Colombia in 2023, but lasted just 15 minutes, rupturing his cruciate ligament, putting him out for months, but he remained optimistic. "A dream come true ended in the worst way. Unfortunately, I suffered an ACL injury, but I will do everything I can to come back with God's grace." The player had been there before, tearing the cruciate ligament in the same knee at 18. That happened just nine months after a goalscoring debut in the Allsvenskan. Yakob had been considered one of Sweden's most promising youngsters, but the injury set him back. At 22, he had to do it all over again, and 643 days later he was back playing, and was on the field in Monterrey, guiding Iraq to the World Cup to cap an extraordinary return to football.

players.name: Aimar Sher
players.bio: When Sher came into the Iraqi team, it was a moment the midfielder had long been waiting for, to represent the country of his birth. Named after Argentina's 2002 World Cup member and Valencia player Pablo Aimar, Sher was born in Kirkuk but moved to Sweden when he was only four, later becoming Hammarby's youngest Allsvenskan debutant at 16 years and nine months. At just 20, he earned a €1.5m move to Italy's Spezia, but there his career stalled, living away from his family for the first time in his life and not knowing the language. He was unable to settle in Liguria, making only one appearance in Serie A. At the end of the 2022-23 season, Sher’s contract with Spezia was terminated by mutual consent. Without a club for almost four months, he noted it was the most important period of his career. "It was a really good lesson, because that's how football is, it goes up and down. I grew a lot, not only as a football player but also as a human."

players.name: Marko Farji
players.bio: In October 2025, Farji was asked what he had done during the international break, his reply was slightly flippant: “Ate me fat … slept to death." It was a display of frustration from the Grimstad-born winger, whose opportunities with Iraq had been few and far between. But Farji, or Faraj as he is known in Arabic, remained patient and, in the end, assisted Iraq's winner in the final World Cup playoff just 68 seconds after coming on. "It’s crazy. It's a wonderful feeling," he told Norway's TV2. “It's a dream. It's absolutely sick, but it feels weird to meet Norway [in Group I], the place where I grew up and come from. I'm excited," added the former Strømsgodset player, now with Venezia, who will play in Serie A next season. Farji was something of a child prodigy, who at 11, was offered a week-long trial at Manchester City, and two years later, trained with Ajax at a camp. Behind him was an ambitious father, a structural engineer originally from the city of Sulaymaniyah who, from early on, saw the true potential of his son.

players.name: Ahmed Basil
players.bio: In November 2024, Basil was drafted into the squad as fourth-choice keeper on the eve of two crucial World Cup qualifiers. Two days before the game with Jordan, the Al-Shorta keeper had received a surprise call at 2am from the Iraq FA. Nine hours later, he was out on the training pitch with the squad. Astonishingly, with an injury to Iraq's No 1, Jalal Hassan, Basil was handed his first start in the World Cup qualifiers – only his sixth international appearance – and he excelled in the 0-0 draw with Jordan in Basra, followed by a 1-0 win over Oman, Iraq’s first victory in Muscat for 30 years. The keeper later revealed, in tears, how he had left his bedridden father, a former Iraq player, at home recovering from surgery. Since then, Basil has proved to be a reliable understudy to Hassan, deputising in his absence including for the final qualifier against Bolivia in Monterrey.

players.name: Merchas Doski
players.bio: The moment this cultured Hannover-born left-back with roots to Zakho came into the team, everyone knew he would become a key player. Merchas, meaning "brave knight" in Kurdish, was first selected by Olympic coach Miroslav Soukup. After appearing at the 2022 Asian Under-23 Cup in Tashkent while without a club, he had a trial at Czech Cup winners FC Slovacko through Soukup’s contacts. After just six days, he earned a contract and played in Europe for the first time. Just three years earlier, he had been an amateur in Germany's fifth tier and working as a bricklayer. With a growing reputation, Doski later signed for Viktoria Plzen and played in the Europa League. With Iraq, he slotted in as if he had played there for years, achieving one of his lifelong dreams. ”The road to get here hasn't been easy. But a World Cup – that's every child's dream. Every boy who played on the streets, like I did, saw the great players at a World Cup.”

players.name: Zaid Ismail
players.bio: One of the newcomers brought into the national setup by Graham Arnold. The Australian took just 15 minutes to make up his mind about the 24-year-old holding midfielder after watching him in an Iraq Stars League game, asking his team manager for the name of Al-Talaba's No 6. He earned a first call-up at the Arab Cup at the end of last year. After his debut in the 2-1 victory over Bahrain, Arnold took the player to one side and told him he would be in his squad for the play-off game in Monterrey. True to his word, Ismail was in Mexico and came on for the last 38 minutes, making crucial interceptions in the win over Bolivia. At the final whistle, the player dedicated Iraq's World Cup qualification to his late father, a deputy intelligence officer who was killed in 2006 during the sectarian violence that erupted in the country after the US-led invasion, leaving behind a young wife and three sons, the youngest of whom was Zaid, only four at the time.

players.name: Mustafa Saadoon
players.bio: After an early exit from the Gulf Cup in Kuwait at the end of 2024, the then-Iraq coach Jesús Casas came out defending his players from criticism, especially one of the youngest, Mustafa Saadoon. Fans had nicknamed the right-back “Saadoon Street”, believing he was the reason for that side of the Iraqi defence being wide open for opponents to exploit. “It is very easy to be a fat man sitting in front of the TV,  talking bad about our players,” Casas said. “Mustafa is a very good player, he is young, and all players make mistakes – in Iraq, at Real Madrid, in Argentina, in all teams in the world.” Saadoon comes from the town of Al-Nahrawan, 35 km from the Iraqi capital, and has worked hard to get where he is at one of Iraq’s biggest clubs, Al-Shorta. When he first started, he studied and worked in a brick factory while playing, eventually graduating from university. After progressing from the lower divisions to the Iraq Stars League, he has gone on to represent the national side and played at the 2024 Olympics.

players.name: Frans Putros
players.bio: Both of his parents hail from Baghdad, where his two elder brothers were also born. Frans is the only one in the family who started life in Denmark, in the city of Aarhus, where he began his career at AGF. However, while playing in the Danish Superliga for Silkeborg and called up for Denmark’s Under-21s, he went into “half a year of hell” after issues with his agent, suddenly finding himself without a club. He resurrected his career at second-tier side Fredericia, managed by his former coach Jesper Sørensen, before moving abroad to play in Thailand and Indonesia. Initially a right-back, Putros has developed the ability to play at centre-back and in midfield. When he came into the Iraqi team in 2018, he played his first three games at left-back and had to wait three years before he started in his natural position. At the 2023 Asian Cup in Doha, he was lauded for his performance in Iraq’s 2-1 victory over Japan after earning a surprise start in midfield.

players.name: Zion Suzuki
players.bio: The first Japanese goalkeeper to establish himself as a Serie A regular, Suzuki is athletic, a solid shot-stopper, and has strong distribution, too – capable of reaching deep into the opposition half. Since joining Parma in 2024 from Belgium’s Sint-Truiden he has dedicated himself to learning Italian and now speaks it fluently. He also speaks openly about his ambitions: “I want to become one of the best keepers in the world and win the Champions League … That might sound unrealistic, like something out of an anime, but you never know what could happen.” His determination and rapid development continue to fuel high expectations. Off the pitch he is a big coffee fan: he has embraced Italy’s espresso culture, but also bought a grinder to grind his own beans at home.

players.name: Yukinari Sugawara
players.bio: Sugawara’s big asset is his explosive running down the right flank, making him a real attacking threat. But he is solid at the back, too, able to switch gears to deliver aggressive one-on-one defending. At 18, when he moved to AZ Alkmaar in the Netherlands, he reached out to international teammate Yuto Nagatomo’s personal chef to learn about diet and nutrition from scratch. “I just felt a huge gap between Dutch and Japanese food,” he said, “and I had a sense that I might get injured if I didn’t address it.” Living with a host family meant he had limited access to the kitchen, but he made the most of it. He moved from there to England with Southampton in 2024, and joined Werder Bremen on loan last season.

players.name: Shogo Taniguchi
players.bio: A versatile defender capable of playing anywhere across the back line, Taniguchi’s football intelligence and distribution set him apart. He places particular importance on his field of vision, being aware of everything around him, partly because his eyesight deteriorated during his student years. Since then he has been meticulous about preparing contact lenses and taking proper care of his eyes. His high school coach once described him as someone who “demonstrates dedication and leadership for the team even off the pitch … there isn’t a single person who would speak ill of Shogo.” His character and leadership have long been widely respected. He has played his club football in Belgium since 2024 and, in the national team, continues to show his reliability as a defensive leader.

players.name: Ko Itakura
players.bio: After spending three seasons at Borussia Mönchengladbach, Itakura joined Dutch giants Ajax last year, becoming the first Japanese player in the club’s history. A solid, experienced defender who was on Manchester City’s books from 2019 to 2022 without breaking through, he excels in one-on-one defending, but also brings real versatility. He played as a defensive midfielder during his academy years, so has the composure and technique to turn under pressure and contribute to the build-up from the back. He is also known for his cheerful and open personality. His mother believes his remarkable ability to adapt quickly and fit in may stem from his early childhood when he spent the first six-and-a-half years of his life living in company housing shared by around 60 families.

players.name: Yuto Nagatomo
players.bio: As the oldest member of the squad, Nagatomo brings famously passionate leadership backed by a long and distinguished career. His repeated cries of “Bravo!” in post-match interviews after Japan’s victories over Germany and Spain at the Qatar World Cup have since become his trademark. Turning 40 this September, and second only to Yasuhito Endo in Japan’s most-capped rankings, he shows no signs of slowing down. “My cells are exploding,” he jokes. “If anything, you should be worried about me improving.” With relentless work rate, exceptional physical conditioning and an uncompromising attitude, he continues to set the standard for younger players, and brings broad European club football experience, having played for Inter Milan, Galatasaray and Marseille before signing for FC Tokyo in 2021. With a fifth World Cup appearance coming up, his passion and obsession with performing on the biggest stage continue to burn as fiercely as ever.

players.name: Wataru Endo
players.bio: Nicknamed “The Duel King” in Japan, Endo excels at reading the game, using his powerful upper-body balance to shut down opponents. What’s his secret? “Some of it is timing,” he told The Times. “A tackle is not just hitting against your opponent, you have to be in the right position and maybe be clever and guess what an opponent is thinking, or the play they want to make. Japanese, I think, are clever, so you can use the benefit of that to win a duel. That’s my opinion anyway, and it works for me.” In February he sustained what looked like a season-ending injury in Liverpool’s game against Sunderland – it has been an anxious battle to regain fitness for this summer. He spoke of the emotion of the moment at the time, saying: “The doctor asked me if I could stand up and walk off the pitch but I said no and I was crying a little bit … I was not crying because it was painful, it was because our fans were singing my song and that means a lot to me.” Off the pitch he has a big online following in Japan for his video blogs and articles about football, life and parenting, and has written a self-help book called “Duel”, subtitled “30 ways of thinking that don't need correct answers”.

players.name: Ao Tanaka
players.bio: Developed in the Kawasaki Frontale academy, Tanaka’s technical quality is clear, but it’s his ability to cover wide areas at both ends of the pitch and anticipate shifts in play that gives him the edge. He reads danger early, and has a strong eye for goal, too, scoring in Japan’s win over Spain at the Qatar World Cup. He is known as an avid reader, organising his reading notes around three axes: knowledge, information and action. He’s all about structure and efficiency. His first name means “blue” – his parents had a different name in mind, but the sky was so blue on the day he was born, they chose that instead. One story from his elementary school days sums up his drive and determination: during his first trial for the school team he was playing happily at first, but halfway through the teacher spotted him crying in the corner of the schoolyard. When the teacher asked him what was wrong, he replied: “I'm bored with this practice. I want to practice harder."

players.name: Takefusa Kubo
players.bio: A huge talent, often dubbed “the Japanese Messi”. Kubo was talent spotted by Barcelona at the age of eight, and joined their La Masia academy at 10. In 2023 he revealed how hard the experience had been, saying: “There were a lot of kids with strong personalities at school and in the dorms, so I tried not to get lost in the crowd, or rather, I thought that if I was looked down upon even a little, it would be the end, so even though I was small I tried my best not to lose, but I still got punched by bigger kids. I can't really say much, but it was a really tough environment.” His progress there was halted, anyway, when the club was found to have violated Fifa international transfer policy for Under-18s. Instead he developed at FC Tokyo before returning to Spain with Real Madrid in 2019. His ability to draw in defenders with sharp, explosive dribbling makes him a huge threat, and he is equally potent as a playmaker, delivering decisive final passes. He has plenty of self-confidence too, saying recently: “I’m not just good with the ball at my feet – I think I’m good at football as a whole.” This summer is a chance to showcase his talents on a global stage, with clubs in England tracking his progress.

players.name: Keisuke Goto
players.bio: As a child Goto tried his hand at a wide range of sports from volleyball and track and field to swimming and tennis, before settling on football. A towering striker at 1.91m, he made the move to Europe in 2024 with Anderlecht, initially in their reserve side RSCA Futures, and made his senior debut for them last year before joining Sint-Truiden on loan to continue his development. Plenty of big clubs in England and Germany have been tracking him, but he is shy about being called “the Japanese Erling Haaland” by Belgian media, telling The Times in March: “No, no, no!” But he did admit he tries to model himself on Harry Kane because of the England striker’s “movement off the ball, knowing how to drop deep, to create and shoot from outside the box”.

players.name: Ritsu Doan
players.bio: At the Qatar World Cup Doan scored against both Germany and Spain, staking his claim to be Japan’s leading attacking threat. Cutting in from the right at speed he poses a constant threat with his powerful left-footed shot. Despite being just 1.73m he is physically strong, too. His ability to deliver on the biggest stage means he has often been compared to former Japan star Keisuke Honda, not only for his style but also for his strong sense of self-belief. He said in February: “I do have the sense that people are depending on me … I want to be the kind of player who makes his team a winning team. I think we need the kind of players where the coach and the team can feel like ‘I know that I can count on Ritsu when he’s around’, or ‘Ritsu can help us settle down even when things get tricky’, or ‘if he’s standing strong, we stay solid as well’, that sort of thing. The best teams always have players like that.” He has played his club football in Germany since 2020, initially on loan at Arminia Bielefeld from PSV, then with Freiburg and, since last year, with Eintracht Frankfurt following an £18m move.

players.name: Daizen Maeda
players.bio: A versatile, physical attacker, Maeda’s big strengths lie in his relentless pressing and explosive sprinting: he’s among the fastest players in the national team. His knack for closing down opponents with subtle, quiet movement earned him the nickname “Prius” in high school. His time there wasn’t always smooth, though: in his first year he was involved in a disciplinary incident that led to a lengthy spell of no school football; he responded by committing himself to extra training and playing for a local amateur side, while also building character by working part-time at a bakery in the mornings before school started. He went on to build a successful career, and has been a big player at Celtic since moving to Scotland in 2021, earning his own chant, with fans singing his name to the tune of The Champs’ classic “Tequila”. Maeda has thanked them online for “their wonderful song”.

players.name: Keisuke Osako
players.bio: In his first professional season with Sanfrecce Hiroshima back in 2018 Osako devoted himself to relentless self-improvement, adding extra individual sessions after morning training and even joining youth team training in the afternoons. That work ethic has taken him to a World Cup. A solid shot-stopper, he is also known for being a brilliant communicator when marshalling the defence in front of him. He names former Japan internationals Yoshikatsu Kawaguchi and Eiji Kawashima as his role models, but says he’s more focused on being one himself: “When people think of Japan’s guardian, I want them to think of Osako.”

players.name: Keito Nakamura
players.bio: Nakamura’s signature move is cutting in from the left to shoot. In recent years he has moved away from upper-body-focused weight training, instead prioritising developing his fluid, evasive style that allows him to glide past opponents. He’s also known for wearing his socks low, Jack Grealish-style, which he says helps reduce his tendency to suffer from muscle cramps. He has played his club football in Ligue 1 since 2023, and has been linked with clubs in England. Before this tournament he spoke to Fifa.com about his World Cup memories, saying: “The 2018 and 2022 tournaments left their mark on me. Both times we got our hearts broken in the Last 16, but they signified one more step forward for us as a country. I remember getting goosebumps just watching the team play, and it reinforced my ambition to appear there one day myself. We’ll be hoping to reach the knockout stages again and go even further. I would love to be able to contribute towards that goal, even just a little … I’ve dreamed about playing in the World Cup since I was a little boy.”

players.name: Junya Ito
players.bio: Relatively unspectacular throughout high school, Ito went on to study at Kanagawa University, whose footballers play in Japan’s Kanto Division 2. He steadily built his reputation there, quickly gaining attention with his exceptional pace. After graduating he joined J1 side Ventforet Kofu in 2015 and Kashiwa Reysol the following year. He moved to Europe in 2019, first to Genk in Belgium, then to France with Reims in 2022 before returning to Genk last year on a three-year deal. He is great as an impact player, often coming off the bench, with the ability to completely change the flow of a match. Despite his explosive and energetic presence on the pitch, he is, by his own admission, quite shy in private, a self-described introvert who is happier at home than going out on his days off.

players.name: Daichi Kamada
players.bio: A player with a high football IQ, defined by a tireless work rate and sharp reading of the game. Since moving to England with Palace from Lazio in 2024 Kamada has again proven his tactical flexibility, capable of operating both as an attacking midfielder and in a deeper role. His focus has always been on his work ethic, including in the pre-season periods, which he once called “more like hardship than training, and no one trains as much as I do”. He brings a wealth of experience to the national side, having played in the top divisions in Japan, Germany, Italy, Belgium and England, and he helped Palace win the FA Cup in his first season. He released his official biography in 2023, called: “Unwavering Belief: 12 People Testify to the Growth Story of Japan National Football Team Player Daichi Kamada.”

players.name: Tsuyoshi Watanabe
players.bio: After moving from FC Tokyo to Gent in 2021 he developed into one of the Belgian team’s standout players, earning the club’s Player of the Year award in the 2024-25 season. Built on a powerful physical foundation, he rarely shows any weakness in duels. He is also dominant in the air, using his anticipation to win headers at a high point, and poses a threat in set-piece situations with his goalscoring ability. Having raised his profile in the Belgian league he secured a move to Dutch giants Feyenoord last year. “I don’t want to leave defending to others, I want to organise and lead the back line myself,” he says. “I don’t feel pressure right now; I’m excited to see how far I can go.”

players.name: Yuito Suzuki
players.bio: A versatile attacker who likes to glide past opponents with deft feints and smooth control. Capable of playing on the wing, in the middle or as a shadow striker, Suzuki creates chances and finishes them too. At the core of his game lies a clear personal philosophy based around strength of character. “It’s important to grow as a person,” he says. When he moved to Brøndby in Denmark he chose not to reveal to everyone he met that he was a footballer, wanting to be judged as a “normal” person instead. "I was shy in Japan,” he told GQ, “but after going to Denmark I started to proactively strike up conversations. I became friends with café staff and people I was introduced to. I wanted to be just a normal friend, so there are some I didn't tell that I was a player." He signed for Freiburg last year for a reported fee of up to £8m, making him Brøndby’s most lucrative sale. Freiburg's chief sporting officer, Jochen Saier, said they had been after Suzuki for “a really long time … he can both score goals and set up his teammates. His pace, flexibility and technique will improve our overall attacking play.”

players.name: Ayase Ueda
players.bio: Inspired by his dad who wore the No18 in homage to his hero, the former Germany international Jürgen Klinsmann, Ueda chose the same number for the national team. He is a talented forward, known for smart reading of the game, full of neat diagonal runs and intelligent positioning. “Only the final 10 percent of an attacking move – the goal itself – is down to me,” he said. “The other 90 percent comes from the build-up, so my role is to bring out the strengths of my teammates and turn it into a successful move.” At the World Cup four years ago he featured in just one match, but this summer, with more experience and a growing sense of responsibility, he is determined to make his mark on the world stage. He joined Feyenoord in 2023 for £7m from Belgium’s Cercle Brugge.

players.name: Koki Ogawa
players.bio: A strong, gritty all-round forward capable of heading, holding up the ball and finishing with either foot, Ogawa models himself on his idol, Robert Lewandowski. In 2017, he suffered a serious knee injury and was left out of the Tokyo Olympics squad, facing a long road to recovery. Opportunities have come and gone since, but it taught him plenty: “I approach every day as if it’s my last chance, throwing everything into football,” he says. “I play every match with the feeling that I’m desperate for goals.” He is starting to make his mark in the Eredivisie, having joined NEC Nijmegen in 2023. At international level, he knows there is competition for places, but backs himself. “I think I’m probably the best natural goalscorer,” he told Fifa.com last year. “A forward’s job is to score, and, more than anything, I back myself to find the net and help the team win matches … I want to be the guy who pops up with a goal when the team needs it the most.”

players.name: Ayumu Seko
players.bio: A defender who excels in reading the game and outmaneuvering opponents through intelligence and anticipation. He is also effective in possession, with precise passing to build from the back. He joined Ligue 1 Le Havre on a free from Grasshopper Zurich last year, becoming the club’s first ever Japanese player. As a child he was inspired by the popular Japanese manga Detective Conan. “When Conan presses the button on his belt, a ball comes out. I always dreamed of kicking it like him,” he recalls. He is versatile like Detective Conan, too. “I’m the only one who can play all positions in a back three,” he says. His ability to perform at a high level regardless of position makes him an invaluable asset this summer.

players.name: Hiroki Ito
players.bio: After moving from Júbilo Iwata in J2 to Stuttgart in 2021 Ito quickly established himself as a real talent in European football. Renowned for his exceptional left-footed precision and his long-range distribution, he combines physical strength in duels with an aggressive mindset, while his intelligent positioning and covering deny opponents time and space. He made a big-money move to Bayern Munich in 2024 and, despite suffering three serious injuries soon after joining, he made a strong recovery to become a big presence in their back line. He feels at home in Germany, telling Bayern’s website last year there’s “really good sushi in Munich”. He added: “I’m a typical Japanese person. Politeness is very important to me. The German and Japanese mentalities are similar, with cleanliness, politeness and punctuality. But I’m not shy. I always fight for 90 minutes on the pitch.”

players.name: Takehiro Tomiyasu
players.bio: Few players inspire as much trust through their consistency and integrity as Takehiro Tomiyasu. His character has remained unchanged since childhood. During his middle school years he would arrive for football sessions before everyone else to set up, and stay behind to clean up. “If I arrive early and prepare everything, training can start right away,” he said. Dominant in the air, physically strong in duels, and equipped with impressive agility, he possesses all the attributes required of a top-level defender. While he can do a job on either flank as a full-back, it’s at centre-back where his composure and stability stand out. He moved to Arsenal in 2021 from Bologna for £17m but his time in London was hampered by injuries, and was ultimately ended by a serious knee injury which required surgery. He left the club last summer, but was picked up by Ajax on a short-term deal in December. If he can prove he has fully recovered match fitness, he should be a key player this summer.

players.name: Tomoki Hayakawa
players.bio: A late bloomer who earned his first call-up at 26. Hayakawa, known for quick reflexes and being sharp reacting to second balls, played a key role in helping Kashima Antlers secure their first league title in nine years last season. A couple of years ago he began working with a personal trainer to reassess his body mechanics and range of motion, and has continued to elevate his performance levels. He made the bench in last October’s international friendly against Brazil, but won’t be satisfied until he earns first-choice status – and knows he will have to prove his ability at the top level to do so. “Unless I can stop someone like Mbappé one-on-one, I can’t truly compete for a starting spot.”

players.name: Kaishu Sano
players.bio: Following the advice of his father, a former skier, Sano spent much of his childhood wearing traditional wooden geta sandals. “It definitely helped me develop my balance,” he said, and it still shows now, with outstanding core strength and movement. He’s always difficult to knock off the ball, combining stability in possession with the strength to win it back. His younger brother, Kodai, plays for NEC Nijmegen in the Dutch league, and during the Asian final qualifiers, both brothers were called up to the national team together. In 2025 he was recalled by Japan having been left out of the squad since his arrest the previous summer on suspicion of sexual assault. The investigation was closed by a public prosecutor without charges. Japan’s manager, Hajime Moriyasu, said Sano had given him the impression that he had "reflected deeply" on the episode, saying: “Should someone who was part of the family of the team be cast out of society and out of football for making a mistake?" Sano issued a statement saying: “I am truly sorry for causing trouble to so many people with my actions.”

players.name: Junnosuke Suzuki
players.bio: In his third professional season Suzuki transitioned from a defensive midfielder to a centre-back, and the decision paid off. Last year he made the move from J1 side Shonan Bellmare to FC Copenhagen, and has earned plenty of praise in Denmark for his precise passing with both feet and his ability to read the game. A first call-up soon followed and, in the international friendly against Brazil last October, he played the full match, looking strong and assured against some of the world’s top attackers. His experience as a midfielder gives him a wide field of vision and composure on the ball. A former high school coach describes him as someone who is “never influenced by his emotions” and “always listens carefully, even when the feedback isn’t directed at him”.

players.name: Kento Shiogai
players.bio: Remained largely unknown until the summer of his final year of high school and went on to study at university. There, he wore the No 10 and quickly stood out. While still enrolled, he secured a move to Yokohama F. Marinos, making his J.League debut and scoring his first goal just three months later. He then moved to NEC Nijmegen before joining Wolfsburg in January this year, reaching one of Europe’s top five leagues in the span of just a year and a half. His greatest weapon is his extraordinary acceleration, having recorded a sprint speed of 36.2 km/h. He even studied Usain Bolt’s running form to refine his explosiveness, combining that pace with powerful shooting and dynamic dribbling. In March this year, he earned his first call-up to the Japan national team and assisted the winning goal on his debut. Now he is part of the World Cup squad.

players.name: Yazeed Abulaila
players.bio: Has emerged as Jordan’s first-choice goalkeeper in recent years, earning widespread trust from fans because of his composure, reflexes and ability to perform in high-pressure situations. His reputation grew significantly during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup, where he played a crucial role in Jordan’s journey to the final. He also contributed to Jordan’s run to the Arab Cup final, further reinforcing his image as a goalkeeper capable of stepping up in key moments. At club level, he was part of the squad that won the Jordanian Pro League title in 2025. Earlier in his career, Abulaila gained experience abroad on loan with Al-Jabalain in Saudi Arabia. While not immune to occasional mistakes, his overall consistency ensures he remains the most trusted goalkeeper in Jordanian football.

players.name: Mohammad Abu Hashish
players.bio: Born to an Iraqi mother, he made his name in Jordan with Al-Baqa’a, Al-Salt and Al-Wehdat, before gaining valuable experience with Al-Ahed in Lebanon and the Iraqi sides Al-Zawraa and Naft Al-Wasat. He currently plays for Al-Karma in Iraq. Scored his first international goal during the 2025 Arab Cup in the 3-0 win over Egypt. A defensively solid full-back, he is also known for his pace and ability to push forward effectively down the left flank. "Seeing the Jordanian flag and our national team alongside the world's best will be an incredible feeling," he told Mojeh Men last month.⁠

players.name: Abdallah Nasib
players.bio: An indispensable figure who played a key role in the Nashama’s run to the 2023 AFC Asian Cup final. A consistent and disciplined defender, he forms a strong partnership with Yazan Al Arab, particularly in a 3-4-3 system. Began his career with That Ras before spells at Al-Ramtha and Al-Wehdat, then impressed abroad with Al Ittihad Alexandria in Egypt. He later returned to win back-to-back Jordanian league titles with Al-Hussein, and now plays for Al-Zawraa in Iraq. Known for his fighting spirit and aerial dominance, Naseeb is effective both defensively and in attacking set-pieces.

players.name: Husam Abu Dahab
players.bio: Earned his breakthrough during the Arab Cup, where he delivered consistent performances in the absence of key defenders, helping Jordan reach the final. A tall and physically imposing centre-back, he excels in aerial duels and clearing high balls. His presence adds strength and stability to the backline. A product of Al-Faisaly, he has spent his entire career with the club. He came close to joining Al-Salmiya in Kuwait at the start of the season and even trained with the team, but registration issues prevented the move, leading to his return to Al-Faisaly.

players.name: Yazan Al-Arab
players.bio: Widely regarded as a cornerstone of the team, admired for his leadership, physical strength and commanding presence in defence. His ability to organise the defence has made him one of the most recognisable and respected players in Jordanian football. He began his career with Al-Jazeera and later Al-Wehdat, where his consistent performances earned him national recognition. His move abroad included a spell at Selangor in Malaysia, marred by controversy involving a referee that earned a lifetime ban in the country. Despite this setback, he showed resilience by rebuilding his career in Iraq and Qatar before eventually joining FC Seoul. In South Korea, he quickly became a fan favorite beside his friend Jesse Lingard. Has been a key defensive leader for Jordan and also contributes offensively, particularly from set pieces. His goal against Iraq in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup knockout stage remains one of the defining moments of Jordan’s historic run to the final.

players.name: Amer Jamous
players.bio: A product of Al-Jazeera, he later moved to Al-Wehdat, where he gained recognition as a versatile midfielder. Earned him a move this season to Al-Zawraa in the Iraqi league. His forward runs and willingness to shoot make him a constant threat. Scored a stunning long-range goal against Tunisia in November and is looking forward to facing Argentina this summer. “First of all, I am a Barcelona fan and for me, Lionel Messi is the greatest player in football history,” he said recently. “All the national team players wish for Messi to participate in his very best form and for the Argentina team to be at full strength.”

players.name: Mohammad Abu Zrayq
players.bio: A quick and skillful winger, known for his flair, dribbling ability and attacking creativity. Has built a diverse professional career across African football, gaining valuable experience in different competitive environments. A product of Al-Ramtha, he moved at a young age to Al-Sailiya in Qatar before returning to his hometown club, where he helped them win the Jordanian league title in 2021. He later joined Al-Ahli Tripoli in Libya, which paved the way for a move to Tunisian giants Espérance. His journey also included spells with Al-Wehdat, Al-Hussein, Al-Ramtha again and Al-Shorta in Iraq, before settling at Raja in Morocco. Known as “Sharara”, his humorous personality, love of singing and talent for imitating others has made him popular within the squad.

players.name: Noor Al-Rawabdeh
players.bio: Has re-emerged as an important figure for Jordan after overcoming a serious injury, playing a key role in the Nashama’s victories during the World Cup qualifiers. Began his career with Al-Jazeera, where he featured alongside Mahmoud Al-Mardi and was part of their AFC Cup-winning squad in 2021. After a brief spell with Al-Faisaly, he moved to Malaysia’s Selangor, linking up with fellow Jordanian Mohammad Abualnadi. At international level, Al-Rawabdeh has scored three goals at the time of writing and is regarded as the team’s midfield orchestrator. Known for his composure, intelligence and ability to control the tempo, he is one of the experienced players in the squad.

players.name: Ali Olwan
players.bio: One of Jordan’s most potent attacking weapons, finishing as the top scorer of the 2025 Arab Cup with six goals. Their primary penalty taker, he scored nine goals during Jordan’s 2026 qualifying campaign. He recently suffered an injury with Al-Sailiya in Qatar, but has returned just in time for the World Cup. His strong on-field understanding with Yazan Al-Naimat has become a defining feature of Jordan’s attack with the duo sharing a close bond that extends beyond football into media appearances and commercial campaigns. “As everyone knows, we’ve got some excellent players in the national team who are capable of making Jordan proud,” he told Fifa.com.

players.name: Musa Al-Taamari
players.bio: The face of the nation’s football on the international stage. Since beginning his European journey in 2018, he has consistently demonstrated his quality across multiple leagues, earning recognition for his dynamic attacking style and technical ability. His career reached a defining peak during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup, where he played a central role in leading Jordan to a historic final. Operating mainly from the right wing, he combines pace, close control, and decisive finishing. Has been instrumental in Jordan’s qualification for the World Cup, scoring seven goals and delivering key performances. “Jordanian players are defined by grit and determination,” he told Fifa.com. “The backroom staff also do their work to perfection, through proper planning, well-structured programmes, training camps and some challenging matches.”

players.name: Odeh Al-Fakhouri
players.bio: An exciting emerging talent, he made headlines after becoming the first Jordanian to score in the CAF Champions League following his move to Pyramids last winter. His transfer came amid strong competition for his signature, particularly from Egyptian giants Al Ahly. His talent was evident from a young age, having appeared in children’s television programmes before rising through the ranks with Amman FC. He earned his opportunity with the Jordan national team following Yazan Al-Naimat’s injury during the recent Arab Cup, stepping in to fill the attacking void. His emergence on the international stage further boosted his profile and paved the way for his move to Egypt.

players.name: Nour Bani Attiah
players.bio: Bani Attiah has remained a reliable option in Jordan’s goalkeeping ranks, despite missing the recent matches against Costa Rica and Nigeria because of injury. He is expected to regain full fitness in time for the World Cup. Began his career with Al-Jazeera before moving to Al-Faisaly in 2019, where he gradually established himself as the club’s first choice. His consistent displays at the 2024–2025 AFC Champions League 2 caught the eye, while on the international stage, Bani Attiah was handed a rare opportunity during the 2025 Arab Cup. Starting in the final group-stage match against Egypt, he impressed by keeping a clean sheet in a 3-0 victory, producing a series of crucial saves.

players.name: Mahmoud Al-Mardi
players.bio: Known for his tactical intelligence and adaptability across different systems, he brings a lot of experience to this squad. But, even as he approaches his mid-30s, he continues to play an important role for both club and country, maintaining the trust of coaching staff thanks to his consistency and versatility. His ability to adjust his playing style over time has allowed him to remain competitive at a high level despite the natural physical decline that comes with age. Internationally, Al-Mardi has been a reliable presence since making his debut in 2015. He rose to prominence during the 2021 Arab Cup, where he scored two goals and proved his ability to influence major tournaments. His versatility also allows him to operate in different roles, including as a wing-back.

players.name: Rajaei Ayed
players.bio: One of Jordan's most experienced players, Ayed is a composed defensive midfielder who avoids unnecessary risks and prefers to move the ball efficiently with simple, effective passing. He began his career with Al-Wehdat and has also played professionally for Ratchaburi in Thailand and Al-Mesaimir in Qatar. Since 2023, he has been a key member of Al-Hussein Irbid, helping the club establish itself among Jordanian football's leading teams. His playing style closely resembles that of the former Arsenal midfielder Mohamed Elneny, relying on positional discipline, ball retention and tactical intelligence rather than flair. Ayed is also one of the most respected figures within the Jordan national team camp, valued for his experience and professionalism.

players.name: Ibrahim Sadeh
players.bio: Another product of Al-Jazeera’s youth system, emerging as one of Jordan’s most complete midfielders in recent years. Reached a milestone when he was named the Jordanian Pro League’s best player while at Al-Hussein in 2023. Plays in Iraq with Al-Karma and has won the Bahraini league title with Al-Muharraq and the Qatari second division title with Al-Shamal in 2024, showcasing his ability to adapt and succeed in different environments. A well-balanced midfielder, Sadeh contributes defensively and offensively, and is known for his powerful long-range shooting and high workrate.

players.name: Mohammad Abualnadi
players.bio: Born in Kansas, USA, Abualnadi played youth football in America before being picked up by the Jordan Football Association. “Having been with Sporting Kansas City academy, trained with the first team and played with the second I took a bit of a different route to others as I ended up going to university,” he told the #TAGpodcast. “University was super important to me. I got into Notre Dame and then I transferred to Pittsburgh, top universities and very difficult to get into.” With both his parents being from Jordan, he speaks Arabic, and went on to represent the Olympic team and then the senior national team.  At club level, he featured for Al-Hussein before moving abroad to Malaysia, where he now plays for Selangor. Stands out for his composure and confidence despite limited international experience while his calm presence at the back and ability to read the game have quickly earned him recognition within the national team setup.

players.name: Salim Obaid
players.bio: An experienced central defender who has earned 10 international caps at the time of writing, though he continues to fight to retain his place after disappointing performances in the Arab Cup. He has played for several Jordanian clubs, including Al-Ahli, Al-Wehdat, Shabab Al-Ordon and Sahab, before settling at Al-Hussein, where he won the Jordanian Pro League title last season. Started as a regular with the national team at the beginning of the latest Arab Cup campaign, but lost his place as the tournament progressed.

players.name: Ibrahim Sabra
players.bio: One of the most promising young talents in Asian football, he joined Turkish side Göztepe earlier this season before moving on loan to Lokomotiva Zagreb. Sadly he was injured just before the World Cup and is not travelling. Jordan are still deciding on a replacement for him.

players.name: Saed Al-Rosan
players.bio: The captain of Al-Hussein, he has led the club to back-to-back Jordanian Pro League titles. His rise to prominence was cemented by his key role in Jordan’s run to the 2025 Arab Cup finals. He began his career with Al-Arabi before moving through Mansheyat, Al-Baqa’a and Ma’an, eventually joining Al-Hussein in 2021, where he found stability and success. Made his mark internationally by scoring in Jordan’s 3-1 group stage win over Kuwait in the Arab Cup. Known for his calm personality and work-rate, he is valued for his leadership on and off the pitch.

players.name: Mohannad Abu Taha
players.bio: His reliability and tactical discipline have made him an essential option on the left flank, despite being naturally right-footed. Possesses a powerful strike which he showcased with a superb goal from a free-kick against Kuwait in the group stage of the 2025 Arab Cup. His performances have placed him among the players tipped for a future move to Europe. A product of Al-Wehdat’s academy, he also played for Al-Karkh in Iraq and Al-Orobah in the Saudi Pro League before settling at Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya. Came close to joining Belgian side Gent in 2023, but the transfer collapsed due to delayed paperwork.

players.name: Nizar Al-Rashdan
players.bio: A key figure in Jordan’s midfield, combining strong defensive duties with the ability to push forward and threaten from long range. A graduate of Al-Hussein, he later moved to Al-Faisaly before building a diverse career abroad. He featured for Newroz in Iraq and Emirates Club in the UAE, where he played alongside Andrés Iniesta, in addition to spells with Al-Khaldiya in Bahrain and Al-Zawraa in Iraq. Asked about playing with the Spanish maestro, he told Kooora: “I am living a beautiful experience and it has been made more beautiful by my presence alongside international stars such as Andrés Iniesta and Paco Alcácer.” Al-Rashdan now plays in the Qatari league with Qatar SC. For Jordan, Al-Rashdan has scored four goals at the time of writing, most notably a dramatic stoppage-time winner against Iraq at the 2023 AFC Asian Cup.

players.name: Abdallah Al Fakhouri
players.bio: Al-Fakhouri has worked his way back into the plans of Jordan head coach, Jamal Sellami, after a difficult period marked by a dip in form and his selection for Jordan’s final squad for the World Cup reflected this renewed confidence in his abilities. Made his international debut at just 18, but has since been limited to 16 caps at the time of writing, largely due to Yazeed Abulaila’s dominance. He faced criticism after conceding a costly goal against Kuwait in the qualifiers, yet continues to stand out for his agility and shot-stopping ability. Maintaining focus and composure in key moments will be crucial on the international stage.

players.name: Ehsan Haddad
players.bio: The captain and one of Jordan football’s most respected leaders. He recently returned to training after a serious injury that kept him sidelined for a long period. An international since 2015, Haddad has represented all four of Jordan’s biggest clubs – Al-Faisaly, Al-Wehdat, Al-Ramtha and Al-Hussein – before two successful spells in Iraq with Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya and Al-Shorta. His experience across different leagues has shaped him into a complete and disciplined full-back who delivered standout performances as captain during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup. Haddad’s influence extends beyond matchdays to the extent that the Jordan FA included him in training camps even while he was still recovering from injury.

players.name: Ali Azaizeh
players.bio: The Germany-born young forward rose to prominence during the AFC Under-23 Asian Cup earlier this year. After receiving approval from Kuwaiti side Kazma to participate, he scored four goals, attracting attention despite Jordan’s quarter-final exit to Japan on penalties. That breakthrough earned him interest from several regional clubs, leading to a loan move to Al-Shabab in the Saudi Pro League. He began his career with Al-Ramtha, contributing to their league title in 2021. Later enjoyed a successful spell with Al-Khaldiya in Bahrain, winning the Khalid bin Hamad Cup in 2025 and scoring the decisive goal in the final against Al-Riffa. Combines sharp finishing with confidence, marking him as a key talent for Jordan’s future.

players.name: Mohammad Al-Dawoud
players.bio: Revived his international career after joining Al-Wehdat midway through the 2025-26 season, where his strong performances in the Jordanian Pro League earned him a return to the national team. Scored his first goal for Jordan in the 2-2 draw against Costa Rica in March, marking a remarkable comeback. The son of the former Jordan international  Rateb Al-Dawoud, he has played for several domestic clubs plus a spell in Bahrain with Al-Hidd before settling at Al-Wehdat.

players.name: Anas Badawi
players.bio: Badawi earned his surprise call-up to the World Cup squad after injuries to Adham Al-Quraishi and Issam Al-Samiri, as well as the unexpected exclusion of Ahmad Assaf. He is expected to serve as the backup right-back behind the captain, Ehsan Haddad. Badawi earned his first cap in May’s 4-1 friendly defeat to Switzerland. He began his career with Al-Jazeera before joining Al-Faisaly last summer, but an injury shortly after his move sidelined him for a period of time.

players.name: Raúl Rangel
players.bio: Rangel’s rise at Chivas was built on patience and presence. Nicknamed Tala, he carries himself with the calm authority of a goalkeeper older than his 26 years, commanding his area and trusting his instincts. His breakout came when Chivas needed reliability and he delivered, combining sharp reflexes with strong positioning. Unlike the traditional loud, constantly shouting keeper, Rangel’s style is more measured, choosing his moments to make an impact. Off the pitch, he’s known for keeping a low profile, grounded and focused on steady improvement rather than hype. That mentality shows in his game: rarely rushed, rarely shaken. He may not yet have the global spotlight, but Rangel goes into the World Cup as Mexico’s likely first choice and has the tools and temperament to grow into something much bigger.

players.name: Jorge Sánchez
players.bio: Sánchez built his career on speed, intensity and a refusal to back down. From his early days at Santos Laguna to becoming a key figure at Club América, he established himself as a dynamic right-back who thrives on constant movement. His European adventure has taken him through Ajax, Porto and now PAOK, where he continues to adapt to different tactical demands. Sánchez is at his best when making attacking overlapping runs and quick combinations, demonstrating his remarkable energy. “I never stop running, that’s my game,” he once said. Off the pitch, he’s known for his confidence and strong personality, traits that sometimes mirror his aggressive style on it. When he finds balance between attack and defence, he becomes an interesting weapon on the right side.

players.name: César Montes
players.bio: César Montes might be called El Cachorro – The Puppy, a nickname bestowed on him by an uncle just before he made his professional debut – but there’s a quiet authority to everything he does. Tall, dominant in the air and composed under pressure, he built his reputation at Monterrey before taking his game abroad, now anchoring the defence at Lokomotiv Moscow. His style blends traditional centre-back strength with modern ball-playing ability, rarely rushed and always aware. “I’ve always liked to defend strong, but fair,” he once said, and that balance defines him. Off the pitch, Montes is known for his calm demeanor and close-knit family life, far removed from the noise that surrounds many professionals. On it, though, he’s decisive: a constant threat on set-pieces and a defender who organises those around him. He doesn’t chase attention, he lets it come to him.

players.name: Edson Álvarez
players.bio: Álvarez plays football like it’s a personal battle. From his breakthrough at Club América, where he once scored twice in a title-winning final, to his rise in Europe, he has built a reputation on intensity and presence. At Ajax, West Ham and now Fenerbahçe, he became the kind of midfielder opponents notice immediately: strong in tackles, dominant in duels and relentless in protecting the back line. “I like the dirty work,” he has said, embracing the less glamorous side of the game. Off the pitch, Álvarez carries the story of a player who fought his way up from modest beginnings in Tlalnepantla, staying deeply connected to his family and roots. On it, he brings edge, leadership and a sense that nothing comes easy, because for him, it never did.

players.name: Johan Vásquez
players.bio: If Vásquez plays like nothing was ever handed to him, it’s because it wasn’t. Raised in Navojoa, far from Mexico’s traditional football heartlands, he climbed step by step through the lower divisions before reaching Europe. At Genoa he has earned respect the hard way, adapting to the tactical demands of Serie A with grit and intelligence. “I thought I knew how to defend in Mexico, but I didn’t,” he said. “When I arrived here, there were things I was never taught. There, they forgive you. Here, they don’t forgive a single mistake.”  A left-footed centre-back, he’s aggressive in duels but composed on the ball, often stepping out to start attacks. Off the pitch, he keeps a low profile, grounded by his family and roots, rarely drawn to the spotlight. That humility carries into his game: no theatrics, just timing, positioning and consistency. Vásquez isn’t loud, but his performances speak clearly, a defender who reads danger early and treats every challenge like it matters. He was a member of Mexico’s Olympic bronze medal-winning team in 2021.

players.name: Erik Lira
players.bio: Lira is the type of player coaches trust instinctively. Developed at Pumas, he built his reputation on discipline, awareness and a tireless work ethic that rarely dips. At Cruz Azul, he has grown into a modern holding midfielder, always positioned correctly, always offering a passing option. His game is built on reading situations early rather than reacting late. Off the pitch, Lira is known for his quiet personality and analytical mindset, the kind of player who studies matches and opponents in detail, almost like a chess player thinking moves ahead. He doesn’t rely on flair, but on control and balance. In a team full of attacking talent, Lira is the one making sure everything holds together, unnoticed by some, indispensable to those who understand the game.

players.name: Luis Romo
players.bio: Romo’s career is proof that versatility can be a strength rather than a compromise. He has played as a defensive midfielder, a box-to-box runner and even as a centre-back when needed, always adapting, always contributing. His rise with Cruz Azul, where he was instrumental in ending the club’s long title drought, showcased his timing in the box and his ability to deliver in key moments. “I just try to help wherever the team needs me,” he once said. Off the pitch, Romo is known for his grounded personality and work ethic, a player who built his career step by step rather than through early hype. On it, his intelligence and positioning allow him to influence games in subtle but important ways.

players.name: Álvaro Fidalgo
players.bio: Fidalgo never quite followed the script expected of a Real Madrid academy graduate and that’s what makes his story so interesting. After developing in Spain, it was in Mexico, with Club América, where he truly found his identity: a midfielder defined by elegance, intelligence and constant movement. Now at Real Betis, he brings that same rhythm to La Liga, dictating play with quick touches and sharp positioning rather than physical dominance. Nicknamed El Maguito, the Magician, for his ability to make the ball disappear under pressure, Fidalgo thrives in tight spaces. Off the pitch, he is thoughtful and articulate, often reflecting deeply on the game and his journey. “Football is about understanding spaces,” he once said – and few read them as naturally as he does.

players.name: Raúl Jiménez
players.bio: After the skull fracture in 2020 that threatened his career and forced him out of the game for eight months, Raúl Jiménez re-emerged with a protective headband and a completely different outlook. “I almost lost everything, even my life. So I’m trying to enjoy football as much as I can,” he explained. “Everything, not just football. Everything I try to enjoy more.” In England he’s admired for his composure and link-up play, but his story carries more weight than goals alone. But there has been some criticism of late, amid poor results for the national team and a personal goal drought (penalties excepted) – he has criticised fans for booing, while José Ramón Fernández, the doyen of Mexican football journalism, recently named him the most overrated player in the setup.

players.name: Alexis Vega
players.bio: Vega has always played on instinct. Explosive, unpredictable and capable of moments others don’t even attempt, he built his reputation as one of Mexico’s most naturally gifted attackers. From his early days at Toluca to his time at Chivas and back again, Vega has been the kind of player who can change a game in seconds: a sudden dribble, a powerful shot, an unexpected decision. “I like to enjoy football, to play freely,” he has said, and that freedom defines both his best moments and his inconsistencies. Off the pitch, he has faced scrutiny and pressure, part of the reality of being a high-profile talent in Mexico. But when focused and confident, Vega remains a difference-maker few defenders can comfortably handle.

players.name: Santiago Gimenez
players.bio: Gimenez grew up around football, but his rise has been anything but automatic. The son of former Mexico international Christian “Chaco” Gimenez, he had to carve out his own path, first at Cruz Azul and then in Europe, where his instinct inside the box quickly stood out. At Milan, he continues to refine a striker’s craft built on movement, anticipation and clinical finishing, though he comes into the World Cup on the back of a difficult season. “Goals are about being in the right place at the right time,” he has said, and that is normally where he can be found. Deeply religious, Gimenez often speaks about faith as a guiding force in his life, celebrating goals with gestures that reflect that belief.

players.name: Carlos Acevedo
players.bio: Acevedo doesn’t just guard the goal; he lives in it. Passionate, vocal and often spectacular, the Santos Laguna captain has built a reputation as one of Liga MX’s most exciting goalkeepers. His reflex saves and willingness to throw himself into impossible situations have made him a fan favourite in Torreon. “I like to feel the game, to live it intensely,” he has said. That intensity is constant: shouting instructions, celebrating blocks, reacting to every play. Off the pitch, Acevedo is equally driven, known for his leadership and strong connection with teammates. He’s also open about the mental demands of his position, embracing pressure rather than avoiding it. In a role where mistakes are magnified, Acevedo chooses to play on the edge and more often than not, it works.

players.name: Guillermo Ochoa
players.bio: Guillermo Ochoa has made a career out of showing up when the world is watching. From his unforgettable performances at World Cups, especially Brazil 2014, where he seemed to repel everything, to a globe-trotting club journey across France, Spain, Italy and now Cyprus, “Paco Memo” has built a legacy on reflexes and resilience. “The World Cup is where I feel most alive,” he once said. Known for his cat-like saves and ability to rise in big moments, he has often been Mexico’s last line and biggest hope. Off the pitch, Ochoa is calm, almost reserved, a contrast to his explosive style in goal. A father and family man, he has balanced longevity with consistency, staying relevant across generations. Few goalkeepers define an era like Ochoa, and fewer keep doing it into their 40s – Ochoa turns 41 during the knockout stages – even if he has only started one international in the past two years at the time of writing.

players.name: Armando González
players.bio: González is known as “La Hormiga” (The Ant), a nickname that started with a childhood fear rather than strength. “When I was very little I thought ants were something very bad, like they killed you or were poisonous or something,” he explained. “I was scared, and then an uncle started calling me Hormiga and it stuck. Nobody calls me Armando, but I really like being called Hormiga.” The irony is hard to miss: on the pitch, González plays like someone who is not scared of anything. Energetic, persistent and constantly pressing, he thrives on chasing defenders and stretching back lines. At Chivas, he is beginning to earn opportunities through sheer intensity. His development has not gone unnoticed either, with clubs such as Borussia Dortmund and Feyenoord reportedly keeping an eye on his progress. Off the pitch, he remains grounded, aware that attention is only the beginning. “La Hormiga” may have started as a joke – but now it fits a player who never stops moving.

players.name: Israel Reyes
players.bio: Reyes has quietly become one of the most versatile defenders in Mexican football. Comfortable as a centre-back or full-back, he built his reputation at Puebla before stepping into the pressure cooker that is Club América. There, expectations are relentless but Reyes adapted quickly. Calm on the ball, tactically aware and rarely rushed, he reads situations well and prefers anticipation over last-ditch defending. Off the pitch, he is known for his reserved personality, keeping a low profile despite playing for one of the most scrutinised clubs in the country. That composure carries into his game: no unnecessary risks, no panic. Reyes may not always grab headlines, but coaches value him for exactly that reason, he brings balance, discipline and reliability wherever he plays.

players.name: Julián Quiñones
players.bio: Born in Colombia but a naturalised Mexican, with a wife from Monterrey – Quiñones turned down a callup from the country of his birth in 2023 as he revelled in Mexican football, with one local journalist reporting he had grown “tired of being ignored”. His journey has always carried a sense of proving himself, and it shows in his game. Powerful, direct and relentless, he built his reputation in Liga MX as a forward who attacks space aggressively and never stops pressing defenders. Atlas and Club América turned him into a title winner and a constant threat. “I like to fight for every ball,” he once said, and that mentality defines him. Off the pitch, Quiñones keeps a relatively low profile, but his story, crossing borders, adapting cultures adds another layer to his identity. On it, he brings intensity, strength and a forward’s instinct that rarely switches off.

players.name: Orbelín Pineda
players.bio: Pineda plays football with a smile and that’s not just a cliché. Whether in Liga MX or now in Greece with AEK Athens, his energy and joy are a constant, reflected in the way he moves across the pitch. Versatile, technically sharp and full of running, he can operate in multiple midfield roles while always looking to connect play. His career has taken him through key Mexican clubs such as Chivas and Cruz Azul and into Europe, initially with Celta Vigo, adapting each time without losing his identity. “I just enjoy playing, wherever I am,” he once said. Off the pitch, that same lightness of spirit defines him, approachable, relaxed and well-liked in every dressing room. On it, though, he is relentless, pressing, creating and constantly offering solutions. Few players combine work rate and creativity as naturally as Pineda.

players.name: Obed Vargas
players.bio: Vargas is arriving earlier than most and learning faster than many. At 20 he has already experienced the kind of challenges that shape careers, including standout moments against top-level opposition such as Barcelona. At Atlético Madrid, working under Diego Simeone has added another layer to his development: discipline, tactical awareness and an understanding of what it takes to compete at the highest level. “You have to earn everything here,” is a message often associated with Simeone’s teams and Vargas is living it daily. A composed midfielder who reads the game well and keeps the ball moving, he blends technical calm with growing physical edge. Off the pitch, he carries himself with quiet confidence. The talent is evident but it’s the environment he’s in that could define how far he goes.

players.name: Gilberto Mora
players.bio: Mora is quickly learning what it means to be watched. After his performances at the Under-20 World Cup his name began circulating far beyond Liga MX, with scouts from clubs such as Barcelona, Real Madrid, Arsenal and Manchester United reportedly tracking his progress. It’s a level of attention that can overwhelm young players, but so far Mora has leaned into it. At Xolos, as Club Tijuana is known, he plays with a maturity that stands out: technically clean, confident under pressure and always looking to move the game forward. He doesn’t hide, even when games get tight. Off the pitch, “the Mexican Pedri” remains grounded. Mora is still developing, but the trajectory is clear and now, so is the spotlight.

players.name: Mateo Chávez
players.bio: Football was never a distant dream for Mateo Chávez, it was part of everyday life. The son of former professional player Tilón Chávez, he grew up around the game, absorbing its rhythms long before stepping onto the pitch himself. That background shows in the way he plays: composed, aware and rarely rushed, even under pressure.
