At Alkmaar, a club known for developing young talent, Chávez is refining a modern defensive style built on positioning and clean distribution from the back. Moving to Europe at a young age – he left Chivas for the Netherlands last summer, aged 21 – demanded adaptation, but also accelerated his growth. Off the pitch, he carries that footballing lineage with quiet pride rather than noise. Still early in his career, Chávez isn’t just following a path, he’s trying to build on a legacy.

players.name: César Huerta
players.bio: Huerta’s career has never followed a straight line. A product of the Chivas academy, he was unable to establish himself at Mexico's biggest club and spent years searching for an opportunity through loans at Atlético Morelia and Mazatlán. For many players, those detours become dead ends. For Huerta, they became motivation. Everything changed at Pumas, where his fearless dribbling, energy and personality turned him into one of Liga MX's most exciting attackers. His performances eventually earned him a dream move to Europe. Injuries have made his first season with Anderlecht in Belgium frustrating, sidelining him for much of the campaign, but Huerta has refused to let adversity define him. “You have to keep believing even when nobody else does,” he said in the Claro Sports documentary series La Selección en Tus Manos. Direct, unpredictable and full of confidence, “Chino” arrives at a (largely) home World Cup as proof that perseverance can sometimes be the greatest talent of all.

players.name: Guillermo Martínez
players.bio: Martínez’s path to the top level has been anything but straightforward. Known as ‘Memote’, he spent years moving through different teams, mainly in Mexico’s second division, before finally establishing himself in Liga MX with Pumas. “I would think I was doing well, but I never got the opportunity and many times I thought about giving up,” he said. “I didn’t know if I was good enough.” That process shaped a striker who values every opportunity he now gets. Tall, strong, physical and always ready to battle defenders, his style is direct: hold-up play, aerial duels and a willingness to do the hard work inside the box. “Nothing has come easy for me,” he has said, reflecting on a career built step by step. He may not fit the mould of a traditional star, but his determination has made him impossible to overlook.

players.name: Jesús Gallardo
players.bio: Gallardo has reinvented himself more than once. Originally a winger at Pumas, he evolved into a left-back capable of covering the entire flank, combining attacking instincts with defensive responsibility. That transformation made him a constant presence for Mexico over the years. Fast, direct and relentless in his runs, Gallardo thrives on repetition: sprinting forward, tracking back, doing it all again. “I like to go up and down all game,” he has said, and few embody that better. Off the pitch, he’s known for his straightforward personality and strong connection to his roots, never straying far from the values that shaped him. At Toluca, his experience now complements his physical tools, making him a reliable option in a role that demands both endurance and discipline.

players.name: Luis Chávez
players.bio: Chávez knows what it means to wait. As a child, he spent nine years separated from his father after he emigrated to the United States in search of work. Their reunion finally came years later at a petrol station, a moment Chávez recently described in a Claro Sports documentary as one he will never forget. His football journey has been equally demanding. He battled his way through Mexican football before emerging as a star with Pachuca, then turned down lucrative offers in Liga MX to pursue his European dream with Dynamo Moscow. Like César Huerta, injuries threatened to derail his World Cup hopes, keeping him sidelined for more than 300 days this season. Yet Chávez has never been one to choose the easy road. Mexico fans still remember the stunning free-kick he scored against Saudi Arabia at Qatar 2022, one of the finest goals in the country's World Cup history. A gifted left foot, relentless determination and a life shaped by sacrifice make him one of the most compelling figures in Mexico's squad.

players.name: Roberto Alvarado
players.bio: Roberto Alvarado, who has inherited the nickname El Piojo – the Louse – from his Argentinian hero Claduio Lopez, plays with the freedom of someone who never lost his street-football instincts. From his early days at Necaxa, his quick feet and fearless dribbling stood out, and at Chivas he has embraced the responsibility of being a difference-maker. Left-footed, explosive and always looking to take on defenders, he thrives in chaotic attacking moments. “I like to play without fear,” he has said. Off the pitch, Alvarado is known for his easy-going personality, sense of humour and love of music, often sharing glimpses of a lighter, relaxed side that contrasts with his intensity in games, listing his hobbies as walking his dogs and watching movies. He’ll try things others won’t and sometimes fail but that unpredictability is exactly what makes him dangerous. When he’s confident, he can tilt matches on his own.

players.name: Brian Gutiérrez
players.bio: Gutiérrez represents a modern, attack-minded midfielder shaped by confidence and creativity. Developed in a system that encourages technical expression, he stands out for his ability to glide past opponents and find pockets of space between the lines. Still early in his professional career – he turns 23 the day before Mexico play South Korea – he plays with a sense of freedom, always looking forward, always trying to make something happen. That boldness means he can be inconsistent, but it’s also what makes him dangerous. Off the pitch, Gutiérrez is part of a new generation more comfortable with visibility, embracing social media and the spotlight that comes with playing for a club like Chivas. His challenge now is turning flashes of brilliance into reliable excellence, but the raw talent, quick feet, vision and instinct, is already there.

players.name: Yassine Bounou
players.bio: One of the main characters in Morocco’s deep run at Qatar 2022, Yassine Bounou is a fan favourite. Born in Montreal but raised in Casablanca, “Bono” loves the game and has a special affection for River Plate despite an early soft spot for their rivals. “When I was young I always followed football in South America, particularly Boca Juniors,” he said. His love for River would grow while he was at Atlético Madrid, where Diego Simeone (the former River manager) and Germán “Mono” Burgos (the former River goalkeeper) were in the dugout. Bono has even named his dog “Ariel” in an homage to River’s iconic No 10, Ariel Ortega. After nine years - and nearly 160 La Liga games - in Spain, Bounou moved to Saudi Arabia and Al-Hilal in 2023.

players.name: Achraf Hakimi
players.bio: Last year’s African footballer of the year, Hakimi is considered the best right-back in the game and perhaps the first Moroccan superstar. But off the pitch the 27-year-old confirmed in February that he would face trial after being accused of rape in 2023 He denies the allegations. "Today, a rape accusation is enough to justify a trial," Hakimi said on social media. "This is as unjust to the innocent as it is to the genuine victims. I calmly await this trial, which will allow the truth to come out publicly." One of the key players of PSG’s first Champions League title in 2025, Hakimi still has a close friendship with Kylian Mbappé, his former teammate now at Real Madrid. “He was the one who helped me with my French when I first got to Paris,” he said.

players.name: Noussair Mazraoui
players.bio: A devout Muslim, Mazraoui’s career has been shaped by his faith. In 2024 he asked for special permission from the Moroccan federation to make the pilgrimage to Mecca during an international break. “The link between Manchester United and the Muslim community is very important because the community is close to my belief. And if I can give them a little back, that’s something good to do,” he said in an interview with his club. After suffering his fair share of injuries, Mazraoui revealed that a change to his diet has helped him greatly. “I’m not drinking any sodas any more – fizzy drinks. It changes a lot because of the sugar, you don’t get it in your body any more. Just water makes a huge difference eventually,” he told the Athletic last year.

players.name: Sofyan Amrabat
players.bio: Nicknamed “The Gladiator”, Amrabat has never really fulfilled a promising career. After a brilliant World Cup in Qatar, the midfielder joined Manchester United on loan from Fiorentina and, despite winning the FA Cup, they decided not to activate his buyout clause. Amrabat doesn’t think it was Erik ten Hag’s choice. “I’ve had a special relationship with Ten Hag since I was a 20-year-old at Utrecht," he said before facing United with Fenerbahce in the Europa League. "He wanted to keep me this summer so I wish him the best but not for Thursday. I hope they lose,” he told the Daily Mirror. The 29-year-old joined Real Betis on the last day of the 2025 summer transfer window. “It was crazy. I had offers from Saudi Arabia and Sevilla, but when Betis showed interest it was clear for me that I wanted to join them,” he said.

players.name: Nayef Aguerd
players.bio: Having been part of the team that reached the semi-finals in Qatar, Aguerd missed the game against France after picking up an injury during the warm-up. Physical set-backs have slowed the centre-back’s career but he has always found inspiration within his family. In 2023, after a difficult season with West Ham, Aguerd went home. “It was a good summer. I got to spend it with my family in Morocco. Throughout the year, they don’t see me enough,” he told the club website. “My little sister is at school in Morocco and the holidays don’t match up, so it’s hard to see each other. When she was on holiday, I was with the national team. I only saw my family twice last season, so I took the decision to spend my summer completely in Morocco with them. It was so wonderful.” After having surgery on a sports hernia in March, Aguerd chose to go back to Morocco in order to be in the best shape for the World Cup.

players.name: Ayyoub Bouaddi
players.bio: One of the most sought-after talents in Ligue 1, Bouaddi made his senior debut for Lille in the Conference League aged 16 years and three days, making him the youngest player in the history of Uefa’s club competitions. Not only does the midfielder possess a composure beyond his years, but he is also a very astute student. In June 2023 he won the French football academies’ eloquence competition held at Paris’s Elysée Palace in front of the first lady, Brigitte Macron. He went on to obtain his high school diploma and is now studying for a degree in maths and physics. According to Bruno Génésio, his Lille coach, Bouaddi “must be protected from what surrounds him, in order to bring him to the highest level. He’s got such big potential. It is rare to come across players of his age with the qualities that he has.” After the teenager represented his native France from Under-16 to Under-21 level, Morocco won the battle for his international allegiance.

players.name: Chemsdine Talbi
players.bio: Born in Belgium to a Moroccan father and Belgian mother, Talbi’s talent shone through in Club Brugge’s 2024-2025 Champions League campaign. It was hard to imagine that a few months before, he wasn’t even considered a starter. Talbi had missed a large part of the previous season with a knee injury but resilience was part of his young career. “He’s always been smaller than the others,” says his former coach Dwight Waeytens. “He was explosive and always managed to go past his opponents, but he needed to work on his physicality. He hasn’t always done it with a big smile on his face, but he still did it.” Talbi’s father was on hand to help, even if it meant going from Brussels to Bruges when his son’s fridge needed a restock. The winger’s first season in the Premier League has yielded big goals against Liverpool and Chelsea.

players.name: Azzedine Ounahi
players.bio: Nicknamed “The Bee” or “El Número Ocho” after Luis Enrique spoke highly of him following Morocco’s historic win against Spain in the Last 16 in Qatar, Ounahi is one of the most talented players in the Morocco squad. Yet his move to Marseille and subsequent loan to Panathinaikos did not help him showcase his quality. Last summer’s transfer to Girona was seen as a wake-up call for the midfielder. “I feel at ease here, I found my desire and my smile playing football,” he said after reviving his career in Catalonia. “I started working with a personal coach who is responsible for many things, including my diet and additional exercises. I train at the club in the morning and I practice with my coach in the evening. It helped me a lot, both physically and mentally.” His Afcon tournament last winter, with Morocco reaching the final, was cut short by injury.

players.name: Soufiane Rahimi
players.bio: In Morocco, people refer to a player that has spent most of their career at one club as “Weld L’ferqa” (the team’s child). Rahimi was, in fact, born in Raja Casablanca’s training ground, meaning he literally is the “Weld L’ferqa”. His father, Mohamed, was Raja’s kit manager for decades and the club offered him a tiny house at the training ground to live with his family. But Rahimi didn’t benefit from his father’s connections at the club and was let go as a youngster. After a stint at neighbouring club l’Étoile, Rahimi was reintegrated at Raja. His performances made him a fan favorite and Al Ain seized the chance to recruit him. Rahimi, who is a skilful dribbler, helped the Emirati club win the 2024 AFC Champions League and qualify for the 2025 Club World Cup. His younger brother Houssine joined him at Al Ain last summer.

players.name: Brahim Díaz
players.bio: The protagonist of January’s controversial Afcon final, winning the dubious late penalty that caused Senegal’s players to leave the pitch in protest and then failing miserably with his Panenka once they had returned. The next day Díaz expressed remorse, stating that his “soul ached” and took full responsibility for the defeat that was eventually overturned by Caf, pending Senegal’s appeal. The penalty miss came after a sensational first tournament for Díaz, who admitted the moment will be hard to overcome and vowed to keep fighting “not for me, but for everyone who believed in me”. The decision of Díaz, playing week in week out for Real Madrid, to switch to Morocco from Spain in 2024 was a significant breakthrough. Born in Malaga to a Moroccan father, Díaz never shies away when he’s asked about his allegiance. “I am 100% Moroccan and 100% Spanish. I love both countries,” he told Cadena Ser in 2024.

players.name: Ismael Saibari
players.bio: Born in Barcelona, Saibari struggled in his early years, not taking his first steps until the age of two owing to a congenital condition. To correct the condition, he had to wear a special orthopedic device for over a year to keep his feet straight and help him walk. “It wasn’t even about becoming a footballer or anything like that,” Saibari has said. “That machine helped. It’s a device that helps babies walk straight. And yeah, luckily, it worked.” Saibari started playing football in Terrassa near Barcelona, but the economic crisis in 2008 forced his parents to seek a better life elsewhere and they chose Belgium. After playing in several academies, Ismael joined Anderlecht, only to be released. “They told me I was too fat,” he said. “I was playing well. I was in a good moment. It was an honour to play there. Then, just one day before the new season started, I got the news. It was really painful.” Saibari moved to Genk, where he scored a decisive goal in a youth game against … Anderlecht. He has since helped PSV achieve back-to-back Dutch titles.

players.name: Munir El Kajoui
players.bio: A national team player for over a decade, Munir is one of the veterans in the Atlas Lions squad. He’s got a unique status, as the only player born in the Spanish enclave of Melilla (northern Morocco) in the setup. As a young player still trying to find his way to professional football, Munir would work for his father after training. “I had to help, but that was a bit odd. Some of the fans who watched and cheered for me during the games were also asking me for a coffee or a meal in my father’s restaurant,” he said. After playing in Spain, Turkey and Saudi Arabia, Munir chose to join the Moroccan side RS Berkane in 2024 and played a key role in the club’s maiden top-flight title last year.

players.name: Zakaria El Ouahdi
players.bio: A key figure in the Morocco team that won the Under-23 Africa Cup of Nations in 2023 and clinched a spot on the Olympic podium a year later, El Ouahdi is a modern full-back who contributes a lot going forward. Born near Antwerp, Zakaria joined Genk in 2023. Whereas his teammate Bilal El Khannouss moved to Leicester in 2024, El Ouahdi insisted on staying, despite interest from Benfica. He revealed months later that he decided to postpone any move because his father, Mohamed, was fighting leukemia. Mohamed has played a huge part in Zakaria’s career and was the one encouraging him to choose to represent Morocco. When rumours surfaced that El Ouahdi was considering a switch to Belgium, his father denied it across the Moroccan media. “Of course, Belgium is a great country, but my heart is Moroccan,” Zakaria told Het Laatste Nieuws.

players.name: Issa Diop
players.bio: Born in France to a Moroccan mother, Diop is the grandson of Lybasse Diop, the first Senegalese to play in Ligue 1. But Diop is most known for having a strong opinion when asked which team he would represent at international level. “No other team than France,” he replied frankly in an interview with Canal+. Naturally, when Morocco’s new head coach, Mohamed Ouahbi, gave the centre-back his first call-up in March, he was asked about those comments. “He never said that he dislikes Morocco, that was fake news,” Ouahbi said. “I had a very honest and straightforward conversation with him, and it convinced him to join us.” With almost 170 games in the Premier League at West Ham and Fulham, Diop could be the missing piece in the Morocco backline.

players.name: Samir El Mourabet
players.bio: Thriving in a very promising Strasbourg team, El Mourabet earned his first Morocco cap in March. The 20-year-old performed well as a defensive midfielder in friendlies against Ecuador and Paraguay but his young career hasn’t been without its challenges. Born in Strasbourg, El Mourabet had to put his career on hold in 2020 when the club let him go aged 13, deeming him overweight. He then joined Lyon’s futsal team, where he was clearly better than most. After turning down a professional contract to play football in Spain, El Mourabet went back to Strasbourg, where he played his way into the Under-19 team within six months. “My son is not the kind of boy who’s going to start crying and say that everything is ruined,” his father, Tijani, said later. After establishing himself in the first team under Liam Rosenior, El Mourabet was frequently called up by the Morocco Under-20 manager, Mohamed Ouahbi, now in charge of the senior side.

players.name: Gessime Yassine
players.bio: “His moves remind me of Hatem Ben Arfa,” said the former Senegal striker Demba Ba after he recommended Yassine to the French team USL Dunkerque while he was their sporting director. The natural-born dribbler from southern France switched his allegiance in 2024 and played in the Under-20 World Cup last October, which Morocco won. “He’s always had a ball with him,” his mother told Le360. “As a kid, he never played with cars or other toys. When he was little he’d open the fridge and throw some eggs and try to juggle them.” After the skinny teenager struggled at club trials, he landed that move to Dunkurque in Ligue 2. Gary O’Neil has given him opportunities since he joined Strasbourg in January and he earned his first senior cap for Morocco in March.

players.name: Abdessamad Ezzalzouli
players.bio: “Ez Abde”, as the talented winger is known in Spain, failed to impress last time out in Qatar but he has since become one of the most important players for club and country. Born in Beni Mellal in central Morocco, Ezzalzouli moved to Spain at a young age. Barcelona scouts spotted him at Hércules and he made his first-team debut under Xavi. After a loan spell at Osasuna, he is now thriving under Manuel Pellegrini at Betis, scoring the opener in the 2025 Conference League defeat to Chelsea. It was while at Barcelona that Ezzalzouli tried to convince a 16-year-old Lamine Yamal to play for Morocco instead of Spain … “but nothing,” he told AS in 2024. “His decision must be respected. I hope Yamal will do the best possible because he is a spectacular footballer.”

players.name: Chadi Riad
players.bio: Riad was destined for the top. After winning the Under-23 Africa Cup of Nations and graduating from Barcelona’s La Masia academy, he moved to Crystal Palace after a good stint at Real Betis and a first call-up and goal for Morocco. But the defender’s young career has been riddled with injuries since he set foot in London, spending almost two years out with a knee issue. “To go from a recognised player to a nobody has been the hardest part. I was imprisoned in a gym for over a year, watching my teammates train while I had to lift weights. But thanks to my family who moved to London, it all got easier,” he told Le360Sport.

players.name: Youssef Belammari
players.bio: Trained in FUS Rabat, Belammari moved back to his hometown of Casablanca in 2023 to join giants Raja. Raja’s unbeaten league-and-cup double season then became a distant memory after major changes at the top of the club and the imprisonment of the former president. The situation became insufferable for Belammari, who voiced his concerns after a loss in the league. “You can’t expect to reap roses when you plant thorns,” he told the media in the mixed zone. Belammari’s gifted left foot, and his ability to find space and free the flank for his winger, make him a key piece in Mohamed Ouahbi’s gameplan. His willingness to be a back-up made him a natural choice for Morocco’s manager.

players.name: Ayoub El Kaabi
players.bio: The veteran striker is ready to give the World Cup another go. El Kaabi went to Russia in 2018, starting the opening 1-0 loss to Iran but not again. He wasn’t called up for the 2022 edition but he kept going, just like he always has. El Kaabi was trying to make a living as a carpenter when he was first scouted by Racing Casablanca. After winning the Golden Boot in Morocco’s second tier and in the African Nations Championship, he left to play abroad at Hebei in China. He was playing for Hatayspor in Turkey when the city of Antakya was devastated by the 2023 earthquake that killed his teammate Christian Atsu. At Olympiakos, El Kaabi is a legend for scoring vital goals in their run to Conference League glory in 2024, including an extra-time winner against Fiorentina in the final. “What I like about him is that he shows you that he is calm,” said the Olympiakos sporting director, Darko Kovacevic. El Kaabi is most famous for his ability to pull off a bicycle kick – two of his three goals at last winter’s Afcon came in that fashion, earning him the nickname “Bicycle man”.

players.name: Ayoube Amaimouni
players.bio: Undoubtedly the biggest surprise in the squad, Amaimouni has enjoyed a meteoric rise in the last few months. Born in Catalonia to Moroccan parents, Amaimouni moved to Germany aged 10. His family settled in Dortmund and he trained at the German federation’s facilities. After stints in Germany’s fifth tier, he was recruited by Hoffenheim, where he helped the under-23 team earn promotion. This season, Amaimouni continued his scoring form, attracting interest from Frankfurt and a €200,000 January move. A pure winger with a wand of a left foot, there have been comparisons to Lionel Messi, especially for a kid who grew up an hour’s drive from Barcelona. “I actually prefer Real Madrid and am a fan of Cristiano Ronaldo,” he told Hessenchau.de in April. “But my footballing idol is Neymar. He's a street footballer and, like me, likes to try things out.” One idol that he might face in his first World Cup game.

players.name: Ahmed Reda Tagnaouti
players.bio: After being part of a World Cup squad for the second time in Qatar 2022, it seemed that Tagnaouti’s life following Morocco’s performance would be easy. But a footballer’s life goes through many ups and downs. The next season, he was shown the door by Wydad Casablanca despite being one of the heroes that delivered the 2022 Caf Champions League. Tagnaouti struggled with physical and mental health problems, going through depression, but after a relatively unnoticed stint at his hometown club Maghreb Fez, he was back on track after joining AS FAR in 2024. The club have put their faith in him despite fans’ scepticism. Tagnaouti seized his chance and was one of the central figures of the AS FAR team as they reached their first continental final in four decades.

players.name: Bilal El Khannouss
players.bio: In Qatar, El Khannouss made history as the youngest Morocco player to feature in a World Cup at 18. His choice to play for the Atlas Lions came very early on and has since created a trend within dual nationality holders. “It was his own choice. Morocco didn’t want to miss the chance,” his father, Mohamed, told l’Avenir. “He went straight into the senior team. Between this and an interview with [Belgium’s Roberto] Martínez, there was no debate.” El Khannouss made the choice, but it pleased his dad. “His grandparents would have wanted it. My father died two decades ago and he was crazy about football,” Mohamed said. The midfielder, who joined Leicester from Genk in 2024 and spent the 2025-26 campaign on loan at Stuttgart, has been an inspiration to many Belgian-Moroccan players, but he denies being a role model. “Whether it’s Morocco, Senegal, Algeria or Côte d’Ivoire, I think Africa has got a lot of talent,” he said. “If we choose our African countries, Africa could become very big.”

players.name: Neil El Aynaoui
players.bio: In Morocco, the name “El Aynaoui” comes with a lot of pressure. Neil is the son of the Moroccan tennis legend Younès El Aynaoui and had to make a choice between tennis and football when he was a child. Neil started playing football aged four in Barcelona – “in a country that breathes football, in a city where you live well”. His father was a great inspiration but never tried to influence his son’s choice of sport. “My parents taught me all the details about being a good professional,” Neil says. “My father never tried to force anything on me. He let me try my hand at a bit of everything. Football was my passion.” El Aynaoui had to face a very disturbing event earlier this year, when he was the victim of an armed robbery at his home in Rome. He and various members of his family, including his mother, were held at gunpoint. No one was injured.

players.name: Redouane Halhal
players.bio: Another Under-23 Afcon winner from 2023, Halhal has flown under the radar since. He refused to sign his first professional contract at Montpellier, where he was born and raised, with clubs across Europe already showing an interest. The shift in his career happened when he moved to Atlético Madrid where, despite playing for the B team, he experienced the intensity of Diego Simeone. “He loves football too much; he truly lives it,” Halhal says. “It is good to have a coach like him: what might be a detail to you is crucial to him. That way, you can learn from your mistakes and grow.” Having left Atlético for Mechelen in 2024, he had a convincing spell in the Dutch second tier with Helmond Sport before establishing himself back with the Belgian club and subsequently winning a place in the Morocco team.

players.name: Anass Salah-Eddine
players.bio: Born and raised in the Netherlands, just like many of his teammates, Salah-Eddine played for the Oranje at youth level, up to Under-21, before switching to Morocco. At 24, the PSV defender has come into a Morocco team lacking in real quality at left-back. His battling spirit comes from when he had to fight his way into Ajax’s senior team and he always underlines the help he got from his family to come through his struggles: “I’ve always worked hard, in strong youth academies. And I’ve also overcome serious injuries. I broke my ankle, tore my ligaments. That makes you mature quickly. I learned that I had to live like a monk to reap the rewards later, and my parents helped me tremendously.”

players.name: Bart Verbruggen
players.bio: Verbruggen, still only 23, has been first choice since the 2024 Euros, with manager Ronald Koeman relaxed about his relative youth. “I’m not at all worried,” he said. “At Brighton he’s proving he can handle the highest level.” Brighton’s coach, Fabian Hürzeler, agrees, pointing to the keeper’s growing confidence. “There’s now an even stronger personality between the posts. Even without the ball, he’s becoming much more of a presence.” Verbruggen signed his first professional contract with NAC Breda, but never played in the Dutch Eredivisie, instead making his breakthrough at Anderlecht. Then came 2023’s £16.3m move to England, a record fee for a keeper moving from the Belgian top-flight. His personal philosophy: “The most important thing is to be honest with yourself,” he told the Athletic last year. “Look in the mirror. See what you’re doing really well, see what you want to develop and work on it with a lot of joy, hard work and dedication. Life will give you back what you give it.”

players.name: Jurriën Timber
players.bio: One of the world’s best right-backs, he joined Arsenal in 2023 for £34m from Ajax, but was sidelined in March due to injury, and had to battle hard to be ready for the World Cup. He comes from a strong footballing family: his twin brother Quinten plays for Marseille, and one of his older brothers plays for VVV-Venlo in the second tier of Dutch football. Faith plays a very important role in his life. The former Feyenoord youth and Ajax player always takes a Bible with him to matches and reads Psalm 91 before kick-off, following his mother’s advice. “She told me: ‘Read this before every match, it will give you strength.’ Since then I’ve done that and it has brought success. I take as much as possible from the Bible and try to live as Jesus did, even though that isn’t always easy.” He uses his X account to tweet the passages he’s reading to his 226,000 followers. Sample tweet: “1 Thessalonians 5:8”

players.name: Marten de Roon
players.bio: When De Roon missed Euro 2024 with a knee injury it looked like his last shot at a big international tournament had gone. But here he is, at 35, one of several surprise picks by Ronald Koeman. The defensive midfielder, who had a season with Middlesbrough before returning to his former club Atalanta in 2017, played his 436th game for the Serie A club in March, breaking their record for most appearances. The qualities he brings are wide-ranging: tactical intelligence, relentless running and a big personality, which extends to social media, where he’s known for posting self-deprecating memes, Photoshops and comedy skits. Among them was 2021’s comedy vignette filmed in the Atalanta clubshop where he was on hand to sign and pay for the first three "De Roon" replica shirts purchased over the counter. Nobody turned up before closing time, with De Roon seen doing a jigsaw and playing cards while he waited, saying: “I bet Messi never has to deal with this.” His X profile biog reads: “Summary of playing football in Italy: when we win I get the best table and the food for free. When we lose, my wife has to go and pick it up.”

players.name: Virgil van Dijk
players.bio: Uefa has handed out the European Footballer of the Year award since 2011. Only once has the prestigious prize gone to a defender – in 2019, when Virgil van Dijk beat Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo. “I had to work hard for every step of the way – that's part of me and I'm very happy about that,” he said as he accepted the award. “I'm very proud, it's all credit to everyone that's helped me along the way.” The son of a Surinamese mother and Dutch father, he was initially criticised in the youth ranks at Willem II after being deemed too “laid-back”. Via FC Groningen, Celtic and Southampton, he arrived at Liverpool in 2018. A proper leader for club and country, even though in the Netherlands he still rarely gets the credit he deserves, being held in higher esteem in England. That’s not true back at Willem II, though, where they named a stand after him at their Sportpark Prinsenhoeve youth facility in Tilburg last year. The club said: “With this, his name is not only immortalised in the rich history of Willem II, but also emphatically linked to the development of youth football in the city.”

players.name: Nathan Aké
players.bio: The left-footer spent some time undecided about his international identity. Born in the Dutch town of Voorburg to a Dutch mother and Ivorian father, Aké played for Netherlands youth teams, then in 2017 held several sets of talks with the Ivory Coast head coach Marc Wilmots about switching allegianc. But when Dick Advocaat became Netherlands manager, he swiftly called Aké up, giving him his first, binding international appearance. A product of the Feyenoord and Chelsea youth systems, he has won it all since joining Manchester City from Bournemouth in 2020. Like Stefan de Vrij, he’s a big pianist, having taken to it during the coronavirus pandemic. “In the first lockdown we had a lot of spare time, so I taught myself from a few apps,” he told the Telegraph. “At City, I’ve played in front of the guys once in Dortmund at the hotel. I got a good reaction, but I don’t like too many eyes on me. I actually played at my father’s funeral too, but I found it difficult – there were too many eyes on me. It’s different with football, you’re used to it, but with the piano your hands start to shake and then you lose the tempo and everything.”

players.name: Jan Paul van Hecke
players.bio: AKA “the Giant of Arnemuiden”, a nickname honouring his time at his local amateur club in the Zeeland town of Arnemuiden. The robust centre-back was snapped up by Brighton from NAC Breda six years ago but his breakthrough took some time, via loan spells at Dutch club Heerenveen and in the Championship with Blackburn. Now, though, he’s a Premier League regular, and last year won both Brighton’s player of the year award and the players’ player of the year too. He is the nephew of former international Jan Poortvliet, who played in the 1978 World Cup final loss to Argentina. Van Hecke said in 2024: “My uncle is enjoying my journey, he said he is proud and he watches me every game. When I played against Ajax, winning away made him emotional, he was in tears … He told me all the stories from the World Cup he played in. He said they were robbed against Argentina!”

players.name: Justin Kluivert
players.bio: It definitely helps, as a youngster making your way in the game, if your dad was a global icon who has the phone numbers of top managers and players. It was a call between his dad Patrick and Roma legend Francesco Totti that led to Kluivert’s 2018 move from Ajax to Roma. He spent five years on their books, though three of them were on loan at RB Leipzig, Nice and Valencia, before he was sold to Bournemouth in 2023. That was the moment his career really took off and he earned a place back in the national team, having first made his debut in 2018. “I’m proud to wear the shirt of the Netherlands again,” he said after the recall. “I feel so much better, both on and off the pitch, in England. I feel so happy playing for Bournemouth.” His serious injury over the winter made him a doubt for this summer, but Ronald Koeman always said he would include him if he could prove his fitness.

players.name: Ryan Gravenberch
players.bio: Gravenberch signed a new long-term deal at Liverpool in March, reflecting his massive improvement at Anfield since joining from Bayern in 2023. Still only 24, his future looks bright – and much brighter than it had, for a time, in Munich. His father, Ryan Sr, revealed earlier this season that his son struggled badly to adjust when he first arrived in Germany, which is why they moved out there to support him. “The football world is hard,” his dad said. “Ryan was only 21 at that time and had a lot of problems. The move to Liverpool was the best thing that could happen: he went from that hell to his heaven.” As a child he learned a lot from his brother Danzell, who is eight years older. “When I was little I watched him play outside,” he told The Guardian in 2024, “and when we were a bit older I would go with him to play on the streets. He’s a big boy so it was difficult for me, but he toughened me up.” Danzell went on to have a nomadic football career of his own, playing for 13 clubs, while Ryan became Ajax’s youngest ever player when he made his debut aged 16 years and 130 days.

players.name: Wout Weghorst
players.bio: Argentina won’t want to face the Netherlands at this World Cup. If it happens, Weghorst will be ready to go to war again. The 1.98m striker proved his value as a supersub during the last World Cup when he changed the game against Argentina, scoring twice and leading the Dutch to extra time. But all his aggression on the pitch and in the tunnel – ending with a war of words with Lionel Messi who shouted “Qué mirás, bobo? Andá pa' allá, bobo” ("What are you looking at, fool? Go away, fool”) – left the Argentinians steaming. A real grafter, Weghorst climbed the football ladder, supported by his millionaire father, all the way to his dream club, Manchester United. But in his six months there on loan from Burnley in 2023 he only managed two goals in 31 appearances, none in the league. He earned an affectionate nickname at Old Trafford from his teammates, who called him Bobo, in honour of the Messi incident. He wasn’t a regular at Ajax last season, but Ronald Koeman will still be ready to use him from the bench to change games this summer.

players.name: Memphis Depay
players.bio: Memphis initially played with the name Depay on the back of his shirt, but in 2012 decided to drop his father’s surname, because he had walked out on the family when his son was four. A difficult childhood has been reflected in sometimes erratic behaviour. He famously drew criticism from animal rights groups in 2020 when he posted photos of himself with a baby liger, a cross between a lion and a tiger. He’s also known for his fashion, his music career, his in-play white headband and his skill move, standing on the ball with both feet. He did it during a Corinthians game against Palmeiras last year, resulting in a mass brawl. Brazil’s FA later said the move would be a yellow card offence in future, because it represents “provocation to a rival, and disrespects the game”. The documentary With Both Feet showed another side of him, centred on the enormous willpower it took to recover from a cruciate ligament injury in 2019. After mixed spells in Europe, including Manchester United and Barcelona, he moved to Brazil in 2024. During the World Cup qualifiers he left his passport at home in Brazil, missed his flight and arrived late. Typical Memphis, said his critics. But he remains the national side’s all-time top scorer, and a valuable part of this squad.

players.name: Cody Gakpo
players.bio: Religion plays a big part in Gakpo’s life and career. He listens to gospel music in his car, with The Blessing by singer Eline Bakker among his favourites; he often wears an “I belong to Jesus” T-shirt under his top, as made famous by his football idol Kaka; he led a Bible group in the Dutch squad at the last World Cup and he prays before games. “If I can’t go to church, I call the pastor,” he told Dutch paper NRC. “Faith gives me peace. And strength when I need it. I often read the Bible before I go to sleep. It is a kind of love letter that tells us how we should treat each other.” He joined Liverpool from PSV in 2023 for an initial £37m, and signed a new long-term deal last year. In his spare time, he’s a big reader. “I mainly read books about psychology, faith and mindset,” Gakpo told Algemeen Dagblad. “Black Box Thinking [by Matthew Syed] is about making mistakes … If you create a culture where admitting your mistakes is key, people will develop more. I try to translate this to the football pitch.”

players.name: Mats Wieffer
players.bio: Wieffer’s call-up was a bit of a surprise. Despite having 14 caps he hadn’t featured in the previous four international windows. But his club manager, Fabian Hürzeler, was among his biggest cheerleaders for a recall, pointing out his huge versatility. “He has a really good physicality, a good understanding of the game … he's an absolute team player.” Wieffer signed from Feyenoord in 2024 for a reported £25.4m, making him the Eredivisie side’s record sale. He wasn’t that impressed when he first arrived in Brighton, though. “It was summer, but the weather was terrible: grey, rain, wind. I was in a hotel with my family and seriously thought: where on earth have I ended up?” he told Dutch outlet tubantia.nl in March. “But my parents stayed a bit longer after I left, and the next day the weather was suddenly beautiful. They sent me photos and I thought: OK, this is quite a different city.” He has grown to love the ice cream on the sea-front, too. "They have the best ice creams in South England. Just as good as at Van der Poel in Hengelo, but much bigger. One scoop is enough."

players.name: Robin Roefs
players.bio: The 23-year-old only needed one season as a first-choice keeper in the Eredivisie to secure a move to the Premier League. And shortly after that switch from NEC to Sunderland last year, Roefs was handed his first call-up by the national team coach, Ronald Koeman. “It’s all happening so fast,” the keeper said after getting the call. “You could say I’m living in a fairytale.” He joined NEC at the age of 11 and, before breaking through as a professional, spent years coaching keepers at his old amateur club Heeswijk. As he left his hometown club at such an early age they were not entitled to any compensation when he made the big transfer to England, but after the move Roefs went back to visit, and presented them with a cheque for €25,000. “Heeswijk is the club where it all started for me,” he said, “and I’m happy that I can now give something back.” The club said in response: “The majority of the money will go to the youth. The youth are the future. Robin’s a real role model, and one of our youth goalkeepers even received some training from Robin today. It was a truly wonderful day for us.”

players.name: Tijjani Reijnders
players.bio: Reijnders’ parents were massive fans of the former Ajax great Tijani Babangida, so choosing what to name their baby was easy. His mum even made sure to go with the double j in Tijjani – the correct spelling of Babangida’s first name on his passport, rather than the version wrongly registered when Babangida moved to the Netherlands in 1991. Last year the two men finally met, when Babangida gave Reijnders a signed copy of his autobiography. Reijnders’ dad, Martin, was also a footballer, and these days manufactures tactical whiteboards, while Reijnders’ brother Eliano plays for Indonesian club Persib Bandung and, thanks to his mum’s nationality, the Indonesian national team. Reijnders joined Manchester City for £46.5m from Milan in June last year – some journey from his old part-time job in his local Aldi, which he did while he was a youngster at Zwolle. In 2023 he said: "I used to stock the shelves, work behind the till and earn a few hundred euros a month … my time at Aldi was really good for me. I've seen both sides of the coin."

players.name: Micky van de Ven
players.bio: One of the world’s fastest footballers. Van de Ven clocked a Premier League record top speed of 37.38km/h (23.23mph) against Brentford in January 2024 – and also occupies positions four and five in the top five. It didn’t always come naturally to him: at his first professional club, FC Volendam, he was nicknamed “Tractor” because of his awkward movement. “They thought at the time that I wasn’t good enough for professional football,” he said, “so I worked hard on my agility and speed”. He won the Europa League with Spurs last year, but arrived at the World Cup after a difficult season battling relegation, and with his club future the subject of plenty of speculation. He wears the number 37 shirt at Spurs, explaining last year: “When I was 15, we had a close group of friends. One of our best friends became ill, and he sadly did not survive. When I was here at Spurs for my signing day my best friend was with me. There were the three of us back then … We saw that number [37] was available and it was a special number to our friend who passed away.”

players.name: Guus Til
players.bio: Born in Zambia, Til’s family moved to Amsterdam when he was three. He was raised in AZ’s youth system, and grew into a solid attacking midfielder who can also do a job up front. He’s an unconventional player, and he has had his detractors – but he isn’t bothered by them. “I think it’s nonsense when people say I’m not good on the ball… And why does it have to look good anyway? I don’t consider myself a beautiful footballer; I’m quite gangly. Have you ever seen Medvedev play tennis? It looks like he has a fly-swatter in his hand. But he is one of the best tennis players in the world.” Speaking before his surprise call-up by Ronald Koeman he told de Volkskrant: “It is nice he is thinking about me. But if it happens, I don’t want it to look like I’m going along as a tourist. I want to go because I can play football well. I’m not going to sit on the bench just for the fun of it.” Whatever happens for Til this summer, don’t expect him to tell you about it: he really doesn’t like interviews. “No, I don’t like them, not at all. Others can talk about me, I’ll show it on the field.”

players.name: Noa Lang
players.bio: It’s been a lively journey so far. The controversial winger has had six clubs in the last five seasons. His teammates in the national squad call him “trouble”. But Lang proved several times that he can play great football, and never lacks self-belief. When he left Belgium, where he played for Club Brugge, he said: “I made Belgian football hot. I honestly think so. Believe me: Belgium is going to miss me when I'm gone. They are going to pray there will be a new Noa Lang.’’ And in 2022, after a scoring display for the national side, he said: “Was I nervous? No. I play in the Dutch national team because I can play football well. Why should I be nervous about something I can do very well?” Asked if he had been encouraged to tone it down a bit, he said: “This is how I am. Noa Lang is being interviewed, then you will get Noa Lang … I like to just say what I think." This summer he will rival Cody Gakpo for the left-wing position.

players.name: Donyell Malen
players.bio: Leaving Aston Villa for Roma on loan last year definitely paid off. Malen scored 10 goals in his first 12 Serie A games, matching Gabriel Batistuta’s record from 2000. “I am very proud of the comparison with Batistuta,” he said. “I’ve seen so many clips of him; he was a great player. My objective is to keep scoring more and more.” He made the move partly in a bid to keep his spot in the national team, because he was struggling for regular starts at Villa. He started out in Ajax’s youth system before moving to Arsenal in 2015, but returned to the Netherlands with PSV two years later. He moved to the Bundesliga with Borussia Dortmund in 2021, and to Villa, for £21m, last year. Ronald Koeman is likely to use Malen as his striker this summer if Memphis Depay struggles with injuries. If not, Malen will play on the wing. His personal philosophy: “I'm a forward. I try to create, score goals, give assists and just give the stadium a buzz. Try to do special things.”

players.name: Brian Brobbey
players.bio: After a disappointing season at Ajax, Brobbey’s place in this World Cup squad seemed light years away. But his £17.3m move to Sunderland changed all that, as he showcased his physicality, movement and ability to dominate Premier League defenders. He hasn’t always made the most clever career moves, though. Early on he left Ajax, tempted by RB Leipzig, but came back soon after. “It was a hard lesson for me,” he said. “If I had stayed at Ajax it would have been much better for my development.” His return didn’t go smoothly at first: goals were lacking, and then, in October 2023, he was involved in a horrible collision with RKC Waalwijk goalkeeper Etienne Vaessen who was left unconscious. The match was abandoned and Vaessen was out of action for weeks. When the final 10 minutes of the game were eventually played out in December that year, with Vaessen back in goal, the pair exchanged hugs. Vaessen said: “The frequent contact with Brian [during my recovery] did me and my family good. I didn't hold anything against him. He is a nice guy, and he gave me his shirt for my son. My son is a total Brobbey fan.”

players.name: Teun Koopmeiners
players.bio: As children, Teun and his little brother Peer both loved the Koen Kampioen series of football books by Dutch writer Fred Diks. Inspired by the stories, they both grew up to be professionals – Peer plays for AZ Alkmaar – and are now collaborating with Diks on their own series of football adventures. Teun will have plenty of stories to share. Before 2024’s £46m move from Atalanta to Juventus, where he has struggled at times and been criticised by fans, he was heavily linked with Liverpool. But Peer said last year that Teun had no regrets about the decision to stay in Italy rather than push for a move to England. “Teun loves Juve. As his brother, I can tell you I had never seen him as happy as he was in the photos he sent me on the day of his presentation. His face said it all – I had never seen that kind of happiness on him before.” Teun made his international debut in 2020 but missed Euro 2024 because of injury.

players.name: Frenkie de Jong
players.bio: As a youngster in Ajax’s youth system De Jong was once tasked with providing some opposition for first-team players during a training session. Instead of keeping his head down and sitting back, he started dribbling around them and scoring, until the coach shouted at him to stop. It was a glimpse of what was to come. Paris St-Germain, Manchester City and Manchester United all made moves for him before he left Ajax in 2019, but his heart was set on Barcelona. As a boy he and his family used to holiday 50 miles away from the city on a campsite near L’Escala. “Those holidays were great,” he recalled, soon after sealing the €75m transfer. “There were three football pitches on the campsite. One summer, we even went to watch a Barça friendly. I remember Ronaldinho playing and it was incredibly exciting to watch him live.” These days, he is one of the legends. He wears the No 21 shirt as a tribute to his grandfather. “I brought my whole family to my presentation in Barcelona,” he said in 2019, “except my grandfather Hans de Jong, my father's father. He died on the day of my 21st birthday. It is kind of a tribute. He was a huge football fan."

players.name: Denzel Dumfries
players.bio: Named after the American actor Denzel Washington by his Aruban father and Surinamese mother, Dumfries spent many years playing for amateur clubs and was only signed by a professional team, Sparta Rotterdam, at the age of 18. He had to face down the doubters throughout his youth career. “It was always tough when they were laughing when I said I’d be a pro,” he told Sports Illustrated last year. “To be honest, I never thought I would reach this far … I’ve played at this level for a very long time now. It’s not something I was expecting when I was younger. But I knew I would be a professional football player, I knew.” The experience shaped his character, helped him build tremendous stamina and work rate, and brought him considerable success. With Inter he has amassed numerous major trophies, and he’s a key player for this Netherlands side. In 2014 he played two friendly matches for Aruba, but switched to the Dutch international set-up two years later. In 2023, together with his family, he opened a specialist youth care and education centre in his home city of Rotterdam for young people needing specialist support outside mainstream education.

players.name: Mark Flekken
players.bio: A real late bloomer. Flekken made his top-flight debut for Freiburg at the age of 25, after spending years playing in Germany’s lower divisions, where he once scored with a backheel for MSV Duisburg. At Freiburg he caught the eye of national team coach, Louis van Gaal, in 2022, and while he didn’t make the cut for the World Cup that year, he was named second-choice for Euro 2024 by Ronald Koeman, having impressed in England following 2023’s £11m move to Brentford. He left London last year for Bayer Leverkusen, with Phil Giles, Brentford’s director of football, saying: “We were expecting Mark to be our goalkeeper for many more years, but … he made it clear that he was interested in moving closer to home and with a possibility of Champions League football.” He sustained a knee injury in January, then struggled for consistency on his return, admitting in April his form had been patchy. “I'm a prime example of our problem. My performances haven't been consistent. It doesn't have to be a 10 out of 10 in every game, or even an 8. But an average of 6.5 or 7, then you're a very good keeper.”

players.name: Crysencio Summerville
players.bio: It was a grim season for West Ham, but a handful of players came out of it with credit, and Summerville was among them. After signing from Leeds for £25m in August 2024, his first season was disrupted badly by a hamstring injury. But last season he was at the centre of many of the team’s better moments. When relegation was confirmed he posted online: "West Ham United is too big, too special, and too powerful to stay down for long. The comeback starts now." But whether he will be part of that comeback attempt remains to be seen; plenty of rival clubs have shown an interest. Born in Rotterdam, he joined Feynoord’s academy in 2008, and caught the eye during a loan spell at ADO Den Haag. Away from football, he’s a big basketball fan. “I love to play on a basketball court because I love watching NBA,” he said in 2024. “My favourite players are LeBron James and Stephen Curry, both all-time greats, and I always try to replicate their shots when I’m on court.”

players.name: Jorrel Hato
players.bio: Long considered one of the brightest defensive prospects in Europe, Hato captained Ajax at 17 and moved to Chelsea last year for an initial fee of £35.5m. But he struggled to find his feet at first. “I would lie if I say the first couple of months were easy, because they weren't,” he said in May. “It was a difficult time, but the second half of the season you saw the better side of me. I’ve showed my qualities, the ones I showed in Ajax.” His former club manager, Liam Rosenior, was a big fan, saying: “I've been so impressed with his temperament, his physicality, technically. He is a joy to work with every day.” Hato is a big fan of Arsenal’s Jurriën Timber, a fellow Ajax product. “My coaches always told me that I needed to look up to Virgil van Dijk, but when I played in the Under-18s, I always looked up to Jurriën Timber,” Hato said last year. “I played with him for six months before he left, and I learned so much from him. When I came into the first team, he was my mentor.”

players.name: Quinten Timber
players.bio: Jurrien’s twin. It’s not actually that unusual for two brothers to play for the Netherlands - other examples include Frank and Ronald de Boer, Willy and René van der Kerkhof, Rob and Richard Witschge and, of course, national coach Ronald Koeman and his now-assistant Erwin. The Timber twins also have three older brothers: Dylan, a defender for Dutch club VVV-Venlo and the Curaçao national team, plus Shamier and Chris. In 2019 Chris spoke about the issues watching the twins develop as young players. “I always had trouble telling them apart. I would say that Quinten had made a nice move, but my mother would say it was Jurriën. They also had the same hairstyle, which didn't help either. Now, one is a bit taller and the other has a slightly rounder head.” Quinten arrived at the World Cup after a difficult season when he fell out with Feyenoord coach, Robin van Persie, after refusing a new contract. But his move to Marseille in January allowed him to prove his readiness for this summer.

players.name: Max Crocombe
players.bio: Of Cook Islands heritage, Maxime Teremoana Crocombe was born in Auckland before moving to England as a youngster. He was the man between the sticks in Salford City’s National League North winning season in 2017/18 – his form earning him a national debut for the All Whites in a friendly against Canada in 2018, the first of more than 20 international caps to date. He was a key force for Grimsby Town when it became the first team in history to eliminate five teams from higher divisions in a single FA Cup season, reaching the quarter-finals in 2023. In total, he has been at 10 clubs in the UK before cracking it with Millwall in the Championship and was first choice keeper by Christmas 2025. Along the way there’s been plenty of time on the bench. “I’m not really one to sulk about it,” he told the BBC. “I always believe I will play.” He’s twice earned the Golden Glove award at international tournaments and has retained All Whites head coach Darren Bazeley’s trust. He’s been nominated as having the best banter in the squad, leading fellow All White Ben Waine to say: “sometimes it’s dry humour but he is quite funny.”

players.name: Tim Payne
players.bio: One of the first names to go on the All Whites starting XI, Payne is valued for his versatility. He can fill in at both centre back and defensive midfield, although his main position is that of right-back. “His experience is phenomenal, he can play in a lot of positions across the backline,” one of his Phoenix coaches said. He made his All Whites debut at just 18 in 2012 and the same year played in NZ’s team at the London Olympics. His best break seemed to be signing with Blackburn Rovers but work visa problems limited his appearances at age-group level and, apart from a season with the Portland Timbers in the US, he has mainly stayed with the Wellington Phoenix, making more than 150 appearances for them in the A-League. On the eve of the tournament he became a social media star after an influencer from Argentina called on his followers to unite and make the little-known 32-year-old a “hero” of the upcoming World Cup.

players.name: Francis de Vries
players.bio: De Vries is valued not only for his outstanding defensive skills but also the grounded attitude and mentoring he brings to the squad. He’s had spells with MLS side Vancouver Whitecaps and Swedish club Värnamo but there’ve been setbacks too, prompting him to read up on sports psychology. He credits developing mental skills as helping his performance on the football pitch and has set up a business to help young players in this area. “Football is a teacher of skills that will help you in all areas of your life,” he told The Unused Subs podcast. “Football has to mean something more than results – that’s where real fulfilment, real connections come.” The philosophy is paying dividends in Auckland where fans chose him as Player of the Season in 2024-25 and for the All Whites where he is a regular starter.

players.name: Tyler Bindon
players.bio: Bindon is following in his former Football Ferns goalkeeper mum Jenny’s footsteps by representing NZ at a World Cup. As a child Bindon was a common fixture on the sidelines of the Ferns’ training sessions. His dad, Grant Bindon, is a former New Zealand volleyball captain and met Jenny while they were students in Illinois. Bindon came through the Major League Soccer system but opted for the All Whites despite attracting interest from the US selectors at Under-19 level. It is believed Tyler and Jenny are the first mother and son duo to play football at the Olympics. Former All White captain Ryan Nelsen believes Bindon “ticks every box” to succeed in the Premier League (he has signed with Nottingham Forest and spent the 2025-26 season on loan at Sheffield United) and will go on to become New Zealand’s best ever player. “He’s got all the qualities needed, including his calmness under pressure when on the ball, he says. A goalscoring threat at the set-piece – and leapt high to head home from a corner for his first goal at Sheffield United with his parents over from NZ in the stands.

players.name: Michael Boxall
players.bio: After nine years at Minnesota he is the all-time leader for starts and appearances and is a key figure in its backline. He says he is waiting for all the young guys to run past him, but “I’m still waiting”. Commentators praise his “massive physical presence” and his leadership skills, however, he does not like the off-season and the solo training it requires. “You go to some dark places and I just think of some creative ways to keep me going,” he says. Boxall, who is of Samoan heritage, was once the target of racial abuse during a match against Qatar in Austria. It led to the All Whites refusing to play the second half and they also filed a complaint with Fifa. “I’m proud of my teammates for taking the stand they did.” After 55 appearances for the All Whites without scoring he bagged one in NZ’s 3-0 win over New Caledonia to help the side qualify for this World Cup. Fun fact: He collects sports shoes. “I’ve got a few rare Nikes and a few Jordans as well that I only bust out when it’s not raining or snowing,” he says

players.name: Joe Bell
players.bio: In a squad with star names at both ends of the pitch, Bell is more than content with a role in the shadows, as he told Fifa.com. “I don’t like to be in the spotlight, so I think it’s quite nice to sit there between those who score and those who save goals and it’s an important role in the team. Obviously, playing in the centre of the pitch, there’s a lot of responsibility to be good both in attack and in defence, but especially with the ball. That’s what I enjoy the most about the game, having the ball at my feet.” Bell relaxed at home in Wānaka in Central Otago over the summer having helped his Norwegian club side Viking end a 34-year league title drought which saw fans, including a cabinet minister, invade the pitch. It got his club into Champions League qualifying as well so he could be “ticking some of the two biggest boxes you can tick” in 2026. Rated by his club for being good in individual duels and confident on the ball, Bell is aiming for a starting place come World Cup time but says “supporting and helping the team” is most important – a sensible attitude from a man whose middle name is Zen.

players.name: Matt Garbett
players.bio: The coach, Darren Bazeley, stuck by Garbett when he was let go by Dutch club NAC Breda, admiring his “super competitive attitude” and describing him to Stuff as “a great lad, with a great attitude”. Garbett is expected to be a key member of the World Cup squad and had been in good form for his new club this season until a serious foot injury sidelined him. Previously he said the move to England, where he was born, was “just what I needed”.  The Peterborough manager, Darren Ferguson, was pleased with his ability in midfield and said he was “an unmissable presence at trainings with his work rate and willingness to have a shot”. Garbett has represented NZ at various age-group levels before captaining the OlyWhites (Under-23s) at the 2024 Paris Olympics.

players.name: Marko Stamenić
players.bio: One of the most important players in the All Whites mix for his physicality and technical ability with the head coach, Darren Bazeley, admiring the midfielder’s “consistency in and out of possession”. Bazeley told the NZ Herald: “He’s tactically very astute.” Stamenić wants to help the All Whites “make achievements that probably no one has thought about with the World Cup and how far we can actually go”. In a three-year spell he has lifted trophies in Denmark, Serbia and Greece. A career highlight was the chance to live in his dad’s home country, Serbia, and absorb its culture, food and celebrations. “I’ve met very few Samoan-Serbs …it felt quite special. I love embracing both cultures …” Fun fact: His mum once made 30 Ferrero Rocher chocolate leis (garlands) for the All Whites and presented them to the players after a match against Samoa.

players.name: Chris Wood
players.bio: What Ronaldo is for Portugal, Wood is for the All Whites  - goalscorer and endlessly loyal to his country, so fans have been fretting about his fitness in the World Cup buildup. An inspiration for youngsters, he’s “the poster boy of the All Whites, he's definitely the standout”, RNZ journalist Matthew Nash wrote before the knee injury that derailed his latest Premier League season. Wood had an outstanding campaign in 2024-25, becoming the first Forest player in 30 years to score 20 plus goals in a season and the first Kiwi to be voted Premier League player of the month. The youngest ever All Whites captain says: “Playing for my country makes me extremely proud and honoured.” Returned from knee surgery in April and is hopeful of staying at the top for some time: “I'm not one with blistering pace, so I'm never gonna lose it. So that's good coming into my older years where I can actually still continue to do what I did when I was 22 years old.”

players.name: Sarpreet Singh
players.bio: Singh started all 10 of New Zealand’s internationals in 2025 and will be a key force in the World Cup if he recovers from a major knee injury. His signing for Bayern Munich in 2019 caused huge excitement at home but he could not break into the first team and stints with other European clubs followed. He spent the first half of 2026 back at Phoenix, where his career began, on loan from Serbian club Backa Topola. Born in Auckland to Indian parents who migrated from Punjab, Singh will become only the second Indian-origin player at the World Cup in its almost 100-year history. “I will do my best because I know I represent a lot of people. I want to try and create a pathway for future Indian players,” he told The Times of India. “Our goal [at the World Cup] is to get out of the group stage, and I don’t think we have to change too much [to achieve that].”

players.name: Eli Just
players.bio: Just is of Chinese descent through his mother. Despite his small 1.70m frame, he is comfortable with headers and is a smart attacker who thrives in tight spaces. He is often used on the right-wing or as an attacking midfielder for both club and country. He debuted for the All Whites at just 19 and says it has been rewarding to move from being a fan to wearing the strip, especially having his parents in the stand when the side qualified for the World Cup against New Caledonia in March 2025. It was “pretty special”, he told RNZ. Signing for Motherwell last year was another important step in ensuring his form would help him earn selection and game time at the World Cup, he told the Friends of Football website.

players.name: Alex Paulsen
players.bio: Paulsen is a special talent, who is seeking to displace Max Crocombe for the No 1 position in the All Whites. His four-year deal with Bournemouth - “A big moment for me and my family,” – when he was just 21 made him one of very few Kiwis to clinch a Premier League deal. “The intensity and the difference in how they train is just so competitive,” he told the Unused Subs podcast. The Cherries, no doubt impressed by his agility and reflexes, see him as a long-term project and he went on loan first to Auckland FC, where he was pivotal in them becoming “Premiers” in their first season in the A-League, and Lechia Gdańsk. His attitude, technique and passing are second to none, his All White teammate Ben Old Says. Old recalls the time his best mate was videoing his juggling skills on his new phone and a ball hit the phone which sailed out a small gap in the window of his 13th floor hotel room. “My first thought was, my mum’s gonna kill me,” Paulsen told The Unused Subs website.

players.name: Liberato Cacace
players.bio: Cacace is a key All White, being named as a stand-in captain during Chris Wood’s injury-enforced layoff. He is building on a career that began as a teen at the Wellington Phoenix before attracting the attention of Belgian club Sint-Truiden. That led to four seasons with Italian Serie A club Empoli – the first Kiwi to play in Serie A, much to the delight of his dad, who is from Naples and introduced his son to football when he was just three years old. Cacace’s signing with Wrexham attracted huge publicity at home due to the popularity of the TV series, however, a run of injuries has made for a difficult start. Fun fact: Cacace’s dad toasted the deal with the Wrexham manager, Phil Parkinson, and other club officials when they visited his restaurant in Wellington, as the side was preparing for a friendly against the Phoenix.

players.name: Alex Rufer
players.bio: Rufer is from Kiwi football royalty – his dad Shane is a former All White while uncle Wynton won four titles with Werder Bremen and was named the Oceania Footballer of the Century. Alex is his club’s longest serving player having debuted as a 17-year-old and in 2024 his leadership, ball-winning ability, and distribution led to him being named in the PFA A-League men’s team of the season. Fun fact: His family are all fishing fans and the rods are usually in the back of the car, he told The Unused Subs podcast. “It actually became something that took my mind away from football and the pressures of that.”

players.name: Nando Pijnaker
players.bio: Of Dutch heritage, Pijnaker has played in Sweden, Switzerland, Portugal, Denmark and Ireland, before returning to New Zealand to join new A-League club Auckland FC in 2024. His Irish experience was a standout. He joined Sligo Rovers, which he recalls as being “in the middle of nowhere and it has terrible weather all year round”. However, this was offset by “amazing” people, who welcomed Pinjaker and fellow All White Max Mata, he told WaiBOP Football. He made 77 appearances for Sligo. Fun fact: The jersey Pijnaker wore when he scored the winning goal in the 97th minute in Auckland’s match against Sydney FC is on display at Auckland Museum, representing the club’s hugely successful first season in the A-League.

players.name: Finn Surman
players.bio: After a slow start Surman was outstanding in the Timbers’ 2025 season and named as fans’ Player of the Year. He relishes the chance to learn from former England player and now the club’s head coach, Phil Neville. “He’s a very enthusiastic person … It gees me up a bit,” he told The Unused Subs podcast. The 1.90m defender is impressed with the league too, rating its intensity, flair and attackers’ speed as a big step up from what he was used to in the A-League. Surman left home at 15 to sharpen his football skills but any initial nerves were eased when he discovered living at an academy hostel in Wellington “was like a massive extended sleepover”.  He’s made his mark at age-group level, including captaining the All Whites at the 2023 Fifa Under-20 World Cup, but these will be eclipsed by getting some game time at “the pinnacle of football”. Fun fact: Surman is impressed that a fan with a chainsaw cuts off a piece of log to present to each of the team’s goalscorers at Portland’s matches – carving a coffee table is the plan if he manages to earn one.

players.name: Kosta Barbarouses
players.bio: Of Greek heritage, Barbarouses is the second most capped All White in the squad after Chris Wood and was first called up 18 years ago. He’s the first Kiwi to score 100 goals in the men’s A-League, has been named in the A-League men’s team of the decade and has won the championship with three different clubs. His career started as a 17-year-old schoolboy with the Wellington Phoenix in their inaugural 2007–08 season and he has returned to the club three separate times, becoming a fan favourite. He told RNZ a World Cup campaign comes at the cost of missing his daughters’ milestones such as starting school but added that he hopes they will understand when they are a bit older.

players.name: Ben Waine
players.bio: Waine has tasted both sides of life as a footballer – scoring on debut for the Under-20s and senior All Whites but also being frustrated by non-selection at club level and a goal drought. He has tapped into the expertise of a Kiwi sports psychologist to help him “dig in”. "I'm big on the mental side of the game and I do a lot of work on that,” he told the BBC. Having the tools to cope with the hard times has been “a massive help”, he says. During his time at Plymouth Argyle, Waine briefly played under the orders of England and Manchester United star Wayne Rooney. Waine has a Geordie mother so he is a supporter of Newcastle United and enjoys golf and going to the beach. Hit the headlines in England in March when his goal eliminated Sunderland – of all clubs – in the FA Cup fifth round.

players.name: Ben Old
players.bio: In the New Zealand context Old is a rare talent, according to the NZ Herald journalist Michael Burgess, highlighting the player’s raw pace, close control and ability to dribble past players. Old’s assets make him a lethal weapon on the counter attack and he is only the second New Zealander after Bill Tuiloma to play in France’s Ligue 1, where he has had to adjust to a more aggressive, less structured style than his Kiwi club the Phoenix. Setbacks have included a serious knee injury and the side’s relegation to Ligue 2 – but peace of mind comes with meditating before matches to use some of his best moments for inspiration. Instead of the photo shoot he was expecting on joining St Étienne an artist painted him. “It was a truly amazing experience and a memory I’ll remember forever,” he told Channel magazine. Fun Fact: Old was a child prodigy at golf, visiting the US aged just seven to compete against other young talents, and he hopes to play at the UK’s top courses as part of his desire to relieve the pressures of top class football.

players.name: Callum McCowatt
players.bio: Was voted Silkeborg player of the 2025 season by fans as well as being the club’s top scorer in all competitions. The main difference he’s noticed being based for six years in Scandinavia? “Players are much more mentally stronger and it's evident from the moment you walk in the door, which is something that I think New Zealand players should work on – myself included. I've taken years [working on it] and it's something I'm working on actively still,” he told Stuff. After moving through the age-group levels for New Zealand, he got his first cap in 2019 and scored a goal on debut against Ireland. He has taken to life in Denmark, which “feels like home now”, and is an admirer of how people are treated and is on the way to becoming fluent in the language. “I think maybe I've come as a boy and I'm a man now,” he says.

players.name: Jesse Randall
players.bio: Being able to play in attack and on the wing has helped propel the seven-cap rising star into the World Cup squad. His excellent form with Auckland FC in the A-League, including being their top goalscorer in the latest campaign, also helped his cause. He rates his pace as his best asset. Randall’s career is now taking off after a slow start – he was never approached by an academy in his home city, Wellington, and trials in England were “pretty ruthless”, he told Stuff. A spell with Charleston in the US didn’t lead to much game time and as the doubts grew, his career was only revived by a late call-up from All Whites coach Darren Bazeley to join his Under-23 squad for the Paris Olympics. After the World Cup Randall will be Scotland bound to start a two-year contract with Dundee United. Fun fact: Randall was just seven when he attended the match against Bahrain that saw NZ secure their last World Cup qualification in 2009. “That was a massive moment for me, knowing that’s what I wanted to do is play football.”

players.name: Michael Woud
players.bio: Woud has faced many obstacles in his career, including going 620 days without a professional match. Despite being the first signing for his newly established Auckland club he was promptly benched in favour of fellow All White Alex Paulsen. “It was tough and there were some dark moments,” he told The Unused Subs podcast.  As a teen Woud left NZ after signing for Sunderland but never made it further than the academy. There were spells in the Netherlands and Japan too and his luck has changed this year - he’s World Cup bound as the third choice keeper thanks to an injury to another contender Oli Sail.

players.name: Ryan Thomas
players.bio: Arguably the most gifted Kiwi player in a generation, he has had a career blighted by knee injuries – leading to gruesome surgery requiring his knees to be broken and lowered. He was only 19 when he made his debut for the All Whites and also starred for PEC Zwolle as a teenager, scoring twice in its shock 5-1 defeat of Ajax in the 2014 Dutch Cup final. He moved to PSV Eindhoven but in his first week ruptured his ACL. Out of the national side for six years, he went through dark times struggling with the mental impact of his injuries which led to him not watching football anymore. However, the surgery has now made him pain-free and he returned to the All Whites last year. “I'm just really happy to be part of the group again and being a part of something bigger than just club football,” he told RNZ regarding his World Cup hopes. The Dutch-Kiwi has been speaking only Dutch for five years and in an All Whites’ setting feels “a little bit of an idiot”, trying to converse in English. “I'm not exactly a Kiwi anymore and it kind of annoys me," he says.

players.name: Callan Elliot
players.bio: Elliot started as an attacker and morphed into a defender in part because he saw shortages at his club. He describes himself as a "modern-style" fullback who likes to get forward. After starting out at the Wellington Phoenix, he signed for Greek side Xanthi and then Motherwell on loan but they weren’t happy times. He told Stuff about breaks in his career when he hasn’t got game time or didn’t have a club: “Periods like those suck, but at the end of the day, playing football is still what I want to do, so that keeps me going.” For the All Whites he’s a back-up to Tim Payne and Storm Roux. Fun fact: Scottish-born, he loves Edinburgh. “I just think it’s a beautiful place. I always say to people if you get Edinburgh on a sunny day, it’s absolutely unbelievable,” he told the BBC.

players.name: Lachlan Bayliss
players.bio: Bayliss only won his first cap in March with the coach, Darren Bazeley, saying he’s “a bit of a bolter”.  Bayliss has met Bazeley’s challenge to aim higher after he played well for NZ Under-23s at the 2024 Olympics. Bayliss has had an outstanding season in the A-League with the Newcastle Jets, both assisting and scoring goals. “He’s an exciting player – very dangerous off the bench,” the pundit Paul Ifill said.
