Australian-born, he qualifies through his late father, who was a Kiwi. Playing for the All Whites would make his late father “very proud” he says.  “It would mean everything. I definitely feel a strong connection with New Zealand through him,” Bayliss told 1News.

players.name: Tommy Smith
players.bio: Only Smith and captain Chris Wood remain from the 2010 squad that went to the World Cup in South Africa. Now the fifth-choice centre back, coach Darren Bazeley rates his values and leadership: “Tommy has been very strong in that space.” Smith, meanwhile, told The Unused Subs podcast: “The centre-backs who are ahead of me in the pecking order [for the All Whites] are playing at a fantastic level, and my minutes are probably going to be limited, and that’s fine. I’ve come to terms with that.” It has been a distinguished career for a man once told by Manchester United legend Roy Keane: “My nan could have run faster than you and she’s dead.” The pair crossed swords at Ipswich when Keane was an “intimidating” manager. However, it was Keane who encouraged English-born Smith to switch his allegiance to NZ and he says he has never regretted it. Former All Whites skipper Ryan Nelsen believes it was only bad luck that stopped Smith from signing for a Premier League club. “I played alongside players who weren’t as good as Tommy,” he says. After a successful season with Auckland, Smith is playing in the English fifth tier for family reasons.

players.name: Ørjan Håskjold Nyland
players.bio: He may not be the most famous member of the Norwegian national team, but Nyland is one of the longest serving and is calm and reliable. He made his debut against Scotland back in November 2013 and has been the first choice for the past five years. Before that, Nyland was a multitalented athlete who excelled in other sports. In addition to being among the country's best goalkeeping talents in both football and handball, he was on the national alpine skiing team at the age of 13. "There was a tug-of-war and fierce competition for Ørjan, especially between the football and handball teams. Everyone wanted him, and everyone had to have him," his father, Jostein, has said. Nyland has had to contend with being a back-up goalkeeper at several of his clubs and in the past year he has started more matches for Norway than Sevilla.

players.name: Morten Thorsby
players.bio:
Thorsby is a midfield warrior and opponents should not be fooled by his gentle appearance.
In football, the No 2 shirt is traditionally worn by the right-back but Norway’s No 2 is by no means traditional. Thorsby has dedicated most of his adult life to the fight against climate change and global heating. His shirt number refers to the international goal of limiting the increase in global average temperature to below 2 degrees celsius. In 2022 Thorsby was appointed a European Climate Pact ambassador. He normally takes his bike to training and, if possible, travels by train rather than plane. When at Heerenveen, Thorsby convinced the club to invest in solar panels on the stadium roof. When asked about his thoughts of Norway’s tough World Cup group, he responded by focusing on the locations in Boston and New York: “I’m happy because we don’t have to fly so much.” If you see Norway arriving at the World Cup stadium by electric bus, you now know why.

players.name: Kristoffer Ajer
players.bio: If you follow the Premier League, you have probably noticed that long throw-ins have become popular. Brentford use this weapon to great effect and often the ball is launched on to the head of a tall Norwegian bloke, who flicks it on towards the opponent’s goal. At 1.98m, Ajer is the tallest outfield player for his club and the national team – and a threat at set pieces. As a youngster, Ajer was as good at school as he was at football and often got the highest grades – a big contrast to how the media judged his performance in Ståle Solbakken’s first game in charge for Norway, a 3-0 defeat by Turkey. Ajer was given a 2 out 10 by most people. "The player rating was deserved. I played really bad," he admitted later. Things have looked up for the 28-year-old since then and last year he established himself as the leader of the Norwegian defence – a role he will have at the World Cup.

players.name: Leo Østigård
players.bio: Østigård is among the shortest players on the team, yet he is perhaps the best header of the ball. "My father and I have practised heading a lot. I've been heading a ball since I was three years old, so by now I should be good at it," Østigård told NRK Sport. There were signs early on that the boy from the small town of Åndalsnes on the west coast was destined to become a top defender. He watched his first World Cup in 2006 and one player in particular made an impression on him. "Fabio Cannavaro isn't the tallest, but he was 'crazy' in the head and won every duel. He inspired me." Remarkably, Østigård went on to join one of Cannavaro’s former clubs, Napoli, in 2022 and won the Scudetto the following year. With Norway, Østigård lost his starting place in 2025 after some costly mistakes but he is a fighter and he will do anything he can to reclaim his place in the team.

players.name: David Møller Wolfe
players.bio: David Møller Wolfe is living proof that you don’t have to join an academy early to reach the Premier League. While most of the best talents from the Bergen area joined the city’s main club, Brann, early, Møller Wolfe, chose to stay with his childhood club Bergen Nord for as long as he could. He was still playing in the fifth tier in Norway when he was being picked for the Under-17 national team. The lightning-fast and attacking left-back grew up in Spain but moved back home to Bergen when he was 11 years old. It was in Spain he fell in love with football. Eventually, he developed so much that he joined Brann, where he won the Norwegian Cup, before being sold to AZ. Last summer, Wolfe fulfilled his destiny by joining Wolves for £10m.

players.name: Patrick Berg
players.bio: Eight years ago Berg’s contract at Bodø/Glimt was expiring. His form had been poor and he was uncertain about his future. “I was actually thinking about quitting football completely and finding something else to do,” he has said. A few months later he ended up signing a new contract with Glimt and has not looked back. He is a powerful midfielder who covers vast areas of the pitch. Only eight players ran more than him in the league phase of the Champions League. The name Berg is part of the furniture at Bodø/Glimt. His grandfather Harald “Dutte” Berg is one of Norway’s greatest players. His father, Ørjan, won 19 caps for Norway and his uncles, Runar and Arild, were also professional footballers. Having decided to stay, Patrick Berg became captain and has now won the Norwegian league four times, reached the semi-finals of the Europa League and helped to stun Manchester City and Inter in the Champions League.

players.name: Alexander Sørloth
players.bio: For a long time Sørloth was the journeyman who struggled to settle down. Before turning 25, he had played in Norway, Denmark, the Netherlands, England, Belgium, Turkey and Germany. Few would have thought that it was in La Liga that the big, strong striker, who earned the nickname "King of the North" from Trabzonspor supporters, would find his place. But it was at Real Sociedad, surrounded by players such as David Silva, Mikel Merino and Alexander Isak, that Sørloth truly made his breakthrough at the highest level. His father, Gøran, who was part of Norway's squad at the 1994 World Cup, was known in his home country as "the world's best striker with his back to goal". Alexander is also tough, but is most dangerous facing the goal at full speed. He is coming off a fine season at Atlético Madrid and rarely has a bad game for Norway. Sørloth may well soon become second to you-know-who in Norway’s all-time scoring charts, having already surpassed players such as Ole Gunnar Solskjær, Tore André Flo and John Carew.

players.name: Sander Berge
players.bio: You will struggle to find anyone who has a bad word to say about Sander Berge. The midfielder always seems to have a smile on his face and goes about things, seemingly, without a worry in the world. Yet life on the pitch has not always been easy for the 28-year-old as he has had to shoulder a lot of responsibility at the heart of the Norway midfield, while also being relegated with Sheffield United and Burnley. However, there have been several highs as well and since 2025 he has been an established Premier League player and was among the best players on the pitch as Norway qualified for the World Cup. He also became a father for the first time. Berge's height, which makes him a towering figure on the field, comes via his family: his mother, father and older brother have all played basketball at an international level.

players.name: Erling Braut Haaland
players.bio: It feels almost surreal to think that one of the world’s best footballers grew up in Bryne, a small and unremarkable town in the south-west of Norway. Could it be because people from there work extra hard to get ahead? Is it all the hours spent in the indoor hall that was built because it's difficult to play football outdoors all year round? Maybe it's the fresh milk from the local cows. Perhaps it is because Haaland was so obsessed with football he watched every game he could on TV and even studied Jamie Vardy's ability to stay onside? Or is it because his mother was a prominent heptathlete and his father, Alfie, played in the Premier League? Probably it is a mix of everything. What’s for certain is Haaland’s astonishing goal return – becoming his nation’s all-time top scorer before he turned 25, three Premier League Golden Boots in four seasons at Manchester City, 57 Champions League goals in 58 games – marks him out as a phenomenon and the greatest centre-forward of his era.

players.name: Martin Ødegaard
players.bio: When he was six years old, Ødegaard kicked a football at 60km/h. He played his first senior match at the age of 13. Two years later, he had dominated Norway's top division to such an extent he became the world's most sought-after wonderkid. When he made his debut for the national team at the age of 15, Ødegaard’s technique, ability to read the game and dictate the tempo made him look like a seasoned pro. "I needed five minutes to see that he has what it takes," said the then Bayern Munich manager Pep Guardiola. Having joined Real Madrid in 2015, he made his debut as a substitute, replacing Cristiano Ronaldo. His rise to the top was not linear though and there were setbacks with the national team and loan spells at Heerenveen, Vitesse and Real Sociedad before a transformative move to Arsenal. Now, though, he is the captain for club and country and will lead Norway in their first World Cup since the year he was born. It is safe to say that the wonderkid has become a man.

players.name: Jørgen Strand Larsen
players.bio: In 2017 a tall boy from Halden packed his bags and left for Milan. The 17-year-old Strand Larsen was invited to play for Milan’s Primavera team on loan. Their coach at the time: Gennaro Gattuso. The striker made a good impression but Milan decided against making the transfer permanent. Fast forward eight years and at San Siro, of all places, the Italian home crowd is leaving the stadium as Strand Larsen, now a Premier League star, bursts through on goal, dribbles past Gianluca Mancini with ease and shoots past Gianluigi Donnarumma. The goal to make it 4-1 is as iconic for Norway as it is embarrassing for Italy and their coach is … Gennaro Gattuso. Strand Larsen would be the first choice for many countries but not Norway – and he is happy with his back-up role. “Erling [Haaland] is one of my best friends and he is ridiculously good,” says Norway’s supersub.

players.name: Sander Tangvik
players.bio: Tangvik has been seen as Norway’s future first-choice goalkeeper for several years. Still only 23, he made 85 appearances for Rosenborg before joining Hamburg in January. He has tremendous reflexes and is a very good shot-stopper, which goes some way to explain his excellent record with penalties: at the time of writing he has saved every third spot-kick he has faced. As befits a modern goalkeeper he is also very good with his feet, having grown up with Pepe Reina as his role model. Tangvik is unlikely to get any playing time at the World Cup but being part of the squad will be a valuable experience. In 2025, he got a knock on his nose and bled profusely but the Rosenborg team doctor stitched the wound together and he carried on. “This is the Eliteserien, there should be some knocks during a game, especially against Bryne,” he said.

players.name: Egil Selvik
players.bio: Selvik’s story is a bit unusual. By the time he signed for FK Haugesund in 2021, he was 23 years old and had played only one top division match in Norway. But very quickly he established himself as one of the very best goalkeepers in the country. Now 28, he is living the professional dream in England playing for Watford, where he is the club’s No 1. At international level he has been back-up to Ørjan Håskjold Nyland for several years but because of Nyland’s limited playing time at Sevilla, there have been suggestions Selvik may start at the World Cup. Spent part of his childhood in Aberdeen as his father worked in Scotland in the oil industry, learning English while attending primary school. When he joined Watford he admitted it had taken some time to get used to his new car. “I find it very different. If I go on autopilot, I usually enter on the left side – and then I remember the steering wheel is on the right side! I’m sure I will get used to it,” he told the Watford website.

players.name: Fredrik Aursnes
players.bio: Portugal is the largest market for Norwegian clipfish (dried and salted cod) with an approximate annual export of €350m. The main ingredient in Cristiano Ronaldo’s favourite dish, bacalhau, comes from the Sunnmøre region. This is also where Norway’s second-most famous export to Portugal, Fredrik Aursnes, grew up. He has been influential for Benfica since joining in 2022 but, two years later, he suddenly quit the national team. “It got too hectic,” he said. “It was a rush the whole time, and it felt like I couldn’t breathe. I was mentally tired.” So it was somewhat controversial when he reported back for duty four months before a World Cup he had played no part in reaching. But most Norwegians are happy Aursnes is back. He doesn’t have an X factor but is functional and versatile – a proper all-round midfielder who always makes his team a little bit better.

players.name: Fredrik André Bjørkan
players.bio: Bjørkan knows what the word pressure means. The attacking left-back grew up in Bodø, a place that has now been put on the map by Bodø/Glimt. About 50,000 people live there and Bjørkan grew up as the son of one of the most famous among them. His father Aasmund Bjørkan is a legend at Bodø/Glimt and, when Fredrik was good enough and old enough to become a part of the first team, his father was the club’s coach. In an interview with the newspaper VG, Aasmund said that when his son had a poor game he could hear the murmurs about Fredrik only playing because his father was the coach. “I found it a very difficult situation,” Aasmund said. “Especially in 2016. There were several times that I didn’t pick him, because I couldn’t stand all the talking. But he was definitely good enough even then.” Arrives at the World Cup having been part of Bodø/Glimt’s incredible Champions League journey in 2025-26.

players.name: Marcus Holmgren Pedersen
players.bio: Pedersen has followed in the footsteps of former national team and Premier League players Morten Gamst Pedersen and Stefan Johansen, in that they are all role models for children growing up in Norway’s northernmost region. It is not unusual for Marcus Holmgren Pedersen’s hometown, Hammerfest, to be snow-covered for more than half the year. Hammerfest is located at a latitude of 70.6634° and claims to be the world’s northernmost city. The summers are short, and the winters very long. From midnight sun in the summer to freezing, snowy, windy and pitch-black winters. It is the Norwegian climate at its best and worst. Pedersen is shaped by the conditions he grew up in. The right-back is tough on the pitch and leaves everything out there. His journey has taken him from Tromsø in the north to Molde then Feyenoord and a loan at Torino, before a permanent switch to the Serie A club in 2025.

players.name: Torbjørn Heggem
players.bio: “I used to tell myself that it takes 10,000 hours to become good at something,” Heggem once told TV2 and he lived by this idea growing up. However, if you train for “only” one hour a day it takes more than 27 years to reach that – and you are unlikely to have started from day one. Heggem didn’t have 27 years to get to what he wanted so he trained as much as he could and in as many disciplines as possible, all with the aim of becoming a professional footballer. He did cross-country skiing, long jump and javelin – but always had a football nearby. His father, Trond, has said that he often thought: “If Torbjørn doesn’t become an athlete, then I won’t understand anything.” The result of this targeted training is a 1.92m centre-back who rarely loses a foot race on the pitch and is almost unbeatable in the air.

players.name: Kristian Thorstvedt
players.bio: Thorstvedt is a household name in Norway. Kristian’s father, Erik, won the FA Cup with Tottenham and played at the 1994 World Cup. Kristian may not be the most precocious talent of his generation but he is a fighter with a nose for goal. Thorstvedt developed late physically so, when his best friends signed professional contracts, he decided to study and play college football in the US. It was only when his father made one last call to his old club, Viking, to see if Kristian could train with them that his career took off. Two years later he was promoted to the Eliteserien, reached double figures in goals, won the Norwegian Cup, was sold to Genk and made his Norway debut. “I never thought that Kristian would play for the national team. Never!” Erik has said. This summer the dynamic and intelligent midfielder goes to the US again – but this time to represent his country rather than as a student.

players.name: Thelo Aasgaard
players.bio: Aasgaard arrived as a surprise to most Norwegians during the World Cup qualifiers. He was born and raised in Liverpool and has never lived in Norway. He understands some Norwegian but prefers to converse in English. At the age of nine he joined the Liverpool academy, where he played with Curtis Jones among others. Through his father, he was eligible for Norway. Aasgaard comes from a musical family. His father is a world-renowned cellist. His mother also plays the cello, and his two siblings are jazz musicians. Thelo, meanwhile, makes music in his spare time to switch off from football. “I look up to my parents a lot. We have related jobs, because both are judged on performance. When I’m at a concert with my dad, I know the pressure he’s under in front of a large audience.”

players.name: Antonio Nusa
players.bio: Among all the stars in the Norwegian national team, no one is more appreciated by the fans at Ullevaal Stadium than Nusa. Every time he gets the ball, the crowd's anticipation can be heard – and he almost always responds by dribbling past his opponent. The highlight so far was his dream goal in the 3-0 home victory over Italy, which he followed up with another beauty into the top corner in the return match at San Siro. Two years ago, when asked how he became so good, he said: “I watched clips of Neymar on YouTube and went out to try to copy him.” The unusual thing with Nusa is that, so far, he has been better for the national team than for his club sides. He has been with RB Leipzig since 2024 but as more people are likely to make note of Norway's fan favourite at the World Cup, a move to one of Europe’s biggest clubs could be on the cards.

players.name: Andreas Schjelderup
players.bio: At the age of 13, Schjelderup already had a plan mapped out for how the next few years of his life would look. He was going to leave Norway as soon as he turned 16 and the message to his parents was blunt: “You are not coming with me.” Schjelderup wanted to do it his own way. “I thought that by joining me, they would have to sacrifice the life they lived in Bodø.” It did not take long before Europe’s top clubs were aware of Schjelderup’s talent. He had trials at Ajax and PSV, and visited big clubs such as Barcelona, Liverpool and Bayern Munich. In the end he left Bodø/Glimt for the Danish talent factory FC Nordsjælland at the age of … you’ve got it: 16. All his sacrifice, determination and hard work paid off. Since joining Benfica in 2023 he has become a star and scored twice against Real Madrid in the remarkable 4-2 win that took Benfica to the Champions League playoff round. The World Cup could be this creative and technical winger’s international breakthrough.

players.name: Oscar Bobb
players.bio: Most footballers become known to the public when they are approaching their 20s. Some have to endure attention in their early teens. In Norway, Bobb hit the headlines when he was 12. When the Norway Cup – football’s biggest youth tournament – was broadcast on TV, it was impossible not to notice the quick, left-footed midfielder from Lyn, Oslo. "He is like a little Messi," the commentators said. When Bobb returned to TV screens, it was for Pep Guardiola's Manchester City. But after winning the league in his first season, he fractured his leg before the 2024-25 campaign. Bobb began the 2025-26 season in the starting XI yet soon found himself out of favour and he was sold to Fulham in January for £27m. He has had hard times for the national team too, Ståle Solbakken laying into him after a friendly against New Zealand. “[Bobb] lacked everything today; spent a long time with the ball, positioned himself strangely and didn’t hit the pressing game. If you ask Oscar, I think he’ll say that the first half was his weakest performance for the national team.” Off the pitch, Bobb is a polite and modest person who, with thousands of hours on the PlayStation, will be the man to beat in the FC 26 matches at the players' hotel in North Carolina.

players.name: Jens Petter Hauge
players.bio: “He is their best player,” the Atlético Madrid manager, Diego Simeone, said of Hauge before his side took on Bodø/Glimt in January. And Simeone is often right. Hauge had a starring role in Glimt’s Champions League run, scoring six times, including goals against Manchester City and in both legs against Inter in the playoff round. Hauge has bounced back from a tough spell at Milan, whom he joined in 2020. It did not work out for him at Eintracht Frankfurt either but he has been playing the best football of his career since returning to Glimt last year. While at Milan he was impressed by the 39-year-old Zlatan Ibrahimovic – on and off the pitch. He told CBS: "I remember I came to the dressing room with a new cap on and Zlatan said: 'Are you making pizzas or are you a footballer?' I like when people are joking.”

players.name: Sondre Langås
players.bio: Three years ago Langås was playing in the second tier in Norway for Ranheim. He was 22 years old and had not represented any of the Norwegian youth teams. It is fair to say that he was relatively unknown. However in the summer of 2023, the top-flight club Viking signed him and 18 months later he made his Norway debut and moved to Derby for £4.5m. Langås was captaining the Championship side when he suffered a hamstring injury in January. In an interview with the TV channel Viaplay, Norway’s head coach, Ståle Solbakken, said: “He has to get on the pitch and have a very good month in April if he is to get on that plane to the World Cup.” In the end he managed just that. A very talented handball player, he combined it with football until the age of 18.

players.name: Henrik Falchener
players.bio: Falchener admitted he shed a few tears after his shock inclusion by Ståle Solbakken. “I never thought it would happen so quickly. I really have no words,” he told TV2. The 23-year-old only had one cap to his name and it came in March, after Norway had qualified for the World Cup. The road to the top has been long for Falchener, although it has gone very quickly in recent years. After being rejected from an academy at the age of 16, he went on to Sandefjord but struggled for playing time, which made him drop down to the third tier. He eventually landed a move to Viking and immediately became one of the best defenders in the top flight, perhaps the very best. The centre-back has contributed up the other end too, scoring six times as Viking won the 2025 Eliteserien in his first season at the club.

players.name: Julian Ryerson
players.bio: Ryerson is the closest thing to a loose cannon in the Norway squad. “I would rather not comment on his brain ... the things that go on in there are not normal,” the Norway coach, Ståle Solbakken, once said with a smile, before adding: “but that is good.” He was referring to the special qualities that have taken Ryerson to the very top: fearlessness, focus and the love for an old-fashioned tackle. Having left Viking for Union Berlin in 2018 he stayed in Germany’s capital for five years before joining Borussia Dortmund, where one of his finest performances came against PSG in the 2023-24 Champions League semi-finals. Dortmund’s defence kept Kylian Mbappé, Bradley Barcola and Ousmane Dembélé quiet, not conceding in either leg. “We had no special plan to stop Mbappé,” Ryerson said afterwards. “They also had Dembélé and the other guy, I don’t know his name [Barcola]. We had to be best one v one, and we were.” That mindset will come in handy when Ryerson once again faces the French superstars at the World Cup.

players.name: Luis Mejía
players.bio: Given the nickname ‘Manotas’ (‘mitts’) because of his big hands, Mejía made his Primera División for Tauro aged 16 before moving to sign for Fénix in Uruguay. After a career path that has also gone via Chile and, very briefly, France, the goalkeeper returned to Uruguay with Nacional in 2024, where he has won plenty of silverware. Something of a veteran in the international setup having earned his first cap in 2009, the 31-year-old has rarely been first choice at a major tournament and missed out on the squad for the 2018 World Cup all together. He is the back-up goalkeeper behind Orlando Mosquera in North America this summer. “We have to demonstrate that hierarchy and that experience,” he has said of the senior figures in the Panama squad.

players.name: César Blackman
players.bio: At the age of 19, Blackman left Panama behind to move to Slovakia. He was already a league champion with El Chorrillo having made his debut aged 17 before securing a transfer to Dunajska Streda, following in the footsteps of his compatriot Eric Davis. He had to adapt to a new country, language and way of life in Europe. His attacking qualities and pace when defending one-on-one attracted the attention of Slovan Bratislava in 2023. He is now an established starter at Slovakia’s biggest club, having won two league titles, and has become the first Panamanian in history to play in the Champions League. He celebrates each goal by pointing to a heart tattoo on his arm – a tribute to his parents, who died when he was less than five years old. “How I wished my mum and dad were here,” he said, wearing a t-shirt with a picture of them on, after Panama secured qualification for this World Cup.

players.name: José Córdoba
players.bio: Córdoba’s European adventure began at the age of 19 with a move to Celta Vigo, where he played for the reserves. The defender made his name with Etar in Bulgaria and earned a move to Levski Sofia, winning the 2024 Bulgarian Cup. Norwich came calling in 2024 and the left-footed centre-back has become a mainstay with the Championship side. “Today is a day to be happy. This is a childhood dream and I’m excited for this next step in my career,” he said upon his arrival in England. Córdoba is elegant with the ball at his feet, a threat in the air and cuts out opposition attacks at source. The ‘Prince of Panama’, as he’s known at Carrow Road, already has more than 30 caps for the selección.

players.name: Fidel Escobar
players.bio: Escobar was still having kickabouts on the street in his home neighbourhood of El Chorrillo in Panama City when he made his first-team debut for San Francisco aged 17. “El Comendante” is now an imposing figure at the back and a leader. After breaking through at New York Red Bulls alongside compatriot Amir Murillo, he had spells in Spain’s lower leagues before a move to Saprissa in 2022. The defender has become an idol in Costa Rica, winning four league titles and scoring in the Torneo de Copa final victory in April this year. When he won his first trophy with the club, he looked to the sky and dedicated it to his late mother. “This is what she wanted,” he said. After overcoming a hernia problem, he will play at his second World Cup after 2018.

players.name: Edgardo Fariña
players.bio: Fariña stands 1.93m tall and has been called “the gigantic Panamanian”, making him a useful player to have in both boxes. He came through the academies of Río Abajo and Plaza Amador before making his senior debut with Atlético Nacional in Panama’s second tier. Impressive spells at Independiente and Universitario led to a move to Municipal in Guatemala, where his defensive solidity helped them win the Clausura title in 2024. “You feel like Municipal is a very united family,” he said on his arrival. Dominant in the air and a good reader of the play, the 24-year-old landed a move to Russia with Khimki in 2024 before joining Nizhny Novgorod a year later. The centre-back was part of the Panama team that won the Maurice Revello Under-23 trophy in 2023.

players.name: Cristian Martínez
players.bio: A combative tackler and effective attacker, Martínez is your typical box-to-box midfielder. He is from El Chorrillo in Panama City, making his debut for the local side as a teenager. His dynamism and stamina earned him a move to Columbus Crew in MLS – where he became the club’s youngest scorer – before joining Cádiz in Spain and having other spells in Venezuela, Saudi Arabia and back in Panama. The 2023 Gold Cup runner-up signed for Kiryat Shmona in Israel last year and was a key part of the successful World Cup qualification campaign. “My childhood dream was to wear the national team’s shirt and put my soul into each game,” he says. Nicknamed “El Fulo” for his tendency to dye his hair bleach blonde.

players.name: José Luis Rodríguez
players.bio: Rodríguez lost his father at the age of 10 but the youngster from Panama City found the strength to dream big through football and his family – his brother Marcos is a former captain of El Chorrillo. A winger with lightning speed, “El Puma” has made a name for himself in Belgium, Spain, Portugal and Serbia. He isn’t afraid to try his luck from distance either, scoring via a deflection off Tunisia’s Yassine Meriah in the group stage of the 2018 World Cup. The 27-year-old, now playing in Mexico, has found a mentor in the Panama head coach, Thomas Christensen. “I’m very grateful to ‘El Profe’ … we know each other very well and we’re like a family,” Rodríguez has said. His mother still celebrates every goal with tears of pride in her eyes.

players.name: Adalberto Carrasquilla
players.bio: “Coco” was running his own business aged 16 when he made his first-team debut for Tauro, cutting his teammates’ hair for a few dollars in order to help out his family. This graft and humility have taken him far. The 27-year-old is the driving force of Panama’s midfield with his tireless running, good feet and great vision. He works hard to recover possession but is also known for taking up dangerous positions in the final third. After winning two league titles with Tauro, he was a US Open Cup winner with Houston Dynamo in 2023 before signing for Pumas in Mexico for a club-record fee in January 2025. At the 2023 Gold Cup, when Panama were runners-up to Mexico, Carrasquilla won the Golden Ball for best player at the tournament. Then he was key to World Cup qualification. “It was our dream,” he said. Whenever he returns to Panama, he still gets his scissors out to cut his friends’ hair.

players.name: Tomás Rodríguez
players.bio: “It’s a big club and it wins things,” said Rodríguez upon his arrival at Saprissa in Costa Rica last January. He had earned the big move after his performances for Monagas in Venezuela, where he became the club’s top foreign goalscorer in just two years. But Rodríguez struggled to adapt to the pressure of Saprissa. “I was very miserable, very depressed,” he said. The striker wears his heart on his sleeve and is often emotional after he finds the net. “TomyGol” has now started to find his feet in Costa Rica and Panama will hope he can reproduce the form he showed at last year’s Gold Cup, scoring in each of the group games at his first major tournament.

players.name: Ismael Díaz
players.bio: Díaz made his first-team debut for Tauro aged 15 and attempted to launch his career in Europe at Porto. Injury problems hampered his progression in the B team but he came back stronger, emerging as a goalscoring machine with Universidad Católica in Ecuador. After 55 goals in three seasons made him a star of the Liga Pro, the winger landed a move to Club León in Mexico last year. The 29-year-old has explosive speed and is a lethal finisher, having scored a hat-trick for Panama during their run to the 2023 Gold Cup final. Nicknamed “El Príncipe” (the prince) since childhood, Díaz is one of Panama’s greatest hopes of upsetting the applecart at this World Cup. Asked by Fifa.com why Panama play with so much passion, Díaz replied: “A lot of us come from the streets, we come from humble backgrounds, where maybe we had enough to be able to play in football schools, which I remember really well.“

players.name: Édgar Yoel Bárcenas
players.bio: “El Mago” earned his nickname owing to his outstanding technical ability with the ball at his feet. The mazy dribbler made his senior debut for Árabe Unido, his boyhood club, and won multiple league titles before an initial move to Europe with Croatia’s RNK Split. Having won promotion to Mexico’s top flight with Cafetaleros in 2018, Bárcenas got a second shot at Europe with Real Oviedo. He shone in Spain, also playing for Girona and Leganés over a number of years in the second tier. The 32-year-old has become one of Panamanian football’s leading lights – three of his 100+ caps came at the 2018 World Cup and he was named in the team of the tournament at the 2023 Gold Cup. “I want to be more important for Panama and make history at the 2026 World Cup,” he says.

players.name: César Samudio
players.bio: The 32-year-old has been canonised by supporters of Marathón in Honduras – they call him ‘Saint Samudio’ for his heroics in goal. He made his senior league debut in Panama for Chepo aged 20 and went on to play for a series of modest clubs such as Atlético Veragüense, Costa del Este and Plaza Amador before winning the title with Independiente in 2022, when he was named player of the season and kept the most clean sheets. He is now an idol in Honduras, where he has set records for clean sheets, and says he has “fallen in love with these colours” at Marathón. He is a back-up option for the coach, Thomas Christensen, this summer.

players.name: Jiovany Ramos
players.bio: “I will play every match as if it were a final,” Ramos said in 2022, when he signed for Deportivo Táchira – then the third Venezuelan club of his career. The fiercely competitive centre-back lifted the Liga Futve the following year and earned a move to Alianza Lima, becoming one of only a few Panamanians to play for the Peruvian giants. A return to Venezuela followed and the defender now turns out for Academia Puerto Cabello. Ramos won the Panamanian Clausura with Independiente in 2020 and has built a reputation as a born leader while representing the national team at the 2023 Gold Cup and in the Concacaf Nations League.

players.name: Carlos Harvey
players.bio: Harvey’s American dream began in Los Angeles, where he secured a loan move from Panama’s Tauro to LA Galaxy’s second team. His performances for Galaxy II were enough to earn a permanent move and a first-team contract but it was at Phoenix Rising where he made his breakthrough, winning the USL Championship in 2023. A defensive midfielder, who can also play at centre-back, the 26-year-old now calls James Rodríguez his teammate at Minnesota and has renewed his contract with the MLS club until 2028. “I’ve grown a lot as a player and a person,” he said upon signing his new deal. Harvey will be hoping his mix of tenacity and tactical intelligence can impress at his first World Cup.

players.name: Eric Davis
players.bio: The veteran defender was given a guard of honour by his teammates at Dunajska Streda at his final game for the Slovakian club in 2023. They were his first port of call in Europe and he stayed for eight years, racking up 200 games, 20 goals and 32 assists. Davis had a big reputation even before leaving Panama, winning two league titles with Árabe Unido after making his debut aged 18. An accomplished crosser of the ball, the left-back has now returned to Panama after spells with MLS’s DC United, Slovakia’s Kosice and Brazil’s Vila Nova. “Returning to my country is something very special,” he said after signing for Plaza Amador. The 35-year-old has more than 100 caps for the national team and travels to his second World Cup.

players.name: Andrés Andrade
players.bio: Andrade became the first player from Panama to feature in the German Bundesliga when at Arminia Bielefeld but it is in the Austrian Bundesliga where he has established himself in European football. He arrived at LASK as a 19-year-old in 2018 and, after that spell in Germany, returned in 2023 as a regular starter. The defender’s ability to establish himself in Austria is even more impressive when you consider he had a difficult time as a teenager with Querétaro and Toluca in Mexico. His perseverance has yielded rewards and this will be his first World Cup. The 2023 Gold Cup runner-up says “it’s always a proud moment to be able to represent Panama”.

players.name: José Fajardo
players.bio: Fajardo knows what it’s like to star in North America. During the 2024 Copa América he netted vital goals against hosts United States and Bolivia that earned Panama a place in the knockout stages. The former DC United striker also scored crucial goals in Panama’s World Cup qualification campaign, adding to his national hero status. But Fajardo did not play a game of senior football until the age of 24, working jobs on the Panama Canal to support his young family instead. “Life is not easy, you have to suffer. I’m motivated to keep going with my football and be an example to children and the people from my hometown,” he said. He soon proved his worth for Independiente de La Chorrera, his goals helping to propel the team to two league titles. After spells in Peru, MLS and Saudi Arabia, the 32-year-old is now playing in Ecuador.

players.name: Cecilio Waterman
players.bio: When Waterman scored a stoppage-time winner to send Panama into the 2025 Nations League final at the United States’ expense, he jumped on top of the pitchside hoardings and spotted Thierry Henry doing TV punditry. “You’re my idol!” Waterman shouted at him before the whole Panama squad jumped on the World Cup winner in celebration. The 35-year-old is nicknamed ‘Aquaman’, for obvious reasons, and his superpower is scoring goals. He has an envious record for a number of clubs in Chile and lifted the league title for the first time with Coquimbo Unido in 2025. He counts Cobresal and Everton among his previous employers, as well as Fénix in Uruguay, and now leads the line for Universidad de Concepción.

players.name: Alberto Quintero
players.bio: Quintero made his professional debut two decades ago with El Chorrillo in Panama City. He comes from the humble neighbourhood of the same name and pursued a career in football as a way of providing for his family. “My main strength is perseverance, 100%,” he has said. Gifted and skilful but also tireless and versatile, Quintero became a modern winger with his ability to go past players and set up opponents. His 20-year career has taken him to Spain, Colombia, Mexico and the United States, while he is an iconic figure at Universitario de Deportes in Peru. He returned to Panama two years ago to finish his career with Plaza Amador. He played a key role in Panama’s historic qualification for the 2018 World Cup but broke his foot in a warm-up match before heading to Russia. After over 140 caps for his country – and just a couple since 2023 – the 38-year-old can make his World Cup bow.

players.name: Aníbal Godoy
players.bio: A selección stalwart for 16 years and now the captain, Godoy is the leading figure of this Panama squad. He has over 150 caps, three of which came at the 2018 World Cup, and has played in two Gold Cup finals. “Each time I play for Panama, it’s like touching the sky,” he has said. Two decades ago Godoy made his league debut as a teenager for Chepo and immediately became a regular in midfield. His big break with Godoy Cruz in Argentina was followed by a spell in Hungary before his arrival in MLS. After a few years apiece at San Jose Earthquakes and Nashville, he is one of the leaders at San Diego, one of the league’s newest franchises. The left-footed veteran is now a naturalised citizen in the United States.

players.name: César Yanis
players.bio: One of many to come out of Panama City’s El Chorrillo neighbourhood, Yanis spent his childhood playing football with friends. “We played until it went dark and we dreamed big,” he says. Nicknamed ‘Yanisinho’ for his dribbling skills akin to a Brazilian, the winger took his first steps with Potros del Este, scoring decisive goals in promotion campaigns and winning league and cup titles. After adventures in Spain and Costa Rica, he has found a home in Chile with Cobresal. With Panama he is a 2023 Gold Cup runner-up and took just 33 seconds after coming off the bench to score the third goal in a 3-1 win over Bolivia that secured Los Canaleros’ passage into the knockouts. “We were born to make history,” he said then. A family man, Yanis once flew home from Chile to surprise his mum on Mother’s Day.

players.name: Orlando Mosquera
players.bio: Mosquera is up against some of football’s greatest goalscorers each week in the Saudi Pro League but he himself actually knows what it’s like to find the net. While a young goalkeeper at Tauro in 2015, he scored a last-minute equaliser as his team chased the league title but then got injured. “It’s not how you start, it’s how you finish,” he told his coach. “I’ll recover and we’ll be champions.” He left Tauro as a three-time Primera División winner and spent time in Turkey, Bolivia, Venezuela and Israel before arriving at Al-Fayha, where he plays behind Chris Smalling. “Kuty” is a commanding presence between the posts and takes charge of his six-yard box. He was Panama’s No 1 for the 2023 Gold Cup run and is appearing at his first World Cup.

players.name: Michael Amir Murillo
players.bio: Murillo made his senior league debut in Panama for San Francisco in 2014 after standing out in a youth trial that included more than 130 players. He grew up in Colón, sharing a room with his mother and siblings but, while baseball was the family’s favourite sport, he chose football and wanted to provide. “My main strength is my mentality, 100%,” he has said. His pace, crossing ability and aerial prowess make him a modern full-back. Murillo’s big break came in MLS with New York Red Bulls, where he was the first player from Panama to feature in an All-Star game and won the 2018 Supporters’ Shield. After spells with Anderlecht and Marseille, he signed for Besiktas in February. A key part of the Panama side that qualified for the 2018 World Cup and performed well at the 2023 Gold Cup, the defender is a national treasure and has never forgotten his roots. “Be proud of your family, fight for your family and believe in your family,” he always says.

players.name: Azarías Londoño
players.bio: Londoño enjoyed a successful first season with Universidad Católica in 2025, on loan from Alianza Lima, scoring seven goals and providing 10 assists in the Liga Pro and lifting the Copa Ecuador. “Winning this trophy is valuable for the club, it was the goal that we were pushing for,” he said. The 24-year-old striker is good with his head, tending to win most of his aerial duels, but is also capable of holding his nerve one-on-one with the goalkeeper. As a 19-year-old, he scored twice on his first-team debut for Árabe Unido and went on to win the apertura title in 2022. After 14 goals in his first season at Comunicaciones in Guatemala, he failed to find form in Portugal’s second tier with Torreense. After two Gold Cup inclusions, this will be his first World Cup.

players.name: Roderick Miller
players.bio: Miller will not be overawed by the travel schedule of this summer’s World Cup. He has played in seven different countries throughout his career and has found a home in Azerbaijan. The left-footed centre-back has become a fan favourite at Turan Tovuz with his performances in their backline. Since joining in 2023 he has impressed with his aerial prowess and his composure on the ball. The 34-year-old has had spells in Mexico, Portugal, Kazakhstan, Peru and Iraq but still has his family at heart. “I’m lucky to have a princess by my side,” he said of his daughter Anahís, his biggest motivation. “I was a young dad and, while it’s not easy, it has been a marvellous experience.” A Panama international since 2011, Miller has over 40 caps to his name.

players.name: Jorge Gutiérrez
players.bio: In Tocumen, a stone’s throw from Panama City’s airport, a boy called Jorge used to kick a ball around in an academy that did not yet exist. Club Atlético Tocumen was set up in the years that followed and “Guti” had trials with Tauro, in his words “the biggest and most successful club in Panama”, and made his Primera División debut aged 16 and won four league titles before the door to Europe opened. He was loaned to Melilla, a team in the Spanish enclave in Morocco that played in the third tier. It didn’t work out and he moved to Venezuela. Inspired by Marcelo and Dani Alves, Gutiérrez is a Brazilian full-back at heart – great at going past players and a threat in the opposition third. He is one of the best full-backs in Venezuela and competes with the veteran Eric Davis for a starting place for Panama this summer.

players.name: Roberto Junior Fernández
players.bio: Junior by (middle) name, Paraguay’s oldest player by nature, Fernández is widely known as Gatito, the Kitten. His father, also Roberto Fernández, won 78 caps in goal for Paraguay and became El Gato. Both players also made more than 100 appearances for Cerro Porteño, the Asunción-based club where Fernández is currently in his second spell. He has fought his way back into the national team after a long-term knee injury almost ended his career: “I used to go to Botafogo matches to watch my teammates,” he recalled. “They would go onto the pitch and I would go to the bathroom to cry. I couldn’t cope with everything I was going through. I couldn’t see a light at the end of the tunnel.” Soon after he finally came back from that injury, he broke his shoulder. He returned to the Paraguay side in 2024 after a four-year absence and was a key part of the side that won qualification, though Orlando Gill has been preferred in recent friendlies.

players.name: Gustavo Velázquez
players.bio: Velázquez’s emergence has been the most remarkable story in the recent history of the Paraguay team. Excluding a single game he spent on the bench in 2017, he was called up for the first time at 33 years of age, when most uncapped players have already given up on the idea of international honours. But he got a chance before the 2024 Copa América and impressed everyone from the first moment with his defensive qualities: aggression, speed, great vision, and a strong aerial game, despite his 1.79m height, and he has not been left out of a squad since. Velázquez is respected by his teammates and considered one of the leaders of the group, even if he is most often used as a substitute and he has admitted that “my performances have dropped a bit” during a difficult and injury-affected season for Cerro Porteño.

players.name: Omar Alderete
players.bio: With a prodigious left foot and a strong aerial game, coupled with a strong character and composure under pressure, Alderete has become an undisputed starter for Paraguay, playing every minute of every qualifier except the one he missed through injury – and he scored the winner in the 2-1 victory over Argentina. As a child he dreamed of being a forward but he is not known for his prowess in front of goal - Alderete’s great strength is in the art of defending. After coming through the soccer school run by Pedro Fleitas, who won the Copa América with Paraguay in 1975 and Alderete describes as “a great friend” who “helped me a lot”, he began his journey with Cerro Porteño in Paraguay. He has gone on to play in Argentina, Switzerland, Germany and Spain, and moved to Sunderland in 2025.

players.name: Juan José Cáceres
players.bio: Another Argentina-born Paraguay international, Cáceres started in youth football as a goalkeeper, moved into midfield, progressed through the Boca Juniors youth system as a striker, and then changed positions again after they released him and he arranged a trial at Racing. “I talked to my friends and family and decided that I would try my luck as a defender – they had so many strikers,” he said. Racing liked what they saw and took him on; he made his debut at centre-back before shifting one last time, to right-back. His parents are both Paraguayan, and he was raised in the poor Buenos Aires district of Dock Sud – “It was a humble little house, made of corrugated iron,” he said. “When it rained the pipes would get clogged and water would start pouring in” – but he had spent his entire life in Argentina until he moved to play in Russia in 2025. Still, there was never any doubt about which nation he would play for. “I want to represent Paraguay because it would bring so much joy to my parents,” he said.

players.name: Fabián Balbuena
players.bio: “The General” is one of the oldest and most respected players of the Paraguayan national team, even though he has started more games on the bench than on the pitch. He is composed and eloquent when speaking to the press, but when he pulls on his kit he can be fiery. Balbuena offers leadership, composure, aggression, and a strong aerial game to the Albirroja’s defense. The former West Ham player’s love for the national team is not in doubt – to the extent that he chose to represent his country at the Paris Olympics even though it meant he would forego his salary for the duration.  “Every time I go away on international duty it feels like the first time,” he said in 2020. “Our intention is to qualify for the World Cup, but we have to be realistic. I don’t lose my mind if I don’t reach those things, but if I reach my goals, then that’s good. My philosophy is that if you do your job well, your prize will come.”

players.name: Junior Alonso
players.bio: A left-footed defender known for his total commitment, Alonso is also very versatile, a centre-back who has become the starting left-back for Paraguay. Decisive in big matches, not only with his defending, but also in attack. Alonso is synonymous with resilience, and there is a phrase that perfectly sums up his career: “I have lost more finals than I have won, I’ll cry in silence and tomorrow I’ll be back to work”, he said after losing a Copa Libertadores final. This has been a virtue: hard working, bringing precisely the same commitment, win or lose. Junior Alonso developed at Cerro Porteño, made the leap to Europe, playing for Lille, the Russian side Krasnodar and Celta Vigo, and today he is one of the best defenders in Brazilian football.

players.name: Ramón Sosa
players.bio: Until 2019 Sosa had played only in lower tier football, but his talent flourished after the pandemic, much of which he spent locked down at the training ground of Paraguayan club River Plate. “It was a terrible time,” he said. “When the pandemic hit most people left, and I ended up staying there with a teammate. Nobody could come in and nobody could go out, it was just us two, training by ourselves. A big training ground with just two of us there.” The practice did him good, though, and by 2022 he was playing for Olimpia, Paraguay’s biggest club, and for the national team, winning over fans with his speed and dribbling ability, and by speaking in the country’s indigenous language, Guaraní. He dreamed of playing in the Premier League and in 2024 he signed for Nottingham Forest, where a troubled spell lasted just one season and featured one league start. He has since become a star at Palmeiras in Brazil. Last year Sosa was named by his international teammate Julio Enciso as the worst-dressed member of Paraguay’s squad.

players.name: Diego Gómez
players.bio: “I’m a warrior,” Gómez says. “I never give up on the ball. I always give everything.” He demonstrated his commitment to the game at a young age: just to watch his country in their last World Cup appearance, in 2010 when he was seven, he had to repeatedly climb onto the roof of his family’s home, in a remote area of San Juan Misiones where the TV signal was poor, to move the antenna. His family don’t live in a remote and ill-equipped house any more: in 2024 he built a mansion for his mother, complete with swimming pool. He was called into the international setup when he had made only seven senior club appearances and is an undisputed starter in Gustavo Alfaro's team. He graduated from Libertad academy, improved at Inter Miami under the wings of Lionel Messi and Sergio Busquets, and now shines in the Premier League with Brighton. Gómez can play as a central midfielder, to the left or the right, as a pivot, as an attacking midfielder and even on either wing.

players.name: Antonio Sanabria
players.bio: A product of La Masia, Barcelona’s world-famous academy, where he arrived at the age of 13. Born in San Lorenzo, 15km from Asunción, “Tonny” moved to Spain with his family when his mother emigrated. He was spotted by Barcelona scouts while playing at a local football school in Catalonia and his life changed forever. At 30, Sanabria will finally make his World Cup debut. He captured the moment perfectly on social media: “Keep showing to that inner child that everything is possible. Dreams do come true. Sacrifice pays off and from San Lorenzo, you can reach the world.” Training and sometimes sharing the pitch with Lionel Messi and Neymar at Barcelona helped him develop the elegance and refined technique that still distinguish him from every other striker in the Paraguay squad. A shining example of that class was his spectacular overhead kick goal against Argentina during the qualifiers – a strike that has already been immortalised as one of the greatest in La Albirroja’s history.

players.name: Miguel Almirón
players.bio: World Cup qualification was, Almirón says, “one of the greatest achievements of my career, what I always dreamed of as a child”. But success at international level did not come easily to Almirón, and it is only since the arrival of Gustavo Alfaro that he has regularly shown the extent of his talents. Until the Argentinian coach arrived, Almirón was the subject of near-constant criticism from the fans, despite his success in the Premier League with Newcastle. “At one point, I thought about retiring from international football, I thought it wasn’t for me, that I had to give the chance to someone else,” Miggy, as he is known, admitted. Almirón stands out for his speed and intensity, and for being a great leader of counter attacks. He doesn’t score many goals, but he offers tactical discipline.

players.name: Maurício
players.bio: Just five months ago, not only was Maurício not in the Paraguay team, he wasn’t even Paraguayan. He became a naturalised citizen in February thanks to a paternal grandparent and was fastracked into the squad, thanks in large part to the strong backing of Gustavo Gómez, his captain for club and now country, who went as far as helping him with his paperwork during the naturalisation process. His intelligence, technique and ability to be part of the build up make him precisely the kind of player Paraguay required. The left-footed midfielder graduated from Cruzeiro academy and currently plays for Palmeiras, where he can operate as a centre midfielder, winger and even second striker.

players.name: Orlando Gill
players.bio: In 2018 Gill was playing amateur football and even last year he was a back-up goalkeeper for San Lorenzo de Almagro. This summer he is going to the World Cup. His career got off to a difficult start but the nearly 2m-tall goalkeeper has now seen most of his dreams come true. “Orlando sold some of his gear to cover costs [so that we could eat],” Gill's wife said with tears in her eyes after he had made his international debut in the historic World Cup win for Paraguay against Peru in Lima. “Our son was fighting for his life, and his father was always there for him.” He was never trusted in domestic football back in Paraguay, hence the move to Argentina in 2024. He then had a stroke of luck. San Lorenzo were trying to sign Keylor Navas but ran into financial difficulties and with no new goalkeeper arriving Gill was given a chance by the manager, Miguel Russo. He impressed – and has been the No 1 ever since.

players.name: José Canale
players.bio: If someone had told Canale a year ago that he would make Paraguay’s World Cup squad, he would have thought they were joking. But persistence and consistency paid off. Gustavo Alfaro had no choice but to call him up. Born in Itauguá, 30km from Asunción, Canale came through the youth ranks of 12 de Octubre before joining Libertad’s academy – although his senior career started with a series of loans. He finally found stability at Lanús. After one final loan spell at Mexico’s Querétaro, he returned to the Argentinian club and became a standout performer: an undisputed starter, defensive leader, 2025 Copa Sudamericana champion and the scorer of one of the club’s greatest goals – a header against Flamengo in the Recopa Sudamericana final at the Maracanã. Reflecting on that moment, he said: “As a child, when I played video games, I used to say: ‘One day I will score at the Maracanã’. And here I am. Unbelievable.” Canale began his career as a central midfielder. Today he is known for his tireless defending, his never-say-die attitude and his distinctive long curly hair.

players.name: Andrés Cubas
players.bio: Cubas is often underestimated – which perhaps comes with being a 1.66m defensive midfielder – but when he is on the pitch he proves his value to everyone. He is Paraguay’s heart and lungs, running, jostling, winning back possession. This will not be his first World Cup - he played the Under-20 version in 2015 for Argentina, the land of his birth - but his true allegiance was always clear. “I was born in Argentina but since my father and all of his family were from Paraguay, I grew up with their customs,” he said. Cubas came through the youth system at Boca Juniors, making his debut at 17, and has since had spells in Italy, France and now Canada, having joined Vancouver in 2022. He missed Paraguay’s two friendlies last November because his passport was in the pocket of his trousers when they went in the wash, and was unusable (if clean) by the time it came out.

players.name: Gustavo Gómez
players.bio: “I would trade all the titles in my career to play in a World Cup with Paraguay,” Gustavo Gómez said after winning his second Copa Libertadores with Palmeiras. Despite lifting South America’s most important trophy as a captain, the Paraguayan defender’s heart could not find complete happiness. Being involved in a World Cup was his dream and his obsession, and after 13 years of trying Gómez finally got his reward. The captain of club and country, Gómez is considered by many to be the best defender in South America – although not by the player himself, who says that “I go and do my best in training and on the pitch, and I left others talk about that” – standing out for his leadership, his aerial prowess, and physical toughness.

players.name: Damián Bobadilla
players.bio: One of two members of the squad whose father played in goal for Paraguay, Damián follows in the footsteps of Aldo, who made three appearances in the 2006 World Cup, including one as a substitute in the 1-0 defeat to England. He was only five when his dad was in Germany and can't even remember it, but there are some blurry memories from South Africa 2010, although his father was on the bench the whole competition. “I always compare myself with him, try to achieve just a fraction of what he achieved in his career,” Damián says. “Whenever I have fallen, he has been there to pick me back up. I am who I am today thanks to him.” As a player and a person he is calm and clever, and he is considered one of the key pieces of the Albirroja jigsaw.

players.name: Alejandro ‘Kaku’ Romero
players.bio: Five years have passed since Kaku moved from New York Red Bulls to Saudi Arabia, initially with Al Taawoun, since when he has practically circumnavigated the globe for each Paraguay game, often for just a few minutes on the pitch and sometimes not even that: of his first 55 international games he watched 22 from the bench and started only nine. When given a chance he demonstrated his class and an exquisite left foot, while never complaining about playing so little, despite all the travel time he endured. His positivity is always highlighted by everyone. He can play as a right offensive midfielder or as a more central playmaker. Although he was born in Argentina, he chose to represent the land of his mother's birth.

players.name: Álex Arce
players.bio: A cruciate ligament injury almost ended Arce’s career when it had only just begun, and when he finally fought his way back he did not seem the same, and he dropped into the amateur leagues. When the pandemic hit the league was suspended and Arce, who as a youth at Cerro Porteno earned the nickname Kanu because of he played like the Nigerian forward, made ends meet by selling the handcrafted hammocks famously made by artisans in his hometown, Carapeguá. But then, an extraordinary turnaround. He was signed by the Asunción club Ameliano, and from there by Independiente Rivadavia in the Argentinian second division, where he scored 28 goals in 37 matches as they secured promotion. That earned him another move to another country, with Ecuador’s Liga de Quito, but after a single season he returned to Rivadavia last summer, where he has continued to demonstrate his goalscoring instinct, aerial ability and link-up skills.

players.name: Julio Enciso
players.bio: “Dear grandpa, I dedicate this to you in heaven with all my heart. I always remember when you pointed at me and told me that one day I would be there,” Enciso wrote emotionally, as Paraguay sealed their return to the World Cup. Enciso made the promise to become a professional player to save his family from poverty. He pledged to play for his country – he was only 17 when that was ticked off – and promised that his grandfather would see him playing in a World Cup. That second promise was not to be, as his grandfather passed away before Julio had played for his country. He made his debut at Libertad in the Paraguayan first division aged only 15 and moved to Europe at 18, signing for Brighton. He joined Strasbourg last summer, and is now established as Paraguay’s top attacker, offering flair, dribbling, assists and goals.

players.name: Braian Ojeda
players.bio: “We’re not going just to make up the numbers,” Ojeda said in March. “We’re going to fight for the title.” He too is not just padding out Paraguay’s squad: five years after his first call-up he has become integral to the squad only since their qualification – his only appearance of the qualifying campaign came as a substitute in their final game, against Peru. A dynamic midfielder who assists at both ends of the pitch, he has risen to the challenge every time the team needed him, his commitment earning him a place in Gustavo Alfaro's affections. A graduate of the Olimpia academy, where he astonished everybody with his great build-up play, he spent one disappointing season at Nottingham Forest in 2021-22, making just three appearances, before moving to the MLS, first at Real Salt Lake and since January for Orlando City.

players.name: Gabriel Ávalos
players.bio: When Ávalos joined Independiente in 2024, at the age of 32, he declared that he was “at the peak of my career”. As it turned out, he had a bit further to rise, and this year he has been in the form of his life. Ávalos played in his homeland for only two seasons before taking a tour of South America, starting in the second division and rising eventually to spend three years with Argentinos Juniors and finally earn the move to Independiente. Since his international debut in 2021 he has had several runs in the team interspersed with long periods out of it but his form this year meant Gustavo Alfaro could not ignore him. Ávalos is not known for having great technical qualities, but he is an opportunist inside the penalty area and an aerial play expert.

players.name: Gastón Oliveira
players.bio: “The dream of playing in a World Cup was always there,” Olveira says. His dream might have involved playing for his native Uruguay, but he has grabbed with both hands the chance to play for Paraguay, which came when he became a naturalised citizen earlier this year, five years after joining Olimpia. “Playing for the national team can be a way of showing how grateful I am to the country,” he said. He was forced to learn the words to the national anthem for his naturalisation exam, but says he is still getting the hang of the tune. He did a pretty convincing job of singing it before his international debut, in Greece in March. Paraguay won that game, Olveira kept a clean sheet, and he showcased what was expected: calmness, confidence and reliability. He stands out for his reflexes and agility, and is a strong character always in communication with the defence.

players.name: Matías Galarza
players.bio: Galarza’s grandfather, Arturo, was a legendary goalkeeper who was born in Paraguay but moved to Bolivia and played for both national teams in the 1960s and 70s. His father, Rolando, was born in Bolivia – where he played professional football, also as a goalkeeper, and moved to Paraguay. Finally, Matías was born in Paraguay and moved to Bolivia – his brother Lucas played for Bolivia’s Under-21s, in goal of course – before playing in Brazil, Argentina, where his mother comes from, and as of March in the USA, but he was always destined to play for the land of his birth. “His dream was always to play for Paraguay,” his father said. Known for his energy and a wonderful left foot, he has become one of the most popular squad members.

players.name: Gustavo Caballero
players.bio: Caballero’s rise has been swift, but his feet are even swifter and it is the player’s remarkable speed that has propelled him into the national team, where he made his debut this year after a loan move to Portsmouth. “We need a game-changer,” said Gustavo Alfaro, “a player with that directness, that speed, that different gear at key moments.” Though he played for his country in the Paris Olympics Caballero made his full debut only in March, but a couple of friendly appearances – and a goal against Morocco in the second of them – cemented a place in the World Cup squad. As he said after moving to England: “I can play on the right or the left, and also up front, and want to bring goals and assists. I’m looking forward to bringing the fans some joy.”

players.name: Isidro Pitta
players.bio: Pitta had already planned a summer holiday in Spain with his family, almost certain he wouldn’t be called up. But football had other plans. Just hours before the official announcement, Gustavo Alfaro called him to say he was Paraguay’s 26th and final player for the World Cup. “I wasn’t sleeping thinking about this. I was anxious, but I never lost hope,” Pitta said. Born in Tobatí (74km from Asunción), Pitta began his career at Cerro Porteño but failed to break through into the first team. He took the harder road, moving to Deportivo Santaní – 151km from the capital – where he fought to establish himself. Spells at Sportivo Luqueño and Olimpia followed, with the latter proving to be the club where he finally exploded. After a brief stint with Huesca in Spain, Pitta moved to Brazil. He impressed at Juventude and Cuiabá before joining Red Bull Bragantino, where he is currently shining under the nickname of ‘Viking’ due to his long hair and big ginger beard. A fighter, a tireless worker and a constant nuisance for opposition defences, Pitta presses high, wins duels and possesses a genuine goal threat.

players.name: Alexandro Maidana
players.bio: The unexpected name on the list. His rise has been meteoric. Just two years ago he was playing in Paraguay’s second division. Now, at just 20, he is heading to the World Cup. Maidana admits he is still struggling to process his rapid ascent. “It’s impossible for it to sink in because in the space of two years everything has changed in my life,” he said. After coming through the ranks at Atyrá and Resistencia, he made his Primera División debut for Guaraní in February 2025. Fewer than 40 senior appearances were enough to earn a move to one of Argentina’s biggest clubs, Talleres of Córdoba. He had represented Paraguay at the 2025 Under-20 World Cup, but before this tournament he had only played 14 minutes for the senior team – in a friendly against Greece in March. That was enough to convince Gustavo Alfaro to take him to the World Cup. Like his parents, Maidana is more comfortable speaking Guaraní than Spanish, and the family usually communicates in Paraguay's indigenous language. An attacking left-back, Maidana stands out for his pace, willingness to push forward, and powerful long-range shooting ability.

players.name: Diogo Costa
players.bio: On 23 December, Porto fans received the Christmas present they had been asking for: the goalkeeper renewed his contract until 2030. "I wasn't sure if I would ever be a football player for Porto, but that has always been my biggest dream", he said at the time. He now has the highest salary at the club, but his release clause dropped from €75m to €60m. A major transfer is still on the horizon, but Diogo Costa has been a worthy successor to Vítor Baía, both at club and national team level. European Under-17 and Under-19 champion with Portugal, as well as a Nations League winner, he now dreams of a World Cup title with the national team. He has been wearing the No 99 shirt at Porto but the Dragons' president, André Villas-Boas, recently asked whether he could play in the No 2 next season. That number carries an enormous importance at the club, traditionally worn by captains, and it would be a tribute to Jorge Costa, the former club legend and director who died in August after suffering a heart attack at the training centre.

players.name: Nélson Semedo
players.bio: Left Wolves after five years in 2025 and had an approach to rejoin Benfica but felt it was too early to return home. Instead he chose to join Fenerbahce with one of the main reasons behind that decision was that the Turkish club was coached by a certain José Mourinho. A month later Mourinho was sacked and joined … Benfica. The 32-year-old has Cape Verdean ancestry and was delighted when the country qualified for the 2026 World Cup. "Like Portugal, Cape Verde is a very small country, but with an abundance of quality and a will to win,” he said “and that was crucial for them to make history and qualify for the World Cup." Asked by Sky which black athlete had inspired him he said: “For me it is Muhammad Ali, he inspired me the most. He was a champion, he fought for us, he fought against racism during a very difficult time. He lost a lot of things himself but he gave us, black people, hope.”

players.name: Rúben Dias
players.bio: Pulling his shorts up has become his trademark, especially when defending a corner kick. "It's a terrible habit. It's a concentration trigger, I do it unconsciously," he told the Portuguese FA TV channel. A natural leader, he is part of a generation that learned a lot from Cristiano Ronaldo about how to take care of their bodies. "The culture has changed [in the national team] and Cris helped a lot," Dias has said. The defender, in fact, has even tried to inspire his girlfriend – the English TV presenter Maya Jama – with healthy routines, not only thinking about the physical aspect but also the mental one. Tremendously competitive, the 29-year-old says he is often doing a mental reset to wipe out past achievements. Having joined Manchester City from Benfica in 2020 he has won every conceivable title, including four Premier Leagues.

players.name: Tomás Araújo
players.bio: Roberto Martínez wanted to avoid any controversy with this pick, saying: "It's not António Silva being left out, it's Tomás Araújo coming in." But the fact is, Araújo ended up taking his friend and club teammate’s place in the squad. The two players’ paths have crossed for some time now. A year older, Araújo made it into the Eagles' first team earlier, but then found it harder to establish himself. António Silva made an almost immediate impact later on, but then suffered a dip in form, meaning that, in recent months, Araújo was the first choice playing next to Nicolás Otamendi. It was still a big call, though. Araújo only had three caps when the squad was announced, two of them coming in March and April. But the squad announcement went down very well back at his family home, where relatives and neighbours had gathered to watch. His mum Idalina said: “When his name appeared, it was madness. An explosion of emotions among us and the friends who were here. I would love to go to the United States to see a game.”

players.name: Diogo Dalot
players.bio: Dalot admits he’s been on an “emotional rollercoaster” since joining Manchester United in 2018. He fell out of favour in his second season, writing in the Players’ Tribune in June: “I was ashamed of sitting in the stands. We’d be watching the game in the directors’ box, and the fans right next to us would ask me, ‘Why are you not playing?’ I didn’t even know what to say. I wasn’t injured. Just not selected. I was so embarrassed that I started going down to the dressing room to watch the game on the TV alone. When I got home I barely even said hello [to my mum]. I’d go downstairs, fire up the PlayStation, put Paranoid by Post Malone on repeat, and start thinking and thinking … What am I doing here?” He moved to Milan on loan in 2020, where he got to know Zlatan Ibrahimovic, who told him to invest in a padel business. “It was my first major investment on a personal level,” he told Forbes. Back at United a year later he worked hard to prove himself, and, in 2023-24, won Players' Player of the Year. Comes from a musical family. His parents met in a choir, his sister Mariana is a professional musician, and Diogo is United’s dressing room DJ. Matheus Cunha: “Diogo is an unbelievable DJ … he plays a little part of the world for everyone.”

players.name: Matheus Nunes
players.bio: Even as he joined Wolves from Sporting in 2022 he said that Manchester City was his dream destination – and he only had to wait one year before he completed a move to the Etihad. Pep Guardiola gave the Portuguese a new lease of life, moving him from a midfield position to right-back, which has reopened the door to the national team. It took some time getting used to though. "At the beginning I was stubborn,” Nunes told DAZN. “But after listening to different people and talking to my psychologist I realised that adding different nuances to my game will only make me a better player. Guardiola believes Nunes can become one of the best right-backs in the world.

players.name: Cristiano Ronaldo
players.bio: Cristiano Ronaldo may have chosen to play in Saudi Arabia instead of the USA but in November he was still invited to meet president Donald Trump at the White House as part of a visit by Saudi Arabia's crown prince Mohammed bin Salman. Ronaldo's sixth participation at the World Cup (the same as Messi) is unequivocal proof of his consistency, ambition, and the rage against time. So much so that no one dares to speak of an epilogue as his extraordinary career could still see him score his 1,000th goal. At 41, he manages to carry the feeling that there is still more to write, which is in line with the demanding nature he has always displayed. He is the leader, the point of reference, the bridge between generations. “Of course, I really want to win [the World Cup] but honestly it won't change my standing in football,” he told Piers Morgan.

players.name: Bruno Fernandes
players.bio: Fernandes revealed recently in an interview with the Portuguese FA TV channel that he refused to leave Sporting for rivals Benfica in the aftermath of the attack by Sporting fans on the club academy, back in 2018. Seven years later, he turned down an astronomical salary to join Al-Hilal in Saudi Arabia, even though he felt that the Manchester United management was pushing him out: “The club wanted me to go. I told the directors that. I think they didn't have the courage to make that decision because the coach [Ruben Amorim] wanted me to stay. If I had said I wanted to leave, the club would have let me go.” Captain at United, he is also one of the leaders in the national team. He is also a staunch defender of Cristiano Ronaldo. “I know there are people who say the national team plays better without Cristiano, that the players are freer,” he says. “If that is the case, it is partly our fault. We don't need to be restricted just because he is on the pitch. He remains a top-level player inside the area and he draws defenders and creates spaces.”

players.name: Gonçalo Ramos
players.bio: A striker who spends most of his time in the shadows. He almost always starts on the bench, both for the club and country, but is always ready to make a contribution when he comes on; his commitment often being converted into goals. In 2025, within the space of a week, he won the Champions League and the Nations League and, in between, became a father for the first time. Éder, the player who scored the goal that Portugal Euro 2016, believes Gonçalo Ramos can do something similar at the World Cup: "Because of his mentality and the way he has managed his career at PSG I think he can be a super important player,” he said.

players.name: Bernardo Silva
players.bio: To get some kind of variation of the usual praise Pep Guardiola heaps on a player he considers "irreplaceable" and with whom he wanted to renew for "many centuries," we can listen to what his national team coach, Roberto Martínez, has to say. "For me, he's the perfect example of what a Portuguese player should be," he told the Stick to Football podcast.
