"He's a tactical person, he wants to have information, he wants to know where the space is. He knows how to play inside, he knows how to play outside. He's not a fast player, but he's very intelligent in how he draws the opponent out. Tactically, he's a gift; he can use both feet – and if you can do that then you're a competitor.” Joined Manchester City from Monaco in 2017 and has become a club legend, winning every possible trophy. Fans are now clamouring for him to have his own statue outside the Etihad. As Martínez says:  Usually you have the more tactical and creative player or a more competitive player. But then you can have both, and that's Bernardo Silva. If you want someone reliable, it's Bernardo. If you want someone marking the tallest player in the box, it's Bernardo."

players.name: João Félix
players.bio: He may still be only 26 years old but it feels as if João Félix’s career has been standing still for some time now. After the 2024-25 season, which was split between Chelsea and Milan with equally modest returns he signed for Al-Nassr. "I was about to return to Benfica for the last three summers, it was always my wish and I always told my agents that, but there was never a serious desire on the part of the club," he told Sport TV. The Benfica president, Rui Costa, did not agree, saying: "I'm very sorry that he said that. If it depended on Benfica, he would have returned three years ago. In those three years it was probably the case that Chelsea, Barcelona, Milan and now Al-NAssr  wanted him more than Benfica.” Has delivered in Saudi Arabia with 33 goal involvements in 32 Pro League matches at the time of writing.

players.name: José Sá
players.bio: The last year was anything but easy, with the death of several close people – including his “brother” Diogo Jota – but Sá has always faced adversity with grit, even professionally. He didn't succeed in Benfica's youth system, but ended up signing for Porto. He left the Dragão with little fanfare, but later managed to reach the national team and accumulate more than 150 games in the Premier League. In October, with Wolves struggling, he was filmed arguing with fans outside Molineux but won his audience over with his willingness to talk. The beard is his trademark and has been nicknamed António Variações after the renowned Portuguese singer who died in 1984 and sported similarly impressive facial hair. “They started to call me that after the European Under-21s [in 2015]. It must be the voice,” Sá once joked.

players.name: Renato Veiga
players.bio: The son of Nélson Veiga, a former Cape Verde international who played in the Portuguese top flight, Renato joined Sporting back in 2019 but did not make the first team. What he did, however, was to find an alternative route to the national team. He played for Augsburg and Basel before joining Chelsea. A loan spell at Juventus followed before he joined Villarreal in 2025. "I speak seven languages and I really enjoy learning them,” he says. “It helps me integrate more quickly into each country." One day during one of his first training camps with the national team, he became very emotional when speaking about his late grandfather. "I am so grateful for everything he did for me," the 22-year-old said.

players.name: Gonçalo Inácio
players.bio: The Almada president, Andreia Santos, perhaps tells the story best of how much Inácio means to the club where he started his career. "During one match, after Gonçalo had scored a goal, his mother, who was in the stands, went on to the pitch to celebrate. That goal, where he scored, now has his signature on it," Santos told A Bola.  At Sporting since 2012, he made his first-team debut in 2020 and is now a mainstay in central defence for his club. He has a discreet playing style but his effectiveness has earned him a starting place for his country too.

players.name: João Neves
players.bio: It's the future that honours the past, through elegance and poise. The shirt remains tucked into the shorts, even after winning the Champions League at the age of 20. Neves benefitted from the move from Benfica to PSG in 2024 not only on the pitch but off it too, according to former teammate Tiago Gouveia, who also left Lisbon for France (Nice) a year later. "Signing for PSG did him a world of good. He was so addicted to sandwiches and supermarket lasagnas that it was starting to get worrying," he joked. The move may have changed Neves’ diet, but not his personality. "I go to the café and try to go unnoticed like an ordinary person,” he told Uefa. “I know that two or three people will recognise me and ask to take a picture, but I believe that the others will leave me alone. I don't have, for example, Vitinha's mustache." Says he always goes by his dad’s motto (he was a policeman): “the hardest thing is not getting there, it's staying there." Has reached such an appeal at such a young age that he was recruited - together with his girlfriend, the actress Madalena Aragão – to inspire the Portuguese people to vote in the 2026 presidential elections.

players.name: Francisco Trincão
players.bio: Trincão's impressive moustache has become his trademark. A very stylish dresser, he has even been on the cover of GQ magazine, but in the end he (mainly) wants to be known for the elegance he has on the pitch. The highlight so far for the national team? A double against Denmark in the quarter-finals of the 2025 Nations League, which Portugal went on to win. He is still looking to solidify his place in the national team and, apart from playing with Cristiano Ronaldo at international level, he shared a dressing room with Lionel Messi at Barcelona. "I have been able to play with the two best players in the world, each in their own style, and I learned a lot from what they transmit in the dressing room. It's very good to have experienced that." Back at Sporting now after his adventure in Spain, he also had one season on loan with Wolves in the Premier League.

players.name: Rafael Leão
players.bio: "Zlatan created Leão," they say as the Portuguese flourished under the Swede’s leadership and protection at Milan, especially the season they won the Scudetto together, in 2021-22. Sadly, though, the Swede’s retirement the following year did not lead to Leão becoming the team’s biggest star. Frustratingly inconsistent he is capable of deciding games on his own and mesmerising fans with his skillful moves but can also disappear for long spells and has twice angered the Milan coach, Max Allegri, with his attitude when substituted. Off the pitch he is continuing his career as the musician WAY 45. "Zlatan laughed when he heard it. He told me to focus on the football," Leão said once.

players.name: Pedro Neto
players.bio: Pedro started out playing roller hockey in Viana do Castelo but switched to football at age 10. He was the first footballer born in 2000 (or later) to play for the national team but took an unconventional route to the top, not playing for one of the big threes in Portugal, going from Braga to Lazio to Wolves before arriving at Chelsea in 2024. When the Portugal coach, Roberto Martínez, was asked on the Stick to Football podcast, about comparisons between Neto and Eden Hazard, he said: "He's not like Hazard. Eden stopped the game to look for the 1v1. Pedro is the opposite, he speeds up the game.” In March Neto hit the headlines for all the wrong reasons, pushing a ballboy during the game against Paris St-Germain. He was very contrite, though, and offered his shirt to the youngster after the game and escaped with a warning from Uefa.

players.name: Gonçalo Guedes
players.bio: Scored the goal that secured Portugal’s first Nations League title in 2019 and was good at taking the limited chances he got with the national team but disappeared from the scene after joining Wolves from Valencia in 2022. Has not been able to find the consistency required to become a regular for his country, partly because of injury problems and there was even a return to Benfica on loan in an attempt to rediscover his form. A move to Real Sociedad in 2025 reignited his career and his form in Spain earned a place on the plane to North America. "He's versatile, which is important for us,” Roberto Martínez said in March. “He's a different kind of forward, which is very interesting. I've already said that the striker positions belongs to Cristiano Ronaldo and Gonçalo Ramos s we're looking for a player with a different profile.”

players.name: João Cancelo
players.bio: At the start of the year Cancelo made a surprise return to Barcelona and La Liga after a year and a half in Saudi Arabia with Al Hilal. "For me, Barcelona is the best club in the world. It's the only club that gives me goosebumps," said the full-back, who has also played for Benfica, Valencia, Inter, Juventus, Manchester City and Bayern Munich. His CV leaves little doubt as to his quality but also hints at some instability – and that goes for the national team as well. "If we win the World Cup, I'll quit international football,” he said during the World Cup qualifiers. “Finishing strongly is always better than not winning any titles. If we don't win it this time, I'll try again.” After falling out with Pep Guardiola and leaving Manchester City he told A Bola: “Lies were told. They were ungrateful to me. I never failed in my commitment to the club, to the fans and I always gave everything. I remember a time when I was robbed and attacked and the next day I was playing at the Emirates against Arsenal.” After securing the La Liga title in May he has won the league in four out of the top five leagues in Europe - only France is missing.

players.name: Rúben Neves
players.bio: Now wears the No 21 for the national team as a tribute to his “brother” Diogo Jota, with whom he played at Porto, Wolverhampton and the national team. Neves, like so many others in the national team, was devastated by the death of the forward last summer and on his left calf he tattooed an image of an embrace with his friend. At the funeral he carried the coffin of his friend and he wrote on social media: "More than a friendship, we are family, and we won't stop being that, just because you've decided to sign a contract a little further away from us. Whenever I go to the national team you will still be by my side at the dinner table, on the bus, on the plane. You will always be there with me, as usual." Neves broke through at Porto as a 17-year-old and tends to stay at his clubs longer than most players. After six years at Wolves he joined Al-Hilal in Saudi Arabia in 2023.

players.name: Rui Silva
players.bio: "I loved scoring goals. Even at school I never went in goal," Silva told A Bola a few years ago when discussing his childhood. In the end he became a goalkeeper because of his height and has built his career through hard work. He trained with Sporting when he was 17 but was not offered a professional contract. It was only a decade later – and spells at Nacional, Granada and Real Betis – that he joined Sporting and became a Portuguese champion. He was included in the Euro 2020 squad but did not play. He may travel to North America in Diogo Costa’s shadow but he now has a different status in the squad.

players.name: Vitinha
players.bio: Last year was "unbelievable", according to the midfielder, who won the Champions League, Ligue 1, the French Cup, the French Super Cup and the European Super Cup with PSG, as well as the Nations League with Portugal. To cap it off he finished third in the Ballon d'Or voting. "There were many moments in my professional career when I doubted myself a lot,” told A Bola, the publication who named him Personality of the Year. “It wasn't expected that Vitinha would become a great player or that he could achieve anything. At least, that's the feeling I always had. That's what helped me, it was my fuel.” In fact, he added, “Luis Enrique believed more in me than I did myself.” The PSG coach is not afraid to give the midfielder some “constructive” criticism though. "My left foot is almost useless,” Vitinha says. “It's really bad. It's something Luis Enrique is on at me day and night to improve." A very humble person, he has said he wants to do everything he can to have the same influence with the national team that he has at PSG.

players.name: Samú Costa
players.bio: He may have been part of Roberto Martínez’s squad for the spring friendlies but the 25-year-old was still the main surprise when the World Cup squad was announced. "He showed his energy, grit and tactical awareness in March," the coach explained. Samú was selected ahead of players with real international pedigree such as João Palhinha and Mateus Fernandes, and reached the national team without playing for one of the “big three” in Portugal. He came through the youth ranks at Braga but only played one game for the first team before moving to Spain. In February, he told A Bola that: "it would be easier to reach the national team" if he were at a bigger club, adding: "the manager himself told me that” – but he managed it anyway. When the squad was announced he told Canal 11: "Perhaps no one expected me to be called up, and that was extra motivation for me. Being able to go against all odds and surprise people.”

players.name: Nuno Mendes
players.bio: "I don't know if the perfect player exists, I don't think so,” the Paris St-Germain coach, Luis Enrique, said recently, before adding: “[But] if we think about a player with certain qualities in physical, technical and personality terms then Nuno clearly would be close to perfection." Despite the constant praise being flung at Mendes, by Enrique and others, the defender continues to impress with his humility and discretion. "I don't like to say I'm the best. You have to show it on the pitch and I always try to do that," says the player who finished 10th in the Ballon d’Or ranking in 2025. Not bad for a left-back. Not even in his mid-twenties, Mendes has already won the league in Portugal and France as well as the Champions League, the Intercontinental Cup and the Nations League. Has even made a habit of keeping Lamine Yamal in his pocket when facing the Spanish youngster.

players.name: Francisco Conceição
players.bio: The tricky and fast winger left Porto after the fallout between his father Sergio, who was the coach, and the club president André Villas-Boas, in 2024, joining Juventus, where he wears the No 7 shirt that once upon a time belonged to Cristiano Ronaldo. "I didn’t get to speak to him before I joined,” Conceição told Sport TV. “But afterwards he said it is a huge club that demands everything from the player, a club that is used to winning, where you have to win every single competition you enter.” Francisco says his father was the best coach he ever had – "there are few like him" – but Sergio told La Gazzetta dello Sport that he had to use stick as well as carrot to get the best out of his son. “I gave him his debut during lockdown but he was a bit chubby so I said: ‘If you are hungry, drink water’. To go far you need to make sacrifices and have a certain mentality.

players.name: Mahmoud Abunada
players.bio: At over 190 cm, the Al-Rayyan No 1 is one of Asia’s tallest goalkeepers and while he has impressed with his consistency in the Qatar Stars League over the years, his international career has been an unusual one. The Doha-born shotstopper was first called up way back in 2020 and while he was in and out of the squad over the next five years – and sometimes in action with the Under-23 team – he never made it off the bench. When the call finally came it was in the two World Cup playoffs last October, the games that clinched Qatar’s first ever qualification. In some of the biggest games in the country’s history, he stood firm.

players.name: Pedro Miguel
players.bio: Also known as ‘Ro-Ro’, the defender could, had history gone differently, be turning out for Cape Verde this summer as he represented the African country at youth level. Born in Portugal, spending years with Benfica at youth level without ever making a first-team appearance, he can play as right-back or in the middle of defence or even in midfield if necessary. Was a star at the 2019 Asian Cup and there were high expectations going into 2022 that were not quite met. Has since broken the century barrier in appearances for Qatar.

players.name: Lucas Mendes
players.bio: For a while it looked as if this left-footed Brazilian-born centre-back was going to be the hero of qualification with a winning goal against Uzbekistan in the 12th minute of injury time, controlling a header in a crowded area to fire into the top corner. In the end, Qatar still needed the playoffs to qualify but here was a player for the big occasion. In 2014, he left Marseille to join El-Jaish and although nobody really thought that he would be there for nine years and being naturalised as a Qatari citizen, that is exactly what happened. He could have been useful a few months earlier at the previous World Cup, and he will look to make up for lost time in June and July.

players.name: Issa Laye
players.bio: The Senegal-born defender received Qatari citizenship in 2025 after five years on the books at second-tier Lusail. At 1.88m, he brings height and power into the backline, though can also play in midfield if needed. Given that Qatar’s defence has looked vulnerable and that making the most of set pieces is seen as crucial by the coaching staff, Laye could have a significant part to play in North America.

players.name: Jassem Gaber
players.bio: An energetic defensive midfielder who can also play further back if needed, Gaber offers industry and intelligence in his position. He does not get forward too much – his only international goal came via a hefty deflection in the semi-final of the 2023 Asian Cup – and his work goes more unnoticed than most, by fans at least. The coaching staff knows the player’s worth though. “He is the perfect team player, he does the simple things very well and opponents don’t enjoy facing him as they know it will be a difficult 90 minutes,” said team leader Hassan Al-Haydos after the Asian Cup triumph. At 24, the World Cup could come at a perfect time to make an international name for himself.

players.name: Abdulaziz Hatem
players.bio: Made his debut even before Sepp Blatter announced that Qatar had won the bidding for the 2022 World Cup, and is still going strong, though is not quite the fixture he once was in the centre of midfield, not surprising given that he is closer to 40 than 30. Still has a reputation for scoring important goals – such as the winner in the quarter-final in the 2019 Asian Cup and then hit the target in the final –and spectacular ones too. More often on display are his excellent close control skills, especially as he receives the ball from the defence. Won’t expect too many minutes in the United States.

players.name: Ahmed Alaaeldin
players.bio: Has a good leap – just look at his towering header in the Arab Cup against Syria – and never stops buzzing around the frontline. Moved to Doha from Egypt at the age of 10 with his engineer father, a typical Qatari story. Less common is that he made his international debut at the age of 20 way back in 2013. He now has close to 70 appearances, but has never quite established himself as the main man in attack for the national team and has been in and out of the side for 13 years.

players.name: Edmilson Junior
players.bio: Eligible to represent the country of his birth, Belgium – where he played seven seasons for Sint-Truidense and Standard Liège – as well as Brazil, the winger finally made the switch to Qatar in October 2024. It’s easy to see why he was in demand. Can play on the right or left and also in the middle and takes a mean free-kick too. Has an air of Son Heung-min when he is in possession on the edge of the area, curling shots into far corners or drilling balls into the bottom of the net.

players.name: Mohammed Muntari
players.bio: Scored Qatar’s only goal at the 2022 World Cup and, in 2025, scored the fastest goal in the history of the Qatar Stars League at just eight seconds. Born and raised in Ghana, trained at the academy owned by Nii Lamptey, ex-Aston Villa, Coventry City and Anderlecht forward and one-time ‘African Pele’. An aerial threat at over 190cm, he made the switch back to don the Maroon back in 2014. "We play football first because we love to play football," he explained just before the last World Cup "Second because we want to take care of the people we want to take care of. "It wasn't financially motivated. I could have been playing for Ghana and still be earning much."

players.name: Hassan Al-Haydos
players.bio: Returned from 469 days of international retirement in 2025 at the request of Julen Lopetegui and it came as no surprise. “He brings a presence to the team and is a real leader and that can sometimes make the difference,” said the coach. Then there is the experience too. Al-Haydos is Qatar’s most-capped player with more than 180 appearances for his country and over 40 goals. Played a pivotal role in Qatar’s 2019 and 2023 Asian Cup triumphs. A one-club man who has won over 30 trophies in his career; the first league title with Al-Sadd came back in 2007. He won the Asian Champions League four years later.

players.name: Akram Afif
players.bio: The country’s big star, big hope and the magician on the wing, and not just because he has been known to pull playing cards out of his socks upon scoring. If the two-time Asian Player of the Year plays well, then Qatar have a chance. Did not have the best World Cup in 2022, when hopes were high, and it was also disappointing that he never made it in Europe so this is his big chance to show that he is capable of mixing it with the big boys. Can dribble past defenders and create – there were 10 assists as Qatar won the 2019 Asian Cup – and score: he hit eight goals as Qatar won the 2023 Asian Cup, including a hat-trick in the final. Qatar need Afif to shine.

players.name: Karim Boudiaf
players.bio: Scored one of the best goals ever seen at Crewe Alexandra’s Gresty Road, his first for Qatar, a left-footed curler from outside the area to earn a draw with Northern Ireland in 2015. “Every team needs a player like Karim Boudiaf, someone that the other players can look to when the game becomes tense or chaotic,” said the former Qatar coach Carlos Queiroz. “He is a calm presence.” The Algerian-born midfielder is as aggressive as he has always been as he approaches his 36th birthday. Boudiaf has more than 120 caps and a big part of his longevity is that he longer dashes around in defensive midfield to no purpose but reads the game, breaks up an attack and starts one of his own.

players.name: Ayoub Alawi
players.bio: A late addition to the team with Julen Lopetegui handing the youngster his international debut in October 2025, throwing him into the mix for the latter stages of qualification. Bringing some much-needed youth into the squad, he has continued to feature since and could be in line for a starting spot. It is a just reward for a fine domestic season for Al-Gharafa where he was named the best Under-23 player in the Qatar Stars League in May. Brings physicality to the right-back position, though has played both at centre-back and in defensive midfield for his club.

players.name: Homam Ahmed
players.bio: Played all but seven minutes of the 2022 World Cup and still has a point to prove. At the start of the year, the rangy left-back, who can play further up the pitch, made the move to play in Spain’s second tier. It took the defender time to settle but as the second half of the season progressed, he has started to get more and more time on the pitch, which can only help get in the good books of coach, Julen Lopetegui, who wants Ahmed to add goals to his game. If so, he could have a great World Cup.

players.name: Yusuf Abdurisag
players.bio: The speedy winger was at the centre of an incident in 2023 as Qatar played New Zealand in a friendly with the All Whites refusing to play the second half after alleging racist abuse. The day after Abdurisag said: “During last night’s game, I was the target of racist abuse from a member of the opposing team,” he said. “To my complete shock, that same player accused me of using offensive language and the game was abandoned.” Has struggled to get playing time for Qatar in recent months and is likely to be a squad player at the World Cup.

players.name: Boualem Khoukhi
players.bio: An old warrior who started out as a youth player at Algeria’s Cheraga. “He outclassed both opponents and teammates. He had a good understanding of the game, which allowed him to anticipate the opposing team's attacks,” says the coach Farid Zemiti. “He was just as good on the ball as he was covering.” After starting out as a winger, has since played well over 100 games in defence and midfield, and his 21st international goal came in the play-off win over the UAE in October that clinched a first-ever qualification. Strong in the shot and commanding in the air, a legend of Qatar whatever happens in the summer.

players.name: Ahmed Al-Ganehi
players.bio: The winger, who plays with a smile on his face, has come up through the ranks of the Qatar national team to become a solid squad player. Has earned something of a reputation for being a super sub and scoring goals in big domestic games. Can play on either flank, but usually appears on the right, and is adept at running into space behind the defence. Has yet to really catch the eye for the national team since making his debut in 2024 but the World Cup could be the stage.

players.name: Sultan Al-Brake
players.bio: The left-back spent time in Spain with Cultural Leonesa in the early part of his career and perhaps it was there where he perfected the kind of cross that hangs in the air just waiting for someone to get on the end of it. “Tactially it was an education,” he said later, reflecting on his European time. “It was also a chance to see a different lifestyle and how the players prepared for games.” It can’t have done his confidence any harm either. Good on the ball, he likes to get forward, using his pace to drive at the opposition. Never quite managed to become an automatic starter for his country but has proved to be a solid back-up whenever called upon.

players.name: Almoez Ali
players.bio: It’s hard to know what to make of Ali’s career. “He comes alive in the area but remains calm when there is a sight of goal and that is why he is a top-class striker,” said the then Qatar coach Félix Sánchez after the 2019 Asian Cup. That’s when Ali rose to fame with nine goals and has since become Qatar’s leading goalscorer. Had early spells in Austria and Spain but has now been with Al-Duhail since 2016. At one time, the Sudan-born striker, fast and able to find space, looked set to become Asia’s number one goalscorer – but it never quite happened. Still a threat.

players.name: Ahmed Fathy
players.bio: Son of an Egyptian football coach who also worked as a youth coach for Al-Sadd, Qatar’s leading club, for over 25 years, Fathy always had a decent chance of making it. Working hard also helped and his energy as a defensive midfielder has stood him in good stead even if he does not quite have the same engine as a few years ago. Fathy captained the national team at times, notably at the 2023 Gold Cup and really grew into the responsibility of the role.

players.name: Salah Zakaria
players.bio: Long seen as the next goalkeeper to take the gloves, but it has not happened yet. Now 27, Zakaria has established himself as Al-Duhail’s No 1 but was linked to Egyptian giants Zamalak in 2025, partly because he has Egyptian citizenship. Not only a solid shotstopper, Zakaria is valued by coaches for his distribution as he is equally comfortable with both feet, whether launching long or short. It is unlikely that he is going to start games at this World Cup but has shown at club level that he is a reliable back-up.

players.name: Meshaal Barsham
players.bio: Brother of 2020 Olympic high jump gold medal winner Mutaz, Barsham has similar agility and has made a name for penalty saving prowess. Born in Sudan and, after the dismal opener at the 2022 World Cup, came in for the remaining two group games against Senegal and the Netherlands but still conceded five. Bounced back to be the best goalkeeper at Qatar’s 2023 Asian Cup triumph and is not short on mental fortitude. Not the tallest shotstopper at just below six feet but has the Barsham spring and can reach shots that others can’t. “He is capable of making amazing saves,” said former Qatar coach Felix Sanchez. “And he’s very professional, the kind of goalkeeper that all coaches want.”

players.name: Assim Madibo
players.bio: Had an interesting start to his career with spells in France, Spain and Belgium before coming to the Qatar Stars League. The midfielder has been compared to N’Golo Kante with his tireless running and ability to read the game. Has been in and out of the team but he did play a significant part in qualification, including every minute of the two play-off games that finally sealed the deal for the Maroons. “He did everything I asked of him, especially when the tension in the stadium was high with so much on the line,” said the Qatar coach, Julen Lopetegui. “It was an impressive performance.”

players.name: Tahsin Mohammed Jamshid
players.bio: Fans in India don’t have a team to cheer on at the World Cup and while many will be backing Spain, England, Brazil or Argentina, some will be cheering on Jamshid. The teenager’s parents came to Qatar from football-loving Kerala in India and the winger, born during the World Cup in 2006, is unfazed by the challenge. “For me, Qatar is my own country. I'm a Qatari citizen. Having already played for the Under-17 and Under-19 teams, this selection didn't come as a surprise," he said.  Quick with an appetite for running at defenders, Jamshid, who plays on the left but can do a job on the right, looks to have a bright future.

players.name: Hashmi Hussein
players.bio: No stranger to controversy, Hussein managed to upset the nation of India during the qualifiers. With Qatar losing 1-0 in a qualifier, he pounced on a ball that had looked to have clearly gone out of play, passing for a teammate to score and put a huge dent in the dreams of Indian fans. The Indian FA president, Kalyan Chaubey, asked for a “thorough investigation” into the “grave error”. The AIFF added in a statement: “Furthermore, we have urged them [the AFC Head of Referees] to explore the possibilities of sporting compensation to address the injustice.” Nothing came of it in the end, though. Hussein is a rarity in Qatari circles as he has spent time playing in Europe, the lower leagues of Spain to be exact, and has recently returned to the national team.

players.name: Mohamed Al-Mannai
players.bio: Made his debut for Al-Sadd as a teenager before being loaned out to Al-Shamal. It was the right move and he helped the club have an impressive season with five goals catching the eye and earning the Under-23 player of the season award. Julen Lopetegui seems to be a fan too. Mannai made his international debut in 2025 but has looked assured in making the step up. Started out mainly as a defensive midfielder but has slowly been moved further up the pitch and has shown considerable passing ability that could make a difference.

players.name: Nawaf Al-Aqidi
players.bio: Saudi Arabia squeezed through to the World Cup thanks in part to a fine injury-time save from Al-Aqidi in the final playoff against Iraq. Saudi Arabia needed a draw and the game finished 0-0. “We had to hold our breath but he came through for us as we knew he would,” said the previous coach Hervé Renard. The goalkeeper added: "We have achieved something extraordinary, truly unbelievable.” Al-Aqidi was in the squad for the 2022 tournament but very much the third choice and never saw a single minute but in the same year was the star of the Under-23 team which won the West Asian Football Federation Championship title without conceding a single goal. Since then, the Riyadh-born shotstopper star has, like most Saudi goalkeepers, been looking for regular playing time at home amid an influx of foreign goalkeepers. Went on loan to Al-Fateh for the second part of the 2024-25 season and did well but returned to Al-Nassr to be in and out of the side.

players.name: Ali Majrashi
players.bio: Came through the Saudi Scholarship Program for Developing Football Talents as a teenager,  Ali Majarshi has matured impressively as Al-Ahli’s resurgence has gathered pace. Calm in possession and energetic in his forward bursts, he has become a reliable outlet down the right flank, contributing a steady stream of assists and overlapping runs that stretch opposition defences. His versatility allows him to operate further up the pitch when needed, adding flexibility to the Jeddah club’s attacking patterns. A product of Saudi football’s new generation, Majarshi blends technical quality with aggression – though his competitive streak occasionally sees him tread a fine disciplinary line.

players.name: Ali Lajami
players.bio: Lajami upset Al-Nassr by leaving on a free transfer to join bitter rivals Al-Hilal in 2025. It was quite the controversy but the move was seen as the right one for the intelligent centre-back, who is able to bring the ball out of defence. “Lajami has great potential and I think Al-Hilal see in him the qualities they want – he needs the right environment,” said the former international midfielder Mohammed Noor. It looked as if his World Cup was over and he was dropped from the March squad by Hervé Renard but has been given a reprieve by new coach Georgios Donis. His twin brother, Qassem, has three caps for Saudi Arabia and was most recently on loan at Al-Taawoun.

players.name: Abdulelah Al-Amri
players.bio: A leader on and off the pitch, perhaps it is the fact that the centre-back hails from the city of Taif in the Hijaz mountains in the west of the country, that he seems to have an air of calmness and stability. Any games at altitude will also not be a problem. Played all three games at the 2022 World Cup but has been in and out of the national team since, though Hervé Renard was more of a fan than his predecessor Roberto Mancini was. Now it remains to be seen what Georgios Donis makes of him. He may be strong in the air and tidy on the ball but he is no longer an automatic choice for club or country but gives everything when called upon. Back at Al-Nassr for the 2025-26 season having been on loan at Al-Ittihad the previous campaign.

players.name: Hassan Al-Tambakti
players.bio: “Tambakti is the best Saudi player at the moment, without a doubt,” said the former international Abdullah Otayf on TV in November. “He should be playing in Europe as he has all the tools.” There has been interest from clubs in France and Portugal but the 27-year-old has stayed in Riyadh. A leader on the pitch and more than holds his own against the top-class strikers that have been imported into the Saudi Pro League. Powerful and confident with the ball, he has been compared to former Inter defender Milan Skriniar, and has the physical strength to mix it with any forward.

players.name: Nasser Al-Dawsari
players.bio: According to local reports, several European clubs – such as Cercle Brugge and Sporting Braga – inquired about the technical 27-year-old last summer but got short shrift from the four-time Asian champions after his impressive performances at the Club World Cup when he outshone some bigger names on the global stage. He does like a big game as he showed with a spectacular strike in the final of the 2021 Asian Champions League. Plays most of the games for Al-Hilal as his coach, Simone Inzaghi, is a fan of his versatility and intelligence. He is in fine form and looks ready to step out of the shadow of his more famous namesake in the team.

players.name: Musab Al-Juwayr
players.bio: “He makes the game look easy and has a very bright future, performing better when the game is bigger and that is a special gift,” said Vitor Pereira, Al-Juwayr’s former coach who has reportedly been interested in taking the playmaker to England.  In a league where young players do not always get opportunities, Al-Juwayr has been a shining light. A deserved winner of the 2024-25 Young Player of the Season award, he won the monthly gong no fewer than eight times. Blessed with composure and vision, he has been assisting goals like it is going out of fashion and played a leading role in World Cup qualification.

players.name: Ayman Yahya
players.bio: Was the player of the tournament and top scorer as Saudi Arabia won the U-23 Asian Cup in 2022 and the winger, who came through Al-Nassr’s youth academy, was tipped as the next big thing. Hervé Renard gave Yahya his international debut as a teenager in 2022, rare in Saudi Arabia, but he has not quite kicked on. Perhaps his versatility, he can play on either side in defence or attack as well as an advanced central role, has counted against the Riyadh-born player. A fast player who likes to get forward, has suffered a couple of injuries this season for Al-Nassr.

players.name: Firas Al-Buraikan
players.bio: The perfect example of what many, many Saudi strikers are going through at the moment. Started out at Al-Nassr, before moving to Al-Fateh for more minutes. There, he found playing time and the net on a regular basis, showing composure in front of goal and an ability to find space in crowded areas. Impressively, he finished as the league’s fourth top scorer in 2023 and fifth the year after. Then there was a move back to a big club, Al-Ahli, where he started well, but after the Jeddah club signed Ivan Toney Al-Buraikan was shifted to the wing.

players.name: Salem Al-Dawsari
players.bio: His winner and somersault against Argentina was one of the moments of the 2022 World Cup and he has continued to be one of the standouts in the star-studded Saudi Pro League thanks to his creativity and vision . There are concerns however, that his national team form is not what it was and a number of missed penalties in qualification almost cost Saudi Arabia a place at the World Cup. When the going gets tough, however, the 2025 Asian Player of the Year is still the one the others look to.

players.name: Saleh Al-Shehri
players.bio: An old-fashioned number nine who likes to let defenders know he is around, get stuck in, chase lost causes and generally be a pain. Scored the equaliser against Argentina in the 2-1 win at the 2022 World Cup but he has still suffered from the same problem that all Saudi strikers have at the moment in that he is now a bit-part player for Al-Ittihad and is likely to be the same for the national team.  "The presence of a large number of foreigners in the league has two sides,” he said recently. “The first is positive, with the opportunity to interact with the best international players, such as playing alongside Karim Benzema. The negative side is somewhat limited [opportunities], with fewer playing minutes.”

players.name: Saud Abdulhamid
players.bio: The only senior Saudi player with substantial European experience in one of the big-five European leagues. While the goalscorers got the headlines after the 2-1 World Cup win over Argentina, most at home agreed that the attack-minded right-back was the standout for Saudi Arabia at that tournament. There was pride when Juventus were interested and excitement when he signed for  Roma. After struggling for gametime in Italy, however, he was loaned out to title-chasing Lens. Is not a regular starter in northern France but has filled in without much fuss when called upon, leading to links with Everton and Newcastle United. In March he became the first Saudi player to score in France’s top flight. Expectations will be high in the summer.

players.name: Nawaf Boushal
players.bio: Hails from the Unesco heritage site of Ahsa, which is famous for producing dates, the speedy right-back, who can play on either side, has carved out a name for himself as well as a furrow up and down that side of the pitch. “His is a true story of perseverance,” said TV pundit Ali Al-Marshud. “A player who was once close to leaving [Al-Nassr] because it was said his services were no longer needed, but he made great sacrifices and became a top performer, earning the trust of coaches at both club and the national team.” As well as defensive discipline, he has become an increasing threat going forward, making 11 goal contributions for Al-Nassr in 2024-25.

players.name: Hassan Kadesh
players.bio: Was first selected by Bert van Marwijk back in 2016 but Roberto Mancini was the first national team coach to really give this genial centre-back a run in the team. Born in the eastern city of Dammam, Kadesh became the most expensive Saudi player in history in 2023 with a £12m transfer to Al-Ittihad, a move made even more impressive by the fact that he was just a month short of his 30th birthday and a defender to boot. There were more headlines when he became the joint top scorer for Saudi Arabia in the main stage of World Cup qualification scoring both goals, one in the last-minute, in a crucial win over China. His heading ability has been useful in both areas.

players.name: Abdullah Al-Khaibari
players.bio: A no-nonsense and intelligent defensive midfielder, Al-Khaibari rarely makes headlines but then rarely makes mistakes. “Every successful team needs a player like him,” said the Al-Nassr coach, Jorge Jesus. “He reads the game well and uses the ball well.” The early signs are that coach Georgios Donis is a fan and the Riyadh-born midfielder is in North America after a successful season in which he played a big role in Al-Nassr winning the Saudi Pro League title for the first time since 2019. He has noticeably improved since playing and training alongside stars such as Cristiano Ronaldo, Marcelo Brozovic, Sadio Mané and João Félix.

players.name: Ziyad Al-Johani
players.bio: Will be one of the fittest players at the World Cup, never stops running and is a more than competent link between defence and midfield. Came up through the youth ranks at Al-Ahli and has stayed there ever since, helping the club to the Asian Champions League in 2025. There was interest from Al-Nassr in 2025 with their coach, Luís Castro, a fan of the midfielder’s vision and leadership qualities on the pitch, but Al-Ahli decided that his partnership with Franck Kessié was too valuable to break up.

players.name: Khalid Al-Ghannam
players.bio: Such was Al-Ghannam’s form this season that the left winger could not be ignored and he was recalled in March after a gap of more than two years. He has simply been exceptional for Al-Ettifaq and was one of the best players in the Saudi Pro League this season. He has always been fast but has more control these days, with 13 goals and seven assists, and loves to beat players with pace and trickery. He could be a great option late in the game in hot conditions and if he shines in North America, the clamour for his signature at home is just going to increase with local rivals Al-Qadsiah leading the line of suitors.

players.name: Alaa Al-Hejji
players.bio: It’s fair to say the 30-year-old would not have made his international debut in May under Hervé Renard. New coach Georgios Donis, however, had worked with the midfielder over two spells at Al-Wehda and quickly came to trust the player who started his journey in futsal. In the beginning, Al-Hejji was a defensive midfielder but, under Donis, he was also used further up the pitch. After spending his career at smaller clubs, finally got a big move to the ambitious Neom last summer. It took time to get playing time in the early part of the season but soon was in the starting XI to help the newly-promoted club finish in mid-table.

players.name: Abdullah Al-Hamdan
players.bio: The son of former Al-Shabab goalkeeper Abdulrahman Al-Hamdan, the striker broke through domestically with his goalscoring instincts and was at one point seen as the great hope of Saudi Arabian strikers. In 2021 however, the mighty Al-Hilal swooped to sign the youngster in what was seen as a huge move for Saudi football, the biggest club paying big for local potential. It did not work out, however, with restricted minutes and a handful of league goals over five seasons. Moved across Riyadh in February to join Cristiano Ronaldo but is still struggling for minutes. He offers an aerial presence, hard work and the ability to play across the frontline.

players.name: Sultan Mandash
players.bio: A late bolter for the squad and, possibly, the starting XI. That may sound strange for a 31-year-old who from his international debut in 2019 until 2025, had made just three appearances. The right winger likes to play high up the pitch and use his speed to make things happen and caught the eye after three goals in the last four games of the season for Al-Hilal, especially given Georgios Donis is in charge of a team with attacking issues. Mandash came off the bench in May’s friendly against Ecuador and scored in a 2-1 defeat. The 31-year-old is sure to play some part this summer, either starting or off the bench when goals are needed.

players.name: Mohammed Al-Owais
players.bio: Was named man of the match in the famous 2-1 win over Argentina at the 2022 World Cup, a feat made even more impressive given that the Saudi Arabia No 1 was No 2 for his club at the time and that he had to regain his focus after a collision which left teammate Yasser Al-Shahrani unconscious and unable to carry on. In 2025 Al-Owais tired of playing second fiddle to Yassine ‘Bono’ Bounou at Al-Hilal and dropped down a division to help Al-Ula’s promotion push, where he has enjoyed a new lease of life. A calm presence around the team, which means he may well have an important role to play at the World Cup despite the fact he is not expected to be the No 1 this time around.

players.name: Ahmed Al-Kassar
players.bio: The oldest player in the squad and, indeed, the oldest Saudi Arabian ever to go to a World Cup. Has been a bit of a journeyman in the domestic league, never playing for one of the Big Four. Despite his advancing years, he only made his international debut in 2024 during the earlier stages of qualification under Roberto Mancini but played little under Hervé Renard. He hasn’t played much this season for Al-Qadsiah either, with Koen Casteels getting the nod from Brendan Rodgers but he came in and did a job when needed and that will be the case at the World Cup too.

players.name: Mohamed Kanno
players.bio: This leggy and dynamic box-to-box midfielder has held his own at the all-conquering Al-Hilal since 2017 and is still going strong. “With a bit more maturity, he could be a top-class player,” said the Al-Hilal coach in 2022, Ramón Díaz,. Still loses his head from time to time but a succession of national team coaches, especially Hervé Renard, have selected the player born in Khobar, on the east coast, just across the bay from Bahrain as they appreciate his energy and work-rate. Also possesses a fierce shot.

players.name: Moteb Al-Harbi
players.bio: “Al-Harbi has everything needed to become a top-class left-back,” Hervé Renard said when selecting him for an international debut back in 2021. Many agreed. After impressing for Al-Shabab, playing over 100 games, scoring five and getting forward to record 12 assists, he became the most expensive Saudi Arabian player in 2024 when Al-Hilal reportedly paid over £20m for his services – though there was controversy when Al-Nassr claimed the player had actually agreed to join them. Has had a solid season for the Blues but has yet to make his mark internationally. That could be about to change.

players.name: Jehad Thakri
players.bio: It didn’t take long for Brendan Rodgers, after he arrived to take over Al-Qadsiah, to offer Thakri a new contract. The 25-year-old has earned plenty of praise from central defensive partner Fernando Nacho in the past season and he has become more confident on the ball and more active out of possession. Only made his international debut last year but quickly earned the trust of the Saudi Arabia coach, Hervé Renard, and played in the crucial game against Iraq that clinched qualification, barely putting a foot wrong and proving he has the mindset for the big occasion.

players.name: Mohammed Abu Al-Shamat
players.bio: Wahid Abu Al-Shamat must be a proud father. Not quite able to make the footballing grade, he ended up working in the public sector but still found time to ensure that his twin sons had the opportunity to give it their best shot. For a while, he trained them himself, before enrolling them in a Jeddah academy. That was just the start. Having impressed at Al-Qadsiah, Mohammed Al-Shamat was recently the subject of a number of bids from Simone Inzaghi’s Al-Hilal, going up to as much as £10m. However, Brendan Rodgers and Al-Qadsiah were not interested and rightly so, as Abu Al-Shamat, has become one of the best full-backs in the country.

players.name: Angus Gunn
players.bio: Gunn took the unusual step of publicly admitting he may have made a transfer mistake, having joined Forest on a one-year deal from Norwich City last summer. By the time Gunn reached the October international camp, he was yet to play any club football. “I went into a situation at the club where I thought I'd be getting, obviously not regular game time, but some game time in terms of cups and things like that," he said. “It's not really materialised, which I'm disappointed about.” Gunn’s first domestic action of the season came from the bench on 1 February. Gunn has, though, routinely performed well for Scotland despite his club inaction. Gunn’s father is the Norwich City legend and former Scotland custodian Bryan Gunn. Earlier in his career, there was a theory Angus had eyes on the England squad. “His name is Angus Fraser James Gunn – he was always destined to play for Scotland,” insisted Bryan.

players.name: Aaron Hickey
players.bio: A player pursued by Bayern Munich after bursting onto the scene as a 16-year-old at Hearts. Hickey has a tendency to do things his own way. Having moved from the Hearts academy to that of Celtic, he returned to Tynecastle through belief he would have more opportunity of first team football in Edinburgh. Hickey was proven right then and proved right once more; he snubbed the Bavarian giants to sign for Bologna, where his rise continued. But for injuries, it is hardly a sign of disrespect to Brentford to suggest he would be at one of the Premier League’s bigger clubs. Equally comfortable on left or right foot. Has emerged as the best option for Scotland at right-back. A tenacious, now powerful defender with technique which almost feels wasted in defence.

players.name: Andy Robertson
players.bio: Robertson remains in close pursuit of Kenny Dalglish’s record of 102 caps for Scotland. The left-back’s story is widely known; having been released by Celtic, he stacked shelves in Asda and played as an amateur for Queen’s Park. “Life is rubbish at this age with no money,” posted Robertson on social media in 2012. Seven years later, he was a Champions League winner. Robertson’s career took off at Dundee United and Hull City but was placed on another level by Jürgen Klopp. Captaincy of Scotland initially looked a heavy burden for Robertson but he has looked comfortable in the role for a long time now. One of a number of Scotland players who may privately sense this marks their final World Cup opportunity.

players.name: Scott McTominay
players.bio: Did he launch himself 8ft, 18ft or 88ft into the air before planting an outrageous overhead kick beyond the reach of Kasper Schmeichel? Who cares? McTominay’s opening goal against Denmark set the tone for a remarkable night as Scotland ended almost three decades of World Cup hurt. Alex McLeish’s greatest gift to his nation during a second spell as the Scotland manager was convincing McTominay to don navy blue. Without McLeish’s refusal to take no for an answer, the Lancaster-born McTominay could be an England player by now. Instead, he is idolised by the Tartan Army. There is lauding, too, in Naples, after he was part of the 2024-25 Scudetto winning squad. “If Diego was God, then Scott is Jesus,” insisted Diego Maradona Jnr, in reference to the status both his father and McTominay have secured in the eyes of Napoli fans. McTominay will arrive at the World Cup with a point to prove, having disappointed in Euro 2024.

players.name: Grant Hanley
players.bio: Doubts were raised about Hanley’s World Cup involvement after he missed March’s friendlies when recovering from injury. Yet the centre-half has been a regular under Steve Clarke and has rarely let his country down: he started two of Scotland’s three games in Euro 2024. Hanley was central to a rumpus early in the last campaign. Motherwell’s manager Jens Berthel Askou was accused of disrespect towards the veteran after Hibs were defeated 2-0 in Lanarkshire. Askou said Motherwell prevailed after “cutting off their supply from their midfielders leaving Hanley to bring the ball forward.” Cue suggestions Askou had needlessly highlighted Hanley’s shortcomings.

players.name: Kieran Tierney
players.bio: The stunning, left-footed strike that nudged Scotland ahead of Denmark on the night of World Cup qualification felt hugely appropriate for Tierney. He remains a factor on the big stage. There should be no shedding of tears for a player who has served Celtic – twice – and Arsenal but it is undeniable that physical problems have prevented Tierney from sitting among Scotland’s all-time greats. How he will relish a World Cup. Tierney once ran through crowds outside Hampden to join Scottish Cup final celebrations with his Celtic team-mates. He had suffered a broken jaw and teeth early in the game but raced back from hospital to play a part in the party.

players.name: John McGinn
players.bio:
It remains extraordinary in a way that McGinn has forged a top level career at all. In 2015, a spot of high jinks at St Mirren’s training ground saw team-mate Steven Thompson throw a spiked pole at McGinn. The pole cut McGinn deeply and tore a muscle. “There was a lot of blood,” McGinn said. “I just remember looking down and being glad the pole came back out.”
Whether through fear over the impact of that incident or otherwise, there were a shortage of takers when McGinn decided to leave Paisley. Hibernian took a chance and the rest is history. McGinn excelled in Edinburgh and has become a fan favourite at Aston Villa. The Tartan Army hold the midfielder nicknamed “Meatball” in similarly lofty esteem.

players.name: Tyler Fletcher
players.bio: A horror moment for Billy Gilmour triggered the stuff of dreams for Fletcher. The midfielder had been brought in among five youngsters to boost training numbers as Scotland prepared for their friendly against Curaçao. Gilmour sustained a knee injury in the first half of that game, ruling the Napoli player out of the World Cup. Fletcher had impressed in training and during the second half of the Curaçao game to usurp Lennon Miller, Andy Irving and Connor Barron when named as Gilmour’s replacement. The son of the former Scotland captain Darren Fletcher, Tyler’s brother Jack also plays for Manchester United but represents England at youth level. Could Tyler’s elevation trigger an international rethink from Jack?

players.name: Lyndon Dykes
players.bio: Scotland have lost players born in the country to other nations – James McCarthy, Aiden McGeady and Harry Souttar are examples - but Dykes took the opposite approach. He lived in Australia until visiting Dumfries, from where his parents hail, with Queen of the South sufficiently impressed during a prolific spell in their under-age teams to bring Dykes back to Scotland permanently. He later had a straight choice between representing Australia and Scotland. Dykes “went with my heart and my gut.” A horrible striker to play against and one who has delivered big moments for Scotland. Will now seek to make up for lost time after being devastated to miss Euro 2024 through injury.

players.name: Che Adams
players.bio: Emulated Scottish icon Denis Law by scoring his 10th goal for Torino last October. Steve Clarke believes Adams is underappreciated by observers of the Scotland team, given the forward’s level of selfless running. The movement of Adams is key to the Scots having a strong scoring threat from midfield. Adams played for England at Under-20 level and was also eligible to represent Antigua. He committed to Scotland shortly before Euro 2020 after long-term speculation about his international future and is likely to be Clarke’s first choice as centre forward in the World Cup. Adams backed Scotland’s fans after full-time booing when Belarus were narrowly seen off during qualifying. “I think they were right,” he said. “It was some poor decision-making, not concentrating in key moments of the game. We need to do better.”

players.name: Ryan Christie
players.bio: Christie’s father, Charlie, was a hugely talented midfielder who never played on the stage his ability deserved despite a brief stint at Celtic. Charlie and Ryan stand out in the domain in hailing from Inverness, a Scottish city which has curiously offered little by way of blue chip footballers over several decades. A mainstay in Steve Clarke’s squads and a useful goal threat, Christie’s own career at Celtic looked over before circumstances afforded him an opportunity he gleefully seized in late 2018. Three years later, the Premier League beckoned. Was memorably reduced to tears during a live television interview as Scotland reached Euro 2020. Charlie, so taken by the depiction of that moment in a Guardian cartoon strip, ordered a framed copy for his son’s birthday.

players.name: Liam Kelly
players.bio: Took a risk when swapping Motherwell for Rangers in the summer of 2024, given he was trading guaranteed first team football for a likely understudy position at Ibrox. Kelly has indeed played second fiddle to Jack Butland but has remained involved in the Scotland scene. He is a hugely popular character within the Scotland dressing room. He was never likely to play in Euro 2024 but earned a spot in the finals squad, as again now, partly through the role he plays in squad camaraderie. Kelly’s two brothers, Sean and Shay, also play league football in Scotland. Liam’s Scotland debut, against France in a friendly at the age of 27, was an emotional moment for all concerned.

players.name: Jack Hendry
players.bio: Hendry was tipped for great things by Brendan Rodgers back in 2018. “He has the quality to play for Celtic and the international team for the next 10 years,” said Rodgers. “His profile suits the highest level. He's quick and can defend and it's important you can deal with the ball.” Hendry’s path has not been exactly as Rodgers predicted but a move to Saudi Arabia has not diminished Steve Clarke’s faith in the centre-back. Rodgers’ words seemed to resonate with Steven Gerrard, who took Hendry to the club. Hendry had earlier excelled in the Belgian league. When Scotland opened the last Euros, Hendry was in the heart of the defence. While international opportunity has been limited since, it would be a surprise if Hendry does not feature at all in the World Cup.

players.name: Ross Stewart
players.bio: His nickname among fans is undeniably the finest in the Scotland squad: The Loch Ness Drogba. We will never know for sure what part fate played in Stewart’s World Cup call-up. His end of season form with Southampton means he merited his place on the flight to North America but the injury sustained by Tommy Conway, who had returned to the Scotland squad in March, seemed to accelerate Stewart’s chances. The Southampton man had not featured for the national team since 2022. A decade ago, he was a non-league player in Ayrshire. His ascent since leaving St Mirren in 2018 has been sharp despite serious injury woes of his own. Via Ross County and Sunderland, Stewart became a £10m player. At one point, Albion Rovers bought him for just £1,500.

players.name: John Souttar
players.bio: Souttar shared a flat with Andy Robertson and Ryan Gauld as the trio were youth players at Dundee United. All three have gone on to impressive things thereafter. Souttar does not remember the World Cup of 1998 but knows his father and other family members travelled to France to follow Scotland. Souttar’s brother, Harry, has already featured in a World Cup for Australia, having switched international allegiance. John’s off field interests include a coffee shop business. He has recovered from two significant Achilles injuries to prolong his football career. Souttar was at the centre of a bizarre row in 2018 after his manager at Hearts, Craig Levein, claimed Dundee United “nearly ruined” the player by deploying him in different positions. United hit back with a fierce statement saying they were “very disappointed” by Levein’s words.

players.name: Dominic Hyam
players.bio: Born in Leuchars, Fife, where his father Steve was stationed with the Royal Air Force, Hyam was in England before the age of two. “I feel Scottish,” he says. “I've got more Scottish than English in me because my dad's mum is from Dundee originally and mum's dad is from Glasgow. So I'm definitely more Scottish than English.” Waited until 27 to make his Scotland debut but did so in hugely dramatic circumstances. Having trailed Norway in a crucial Euros qualifier in Oslo, Scotland scored twice in the closing four minutes to claim a vital three points. Hyam was thrown on in stoppage time to repel the desperate Norwegians. A consistent, reliable defender who has been trusted by umpteen managers; hence spells spanning more than 100 games at each of Coventry City and Blackburn Rovers.

players.name: Ben Gannon-Doak
players.bio: Gannon-Doak recently revealed how he reconnected with religion after leaving home as a 16-year-old to join Liverpool. “I think God just keeps you grounded and humble because he always tells us that we wouldn't have any of this without him,” he said. “I heavily rely on God to keep me strong.” Gannon-Doak excelled in Celtic’s youth system before being coaxed to Anfield. Jürgen Klopp was a fan. However, a combination of injuries and Klopp’s exit stalled the winger’s progress. He then opted to kickstart his career at Bournemouth. Steve Clarke has sought to temper excitement around Gannon-Doak but the noise is understandable. This player can be a genuine difference-maker for the Scots. He has directness and electric pace, which on one Nations League evening against Croatia visibly terrified £77m full back Josko Gvardiol.

players.name: George Hirst
players.bio: Hirst’s father, David, made just three appearances for England despite his excellent scoring prowess at Sheffield Wednesday. Alex Ferguson tried and failed to sign Hirst Snr for Manchester United on several occasions. George’s hopes of emulating David at Hillsborough failed to materialise but he has found a home at Portman Road and was involved in back-to-back promotion campaigns as Ipswich were catapulted from League One to the Premier League. “He's a tough character and he's done well for us every season in every division,” said the Ipswich manager, Kieran McKenna. “George has has been a massive part of the success here, an incredible servant for the football club and is still a really important person in the leadership group.”

players.name: Lewis Ferguson
players.bio: Ferguson would likely be plying his trade for an Italian giant by now – Juventus and the Milan clubs had credible interest – but for the serious knee injury sustained in April of 2024, which kept him out of the Euros of that summer. “The first thing I asked was ‘Does this mean I’m missing the Euros?’ and they said yes,” Ferguson said. “That was a difficult moment. I’m not an emotional guy but I just sat there thinking that was my chance to play in a major tournament and it was gone. It had been something I’d always dreamed about so it was difficult. But here we are now.” The following year, he emulated Graeme Souness as Scotsmen to win the Coppa Italia. The Bologna captain hails from a family of classy midfielders; namely father Derek and uncle Barry. A combative midfielder who provides a goal threat Scotland are yet to properly tap in to.

players.name: Lawrence Shankland
players.bio: Activated a break clause in his contract at Hearts a week after their season ended, allowing him to join Rangers on a free transfer. As Hearts brushed shoulders with the title, Shankland as the captain was in superb form. Twelve months earlier, there was debate about whether or not the Edinburgh club were doing the right thing in retaining the striker. Derek McInnes, who had released a young Shankland at Aberdeen, subsequently rejuvenated the same player’s career. Scotland’s finest penalty box threat, whose all-round game has improved with age. What Shankland lacks in pace he atones for with game intelligence. He was the scorer of the “other” goal on that famous night against Denmark. Shankland’s two yard tap-in was, however, every bit as vital as its more blistering contemporaries as Scotland qualified for the World Cup.

players.name: Craig Gordon
players.bio: The World Cup feels an appropriate end to an extraordinary career that in Scotland terms began in May 2004. The intervening years saw Gordon move for a British transfer record for a goalkeeper (£9m in 2007) and encounter serious injury which looked like curtailing his career. Gordon bounced back to prolong his career; winning a bounty of honours at Celtic, returning to boyhood club Hearts and prolonging his international involvement for much longer than anybody reasonably anticipated. His insane save for Hearts at Dundee in January will go down not only as his finest but one of the best Scottish domestic football has witnessed in living memory. “That is a moment I will treasure,” Gordon said. So, too, one of the few boxes he had left to tick; a World Cup place.

players.name: Nathan Patterson
players.bio: The World Cup marks the start of a crucial summer for Patterson. Huge things were expected of the full-back when he swapped Rangers for Everton in 2022 but his career has stalled on Merseyside. Patterson needs a transfer and regular football to belatedly build on earlier promise. Changes of Everton manager and a series of injuries have hampered Patterson since his £10m switch. “I’ve experienced a lot of ups and downs,” he says. “Quite a lot of downs, to be honest, in terms of injuries.” When at Rangers, Patterson was one of a group of players fined by police for attending a flat party amid Covid restrictions. The Scottish FA issued a sanction of their own for the incident.

players.name: Kenny McLean
players.bio: It is difficult to imagine McLean doing anything at this World Cup, in fact during the remainder of his career, that matches events of 18 November at Hampden Park. The Scots were bound for the tournament proper but a nation bit fingernails as McLean strode forward during a counter attack, spotted Kasper Schmeichel off his line and unleashed a shot from halfway that brought the roof off the famous old venue. McLean had topped off a night of epic drama with the goal of his life. This was just reward for one of Steve Clarke’s unsung heroes. McLean does, however, have previous for stepping up on the big occasion; he scored the winning penalties in separate matches as Scotland reached Euro 2020. An unfussy, low-maintenance midfielder held in high regard by Clarke and team-mates alike.

players.name: Anthony Ralston
players.bio: A slightly controversial pick for Scotland in the sense that he has recently and frequently been nudged into the position of third choice right back for Celtic. The player who has edged in front of him, Colby Donovan, is also Scottish. Steve Clarke’s persistence with Ralston is a nod to the continuity the manager insists upon within Scotland squads. A boyhood Celtic fan, Ralston’s career at the club was revived by Ange Postecoglou. He had been nicknamed the Bricklayer, the Brickie or the Holy Brickie by Celtic’s followers; a scenario Ralston took in good spirits. "The fans seem to love it, I find it funny and am happy to go along with it,” he said. “Some of the memes are a good laugh.”

players.name: Findlay Curtis
players.bio: Steve Clarke’s appearance at the March meeting of Kilmarnock and Hearts proved vital for Curtis. The Rangers loanee produced a fine performance on the left wing for Kilmarnock, with Clarke calling up Curtis just days later for friendly matches. “I was bursting with pride and told my mum and dad,” Curtis recalled. “My mum started crying.” Scotland are short of wide attacking options, which undoubtedly helped Curtis’s case, with Clarke also keen to demonstrate he has eyes on the next generation after Harvey Barnes rejected Scotland’s overtures. Curtis’s international involvement came late; he only appeared for Scotland’s Under-21 team for the first time last year. Clarke has not unreasonably compared Curtis to Ben Gannon-Doak. The likelihood is Curtis will be given a serious shot at first-team football with Rangers next season. He certainly should be.

players.name: Scott McKenna
players.bio: One of a number of Scotland players of this era who have opted to take roads less travelled. With his time at Nottingham Forest coming to an end, McKenna took a loan move to Copenhagen. Stints at Las Palmas and Dinamo Zagreb have followed. “He’s like a grumpy old man. I call him mi abuelo, my grandad,” said McKenna’s former Las Palmas team-mate Oli McBurnie. An imposing, left-sided centre-half with more pace than he is often given credit for, McKenna has arguably been underused in the Scottish setup. A Zagreb official insisted Dinamo had rejected bids in excess of £10m for McKenna in January. A decade ago, McKenna was trading a loan at Alloa Athletic for the same at Ayr United.

players.name: Yehvann Diouf
players.bio: Calm and excellent at claiming crosses, Diouf has been putting Ligue 1 defenders at ease for a number of years. He had to be patient to become first choice at Reims and is now excelling as Nice’s No 1. Born in the Parisian suburbs, the 26-year-old switched his allegiance to Senegal last year after representing France at youth level. Diouf rose to internet fame at the Africa Cup of Nations in January after footage of him protecting Édouard Mendy’s towel from a number of Moroccan ball boys behind the Senegal goal went viral. In the pouring rain in Rabat he and the ball boys were sometimes seen wrestling for the towel on the ground inside the penalty area while the game was going on. “It was completely surreal,” Diouf said.

players.name: Mamadou Sarr
players.bio: Calm and elegant on the ball, Sarr is the next big thing in the Senegal defence. In choosing to represent Senegal over his native France, the 20-year-old is following in the footsteps of his father, the former Senegal centre-back Pape Sarr who played at the 2002 World Cup. A product of the Lyon academy, Mamadou earned himself a reputation at Strasbourg before joining fellow BlueCo-owned club Chelsea, where he continued to work under his former manager Liam Rosenior. Seen by many as the successor to Kalidou Koulibaly at international level, Sarr deputised for the injured and suspended captain by playing the full 120 minutes of January’s Afcon final against Morocco, just a couple of months after his first senior cap. “Knowing I can play at Afcon makes me think I can play in the Premier League,” the defender said.

players.name: Kalidou Koulibaly
players.bio: Senegal’s regular captain is one of the most respected defenders on the planet. Before his spells at Chelsea and now Al-Hilal, he spent eight years at the heart of Napoli’s defence, winning the Coppa Italia and becoming a club icon. He made the choice to represent Senegal, the country of his parents, in 2015 after representing his native France at youth level. After his first World Cup in 2018 the “Minister of Defence” was Senegal’s rock at the back as they won their first African title in 2022, when the captain refused to let go of the trophy for several hours afterwards. Wearing the armband has become a sacred duty for the 34-year-old: “When you wear the shirt, you carry the hopes of 18 million people. You don’t have the right to be tired, nor to be afraid.” After injury problems towards the end of the season in Saudi Arabia, he has proved his fitness just in time.

players.name: Abdoulaye Seck
players.bio: Another to benefit from the Senegal to Norway pathway as a young player, Seck started his career in Europe with Hønefoss. His time with Royal Antwerp, where he won the Belgian Cup, brought him to the attention of the Senegal selectors once more – he won two caps as a teenager while playing domestically – and he was part of the squad that won the 2021 Africa Cup of Nations. Having featured in European competitions for Israel’s Maccabi Haifa, Seck is an experienced and trusted back-up option should Pape Thiaw need him this summer.

players.name: Idrissa ‘Gana’ Gueye
players.bio: Gueye has been the metronome in Senegal’s midfield for more than a decade. In that time he has earned a reputation as a combative presence in the Premier League across two spells at Everton. The Diambars academy product came away from a three-year spell at Paris St-Germain with plenty of winners’ medals, returning to Everton in 2022. He was sent off in a Premier League game against Manchester United at the start of this season after striking his teammate Michael Keane in the face. Still, he remains a reliable player in the Senegal midfield and his 100+ caps and Afcon medals command respect. He arrives at this World Cup at the age of 36, 15 years after his international debut. “I don’t do anything special. I just stick to my routines, follow a balanced diet and always try to get a good night’s sleep,” he told Fifa in 2024.

players.name: Pathé Ciss
players.bio: Ciss learned his trade in Europe’s lower tiers before making the grade in La Liga with Rayo Vallecano. After spells in Portugal’s second division, the midfielder arrived in Spain’s second tier with Fuenlabrada. He is respected at Rayo for his toughness and reading of the game, and is a reliable figure in tough moments. Called up for the first time by Senegal just before the last World Cup, Ciss started the tournament in Qatar on the bench. By the time Aliou Cissé’s side faced England in the last 16, he was a starter. If Pape Thiaw has any injury problems in defence this summer, Ciss is being tipped as a possible stand-in centre-back.

players.name: Assane Diao
players.bio: After bursting on to the scene in La Liga with Real Betis, Diao chose to pursue his career in Serie A with Como. He is a powerful winger with a rasping shot and brings freshness to the squad. Born in Senegal, Diao moved to Spain at the age of three and represented them at various youth levels but his decision to play for the Lions of Teranga confirms the attractiveness the younger generation are seeing in playing for the country of their roots. After a hugely impactful first few months under Cesc Fàbregas the 20-year-old is still finding consistency in one of Europe’s top leagues.

players.name: Lamine Camara
players.bio: The rise of the 2024 African Young Player of the Year has been startling. In 2023 he won the African Nations Championship, was named best player at the Under-20 Africa Cup of Nations and sealed a move from the Génération Foot academy to Europe. After a remarkable goal from inside his own half at the Stade Louis II in his first season with Metz, Monaco shelled out €15m for his signature. The midfielder has continued to prove himself in one of Europe’s top five leagues and made his Champions League debut in 2024, yet his head remains firmly on his shoulders. “I still feel like the kid who played on those pitches in Diouloulou,” he has said. The 22-year-old has already shone at two Afcons and is the tempo-setter in the Senegal midfield.

players.name: Bamba Dieng
players.bio: The Marseille academy product, now at Lorient, is a player who relies on instinct. Potent from range, the striker was one of the revelations of the 2022 Africa Cup of Nations, scoring in the Last 16 and a penalty in the final shootout victory against Egypt. Despite dry patches with his clubs, he has always found his best form with the national team. Pape Thiaw will likely utilise him off the bench this summer but he can change the game in one turn of pace. He comes into the tournament averaging around a goal every other game for Lorient in France this season. He is out of contract in the summer and is said to be attracting interest from Germany and Spain.

players.name: Sadio Mané
players.bio: Mané is one of the most decorated African players in history. But before winning the Champions League and Premier League with Liverpool, or the Bundesliga with Bayern Munich, he had to batter down some doors. He comes from Bambali, a village from where becoming a pro footballer seemed impossible, and left for an opportunity in Dakar wearing a wrecked pair of football boots – the source of mockery from one coach in his first sessions. Mané’s response became famous: “I am here with the best that I have.” Now a two-time African Player of the Year and the driving force behind Senegal’s first Afcon triumph in 2022, the forward has spent millions building hospitals and schools back home. “I don’t need 10 Ferraris; I want my people to receive a bit of what life has given me,” he has said. A level-headed captain amid the chaos at Afcon earlier this year, the 34-year-old persuaded his teammates to return to the pitch in the final against Morocco.

players.name: Nicolas Jackson
players.bio: Jackson went to the last World Cup without a single cap for Senegal – a purple patch in front of goal for Unai Emery’s Villarreal later in the season earned him his big move to Chelsea. The Gambia-born striker took his first steps in football at Casa Sport Zinguinchor in Senegal and now he is a Bundesliga winner while on loan at Bayern Munich. He is the modern striker, with disruptive runs in behind his main strength, but can also play on the left wing. In and out of the team at the Africa Cup of Nations in January, the 24-year-old is considered the future of Senegal’s frontline. Can he be the present in North America?

players.name: Cherif Ndiaye
players.bio: This is a first World Cup for the globe-trotting 30-year-old striker. His career has taken him from Dakar to Turkey via Belgium, Croatia, China and Serbia. During a prolific spell at Red Star Belgrade, he became the first player since 1968 to score a hat-trick against Partizan in the fierce Eternal derby. “The matches against Partizan are special,” he told FootSenegal. “The energy that you get from the stands, the historic importance of the game – it gave me extra strength.” Experiences like these mean Pape Thiaw can trust Ndiaye to keep his head in the pressure moments that come with tournament football. He will have to wait for his chance from the bench.

players.name: Iliman Ndiaye
players.bio: Having been a bit-part player for Senegal in 2022, Ndiaye arrives at his second World Cup having found his feet in the Premier League at Everton. The former Sheffield United and Marseille winger, who was at Boreham Wood’s academy as a youngster, is a popular figure among Senegal fans for his eye-catching runs with the ball. He was central to the run to the Afcon final at the start of the year. As a child, Ndiaye used to practice his freestyle skills on the beaches of Dakar before honing his technical ability in England’s lower leagues. “My football comes from the street.
