While not particularly tall – he measures 1.79m  - Oyarzabal wears size 12 boots . “People are curious but you get used to it,” he says. “I’ve had big feet since I was very young.”

players.name: Pau Cubarsí
players.bio: At just 19 years old, Cubarsí has become the leader of Barcelona’s defence. The departure of Iñigo Martínez last summer gave the youngster a new status within Hansi Flick’s squad: he stopped being the young apprentice learning off the senior players beside him and started marshalling the defence himself. It has not always gone to plan, a red card against Atlético Madrid in the Champions League a case in point. But his stature in the selección has changed as well, with Luis de la Fuente now considering him a possible starter. Now the coach must decide whether to play him alongside the experience of Aymeric Laporte or gamble with the young pairing of Cubarsí and Dean Huijsen. Comes into the World Cup having just won his second La Liga title.

players.name: Unai Simón
players.bio: One of Luis de la Fuente’s biggest achievements has been the consistency in goal – despite not being so sure about Simón at youth level. Antonio Sivera got the nod in the first game of the 2019 Under-21 Euros, which Spain lost to Italy. Simón started the next few games as De la Fuente’s side went on to win the trophy. There have been few doubts at senior level. Simón saved two penalties in the shootout victory over Croatia in the 2023 Nations League final. “I’m especially pleased for Unai, who has been mistreated for a long time. I hope people recognise how valuable he is,” De la Fuente said at the time. Simón was between the posts the following summer when Spain won Euro 2024, playing through the pain after rupturing a ligament in his wrist. For the moment he’s still the No 1 despite the rise of David Raya and Joan García.

players.name: Marc Cucurella
players.bio: The left-back has become a surprise leader of this Spain team. He was key to the Euro 2024 triumph, setting up Mikel Oyarzabal’s winner in the final, and is now one of the main figures in the buildup to the World Cup. At Chelsea he has gone from being a target of ridicule for his €60m transfer fee to one of the most respected players at the club – even during a difficult season his starting place has been undisputed. His stamina on the wing and ferocity in challenging for the ball have made him such an important player for Luis de la Fuente too. Last year Cucurella and his wife Claudia opened up to the media about their family life and the challenges of being a parent to his autistic son.

players.name: Víctor Muñoz
players.bio: Muñoz is the surprise inclusion in Luis de la Fuente’s squad. The 22-year-old winger has enjoyed a breakthrough season at Osasuna after leaving Real Madrid, who still own 50% of his rights. Having clocked 35km/h this campaign, he is one of the fastest players in La Liga and makes very dangerous runs in behind. A direct dribbler, Muñoz could make a real difference off the bench for Spain this summer. He scored on his first cap in March, a 3-0 win against Serbia. “It would be a dream,” he said after that game when asked about the possibility of going to the World Cup, “but I’m concentrating on the day to day, on working hard, improving, enjoying the process and, if it has to happen, I’m sure it’ll happen.”

players.name: Borja Iglesias
players.bio: Iglesias didn’t get his big break until the age of 25 when he scored 17 top-flight goals in a season for Espanyol. Real Betis shelled out €28m to sign him in 2019 and he established himself as one of La Liga’s best strikers, winning the Copa del Rey and making his international debut in 2022. While in Seville he became great friends with the likes of Aitor Ruibal and Héctor Bellerín, appearing alongside them to promote social causes and call out homophobia in football. After returning from a short spell at Bayer Leverkusen with a Bundesliga medal, Iglesias is now enjoying a new lease of life at Celta. Spain need a striker of his profile – able to hold up the ball with his back to play. “El Panda” isn’t expected to start at the World Cup due to the form of Mikel Oyarzabal but is a very good back-up option for Luis de la Fuente to have.

players.name: Jacob Widell Zetterström
players.bio: Widell Zetterström took a sabbatical in 2020 after suffering two concussions in quick succession. “When things were at their worst, I didn’t dare to believe that a future in football could exist,” he says. He returned the following year, became Djurgården’s No 1 and, as he left for Derby in 2025, he said farewell to the fans with tears in his eyes. “There are a lot of emotions, but it is also a dream for me to play in England,” he said. Widell Zetterström is not your usual footballer. Among other things, he has studied medicine and attended the top-ranked Stockholm School of Economics. He also enjoys reading: “People always raise eyebrows when I pick up a thick book on the team bus, but to me it's just funny,” he says.

players.name: Gustaf Lagerbielke
players.bio: Has a somewhat unusual routine with his national team friends Noel Törnqvist and Benjamin Nygren: “We go to the beach, look at the stars, and discuss life’s big questions. It gets deep – from starting XI discussions to conspiracy theories, whether the moon landing was fake or not, and so on.” The surname Lagerbielke belongs to a noble family with its own coat of arms and Gustaf himself holds the title of “friherre” (baron). “For me, it’s nothing special, but I understand people find it interesting – it’s not very common in football,” he says. He accidentally sprayed champagne on Sweden’s prime minister, Ulf Kristersson, after Sweden had secured World Cup qualification with the win over Poland. “He apologised, but he didn’t need to,”Kristersson says.

players.name: Victor Lindelöf
players.bio: Nicknamed “The Iceman” for his composure on the pitch - a sobriquet he earned during his years at Old Trafford. Despite several challenging seasons, Lindelöf is as high as 56th on the all-time appearance list for Manchester United with 284 games, not bad for the boy from Västerås. Played as a right-back when Sweden became Under-21 European champions in 2015 – a tournament he wasn’t even supposed to play in. Lindelöf was not in the original squad but was called up as a late replacement for the injured Emil Krafth. A year later, he was a starter for the national team and Benfica. “It happened quickly, but I want to play at the highest level,” he says. Praised for his positive attitude by José Mourinho, among others, but he has had a fractious relationship with the Swedish media.

players.name: Isak Hien
players.bio: Began as a forward but at Vasalund an under-19 coach converted him into a centre-back. “The coach said he would play me as a centre-back whether I wanted to or not,” Hien recalls. “I was a bit surprised at first, but he turned out to be right.” After the successful position change, Hien moved from Sweden’s third tier to top-flight Djurgården, where he broke through as a powerful defender with “superb physicality,” according to his then coach, Thomas Lagerlöf. “He’s like a Ferrari engine in a small car.” Fun fact: Isak has a younger brother, Leon, who now plays for Djurgården and was also converted from a forward to a centre-back.

players.name: Gabriel Gudmundsson
players.bio: The Leeds full-back is the son of Niklas Gudmundsson, who played for Blackburn and Ipswich in the 1990s. Gabriel was coached by his father all the way up to the under-19 team at Halmstad: “You have to accept some criticism at home, but he was very good. I learned a lot of valuable things from him,” the 27-year-old says. Despite coming from a football family, Gabriel has no interest in watching the game on TV when he is not playing. He completely switches off: “I feel better that way, and it helps me stay focused.” Gudmundsson is articulate and polite with the media but is unlikely to create any headlines with what he says. After leaving Halmstad in 2019, he played for Groningen and Lille before joining Leeds in 2025.

players.name: Herman Johansson
players.bio: The path to the national team has been long and winding for the likable wing-back. At one point, Johansson considered walking away from the game. At the time his mother did what every self-respecting parent would do – she bribed her son to continue. “Mum gave me 20 krona (now £1.60) per training session,” he said. Johansson was a late bloomer and at 22 he still worked full time as a teaching assistant while playing in Sweden’s third and fourth tiers with Friska Viljor in the northern city of Örnsköldsvik. A few years later he got his breakthrough at top-flight side Mjällby – where he won the Allsvensken last year. Having flown in from Dallas for only his second cap, he battled through jet lag to make a huge contribution to Sweden’s playoff win against Ukraine. “I thought I’d be able to adjust my sleep schedule pretty easily,” he said, “but I had to take sleeping pills.”

players.name: Lucas Bergvall
players.bio: The Tottenham midfielder was seen as a super talent from a very young age. Throughout his upbringing, clips were often shared of him dominating games and when his Brommapojkarna youth team won the Gothia Cup, they were regarded as one of the best sides in Europe at that age group. “It was around 2019 [when Bergvall was 13] that people started noticing me and my team. Our BP team, with players born in 2006, won pretty much everything. We were often dominant.” Bergvall simply had to get used to the attention. Barcelona came close to signing him from Djurgården but it was Tottenham who won the race. “I think they [Barcelona] were extremely disappointed that he chose Tottenham,” says the Djurgården sporting director, Bosse Andersson. After a sensational 2024-25 season for Spurs, this campaign was interrupted by a knee operation but he returned to make a crucial substitute appearance for Sweden in the World Cup play-off against Poland in March.

players.name: Daniel Svensson
players.bio: Svensson is still something of an unknown in Sweden, despite playing for the national team and Borussia Dortmund. That is partly because he left Brommapojkarna for Nordsjælland in Denmark at a young age. He may now play in the Champions League for a top Bundesliga side but said last year that he can still walk around in Sweden without being noticed. “No one has come up to me so far, at least,” he says. In Dortmund, he has been praised for his tireless running on the left flank, as well as his creativity. His coach at Dortmund, Niko Kovac, says: “He’s like Gyro Gearloose [an anthropomorphic chicken who is part of the Donald Duck character set]. He’s a player who doesn’t worry about running 13km every three days.” In the national team, Svensson is close friends with Roony Bardghji and they share an interest in fragrances. “He’s made me more interested in it. It’s fun.”

players.name: Alexander Isak
players.bio: “It was magical, they sang for me.” On 21 September 2016, Alexander Isak turned 17 and became a derby hero. He scored two goals against fierce rivals Djurgården (in a 3-0 win) and was serenaded with a birthday song by the supporters. A super talent who  has gone from strength to strength and last year became the most expensive player in Premier League history. Despite being at the top, is described as a calm and humble person who hasn’t forgotten his roots. Thania Guanoluisa, his Spanish teacher in school, received a call from him when he was playing for Real Sociedad: “Alexander said: ‘Thania, it was you who gave me the foundations in Spanish.’ I was so happy that he remembered and appreciated it.” Enraged Newcastle fans after going on strike to force a move to Liverpool and his first campaign at Anfield has been one to forget because of injuries. Remains a key player for Sweden regardless.

players.name: Benjamin Nygren
players.bio: Nygren scored three goals and made three assists in four matches for IFK Göteborg as he exploded on to the scene at the age of 17 in the spring of 2019. That was all it took before he agreed to join Genk in a record transfer. “It’s crazy. Going from going to school every day and living at home to moving abroad - away from family, friends, girlfriend. From everything,” he said. The road to the national team was bumpier and he only made his senior debut last year. In his second game for Sweden, against Northern Ireland, he scored and managed to celebrate with his close friend Emil Holm, who also found the net on the night. “To experience this with a childhood friend… I almost have to pinch myself,” Holm said. Nygren is now a regular in the national team and, just like at Celtic, continues to deliver goals and assists.

players.name: Anthony Elanga
players.bio: Born in Malmö, he moved to England with his mother and sisters at the age of 11. “Mum always said that the opportunities were in England. I trusted what she said.” That proved true for Elanga, who joined Manchester United’s academy, broke into the first team, and even got his own chant - “Rhythm is a dancer, Anthony Elanga” - before moving on to Nottingham and Newcastle. Football runs in the family, as his father Joseph won the Swedish title with Malmö and played for Cameroon at the 1998 World Cup. Despite the 23-year age gap, father and son were once mistaken for each other when Anthony was with the national team in Malmö. “A lot of people wanted photos and things like that. They thought I was my dad. I said: ‘That’s not me.’” Elanga says he owes his father a lot and that his greatest strength isn’t his speed: “My main weapon is my mentality. I’m very grateful to have had a father with that mentality.”

players.name: Viktor Johansson
players.bio: Johansson’s career took off while on loan at Alfreton Town in the National League North – the sixth tier of English football – at the age of 18. “It was about getting stuck in and using your elbows,” he says. “It made me the goalkeeper I wanted to become.” His journey also took him to Rotherham, where he was nicknamed “The Viking” after taking a kick to the face. When he was about to leave the club, he treated fans to 600 beers, each with a personal message. It cost nearly £2,500. “A very small amount for everything they [the fans] have done for me and my family.” The beers were handed out at a supporters’ bar, where Johansson’s father had once jumped behind the bar and started pouring beer when visiting the city. “Dad was the king of the bar that evening – less so the morning after.” Often described as the “kindest” person in the national team.

players.name: Ken Sema
players.bio: The big surprise in the World Cup squad. The former Watford winger has been in and out of the Pafos starting XI in the Cypriot top flight and was not called up for the play-off matches for the tournament. However, Graham Potter knew the 32-year-old from their time together at Östersund and it is sweet revenge for the winger after being the last player to be left out of the 2018 World Cup squad. "That was the toughest choice for me," said the then national team coach Janne Andersson. His parents, Ndongala och Kia, grew up in Kinshasa, but fled the country after having their first child. Ken was born in Sweden together with his brother Maic, who has also been a professional footballer. Ken is a positive character who always seems to have a smile on his face. Once he went viral – after an interview on Watford's channels – where he discussed his stammer. "I have never seen it as an obstacle,” he says. “I am not the person who is shy or who stands in the background."

players.name: Hjalmar Ekdal
players.bio: Took an unconventional route via college football to elite football: “It’s unusual, but I don’t regret it for a second,” he says. In Sweden, he made the courageous decision to move between two Stockholm rivals – from Hammarby to Djurgården – in 2021. The Hammarby fans did not take kindly to the move and Ekdal endured some abuse. “I was never scared but it was a bit unpleasant.” The younger brother of Albin Ekdal, who played for Cagliari, Hamburg and Sampdoria among others and they came agonisingly close to making history for the national team. In Albin’s final international, Hjalmar was on the bench but never came on. Instead he had to comfort his older brother, who broke down in tears when he was taken off. “It was a very beautiful evening,” says the brothers’ father Lennart, a journalist and television presenter.

players.name: Carl Starfelt
players.bio: Recently received high praise from Williot Swedberg, a compatriot and teammate at Celta Vigo. “Carl might get annoyed now, but I think he’s a top five centre-back in La Liga this season,” the forward said. “Actually, maybe top three. He’s really underrated.” Starfelt spent years in Sweden’s lower leagues before breaking through. In 2021, Ange Postecoglou signed him for Celtic and said the Swede “fitted his style of football”. During Covid, Starfelt was staying at the same hotel as Brendan Rodgers. “He pulled me aside for a chat and then we had a coffee,” Starfelt says. “He seemed to know about me.” A few years later, Rodgers coached Starfelt at Celtic before his move to Spain.

players.name: Jesper Karlström
players.bio: A late bloomer who spent many seasons in the Swedish league before finally making his Serie A debut at the age of 29, after three and a half years at Lech Poznan in Poland. “I try to enjoy it as much as possible and really remind myself that this is my dream that I’m experiencing right now,” he says. What many people did not realise was that he was battling gambling addiction while playing for Djurgården. He had started betting at the age of 15 and it just got worse. “In the end I was so deep in the shit that I couldn’t get myself out,” he admitted in 2021. With help from people at DIF – and his family – he does not gamble any longer.  Karlström was important in Sweden qualifying for this year’s World Cup, but four years ago things were very different after conceding a penalty in the play-off against Poland. After he was abused online afterwards, the Swedish FA reported it to the police.

players.name: Viktor Gyökeres
players.bio: The Arsenal forward with the characteristic goal celebration has given the Batman villain Bane a revival and is currently Sweden’s brightest star. When he fired Sweden to the World Cup, fans celebrated by singing his name and mimicking his goal celebration. “It’s hard to put into words exactly how you feel right now. It’s extremely nice and a relief,” he said after scoring to eliminate Poland in the play-off final. The star has had a winding road to the top, with limited minutes at Brighton, loan spells, and eventually a successful period at Sporting before earning a dream move to Arsenal. Improved gradually after his £64m move to north London after enduring some heavy criticism during the first part of the 2025-26 season.

players.name: Yasin Ayari
players.bio: Ayari had his breakthrough year for Sweden last season, having impressed at Brighton under Fabian Hürzeler. But it could all have been very different with Tunisia competing for his services. Ayari had no doubts, though. “I was born in Sweden and feel Swedish, and Sweden is the country I want to represent,” he says. The technically gifted all-rounder, who has been linked with a move away from Brighton, says he would one day like to play for Pep Guardiola. “I love him. My dream is to play in his team. That would be absolutely fantastic,” he says. Ayari is a close friend of Dejan Kulusevski and is in a relationship with the Spurs winger’s sister, Sandra.

players.name: Mattias Svanberg
players.bio: Emerged as a young talent at Malmö, but things could just as easily have turned out differently: he could have chosen ice hockey as his sport. His father, Bo Svanberg, was a professional ice hockey player who won the Swedish league several times, but his son took a different path. After Mattias won his first league title with Malmö in 2016, Bo said he was not disappointed with his son’s career choice. “Football is a great sport and I’m incredibly proud of him now,” he said. Svanberg, who has had a successful career in Italy and Germany, is the same age as Alexander Isak and they have played together in various Sweden teams for a long time. At Euro 2020, Svanberg caused merriment after momentarily mixing up Isak with Kylian Mbappé: “I’ve played with Kylian…uh, sorry, Alex for a long time,” he said, much to the surprise of his teammates.

players.name: Eric Smith
players.bio: Had not been part of the national team discussion before Jon Dahl Tomasson took over in February 2024. The versatile centre-back, who can also play in midfield, was called up to four straight camps, but had to withdraw from all of them. “It was awful, not fun at all,” he says. It was so bad it almost became a bit comical in the end.” Was then in the squad for the World Cup play-offs – but got injured and had to pull out. Has stayed loyal to St Pauli for five years now after a turbulent spell at Gent, where he was frozen out. “I wasn’t allowed to touch the ball,” he recalls. “I had to jog around the pitch until the B team – not even the A team – had finished training, and then I could go home. I was almost in shock.”

players.name: Alexander Bernhardsson
players.bio: Bernhardsson's journey to the top has not been straight-forward, but here he is, called up for the World Cup after an injury-hit season with the 2. Bundesliga side Holstein Kiel. “He is very versatile and has great charisma,” said the Sweden coach, Graham Potter, about Bernhardsson when he revealed his squad. Bernhardsson played in six different positions for Holstein in 2025-26 but is fairly unknown in Sweden after spending four years at Elfsborg before moving to Germany.  After school he worked in a factory and played in the Swedish fourth division. At that time it is fair to say he wasn’t spending much time thinking about the national team. “I worked in a factory for two years and it was very educational,” he has said. “I wouldn’t want to change a thing. But now I enjoy calling football my full-time job.” Bernhardsson is known for being very humble.

players.name: Besfort Zeneli
players.bio: A rising star at Union SG who could have faced his older brother Arber at the World Cup. Similar to Kevin-Prince and Jérôme Boateng representing Ghana and Germany, Besfort chose to represent Sweden while his brother Arber has made more than 30 senior appearances for Kosovo (who did not qualify). “It was really close between Kosovo and Sweden, but my heart beat faster for Sweden,” Besfort says. He was not popular in Kosovo as a result: “I received a lot of threats and things like that. But, yeah, that’s part of football.” The technically gifted attacking midfielder played with Anthony Elanga at Elfsborg and now enjoys being reunited with the forward in the national team. “We often talk about it and about the times we had together. We talk about the memories,” he says.

players.name: Kristoffer Nordfeldt
players.bio: The “eternal No 2” in Sweden’s national team is expected to finally be the No 1 at the World Cup after getting his chance in the play-offs – and grabbing it with both hands. He had considered quitting international football after years of sitting on the bench watching Robin Olsen. “That discussion did come up,” he said. Nordfeldt has spent several years abroad, including five seasons at Swansea where he was, for a period, coached by Graham Potter, who is now Sweden’s manager. “Potter gave me back my love for football,” the 36-year-old says. Having played ice hockey for AIK until the age of 13, he is now the captain of their football team.

players.name: Elliot Stroud
players.bio: Arguably the best player in the Swedish top-flight, the defender/wing-back broke through last year as his club Mjällby – from nowhere really – won the league for the first time. The Mjällby coach, Karl Marius Aksum, says the sky is the limit for the player who will turn 24 during the World Cup. “I hope he can get a move to a top-five league straight away; he is ready for it,” Aksum says. Stroud had never represented Sweden at any age-level, never mind the senior team, when he was included in the World Cup squad. Mats Winblad, a former sports director at Stroud’s childhood club Oddevold, says the player is "extraordinarily competitive”, adding: “Both at school and at the training ground, he always gave 100 percent in everything he did.” Stroud, who was also a central midfielder before switching to the left wing, is a close friend to Herman Johansson, who was left out of the World Cup squad.

players.name: Gustaf Nilsson
players.bio: “The first thing you think of is Peter Crouch - how he combines height with technique.” That is how the former Sweden international Stefan Selakovic described Nilsson after the 18-year-old had just broken through and scored a spectacular bicycle kick in Sweden’s top flight in 2015. Even then, 11 years ago, people were talking about him as an international player, but it hasn’t quite worked out that way. After several years in Denmark, loans back in Sweden and even a spell with Wehen Wiesbaden in the German third tier, he now finds himself at Club Brugge (although playing time was limited in 2025-26). “I never once thought about quitting or giving up,” he says. Nilsson is a good option off the bench if Sweden are chasing the game.

players.name: Taha Ali
players.bio: A dribbling artist who has made a remarkable journey from being a national futsal player to becoming a top player in the Swedish top-flight. Popular for his individual skill, and as a youngster was inspired by Hatem Ben Arfa. “Ronaldinho, Neymar sure … but Ben Arfa – what a player he was,” he says. As a youngster, he was told he didn’t have the physique to succeed in football, but still made it to the national team. “I’ve always been told: ‘You’re too small, you’re too bad, not good enough, you dribble too much.’” Wants to be a role model for Swedish-Somali people: “I just hope to be an inspiration and show that no matter which path you’ve taken – whether through an academy or lower divisions – it’s possible to make it.”

players.name: Gregor Kobel
players.bio: After a long wait, Kobel has finally become Switzerland’s first-choice goalkeeper following Yann Sommer’s retirement in the autumn of 2024. For the national team he has not yet quite managed to replicate his outstanding form at Borussia Dortmund and it was not until his 13th international that the Zurich-born keeper managed to keep his first clean sheet. Kobel may be the most valuable player in the Swiss squad – according to Transfermarkt – and is an important leader in the squad but he doesn’t like the spotlight and rarely finds himself creating headlines. As a teenager, Kobel was so multi-talented he aspired to a career in tennis, snowboarding or ice hockey as well as football.

players.name: Miro Muheim
players.bio: Moved from Zurich to Chelsea as a 16-year-old and while he did not make it all the way to the first team he has some tremendous memories from his time in London. “You become more mature by moving abroad on your own and the set-up was fantastic. I remember Eden Hazard suddenly being in the changing room as he was playing for the Under-23s while returning to fitness. He was so nice and relaxed. And what a player. He showed straight away what you can do with a ball: simply sensational.” Returned to Switzerland and St Gallen in 2018 and made the breakthrough despite having suffered an ACL injury early on. Joined Hamburg three years later and won his first Switzerland cap as a 2. Bundesliga player before being promoted to the top flight. Is now part of the leadership group at HSV.

players.name: Silvan Widmer
players.bio: Back in form after a difficult spell for country and club, the attacking full-back regained his starting place for both during the 2025-26 season. “Of course, last season was disappointing for me,” he said. “But I’m not the sort of person who harbours frustration. I never had to force myself to get going; I always enjoyed being part of the team and, as a result, was also a role model for others who were going through a difficult time.” He is a very reliable player but opponents of the highest calibre can cause him problems, as was the case in the 4-3 defeat against Germany in March. A captain at Mainz, he is not afraid to discuss sensitive topics and said that if he had to step in as captain during the World Cup in Qatar he would definitely wear the rainbow armband and he has also said he would never play in Saudi Arabia. While injured at Mainz in 2023, he said: “I get to spend more time at home and there I do whatever is needed, whether that is taking packages to the post office or helping my wife Céline with her own company.”

players.name: Nico Elvedi
players.bio: The Zurich-born player with roots in Graubünden moved from Zurich to Mönchengladbach as a teenager in 2015 – and is still there. There have been talks of a move, but it never materialised and he is now, in fact, the club’s all-time leading appearance-holder in the Bundesliga.  Nicknamed ‘Eisvogel’ (Kingfisher) In Germany after a cool and composed debut against Bayern Munich of all teams. Nico’s twin brother Jan is also a professional footballer and was most recently on loan from Kaiserslautern to Greuther Fürth. Jan is full of praise for his brother, although thinks he may be a little bit “too nice” on the pitch. “Nico is an all-rounder and has practically no weaknesses,” says Jan. “But perhaps he could be a bit more aggressive and use his elbows a bit more.” The brothers’ greatest wish: to play against each other one day. Elvedi has made a strong comeback for the national team after a difficult year when he lost his place in the starting line-up for Euro 2024. He was among the best Switzerland players in the World Cup qualifiers.

players.name: Manuel Akanji
players.bio: Has developed into a world-class defender at Manchester City and Inter and, together with Granit Xhaka, an undisputed leader for the national team. His father is from Nigeria and his middle name “Obafemi” means “beloved of the king”, which is why he had a crown tattooed on his left forearm. Another tattoo – “Prove them wrong” – was inked after doubts that he would make it all the way while with the FC Winterthur youth teams. He even overcame an ACL injury at Basel to continue his rise to the top. At the start of 2018, aged just 22, he moved to Borussia Dortmund for £23.5m and four years later to Manchester City, where he won the treble in 2023. Akanji is also good with numbers; he once vowed the whole of Switzerland with his mental arithmetic skills on a TV show. The video went viral.

players.name: Denis Zakaria
players.bio: Borussia Mönchengladbach, Juventus, Chelsea and, now, Monaco. How to keep up with Denis Zakaria? Hardly any Swiss player has represented as many prestigious clubs as him, yet with the national team it has been complicated at times. The Monaco captain has had trouble finding his place under Murat Yakin, mainly because it is not easy getting picked when you have Granit Xhaka and Remo Freuler in central midfield. And when Zakaria was called upon because Xhaka was missing – such as the Euro 2020 quarter-final against Spain or in September 2024 against the same opponents in the Nations League – he often seemed out of place in the game. Now, however, Yakin may have found the right role for Zakaria: as a right-sided player in a defensive back three, which he played for the first time in the friendly against Norway in March. “It was a successful performance from Denis,” said Yakin. He is one of the leaders in the national team squad set-up and “big brother” of the large Geneva contingent in the squad. In 2019, in an interview with Blick, he admitted to making some mistakes when he was younger. “I bought a really expensive car once but I then realised it was a mistake. It brings nothing. So I sold it.”

players.name: Breel Embolo
players.bio: Breel Embolo has undergone an astonishing transformation in recent years. The forward, who had always stood out for his impressive physicality, has now added more goals to his repertoire. At the time of writing he has scored eight times for Switzerland since the start of 2025 – the same amount of goals he had in the previous six years for his country. But it is not only on the pitch that the striker, born in Cameroon but raised in Switzerland, has matured. In the past, Embolo has had a tendency to cause controversy. In early 2021, he attended an illegal party despite the Covid lockdown and hid in a bathtub when the police arrived. In 2023, he was convicted of making multiple threats. His comeback at Monaco was derailed because he allegedly obtained two fake Covid certificates in 2021. Today, however, the man from Basel is a key leader within the team, part of the captaincy team and, thanks to his bilingualism, an important link between the German- and French-speaking players.

players.name: Remo Freuler
players.bio: Has come a long way since those dark days when he failed to make his breakthrough at GC, despite scoring a goal in the derby against FC Zurich at the age of 18. He first went on loan to Challenge League side Winterthur before catching the eye at Lucerne. In January 2016 he joined Atalanta, where, despite many telling him the move abroad came too early, became an instant hit. He spent five years in Bergamo, being a crucial part of Gian Piero Gasperini’s successful time at the club, gaining Champions League experience along the way. After a year with Nottingham Forest in England he returned to Bologna, where he was part of the side who ended a 50-year trophy drought by winning the Coppa Italia. Freuler is this season chasing down Stephan Lichtsteiner as the Swiss player with most Serie A appearances and is a leader within the national team set-ups. “When I don’t like something, I don’t stay quiet, I say something,” he told watson.ch. “If you don’t do that you are a little grey mouse. That is something that has changed in recent years. But at the end what you do on the pitch is always the most important thing. Actions always speak louder than words.”

players.name: Johan Manzambi
players.bio: The big discovery of the past 12 months. Having burst on to the scene from seemingly nowhere at Freiburg Murat Yakin called him up to the summer friendlies in 2025 - and Manzambi responded by scoring and assisting on his first start, a 4-0 win against USA. The technically gifted player can play in any position and at Freiburg he is often deployed as a defensive or attacking midfielder while he has often appeared on the wing for his country. Has matured since moving from the French-speaking part of Switzerland to Germany at the age of 17 but can still lose his cool on the pitch, and was sent off in two consecutive Bundesliga matches during the 2025-26 season. After Freiburg had beaten Basel 2-1 in the Europa League last season Xherdan Shaqiri gatecrashed an interview with Manzambi and said: “It was good to him play, he has a big future ahead of him.”

players.name: Granit Xhaka
players.bio: Captain, record-breaker and the manager’s right-hand man: Granit Xhaka is the undisputed leader of the national team at his seventh major tournament – both on and off the pitch. In the March friendlies against Germany and Norway, the captain was rested for three quarters of the two matches. “We have to be prepared to play without him at times, or when he is marked man-to-man, as he was against Germany,” said Murat Yakin. After a difficult start to their relationship, the pair are now very close. The midfielder has been around for some time now. In 2011, Ottmar Hitzfeld threw the then 18-year-old Xhaka in at the deep end at Wembley during a Euro qualifier against England and the Under-17 World Cup winner is now the record holder for international caps in his country with more than 140 appearances. Many felt that his career was on the way down when he joined Bayer Leverkusen in 2023 after seven years at Arsenal but it was the opposite - and as Xabi Alonso’s leader on the pitch he took the club to a first ever Bundesliga title, won the Cup and reached the Europa League final. Now back in England, he says: “It’s important for me to experience moments of suffering and solidarity too.” His parents are from Kosovo; his father, Ragip, was arrested in 1986 at the age of 22 during a demonstration against the communist central government

players.name: Dan Ndoye
players.bio: Had his breakthrough at Euro 2024 and, after the 3-1 victory against Hungary, he and his father, Saliou, FaceTimed with Dan’s grandmother Bineta, who lives in Senegal and is cheering on her grandson from there. “I keep getting photos from Senegal. People there follow the Swiss national team’s matches regularly,” says Ndoye, who often goes on holiday to the west coast of Africa. Family plays a very important role in the life of the Lausanne native, who has a younger brother and a younger sister. Last summer he became the second most expensive Swiss player ever behind Granit Xhaka as Nottingham Forest paid around £35m to sign him from Bologna. It has been a stop-start first season for the winger in England but he is a guaranteed starter for Switzerland thanks to his speed and dribbling ability. “I am very ambitious and know what I can do,” he said in October. “I am someone who works hard and wants to develop – that is why I am in the Premier League today. That doesn’t mean that I am happy, though, I will always want to go higher.”

players.name: Yvon Mvogo
players.bio: The Cameroon-born goalkeeper is the perfect No 2. He fits in well with the team, accepts his role and is there when needed. "He has always been in a good mood, with a smile on his face – a real fan favourite," his coach at Young Boys, Uli Forte, once said. He has impressed in virtually all of his 12 international appearances, keeping six clean sheets. His club career has not always followed a smooth path, however. In 2017, when he was regarded as one of the most talented goalkeepers on the continent, he left YB but failed to establish himself at RB Leipzig. Via Eindhoven, he ended up at Lorient where he turned down offers from Parma and Celtic before getting relegated last summer. It looked like he was going to leave the French club but ended up resigning. As a child, church service was compulsory for him on Sundays. “I didn’t fancy it, as it was always my only day off,” he says with a smile. “But my mother told me me that it’s important to thank God.”

players.name: Ricardo Rodriguez
players.bio: Alongside the captain, Granit Xhaka, Rodriguez is the player with the most international caps in the history of Swiss football. Xhaka is also his best friend in the national team and the two used to share a room. Together they won the Under-17 World Cup in Nigeria back in 2009. The historic success launched Rodriguez’ career, taking him to Wolfsburg, Milan, Eindhoven, Torino and, now, Real Betis. The son of a Spanish father and a Chilean mother, he grew up in the working-class Zurich neighbourhood of Schwamendingen, but struggled at school, which is why his mother Marcela was very concerned about him. Marcela, sadly, died of cancer in 2016. His brothers Roberto and Francisco also made it as professional footballers, but Ricardo is the most successful of the three. Rodriguez had a difficult start in life: due to a diaphragmatic hernia at birth, doctors gave him only a 50 per cent chance of survival. Rodriguez

players.name: Ardon Jashari
players.bio: A big discussion point in the national team. Jashari is regarded as the most talented midfielder in Switzerland and a possible successor to Granit Xhaka but game time has so far been limited with the Sunderland player and Remo Freuler often preferred to the 24-year-old. Has improved immeasurably since joining Club Brugge in 2024 and is now with Milan. In 2025 he told Sky about his early years in Switzerland. “I grew up in Neuheim next to Swiss families who had a higher social standard as well as Albanian ones, who lived more modestly. The feeling of this difference was painful at times as I had the feeling that I had to look up to the wealthier people, as if I were worth less. But my parents did everything to minimise this difference and to make sure that I was not affected by this social inequality. They worked extremely hard to make sure that we had the same opportunities as the others.”

players.name: Djibril Sow
players.bio: The midfielder is arriving at the World Cup with a spring in his step after one of the best seasons of his career. Especially in front of goal he has displayed a new, improved level of maturity. For the national team, however, it is a different story as, despite winning more than 50 caps, he does not have a clear role under Murat Yakin. He was a starterF at the last World Cup but has not been called upon in the past 12 months, partly because of a toe injury. “Djibbi” can play as a No 6 and a No 8 - and he knows how to win titles. In 2018, he was part of the YB side that won the league for the first time in 32 years. And in 2022, he secured the Europa League with Eintracht Frankfurt.

players.name: Christian Fassnacht
players.bio: The biggest surprise in Murat Yakin’s squad. Last autumn, Fassnacht returned to the national setup after a two-and-a-half-year absence but was again missing from the squad this March. With 18 goals, he was the top scorer in the Swiss Super League in 2025-26 and he is a true team player. “The statistics don’t lie; he knows where the goal is,” said Yakin. Fassnacht’s finest hour came in the Euro 2020 Last 16, when he won possession for both Switzerland’s late goals in a 3-3 draw with France before victory on penalties. The striker was once dropped from the Zurich youth team, forcing him to spend several years in amateur football before making the leap to the top flight via Winterthur, Thun and then Young Boys. In Bern, he has since become a legend. His spell abroad at Norwich was not a success, so he returned in 2025 after a year and a half.

players.name: Ruben Vargas
players.bio: In early 2025, after five and a half years at Augsburg, Vargas moved to La Liga and Sevilla. There, the Lucerne native has been a regular starter when fit and the same goes for Switzerland. He is known as the unofficial favourite player of national team coach, Murat Yakin. One of Switzerland’s heroes against Italy in the Last 16-game against Italy at Euro 2024, scoring the second goal of a 2-0 victory with a stunning long-range strike. The son of a Dominican father and a Swiss mother, he completed a three-year apprenticeship as a painter during his time at FC Lucerne. His father is a golf instructor and Vargas can often be seen on the course as well. In the national team, he has a particularly close friendship with Noah Okafor.

players.name: Eray Cömert
players.bio: Endeared him to the Valencia faithful in February with some lively celebrations after the derby win against Levante. After the final whistle Cömert put his shirt on the corner flag and celebrated exuberantly in front of his own fans. Images that went viral. Levante fans were less impressed and pelted him with objects as he made his way down the tunnel, with a half-full plastic bottle hitting Cömert on the head. He was unrepentant though. “I’ve no problem with that at all,” he said. “Emotions are part and parcel of football, all the more so in a derby.” Joined Valencia in 2002 but it is not until this season that he managed to establish himself as a starter following two loan spells (at Nantes and Real Valladolid). Was up until 2018 playing as Eray Cümart after a mistake by passport officials - affecting the whole family – but was belatedly able to change it to the correct spelling.

players.name: Noah Okafor
players.bio: At the start of the year it seemed unthinkable that Okafor would travel to the World Cup. Things had been bad since the Euro 2024 where he did not play a single minute but is said to have displayed a lack of team spirit and been bad-tempered. He then proceeded to meet the national team director Pierluigi Tami without telling the coach, Murat Yakin. Finally, after being left out of the squad for the World Cup qualifiers, he criticised Yakin in an interview. Remarkably, though, a visit by Yakin to Leeds seems to have solved the issue and Okafor was included for the March friendlies. There he apologised to the team, which is good news for Switzerland because he has qualities that no other Swiss striker has at the moment.

players.name: Michel Aebischer
players.bio: A man for the big moment. After being mainly a substitute for Switzerland he grabbed his chance at Euro 2024 and produced his best performance yet for his country in the opening game against Hungary, scoring a goal and providing an assist. What wasn’t known at the time was that he was playing through the pain because of a groin injury. In the end he had to have an operation and lost his place at Bologna and the national team. In the summer of 2025 he therefore joined Pisa. “I no longer had the role I wanted,” he said. “Because I’m someone who likes to get plenty of playing time and also plays an important role in the team. That’s why I deliberately took a step back.” Fluent in all three official Swiss languages – Italian, German and French, he responded diplomatically when asked if he preferred Italian or Swiss food: “You are not going to struggle to find nice food in Italy, that’s for sure” before adding that he was missing a good fondue.

players.name: Marvin Keller
players.bio: Keller is ‘a cool sock’ according to one of his former Young Boys teammates, Fabian Rieder, “as a person and as a goalkeeper”. That is Swiss-German for being calm and collected. He can be loud on the pitch though. “Maybe that is because he is from Zurich,” a current teammate, Sandro Lauper, suggests. Born in London when his father was working there, Keller grew up and came through the youth teams at Grasshopper in Switzerland. He is regarded as one of Switzerland’s most promising goalkeeping talents and, in the past two years, has fought his way up from the second division to replace David von Ballmoos at serial champions Young Boys. He may well be on the move in the summer and has said: “I want to play in a top-five league.”  Played at the Under-21 Euros but has yet to make his senior international debut at the time of writing.

players.name: Fabian Rieder
players.bio: “You have to take more responsibility,” Rieder said in 2023 in an Interview with Swiss Television when asked about his father dying when the player was only 11 years old. “You are confronted by things you didn’t expect. But I also have to make clear that I had a good childhood. It did mean, though, that I was probably a bit more mature than others, personality wise. The national team coach, Murat Yakin, is a big fan of the tireless worker who often leads the press for the Swiss. In 2023, he left Swiss serial champions Young Boys for France and Rennes in a deal worth £14.2m. It didn’t work out for him there, however, and after an equally hit-and-miss spell on loan at Stuttgart, he joined Augsburg in the summer of 2025.

players.name: Zeki Amdouni
players.bio: The son of a Turkish kiosk owner and a Tunisian mother is still regarded as one of the country’s greatest talents, but he has faced a race against time to recover from an ACL injury suffered in pre-season with Burnley. Bayern Munich’s Vincent Kompany is an admirer and it was the Belgian who took Amdouni to England in 2023. The Switzerland manager, Murat Yakin, also thinks highly of a player whose style is that of a street footballer from Geneva, not dissimilar to that of Xherdan Shaqiri. Yakin invited him to be part of the squad for the March friendlies despite not being match-fit yet. In an interview with Blick he explained why he now prefers Messi to Ronaldo. “When I was young I liked everything about Ronaldo; his hair, his boots, his whole style. When I turned 13, though, and really started to understand football there was only one player for me: Messi.” He also explained why he has adopted a bow and arrow goal celebration: “It was something between me and a friend,” he said. “It comes from a French rapper called Jul he sings about an arrow in one of his songs so I said to the friend that from then on I was going to do that as a celebration. I wanted to do something no one else does.”

players.name: Aurèle Amenda
players.bio: Earlier this season Amenda’s career was going nowhere. He was mainly a substitute under Dino Toppmöller at Eintracht Frankfurt and there was talk of a winter move but a change of manager, to Albert Riera, had a transformative effect for the man from Biel. He has become a regular starter in the Bundesliga and the national team coach, Murat Yakin, was already a fan of the physical defender. In fact he sees him as a future successor to Manuel Akanji. “The move did not work out the way I had envisaged,” Amenda told Frankfurter Rundschau in March. “But I stayed positive and mentally strong, that helped me a lot.” The Swiss-Cameroonian dual national came through the youth ranks at Young Boys, winning two league titles and one Cup with the club before making the leap to the Bundesliga at the age of 21.

players.name: Luca Jaquez
players.bio: “At first I didn’t like it all,” Jaquez told blue.ch in 2024 when asked about the time when the Lucerne legend and Under-18 coach Michel Renggli had moved him from an attacking role to central defence. “I had always dreamed about a career as a forward player. It took me a few weeks before I realised that I could have a future at the back.” Credits his mother and sister for becoming a professional footballer: “My mum is everything for me. Since I was young she has always been by my side and supported me at every tournament, every game. I don’t know how I could have become a footballer without her.” And his sister, who had to stop gymnastics because Jaquez’ football took over and their mum couldn’t drive them to both? “At the time I was so focused on football I am not sure I noticed. But looking back maybe that wasn’t so fair.” Was due to make his debut for the senior national team in November 2024 but Murat Yakin ended up not being able to select Jaquez as he had been sent off for the Under-21s a few weeks earlier. A regular at Stuttgart despite fierce competition because of his superb build-up play, he is the most talented Swiss defender coming through the ranks.

players.name: Cedric Itten
players.bio: “The most important thing is the passion and joy of playing football,” Itten said when asked how he has made it as a professional footballer. “Of course you have to have some talent but, above all, hard work. The goal has to be to improve every single day, that was the key to my success.” During the 2025-26 season he has been the most prolific Swiss striker abroad, although the fact that he has been playing in the second tier in Germany for Fortuna Düsseldorf counts against him. The former Rangers players - he won the title in Scotland - was not part of the squad for the March friendlies but worked hard to get included in the travelling party to the World Cup. He scored six minutes into his international debut and has, at the time of writing, a good record of five goals in 13 games for Switzerland. This will be his first major tournament for his country.

players.name: Mouhib Chamakh
players.bio: “I dream of wearing the shirt of a major club and then taking part in the World Cup with my national team,” Chamakh said in 2019, when he was a teenager playing in Tunisia’s second tier. After a sensational season in goal for first-time top-flight champions Club Africain, he has got his wish – and could even replace Aymen Dahmen as the No 1 this summer. Chamakh impressed in his first cap against Canada in March, keeping a clean sheet in Toronto, and is one of a number of fresh faces in Sabri Lamouchi’s squad. “We must be brave enough to open the door and give our young players a chance,” Lamouchi has said. Having developed at Tunisian sides Médenine and Métlaoui, the 24-year-old is on the verge of fulfilling his childhood dream, with reports suggesting he could get the nod to start in North America.

players.name: Ali Abdi
players.bio: A true success story. Few people believed Abdi could make it in Europe when he left Club Africain for Paris FC in 2019, at the age of 26. But, through spells in Ligue 2 with Paris FC and Caen, followed by a move to Ligue 1 with Nice, Abdi has proven himself to be one of those rare left-backs capable of changing a match on his own. He has also succeeded one of Tunisia’s legendary defenders, Ali Maâloul, in the national side. Before reaching this level, Abdi endured many hardships. He struggled with injuries, including pubalgia, which limited his chances at Espérance before he joined their cross-city rivals. Named Tunisia’s player of the year in 2024 and 2025, Abdi continues to give everything for the national team shirt. Tunisia’s first-choice player in his position remains a key figure in Sabri Lamouchi’s setup thanks to his defensive nous and attacking contributions.

players.name: Montassar Talbi
players.bio: Born in Paris, centre-back Talbi played youth football for Paris FC before his family returned to Tunisia when he was 12 years old. He continued his development at Espérance de Tunis, before making the leap to join Turkish side Caykur Rizespor at the age of 20, moving on to Rubin Kazan and then French side Lorient. Talbi made his first appearance for the national team at 22 and has since become an undisputed starter in Sabri Lamouchi’s well-drilled defence. Named Tunisia’s player of the year in 2023, Talbi is a true warrior and one of the names almost certain to feature in the starting XI at this year’s World Cup.

players.name: Omar Rekik
players.bio: Rekik has made the final 26-man squad after a rocky road with the national team. Called up in 2021 by former coach Mondher Kebaier, the 24-year-old defender had only played a few minutes for Tunisia, and even distanced himself from the national team setup. It was only after Sabri Lamouchi took charge that Rekik agreed to return. His older brother, Karim, has four caps for the Netherlands and Omar earned youth caps with the Oranje. The siblings moved together from Feyenoord to Manchester City, PSV, Marseille and Hertha Berlin, before Omar joined Arsenal in 2021 after Karim moved to Sevilla. Rekik did not play for the Gunners and was loaned out five times before joining Slovenian side Maribor last summer – but he could very well feature at this summer’s tournament.

players.name: Adem Arous
players.bio: A new discovery for many. Born in Saudi Arabia, Arous first broke through at Stade Tunisien. The centre-back then moved to Kasimpasa, a Turkish club previously home to several Tunisian internationals such as Syam Ben Youssef, Yassine Meriah, Oussama Haddadi and Mortadha Ben Ouanes, who still plays there today. His adaptation to this new environment in Istanbul was smooth, and earned him a place in the Tunisia squad for Afcon 2025. Arous did not get off the bench during the Eagles of Carthage’s time in Morocco, but he earned his first cap against Haiti in March. Still only 21, the future belongs to him and his profile could rise this summer, with Galatasaray and Monaco among sides showing an interest in signing him.

players.name: Dylan Bronn
players.bio: One of only two players in the squad set to participate in their third World Cup, Bronn is one of the veterans of this Tunisian team. The 30-year-old centre-back, who has barely played for his club in Switzerland since January, was not guaranteed a place in the final squad. Asked about Bronn’s limited playing time, the Tunisia head coach, Sabri Lamouchi, explained: “Dylan has an important influence within the group. I personally asked his coach at Servette not to play him after his return from injury.” First capped in 2017, Bronn – who was born in Cannes to a German father and Tunisian mother – played at right-back against England at the 2018 World Cup, and also scored a header in the 5-2 defeat to Belgium.

players.name: Elias Achouri
players.bio: He rarely speaks to the media, but on the pitch Achouri is one of Tunisia’s most influential attacking players. Supporters regard the hard-working winger as being capable of changing a match single-handedly. Achouri arrives at the World Cup in peak form after an impressive campaign with Copenhagen, scoring five goals and notching seven assists in all competitions. At Afcon 2025, he scored twice in the 3-1 win over Uganda, but missed a penalty in the shootout loss to Mali. Born on the French island of Réunion to a Tunisian father and an Algerian mother, Achouri’s playing career has taken him to France, Portugal and now Denmark. He appears almost certain to start this summer and could be the team’s key creative spark.

players.name: Elias Saad
players.bio: Very few players successfully make the transition from futsal to professional football, but Saad is one of them. Born in Hamburg, he joined the city’s futsal team in 2019 and became the youngest player to make an appearance for Germany’s national futsal side. He was also playing in local amateur 11-a-side leagues and training for a career in sales when St Pauli came calling. The winger made an immediate impact, scoring in a derby defeat to HSV and impressing coach Fabian Hürzeler, who said “hard work is what he demands, and he’ll be rewarded.” Now 26 years old, Saad is preparing to experience his first World Cup. Having scored three goals in two qualifying matches, he is among the players most eagerly awaited by fans in the United States, Mexico, and Canada this summer.

players.name: Hazem Mastouri
players.bio: Mastouri’s journey to the top has been truly remarkable. At the age of 24, the striker was still playing in Tunisia’s second division. Step by step, he climbed the ladder, delivering goals for Iraqi side Al-Najaf before joining Monastir. Mastouri, who stands 1.91m tall, scored 16 goals as his team finished second in the Tunisian top flight, earning him a move to the Russian Premier League. Last November, he scored Tunisia’s goal in a 1-1 friendly draw with Brazil in Lille. “Playing for the national team and scoring against Brazil feels like a dream,” the 28-year-old told reporters afterwards. “I tell every young player to believe in their dreams, stay strong, and never give up.”

players.name: Hannibal Mejbri
players.bio: Without a doubt, the biggest star of the Tunisian national team. In 2021, the national FA organised a special press conference to officially present Mejbri, in a first for a dual-nationality player choosing Tunisia. The young midfielder was emerging at Manchester United and had been capped by France at youth level, but said: “My heart chose Tunisia. You do not choose a national team by default or keep a country waiting.” Not only is Mejbri a star on the pitch, but he also speaks with remarkable maturity, something that greatly appeals to Tunisian fans. Following Tunisia’s surprise elimination against Mali at Afcon 2025, he criticised the state of football development in Tunisia. “We dream a lot, but we do not work enough. We do not educate ourselves. We must ask ourselves serious questions because we have fallen far behind.” Now at Burnley, the playmaker who earned comparisons with David Beckham while at United’s academy, always stood out, as one of them said: “He had a unique style, with big blond hair. Where you could find a pitch and a ball, you would find Hannibal.”

players.name: Ismaël Gharbi
players.bio: Born in Paris, Gharbi played at youth level for France and Spain, before declaring for Tunisia in August 2025 after years of negotiations. After spending time in Paris FC’s youth setup, the versatile midfielder moved to PSG’s academy in 2016 and broke into first-team contention five years later. The former PSG player Mathieu Bodmer has credited Gharbi with having “cold blood … in the penalty area, time stops for him.” His club career has stalled, though. After moving to Braga on a five-year deal, Gharbi spent last season at Augsburg, making just six Bundesliga appearances. Asked about his call-up for the March training camp despite his limited playing time at club level, Sabri Lamouchi explained: “He is not playing because of choices made at his club. But for me, he can bring a tremendous amount to this national team.”

players.name: Mortadha Ben Ouanes
players.bio: In Sabri Lamouchi’s 26-man squad, Ben Ouanes is listed as a midfielder, but this versatile player can also operate as a left-back or left winger. He has played in all three positions in Turkey since joining Kasimpasa in 2021; clubmate Adem Arous, 10 years his junior, is also in the Tunisia squad for this summer. Regularly called up since 2019, Ben Ouanes has only been used sparingly by the national team. He has made 17 appearances in total at the time of writing, with one particularly memorable match: Tunisia’s 5-1 friendly defeat against Brazil in 2022, where he started as a left-back. Tunisian supporters continue to share the same opinion: Ben Ouanes is a player whose full potential has never truly been harnessed by the national team.

players.name: Rani Khedira
players.bio: At the age of 32, Rani Khedira finally agreed to represent Tunisia, switching allegiance from Germany just three months before the World Cup finals. Capped only at youth level for his country of birth, Khedira – younger brother of World Cup winner Sami – had declined previous offers to represent the Eagles of Carthage. Sources close to the Tunisian FA have claimed that Khedira justified his refusal by saying he was not ready and only spoke German. This year, he finally accepted the call-up after talks with Sabri Lamouchi, who may even start him in a tough group. Many fans are sceptical over his change of heart, and believe Khedira is simply adding another line to his CV: playing in a World Cup. Tunisian supporters have not forgotten similar cases in the past, like David Jemmali in 2006 or Yohann Benalouane in 2018, but Lamouchi appears convinced Khedira will improve his team.

players.name: Khalil Ayari
players.bio: Fans believe Ayari is one of the players destined to shape the future of the national team. Last summer, he earned the opportunity to join the European champions, Paris St-Germain, at the age of 21. Tunisians know Ayari is talented, but they are equally aware he now has the perfect environment to evolve, improve, and reach another level. He received an excellent football upbringing at one of Tunisia’s biggest clubs, Stade Tunisien, before a successful trial took him to Paris. Supporters are also pleased because Ayari, with the support of his club, chose a move to Europe despite receiving enormous financial offers from Gulf-based clubs. Expectations surrounding the young winger are now extremely high, despite winning a solitary cap at the time of writing. It is up to him to seize this rare opportunity and prove himself on the biggest stage.

players.name: Hadj Mahmoud
players.bio: Approaching the end of his contract with Swiss side Lugano, the defensive midfielder is taking his first real steps with the Tunisian national team. After starting against Mali during the recent Africa Cup of Nations in Morocco, Mahmoud earned Sabri Lamouchi’s trust with two appearances in the March friendlies against Haiti and Canada, but still faces a fight to start in a highly competitive midfield unit this summer. Mahmoud joined Étoile du Sahel in his home city of Sousse at the age of seven, before moving to Lugano in 2021 and helping them win the Swiss Cup. Having been linked with Union Berlin and Fenerbahce in the past, this World Cup could also serve as the perfect platform for Mahmoud to find a new club.

players.name: Aymen Dahmen
players.bio: Outstanding during the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, the experienced goalkeeper is preparing to take part in his second World Cup finals. An undisputed starter for the Eagles of Carthage throughout the qualifiers – where he didn’t concede a goal – and during the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, Dahmen missed the friendly matches against Haiti and Canada in March, with coach Sabri Lamouchi handing a start to each of his two other squad keepers. Dahmen plays for his hometown club, CS Sfaxien, who have turned down lucrative offers to keep him as their No 1. It remains to be seen whether Dahmen will wear that shirt for Tunisia’s opening match against Sweden in Monterrey.

players.name: Ellyes Skhiri
players.bio: Elegant in his style of play, physically strong, and incredibly generous on the pitch, Skhiri perfectly embodies the description often associated with him: the quiet strength of Tunisia’s midfield. Like Dylan Bronn, the midfielder is preparing to take part in his third World Cup – but this time, he will be captain after previous incumbent Ferjani Sassi was omitted from the squad. Skhiri featured for Tunisia’s Under-23 team in 2014 but did not earn his first senior cap until 2018. “I decided to take my time,” he explained after choosing to represent Tunisia. “I am ready to balance both club and national team commitments. Until now, I prioritised my club [Montpellier at the time] because I needed to grow and establish myself.” Named the country’s player of the year in 2021 while at Cologne, Skhiri joined Eintracht Frankfurt in 2023 and has now made 200 Bundesliga appearances.

players.name: Rayan Elloumi
players.bio: The youngest player in Tunisia’s 26-man squad, the 18-year-old forward has played for two different nations in 2026. Born in Edmonton, Elloumi chose to represent Tunisia at Under-23 level last October, but featured for Canada in a B-level friendly against Guatemala in January, and informed the Tunisian football federation that he was considering switching his allegiance to the World Cup co-hosts. The response was immediate, with Lamouchi calling him up straight away for a March training camp. Elloumi played for 60 minutes against Haiti, then 30 minutes against Canada, before taking his place in Tunisia’s World Cup squad. Plucked from the local youth leagues by the Vancouver Whitecaps, he became their first homegrown player to score on his home MLS debut, netting in a 7-0 rout of Philadelphia Union.

players.name: Firas Chaouat
players.bio: Top scorer of the Tunisian 2024-2025 season with Étoile du Sahel (17 goals) and again in 2025-2026 with Club Africain (14 goals), Chaouat is a true attacking powerhouse capable of troubling even the toughest defence. “He can score against any team,” says his Club Africain coach, Faouzi Benzarti. Shy in front of microphones but ruthless in front of goal, Chaouat is a natural-born scorer who played a crucial role in Club Africain’s league title triumph this season. The 30-year-old striker has finally reached the peak of his abilities – he earned more caps in 2025 than in the previous six years put together. Chaouat could be a key offensive weapon for Sabri Lamouchi as he looks to create scoring opportunities against Sweden, Japan and the Netherlands.

players.name: Yan Valery
players.bio: When Valery broke into the Southampton first-team setup under Mark Hughes, he was already capped at youth level by France. The right-back first agreed to represent Tunisia in 2019 after a meeting with Alain Giresse, who was then the national team coach. Valery was not called up until 2022, when he featured in a pre-World Cup friendly against Brazil but did not make the squad in Qatar. He did, however, play in every game as Tunisia reached the last 16 at the recent Africa Cup of Nations campaign, and chose to leave struggling Sheffield Wednesday for a loan spell in the Swiss league with Young Boys in January. The gamble to try and secure his place in Sabri Lamouchi’s 26-man squad has paid off. Valery made the cut and is even considered Tunisia’s first-choice right-back heading into the World Cup. He would be the first Wednesday player to feature at a World Cup since John Sheridan played for the Republic of Ireland in 1994.

players.name: Mohamed Amine Ben Hmida
players.bio: Primarily a left-back who can also operate in central defence, Ben Hmida has been described as “every coach’s dream” by Sabri Lamouchi, who values the 30-year-old as a “versatile, serious, and hard working” defender. Ben Hmida has limited experience in major international tournaments for Tunisia. He was an undisputed starter at the 2021 Arab Cup in Qatar, where Tunisia reached the final, but only played one minute across three matches in the 2025 edition, which saw the team go out in the group stage. Ben Hmida describes himself as a “master of mental preparation” on social media, and has won five Tunisian titles in seven years at Espérance de Tunis.

players.name: Sabri Ben Hessen
players.bio: The goalkeeper  who represented Tunisia at every youth level – including at the 2013 Under-17 World Cup – finally earned his first senior cap in March’s friendly against Haiti, where he kept a clean sheet in a 1-0 win. The wait was long and he had turned 29 by the time it happened, but Ben Hessen’s hard work and perseverance eventually paid off. Like Dahmen, he was born in Sfax and developed at his hometown club, where competition for his position was fierce. Ben Hassen moved to Étoile Sportive du Sahel last summer, where he has truly flourished. Delighted to be part of the final squad, he has said: “I fought to be here, I worked in silence, I stayed patient, and my dream is finally coming true.”

players.name: Moutaz Neffati
players.bio: One of many young talents included in the 26-man squad, right-back Neffati carries the hopes of many Tunisian supporters, but must first establish himself within the national team setup before aiming for a starting role. Born in Norrköping, Neffati represented Sweden at youth level before choosing to play for Tunisia last year. After a short loan in Iceland with KR, Neffati has become a regular with his hometown club and contributes goals and assists in the Swedish second tier. He now needs to confirm his potential with the national team. “Always proud and honoured to represent my country,” Neffati posted on Instagram after his Tunisia debut. “Inshallah, we have more to achieve.”

players.name: Raed Chikhaoui
players.bio: The surprise inclusion in Sabri Lamouchi’s squad. Chikhaoui will turn 22 just before this summer’s World Cup kicks off, and had never been called up to the national team before Lamouchi’s appointment as Tunisia head coach in January. Chikhaoui moved to the coastal city of Monastir from Hammam-Sousse in 2024, and has become an undisputed starter in defence, helping USM finish fifth in the league last season. Primarily a left-sided centre-back, Chikhaoui is nicknamed “the watchtower” due to his 1.90m height and versatility. His selection represents a major opportunity that this talented young defender will now have to seize, and could earn him a move to Europe with several clubs monitoring his development.

players.name: Anis Ben Slimane
players.bio: A midfield talent unlike any other in the squad, there is a widespread feeling that Ben Slimane has never truly been used to his full potential with the national team. Having represented Denmark at Under-19 level before playing for Tunisia at the 2022 World Cup, the Copenhagen-born playmaker is still waiting for that one match to fully showcase his immense talent. Absent from the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations squad, he is coming off an excellent season with Norwich despite injury setbacks: 26 matches, five goals, and six assists. The Canaries decided to make his loan move from Sheffield United permanent in January. Back in the fold internationally, Ben Slimane has more than enough in his locker to earn a place in the 26-man squad.

players.name: Sebastien Tounekti
players.bio: Having performed exceptionally for Celtic in his first season in Scotland, Tounekti is another player who is expected to shape the future of the Tunisian national team. The left winger was born in Tromsø and chose to represent the Eagles of Carthage after turning out for Norway at a number of youth levels. Following his debut in 2021, Tounekti had to wait four years to earn another call-up. Despite that, he appears to have a very strong chance of starting in Tunisia’s attacking line at his first World Cup, having scored the winner against Haiti in a Toronto friendly in March. Tounekti’s blistering pace is expected to cause serious problems for opposing defenders. Expectations are high, and he possesses all the talent necessary to deliver and confirm his potential.

players.name: Mert Gunok
players.bio: There was perhaps never any doubt about which position Mert Günok would play in as his father was a former goalkeeper and then a goalkeeping coach. Despite this Mert nearly chose a career in basketball before football. He is 37 years old now but despite making his international debut in 2012, he had not reached 40 caps by the time this World Cup started, being in and out of the side. He was first choice, however, as Turkey reached the Euro 2024 quarter-finals, with his fingertip save against Austria one of the key moments of his country’s campaign. With Turkey leading 2-1 and the game deep into added-on time Christoph Baumgartner steered a powerful header into the ground and towards the Turkey goal but Günok somehow launched himself to his right to push the ball beyond the post. “It was one of the best moments of my life,” he said. “I have a photo of the save which I will frame and put up on my wall.”

players.name: Zeki Celik
players.bio: The youngest of nine brothers, he was raised in Bursa but has spent most of his professional career in Ligue 1 (Lille) and the Serie A (Roma). He is a self-confessed football obsessive who once admitted that he spent 45 consecutive days during the 2020 lockdown playing football video games to maintain his tactical sharpness. "I sleep thinking about football and I wake up thinking about football," he has said. One of the most consistent and reliable players in the team and he is set to be a certain starter in North America. Having arrived at Roma when José Mourinho was the manager, Celik told Il Romanista what he made of the Portuguese: "Seen from the outside he gives the impression of being a character, and … basically he is. Seen from the inside he is someone who treats players very well and who knows how to make them love him."

players.name: Merih Demiral
players.bio: Demiral has rediscovered his form and confidence after a rocky period, which included an ACL injury while at Juventus in January 2020. After a spell at Atalanta he now appears settled at Al-Ahli. The 1.90m tall defender is a natural leader; the type of centre-back who celebrates a successful sliding tackle as if he has scored the most important goal ever. The 28-year-old also poses a threat from set-pieces going forwards and scored both goals in the Euro 2024 quarter-final against Austria, the first one coming after 57 seconds. He has been involved in controversy too; first in 2019 as he was one of several Turkish players making a so-called “military salute” goal celebration against Albania, and then for making a “wolf-salute” after the win against Austria. Demiral was unrepentant in face of criticism and vowed to do it again while the incident led to Turkey’s ambassador to Germany being summoned to the foreign ministry in Berlin to explain the gesture and take measures to prevent its further use. Demiral was banned for two matches by Uefa.

players.name: Caglar Soyuncu
players.bio: Not long ago the man with the most recognisable jawline in football - and a distinctive haircut to go with it – was a guaranteed starter at the heart of the Turkish defence, but these days he is rarely picked by Vincenzo Montella. In fact, he did not even make the squad for the World Cup play-off games against Romania and Kosovo. Was probably at the peak of his career at Leicester, between 2018 and 2023, winning the FA Cup. He then joined Atlético Madrid and is now back in Turkey with Fenerbahce. In 2022 he told the Times about leaving home as an 11-year-old to board and play at Bucaspor, a club on the outskirts of Izmir. “Leaving home when I was 11 was very painful,” he said. “Tears? Yes, at night. Some players would go back to their homes after training but there were about 25 of us there, staying in like a dormitory, working really hard, cleaning toilets, sweeping the floor, doing washing up in order to learn about life. The lights would go off at 9pm. We were like orphans.”

players.name: Salih Özcan
players.bio: A quirky detail: Özcan is a highly skilled amateur wrestler, which is befitting the style of football he plays. A disciplined and combative midfielder with years of Bundesliga experience, he grew up in Cologne and represented Germany at youth levels before switching to Turkey, driven by a desire to represent his heritage. Known for his professionalism, he is often the first player on the training pitch and the last to leave. Nicknamed ‘The cleaner’ in Germany, he made almost 130 appearances for Cologne before the switch to Borussia Dortmund in 2025. Talking to the BVB website about why enjoys wrestling so much he said: “Probably that it's completely different from football. In wrestling, you use your entire body; from head to toe, you have to be tense every second. And there are always new techniques you hadn't even thought of before. Once you start, you always want to keep going, always want to learn more.”

players.name: Orkun Kokcu
players.bio: Kokcu made the surprise move from Benfica to Besiktas this summer, saying: "Finally, I am playing for the team of my childhood dreams." And he has excelled in Istanbul. The midfield dynamo is one of the unsung heroes of his team: a lot of what he brings to the table goes unnoticed but he puts in a lot of hard work and keeps things ticking over. Like Kadioglu he was born in the Netherlands and, like Kadioglu, expect him to make a big impact in North America. Things did not go according to plan at Benfica after a £26m move from Feyenoord in 2023 and it all came to a head with an almighty fallout with the coach, Bruno Lage, at the Club World Cup in 2025. Benfica were winning 2-0 against Auckland when Lage took off the No 10, which led to a row between the two and ended with the manager shushing his own player.

players.name: Kerem Akturkoglu
players.bio: Known to fans as Harry Potter for his signature goal celebration, waving an imaginary magic wand; Akturkoglu is one of few footballers to have played for both Galatasaray and Fenerbahce. The winger wants to demonstrate magic of a different kind this summer: "I want to show the world that Turkish players have magic in their feet," he says. Has been playing up front for Turkey despite being a traditional winger as Vincenzo Montella has lacked options at No 9 because of injuries but his place in the side is not guaranteed: it is the one position that is still up for grabs.

players.name: Arda Guler
players.bio: “The last time we were able to go to the World Cup, I didn’t even exist,” Guler said after Turkey had qualified for the tournament for the first time since 2002 – and the Real Madrid man is the undisputed face of this side. After proving himself at one of the biggest clubs in the world and shining for the national team he is now preparing to take centre stage in his biggest test yet. “We are talking about an undisputed, elite world-class talent,” is how Thierry Henry recently described the Turkish attacking midfielder. The 21-year-old may have a babyface but he is no pushover and not a luxury player.
