The midfielder was involved in a staggering 44 percent of the Red-Whites goals in games he played in for the 2025-26 qualifying campaign and he showed his passion and precociousness in the Champions League quarter-final away leg against Bayern. First he scored an outrageous long-range goal in the first minute, then a second within half an hour before getting sent off in added-on time for dissent.

players.name: Deniz Gul
players.bio: Born in Stockholm and raised in the Hammarby youth system, Gul was a staple of Sweden’s youth setups until a late-night phone call from Vincenzo Montella in late 2024 changed his trajectory. He is a modern, mobile No 9 with a 1.90m frame and on paper just what the national team have been missing up front. Since moving to Porto for a mere €4.5m, his development has accelerated. "I followed my heart; representing Turkey is a dream for my family," he said upon receiving his first senior call-up. He has yet to cement his place with the national team but his is the position that remains up for grabs heading into the World Cup.

players.name: Hakan Calhanoglu
players.bio: Never one to shy away from self adulation, Calhanoglu has a reputation as being a free-kick master - despite not having a great scoring record from setpieces - and in 2024 described himself as “the best deep-lying playmaker in the world right now”. Rodri was second on Calhanoglu’s list and Toni Kroos third. "I may be further away from the box now," he says of his more defensive role, "but I have greater control over the game." To be fair, he was instrumental as Inter reached the Champions League final in 2025 and, although he has never quite replicated his club form at international level, the fact that the spotlight will be on Arda Guler and Kenan Yildiz this summer may have a liberating effect on him.

players.name: Kenan Yildiz
players.bio: All eyes will be on Guler but Yildiz could end up stealing the show this summer with his raw power, skill and speed set to trouble USA, Australia and Paraguay. Alessandro Del Piero, whose iconic tongue-out celebration Yıidiz has adopted, has been full of praise. "He has a personality that defies his age," Del Piero said. "When I see him play, I see a bit of myself, but he has his own unique magic." The 21-year-old is in red hot form heading into the World Cup, having tormented defenders both domestically and internationally in 2025-26. Make no mistake, Yildiz has just as much potential as Guler to shine this summer. Born in Germany to a Turkish father and German mother, he made his international debut for Turkey – and scored – against Germany in Berlin during a 3-2 win. One of his parents was at the game. “My dad was there, which meant a lot to me," he told Fifa.com. "My mother couldn't make it because we have a small dog and she had to stay at home, so she was watching on TV. I think they were both very proud of me.”

players.name: Altay Bayindir
players.bio: Bayindir started his career off as a striker: “I grew up playing on the streets and I used to play as a striker but then I would go in goal to protect our lead after racking up the score because I was the tallest kid,” he has said. Played for Bursaspor and Ankaragucu during his youth career before four years at Fenerbahce. In 2023 he joined Manchester United but playing time has been limited. First he was behind André Onana in the pecking order and then, having started the 2025-26 campaign as the No 1, he was quickly replaced by Senne Lammens. Bayindir is an agile goalkeeper but he is also prone to the odd mistake. The former United goalkeeper, Edwin van der Sar was one of Bayindir’s idols growing up and told the official club website that his family was so happy that he had joined the Premier League side: “We are talking every day, they are incredibly happy for me and I know I have a big responsibility representing the club.”

players.name: Eren Elmali
players.bio: Life was good for Elmali in 2025. In February he had joined Galatasaray and by May he had picked up his first Süper Lig winner’s medal. In November, however, his world came crashing down as he was suspended for 45 days by the Turkish FA after a widespread investigation into illegal betting on football in the country. He was also removed from the national team squad that was due to play Spain. He said he had put a bet on five years ago “on a game that did not involve his own team at the time, Kasimpasa. He said: "I didn't know I would become this kind of football player back then” and credits his older brother for intervening before the habit escalated: "My brother told me it was forbidden for a professional [to bet on football games]; he saved my career before it really began." He has since been welcomed back to the fold and earned the trust of Vincenzo Montella.

players.name: Abdulkerim Bardakci
players.bio: A late bloomer who has become the bedrock of the Turkish defence, it is hard to believe he spent years in the footballing wilderness before his ascent onto the main stage. After many years flitting between clubs such as Konyaspor, Samsunspor and Giresunspor, Galatasaray gambled on him in 2022 – and have not regretted it. He is now the vice-captain at the club; dominant in the air and surprisingly adept on the ball for such a burly player. Having been part of the team that reached the quarter-finals of Euro 2024, he is going into the World Cup with high hopes: “There is nothing we cannot achieve with this team,” he says. “We have become like a family.”

players.name: Ozan Kabak
players.bio: "I've learned something from every league I've played in," says Kabak and it is that breadth of knowledge that makes the central defender such a vital asset for the Turkey national team coach, Vincenzo Montella. Having joined Galatasaray at a young age, he moved to Germany and Stuttgart at the age of 18 in 2019. He has also played in the Premier League for Liverpool and in the Championship with Norwich. After initially struggling under Jürgen Klopp during a loan spell in 2021 during an injury crisis he became a fan favourite and even had a song dedicated to him. Sung to the tune of the video game Tetris it went like this: “He’s Ozan. He’s Ozan. He’s Ozan Kabak. Ozan Kabak. Well, he plays centre-back. Well, he plays centre-back for Liverpool.” He speaks four languages and has filled in as the team's unofficial translator. On the pitch he brings tactical intelligence to the defensive unit and is known for his physical bravery and ability to read the game.

players.name: Ismail Yuksek
players.bio: Not long ago Yuksek was playing semi-professional football in the Turkish third tier with Gölcükspor, acting as a shield for his defence. "I will ensure the most talented players don't have to worry about the ball," he said. He is happy to do the dirty work so that his more technically gifted teammates in attack have more time and space to work their magic. Joined Fenerbahce back in 2020 but had to go on several loans before establishing himself in the Super Lig side’s engine room. For the national team he has also become an important player alongside Hakan Calhanoglu in central midfield. In 2024, when asked if he would consider a move abroad, he said: “The important thing is the right time, right conditions, and the system they play in. My adaptation process there is also very important. The club I would like to go to in Europe is my childhood dream, Manchester United.”

players.name: Irfan Can Kahveci
players.bio: To salvage his World Cup hopes, Kahveci made a high-profile loan move to Kasımpaşa in January, where he has recaptured his form. Despite his fiery temper, he remains a technically gifted player and, listen to this, is one of only three players in history – alongside Ronaldo (R9) and Gareth Bale – to score a Champions League hat-trick and still lose the match. He celebrates his goals with a "coffee-drinking" gesture and his surname does actually mean coffee maker. In November he was thrown out of the Fenerbahce squad together with Cenk Tosun and then took a break from social media. After a short silence he returned with the following message: “ I will write my own thoughts and feelings I believe you [the fans] deserve more than this. During this process, I was completely away from social media and focused on my injury and my family. First of all, I completely respect the decision made. I have no problem with either our management or my coach, and I have not had any throughout this process. The same goes for all my teammates.”

players.name: Mert Muldur
players.bio: Born in Vienna to Turkish parents, he made his way through the Rapid Vienna youth teams before making his first-team debut as a 19-year-old in 2018. He had started out as a striker but one day his Under-14 coach moved all the players around for a single training session and Muldur ended up as centre-back. He excelled – and never looked back. He speaks five languages: Turkish and German from home, English from school, and Bosnian and Serbian to communicate with his long-term partner. Before Turkey played Austria at Euro 2024 he said: “I grew up in Austria. I went to school in Austria. I learned to play football there and of course there’s a specific connection to Austria. But in Turkey I went through the Under-17, Under-19 national teams and then the senior national team. And, basically, you don’t have another chance to say yes. So for me, it wasn’t an option to go to Austria.” Having overcome a horrific ankle injury that threatened to derail his career while with Sassuolo in Italy, he has reinvented himself at Fenerbahce.

players.name: Yunus Akgun
players.bio: A tireless winger with electrifying pace, he has been a favorite of the Turkey head coach, Vincenzo Montella, since their time together at Adana Demirspor. He is not a starter but is a player Montella trusts. Having initially joined Galatasaray from Adana in 2021 he went out on loan – twice, the second time to Leicester – before establishing himself in the GS first team. While in England his manager, Enzo Maresca, said: “Yunus is struggling a little bit with the rhythm, probably because he said in Turkey the rhythm is a little bit low. Here it’s more quick, the pace of the game. I like Yunus, I would like to give him more minutes, but he needs to adjust and understand how English football is, that it’s more physical.” Had 26 goal involvements in 2024-25 and is not far off those numbers this campaign at the time of writing.

players.name: Ferdi Kadioglu
players.bio: One of Brighton’s players of the season, Kadioglu has excelled in the Premier League following his move from Fenerbahce in 2024. His manager, Fabian Hürzeler, recently described him as the "ultimate total footballer," noting that his "understanding of the game is so elite that he makes every tactical shift look seamless”. Born in Arnhem to a Turkish father and a Dutch-Canadian mother he could have represented either of the three countries. After becoming the youngest ever player to represent NEC Nijmegen in the Eredivisie at the age of 16 years and 326 days in 2016, he moved to Fenerbahce two years later. “It was a big step, coming from a small city in Holland to move to Istanbul,” he told the Athletic. “It was huge for me and it was not easy; a new team, a new style of play. Everything was new, so I needed time to adapt.” For the national team he has built a strong connection with Kenan Yildiz down the flank and the duo are a nightmare for the opposition when they get going.

players.name: Baris Alper Yilmaz
players.bio: They call him ‘The Tank’ and he works tirelessly down the wing. He is strong, fast and skillful for such a physical player. He traces his raw power back to his roots in Rize and his father’s trade. "My father was a butcher," he once said. "I grew up eating a lot of meat because of him; and I believe that is where my strength comes from." He may lack the finesse of some of the more technical players in the team, and that can frustrate fans at times, but at his best he is pretty much unplayable. Along with Yildiz he adds physicality to a side that has lacked in this department in the past.

players.name: Kaan Ayhan
players.bio: Ayhan can operate at centre-back, right-back or defensive midfield and his utility is one of the main reasons he is in the squad. He is famously close with his family and when he signed for Galatasaray in 2023 he admitted that he still consults his father on almost every major career move. "My father has always been my most honest critic," he said. His tactical intelligence allows him to move between positions and Vincenzo Montella once remarked that he “understands the game better than most coaches". The 31-year-old rarely starts for Turkey but he does offer Montella flexibility from the bench.

players.name: Ugurcan Cakir
players.bio: Cakir had big gloves to fill when he moved to Galatasaray in a record-shattering €27.5m deal in 2025, replacing the legendary Fernando Muslera, but he has stepped up to another level and arrives at the World Cup in the form of his life following an impressive season in the league and the Champions League. Born in Antalya, he started his professional career with boyhood club Trabzonspor and made nearly 300 appearances for the club before last year’s switch to GS. The highlight? Winning the Süper Lig in 2021-22. “Being part of a team that won the league for the first time in 38 years, contributing towards that triumph and being captain of the team was very special,” he told Unfiltered in 2024. “I will never forget that feeling for the rest of my life.” Representing Turkey means everything to the 30-year-old. "The flag is my motivation; the jersey is my armour,” he has said.

players.name: Oguz Aydin
players.bio: The winger is one of several players in the squad born in the Netherlands – alongside Ferdi Kadioglu and Orkun Kokcu. Following a productive stint at Alanyaspor, he earned a move to Fenerbahce in 2024. His development took another step forward while playing under José Mourinho. "He [Mourinho] always told me to take it ‘step by step.’” he said “I kept myself ready and was waiting for my chance. I believe I made the most of it.” Direct, two-footed, and devoid of ego, Aydin is a reliable squad player the Turkey head coach, Vincenzo Montella, can call on when required. He can play across the forward line and is happy being a utility man.

players.name: Samet Akaydin
players.bio: His rise to prominence was so unlikely that, when he finally made it to the big time with Adana Demirspor, he admitted that he had kept his old kits from the lower leagues as a reminder of the struggle. "I never forget where I came from; those pitches in the third tier made me the defender I am today," he says. Now 32, he is at Caykur Rizespor after spells with big clubs such as Panathinaikos and Fenerbahce. He remains a polarising figure amid the fan base because of his occasional lapses of concentration, but his physical edge has seen him called up to the national team regularly by the coach, Vincenzo Montella.

players.name: Can Uzun
players.bio: Another member of the 2005 golden generation, Uzun has the potential to join Guler and Yildiz as another genuine star of this team. In 2025-26 he showed glimpses of his talent but the campaign was also restricted by injuries and he has yet to establish himself as a starter for the national team. Born in Regensburg, the German FA was keen to convince him to play for the Nationalmannschaft, but Uzun opted to represent Turkey. "I listened to my heart," he said of his decision to wear the red shirt of the Crescent-Stars. The national team coach, Vincenzo Montella, is a big admirer, saying: "Our belief in his potential is immense." A silky, versatile attacker who can play as a false nine or a traditional No 10, drifting between lines, he made his international debut while playing in the German second division with Nuremberg. Keep an eye on him – he could be the surprise package of this team at the World Cup.

players.name: Sergio Rochet
players.bio: Was fairly unknown when he appeared in Danubio’s league title celebrations in 2014. Then the third-choice keeper at 21, Rochet was soon packing his bags for the Netherlands and AZ Alkmaar. In 2019 he was brought back to Uruguay by Nacional to be the back-up to Luis Mejía. “He was on holiday back home, in his house on the water, when he told us that his move to Nacional had been confirmed. It was his dream,” said his mother, Graciela. She could barely believe it herself when he played for Uruguay at the 2022 World Cup. “The day I saw him in the first game was a shock. I stopped in front of the TV and the only thing I did was cry,” she said.

players.name: José María Giménez
players.bio: When he was playing in Danubio’s academy at 17, his 14-year-old sister Agustina fell pregnant. ‘Josema’, his eyes filled with tears, told his parents that he would provide for the baby. He considered leaving football and finding a job but Danubio helped him to keep playing. At 18, he signed for Atlético Madrid and earned his first international cap, facing Colombia’s Radamel Falcao on his debut. “I would say things to distract him. For example, during an attack, I asked him: ‘Got a car in Madrid?’” Days later, Giménez inked the date of the game on his body. Now with over 100 caps and three World Cups under his belt, the centre-back goes into this summer as captain after the retirement of Diego Godín, his “partner of a thousand battles” and fellow La Liga winner at Atlético.

players.name: Sebastián Cáceres
players.bio: Days before the final of the Torneo Intermedio that falls between the two halves of the Uruguayan domestic season, Cáceres had an offer on the table from América in Mexico. Instead of accepting the move and the millions that would set him up for life, he decided to stay at his boyhood club, Liverpool, in Montevideo. The final ended in glory. “I wanted to be a champion with Liverpool. I wanted to play that game for the club that made me,” he said. América came back the following year and the centre-back has won three league titles there. Has played for Uruguay at every level from under-17 upwards, making his senior bow in 2022.

players.name: Ronald Araújo
players.bio: Araújo has come through a difficult spell in his life. He made his senior debut for Rentistas aged 16 and has been under the spotlight ever since. Towards the end of last year, after a red card against Chelsea in the Champions League, he decided he needed to step away from the game to receive treatment for depression, which had been affecting him for more than a year already. “We have to understand that aside from being footballers, we are people,” he said when he made his return 47 days later. The centre-back, who was training with Boca Juniors aged 10, made his international debut at 21. He went to the last World Cup but injury prevented him from making an appearance in Qatar. A two-time La Liga champion, he is now part of Barcelona’s captaincy team.

players.name: Manuel Ugarte
players.bio: This year is Ugarte’s 25th on this planet and his 10th in professional football. He was a 15-year-old prodigy at Fénix and, in four years in Europe, he went from Famalição in Portugal to Manchester United via Sporting and PSG. The midfielder made his international bow aged 20, his mother saying: “Manuel called me and his voice was trembling because he was sharing a dressing room with teammates that, until a few days before, he was playing with on the PlayStation.” ‘El Poeta’ (the poet), as they call him, has appeared on social media alongside his mum dancing to plena, a genre of music with afro-caribbean roots. On the football pitch his defining characteristic is hard work. “You have to leave it all out there,” he says.

players.name: Rodrigo Bentancur
players.bio: A 12-year-old Bentancur left his little inland town of 11,000 people to take the plunge in Buenos Aires. Boca Juniors thought they had found a special talent in the tall and skinny kid. “It was difficult. I didn’t want to know anything about it, but my dad convinced me,” he said. He was part of the Uruguay team that won the Under-20 South American title in 2017 and, by the end of the year, was a full international and a Juventus player. At his first World Cup he set up Edinson Cavani’s winner against Portugal in the Last 16. “I celebrated as if I’d scored it myself,” he said. Injury problems have stalled his promising career but the Spurs midfielder is now a senior member of Marcelo Bielsa’s squad. “He has the qualities to play anywhere on the pitch,” the head coach says.

players.name: Nicolás de la Cruz
players.bio: De la Cruz was offered a contract by the Montevideo side Liverpool before he’d even kicked a ball in his first training session. His ability and attitude had been obvious from the start. “He loved getting kicked,” said a youth teammate of his. He was as crafty off the pitch as he was on it. In meetings that took place at the training ground he would sit in the chair of Liverpool’s historic president, José Luis Palma, with such impudence that instead of resenting the youngster, Palma could only smile. His biggest idol was his half-brother, the former Uruguay winger Carlos Sánchez, and the pair have both played for River Plate. De la Cruz went to the 2022 World Cup and has since come back from a knee injury that a Flamengo club doctor said he would not recover from. “Never let anyone tell you that you can’t do anything,” he said.

players.name: Federico Valverde
players.bio: One of the world’s best had humble beginnings. He doesn’t like admitting it, simply saying his parents “were very hard-working”. He would accompany his mother to her market stall and started playing football so young that he was still wearing nappies. When he arrived at Real Madrid in 2016 feeling like “Cristiano Ronaldo and Messi put together”, he was self-conscious when comparing his clothes with those of his peers. “You’re wearing a €2 T-shirt,” he said to himself. Ironically for a player who used to annoy his youth coaches because he didn’t like running, the midfielder has risen up the ranks at Madrid thanks to his outstanding physical attributes. He scored on debut for Uruguay aged 19 but missed out on the World Cup in 2018, which was a blow. He arrives at this one as his country’s talisman. A hat-trick against Manchester City in the Champions League in March confirmed his elite status. His 2025-26 season ended on a low, however, as he was taken to hospital after an altercation with his Real teammate Aurélien Tchouaméni. In a statement Valverde said: “At no point did my team-mate hit me, and I didn’t hit him either.”

players.name: Darwin Núñez
players.bio: “One day you’ll see me playing in Europe,” Núñez used to tell his coaches at youth level in Artigas in northern Uruguay. His parents worked overtime to try to provide for him, including buying him shoes to play football in. “I don’t forget where I came from … My mother used to go out to collect bottles on the street that she could sell,” he said. Serious knee injuries as a teenager made Núñez consider quitting football but he chose to fight. A rapid rise took in Peñarol, Almería and Benfica before a big move to Liverpool. In three years at Anfield he won the Premier League and the League Cup. The 26-year-old is now in Saudi Arabia. He spent months without playing in the Pro League after Karim Benzema took his place as a designated foreign player in January. This will be his second World Cup.

players.name: Giorgian de Arrascaeta
players.bio: A move to Europe is the ultimate goal for most footballers in South America. Not for De Arrascaeta. The 32-year-old is one of the best players on a continent he has never left. He won two Brazilian cups with Cruzeiro and is now an idol at Flamengo after picking up 20 trophies in seven years in Brazil, including three Copa Libertadores. Despite these feats, he has had his fair share of critics with the national team because he often fails to produce his club form in celeste colours. “Criticism is part and parcel of football and you get used to it with experience,” he says. He scored twice against Ghana in the final group game of the 2022 World Cup but events elsewhere consigned Uruguay to an early exit.

players.name: Facundo Pellistri
players.bio: Manchester United paid US$10m to sign an 18-year-old Pellistri from Peñarol in 2020. He has not hit the heights once expected of him, when the former Uruguay international Carlos Aguilera said he had the potential to be “one of the best in the world”. The son of an accountant and a paediatrician whose godfather is a singer in a famous Uruguayan rock band, Pellistri struck the right chords in his first World Cup. He shone as an unexpected starter in Diego Alonso’s team of 2022 but has seen his club career stall in multiple underwhelming loan spells in Spain. Now in Greece on a permanent basis, playing under Rafa Benítez.

players.name: Santiago Mele
players.bio: Mele was all over the Uruguayan front pages when he made two saves in a penalty shootout victory against Portugal at the 2017 Under-20 World Cup. After the game, he revealed his secret: “I was watching them. I wanted to play mind games because I know that has a really big influence.” A move to Europe followed soon after but he played very little during spells in Turkey and Spain. Plaza Colonia offered him a chance to start over and, after spells in Argentina and Colombia, the national team started to take an interest. “Those three years in Turkey were difficult – so was returning to Uruguay to take charge of my career again. Alongside giving myself to God and trusting in him, these are the fundamental reasons why I am where I am,” he said in 2023 after his first senior cap.

players.name: Guillermo Varela
players.bio: Has revived his career in Brazil after initially leaving Europe under a cloud. His performances at the Under-20 World Cup in 2013, where Uruguay reached the final, were enough to attract interest from Manchester United. From there, he went on loan to Real Madrid – only playing for the B team – and Eintracht Frankfurt. His time in Germany ended on a sour note because of a swollen tattoo he got shortly before the DFB-Pokal final. “All the other players had tattoos,” he said. “In my case, I didn’t get an infection but the coach said I’d disrespected him because I missed a training session a few days before the final.” After spells in Denmark and Russia, the defender has won two Copa Libertadores titles with Flamengo.

players.name: Agustín Canobbio
players.bio: Canobbio has a very particular story. He was a late grower and was left out of national team age groups despite his obvious talents. The set-backs kept coming: aged 22 he suffered a double fracture of his leg. But he refused to give up and after making his return he said: “A year after my injury I remember what I have been through and the path I travelled and now I value more than ever the opportunity to be able to enjoy this beautiful sport again.” And it showed on the pitch. At 23 he was chosen as the best player in the Uruguayan league and moved to Brazil after which he was given his national team debut. He is the son of the former Uruguayan striker Osvaldo, and has a motto that inspires him daily: “You have to live for football and then live from football.”

players.name: Mathías Olivera
players.bio: Although he has made his name as a left-back, Bielsa deploys him as a centre-back. “He’s quite a slight centre-back who is comfortable in wide positions and I like this type of centre-back with a past life as a full-back,” the head coach said in 2024. Olivera, the son of teenage parents, stood out from a young age because of his natural speed, power and ball striking. His first steps in Europe were hampered by homesickness. He passed a medical at Galatasaray aged 19 but did not sign a contract, joining Getafe in Spain instead. “I really missed home. I cried every day and wanted to go back but obviously I couldn’t.” He became a mainstay under José Bordalás and made his Uruguay debut in 2019. Since playing at the 2022 World Cup – “a dream” – he has won two Serie A titles with Napoli.

players.name: Matías Viña
players.bio: Resilience is one of his biggest strengths. When on the verge of signing his first pro contract with Nacional, the defender broke his collarbone. The club paused the deal but waited for him to complete his recovery. He competed for the left-back place during his academy days with Mathías Olivera, his competition in the Uruguay squad now. He struggled to establish himself in the Nacional first team as a result, sometimes playing at centre-back but often not at all, and didn’t become a regular until the age of 22. “It got me down at times because I wasn’t playing but my family and friends helped me a lot. They kept telling me my time would come,” he said. In 2019 he made his international debut and kickstarted his club career. He’s a three-time Copa Libertadores winner and was signed by José Mourinho at Roma, where he had a loan spell at Bournemouth.

players.name: Brian Rodríguez
players.bio: Rodríguez left his home town of Tranqueras, population 7,000, on the northern border with Brazil to join the youth ranks of Tacuarembó, landing a trial at Peñarol at the age of 15. The first training session was tough. “They kicked him and stole the ball off him, but he put up with it well,” remembered his coach, José Perdomo. Rodríguez’s conduct cost him at times. He was made to train with younger years twice because of bad behaviour but made his first-team debut at Peñarol in 2018. “He recognises and learns from his mistakes,” said Alejandro Valenzuela, his personal coach from a young age. When playing in MLS with LA FC aged 19 he was given a first senior Uruguay call-up. Has won three league titles in Mexico. “Those who know me know what it’s taken for me to get here,” he says.

players.name: Rodrigo Aguirre
players.bio: Has been through the ringer during his career. At the Under-17 World Cup in 2011 the striker was knocked out in the final against Mexico and woke up in hospital. “The first thing I remember was when the doctors asked me what my name was and what day I was born,” he said. Two years later he missed the Under-20 World Cup owing to a heart problem. Aguirre is well travelled but it wasn’t until a purple patch at América in Mexico that he caught Marcelo Bielsa’s eye. He made his full international debut aged 30 and scored against Colombia in a World Cup qualifier at the Estadio Centenario. “I couldn’t have dreamed it better,” he said.

players.name: Maximiliano Araújo
players.bio: In 2012 a video went viral in Uruguay showing a small child with the No 10 on his back running down the left wing and setting up a goal in a youth football match. This was Maximiliano Araújo crossing the ball for his brother, César, to win the prestigious Danone Cup. The video shows César holding back tears after some wild celebrations. Both boys are now professional footballers and have shared a dressing room at international level. “Maxi” is still running down the left wing. Marcelo Bielsa was once asked to describe the Sporting player. “I think Maxi’s a terrible player who got where he is because of luck. He won’t get any further. I’ve never seen him play – he was recommended to me.” It was a joke, though no one got it. “I like players who cause problems, who run directly from one box to the other, who get lots of crosses in and Maxi has all those traits,” he clarified.

players.name: Federico Viñas
players.bio: “I’m Federico Viñas, it’s possible they confused me with Matías Viña,” the striker said when he was called up by Uruguay for the first time – he couldn’t quite believe it. Playing international football is a far cry from the Casabó neighbourhood in Montevideo where Viñas earned a living delivering meat produce. One day he went to try out for the amateur team Mar de Fondo. From there he went to Juventud in Las Piedras. The striker, nicknamed ‘El Tanque’ (the tank), arrived 15kg overweight but made his first-team debut a year later in Uruguay’s second tier. Interest from América in Mexico was almost as unexpected as the international call-up that followed a few years later. Has spent the last two seasons on loan at Real Oviedo, winning promotion to La Liga in the first.

players.name: Joaquín Piquerez
players.bio: Nine months after making his senior debut for Defensor Sporting, a 19-year-old Piquerez stepped up to take a penalty in the Uruguayan championship final against Peñarol and missed, Defensor losing the shootout 4-2. Two years later, when he was 21, he was released. “I grew up at Defensor and this isn’t the way I wanted to leave,” he said. He could have fallen by the wayside but he used that setback as fuel. Six months at Uruguay’s River Plate opened the door to Peñarol and his knack of scoring from distance saw Palmeiras come calling. In 2021 Piquerez went full circle, lifting the Copa Libertadores at Montevideo’s Estadio Centenario, where he had missed that penalty four years earlier. Has become a regular under Marcelo Bielsa but picked up an ankle injury in March’s friendly draw with England.

players.name: Fernando Muslera
players.bio: Muslera was born in Buenos Aires on the day a Maradona-inspired Argentina beat Uruguay in the Last 16 at the 1986 World Cup, but there was never any doubt about which country would win the knockout tie for his allegiance – he was named after the Uruguayan striker Fernando Morena, and his family moved to Uruguay when he was an infant. “My passport says that I have Argentinian nationality, but I feel one hundred per cent Uruguayan,” he says. His life changed one day as an eight-year-old, already a keen footballer and prolific goalscorer, when his team’s goalkeeper failed to turn up for a match. “Like any kid I just wanted to play,” he recalled. “I didn’t care what position – though back then I didn’t like playing in goal at all.” It would be fair to say he was not temperamentally suited to the position. “Whenever I conceded a goal, I would start crying,” he said. “I didn’t want my team to lose. I took it very personally, as if it was my fault.” Now he is making history, as the first Uruguayan to feature in five World Cups.

players.name: Santiago Bueno
players.bio: In the middle of the Under-20 World Cup in 2017, while he was starting at centre-back for Uruguay, Bueno signed a contract with Barcelona. It was a massive jump for a player who still hadn’t made a senior appearance for Peñarol in the Primera División. He arrived in Catalonia as an under-20 South American champion but struggled to gain a foothold in the European game. Two years later, he left Barcelona as a free agent and joined Girona. “I had to take a step back in order to take two steps forward,” he said. The gamble paid off and within a couple of years Bueno was playing in La Liga. A move to the Premier League with Wolves followed and he has been dreaming “every day” for a spot on the plane to the World Cup.

players.name: Juan Manuel Sanabria
players.bio: After winning the Under-20 Copa Libertadores with Nacional, an 18-year-old Sanabria signed for Atlético Madrid. It was a big change for a kid who used to travel the 100km to training and back in Montevideo so he could “chill out at home” in Florida during his time in the Nacional academy. In a country such as Uruguay, that sort of daily commute is an immense undertaking. Sanabria played for Atlético’s B team before leaving for their feeder club in Mexico, Atlético San Luis, where he swapped midfield for left-back. “He’s technically excellent. No one has more rhythm than him and he can play as a full-back, in central midfield or as a winger,” said Marcelo Bielsa, who called him up for the first time last year.

players.name: Rodrigo Zalazar
players.bio: José Zalazar was one of a number of 1980s Uruguayan footballers who started the trend of upping sticks for Europe. Central to his game was his powerful shooting, on display in Spain for more than 10 years. Rodrigo was born in the city of Albacete, whose club his father made over 200 appearances for. His youth career was spent in Spain before spells at senior level in Germany and Poland. Unlike his brother Kuki, who played at youth level for Spain, Rodrigo’s national allegiance was never in doubt. “I never thought about playing for Spain. I always dreamed of playing for Uruguay,” Zalazar said when he arrived in Montevideo after his first call-up in Bielsa’s first squad in 2023. Since scoring twice on his debut against Nicaragua, international minutes have been few and far between.

players.name: Matt Turner
players.bio: The stories you hear about Turner’s high school soccer career feel like borderline mythology. Teammates will tell you he saved 30, or sometimes 40 shots a game, insane statistics that are, shockingly enough, backed up by newspaper reports from the time. Turner only started playing the sport when he was 14, and he was a late bloomer to the national team as well, first joining it in 2021; a year later, he’d start all four of the USA’s matches at the 2022 World Cup and for a while he appeared to be the closest thing the US had to a lock starter in goal. He’s recently been supplanted, and his career abroad also did not quite pan out, with Turner returning to the New England Revolution ahead of the 2026 season. Regardless, Turner’s career arc remains remarkable given his origin story.

players.name: Sergiño Dest
players.bio: Dest has always done things a little differently. He became the first American player to ever appear for Barcelona, playing alongside Lionel Messi. When the Argentinian legend announced he’d be leaving the club, most of his teammates showed up in a suit and tie. Dest showed up in a Chicago Bulls jersey and shorts, owing to the fact that he’d forgotten to set an alarm. Dutch by birth, Dest idolized Brazilian legend Ronaldinho as a child and it shows in his play; he possesses flair and style that is not often seen in American players and particularly not defenders. Dest’s passion occasionally gets the best of him, as it did a few years ago in Concacaf Nations League play, when he was sent off for yelling not only at the ref, but also his own teammates.

players.name: Chris Richards
players.bio: The reigning USMNT player of the year. Hailing from Hoover, Alabama, Richards chose soccer over basketball after his under-16s team visited Argentina. Was cut from his first trial with FC Dallas’ academy before the MLS side extended an offer one year later. Something of a late bloomer, having signed his first pro contract with Bayern Munich just before turning 19. A loan with Hoffenheim set him on the path that sees him regularly start for Crystal Palace. Quickly emerging as a vocal leader for club and country alike; he was the only one to play all 540 minutes of Palace’s triumphant FA Cup run in 2025. Already the 10th-most appearances among USA outfield players in Premier League history. His leg bears a tattoo of a cartoon he drew at age three of himself playing soccer. If only we all could have such career clarity so soon …

players.name: Tyler Adams
players.bio: So important to the USA midfield that the fanbase’s morale plummets with each of his unfortunate injuries. Was the ballboy who supplied Thierry Henry with the ball for his iconic 2012 Olimpico for New York Red Bulls against Columbus Crew. Shoehorned into the Red Bulls lineup as a right-back before turning 17, he is now an established Premier League anchor. Studied Philipp Lahm, Pep Guardiola and Joshua Kimmich to refine his approach to defensive midfield work. The Scottish side of his family backs Rangers, and one uncle trained his dog to growl when they say “Celtic”. Every coach he has had says Adams is built for the big occasions; keeping him available is at the top of the physio department’s priorities.

players.name: Antonee Robinson
players.bio: The greatest American player to hail from Milton Keynes; a naturalized citizen as his father had moved to New York at age nine. Already a starter for this team in 2022, arguably saw his career peak soon after winning Fulham's player of the season award in 2023-24. Injuries plagued him throughout 2025-26, minimizing his impact before the season’s business end. Can execute an impressive strutting celebration on the rare occasions that he scores. Proudly nicknamed ‘Jedi’ in honor of his five-year-old obsession with Star Wars. “I introduce myself as Jedi these days just to make it easier,” Robinson told Wigan's website upon joining in 2018. “There’s quite a bit of force in it.” Has not publicly stated his views on Disney’s sequel trilogy. Also a budding illusionist who won’t shy away from learning a card trick.

players.name: Auston Trusty
players.bio: Trusty grew up in Media, Pennsylvania, surrounded by a friend group in which he was the only one who cared seriously about soccer. His connection to the game, then, came via family: Trusty’s sister, Onnie Nicholson, played with the USA women’s team at youth level alongside future stars such as Alyssa Neher, Kelley O’Hara and Tobin Heath. His journey, he says, has been almost single-mindedly geared towards being in the picture for the 2026 World Cup. After coming through the vaunted local Philadelphia Union youth academy, Trusty moved to Colorado before being a surprise sale to Arsenal. His stock really began to rise after establishing himself in the Premier League with Sheffield United, and then in Scotland with Celtic. At each stop, Trusty has remained, well, trusty, eventually finding a consistent, durable place along the backline.

players.name: Gio Reyna
players.bio: Reyna is descended from soccer royalty, the son of former USMNT captain and World Cup veteran Claudio Reyna. Reyna has sometimes struggled to escape that shadow, though it has nothing to do with his play on the field. During the 2022 World Cup, Reyna’s absence from the USA lineup was attributed to a “lack of commitment” by coach Gregg Berhalter and less than a year later, it was revealed that his mother had privately informed US Soccer during the tournament about damaging information regarding Berhalter’s past. The entire series of events has hung over Reyna’s national team career, and poor form and fitness at Mönchengladbach haven’t helped his case recently. A hugely talented player, he remains as polarizing as ever.

players.name: Weston McKennie
players.bio: McKennie is among the most talented players the USA has ever produced, and he is also among the most doubted. Formed in the youth system of FC Dallas and at the now-defunct US Soccer youth residency program in Bradenton, Florida, McKennie moved abroad to the Bundesliga before becoming the first American to appear for Italian giants Juventus in 2020. McKennie has at times struggled to be appropriately recognized at that club, and a disastrous loan spell to Leeds in 2023 didn’t help his case much. Even as a consistent contributor, the 27-year-old was frequently the subject of transfer rumors, or talked about as being dropped from the club’s plans. All of this despite the fact that very few on Juventus’ current roster have performed as consistently well as McKennie has over the past two years. At this point, the kid who grew up in Little Elm, Texas is among the club’s top 10 Champions League goalscorers. His recent form seems to have finally convinced someone that McKennie is among the world’s top midfielders – in March of this year, Juventus signed him through 2030.

players.name: Ricardo Pepi
players.bio: Among the last and more agonizing cuts from the 2022 World Cup roster, Pepi has done plenty to prove he deserves his spot in the USA team in the four years since Qatar. He has scored 10 of his 13 international goals in that span at the time of writing, and went from riding the bench at Augsburg to being a key part of a PSV side that have won three consecutive Dutch league titles. Born on the US-Mexico border, Pepi’s breakthrough came with FC Dallas after turning heads due to his impressive scoring rate at youth level. The rate was so impressive that fans began talking about the Ricardo Pepi hype train – lending him a nickname, El Tren, that sticks among some USA fans to this day.

players.name: Christian Pulisic
players.bio: This generation of USA players has more than ever playing at elite clubs in Europe, but none of them are as important to the team’s success as Pulisic. When he is on form, “the kid from Hershey, Pennsylvania” is more capable than anyone in the team of finding a way through defences, and finding small windows in which to take shots and, ideally, score goals. In 2025, he said “his body had started talking to him” and he opted out of the Gold Cup squad, a decision he was criticised for by certain former players, such as Landon Donovan and Tim Howard. “When it comes to [Donovan, Howard, and other ex-national team critics], it is tough because I looked up to those guys growing up,” he told CBS. “Some of these guys were my idols, and I respect them so much as players. Privately, the way they talk to me and clearly want to show support and be your friend and everything, and then they say something slightly different publicly. Does it hurt me? Am I surprised by it? I don’t know. Not really.” Though he’s surrounded by a more than capable USA roster, the pressure on Pulisic to perform at this World Cup is immense. He is the face of the program, even if he himself is more of an introvert.

players.name: Brenden Aaronson
players.bio: Long before Aaronson made his debut at Elland Road, the former Philadelphia Union midfielder was honing his skills in a Medford, New Jersey basement. His father, Rusty, was a former professional and he had set up the lower level of his house as a mini pitch of sorts, one where Aaronson, along with his brother Paxten, could hone their skills. The work paid off, with both players having plied their trade in Europe. A product of the Union’s academy, Aaronson’s 2020 transfer to RB Salzburg set what was at the time a record fee for an MLS homegrown player. By the time he joined Leeds years later, his value had only risen, and he has proven a key piece for Leeds in their current relegation battle. Not bad for a kid his teammates once called the “Medford Messi”.

players.name: Miles Robinson
players.bio: At age nine, Robinson stood 1.75mn and wore a size 13 shoe. Those are typically the characteristics of an up-and-coming basketball player, and indeed that was the sport where Robinson first impressed. Once he switched to soccer, though, he excelled – despite his first coach recalling that he was “looking like Bambi” out on the pitch. Robinson developed at Syracuse University, and broke through as a professional at Atlanta United, where his physical profile combined with impressive one-v-one defending ability and an advanced reading of the game to become one of the league’s best center-backs straight away. He was near-certain to play a big role for the USA at the 2022 World Cup, but a ruptured achilles months before the tournament ruled him out. A devastating injury, to be sure, but Robinson didn’t let the heartbreak keep him from watching with friends and family. “I was outside watching that shit,” he told Goal.com.

players.name: Tim Ream
players.bio: Ream probably never imagined he’d be the USMNT’s elder statesman some 20 years ago. At the time, he was driving a beat-up Pontiac Grand Am five hours from St Louis to Chicago to play developmental soccer. Now, Ream isn’t just the elder statesman, he’s something approaching an elderly statesman, in line to become the oldest player in the history of the men’s national team to appear in a World Cup. He has unquestionably lost a step or two, but his experience and calming presence is badly needed on a USA backline that’s still very young and occasionally error prone. Ream doesn’t chalk his longevity up to any given workout routine, instead citing the smaller things: walking his dogs, fishing and even zoning out while playing with Lego. “Grandpa,” as his USMNT teammates sometimes call him, has no plans to retire anytime soon, having said he feels he could play another two or three years. Hopefully the USA can find a replacement by 2030.

players.name: Sebastian Berhalter
players.bio: Perhaps not the Berhalter many fans expected to be involved when the 2026 World Cup cycle kicked off. Earned his USA debut under Mauricio Pochettino, going entirely uninvolved during his father’s six-year stretch as USMNT coach. Credits the ability to train with his dad during the Covid lockdown as unlocking more of his athleticism and potential. Briefly considered hanging up his boots after Austin FC declined a purchase option after his 2021 loan but Vancouver provided the launchpad he needed. One of MLS’s breakout stars in 2025, making the year-end best XI after joining forces with Thomas Müller to reach the club’s first MLS Cup final. His mother, Rosalind, was a two-time national champion on the University of North Carolina’s vaunted soccer team.

players.name: Cristian Roldan
players.bio: Roldan was still many years away from being a professional when he had his first brush with fame. The Seattle Sounders stalwart was just nine years old when he starred in a classic Adidas commercial, playing a young soccer player who spent the day gathering plastic bags, which he then made into a ball to juggle in a dirt plot beside a highway. That nine-year-old has gone on to make more than 400 appearances for the Sounders, standing as one of the most respected players in MLS. A quintessential “locker room guy,” Roldan has built his career on doing the dirty work that makes his teammates better, but he’s not immune to stepping up with a big goal when needed.

players.name: Alex Freeman
players.bio: Freeman had played just 10 minutes of first-division soccer before 2025 and is now heading to a World Cup. The son of former NFL legend Antonio Freeman, the Florida native serves as an example of Mauricio Pochettino’s proclivity towards youth and his willingness to offer opportunities to sometimes unheralded players. The defender has done well with those chances, having played a large part in the USMNT’s run to the Gold Cup finals last summer and even bagging a brace against Uruguay later in the year. A move to Villarreal in Spain's La Liga only solidified his place in Pochettino’s squad. "It's been a year since I really got onto the scene, and now, one year later, I'm in Spain," he told Goal.com. "It's hard to comprehend that aspect of it. You always have time to think as a pro footballer. You have training in the morning or in the afternoon and a decent amount of free time. You have time to think, but not really time to comprehend. It's hard to take it fully in."

players.name: Malik Tillman
players.bio: The latest in a long line of German-American dual-nationals to turn out for the USA team, Tillman’s place was far from a sure thing in 2022, when he filed a one-time switch from the country where he grew up. "It took me a lot of time to make the decision, but in the end I listened to what my heart told me, and, yeah, I hope it's the right decision,” he said. “I hope I like it," said Tillman. "I'm happy to be here." His place in the squad has solidified considerably in the last year, especially with a strong run of games over the summer of 2025, including the Concacaf Gold Cup. Quiet and reserved off the field, Tillman’s creative instincts on the pitch are renowned enough that Bayer Leverkusen brought him in to be Florian Wirtz’s replacement after he moved to Liverpool.

players.name: Max Arfsten
players.bio: Arfsten started 2025 out with an invitation to “camp cupcake,” the January camp the USMNT uses to examine its fringe players and has-beens, guys who rarely go on to become regulars. Sixteen caps later and Arfsten has established himself as one of Mauricio Pochettino’s most consistent contributors. The California-born wing-back is an ideal fit in Pochettino’s system, especially given his lack of left-sided options. Arfsten followed a pretty standard MLS developmental path and in 2023 became the first player in league history drafted out of MLS Next Pro. His form in Columbus – 10 goals and seven assists across all competitions in 2025, outstanding numbers for a largely defensive player – earned him interest from abroad. Making any kind of splash at a home World Cup would likely make those approaches even more concrete.

players.name: Haji Wright
players.bio: Against all odds, this World Cup will include a former New York Cosmo. Born some 58 years after Pelé, Wright’s professional career began in 2015 with three appearances on a squad that included Spain legends Raúl and Marcos Senna, but the second-division club opted not to renew his deal after one season. Wright didn’t stick with Schalke, VVV-Venlo or Sønderjyske, but a loan to Antalyaspor provided him with the finishing chops that endeared him to Frank Lampard at Coventry. One of three squad members with a World Cup goal. More dynamic than most who match his considerable stature, Wright idolized Ronaldinho and Thierry Henry as a kid and punishes defenses by cutting inwards from the left flank with similar glee.

players.name: Folarin Balogun
players.bio: Give the assist for this roster selection to the US commercial airline industry. Balogun was still in utero when his mother visited New York on vacation, but the return trip never happened as the airline refused to let her board the plane due to the size of her belly and how close she was to giving birth. As it happened, Balogun was born in Brooklyn and was off to London months later, where he fell in love with football and came up through the Arsenal academy. Now one of the most promising strikers in Europe, “Flo” is expected to lead the line for the USA, using his speed, tactical awareness, and deft finishing touch to give his birth country something to cheer about.

players.name: Tim Weah
players.bio: His father may be an icon of African soccer and Liberia’s former president, but it was Tim who opened the family’s World Cup goalscoring account in 2022. A nimble dribbler who can capably cross from the right and shoot off-center, Weah is an ideal foil opposite Christian Pulisic for the US. Weah writes and performs trap soul music on the side, having built a studio in his house while with Lille. Admitted that Juventus tested his confidence, as their rotating cast of coaches shuffled him both throughout the right wing and in and out of their teamsheets. More settled with Marseille, after getting a 4am call from Roberto De Zerbi as part of the French club’s recruitment. More fashionable than most of us can dream to be.

players.name: Mark McKenzie
players.bio: While most of his boyhood friends spent Saturday morning watching cartoons, McKenzie and his father fixated on Milan. The left-footed, ball-playing defender from the Philadelphia Union academy quickly became a standout MLS center-back before turning 22. His form oscillated greatly at Genk, but Ligue 1 has seen him go from strength to strength, logging full-90 shifts with regularity. Upon signing with Toulouse, the McDonald’s French menu offered a sandwich named in his honor. (“It was a promo thing, to be honest. [Ligue 1] is sponsored by McDonald’s. I’m American.”) One of the final and harshest cuts from the 2022 squad. Regularly speaks up in support of Black representation in the sport. Has recently picked up a photography hobby – film, since the fleeting nature of digital snaps lets one “lose the meaning behind taking the photo itself”.

players.name: Joe Scally
players.bio: Despite being only 23 years old, Scally is one of the most experienced members of the USMNT, a stalwart in the Bundesliga with 24 caps to his name at the time of writing. Still, he was out of the picture for most of 2025 after a bad performance at the Concacaf Nations League that drew some pointed comments from Mauricio Pochettino. A right-back who is comfortable playing on the opposite flank or as a right center-back in a three-man backline, Scally hails from Long Island, New York and came up through the New York City FC academy. As befitting his origins, he has been known to request his mom bring bagels with her from home when visiting – unfortunately, they often end up stale by the time they arrive.

players.name: Matt Freese
players.bio: For three decades, the USMNT was known for producing elite goalkeepers, and names like Tim Howard, Brad Friedel and Kasey Keller were anchors for the team who kept the USA competitive even when badly outmatched. The current state of the USA goalkeeper pool is the subject of much debate amongst fans and the rise of Matt Freese – an MLS keeper who only got his first international start last summer – is only fueling that dialogue. The relatively unheralded Harvard graduate has gone on to start nearly every game since for Mauricio Pochettino and seems a lock to start at the tournament. A solid showing for the USA this summer and a move to Europe afterwards would go a long way.

players.name: Chris Brady
players.bio: Brady took over as the starter for his club team after much-hyped fellow Chicago Fire academy graduate Gabriel Slonina left for Chelsea, and has been a far better performer than his predecessor. He was named the MLS team’s defender of the year in 2023 – fitting, as the lack of quality ahead of him kept Brady awfully busy. Former USA coach Gregg Berhalter is now in charge in Chicago, and his staff have helped improve Brady's approach to distribution, especially short-range recirculation, while his goal prevention is at career-best levels in 2026 to date. Brady is seemingly contending with USA training camp invitee Diego Kochen to be the program’s future at the position.

players.name: Alejandro Zendejas
players.bio: In a country with plenty of Mexican-American dual-nationals, Zendejas is one of a very small group to have played for both teams. A drawn-out, technical drama regarding his eligibility played out over months, eventually ending with his commitment to the USA. Under Mauricio Pochettino, Zendejas has found a role off the bench as a speedy, creative attacker. In a rarity for a USA international, Zendejas is also a key attacking player in Liga MX, captaining giants Club América and donning the No 10 shirt. Was left out of the March squad by Pochettino but did enough in the last months of the season to make the World Cup cut.

players.name: Utkir Yusupov
players.bio: Yusupov was born in Kazakhstan’s Turkistan region and moved to Tashkent with his family when he was five. He started his footballing life as a midfielder, but his team’s goalkeeper kept making mistakes so he took over the position – and stayed there. To support his family, he also worked in a shop and sold pastries in a market. “I didn’t have money to buy gloves. I would buy construction workers’ gloves at the market and wear two layers,” he recalled. In 2017, Yusupov obtained Uzbek citizenship and made his debut for the national team a year later. “I debuted in a friendly against Qatar,” he recalled. “It unexpectedly snowed in October, and I went out wearing a short-sleeved kit. I was freezing the entire match – but I didn’t concede a goal.” Yusupov is currently the first-choice goalkeeper for the national team, with excellent reflexes, and is considered a penalty-saving expert, unusually adept at reading his opponent's intentions. He saved two penalties in the 2026 World Cup qualifiers.

players.name: Abdukodir Khusanov
players.bio: The first Uzbek player in history to play in the English Premier League and France’s Ligue 1. Khusanov's father, Hikmat Khoshimov, also played for the national team at centre-back. At the age of 17 Khusanov signed for Energetik-BGU in Belarus, where he spent one difficult year. "In Belarus, I suffered being away from my parents,” he said. “Often there were training sessions three times a day. If there was heavy snow in winter, we would clear the field ourselves and then train." Khusanov’s career was transformed in 2023: he became an Asian champion as part of Uzbekistan’s Under-20 team in March, participated in the Under-20 World Cup in May, made his debut for the senior team in June, moved to Lens in July and became the youngest Uzbek player to appear in the Champions League in November. In January 2025 Khusanov moved to Manchester City. "This is one of the best transfers in the history of the club. He does not shy away from learning, takes training seriously and works with all his might. In short, Abdukodir is a very reliable player," said Pep Guardiola.

players.name: Khojiakbar Alijonov
players.bio: Alijonov was taken to football training by his parents at the age of six. His father had also played, but never reached the top level. Alijonov initially trained with boys three years older than him. “There were no age groups for players my age, so I had to train with the ones born in 1994,” he recalled. In 2005, when he was eight years old, he joined Pakhtakor’s academy, and he has now been at the club for 21 years without interruption. Today, he holds the Uzbek league record for playing continuously for the same club. He took his first steps in the academy as a forward, which is why he loves the No 7 shirt. In 2017 he debuted for Pakhtakor’s first team at left-back, and to this day he has made over 230 appearances and won the Uzbekistan championship five times, the national cup on three occasions and the Super Cup twice. He joined the national team in 2019 and has been in it long enough to appreciate the significance of their World Cup qualification. “As soon as the match against the UAE ended, I couldn’t hold back my tears right there on the pitch,” he says.

players.name: Farrukh Sayfiev
players.bio: At 35, Sayfiev is established as one of the country's finest ever full-backs. He is known as Mr Stability in Uzbek football. Born in Samarkand, Sayfiev has spent most of his career with two clubs, with seven years at Nasaf followed by six at Pakhtakor. With these teams he won five Uzbek championships – a sixth followed last year, at Neftchi – multiple national championship medals, two Uzbek Cups, and four Uzbek Super Cups. Sayfiev was once asked at which of these two clubs he would choose to end his career. "I became famous at Nasaf, and I developed into a strong player at Pakhtakor,” he said. “It's very difficult to choose between them." Sayfiev, who gave the contents of his first pay packet as a footballer entirely to his mother, is often compared to Vitaly Denisov, perhaps the best left back in the history of the Uzbekistan national team – by others, at least. "You have to be realistic,” the player himself says, “Denisov is better than me." If he sounds generous and compassionate, he does not always play that way: fuelled by an unbreaking desire to win he has received 80 yellow cards and five reds in his career.

players.name: Rustam Ashurmatov
players.bio: Ashurmatov is the nephew of the famous former international centre-back and now coach Bakhtiyor Ashurmatov, but through a combination of hard work and skill he has been able to step out of his uncle’s shadow and make his own reputation. He has developed into a solid defender for the Uzbekistan national team and, following Khusniddin Alikulov's injury, is expected to form the first-choice central defensive partnership with Abdukodir Khusanov. Ashurmatov is a product of the Tashkent club Bunyodkor, and played in both South Korea and Russia before joining his current side, Esteghlal, in 2025 after catching the eye of the Iranian side’s sporting director, Clarence Seedorf, with his ability not only to protect his own goal but to threaten from set pieces. Ashurmatov has ambitious goals for the upcoming World Cup: "We need to play high-quality football no matter who our opponent is. We need to at least advance from the group stage," the defender said. "Our group is very difficult. But we qualified for the World Cup, and only the strongest qualify."

players.name: Akmal Mozgovoy
players.bio: At 19, Mozgovoy made his debut for Karshi's Nasaf in the Super League and became a key component of Ruzikul Berdiev's team, going on to play 224 matches for them, winning the Uzbek Cup and Super Cup three times. In 2024 he helped bring the club the long-awaited championship, and it was his goal against Lokomotiv Tashkent that sealed the title. "Remembering that goal, memories from childhood tournaments come back to me,” he said. “I used to score that kind of goal often. After the match, I talked to my grandfather, and he reminded me of it. It was an unforgettable experience." At the end of that season he was named the league’s player of the year, and received offers from teams in Poland, Russia, Cyprus and elsewhere. Instead he chose to stay, and though he joined Baniyas of the UAE Championship six months later he was not happy with the decision and within seven months was back in Uzbekistan, signing for Pakhtakor. He is the kind of player you look to in crunch moments, and when Uzbekistan secured victories on penalties over Iran in the final of the Al Ain Cup in November 2025, and against Venezuela in the final of the Fifa Series tournament in Tashkent in March 2026, it was Mozgovoy who converted the decisive spot-kicks on both occasions.

players.name: Otabek Shukurov
players.bio: Another graduate of the Mashal Academy. Shukurov grew up working hard from childhood, washing cars and making shoes. He was the captain of the Uzbekistan team that won the Under-16 Asian Championship in Iran in 2012, and also played in the Under-17 World Cup the following year – Hoffenheim scouted him there and invited him for a trial, but did not proceed with the signing. He made his debut for the senior national team in 2016 and has become more than a player, a conductor of play from the centre of the field, who also has a wicket long shot. The best years of Shukurov’s career came in the UAE with Sharjah, where he spent more than four years, became known as “the Uzbek professor”, and made a huge contribution to them winning the league in 2019, for the first time in 15 years. Shukurov does a lot of charity work in his hometown of Kashkadarya, and during the pandemic provided food for 60 families there. “When I saw some footballers were doing the toilet roll challenge I felt bad, because that doesn’t help anyone. So I thought of a new challenge, something which can truly benefit poor people,” he said.  “When I saw videos of families smiling and feeling happy, this for me was real happiness.”

players.name: Jamshid Iskanderov
players.bio: Iskanderov was fast-tracked into the senior side after a run of impressive performances at the 2013 Under-20 World Cup in Turkey, making his debut that September. He might not quite have reached the level of his idols – “I like the games of Xavi, Andrés Iniesta, David Silva and Lionel Messi,” he said – but he has enjoyed plenty of success, winning the Uzbek league title three times in four years with Pakhtakor between 2012 and 2015 and collecting a fourth winner’s medal with Neftchi in 2024-25. In 2022 he won the Uzbekistan Footballer of the Year prize after an outstanding season with Navbahor Namangan, even though his club lost out on the title by a point. But for all that he has never been a key member of the Uzbek side, and as of the end of May he had been in the squad for 80 games across nearly 13 years, but started only 17. "Every player, no matter how old they are, is waiting for the list of players called up to the national team. I am waiting too," he says.

players.name: Odiljon Khamrobekov
players.bio: An irreplaceable player at the core of the national team: Khamrobekov played all but 16 minutes of Uzbekistan’s 14-game qualifying campaign, and is a guaranteed starter when fit. He also played for his country at various youth levels and in several World Cups and Asian Championships, winning the player of the tournament award at the 2018 Under-23 Asian Championships in China. "That tournament was very memorable, the final was held in heavy snow. It's unforgettable,” he said. He started his career at Nasaf, but after moving to Pakhtakor in 2019 won five successive league titles, a particularly impressive achievement given that in the middle of that run he snuck in half a season with Shabab al-Ahli in the UAE and won a cup there too. In 2025 he moved to Tractor in Iran, where he promptly won another league title. He is considered the engine at the heart of the Uzbekistan national team, and is a master at anticipating and ending opponent attacks. He works extremely hard for the team but he has not always been appreciated by fans.

players.name: Jaloliddin Masharipov
players.bio: It feels symbolic that he was born on Uzbekistan’s Independence Day - 1 September - as he has gone on to become one of the greatest Uzbek players of his generation. Due to financial difficulties during their youth, his older brother gave up his own dream of becoming a footballer in favor of Jaloliddin. When speaking of Masharipov, the phrase "colossal experience" comes to mind as a synonym. He is not only a multiple-time champion of Uzbekistan but has also played with success in foreign leagues, including at Cristiano Ronaldo's Al-Nassr. It was Masharipov who had the No7 shirt when the Portuguese great first joined the club, and after he volunteered to give it up he and Ronaldo became good friends. When the World Cup draw placed Portugal and Uzbekistan in the same group, Masharipov addressed Ronaldo on Instagram: "Dear friend, see you at the World Cup." That was in serious doubt when he sustained a serious knee injury in January, but he recovered in the nick of time.

players.name: Oston Urunov
players.bio: Urunov was born in Navoi, the region named in honor of the great Uzbek poet, thinker, and statesman, the father of the Uzbek literary language, Alisher Navoi. In 2016 Urunov became the captain of the Uzbekistan youth team, made his league debut for Navbahor the following year and in 2018 was named the young player of the year. The award was presented to him by Héctor Cúper, who then gave him his full international debut the following year. At that time, Oston was 18 years and five months old. But after that rapid ascent came a more difficult period: he moved to Ufa in Russia in 2020 and, less than a year later, to Spartak Moscow. "At Spartak everything was going well at first,” he said, “but I couldn't show my full potential because of injury after injury." He spent three years in Russia before returning to Navbahor, but in 2024 was on the move again to Persepolis in Iran, where he won his first league title in 2023-24 – but he still has high ambitions: "Since I was young, I dreamed of playing for Manchester United, and now I dream of PSG," he says.

players.name: Abduvokhid Nematov
players.bio: Nematov moved from Jizzakh to Qarshi at the age of 14 and joined Nasaf soon afterwards; more than a decade later he is still there, having won every major trophy in Uzbekistan. He is particularly good with his feet, down perhaps to the fact he started in football as a striker, and still has an eye for goal: he scored two of them in the 2023-24 season, both from penalties. "If I was afraid of making a mistake, I wouldn't be a goalkeeper. Because if a goalkeeper makes a mistake, a goal is scored," he says. In 2022 Uzbekistan hosted the Under-23 Asian Cup and Nematov was sent off in the 12th minute of their quarter-finals against Iraq, putting the team in a difficult position – and it turned out his mother was watching having made a secret trip to the stadium. "My mother came to Tashkent to watch the game without telling me that day, and I got sent off. It was terrible," Nematov recalled. In 2024 Uzbekistan qualified for the Olympics for the first time, with Nematov saving a penalty from Sergio Gomez in the match against Spain.

players.name: Sherzod Nasrullaev
players.bio: In January, after eight years, 200 games, one league title and three Uzbek cups with Nasaf, Nasrullaev made his first transfer, moving to Tashkent to join Pakhtakor. “This team has helped me grow not only as a player but as a person,” he said. “Every day, every training session, and every game I played for Nasaf will remain an unforgettable memory in my heart. This club’s colours have become part of my life.” None of this would have seemed likely when he was released from their youth system in 2016, at the age of 17, forcing him to drop into the amateur third division. Two years later he was invited back again. Under their tutelage Nasrullaev became a speedy full-back who contributes to attacks and can create dangerous chances with crosses: in 147 league games with Nasaf he scored 10 times and claimed 25 assists, impressive numbers for a defender.

players.name: Eldor Shomurodov
players.bio: Uzbekistan’s captain and all-time record goalscorer, and the first Uzbek footballer to score in Italy’s Serie A, Shomurodov was born in 1995 in Jarkurgan district, Uzbekistan’s southernmost region. His father, two uncles, and his older brother were all football players. “I remember we had many cows and sheep at home,” Shomurodov recalled. “All of my friends and cousins loved football. Parents would say: ‘First we tend to the livestock, then we go to the stadium.’” He began attending the local football school at the age of six, and at 12 he moved 180 miles away to Muborak to join Mash’al’s academy. When Rivaldo arrived at Bunyodkor in 2008, his first match in Uzbekistan was played in Muborak against Mash’al – and Shomurodov was the mascot who walked with him onto the pitch. “It was incredible – one of the best players in the world took me onto the field! I will never forget that moment,” he said. Shomurodov grew up as a fan of Chelsea, after being given a kit by an uncle when he was seven. “I always dreamed of meeting Fernando Torres and Didier Drogba and getting their autographs,” he said.

players.name: Umar Eshmurodov
players.bio: For fans of the Karshi club Nasaf, Eshmurodov is not just a player but a symbol, the heart of their team. It has not been a straightforward journey – they sold him in 2020, bought him back in 2021, sold him again in 2024 and bought him back once more in 2025 – but along the way he has played more than 300 games for them, winning the Uzbek Cup three times and the Uzbek Supercup twice. Now 33, the Nasaf head coach, Ruzikul Berdiev, calls him "a coach on the pitch". Eshmurodov made his debut for the Uzbekistan national team in 2020 and has now been in the squad for more than 50 matches. He described the team’s World Cup qualification as “the greatest achievement in our history”, adding: “This is not only the dream of our players, but also of the entire Uzbek people. We will give our all in every game when we go on the field to make this dream come true. Our current squad is stronger than ever and we are ready to make history." Eshmurodov is distinguished by his composure on the field, his positioning and his superiority in aerial duels.

players.name: Botirali Ergashev
players.bio: Though he showed plenty of early talent, Ergashev’s approach to the game – and with it his career – was transformed after he was selected for the 2015 Under-20 World Cup in New Zealand, where Uzbekistan reached the quarter-finals before losing to Senegal. "After that tournament, my attitude towards football changed radically,” he said. “I was proud to be a footballer. Because you defend the honor of your country, it also awakens the feeling of patriotism in a person." He was also part of the victorious team in the 2018 Under-23 Asian Cup in China. At the beginning of 2024 he moved to Neftchi, and with this team he became the champion of Uzbekistan and the owner of the Super Cup. He made his debut for the national team in 2018 in a match against Iran but had to wait more than five years to get a second cap, and is still largely restricted to back-up duties. "It's nice to wear the national team jersey. We're trying to live up to it," says Ergashev, who turns 29 on the day of Uzbekistan’s game against Portugal.

players.name: Dostonbek Khamdamov
players.bio: A member of the 2012 Asian Under-16 title-winning side, and again at Under-23 level in 2018, in between which he was named the best young player in Asia in 2015. All of that success and attention led to a move to the Russian club Anzhi in early 2018, but he only played nine games for the club. He returned to Uzbekistan in 2019 to join Pakhtakor, where in two years he won the league twice and the domestic cup once. He has attempted to find success abroad on several occasions, with clubs in the UAE and Qatar, but has always returned to Pakhtakor and since 2022 he has stayed there. His involvement in the national team has, like much of his career, been inconsistent but he was back in the squad in late 2025 and has so far stayed there. "Every fan and every player in Uzbekistan lives with this desire,” he said in 2016. “I hope that I can make it to the national team and contribute to the team's qualification for the World Cup."

players.name: Abdulla Abdullaev
players.bio: An experienced, tactically disciplined and versatile performer who can do a job in defence and midfield, Abdullaev has spent the bulk of his club career in Uzbekistan, apart from spells with two Emirati clubs: Dibba, whom he joined last year, and Khor Fakkan in 2023. When Olmaliq-based club AGMK sold him to Khor Fakkan it was reported as the first overseas transfer in the club’s history, and earned them €200,000. At international level, he worked his way through the Under-21s and Under-23s before a senior debut in September 2021 against Sweden. “It went very well, we followed the instructions of the coach,” he said after the 2-1 defeat. “I tried to justify the faith he has shown in me. Of course there were shortcomings, but this was a real education. This match was a big lesson for me.”

players.name: Azizjon Ganiev
players.bio: At 27, nine years after he made his full international debut and 11 after he played his first league game for Nasaf at the tender age of 16, Ganiev is hardly an unknown. But his presence as a key member of the World Cup squad is extraordinary given that of the 14 qualifying games that carried them to this tournament he played not a single minute, in fact received not one call-up. Since 2020 he has played his club football in the UAE, first with Shabab al-Ahli, where he won a league title and several cups but also suffered two serious knee injuries, before in 2024 he moved to al-Bataeh. Though he has spent most of his career in the centre of midfield he has been used increasingly in recent seasons, and particularly since his international reemergence, as an inverted winger on the right.

players.name: Azizbek Amonov
players.bio: Another footballer born in the Navoi region, not that conditions there sound ideal for the production of top-level talent. "I became interested in football in the fourth grade,” Amonov says. “When I was young, we played football around the bus stop, on the concrete pavement. Training sessions were held in such conditions." From the bus stop emerged an attacking midfielder and winger, who began his professional career at Lokomotiv Tashkent, where he won domestic league and cup titles. In 2022, he moved to Esteghlal, one of the most famous clubs in Iran, where he again won domestic league and cup titles. He has been back in Uzbekistan since 2024, and is currently with Dinamo. He made his debut for the national team in 2021 in a match against Sweden, but has not played in successive matches since 2022 – he was in the squad for the 2024 Asian Cup but returned without playing. "It doesn't matter who plays, the most important thing is the team's victory," he said.
