players.name: Igor Sergeev
players.bio: Sergeev is the top scorer in the history of Pakhtakor, Uzbekistan’s most successful club, having scored 145 goals in 262 matches for the Lions at the time of writing, winning the league five times and the cup three times. He was also the league’s top scorer in two seasons. Now 33, his career has taken him to clubs in China, the UAE, Kazakhstan and Thailand. He won the Kazakh league with Tobol, and the Thai League Cup with Pathum United. In the 2024 Asian Cup in Qatar he tore his knee ligaments in a match against Australia. Many thought his career was over but in 2025 he re-signed for Pakhtakor and that year scored 20 goals in the Uzbek championship. After his first goal after his injury, the player celebrated the goal by kissing that knee. "You can't buy the emotions after a victory in football for any money," he said. In January Sergeev moved to another new country when he signed for the Iranian club Persepolis, though competition was halted by the outbreak of war a month later.

players.name: Abbosbek Fayzullaev
players.bio: At the age of 22 Fayzullaev already has two Uzbek player of the year awards, having made his league debut aged just 17. As a child he moved from his family home in Syrdarya to Tashkent to join the Pakhtakor academy, where his uncle took him in and raised him as his own. His uncle died in 2021, the year Fayzullaev made his first-team debut, and the player still celebrates goals with a finger pointed to the heavens in tribute. "Every time I go onto the pitch I look at the stands, hoping that my uncle will come, but unfortunately that is no longer possible," he has said. Fayzullaev's wonderful free-kick against Syria at the 2023 Under-20 Asian Cup made him much more widely known in Uzbekistan and elsewhere, and in August of that year he moved to CSKA Moscow. “Many in Uzbekistan said: ‘He won’t be able to do it there, he’ll be back soon,’” he said. “Even some coaches expressed the same opinion. That gave me more motivation. I think I’ve proven myself.” Across two years in Russia he won the national cup and the super cup, and in Istanbul Basaksehir made him the most expensive player in their history.

players.name: Sherzod Esanov
players.bio: Still only 23, Esanov has already been on a journey with his club football. After spells in Russia with Akron Tolyatti and Belarus with Dynamo Brest, he returned to Uzbekistan with Bukhara earlier this year. He was part of the Uzbekistan Under-20 side that won the Asian Cup in 2023. An energetic, box-to-box midfielder, he spoke of his pride at making this summer’s World Cup squad soon after arriving in the United States: “These feelings are difficult to express in words. Playing in the World Cup is the dream of every player. I’m learning a lot, especially on the tactical side, and I feel I’m growing physically. I think this is the case with all players. Now we just need the fans to back us. We will give our lives to play for our people.”

players.name: Bekhruz Karimov
players.bio: Born in 2007 in the Namangan region, Karimov’s father was a builder abroad, and his mother was a school teacher. "I've been playing football since I was six. When I was in high school, I was rejected by Navbahor’s Under-19 team because I was too young. After that incident, I started trying to prove that age is just a number," Karimov recalls. At 18 he continues to do so, his trajectory having been transformed within 12 extraordinary months of his league debut in March 2025. He scored his first league goal that October – and his second 20 minutes later – was named in the league’s young team of the year in December, played in the Under-23 Asian Cup in January, and made his full international debut in March 2026. In April a broken toe threatened to stall his progress, but swift surgery has allowed him to return in time to participate in the World Cup, something that would have been unimaginable just over a year ago. “After the injury I was very depressed, but everyone around me supported me. This also helped me get back on my feet faster,” he said.

players.name: Avazbek Ulmasaliev
players.bio: A solid, no-nonsense centre-back, Ulmasaliev has spent his whole club career so far in Uzbekistan, moving to AGMK in 2024. He is one of several surprise picks for the final World Cup squad – his international experience has so far been largely limited to Under-23 level, and he didn’t feature during qualifying. But he did impress during Fabio Cannavaro’s experimental training camp in January when the head coach called in fringe players for assessment. Ulmasaliev said after the camp: “Everyone wanted to show what they were capable of. The friendly games were good and I was able to score my first goal. The training sessions under Cannavaro were very high quality, and the team atmosphere was excellent. A call-up to the national team is every footballer's dream. This training camp showed that everyone has a chance to get noticed.”

players.name: Jakhongir Urozov
players.bio: Jakhongir began his professional career at the Bunyodkor academy and made his debut in the first team in 2022. Tall and imposing, it did not take him long to make an impression, and the following year he was transferred to the Turkish club Eyüpspor, where he won promotion under coach Arda Turan. "He is wonderful as a person,” Urosov said. “As a coach, he is very demanding, organised and attentive." He returned to Uzbekistan in 2025 to join Dinamo, where he is one of the key defensive figures. Andrey Pyatnitsky, the former Russia and Uzbekistan dual international who coached at Dinamo, has praised Urozov as “a national team-level player” but also identified a “concentration issue” that can affect his play. Despite being a defender, Urozov can pose a threat in set pieces in front of the opponent's goal, and demonstrated this on his debut, when he scored in a 3-1 win over Gabon.
